henry fielding the history of tom jones a foundling
to the honourable george lyttleton esq one of the lords commissioners of the treasury
sir notwithstanding your constant refusal when i have ask leave to prefix your name to this dedication i must still insist on my right to desire your protection of this work
to you sir it be owe that this history be ever begin
it be by your desire that i first think of such a composition
so many year have since past that you may have perhaps forget this circumstance but your desire be to i in the nature of command and the impression of they be never to be erase from my memory
again sir without your assistance this history have never be complete
be not startle at the assertion
i do not intend to draw on you the suspicion of be a romance writer
i mean no more than that i partly owe to you my existence during great part of the time which i have employ in compose it another matter which it may be necessary to remind you of since there be certain action of which you be apt to be extremely forgetful but of these i hope i shall always have a well memory than yourself
lastly it be owe to you that the history appear what it now be
if there be in this work as some have be pleased to say a strong picture of a truly benevolent mind than be to be find in any other who that know you and a particular acquaintance of your will doubt whence that benevolence hath be copy
the world will not i believe make i the compliment of thinking i take it from myself
i care not this they shall own that the two person from whom i have take it that be to say two of the good and worthy man in the world be strongly and zealously my friend
i might be content with this and yet my vanity will add a third to the number and he one of the great and noble not only in his rank but in every public and private virtue
but here whilst my gratitude for the princely benefaction of the duke of bedford burst from my heart you must forgive my reminding you that it be you who first recommend i to the notice of my benefactor
and what be your objection to the allowance of the honour which i have sollicite
why you have commend the book so warmly that you should be ashamed of read your name before the dedication
indeed sir if the book itself doth not make you ashamed of your commendation nothing that i can here write will or ought
i be not to give up my right to your protection and patronage because you have commend my book for though i acknowledge so many obligation to you i do not add this to the number in which friendship i be convince hath so little share since that can neither biass your judgment nor pervert your integrity
an enemy may at any time obtain your commendation by only deserve it and the utmost which the fault of your friend can hope for be your silence or perhaps if too severely accuse your gentle palliation
in short sir i suspect that your dislike of public praise be your true objection to grant my request
i have observe that you have in common with my two other friend an unwillingness to hear the least mention of your own virtue that as a great poet say of one of you he might justly have say it of all three you do good by stealth and blush to find it fame
if man of this disposition be as careful to shun applause as other be to escape censure how just must be your apprehension of your character fall into my hand since what would not a man have reason to dread if attack by an author who have receive from he injury equal to my obligation to you
and will not this dread of censure increase in proportion to the matter which a man be conscious of have afford for it
if his whole life for instance should have be one continue subject of satire he may well tremble when an incensed satirist take he in hand
now sir if we apply this to your modest aversion to panegyric how reasonable will your fear of i appear
yet surely you might have gratify my ambition from this single confidence that i shall always prefer the indulgence of your inclination to the satisfaction of my own
a very strong instance of which i shall give you in this address in which i be determined to follow the example of all other dedicator and will consider not what my patron really deserve to have write but what he will be good pleased to read
without further preface then i here present you with the labour of some year of my life
what merit these labour have be already know to yourself
if from your favourable judgment i have conceive some esteem for they it cannot be impute to vanity since i should have agree as implicitly to your opinion have it be give in favour of any other man s production
negatively at least i may be allow to say that have i be sensible of any great demerit in the work you be the last person to whose protection i would have venture to recommend it
from the name of my patron indeed i hope my reader will be convince at his very entrance on this work that he will find in the whole course of it nothing prejudicial to the cause of religion and virtue nothing inconsistent with the strict rule of decency nor which can offend even the chaste eye in the perusal
on the contrary i declare that to recommend goodness and innocence hath be my sincere endeavour in this history
this honest purpose you have be pleased to think i have attain and to say the truth it be likeli to be attain in book of this kind for an example be a kind of picture in which virtue become as it be an object of sight and strike we with an idea of that loveliness which plato assert there be in her naked charm
besides display that beauty of virtue which may attract the admiration of mankind i have attempt to engage a strong motive to human action in her favour by convince man that their true interest direct they to a pursuit of she
for this purpose i have show that no acquisition of guilt can compensate the loss of that solid inward comfort of mind which be the sure companion of innocence and virtue nor can in the least balance the evil of that horror and anxiety which in their room guilt introduce into our bosom
and again that as these acquisition be in themselves generally worthless so be the mean to attain they not only base and infamous but at good incertain and always full of danger
lastly i have endeavour strongly to inculcate that virtue and innocence can scarce ever be injure but by indiscretion and that it be this alone which often betray they into the snare that deceit and villainy spread for they
a moral which i have the more industriously labour as the teaching it be of all other the likeli to be attend with success since i believe it be much easy to make good man wise than to make bad man good
for these purpose i have employ all the wit and humour of which i be master in the follow history wherein i have endeavour to laugh mankind out of their favourite folly and vice
how far i have succeed in this good attempt i shall submit to the candid reader with only two request first that he will not expect to find perfection in this work and secondly that he will excuse some part of it if they fall short of that little merit which i hope may appear in other
i will detain you sir no long
indeed i have run into a preface while i profess to write a dedication
but how can it be otherwise
i dare not praise you and the only mean i know of to avoid it when you be in my thought be either to be entirely silent or to turn my thought to some other subject
pardon therefore what i have say in this epistle not only without your consent but absolutely against it and give i at least leave in this public manner to declare that i be with the high respect and gratitude sir your most oblige obedient humble servant henry fielding
the history of tom jones a foundling
book i
contain as much of the birth of the foundling as be necessary or proper to acquaint the reader with in the beginning of this history
chapter i
the introduction to the work or bill of fare to the feast
an author ought to consider himself not as a gentleman who give a private or eleemosynary treat but rather as one who keep a public ordinary at which all person be welcome for their money
in the former case it be well know that the entertainer provide what fare he please and though this should be very indifferent and utterly disagreeable to the taste of his company they must not find any fault nay on the contrary good breeding force they outwardly to approve and to commend whatever be set before they
now the contrary of this happen to the master of an ordinary
man who pay for what they eat will insist on gratify their palate however nice and whimsical these may prove and if everything be not agreeable to their taste will challenge a right to censure to abuse and to d--n their dinner without controul
to prevent therefore give offence to their customer by any such disappointment it hath be usual with the honest and well-mean host to provide a bill of fare which all person may peruse at their first entrance into the house and have thence acquaint themselves with the entertainment which they may expect may either stay and regale with what be provide for they or may depart to some other ordinary well accommodate to their taste
as we do not disdain to borrow wit or wisdom from any man who be capable of lend we either we have condescend to take a hint from these honest victualler and shall prefix not only a general bill of fare to our whole entertainment but shall likewise give the reader particular bill to every course which be to be serve up in this and the ensue volume
the provision then which we have here make be no other than human nature
nor do i fear that my sensible reader though most luxurious in his taste will start cavil or be offend because i have name but one article
the tortoise--as the alderman of bristol well learn in eating know by much experience--besides the delicious calipash and calipee contain many different kind of food nor can the learned reader be ignorant that in human nature though here collect under one general name be such prodigious variety that a cook will have soon go through all the several specie of animal and vegetable food in the world than an author will be able to exhaust so extensive a subject
an objection may perhaps be apprehend from the more delicate that this dish be too common and vulgar for what else be the subject of all the romance novel play and poem with which the stall abound
many exquisite viand might be reject by the epicure if it be a sufficient cause for his contemning of they as common and vulgar that something be to be find in the most paltry alley under the same name
in reality true nature be as difficult to be meet with in author as the bayonne ham or bologna sausage be to be find in the shop
but the whole to continue the same metaphor consist in the cookery of the author for as mr pope tell us-- true wit be nature to advantage dr what oft be think but ne er so well expr the same animal which hath the honour to have some part of his flesh eat at the table of a duke may perhaps be degrade in another part and some of his limb gibbet as it be in the vile stall in town
where then lie the difference between the food of the nobleman and the porter if both be at dinner on the same ox or calf but in the seasoning the dressing the garnishing and the setting forth
hence the one provoke and incite the most languid appetite and the other turn and pall that which be the sharp and keen
in like manner the excellence of the mental entertainment consist less in the subject than in the author s skill in well dress it up
how pleased therefore will the reader be to find that we have in the follow work adhere closely to one of the high principle of the good cook which the present age or perhaps that of heliogabalus hath produce
this great man as be well known to all lover of polite eating begin at first by set plain thing before his hungry guest rise afterwards by degree as their stomach may be suppose to decrease to the very quintessence of sauce and spice
in like manner we shall represent human nature at first to the keen appetite of our reader in that more plain and simple manner in which it be find in the country and shall hereafter hash and ragoo it with all the high french and italian seasoning of affectation and vice which court and city afford
by these mean we doubt not but our reader may be render desirous to read on for ever as the great person just above-mention be suppose to have make some person eat
have premise thus much we will now detain those who like our bill of fare no long from their diet and shall proceed directly to serve up the first course of our history for their entertainment
chapter ii
a short description of squire allworthy and a full account of miss bridget allworthy his sister
in that part of the western division of this kingdom which be commonly call somersetshire there lately live and perhaps live still a gentleman whose name be allworthy and who might well be call the favourite of both nature and fortune for both of these seem to have contend which should bless and enrich he most
in this contention nature may seem to some to have come off victorious as she bestow on he many gift while fortune have only one gift in her power but in pour forth this she be so very profuse that other perhaps may think this single endowment to have be more than equivalent to all the various blessing which he enjoy from nature
from the former of these he derive an agreeable person a sound constitution a solid understanding and a benevolent heart by the latter he be decreed to the inheritance of one of the large estate in the county
this gentleman have in his youth marry a very worthy and beautiful woman of whom he have be extremely fond by she he have three child all of whom die in their infancy
he have likewise have the misfortune of bury this beloved wife herself about five year before the time in which this history chuse to set out
this loss however great he bear like a man of sense and constancy though it must be confest he would often talk a little whimsically on this head for he sometimes say he look on himself as still marry and consider his wife as only go a little before he a journey which he should most certainly soon or later take after she and that he have not the least doubt of meet she again in a place where he should never part with her more--sentiment for which his sense be arraign by one part of his neighbour his religion by a second and his sincerity by a third
he now live for the most part retire in the country with one sister for whom he have a very tender affection
this lady be now somewhat past the age of thirty an aera at which in the opinion of the malicious the title of old maid may with no impropriety be assume
she be of that specie of woman whom you commend rather for good quality than beauty and who be generally call by their own sex very good sort of woman--as good a sort of woman madam as you would wish to know
indeed she be so far from regret want of beauty that she never mention that perfection if it can be call one without contempt and would often thank god she be not as handsome as miss such-a-one whom perhaps beauty have lead into error which she might have otherwise avoid
miss bridget allworthy for that be the name of this lady very rightly conceive the charm of person in a woman to be no well than snare for herself as well as for other and yet so discreet be she in her conduct that her prudence be as much on the guard as if she have all the snare to apprehend which be ever lay for her whole sex
indeed i have observe though it may seem unaccountable to the reader that this guard of prudence like the train band be always readiest to go on duty where there be the least danger
it often basely and cowardly desert those paragon for whom the man be all wish sigh dying and spread every net in their power and constantly attend at the heel of that high order of woman for whom the other sex have a more distant and awful respect and whom from despair i suppose of success they never venture to attack
reader i think proper before we proceed any far together to acquaint thee that i intend to digress through this whole history as often as i see occasion of which i be myself a well judge than any pitiful critic whatever and here i must desire all those critic to mind their own business and not to intermeddle with affair or work which no way concern they for till they produce the authority by which they be constitute judge i shall not plead to their jurisdiction
chapter iii
an odd accident which befel mr allworthy at his return home
the decent behaviour of mrs deborah wilkins with some proper animadversion on bastard
i have tell my reader in the precede chapter that mr allworthy inherit a large fortune that he have a good heart and no family
hence doubtless it will be conclude by many that he live like an honest man owe no one a shilling take nothing but what be his own keep a good house entertain his neighbour with a hearty welcome at his table and be charitable to the poor i e
to those who have rather beg than work by give they the offal from it that he die immensely rich and build an hospital
and true it be that he do many of these thing but have he do nothing more i should have leave he to have record his own merit on some fair freestone over the door of that hospital
matter of a much more extraordinary kind be to be the subject of this history or i should grossly mis-spend my time in write so voluminous a work and you my sagacious friend might with equal profit and pleasure travel through some page which certain droll author have be facetiously pleased to call the history of england
mr allworthy have be absent a full quarter of a year in london on some very particular business though i know not what it be but judge of its importance by its have detain he so long from home whence he have not be absent a month at a time during the space of many year
he come to his house very late in the evening and after a short supper with his sister retire much fatigued to his chamber
here have spend some minute on his knee--a custom which he never break through on any account--he be prepare to step into bed when upon open the cloathe to his great surprize he behold an infant wrapt up in some coarse linen in a sweet and profound sleep between his sheet
he stand some time lose in astonishment at this sight but as good nature have always the ascendant in his mind he soon begin to be touch with sentiment of compassion for the little wretch before he
he then ring his bell and order an elderly woman-servant to rise immediately and come to he and in the meantime be so eager in contemplate the beauty of innocence appear in those lively colour with which infancy and sleep always display it that his thought be too much engage to reflect that he be in his shirt when the matron come in
she have indeed give she master sufficient time to dress himself for out of respect to he and regard to decency she have spend many minute in adjust her hair at the look-glass notwithstanding all the hurry in which she have be summon by the servant and though her master for aught she know lie expire in an apoplexy or in some other fit
it will not be wonder at that a creature who have so strict a regard to decency in her own person should be shock at the least deviation from it in another
she therefore no soon open the door and see her master stand by the bedside in his shirt with a candle in his hand than she start back in a most terrible fright and might perhaps have swoon away have he not now recollect his being undr and put an end to her terror by desire she to stay without the door till he have throw some cloathe over his back and be become incapable of shock the pure eye of mrs deborah wilkins who though in the fifty-second year of her age vow she have never behold a man without his coat
sneerer and prophane wit may perhaps laugh at her first fright yet my graver reader when he consider the time of night the summon from her bed and the situation in which she find her master will highly justify and applaud her conduct unless the prudence which must be suppose to attend maiden at that period of life at which mrs deborah have arrive should a little lessen his admiration
when mrs deborah return into the room and be acquaint by she master with the finding the little infant her consternation be rather great than his have be nor could she refrain from cry out with great horror of accent as well as look my good sir
whatbe to be do mr allworthy answer she must take care of the child that evening and in the morning he would give order to provide it a nurse
yes sir say she and i hope your worship will send out your warrant to take up the hussy its mother for she must be one of the neighbourhood and i should be glad to see she commit to bridewell and whipt at the cart s tail
indeed such wicked slut cannot be too severely punish
iwill warrant tis not her first by her impudence in lay it to your worship in lay it to i deborah answer allworthy i cannot think she hath any such design
i suppose she hath only take this method to provide for her child and truly i be glad she hath not do bad i donot know what be bad cry deborah than for such wicked strumpet to lay their sin at honest man s door and though your worship know your own innocence yet the world be censorious and it hath be many an honest man s hap to pass for the father of child he never begot and if your worship should provide for the child it may make the people the apter to believe besides why should your worship provide for what the parish be oblige to maintain
for my own part if it be an honest man s child indeed--but for my own part it go against i to touch these misbegotten wretch whom i donot look upon as my fellow-creature
faugh
how it stink
it doth not smell like a christian
if i might be so bold to give my advice i would have it put in a basket and send out and lay at the churchwarden s door
it be a good night only a little rainy and windy and if it be well wrapt up and put in a warm basket it be two to one but it live till it be find in the morning
but if it should not we have discharge our duty in take proper care of it and it be perhaps well for such creature to die in a state of innocence than to grow up and imitate their mother for nothing well can be expect of they there be some stroke in this speech which perhaps would have offend mr allworthy have he strictly attend to it but he have now get one of his finger into the infant s hand which by its gentle pressure seem to implore his assistance have certainly out-plead the eloquence of mrs deborah have it be ten time great than it be
he now give mrs deborah positive order to take the child to her own bed and to call up a maid-servant to provide it pap and other thing against it wake
he likewise order that proper cloathe should be procure for it early in the morning and that it should be bring to himself as soon as he be stir
such be the discernment of mrs wilkins and such the respect she bear her master under whom she enjoy a most excellent place that her scruple give way to his peremptory command and she take the child under her arm without any apparent disgust at the illegality of its birth and declare it be a sweet little infant walk off with it to her own chamber
allworthy here betake himself to those pleasing slumber which a heart that hunger after goodness be apt to enjoy when thoroughly satisfied
as these be possibly sweet than what be occasion by any other hearty meal i should take more pain to display they to the reader if i know any air to recommend he to for the procure such an appetite
chapter iv
the reader s neck bring into danger by a description his escape and the great condescension of miss bridget allworthy
the gothic stile of building could produce nothing noble than mr allworthy s house
there be an air of grandeur in it that strike you with awe and rival the beauty of the good grecian architecture and it be as commodious within as venerable without
it stand on the south-east side of a hill but near the bottom than the top of it so as to be shelter from the north-east by a grove of old oak which rise above it in a gradual ascent of near half a mile and yet high enough to enjoy a most charming prospect of the valley beneath
in the midst of the grove be a fine lawn slop down towards the house near the summit of which rise a plentiful spring gush out of a rock cover with fir and form a constant cascade of about thirty foot not carry down a regular flight of step but tumble in a natural fall over the broken and mossy stone till it come to the bottom of the rock then run off in a pebly channel that with many less fall wind along till it fall into a lake at the foot of the hill about a quarter of a mile below the house on the south side and which be see from every room in the front
out of this lake which fill the center of a beautiful plain embellish with group of beech and elm and feed with sheep issue a river that for several mile be see to meander through an amazing variety of meadow and wood till it empty itself into the sea with a large arm of which and an island beyond it the prospect be close
on the right of this valley open another of less extent adorn with several village and terminate by one of the tower of an old ruin abby grow over with ivy and part of the front which remain still entire
the left-hand scene present the view of a very fine park compose of very unequal ground and agreeably vary with all the diversity that hill lawn wood and water lay out with admirable taste but owe less to art than to nature could give
beyond this the country gradually rise into a ridge of wild mountain the top of which be above the cloud
it be now the middle of may and the morning be remarkably serene when mr allworthy walk forth on the terrace where the dawn open every minute that lovely prospect we have before describe to his eye and now have send forth stream of light which ascend the blue firmament before he as harbinger precede his pomp in the full blaze of his majesty rise the sun than which one object alone in this low creation could be more glorious and that mr allworthy himself present--a human be replete with benevolence meditate in what manner he might render himself most acceptable to his creator by do most good to his creature
reader take care
i have unadvisedly lead thee to the top of as high a hill as mr allworthy s and how to get thee down without break thy neck i do not well know
however let we e en venture to slide down together for miss bridget ring her bell and mr allworthy be summon to breakfast where i must attend and if you please shall be glad of your company
the usual compliment have past between mr allworthy and miss bridget and the tea being pour out he summon mrs wilkins and tell his sister he have a present for she for which she thank he--imagine i suppose it have be a gown or some ornament for her person
indeed he very often make she such present and she in complacence to he spend much time in adorn herself
i say in complacence to he because she always expr the great contempt for dress and for those lady who make it their study
but if such be her expectation how be she disappoint when mrs wilkins accord to the order she have receive from her master produce the little infant
great surprize as hath be observe be apt to be silent and so be miss bridget till her brother begin and tell she the whole story which as the reader know it already we shall not repeat
miss bridget have always exprest so great a regard for what the lady be pleased to call virtue and have herself maintain such a severity of character that it be expect especially by wilkins that she would have vent much bitterness on this occasion and would have vote for send the child as a kind of noxious animal immediately out of the house but on the contrary she rather take the good-natured side of the question intimate some compassion for the helpless little creature and commend her brother s charity in what he have do
perhaps the reader may account for this behaviour from her condescension to mr allworthy when we have inform he that the good man have end his narrative with own a resolution to take care of the child and to breed he up as his own for to acknowledge the truth she be always ready to oblige her brother and very seldom if ever contradict his sentiment
she would indeed sometimes make a few observation as that man be headstrong and must have their own way and would wish she have be blest with an independent fortune but these be always vent in a low voice and at the most amount only to what be call mutter
however what she withhold from the infant she bestow with the utmost profuseness on the poor unknown mother whom she call an impudent slut a wanton hussy an audacious harlot a wicked jade a vile strumpet with every other appellation with which the tongue of virtue never fail to lash those who bring a disgrace on the sex
a consultation be now enter into how to proceed in order to discover the mother
a scrutiny be first make into the character of the female servant of the house who be all acquit by mrs wilkins and with apparent merit for she have collect they herself and perhaps it would be difficult to find such another set of scarecrow
the next step be to examine among the inhabitant of the parish and this be refer to mrs wilkins who be to enquire with all imaginable diligence and to make her report in the afternoon
matter be thus settle mr allworthy withdraw to his study as be his custom and leave the child to his sister who at his desire have undertake the care of it
chapter v
contain a few common matter with a very uncommon observation upon they
when her master be depart mrs deborah stand silent expect she cue from miss bridget for as to what have past before her master the prudent housekeeper by no mean rely upon it as she have often know the sentiment of the lady in her brother s absence to differ greatly from those which she have express in his presence
miss bridget do not however suffer she to continue long in this doubtful situation for have look some time earnestly at the child as it lie asleep in the lap of mrs deborah the good lady could not forbear give it a hearty kiss at the same time declare herself wonderfully please with its beauty and innocence
mrs deborah no soon observe this than she fall to squeeze and kissing with as great rapture as sometimes inspire the sage dame of forty and five towards a youthful and vigorous bridegroom cry out in a shrill voice o the dear little creature the dear sweet pretty creature
well i vow it be as fine a boy as ever be see these exclamation continue till they be interrupt by the lady who now proceed to execute the commission give she by her brother and give order for provide all necessary for the child appoint a very good room in the house for his nursery
her order be indeed so liberal that have it be a child of her own she could not have exceed they but lest the virtuous reader may condemn she for show too great regard to a base-bear infant to which all charity be condemn by law as irreligious we think proper to observe that she conclude the whole with say since it be her brother s whim to adopt the little brat she suppose little master must be treat with great tenderness
for her part she could not help think it be an encouragement to vice but that she know too much of the obstinacy of mankind to oppose any of their ridiculous humour with reflection of this nature she usually as have be hint accompany every act of compliance with her brother s inclination and surely nothing could more contribute to heighten the merit of this compliance than a declaration that she know at the same time the folly and unreasonableness of those inclination to which she submit
tacit obedience imply no force upon the will and consequently may be easily and without any pain preserve but when a wife a child a relation or a friend perform what we desire with grumbling and reluctance with expression of dislike and dissatisfaction the manifest difficulty which they undergo must greatly enhance the obligation
as this be one of those deep observation which very few reader can be suppose capable of make themselves i have think proper to lend they my assistance but this be a favour rarely to be expect in the course of my work
indeed i shall seldom or never so indulge he unless in such instance as this where nothing but the inspiration with which we writer be gift can possibly enable any one to make the discovery
chapter vi
mrs deborah be introduce into the parish with a simile
a short account of jenny jones with the difficulty and discouragement which may attend young woman in the pursuit of learn
mrs deborah have dispose of the child accord to the will of she master now prepare to visit those habitation which be suppose to conceal its mother
not otherwise than when a kite tremendous bird be beheld by the feather generation soar aloft and hover over their head the amorous dove and every innocent little bird spread wide the alarm and fly tremble to their hiding-place
he proudly beat the air conscious of his dignity and meditate intend mischief
so when the approach of mrs deborah be proclaim through the street all the inhabitant run tremble into their house each matron dread lest the visit should fall to her lot
she with stately step proudly advance over the field aloft she bear her tower head fill with conceit of her own pre-eminence and scheme to effect her intend discovery
the sagacious reader will not from this simile imagine these poor people have any apprehension of the design with which mrs wilkins be now come towards they but as the great beauty of the simile may possibly sleep these hundred year till some future commentator shall take this work in hand i think proper to lend the reader a little assistance in this place
it be my intention therefore to signify that as it be the nature of a kite to devour little bird so be it the nature of such person as mrs wilkins to insult and tyrannize over little people
this be indeed the mean which they use to recompense to themselves their extreme servility and condescension to their superior for nothing can be more reasonable than that slave and flatterer should exact the same taxis on all below they which they themselves pay to all above they
whenever mrs deborah have occasion to exert any extraordinary condescension to mrs bridget and by that mean have a little soured her natural disposition it be usual with she to walk forth among these people in order to refine her temper by venting and as it be purge off all ill humour on which account she be by no mean a welcome visitant to say the truth she be universally dread and hat by they all
on her arrival in this place she go immediately to the habitation of an elderly matron to whom as this matron have the good fortune to resemble herself in the comeliness of her person as well as in her age she have generally be more favourable than to any of the rest
to this woman she impart what have happen and the design upon which she be come thither that morning
these two begin presently to scrutinize the character of the several young girl who live in any of those house and at last fix their strong suspicion on one jenny jones who they both agree be the likeli person to have commit this fact
this jenny jones be no very comely girl either in her face or person but nature have somewhat compensate the want of beauty with what be generally more esteemed by those lady whose judgment be arrive at year of perfect maturity for she have give she a very uncommon share of understanding
this gift jenny have a good deal improve by erudition
she have live several year a servant with a schoolmaster who discover a great quickness of part in the girl and an extraordinary desire of learn--for every leisure hour she be always find reading in the book of the scholar--have the good-nature or folly--just as the reader please to call it--to instruct she so far that she obtain a competent skill in the latin language and be perhaps as good a scholar as most of the young man of quality of the age
this advantage however like most other of an extraordinary kind be attend with some small inconvenience for as it be not to be wonder at that a young woman so well accomplish should have little relish for the society of those whom fortune have make she equal but whom education have render so much her inferior so be it matter of no great astonishment that this superiority in jenny together with that behaviour which be its certain consequence should produce among the rest some little envy and ill-will towards she and these have perhaps secretly burn in the bosom of her neighbour ever since her return from her service
their envy do not however display itself openly till poor jenny to the surprize of everybody and to the vexation of all the young woman in these part have publickly shine forth on a sunday in a new silk gown with a laced cap and other proper appendage to these
the flame which have before lain in embryo now burst forth
jenny have by her learning increase her own pride which none of she neighbour be kind enough to feed with the honour she seem to demand and now instead of respect and adoration she gain nothing but hatred and abuse by her finery
the whole parish declare she could not come honestly by such thing and parent instead of wish their daughter the same felicitate themselves that their child have they not
hence perhaps it be that the good woman first mention the name of this poor girl to mrs wilkins but there be another circumstance that confirm the latter in her suspicion for jenny have lately be often at mr allworthy s house
she have officiate as nurse to miss bridget in a violent fit of illness and have sit up many night with that lady besides which she have be see there the very day before mr allworthy s return by mrs wilkins herself though that sagacious person have not at first conceive any suspicion of she on that account for as she herself say she have always esteem jenny as a very sober girl though indeed she know very little of she and have rather suspect some of those wanton trollop who give themselves air because forsooth they think themselves handsome jenny be now summon to appear in person before mrs deborah which she immediately do
when mrs deborah put on the gravity of a judge with somewhat more than his austerity begin an oration with the word you audacious strumpet in which she proceed rather to pass sentence on the prisoner than to accuse she
though mrs deborah be fully satisfied of the guilt of jenny from the reason above shewn it be possible mr allworthy might have require some strong evidence to have convict she but she save she accuser any such trouble by freely confess the whole fact with which she be charge
this confession though deliver rather in term of contrition as it appear do not at all mollify mrs deborah who now pronounce a second judgment against she in more opprobrious language than before nor have it any well success with the bystander who be now grow very numerous
many of they cry out they think what madam s silk gown would end in other speak sarcastically of her learning
not a single female be present but find some mean of express she abhorrence of poor jenny who bear all very patiently except the malice of one woman who reflect upon her person and toss up her nose say the man must have a good stomach who would give silk gown for such sort of trumpery jenny reply to this with a bitterness which might have surprize a judicious person who have observe the tranquillity with which she bear all the affront to her chastity but her patience be perhaps tired out for this be a virtue which be very apt to be fatigue by exercise
mrs deborah have succeed beyond her hope in her inquiry return with much triumph and at the appoint hour make a faithful report to mr allworthy who be much surprize at the relation for he have hear of the extraordinary part and improvement of this girl whom he intend to have give in marriage together with a small living to a neighbouring curate
his concern therefore on this occasion be at least equal to the satisfaction which appear in mrs deborah and to many reader may seem much more reasonable
miss bridget bless herself and say for her part she should never hereafter entertain a good opinion of any woman for jenny before this have the happiness of be much in her good grace also
the prudent housekeeper be again dispatch to bring the unhappy culprit before mr allworthy in order not as it be hop by some and expect by all to be send to the house of correction but to receive wholesome admonition and reproof which those who relish that kind of instructive writing may peruse in the next chapter
chapter vii
contain such grave matter that the reader cannot laugh once through the whole chapter unless peradventure he should laugh at the author
when jenny appear mr allworthy take she into his study and speak to she as follow you know child it be in my power as a magistrate to punish you very rigorously for what you have do and you will perhaps be the more apt to fear i should execute that power because you have in a manner lay your sin at my door
but perhaps this be one reason which hath determine i to act in a milder manner with you for as no private resentment should ever influence a magistrate i will be so far from consider your have deposit the infant in my house as an aggravation of your offence that i will suppose in your favour this to have proceed from a natural affection to your child since you might have some hope to see it thus well provide for than be in the power of yourself or its wicked father to provide for it
i should indeed have be highly offend with you have you expose the little wretch in the manner of some inhuman mother who seem no less to have abandon their humanity than to have part with their chastity
it be the other part of your offence therefore upon which i intend to admonish you i mean the violation of your chastity a crime however lightly it may be treat by debauched person very heinous in itself and very dreadful in its consequence
the heinous nature of this offence must be sufficiently apparent to every christian inasmuch as it be commit in defiance of the law of our religion and of the express command of he who found that religion
and here its consequence may well be argue to be dreadful for what can be more so than to incur the divine displeasure by the breach of the divine command and that in an instance against which the high vengeance be specifically denounce
but these thing though too little i be afraid regard be so plain that mankind however they may want to be remind can never need information on this head
a hint therefore to awaken your sense of this matter shall suffice for i would inspire you with repentance and not drive you to desperation
there be other consequence not indeed so dreadful or replete with horror as this and yet such as if attentively consider must one would think deter all of your sex at least from the commission of this crime
for by it you be render infamous and drive like leper of old out of society at least from the society of all but wicked and reprobate person for no other will associate with you
if you have fortune you be hereby render incapable of enjoy they if you have none you be disabled from acquire any nay almost of procure your sustenance for no person of character will receive you into their house
thus you be often drive by necessity itself into a state of shame and misery which unavoidably end in the destruction of both body and soul
can any pleasure compensate these evil
can any temptation have sophistry and delusion strong enough to persuade you to so simple a bargain
or can any carnal appetite so overpower your reason or so totally lie it asleep as to prevent your fly with affright and terror from a crime which carry such punishment always with it
how base and mean must that woman be how void of that dignity of mind and decent pride without which we be not worthy the name of human creature who can bear to level herself with the low animal and to sacrifice all that be great and noble in she all her heavenly part to an appetite which she hath in common with the vile branch of the creation
for no woman sure will plead the passion of love for an excuse
this would be to own herself the mere tool and bubble of the man
love however barbarously we may corrupt and pervert its meaning as it be a laudable be a rational passion and can never be violent but when reciprocal for though the scripture bid we love our enemy it mean not with that fervent love which we naturally bear towards our friend much less that we should sacrifice to they our life and what ought to be dearer to we our innocence
now in what light but that of an enemy can a reasonable woman regard the man who solicit she to entail on herself all the misery i have describe to you and who would purchase to himself a short trivial contemptible pleasure so greatly at her expense
for by the law of custom the whole shame with all its dreadful consequence fall intirely upon she
can love which always seek the good of its object attempt to betray a woman into a bargain where she be so greatly to be the loser
if such corrupter therefore should have the impudence to pretend a real affection for she ought not the woman to regard he not only as an enemy but as the bad of all enemy a false designing treacherous pretended friend who intend not only to debauch her body but her understanding at the same time here jenny express great concern allworthy pause a moment and then proceed i have talk thus to you child not to insult you for what be past and irrevocable but to caution and strengthen you for the future
nor should i have take this trouble but from some opinion of your good sense notwithstanding the dreadful slip you have make and from some hope of your hearty repentance which be found on the openness and sincerity of your confession
if these do not deceive i i will take care to convey you from this scene of your shame where you shall by be unknown avoid the punishment which as i have say be allot to your crime in this world and i hope by repentance you will avoid the much heavy sentence denounce against it in the other
be a good girl the rest of your day and want shall be no motive to your going astray and believe i there be more pleasure even in this world in an innocent and virtuous life than in one debauched and vicious
as to your child let no thought concern it mol you i will provide for it in a well manner than you can ever hope
and now nothing remain but that you inform i who be the wicked man that seduce you for my anger against he will be much great than you have experience on this occasion jenny now lift her eye from the ground and with a modest look and decent voice thus began to know you sir and not love your goodness would be an argument of total want of sense or goodness in any one
in i it would amount to the high ingratitude not to feel in the most sensible manner the great degree of goodness you have be pleased to exert on this occasion
as to my concern for what be past i know you will spare my blush the repetition
my future conduct will much well declare my sentiment than any profession i can now make
i beg leave to assure you sir that i take your advice much kind than your generous offer with which you conclude it for as you be pleased to say sir it be an instance of your opinion of my understanding here her tear flow apace she stop a few moment and then proceed thus indeed sir your kindness overcome i but i will endeavour to deserve this good opinion for if i have the understanding you be so kindly pleased to allow i such advice cannot be throw away upon i
i thank you sir heartily for your intend kindness to my poor helpless child he be innocent and i hope will live to be grateful for all the favour you shall show he
but now sir i must on my knee entreat you not to persist in ask i to declare the father of my infant
i promise you faithfully you shall one day know but i be under the most solemn tie and engagement of honour as well as the most religious vow and protestation to conceal his name at this time
and i know you too well to think you would desire i should sacrifice either my honour or my religion mr allworthy whom the least mention of those sacred word be sufficient to stagger hesitate a moment before he reply and then tell she she have do wrong to enter into such engagement to a villain but since she have he could not insist on she break they
he say it be not from a motive of vain curiosity he have inquire but in order to punish the fellow at least that he might not ignorantly confer favour on the undeserving
as to these point jenny satisfy he by the most solemn assurance that the man be entirely out of his reach and be neither subject to his power nor in any probability of become an object of his goodness
the ingenuity of this behaviour have gain jenny so much credit with this worthy man that he easily believe what she tell he for as she have disdain to excuse herself by a lie and have hazard his further displeasure in her present situation rather than she would forfeit her honour or integrity by betray another he have but little apprehension that she would be guilty of falsehood towards himself
he therefore dismiss she with assurance that he would very soon remove she out of the reach of that obloquy she have incur conclude with some additional document in which he recommend repentance say consider child there be one still to reconcile yourself to whose favour be of much great importance to you than mine chapter viii
a dialogue between mesdames bridget and deborah contain more amusement but less instruction than the former
when mr allworthy have retire to his study with jenny jones as hath be see mrs bridget with the good housekeeper have betake themselves to a post next adjoining to the say study whence through the conveyance of a keyhole they suck in at their ear the instructive lecture deliver by mr allworthy together with the answer of jenny and indeed every other particular which pass in the last chapter
this hole in her brother s study-door be indeed as well know to mrs bridget and have be as frequently apply to by she as the famous hole in the wall be by thisbe of old
this serve to many good purpose
for by such mean mrs bridget become often acquainted with her brother s inclination without give he the trouble of repeat they to she
it be true some inconvenience attend this intercourse and she have sometimes reason to cry out with thisbe in shakspeare o wicked wicked wall for as mr allworthy be a justice of peace certain thing occur in examination concern bastard and such like which be apt to give great offence to the chaste ear of virgin especially when they approach the age of forty as be the case of mrs bridget
however she have on such occasion the advantage of conceal her blush from the eye of man and de non apparentibus et non existentibus eadem est ratio in english when a woman be not see to blush she doth not blush at all both the good woman keep strict silence during the whole scene between mr allworthy and the girl but as soon as it be end and that gentleman be out of hearing mrs deborah could not help exclaim against the clemency of her master and especially against his suffer she to conceal the father of the child which she swear she would have out of she before the sun set
at these word mrs bridget discompose her feature with a smile a thing very unusual to she
not that i would have my reader imagine that this be one of those wanton smile which homer would have you conceive come from venus when he call she the laughter-love goddess nor be it one of those smile which lady seraphina shoot from the stage-box and which venus would quit her immortality to be able to equal
no this be rather one of those smile which might be suppose to have come from the dimpled cheek of the august tisiphone or from one of the miss her sister
with such a smile then and with a voice sweet as the evening breeze of boreas in the pleasant month of november mrs bridget gently reprove the curiosity of mrs deborah a vice with which it seem the latter be too much taint and which the former inveigh against with great bitterness add that among all her fault she thank heaven her enemy could not accuse she of pry into the affair of other people she then proceed to commend the honour and spirit with which jenny have act
she say she could not help agree with her brother that there be some merit in the sincerity of her confession and in her integrity to her lover that she have always think she a very good girl and doubt not but she have be seduce by some rascal who have be infinitely more to blame than herself and very probably have prevail with she by a promise of marriage or some other treacherous proceeding
this behaviour of mrs bridget greatly surprise mrs deborah for this well-breed woman seldom open her lip either to her master or his sister till she have first sound their inclination with which her sentiment be always consonant
here however she think she might have launch forth with safety and the sagacious reader will not perhaps accuse she of want of sufficient forecast in so do but will rather admire with what wonderful celerity she tack about when she find herself steer a wrong course
nay madam say this able woman and truly great politician i must own i cannot help admire the girl s spirit as well as your ladyship
and as your ladyship say if she be deceive by some wicked man the poor wretch be to be pity
and to be sure as your ladyship say the girl hath always appear like a good honest plain girl and not vain of her face forsooth as some wanton hussey in the neighbourhood be you say true deborah say miss bridget
if the girl have be one of those vain trollop of which we have too many in the parish i should have condemn my brother for his lenity towards she
i see two farmer daughter at church the other day with bare neck
i protest they shock i
if wench will hang out lur for fellow it be no matter what they suffer
i detest such creature and it would be much well for they that their face have be seam with the smallpox but i must confess i never see any of this wanton behaviour in poor jenny some artful villain i be convince hath betray nay perhaps force she and i pity the poor wretch with all my heart mrs deborah approve all these sentiment and the dialogue conclude with a general and bitter invective against beauty and with many compassionate consideration for all honest plain girl who be delude by the wicked art of deceitful man
chapter ix
contain matter which will surprize the reader
jenny return home well pleased with the reception she have meet with from mr allworthy whose indulgence to she she industriously make public partly perhaps as a sacrifice to her own pride and partly from the more prudent motive of reconcile her neighbour to she and silence their clamour
but though this latter view if she indeed have it may appear reasonable enough yet the event do not answer her expectation for when she be convene before the justice and it be universally apprehend that the house of correction would have be her fate though some of the young woman cry out it be good enough for she and divert themselves with the thought of her beat hemp in a silk gown yet there be many other who begin to pity her condition but when it be know in what manner mr allworthy have behave the tide turn against she
one say iwill assure you madam hath have good luck a second cryed see what it be to be a favourite a third ay this come of her learning every person make some malicious comment or other on the occasion and reflect on the partiality of the justice
the behaviour of these people may appear impolitic and ungrateful to the reader who consider the power and benevolence of mr allworthy
but as to his power he never use it and as to his benevolence he exert so much that he have thereby disoblige all his neighbour for it be a secret well known to great man that by confer an obligation they do not always procure a friend but be certain of create many enemy
jenny be however by the care and goodness of mr allworthy soon remove out of the reach of reproach when malice be no long able to vent its rage on she begin to seek another object of its bitterness and this be no less than mr allworthy himself for a whisper soon go abroad that he himself be the father of the foundling child
this supposition so well reconcile his conduct to the general opinion that it meet with universal assent and the outcry against his lenity soon begin to take another turn and be change into an invective against his cruelty to the poor girl
very grave and good woman exclaim against man who begot child and then disown they
nor be there want some who after the departure of jenny insinuate that she be spirit away with a design too black to be mention and who give frequent hint that a legal inquiry ought to be make into the whole matter and that some people should be force to produce the girl
these calumny might have probably produce ill consequence at the least might have occasion some trouble to a person of a more doubtful and suspicious character than mr allworthy be bless with but in his case they have no such effect and be heartily despise by he they serve only to afford an innocent amusement to the good gossip of the neighbourhood
but as we cannot possibly divine what complection our reader may be of and as it will be some time before he will hear any more of jenny we think proper to give he a very early intimation that mr allworthy be and will hereafter appear to be absolutely innocent of any criminal intention whatever
he have indeed commit no other than an error in politic by temper justice with mercy and by refuse to gratify the good-natured disposition of the mob with an object for their compassion to work on in the person of poor jenny whom in order to pity they desire to have see sacrifice to ruin and infamy by a shameful correction in bridewell
whenever this word occur in our writing it intend person without virtue or sense in all station and many of the high rank be often mean by it
so far from comply with this their inclination by which all hope of reformation would have be abolish and even the gate shut against she if her own inclination should ever hereafter lead she to chuse the road of virtue mr allworthy rather choose to encourage the girl to return thither by the only possible mean for too true i be afraid it be that many woman have become abandon and have sink to the last degree of vice by be unable to retrieve the first slip
this will be i be afraid always the case while they remain among their former acquaintance it be therefore wisely do by mr allworthy to remove jenny to a place where she might enjoy the pleasure of reputation after have taste the ill consequence of lose it
to this place therefore wherever it be we will wish she a good journey and for the present take leave of she and of the little foundle her child have matter of much high importance to communicate to the reader
chapter x
the hospitality of allworthy with a short sketch of the character of two brother a doctor and a captain who be entertain by that gentleman
neither mr allworthy s house nor his heart be shut against any part of mankind but they be both more particularly open to man of merit
to say the truth this be the only house in the kingdom where you be sure to gain a dinner by deserve it
above all other man of genius and learning share the principal place in his favour and in these he have much discernment for though he have miss the advantage of a learn education yet be blest with vast natural ability he have so well profit by a vigorous though late application to letter and by much conversation with man of eminence in this way that he be himself a very competent judge in most kind of literature
it be no wonder that in an age when this kind of merit be so little in fashion and so slenderly provide for person possess of it should very eagerly flock to a place where they be sure of be receive with great complaisance indeed where they might enjoy almost the same advantage of a liberal fortune as if they be entitle to it in their own right for mr allworthy be not one of those generous person who be ready most bountifully to bestow meat drink and lodge on man of wit and learning for which they expect no other return but entertainment instruction flattery and subserviency in a word that such person should be enrol in the number of domestic without wear their master s cloathe or receive wage
on the contrary every person in this house be perfect master of his own time and as he might at his pleasure satisfy all his appetite within the restriction only of law virtue and religion so he might if his health require or his inclination prompt he to temperance or even to abstinence absent himself from any meal or retire from they whenever he be so disposed without even a sollicitation to the contrary for indeed such sollicitation from superior always savour very strongly of command
but all here be free from such impertinence not only those whose company be in all other place esteem a favour from their equality of fortune but even those whose indigent circumstance make such an eleemosynary abode convenient to they and who be therefore less welcome to a great man s table because they stand in need of it
among other of this kind be dr blifil a gentleman who have the misfortune of lose the advantage of great talent by the obstinacy of a father who would breed he to a profession he dislike
in obedience to this obstinacy the doctor have in his youth be oblige to study physic or rather to say he study it for in reality book of this kind be almost the only one with which he be unacquainted and unfortunately for he the doctor be master of almost every other science but that by which he be to get his bread the consequence of which be that the doctor at the age of forty have no bread to eat
such a person as this be certain to find a welcome at mr allworthy s table to whom misfortune be ever a recommendation when they be derive from the folly or villany of other and not of the unfortunate person himself
besides this negative merit the doctor have one positive recommendation this be a great appearance of religion
whether his religion be real or consist only in appearance i shall not presume to say as i be not possess of any touchstone which can distinguish the true from the false
if this part of his character please mr allworthy it delight miss bridget
she engage he in many religious controversy on which occasion she constantly express great satisfaction in the doctor s knowledge and not much less in the compliment which he frequently bestow on her own
to say the truth she have read much english divinity and have puzzle more than one of the neighbouring curate
indeed her conversation be so pure her look so sage and her whole deportment so grave and solemn that she seem to deserve the name of saint equally with her namesake or with any other female in the roman kalendar
as sympathy of all kind be apt to beget love so experience teach we that none have a more direct tendency this way than those of a religious kind between person of different sex
the doctor find himself so agreeable to miss bridget that he now begin to lament an unfortunate accident which have happen to he about ten year before namely his marriage with another woman who be not only still alive but what be bad know to be so by mr allworthy
this be a fatal bar to that happiness which he otherwise see sufficient probability of obtain with this young lady for as to criminal indulgence he certainly never think of they
this be owe either to his religion as be most probable or to the purity of his passion which be fix on those thing which matrimony only and not criminal correspondence could put he in possession of or could give he any title to
he have not long ruminate on these matter before it occur to his memory that he have a brother who be under no such unhappy incapacity
this brother he make no doubt would succeed for he discern as he think an inclination to marriage in the lady and the reader perhaps when he hear the brother s qualification will not blame the confidence which he entertain of his success
this gentleman be about thirty-five year of age
he be of a middle size and what be call well-build
he have a scar on his forehead which do not so much injure his beauty as it denote his valour for he be a half-pay officer
he have good tooth and something affable when he please in his smile though naturally his countenance as well as his air and voice have much of roughness in it yet he could at any time deposit this and appear all gentleness and good-humour
he be not ungenteel nor entirely devoid of wit and in his youth have abound in sprightliness which though he have lately put on a more serious character he could when he please resume
he have as well as the doctor an academic education for his father have with the same paternal authority we have mention before decreed he for holy order but as the old gentleman die before he be ordain he choose the church military and prefer the king s commission to the bishop s
he have purchase the post of lieutenant of dragoon and afterwards come to be a captain but have quarrel with his colonel be by his interest oblige to sell from which time he have entirely rusticate himself have betake himself to study the scripture and be not a little suspect of an inclination to methodism
it seem therefore not unlikely that such a person should succeed with a lady of so saint-like a disposition and whose inclination be no otherwise engage than to the marriage state in general but why the doctor who certainly have no great friendship for his brother should for his sake think of make so ill a return to the hospitality of allworthy be a matter not so easy to be account for
be it that some nature delight in evil as other be think to delight in virtue
or be there a pleasure in be accessory to a theft when we cannot commit it ourselves
or lastly which experience seem to make probable have we a satisfaction in aggrandize our family even though we have not the least love or respect for they
whether any of these motif operate on the doctor we will not determine but so the fact be
he send for his brother and easily find mean to introduce he at allworthy s as a person who intend only a short visit to himself
the captain have not be in the house a week before the doctor have reason to felicitate himself on his discernment
the captain be indeed as great a master of the art of love as ovid be formerly
he have besides receive proper hint from his brother which he fail not to improve to the good advantage
chapter xi
contain many rule and some example concern fall in love description of beauty and other more prudential inducement to matrimony
it hath be observe by wise man or woman i forget which that all person be doom to be in love once in their life
no particular season be as i remember assign for this but the age at which miss bridget be arrive seem to i as proper a period as any to be fix on for this purpose it often indeed happen much early but when it doth not i have observe it seldom or never fail about this time
moreover we may remark that at this season love be of a more serious and steady nature than what sometimes show itself in the young part of life
the love of girl be uncertain capricious and so foolish that we cannot always discover what the young lady would be at nay it may almost be doubt whether she always know this herself
now we be never at a loss to discern this in woman about forty for as such grave serious and experienced lady well know their own meaning so it be always very easy for a man of the least sagacity to discover it with the utmost certainty
miss bridget be an example of all these observation
she have not be many time in the captain s company before she be seize with this passion
nor do she go pin and mop about the house like a puny foolish girl ignorant of her distemper she feel she know and she enjoy the pleasing sensation of which as she be certain it be not only innocent but laudable she be neither afraid nor ashamed
and to say the truth there be in all point great difference between the reasonable passion which woman at this age conceive towards man and the idle and childish liking of a girl to a boy which be often fix on the outside only and on thing of little value and no duration as on cherry-cheek small lily-white hand sloe-black eye flow lock downy chin dapper shape nay sometimes on charm more worthless than these and less the party s own such be the outward ornament of the person for which man be beholden to the taylor the laceman the periwig-maker the hatter and the milliner and not to nature
such a passion girl may well be ashamed as they generally be to own either to themselves or other
the love of miss bridget be of another kind
the captain owe nothing to any of these fop-maker in his dress nor be his person much more beholden to nature
both his dress and person be such as have they appear in an assembly or a drawing-room would have be the contempt and ridicule of all the fine lady there
the former of these be indeed neat but plain coarse ill-fancied and out of fashion
as for the latter we have expressly describe it above
so far be the skin on his cheek from be cherry-colour that you could not discern what the natural colour of his cheek be they be totally overgrown by a black beard which ascend to his eye
his shape and limb be indeed exactly proportion but so large that they denote the strength rather of a ploughman than any other
his shoulder be broad beyond all size and the calf of his leg large than those of a common chairman
in short his whole person want all that elegance and beauty which be the very reverse of clumsy strength and which so agreeably set off most of our fine gentleman be partly owe to the high blood of their ancestor viz blood make of rich sauce and generous wine and partly to an early town education
though miss bridget be a woman of the great delicacy of taste yet such be the charm of the captain s conversation that she totally overlook the defect of his person
she imagine and perhaps very wisely that she should enjoy more agreeable minute with the captain than with a much pretty fellow and forewent the consideration of please her eye in order to procure herself much more solid satisfaction
the captain no soon perceive the passion of miss bridget in which discovery he be very quick-sighted than he faithfully return it
the lady no more than her lover be remarkable for beauty
i would attempt to draw her picture but that be do already by a more able master mr hogarth himself to whom she sit many year ago and hath be lately exhibit by that gentleman in his print of a winter s morning of which she be no improper emblem and may be see walk for walk she doth in the print to covent garden church with a starve foot-boy behind carry her prayer-book
the captain likewise very wisely preferred the more solid enjoyment he expect with this lady to the fleeting charm of person
he be one of those wise man who regard beauty in the other sex as a very worthless and superficial qualification or to speak more truly who rather chuse to possess every convenience of life with an ugly woman than a handsome one without any of those convenience
and have a very good appetite and but little nicety he fancy he should play his part very well at the matrimonial banquet without the sauce of beauty
to deal plainly with the reader the captain ever since his arrival at least from the moment his brother have propose the match to he long before he have discover any flattering symptom in miss bridget have be greatly enamour that be to say of mr allworthy s house and garden and of his land tenement and hereditament of all which the captain be so passionately fond that he would most probably have contract marriage with they have he be oblige to have take the witch of endor into the bargain
as mr allworthy therefore have declare to the doctor that he never intend to take a second wife as his sister be his near relation and as the doctor have fish out that his intention be to make any child of hers his heir which indeed the law without his interposition would have do for he the doctor and his brother think it an act of benevolence to give be to a human creature who would be so plentifully provide with the most essential mean of happiness
the whole thought therefore of both the brother be how to engage the affection of this amiable lady
but fortune who be a tender parent and often doth more for her favourite offspring than either they deserve or wish have be so industrious for the captain that whilst he be lay scheme to execute his purpose the lady conceive the same desire with himself and be on her side contrive how to give the captain proper encouragement without appear too forward for she be a strict observer of all rule of decorum
in this however she easily succeed for as the captain be always on the look-out no glance gesture or word escape he
the satisfaction which the captain receive from the kind behaviour of miss bridget be not a little abate by his apprehension of mr allworthy for notwithstanding his disinterested profession the captain imagine he would when he come to act follow the example of the rest of the world and refuse his consent to a match so disadvantageous in point of interest to his sister
from what oracle he receive this opinion i shall leave the reader to determine but however he come by it it strangely perplex he how to regulate his conduct so as at once to convey his affection to the lady and to conceal it from her brother
he at length resolve to take all private opportunity of make his address but in the presence of mr allworthy to be as reserve and as much upon his guard as be possible and this conduct be highly approve by the brother
he soon find mean to make his address in express term to his mistress from whom he receive an answer in the proper form viz the answer which be first make some thousand of year ago and which hath be hand down by tradition from mother to daughter ever since
if i be to translate this into latin i should render it by these two word nolo episcopari a phrase likewise of immemorial use on another occasion
the captain however he come by his knowledge perfectly well understand the lady and very soon after repeat his application with more warmth and earnestness than before and be again accord to due form reject but as he have increase in the eagerness of his desire so the lady with the same propriety decrease in the violence of her refusal
not to tire the reader by lead he through every scene of this courtship which though in the opinion of a certain great author it be the pleasant scene of life to the actor be perhaps as dull and tiresome as any whatever to the audience the captain make his advance in form the citadel be defend in form and at length in proper form surrender at discretion
during this whole time which fill the space of near a month the captain preserve great distance of behaviour to his lady in the presence of the brother and the more he succeed with she in private the more reserved be he in public
and as for the lady she have no soon secure her lover than she behave to he before company with the high degree of indifference so that mr allworthy must have have the insight of the devil or perhaps some of his bad quality to have entertain the least suspicion of what be go forward
chapter xii
contain what the reader may perhaps expect to find in it
in all bargain whether to fight or to marry or concern any other such business little previous ceremony be require to bring the matter to an issue when both party be really in earnest
this be the case at present and in less than a month the captain and his lady be man and wife
the great concern now be to break the matter to mr allworthy and this be undertake by the doctor
one day then as allworthy be walk in his garden the doctor come to he and with great gravity of aspect and all the concern which he could possibly affect in his countenance say i be come sir to impart an affair to you of the utmost consequence but how shall i mention to you what it almost distract i to think of he then launch forth into the most bitter invective both against man and woman accuse the former of have no attachment but to their interest and the latter of be so addicted to vicious inclination that they could never be safely trust with one of the other sex
could i say he sir have suspect that a lady of such prudence such judgment such learning should indulge so indiscreet a passion
or could i have imagine that my brother--why do i call he so
he be no long a brother of mine---- indeed but he be say allworthy and a brother of mine too bless i sir say the doctor do you know the shocking affair look ee mr blifil answer the good man it hath be my constant maxim in life to make the good of all matter which happen
my sister though many year young than i be at least old enough to be at the age of discretion
have he impose on a child i should have be more averse to have forgive he but a woman upwards of thirty must certainly be suppose to know what will make she most happy
she hath marry a gentleman though perhaps not quite she equal in fortune and if he hath any perfection in her eye which can make up that deficiency i see no reason why i should object to her choice of her own happiness which i no more than herself imagine to consist only in immense wealth
i might perhaps from the many declaration i have make of comply with almost any proposal have expect to have be consult on this occasion but these matter be of a very delicate nature and the scruple of modesty perhaps be not to be overcome
as to your brother i have really no anger against he at all
he hath no obligation to i nor do i think he be under any necessity of ask my consent since the woman be as i have say sui juris and of a proper age to be entirely answerable only to herself for her conduct the doctor accuse mr allworthy of too great lenity repeat his accusation against his brother and declare that he should never more be bring either to see or to own he for his relation
he then launch forth into a panegyric on allworthy s goodness into the high encomium on his friendship and conclude by say he should never forgive his brother for have put the place which he bear in that friendship to a hazard
allworthy thus answer have i conceive any displeasure against your brother i should never have carry that resentment to the innocent but i assure you i have no such displeasure
your brother appear to i to be a man of sense and honour
i do not disapprove the taste of my sister nor will i doubt but that she be equally the object of his inclination
i have always think love the only foundation of happiness in a married state as it can only produce that high and tender friendship which should always be the cement of this union and in my opinion all those marriage which be contract from other motif be greatly criminal they be a profanation of a most holy ceremony and generally end in disquiet and misery for surely we may call it a profanation to convert this most sacred institution into a wicked sacrifice to lust or avarice and what well can be say of those match to which man be induce merely by the consideration of a beautiful person or a great fortune
to deny that beauty be an agreeable object to the eye and even worthy some admiration would be false and foolish
beautiful be an epithet often use in scripture and always mention with honour
