it be my own fortune to marry a woman whom the world think handsome and i can truly say i like she the well on that account
but to make this the sole consideration of marriage to lust after it so violently as to overlook all imperfection for its sake or to require it so absolutely as to reject and disdain religion virtue and sense which be quality in their nature of much high perfection only because an elegance of person be want this be surely inconsistent either with a wise man or a good christian
and it be perhaps be too charitable to conclude that such person mean anything more by their marriage than to please their carnal appetite for the satisfaction of which we be teach it be not ordain
in the next place with respect to fortune
worldly prudence perhaps exact some consideration on this head nor will i absolutely and altogether condemn it
as the world be constitute the demand of a married state and the care of posterity require some little regard to what we call circumstance
yet this provision be greatly increase beyond what be really necessary by folly and vanity which create abundantly more want than nature
equipage for the wife and large fortune for the child be by custom enrol in the list of necessary and to procure these everything truly solid and sweet and virtuous and religious be neglect and overlook
and this in many degree the last and great of which seem scarce distinguishable from madness i mean where person of immense fortune contract themselves to those who be and must be disagreeable to they--to fool and knave--in order to increase an estate already large even than the demand of their pleasure
surely such person if they will not be think mad must own either that they be incapable of taste the sweet of the tender friendship or that they sacrifice the great happiness of which they be capable to the vain uncertain and senseless law of vulgar opinion which owe as well their force as their foundation to folly here allworthy conclude his sermon to which blifil have listen with the profound attention though it cost he some pain to prevent now and then a small discomposure of his muscle
he now praise every period of what he have hear with the warmth of a young divine who hath the honour to dine with a bishop the same day in which his lordship hath mount the pulpit
chapter xiii
which conclude the first book with an instance of ingratitude which we hope will appear unnatural
the reader from what hath be say may imagine that the reconciliation if indeed it could be so call be only matter of form we shall therefore pass it over and hasten to what must surely be think matter of substance
the doctor have acquaint his brother with what have past between mr allworthy and he and add with a smile i promise you i pay you off nay i absolutely desire the good gentleman not to forgive you for you know after he have make a declaration in your favour i might with safety venture on such a request with a person of his temper and i be willing as well for your sake as for my own to prevent the least possibility of a suspicion captain blifil take not the least notice of this at that time but he afterwards make a very notable use of it
one of the maxim which the devil in a late visit upon earth leave to his disciple be when once you be get up to kick the stool from under you
in plain english when you have make your fortune by the good office of a friend you be advise to discard he as soon as you can
whether the captain act by this maxim i will not positively determine so far we may confidently say that his action may be fairly derive from this diabolical principle and indeed it be difficult to assign any other motive to they for no soon be he possess of miss bridget and reconcile to allworthy than he begin to show a coldness to his brother which increase daily till at length it grow into rudeness and become very visible to every one
the doctor remonstrate to he privately concern this behaviour but could obtain no other satisfaction than the follow plain declaration if you dislike anything in my brother s house sir you know you be at liberty to quit it this strange cruel and almost unaccountable ingratitude in the captain absolutely break the poor doctor s heart for ingratitude never so thoroughly pierce the human breast as when it proceed from those in whose behalf we have be guilty of transgression
reflection on great and good action however they be receive or return by those in whose favour they be perform always administer some comfort to we but what consolation shall we receive under so bite a calamity as the ungrateful behaviour of our friend when our wound conscience at the same time fly in our face and upbraid we with have spot it in the service of one so worthless
mr allworthy himself speak to the captain in his brother s behalf and desire to know what offence the doctor have commit when the hard-hearted villain have the baseness to say that he should never forgive he for the injury which he have endeavour to do he in his favour which he say he have pump out of he and be such a cruelty that it ought not to be forgive
allworthy speak in very high term upon this declaration which he say become not a human creature
he express indeed so much resentment against an unforgive temper that the captain at last pretend to be convince by his argument and outwardly profess to be reconcile
as for the bride she be now in her honeymoon and so passionately fond of her new husband that he never appear to she to be in the wrong and his displeasure against any person be a sufficient reason for her dislike to the same
the captain at mr allworthy s instance be outwardly as we have say reconcile to his brother yet the same rancour remain in his heart and he find so many opportunity of give he private hint of this that the house at last grow insupportable to the poor doctor and he choose rather to submit to any inconvenience which he might encounter in the world than long to bear these cruel and ungrateful insult from a brother for whom he have do so much
he once intend to acquaint allworthy with the whole but he could not bring himself to submit to the confession by which he must take to his share so great a portion of guilt
besides by how much the bad man he represent his brother to be so much the great would his own offence appear to allworthy and so much the great he have reason to imagine would be his resentment
he feign therefore some excuse of business for his departure and promise to return soon again and take leave of his brother with so well-dissemble content that as the captain play his part to the same perfection allworthy remain well satisfied with the truth of the reconciliation
the doctor go directly to london where he die soon after of a broken heart a distemper which kill many more than be generally imagine and would have a fair title to a place in the bill of mortality do it not differ in one instance from all other disease--viz that no physician can cure it
now upon the most diligent enquiry into the former life of these two brother i find besides the cursed and hellish maxim of policy above mention another reason for the captain s conduct the captain besides what we have before say of he be a man of great pride and fierceness and have always treat his brother who be of a different complexion and greatly deficient in both these quality with the utmost air of superiority
the doctor however have much the large share of learning and be by many repute to have the well understanding
this the captain know and could not bear for though envy be at good a very malignant passion yet be its bitterness greatly heighten by mix with contempt towards the same object and very much afraid i be that whenever an obligation be join to these two indignation and not gratitude will be the product of all three
book ii
contain scenes of matrimonial felicity in different degrees of life and various other transaction during the first two year after the marriage between captain blifil and miss bridget allworthy
chapter i
show what kind of a history this be what it be like and what it be not like
though we have properly enough entitle this our work a history and not a life nor an apology for a life as be more in fashion yet we intend in it rather to pursue the method of those writer who profess to disclose the revolution of country than to imitate the painful and voluminous historian who to preserve the regularity of his series think himself oblige to fill up as much paper with the detail of month and year in which nothing remarkable happen as he employ upon those notable aera when the great scene have be transact on the human stage
such history as these do in reality very much resemble a newspaper which consist of just the same number of word whether there be any news in it or not
they may likewise be compare to a stage coach which perform constantly the same course empty as well as full
the writer indeed seem to think himself oblige to keep even pace with time whose amanuensis he be and like his master travel as slowly through century of monkish dulness when the world seem to have be asleep as through that bright and busy age so nobly distinguish by the excellent latin poet-- ad confligendum venientibus undique poeni omnia cum belli trepido concussa tumultu horrida contremuere sub altis aetheris auris in dubioque fuit sub utrorum regna cadendum omnibus humani esset terraque marique of which we wish we could give our reader a more adequate translation than that by mr creech-- when dreadful carthage fright rome with arm and all the world be shake with fierce alarm whilst undecided yet which part should fall which nation rise the glorious lord of all
now it be our purpose in the ensue page to pursue a contrary method
when any extraordinary scene present itself as we trust will often be the case we shall spare no pain nor paper to open it at large to our reader but if whole year should pass without produce anything worthy his notice we shall not be afraid of a chasm in our history but shall hasten on to matter of consequence and leave such period of time totally unobserved
these be indeed to be consider as blank in the grand lottery of time
we therefore who be the register of that lottery shall imitate those sagacious person who deal in that which be draw at guildhall and who never trouble the public with the many blank they dispose of but when a great prize happen to be draw the newspaper be presently fill with it and the world be sure to be inform at whose office it be sell indeed commonly two or three different office lie claim to the honour of have dispose of it by which i suppose the adventurer be give to understand that certain broker be in the secret of fortune and indeed of her cabinet council
my reader then be not to be surprize if in the course of this work he shall find some chapter very short and other altogether as long some that contain only the time of a single day and other that comprise year in a word if my history sometimes seem to stand still and sometimes to fly
for all which i shall not look on myself as accountable to any court of critical jurisdiction whatever for as i be in reality the founder of a new province of writing so i be at liberty to make what law i please therein
and these law my reader whom i consider as my subject be bind to believe in and to obey with which that they may readily and cheerfully comply i do hereby assure they that i shall principally regard their ease and advantage in all such institution for i do not like a jure divino tyrant imagine that they be my slave or my commodity
i be indeed set over they for their own good only and be create for their use and not they for mine
nor do i doubt while i make their interest the great rule of my writing they will unanimously concur in support my dignity and in render i all the honour i shall deserve or desire
chapter ii
religious caution against show too much favour to bastard and a great discovery make by mrs deborah wilkins
eight month after the celebration of the nuptial between captain blifil and miss bridget allworthy a young lady of great beauty merit and fortune be miss bridget by reason of a fright deliver of a fine boy
the child be indeed to all appearance perfect but the midwife discover it be bear a month before its full time
though the birth of an heir by his beloved sister be a circumstance of great joy to mr allworthy yet it do not alienate his affection from the little foundling to whom he have be godfather have give his own name of thomas and whom he have hitherto seldom fail of visit at least once a day in his nursery
he tell his sister if she please the new-bear infant should be breed up together with little tommy to which she consent though with some little reluctance for she have truly a great complacence for her brother and hence she have always behave towards the foundling with rather more kindness than lady of rigid virtue can sometimes bring themselves to show to these child who however innocent may be truly call the live monument of incontinence
the captain could not so easily bring himself to bear what he condemn as a fault in mr allworthy
he give he frequent hint that to adopt the fruit of sin be to give countenance to it
he quote several text for he be well read in scripture such as he visit the sin of the father upon the child and the father have eat sour grape and the child s tooth be set on edge c
whence he argue the legality of punish the crime of the parent on the bastard
he say though the law do not positively allow the destroy such base-bear child yet it hold they to be the child of nobody that the church consider they as the child of nobody and that at the good they ought to be bring up to the low and vilest office of the commonwealth mr allworthy answer to all this and much more which the captain have urge on this subject that however guilty the parent might be the child be certainly innocent that as to the text he have quote the former of they be a particular denunciation against the jews for the sin of idolatry of relinquish and hat their heavenly king and the latter be parabolically speak and rather intend to denote the certain and necessary consequence of sin than any express judgment against it
but to represent the almighty as avenge the sin of the guilty on the innocent be indecent if not blasphemous as it be to represent he act against the first principle of natural justice and against the original notion of right and wrong which he himself have implant in our mind by which we be to judge not only in all matter which be not reveal but even of the truth of revelation itself
he say he know many hold the same principle with the captain on this head but he be himself firmly convinced to the contrary and would provide in the same manner for this poor infant as if a legitimate child have have fortune to have be find in the same place while the captain be take all opportunity to press these and such like argument to remove the little foundling from mr allworthy s of whose fondness for he he begin to be jealous mrs deborah have make a discovery which in its event threaten at least to prove more fatal to poor tommy than all the reasoning of the captain
whether the insatiable curiosity of this good woman have carry she on to that business or whether she do it to confirm herself in the good grace of mrs blifil who notwithstanding her outward behaviour to the foundling frequently abuse the infant in private and her brother too for his fondness to it i will not determine but she have now as she conceive fully detect the father of the foundling
now as this be a discovery of great consequence it may be necessary to trace it from the fountain-head
we shall therefore very minutely lie open those previous matter by which it be produce and for that purpose we shall be oblige to reveal all the secret of a little family with which my reader be at present entirely unacquainted and of which the oeconomy be so rare and extraordinary that i fear it will shock the utmost credulity of many married person
chapter iii
the description of a domestic government found upon rule directly contrary to those of aristotle
my reader may please to remember he hath be inform that jenny jones have live some year with a certain schoolmaster who have at her earnest desire instruct she in latin in which to do justice to her genius she have so improve herself that she be become a well scholar than her master
indeed though this poor man have undertake a profession to which learning must be allow necessary this be the least of his commendation
he be one of the well-natured fellow in the world and be at the same time master of so much pleasantry and humour that he be repute the wit of the country and all the neighbouring gentleman be so desirous of his company that as deny be not his talent he spend much time at their house which he might with more emolument have spend in his school
it may be imagine that a gentleman so qualified and so disposed be in no danger of become formidable to the learn seminary of eton or westminster
to speak plainly his scholar be divide into two class in the upper of which be a young gentleman the son of a neighbouring squire who at the age of seventeen be just enter into his syntaxis and in the low be a second son of the same gentleman who together with seven parish-boy be learn to read and write
the stipend arise hence would hardly have indulge the schoolmaster in the luxury of life have he not add to this office those of clerk and barber and have not mr allworthy add to the whole an annuity of ten pound which the poor man receive every christmas and with which he be enable to cheer his heart during that sacred festival
among his other treasure the pedagogue have a wife whom he have marry out of mr allworthy s kitchen for her fortune viz twenty pound which she have there amass
this woman be not very amiable in her person
whether she sit to my friend hogarth or no i will not determine but she exactly resemble the young woman who be pour out her mistress s tea in the third picture of the harlot s progress
she be besides a prof follower of that noble sect found by xantippe of old by mean of which she become more formidable in the school than her husband for to confess the truth he be never master there or anywhere else in her presence
though her countenance do not denote much natural sweetness of temper yet this be perhaps somewhat sour by a circumstance which generally poison matrimonial felicity for child be rightly call the pledge of love and her husband though they have be marry nine year have give she no such pledge a default for which he have no excuse either from age or health be not yet thirty year old and what they call a jolly brisk young man
hence arise another evil which produce no little uneasiness to the poor pedagogue of whom she maintain so constant a jealousy that he durst hardly speak to one woman in the parish for the least degree of civility or even correspondence with any female be sure to bring his wife upon she back and his own
in order to guard herself against matrimonial injury in her own house as she keep one maid-servant she always take care to chuse she out of that order of female whose face be take as a kind of security for their virtue of which number jenny jones as the reader hath be before inform be one
as the face of this young woman might be call pretty good security of the before-mention kind and as her behaviour have be always extremely modest which be the certain consequence of understanding in woman she have pass above four year at mr partridge s for that be the schoolmaster s name without create the least suspicion in her mistress
nay she have be treat with uncommon kindness and her mistress have permit mr partridge to give she those instruction which have be before commemorate
but it be with jealousy as with the gout when such distemper be in the blood there be never any security against their break out and that often on the slight occasion and when least suspect
thus it happen to mrs partridge who have submit four year to her husband s teaching this young woman and have suffer she often to neglect her work in order to pursue her learning
for pass by one day as the girl be read and her master lean over she the girl i know not for what reason suddenly start up from her chair and this be the first time that suspicion ever enter into the head of her mistress
this do not however at that time discover itself but lie lurk in her mind like a conceal enemy who wait for a reinforcement of additional strength before he openly declare himself and proceed upon hostile operation and such additional strength soon arrive to corroborate her suspicion for not long after the husband and wife be at dinner the master say to his maid da mihi aliquid potum upon which the poor girl smile perhaps at the badness of the latin and when her mistress cast her eye on she blush possibly with a consciousness of have laugh at her master
mrs partridge upon this immediately fall into a fury and discharge the trencher on which she be eat at the head of poor jenny cry out you impudent whore do you play trick with my husband before my face and at the same instant rise from her chair with a knife in her hand with which most probably she would have execute very tragical vengeance have not the girl take the advantage of be nearer the door than her mistress and avoid her fury by run away for as to the poor husband whether surprize have render he motionless or fear which be full as probable have restrain he from venture at any opposition he sit star and tremble in his chair nor do he once offer to move or speak till his wife return from the pursuit of jenny make some defensive measure necessary for his own preservation and he likewise be oblige to retreat after the example of the maid
this good woman be no more than othello of a disposition to make a life of jealousy and follow still the change of the moon with fresh suspicions-- with she as well as he to be once in doubt be once to be resolvd-- she therefore order jenny immediately to pack up her all and begone for that she be determined she should not sleep that night within her wall
mr partridge have profit too much by experience to interpose in a matter of this nature
he therefore have recourse to his usual receipt of patience for though he be not a great adept in latin he remember and well understand the advice contain in these word leve fit quod bene fertur onus in english a burden become lightest when it be well borne-- which he have always in his mouth and of which to say the truth he have often occasion to experience the truth
jenny offer to make protestation of her innocence but the temp be too strong for she to be hear
she then betook herself to the business of packing for which a small quantity of brown paper suffice and have receive her small pittance of wage she return home
the schoolmaster and his consort pass their time unpleasantly enough that evening but something or other happen before the next morning which a little abate the fury of mrs partridge and she at length admit her husband to make his excuse to which she give the readier belief as he have instead of desire she to recall jenny profess a satisfaction in she be dismiss say she be grow of little use as a servant spend all her time in reading and be become moreover very pert and obstinate for indeed she and her master have lately have frequent dispute in literature in which as hath be say she be become greatly his superior
this however he would by no means allow and as he call she persist in the right obstinacy he begin to hate she with no small inveteracy
chapter iv
contain one of the most bloody battle or rather duel that be ever record in domestic history
for the reason mention in the precede chapter and from some other matrimonial concession well know to most husband and which like the secret of freemasonry should be divulge to none who be not member of that honourable fraternity mrs partridge be pretty well satisfied that she have condemn her husband without cause and endeavour by act of kindness to make he amend for her false suspicion
her passion be indeed equally violent whichever way they incline for as she could be extremely angry so could she be altogether as fond
but though these passion ordinarily succeed each other and scarce twenty-four hour ever pass in which the pedagogue be not in some degree the object of both yet on extraordinary occasion when the passion of anger have rag very high the remission be usually long and so be the case at present for she continue long in a state of affability after this fit of jealousy be end than she husband have ever know before and have it not be for some little exercise which all the follower of xantippe be oblige to perform daily mr partridge would have enjoy a perfect serenity of several month
perfect calm at sea be always suspect by the experienced mariner to be the forerunner of a storm and i know some person who without be generally the devotee of superstition be apt to apprehend that great and unusual peace or tranquillity will be attend with its opposite
for which reason the antient use on such occasion to sacrifice to the goddess nemesis a deity who be think by they to look with an invidious eye on human felicity and to have a peculiar delight in overturn it
as we be very far from believe in any such heathen goddess or from encourage any superstition so we wish mr john fr---- or some other such philosopher would bestir himself a little in order to find out the real cause of this sudden transition from good to bad fortune which hath be so often remark and of which we shall proceed to give an instance for it be our province to relate fact and we shall leave cause to person of much high genius
mankind have always take great delight in know and descant on the action of other
hence there have be in all age and nation certain place set apart for public rendezvous where the curious might meet and satisfy their mutual curiosity
among these the barber shop have justly bear the pre-eminence
among the greeks barber news be a proverbial expression and horace in one of his epistle make honourable mention of the roman barber in the same light
those of england be know to be no wise inferior to their greek or roman predecessor
you there see foreign affair discuss in a manner little inferior to that with which they be handle in the coffee-house and domestic occurrence be much more largely and freely treat in the former than in the latter
but this serve only for the man
now whereas the female of this country especially those of the low order do associate themselves much more than those of other nation our polity would be highly deficient if they have not some place set apart likewise for the indulgence of their curiosity see they be in this no way inferior to the other half of the specie
in enjoy therefore such place of rendezvous the british fair ought to esteem themselves more happy than any of their foreign sister as i do not remember either to have read in history or to have see in my travel anything of the like kind
this place then be no other than the chandler s shop the know seat of all the news or as it be vulgarly call gossip in every parish in england
mrs partridge be one day at this assembly of female be ask by one of her neighbour if she have hear no news lately of jenny jones
to which she answer in the negative
upon this the other reply with a smile that the parish be very much oblige to she for have turn jenny away as she do
mrs partridge whose jealousy as the reader well know be long since cure and who have no other quarrel to her maid answer boldly she do not know any obligation the parish have to she on that account for she believe jenny have scarce leave she equal behind she
no truly say the gossip i hope not though i fancy we have slut enow too
then you have not hear it seem that she hath be bring to bed of two bastard
but as they be not bear here my husband and the other overseer say we shall not be oblige to keep they two bastard answer mrs partridge hastily you surprize i
i donot know whether we must keep they but i be sure they must have be beget here for the wench hath not be nine month go away nothing can be so quick and sudden as the operation of the mind especially when hope or fear or jealousy to which the two other be but journeymen set it to work
it occur instantly to she that jenny have scarce ever be out of her own house while she live with she
the lean over the chair the sudden start up the latin the smile and many other thing rush upon she all at once
the satisfaction her husband express in the departure of jenny appear now to be only dissemble again in the same instant to be real but yet to confirm her jealousy proceed from satiety and a hundred other bad cause
in a word she be convince of her husband s guilt and immediately leave the assembly in confusion
as fair grimalkin who though the young of the feline family degenerate not in ferocity from the eld branch of her house and though inferior in strength be equal in fierceness to the noble tiger himself when a little mouse whom it hath long torment in sport escape from her clutch for a while fret scold growl swear but if the trunk or box behind which the mouse lie hide be again remove she fly like lightning on her prey and with envenomed wrath bite scratch mumble and tear the little animal
not with less fury do mrs partridge fly on the poor pedagogue
her tongue tooth and hand fall all upon he at once
his wig be in an instant tear from his head his shirt from his back and from his face descend five stream of blood denote the number of claw with which nature have unhappily arm the enemy
mr partridge act for some time on the defensive only indeed he attempt only to guard his face with his hand but as he find that his antagonist abate nothing of her rage he think he might at least endeavour to disarm she or rather to confine her arm in do which her cap fall off in the struggle and her hair be too short to reach her shoulder erect itself on her head her stay likewise which be lace through one single hole at the bottom burst open and her breast which be much more redundant than her hair hang down below her middle her face be likewise mark with the blood of her husband her tooth gnash with rage and fire such as sparkle from a smith s forge dart from her eye
so that altogether this amazonian heroine might have be an object of terror to a much bold man than mr partridge
he have at length the good fortune by get possession of her arm to render those weapon which she wear at the end of her finger useless which she no soon perceive than the softness of her sex prevail over her rage and she presently dissolve in tear which soon after conclude in a fit
that small share of sense which mr partridge have hitherto preserve through this scene of fury of the cause of which he be hitherto ignorant now utterly abandon he
he run instantly into the street hallow out that his wife be in the agony of death and beseech the neighbour to fly with the utmost haste to her assistance
several good woman obey his summon who enter his house and apply the usual remedy on such occasion mrs partridge be at length to the great joy of her husband bring to herself
as soon as she have a little recollect her spirit and somewhat compose herself with a cordial she begin to inform the company of the manifold injury she have receive from her husband who she say be not content to injure she in her bed but upon she upbraid he with it have treat she in the cruel manner imaginable have tear her cap and hair from her head and her stay from her body give she at the same time several blow the mark of which she should carry to the grave
the poor man who bear on his face many more visible mark of the indignation of his wife stand in silent astonishment at this accusation which the reader will i believe bear witness for he have greatly exceed the truth for indeed he have not strike she once and this silence be interpret to be a confession of the charge by the whole court they all begin at once una voce to rebuke and revile he repeat often that none but a coward ever strike a woman
mr partridge bear all this patiently but when his wife appeal to the blood on her face as an evidence of his barbarity he could not help lay claim to his own blood for so it really be as he think it very unnatural that this should rise up as we be teach that of a murder person often doth in vengeance against he
to this the woman make no other answer than that it be a pity it have not come from his heart instead of his face all declare that if their husband should lift their hand against they they would have their heart blood out of their body
after much admonition for what be past and much good advice to mr partridge for his future behaviour the company at length depart and leave the husband and wife to a personal conference together in which mr partridge soon learn the cause of all his suffering
chapter v
contain much matter to exercise the judgment and reflection of the reader
i believe it be a true observation that few secret be divulge to one person only but certainly it would be next to a miracle that a fact of this kind should be know to a whole parish and not transpire any far
and indeed a very few day have past before the country to use a common phrase rung of the schoolmaster of little baddington who be say to have beat his wife in the most cruel manner
nay in some place it be report he have murder she in other that he have break her arm in other her leg in short there be scarce an injury which can be do to a human creature but what mrs partridge be somewhere or other affirm to have receive from her husband
the cause of this quarrel be likewise variously report for as some people say that mrs partridge have catch her husband in bed with his maid so many other reason of a very different kind go abroad
nay some transfer the guilt to the wife and the jealousy to the husband
mrs wilkins have long ago hear of this quarrel but as a different cause from the true one have reach her ear she think proper to conceal it and the rather perhaps as the blame be universally lay on mr partridge and his wife when she be servant to mr allworthy have in something offend mrs wilkins who be not of a very forgiving temper
but mrs wilkins whose eye could see object at a distance and who could very well look forward a few year into futurity have perceive a strong likelihood of captain blifil s be hereafter her master and as she plainly discern that the captain bear no great goodwill to the little foundling she fancy it would be render he an agreeable service if she could make any discovery that might lessen the affection which mr allworthy seem to have contract for this child and which give visible uneasiness to the captain who could not entirely conceal it even before allworthy himself though his wife who act her part much well in public frequently recommend to he her own example of connive at the folly of her brother which she say she at least as well perceive and as much resent as any other possibly could
mrs wilkins have therefore by accident get a true scent of the above story though long after it have happen fail not to satisfy herself thoroughly of all the particular and then acquaint the captain that she have at last discover the true father of the little bastard which she be sorry she say to see her master lose his reputation in the country by take so much notice of
the captain chide she for the conclusion of her speech as an improper assurance in judging of her master s action for if his honour or his understanding would have suffer the captain to make an alliance with mrs wilkins his pride would by no mean have admit it
and to say the truth there be no conduct less politic than to enter into any confederacy with your friend s servant against their master for by these mean you afterwards become the slave of these very servant by whom you be constantly liable to be betray
and this consideration perhaps it be which prevent captain blifil from be more explicit with mrs wilkins or from encourage the abuse which she have bestow on allworthy
but though he declare no satisfaction to mrs wilkins at this discovery he enjoy not a little from it in his own mind and resolve to make the good use of it he be able
he keep this matter a long time conceal within his own breast in hope that mr allworthy might hear it from some other person but mrs wilkins whether she resent the captain s behaviour or whether his cunning be beyond she and she fear the discovery might displease he never afterwards open her lip about the matter
i have think it somewhat strange upon reflection that the housekeeper never acquaint mrs blifil with this news as woman be more inclined to communicate all piece of intelligence to their own sex than to ours
the only way as it appear to i of solve this difficulty be by impute it to that distance which be now grow between the lady and the housekeeper whether this arise from a jealousy in mrs blifil that wilkins show too great a respect to the foundling for while she be endeavour to ruin the little infant in order to ingratiate herself with the captain she be every day more and more commend it before allworthy as his fondness for it every day increase
this notwithstanding all the care she take at other time to express the direct contrary to mrs blifil perhaps offend that delicate lady who certainly now hat mrs wilkins and though she do not or possibly could not absolutely remove she from her place she find however the mean of make her life very uneasy
this mrs wilkins at length so resent that she very openly show all manner of respect and fondness to little tommy in opposition to mrs blifil
the captain therefore find the story in danger of perish at last take an opportunity to reveal it himself
he be one day engage with mr allworthy in a discourse on charity in which the captain with great learning prove to mr allworthy that the word charity in scripture nowhere mean beneficence or generosity
the christian religion he say be institute for much nobler purpose than to enforce a lesson which many heathen philosopher have teach we long before and which though it might perhaps be call a moral virtue savour but little of that sublime christian-like disposition that vast elevation of thought in purity approach to angelic perfection to be attain express and feel only by grace
those he say come near to the scripture meaning who understand by it candour or the forming of a benevolent opinion of our brother and pass a favourable judgment on their action a virtue much high and more extensive in its nature than a pitiful distribution of alm which though we would never so much prejudice or even ruin our family could never reach many whereas charity in the other and truer sense might be extend to all mankind he say consider who the disciple be it would be absurd to conceive the doctrine of generosity or give alm to have be preach to they
and as we could not well imagine this doctrine should be preach by its divine author to man who could not practise it much less should we think it understand so by those who can practise it and do not
but though continue he there be i be afraid little merit in these benefaction there would i must confess be much pleasure in they to a good mind if it be not abate by one consideration
i mean that we be liable to be impose upon and to confer our choicest favour often on the undeserving as you must own be your case in your bounty to that worthless fellow partridge for two or three such example must greatly lessen the inward satisfaction which a good man would otherwise find in generosity nay may even make he timorous in bestowing lest he should be guilty of support vice and encourage the wicked a crime of a very black dye and for which it will by no mean be a sufficient excuse that we have not actually intend such an encouragement unless we have use the utmost caution in chuse the object of our beneficence
a consideration which i make no doubt hath greatly check the liberality of many a worthy and pious man mr allworthy answer he could not dispute with the captain in the greek language and therefore could say nothing as to the true sense of the word which be translate charity but that he have always think it be interpret to consist in action and that give alm constitute at least one branch of that virtue
as to the meritorious part he say he readily agree with the captain for where could be the merit of barely discharge a duty
which he say let the word charity have what construction it would it sufficiently appear to be from the whole tenor of the new testament
and as he think it an indispensable duty enjoin both by the christian law and by the law of nature itself so be it withal so pleasant that if any duty could be say to be its own reward or to pay we while we be discharge it it be this
to confess the truth say he there be one degree of generosity of charity i would have call it which seem to have some show of merit and that be where from a principle of benevolence and christian love we bestow on another what we really want ourselves where in order to lessen the distress of another we condescend to share some part of they by give what even our own necessity cannot well spare
this be i think meritorious but to relieve our brother only with our superfluity to be charitable i must use the word rather at the expense of our coffer than ourselves to save several family from misery rather than hang up an extraordinary picture in our house or gratify any other idle ridiculous vanity--this seem to be only be human creature
nay i will venture to go far it be be in some degree epicure for what could the great epicure wish rather than to eat with many mouth instead of one
which i think may be predicate of any one who know that the bread of many be owe to his own largess
as to the apprehension of bestow bounty on such as may hereafter prove unworthy object because many have prove such surely it can never deter a good man from generosity
i do not think a few or many example of ingratitude can justify a man s harden his heart against the distress of his fellow-creature nor do i believe it can ever have such effect on a truly benevolent mind
nothing less than a persuasion of universal depravity can lock up the charity of a good man and this persuasion must lead he i think either into atheism or enthusiasm but surely it be unfair to argue such universal depravity from a few vicious individual nor be this i believe ever do by a man who upon search his own mind find one certain exception to the general rule he then conclude by ask who that partridge be whom he have call a worthless fellow i mean say the captain partridge the barber the schoolmaster what do you call he
partridge the father of the little child which you find in your bed mr allworthy expr great surprize at this account and the captain as great at his ignorance of it for he say he have know it above a month and at length recollect with much difficulty that he be tell it by mrs wilkins
upon this wilkins be immediately summon who have confirm what the captain have say be by mr allworthy by and with the captain s advice dispatch to little baddington to inform herself of the truth of the fact for the captain expr great dislike at all hasty proceeding in criminal matter and say he would by no mean have mr allworthy take any resolution either to the prejudice of the child or its father before he be satisfied that the latter be guilty for though he have privately satisfy himself of this from one of partridge s neighbour yet he be too generous to give any such evidence to mr allworthy
chapter vi
the trial of partridge the schoolmaster for incontinency the evidence of his wife a short reflection on the wisdom of our law with other grave matter which those will like good who understand they most
it may be wonder that a story so well know and which have furnish so much matter of conversation should never have be mention to mr allworthy himself who be perhaps the only person in that country who have never hear of it
to account in some measure for this to the reader i think proper to inform he that there be no one in the kingdom less interested in oppose that doctrine concern the meaning of the word charity which hath be see in the precede chapter than our good man
indeed he be equally intitle to this virtue in either sense for as no man be ever more sensible of the want or more ready to relieve the distress of other so none could be more tender of their character or slow to believe anything to their disadvantage
scandal therefore never find any access to his table for as it hath be long since observe that you may know a man by his companion so i will venture to say that by attend to the conversation at a great man s table you may satisfy yourself of his religion his politic his taste and indeed of his entire disposition for though a few odd fellow will utter their own sentiment in all place yet much the great part of mankind have enough of the courtier to accommodate their conversation to the taste and inclination of their superior
but to return to mrs wilkins who have execute her commission with great dispatch though at fifteen mile distance bring back such a confirmation of the schoolmaster s guilt that mr allworthy determine to send for the criminal and examine he viva voce
mr partridge therefore be summon to attend in order to his defence if he could make any against this accusation
at the time appoint before mr allworthy himself at paradise-hall come as well the say partridge with anne his wife as mrs wilkins his accuser
and now mr allworthy be seat in the chair of justice mr partridge be bring before he
have hear his accusation from the mouth of mrs wilkins he plead not guilty make many vehement protestation of his innocence
mrs partridge be then examine who after a modest apology for be oblige to speak the truth against her husband relate all the circumstance with which the reader hath already be acquaint and at last conclude with her husband s confession of his guilt
whether she have forgive he or no i will not venture to determine but it be certain she be an unwilling witness in this cause and it be probable from certain other reason would never have be bring to depose as she do have not mrs wilkins with great art fish all out of she at her own house and have she not indeed make promise in mr allworthy s name that the punishment of her husband should not be such as might anywise affect his family
partridge still persist in assert his innocence though he admit he have make the above-mention confession which he however endeavour to account for by protest that he be force into it by the continue importunity she use who vow that as she be sure of his guilt she would never leave torment he till he have own it and faithfully promise that in such case she would never mention it to he more
hence he say he have be induce falsely to confess himself guilty though he be innocent and that he believe he should have confest a murder from the same motive
mrs partridge could not bear this imputation with patience and have no other remedy in the present place but tear she call forth a plentiful assistance from they and then address herself to mr allworthy she say or rather cry may it please your worship there never be any poor woman so injure as i be by that base man for this be not the only instance of his falsehood to i
no may it please your worship he hath injure my bed manybe the good time and often
i could have put up with his drunkenness and neglect of his business if he have not break one of the sacred commandment
besides if it have be out of door i have not matter it so much but with my own servant in my own house under my own roof to defile my own chaste bed which to be sure he hath with his beastly stink whore
yes you villain you have defile my own bed you have and then you have charge i with bullocke you into own the truth
it be very likely an t please your worship that i should bullock he
i have mark enow about my body to show of his cruelty to i
if you have be a man you villain you would have scorn to injure a woman in that manner
but you an t half a man you know it
nor have you be half a husband to i
you need run after whore you need when ibe sure--and since he provoke i i be ready an t please your worship to take my bodily oath that i find they a-bed together
what you have forget i suppose when you beat i into a fit and make the blood run down my forehead because i only civilly tax you with adultery
but i can prove it by all my neighbour
you have almost break my heart you have you have here mr allworthy interrupt and beg she to be pacify promise she that she should have justice then turn to partridge who stand aghast one half of his wit be hurry away by surprize and the other half by fear he say he be sorry to see there be so wicke a man in the world
he assure he that his prevaricating and lie backward and forward be a great aggravation of his guilt for which the only atonement he could make be by confession and repentance
he exhort he therefore to begin by immediately confess the fact and not to persist in deny what be so plainly prove against he even by his own wife
here reader i beg your patience a moment while i make a just compliment to the great wisdom and sagacity of our law which refuse to admit the evidence of a wife for or against her husband
this say a certain learn author who i believe be never quote before in any but a law-book would be the mean of create an eternal dissension between they
it would indeed be the mean of much perjury and of much whipping fin imprisoning transporting and hang
partridge stand a while silent till be bid to speak he say he have already speak the truth and appeal to heaven for his innocence and lastly to the girl herself whom he desire his worship immediately to send for for he be ignorant or at least pretend to be so that she have leave that part of the country
mr allworthy whose natural love of justice join to his coolness of temper make he always a most patient magistrate in hear all the witness which an accuse person could produce in his defence agree to defer his final determination of this matter till the arrival of jenny for whom he immediately dispatch a messenger and then have recommend peace between partridge and his wife though he address himself chiefly to the wrong person he appoint they to attend again the third day for he have send jenny a whole day s journey from his own house
at the appoint time the party all assemble when the messenger return bring word that jenny be not to be find for that she have leave her habitation a few day before in company with a recruiting officer
mr allworthy then declare that the evidence of such a slut as she appear to be would have deserve no credit but he say he could not help think that have she be present and would have declare the truth she must have confirm what so many circumstance together with his own confession and the declaration of his wife that she have catch her husband in the fact do sufficiently prove
he therefore once more exhort partridge to confess but he still avow his innocence mr allworthy declare himself satisfied of his guilt and that he be too bad a man to receive any encouragement from he
he therefore deprive he of his annuity and recommend repentance to he on account of another world and industry to maintain himself and his wife in this
there be not perhaps many more unhappy person than poor partridge
he have lose the good part of his income by the evidence of his wife and yet be daily upbraided by she for have among other thing be the occasion of deprive she of that benefit but such be his fortune and he be oblige to submit to it
though i call he poor partridge in the last paragraph i would have the reader rather impute that epithet to the compassion in my temper than conceive it to be any declaration of his innocence
whether he be innocent or not will perhaps appear hereafter but if the historic muse hath entrust i with any secret i will by no mean be guilty of discover they till she shall give i leave
here therefore the reader must suspend his curiosity
certain it be that whatever be the truth of the case there be evidence more than sufficient to convict he before allworthy indeed much less would have satisfy a bench of justice on an order of bastardy and yet notwithstanding the positiveness of mrs partridge who would have take the sacrament upon the matter there be a possibility that the schoolmaster be entirely innocent for though it appear clear on compare the time when jenny depart from little baddington with that of her delivery that she have there conceive this infant yet it by no mean follow of necessity that partridge must have be its father for to omit other particular there be in the same house a lad near eighteen between whom and jenny there have subsist sufficient intimacy to found a reasonable suspicion and yet so blind be jealousy this circumstance never once enter into the head of the enraged wife
whether partridge repent or not accord to mr allworthy s advice be not so apparent
certain it be that his wife repent heartily of the evidence she have give against he especially when she find mrs deborah have deceive she and refuse to make any application to mr allworthy on her behalf
she have however somewhat well success with mrs blifil who be as the reader must have perceive a much well-temper woman and very kindly undertake to solicit her brother to restore the annuity in which though good-nature might have some share yet a strong and more natural motive will appear in the next chapter
these solicitation be nevertheless unsuccessful for though mr allworthy do not think with some late writer that mercy consist only in punish offender yet he be as far from think that it be proper to this excellent quality to pardon great criminal wantonly without any reason whatever
any doubtfulness of the fact or any circumstance of mitigation be never disregard but the petition of an offender or the intercession of other do not in the least affect he
in a word he never pardon because the offender himself or his friend be unwilling that he should be punish
partridge and his wife be therefore both oblige to submit to their fate which be indeed severe enough for so far be he from double his industry on the account of his lessen income that he do in a manner abandon himself to despair and as he be by nature indolent that vice now increase upon he by which mean he lose the little school he have so that neither his wife nor himself would have have any bread to eat have not the charity of some good christian interpose and provide they with what be just sufficient for their sustenance
as this support be convey to they by an unknown hand they imagine and so i doubt not will the reader that mr allworthy himself be their secret benefactor who though he would not openly encourage vice could yet privately relieve the distress of the vicious themselves when these become too exquisite and disproportionate to their demerit
in which light their wretchedness appear now to fortune herself for she at length take pity on this miserable couple and considerably lessen the wretched state of partridge by put a final end to that of his wife who soon after catch the small-pox and die
the justice which mr allworthy have execute on partridge at first meet with universal approbation but no soon have he feel its consequence than his neighbour begin to relent and to compassionate his case and presently after to blame that as rigour and severity which they before call justice
they now exclaim against punish in cold blood and sing forth the praise of mercy and forgiveness
these cry be considerably increase by the death of mrs partridge which though owe to the distemper above mention which be no consequence of poverty or distress many be not ashamed to impute to mr allworthy s severity or as they now term it cruelty
partridge have now lose his wife his school and his annuity and the unknown person have now discontinue the last-mention charity resolve to change the scene and leave the country where he be in danger of starving with the universal compassion of all his neighbour
chapter vii
a short sketch of that felicity which prudent couple may extract from hatred with a short apology for those people who overlook imperfection in their friend
though the captain have effectually demolish poor partridge yet have he not reap the harvest he hop for which be to turn the foundling out of mr allworthy s house
on the contrary that gentleman grow every day fonder of little tommy as if he intend to counterbalance his severity to the father with extraordinary fondness and affection towards the son
this a good deal sour the captain s temper as do all the other daily instance of mr allworthy s generosity for he look on all such largess to be diminution of his own wealth
in this we have say he do not agree with his wife nor indeed in anything else for though an affection place on the understanding be by many wise person think more durable than that which be found on beauty yet it happen otherwise in the present case
nay the understanding of this couple be their principal bone of contention and one great cause of many quarrel which from time to time arise between they and which at last end on the side of the lady in a sovereign contempt for her husband and on the husband s in an utter abhorrence of his wife
as these have both exercise their talent chiefly in the study of divinity this be from their first acquaintance the most common topic of conversation between they
the captain like a well-breed man have before marriage always give up his opinion to that of the lady and this not in the clumsy awkward manner of a conceited blockhead who while he civilly yield to a superior in an argument be desirous of be still know to think himself in the right
the captain on the contrary though one of the proud fellow in the world so absolutely yield the victory to his antagonist that she who have not the least doubt of his sincerity retire always from the dispute with an admiration of her own understanding and a love for his
but though this complacence to one whom the captain thoroughly despise be not so uneasy to he as it would have be have any hope of preferment make it necessary to show the same submission to a hoadley or to some other of great reputation in the science yet even this cost he too much to be endure without some motive
matrimony therefore have remove all such motif he grow weary of this condescension and begin to treat the opinion of his wife with that haughtiness and insolence which none but those who deserve some contempt themselves can bestow and those only who deserve no contempt can bear
when the first torrent of tenderness be over and when in the calm and long interval between the fit reason begin to open the eye of the lady and she see this alteration of behaviour in the captain who at length answer all her argument only with pish and pshaw she be far from endure the indignity with a tame submission
indeed it at first so highly provoke she that it might have produce some tragical event have it not take a more harmless turn by fill she with the utmost contempt for her husband s understanding which somewhat qualify her hatred towards he though of this likewise she have a pretty moderate share
the captain s hatred to she be of a purer kind for as to any imperfection in her knowledge or understanding he no more despise she for they than for she not be six foot high
in his opinion of the female sex he exceed the moroseness of aristotle himself he look on a woman as on an animal of domestic use of somewhat high consideration than a cat since her office be of rather more importance but the difference between these two be in his estimation so small that in his marriage contract with mr allworthy s land and tenement it would have be pretty equal which of they he have take into the bargain
and yet so tender be his pride that it feel the contempt which his wife now begin to express towards he and this add to the surfeit he have before take of her love create in he a degree of disgust and abhorrence perhaps hardly to be exceed
one situation only of the married state be exclude from pleasure and that be a state of indifference but as many of my reader i hope know what an exquisite delight there be in conveying pleasure to a beloved object so some few i be afraid may have experience the satisfaction of torment one we hate
it be i apprehend to come at this latter pleasure that we see both sex often give up that ease in marriage which they might otherwise possess though their mate be never so disagreeable to they
hence the wife often put on fit of love and jealousy nay even deny herself any pleasure to disturb and prevent those of her husband and he again in return put frequent restraint on himself and stay at home in company which he dislike in order to confine his wife to what she equally detest
hence too must flow those tear which a widow sometimes so plentifully shed over the ash of a husband with whom she lead a life of constant disquiet and turbulency and whom now she can never hope to torment any more
but if ever any couple enjoy this pleasure it be at present experience by the captain and his lady
it be always a sufficient reason to either of they to be obstinate in any opinion that the other have previously assert the contrary
if the one propose any amusement the other constantly object to it they never love or hat commend or abuse the same person
and for this reason as the captain look with an evil eye on the little foundling his wife begin now to caress it almost equally with her own child
the reader will be apt to conceive that this behaviour between the husband and wife do not greatly contribute to mr allworthy s repose as it tend so little to that serene happiness which he have design for all three from this alliance but the truth be though he might be a little disappointed in his sanguine expectation yet he be far from be acquaint with the whole matter for as the captain be from certain obvious reason much on his guard before he the lady be oblige for fear of her brother s displeasure to pursue the same conduct
in fact it be possible for a third person to be very intimate nay even to live long in the same house with a married couple who have any tolerable discretion and not even guess at the sour sentiment which they bear to each other for though the whole day may be sometimes too short for hatred as well as for love yet the many hour which they naturally spend together apart from all observer furnish people of tolerable moderation with such ample opportunity for the enjoyment of either passion that if they love they can support be a few hour in company without toy or if they hate without spit in each other s face
it be possible however that mr allworthy see enough to render he a little uneasy for we be not always to conclude that a wise man be not hurt because he doth not cry out and lament himself like those of a childish or effeminate temper
but indeed it be possible he might see some fault in the captain without any uneasiness at all for man of true wisdom and goodness be content to take person and thing as they be without complain of their imperfection or attempt to amend they
they can see a fault in a friend a relation or an acquaintance without ever mention it to the party themselves or to any other and this often without lessen their affection
indeed unless great discernment be temper with this overlook disposition we ought never to contract friendship but with a degree of folly which we can deceive for i hope my friend will pardon i when i declare i know none of they without a fault and i should be sorry if i could imagine i have any friend who could not see mine
forgiveness of this kind we give and demand in turn
it be an exercise of friendship and perhaps none of the least pleasant
and this forgiveness we must bestow without desire of amendment
there be perhaps no surer mark of folly than an attempt to correct the natural infirmity of those we love
the fine composition of human nature as well as the fine china may have a flaw in it and this i be afraid in either case be equally incurable though nevertheless the pattern may remain of the high value
upon the whole then mr allworthy certainly see some imperfection in the captain but as this be a very artful man and eternally upon his guard before he these appear to he no more than blemish in a good character which his goodness make he overlook and his wisdom prevent he from discover to the captain himself
very different would have be his sentiment have he discover the whole which perhaps would in time have be the case have the husband and wife long continue this kind of behaviour to each other but this kind fortune take effectual mean to prevent by force the captain to do that which render he again dear to his wife and restore all her tenderness and affection towards he
chapter viii
a receipt to regain the lost affection of a wife which hath never be know to fail in the most desperate case
the captain be make large amend for the unpleasant minute which he pass in the conversation of his wife and which be as few as he could contrive to make they by the pleasant meditation he enjoy when alone
these meditation be entirely employ on mr allworthy s fortune for first he exercise much thought in calculate as well as he could the exact value of the whole which calculation he often see occasion to alter in his own favour and secondly and chiefly he please himself with intend alteration in the house and garden and in project many other scheme as well for the improvement of the estate as of the grandeur of the place for this purpose he apply himself to the study of architecture and gardening and read over many book on both these subject for these science indeed employ his whole time and form his only amusement
he at last complete a most excellent plan and very sorry we be that it be not in our power to present it to our reader since even the luxury of the present age i believe would hardly match it
it have indeed in a superlative degree the two principal ingredient which serve to recommend all great and noble design of this nature for it require an immoderate expense to execute and a vast length of time to bring it to any sort of perfection
the former of these the immense wealth of which the captain suppose mr allworthy possess and which he think himself sure of inheriting promise very effectually to supply and the latter the soundness of his own constitution and his time of life which be only what be call middle-age remove all apprehension of his not live to accomplish
nothing be want to enable he to enter upon the immediate execution of this plan but the death of mr allworthy in calculate which he have employ much of his own algebra besides purchase every book extant that treat of the value of life reversion c
from all which he satisfy himself that as he have every day a chance of this happening so have he more than an even chance of its happening within a few year
but while the captain be one day busy in deep contemplation of this kind one of the most unlucky as well as unseasonable accident happen to he
the utmost malice of fortune could indeed have contrive nothing so cruel so mal-a-propos so absolutely destructive to all his scheme
in short not to keep the reader in long suspense just at the very instant when his heart be exult in meditation on the happiness which would accrue to he by mr allworthy s death he himself--die of an apoplexy
this unfortunately befel the captain as he be take his evening walk by himself so that nobody be present to lend he any assistance if indeed any assistance could have preserve he
he take therefore measure of that proportion of soil which be now become adequate to all his future purpose and he lie dead on the ground a great though not a living example of the truth of that observation of horace tu secanda marmora locas sub ipsum funus et sepulchri immemor struis domos which sentiment i shall thus give to the english reader you provide the noble material for building when a pickaxe and a spade be only necessary and build house of five hundred by a hundred foot forget that of six by two chapter ix
a proof of the infallibility of the forego receipt in the lamentation of the widow with other suitable decoration of death such as physician c and an epitaph in the true stile
mr allworthy his sister and another lady be assemble at the accustomed hour in the supper-room where have wait a considerable time long than usual mr allworthy first declare he begin to grow uneasy at the captain s stay for he be always most punctual at his meal and give order that the bell should be ring without the door and especially towards those walk which the captain be willnot to use
all these summon prove ineffectual for the captain have by perverse accident betake himself to a new walk that evening mrs blifil declare she be seriously frightened
upon which the other lady who be one of her most intimate acquaintance and who well know the true state of her affection endeavour all she could to pacify she tell she--to be sure she could not help be uneasy but that she should hope the good
that perhaps the sweetness of the evening have intice the captain to go far than his usual walk or he might be detain at some neighbour s
mrs blifil answer no she be sure some accident have befall he for that he would never stay out without send her word as he must know how uneasy it would make she
the other lady have no other argument to use betook herself to the entreaty usual on such occasion and beg she not to frighten herself for it might be of very ill consequence to her own health and fill out a very large glass of wine advise and at last prevail with she to drink it
mr allworthy now return into the parlour for he have be himself in search after the captain
his countenance sufficiently show the consternation he be under which indeed have a good deal deprive he of speech but as grief operate variously on different mind so the same apprehension which depress his voice elevate that of mrs blifil
she now begin to bewail herself in very bitter term and flood of tear accompany her lamentation which the lady her companion declare she could not blame but at the same time dissuade she from indulge attempt to moderate the grief of her friend by philosophical observation on the many disappointment to which human life be daily subject which she say be a sufficient consideration to fortify our mind against any accident how sudden or terrible soever
she say her brother s example ought to teach she patience who though indeed he could not be suppose as much concerned as herself yet be doubtless very uneasy though his resignation to the divine will have restrain his grief within due bound
mention not my brother say mrs blifil i alone be the object of your pity
what be the terror of friendship to what a wife feel on these occasion
oh he be lose
somebody hath murder he--i shall never see he more here a torrent of tear have the same consequence with what the suppression have occasion to mr allworthy and she remain silent
at this interval a servant come run in out of breath and cry out the captain be find and before he could proceed far he be follow by two more bear the dead body between they
here the curious reader may observe another diversity in the operation of grief for as mr allworthy have be before silent from the same cause which have make his sister vociferous so do the present sight which draw tear from the gentleman put an entire stop to those of the lady who first give a violent scream and presently after fall into a fit
the room be soon full of servant some of whom with the lady visitant be employ in care of the wife and other with mr allworthy assist in carry off the captain to a warm bed where every method be try in order to restore he to life
and glad should we be could we inform the reader that both these body have be attend with equal success for those who undertake the care of the lady succeed so well that after the fit have continue a decent time she again revive to their great satisfaction but as to the captain all experiment of bleeding chafing dropping c prove ineffectual
death that inexorable judge have pass sentence on he and refuse to grant he a reprieve though two doctor who arrive and be fee d at one and the same instant be his counsel
these two doctor whom to avoid any malicious application we shall distinguish by the name of dr y
and dr z have feel his pulse to wit dr y
his right arm and dr z
his left both agree that he be absolutely dead but as to the distemper or cause of his death they differ dr y
hold that he die of an apoplexy and dr z
of an epilepsy
hence arise a dispute between the learn man in which each deliver the reason of their several opinion
these be of such equal force that they serve both to confirm either doctor in his own sentiment and make not the least impression on his adversary
to say the truth every physician almost hath his favourite disease to which he ascribe all the victory obtain over human nature
the gout the rheumatism the stone the gravel and the consumption have all their several patron in the faculty and none more than the nervous fever or the fever on the spirit
and here we may account for those disagreement in opinion concern the cause of a patient s death which sometimes occur between the most learn of the college and which have greatly surprize that part of the world who have be ignorant of the fact we have above assert
the reader may perhaps be surprize that instead of endeavour to revive the patient the learn gentleman should fall immediately into a dispute on the occasion of his death but in reality all such experiment have be make before their arrival for the captain be put into a warm bed have his vein scarify his forehead chafe and all sort of strong drop apply to his lip and nostril
the physician therefore find themselves anticipate in everything they order be at a loss how to apply that portion of time which it be usual and decent to remain for their fee and be therefore necessitate to find some subject or other for discourse and what could more naturally present itself than that before mention
our doctor be about to take their leave when mr allworthy have give over the captain and acquiesce in the divine will begin to enquire after his sister whom he desire they to visit before their departure
this lady be now recover of her fit and to use the common phrase as well as could be expect for one in her condition
the doctor therefore all previous ceremony be comply with as this be a new patient attend accord to desire and lay hold on each of her hand as they have before do on those of the corpse
the case of the lady be in the other extreme from that of her husband for as he be past all the assistance of physic so in reality she require none
there be nothing more unjust than the vulgar opinion by which physician be misrepresent as friend to death
on the contrary i believe if the number of those who recover by physic could be oppose to that of the martyr to it the former would rather exceed the latter
nay some be so cautious on this head that to avoid a possibility of kill the patient they abstain from all method of cure and prescribe nothing but what can neither do good nor harm
i have hear some of these with great gravity deliver it as a maxim that nature should be leave to do her own work while the physician stand by as it be to clap she on the back and encourage she when she doth well so little then do our doctor delight in death that they discharge the corpse after a single fee but they be not so disgusted with their live patient concern whose case they immediately agree and fall to prescribe with great diligence
whether as the lady have at first persuade her physician to believe her ill they have now in return persuade she to believe herself so i will not determine but she continue a whole month with all the decoration of sickness
during this time she be visit by physician attend by nurse and receive constant message from her acquaintance to enquire after her health
at length the decent time for sickness and immoderate grief being expire the doctor be discharge and the lady begin to see company be alter only from what she be before by that colour of sadness in which she have dress her person and countenance
the captain be now inter and might perhaps have already make a large progress towards oblivion have not the friendship of mr allworthy take care to preserve his memory by the follow epitaph which be write by a man of as great genius as integrity and one who perfectly well know the captain
here lie in expectation of a joyful rising the body of captain john blifil
london had the honour of his birth oxford of his education
his part be an honour to his profession and to his country his life to his religion and human nature
he be a dutiful son a tender husband an affectionate father a most kind brother a sincere friend a devout christian and a good man
his inconsolable widow hath erect this stone the monument of his virtue and of she affection
book iii
contain the most memorable transaction which pass in the family of mr allworthy from the time when tommy jones arrived at the age of fourteen till he attain the age of nineteen
in this book the reader may pick up some hint concern the education of child
chapter i
contain little or nothing
the reader will be pleased to remember that at the beginning of the second book of this history we give he a hint of our intention to pass over several large period of time in which nothing happen worthy of be record in a chronicle of this kind
in so do we do not only consult our own dignity and ease but the good and advantage of the reader for besides that by these mean we prevent he from throw away his time in reading without either pleasure or emolument we give he at all such season an opportunity of employ that wonderful sagacity of which he be master by fill up these vacant space of time with his own conjecture for which purpose we have take care to qualify he in the precede page
for instance what reader but know that mr allworthy feel at first for the loss of his friend those emotion of grief which on such occasion enter into all man whose heart be not compose of flint or their head of as solid material
again what reader doth not know that philosophy and religion in time moderate and at last extinguish this grief
the former of these teach the folly and vanity of it and the latter correct it as unlawful and at the same time assuage it by raise future hope and assurance which enable a strong and religious mind to take leave of a friend on his deathbe with little less indifference than if he be prepare for a long journey and indeed with little less hope of see he again
nor can the judicious reader be at a great loss on account of mrs bridget blifil who he may be assure conduct herself through the whole season in which grief be to make its appearance on the outside of the body with the strict regard to all the rule of custom and decency suit the alteration of her countenance to the several alteration of her habit for as this change from weed to black from black to grey from grey to white so do her countenance change from dismal to sorrowful from sorrowful to sad and from sad to serious till the day come in which she be allow to return to her former serenity
we have mention these two as example only of the task which may be impose on reader of the low class
