much high and hard exercise of judgment and penetration may reasonably be expect from the upper graduate in criticism
many notable discovery will i doubt not be make by such of the transaction which happen in the family of our worthy man during all the year which we have think proper to pass over for though nothing worthy of a place in this history occur within that period yet do several incident happen of equal importance with those report by the daily and weekly historian of the age in read which great number of person consume a considerable part of their time very little i be afraid to their emolument
now in the conjecture here propose some of the most excellent faculty of the mind may be employ to much advantage since it be a more useful capacity to be able to foretel the action of man in any circumstance from their character than to judge of their character from their action
the former i own require the great penetration but may be accomplish by true sagacity with no less certainty than the latter
as we be sensible that much the great part of our reader be very eminently possess of this quality we have leave they a space of twelve year to exert it in and shall now bring forth our heroe at about fourteen year of age not question that many have be long impatient to be introduce to his acquaintance
chapter ii
the heroe of this great history appear with very bad omen
a little tale of so low a kind that some may think it not worth their notice
a word or two concern a squire and more relate to a gamekeeper and a schoolmaster
as we determine when we first sit down to write this history to flatter no man but to guide our pen throughout by the direction of truth we be oblige to bring our heroe on the stage in a much more disadvantageous manner than we could wish and to declare honestly even at his first appearance that it be the universal opinion of all mr allworthy s family that he be certainly bear to be hang
indeed i be sorry to say there be too much reason for this conjecture the lad have from his early year discover a propensity to many vice and especially to one which hath as direct a tendency as any other to that fate which we have just now observe to have be prophetically denounce against he he have be already convict of three robbery viz of rob an orchard of steal a duck out of a farmer s yard and of pick master blifil s pocket of a ball
the vice of this young man be moreover heighten by the disadvantageous light in which they appear when oppose to the virtue of master blifil his companion a youth of so different a cast from little jones that not only the family but all the neighbourhood resound his praise
he be indeed a lad of a remarkable disposition sober discreet and pious beyond his age quality which gain he the love of every one who know he while tom jones be universally dislike and many express their wonder that mr allworthy would suffer such a lad to be educate with his nephew lest the moral of the latter should be corrupt by his example
an incident which happen about this time will set the character of these two lad more fairly before the discerning reader than be in the power of the long dissertation
tom jones who bad as he be must serve for the heroe of this history have only one friend among all the servant of the family for as to mrs wilkins she have long since give he up and be perfectly reconcile to her mistress
this friend be the gamekeeper a fellow of a loose kind of disposition and who be think not to entertain much strict notion concern the difference of meum and tuum than the young gentleman himself
and hence this friendship give occasion to many sarcastical remark among the domestic most of which be either proverb before or at least be become so now and indeed the wit of they all may be comprise in that short latin proverb noscitur a socio which i think be thus express in english you may know he by the company he keep to say the truth some of that atrocious wickedness in jones of which we have just mention three example might perhaps be derive from the encouragement he have receive from this fellow who in two or three instance have be what the law call an accessary after the fact for the whole duck and great part of the apple be convert to the use of the gamekeeper and his family though as jones alone be discover the poor lad bore not only the whole smart but the whole blame both which fall again to his lot on the follow occasion
contiguous to mr allworthy s estate be the manor of one of those gentleman who be call preserver of the game
this specie of man from the great severity with which they revenge the death of a hare or partridge might be think to cultivate the same superstition with the bannians in india many of whom we be tell dedicate their whole life to the preservation and protection of certain animal be it not that our english bannians while they preserve they from other enemy will most unmercifully slaughter whole horse-load themselves so that they stand clearly acquit of any such heathenish superstition
i have indeed a much well opinion of this kind of man than be entertain by some as i take they to answer the order of nature and the good purpose for which they be ordain in a more ample manner than many other
now as horace tell we that there be a set of human being fruge consumere nati bear to consume the fruit of the earth so i make no manner of doubt but that there be other feras consumere nati bear to consume the beast of the field or as it be commonly call the game and none i believe will deny but that those squire fulfil this end of their creation
little jones go one day a shooting with the gamekeeper when happen to spring a covey of partridge near the border of that manor over which fortune to fulfil the wise purpose of nature have plant one of the game consumer the bird fly into it and be mark as it be call by the two sportsman in some furze bush about two or three hundred pace beyond mr allworthy s dominion
mr allworthy have give the fellow strict order on pain of forfeit his place never to trespass on any of his neighbour no more on those who be less rigid in this matter than on the lord of this manor
with regard to other indeed these order have not be always very scrupulously keep but as the disposition of the gentleman with whom the partridge have take sanctuary be well know the gamekeeper have never yet attempt to invade his territory
nor have he do it now have not the young sportsman who be excessively eager to pursue the fly game over-persuade he but jones be very importunate the other who be himself keen enough after the sport yield to his persuasion enter the manor and shoot one of the partridge
the gentleman himself be at that time on horse-back at a little distance from they and hear the gun go off he immediately make towards the place and discover poor tom for the gamekeeper have leapt into the thick part of the furze-brake where he have happily conceal himself
the gentleman have search the lad and find the partridge upon he denounce great vengeance swear he would acquaint mr allworthy
he be as good as his word for he ride immediately to his house and complain of the trespass on his manor in as high term and as bitter language as if his house have be break open and the most valuable furniture steal out of it
he add that some other person be in his company though he could not discover he for that two gun have be discharge almost in the same instant
and say he we have find only this partridge but the lord know what mischief they have do at his return home tom be presently convene before mr allworthy
he own the fact and alledge no other excuse but what be really true viz that the covey be originally spring in mr allworthy s own manor
tom be then interrogate who be with he which mr allworthy declare he be resolve to know acquaint the culprit with the circumstance of the two gun which have be depose by the squire and both his servant but tom stoutly persist in assert that he be alone yet to say the truth he hesitate a little at first which would have confirm mr allworthy s belief have what the squire and his servant say want any further confirmation
the gamekeeper be a suspect person be now send for and the question put to he but he rely on the promise which tom have make he to take all upon himself very resolutely deny be in company with the young gentleman or indeed have see he the whole afternoon
mr allworthy then turn towards tom with more than usual anger in his countenance and advise he to confess who be with he repeat that he be resolve to know
the lad however still maintain his resolution and be dismiss with much wrath by mr allworthy who tell he he should have to the next morning to consider of it when he should be question by another person and in another manner
poor jones spend a very melancholy night and the more so as he be without his usual companion for master blifil be go abroad on a visit with his mother
fear of the punishment he be to suffer be on this occasion his least evil his chief anxiety be lest his constancy should fail he and he should be bring to betray the gamekeeper whose ruin he know must now be the consequence
nor do the gamekeeper pass his time much well
he have the same apprehension with the youth for whose honour he have likewise a much tenderer regard than for his skin
in the morning when tom attend the reverend mr thwackum the person to whom mr allworthy have commit the instruction of the two boy he have the same question put to he by that gentleman which he have be ask the evening before to which he return the same answer
the consequence of this be so severe a whipping that it possibly fall little short of the torture with which confession be in some country extort from criminal
tom bear his punishment with great resolution and though his master ask he between every stroke whether he would not confess he be content to be flead rather than betray his friend or break the promise he have make
the gamekeeper be now relieve from his anxiety and mr allworthy himself begin to be concern at tom s suffering for besides that mr thwackum be highly enraged that he be not able to make the boy say what he himself please have carry his severity much beyond the good man s intention this latter begin now to suspect that the squire have be mistake which his extreme eagerness and anger seem to make probable and as for what the servant have say in confirmation of their master s account he lay no great stress upon that
now as cruelty and injustice be two idea of which mr allworthy could by no mean support the consciousness a single moment he send for tom and after many kind and friendly exhortation say i be convince my dear child that my suspicion have wrong you i be sorry that you have be so severely punish on this account and at last give he a little horse to make he amend again repeat his sorrow for what have past
tom s guilt now fly in his face more than any severity could make it
he could more easily bear the lash of thwackum than the generosity of allworthy
the tear burst from his eye and he fall upon his knee cry oh sir you be too good to i
indeed you be
indeed i donot deserve it and at that very instant from the fulness of his heart have almost betray the secret but the good genius of the gamekeeper suggest to he what might be the consequence to the poor fellow and this consideration seal his lip
thwackum do all he could to persuade allworthy from show any compassion or kindness to the boy say he have persist in an untruth and give some hint that a second whipping might probably bring the matter to light
but mr allworthy absolutely refuse to consent to the experiment
he say the boy have suffer enough already for conceal the truth even if he be guilty see that he could have no motive but a mistaken point of honour for so do
honour cry thwackum with some warmth mere stubbornness and obstinacy
can honour teach any one to tell a lie or can any honour exist independent of religion this discourse happen at table when dinner be just end and there be present mr allworthy mr thwackum and a third gentleman who now enter into the debate and whom before we proceed any far we shall briefly introduce to our reader s acquaintance
chapter iii
the character of mr square the philosopher and of mr thwackum the divine with a dispute concerning---- the name of this gentleman who have then reside some time at mr allworthy s house be mr square
his natural part be not of the first rate but he have greatly improve they by a learn education
he be deeply read in the antient and a prof master of all the work of plato and aristotle
upon which great model he have principally form himself sometimes accord with the opinion of the one and sometimes with that of the other
in moral he be a prof platonist and in religion he incline to be an aristotelian
but though he have as we have say form his moral on the platonic model yet he perfectly agree with the opinion of aristotle in consider that great man rather in the quality of a philosopher or a speculatist than as a legislator
this sentiment he carry a great way indeed so far as to regard all virtue as matter of theory only
this it be true he never affirm as i have hear to any one and yet upon the least attention to his conduct i cannot help think it be his real opinion as it will perfectly reconcile some contradiction which might otherwise appear in his character
this gentleman and mr thwackum scarce ever meet without a disputation for their tenet be indeed diametrically opposite to each other
square hold human nature to be the perfection of all virtue and that vice be a deviation from our nature in the same manner as deformity of body be
thwackum on the contrary maintain that the human mind since the fall be nothing but a sink of iniquity till purify and redeem by grace
in one point only they agree which be in all their discourse on morality never to mention the word goodness
the favourite phrase of the former be the natural beauty of virtue that of the latter be the divine power of grace
the former measure all action by the unalterable rule of right and the eternal fitness of thing the latter decide all matter by authority but in do this he always use the scripture and their commentator as the lawyer doth his coke upon lyttleton where the comment be of equal authority with the text
after this short introduction the reader will be pleased to remember that the parson have conclude his speech with a triumphant question to which he have apprehend no answer viz can any honour exist independent on religion
to this square answer that it be impossible to discourse philosophically concern word till their meaning be first establish that there be scarce any two word of a more vague and uncertain signification than the two he have mention for that there be almost as many different opinion concern honour as concern religion
but say he if by honour you mean the true natural beauty of virtue i will maintain it may exist independent of any religion whatever
nay add he you yourself will allow it may exist independent of all but one so will a mahometan a jew and all the maintainer of all the different sect in the world thwackum reply this be argue with the usual malice of all the enemy to the true church
he say he doubt not but that all the infidel and heretick in the world would if they could confine honour to their own absurd error and damnable deception but honour say he be not therefore manifold because there be many absurd opinion about it nor be religion manifold because there be various sect and heresy in the world
when i mention religion i mean the christian religion and not only the christian religion but the protestant religion and not only the protestant religion but the church of england
and when i mention honour i mean that mode of divine grace which be not only consistent with but dependent upon this religion and be consistent with and dependent upon no other
now to say that the honour i here mean and which be i think all the honour i could be suppose to mean will uphold much less dictate an untruth be to assert an absurdity too shocking to be conceive i purposely avoid say square draw a conclusion which i think evident from what i have say but if you perceive it i be sure you have not attempt to answer it
however to drop the article of religion i think it be plain from what you have say that we have different idea of honour or why do we not agree in the same term of its explanation
i have assert that true honour and true virtue be almost synonymous term and they be both found on the unalterable rule of right and the eternal fitness of thing to which an untruth be absolutely repugnant and contrary it be certain that true honour cannot support an untruth
in this therefore i think we be agree but that this honour can be say to be found on religion to which it be antecedent if by religion be mean any positive law-- i agree answer thwackum with great warmth with a man who assert honour to be antecedent to religion
mr allworthy do i agree-- he be proceed when mr allworthy interpose tell they very coldly they have both mistake his meaning for that he have say nothing of true honour it be possible however he would not have easily quiet the disputant who be grow equally warm have not another matter now fall out which put a final end to the conversation at present
chapter iv
contain a necessary apology for the author and a childish incident which perhaps require an apology likewise
before i proceed far i shall beg leave to obviate some misconstruction into which the zeal of some few reader may lead they for i would not willingly give offence to any especially to man who be warm in the cause of virtue or religion
i hope therefore no man will by the gross misunderstanding or perversion of my meaning misrepresent i as endeavour to cast any ridicule on the great perfection of human nature and which do indeed alone purify and ennoble the heart of man and raise he above the brute creation
this reader i will venture to say and by how much the well man you be yourself by so much the more will you be incline to believe i that i would rather have bury the sentiment of these two person in eternal oblivion than have do any injury to either of these glorious cause
on the contrary it be with a view to their service that i have take upon i to record the life and action of two of their false and pretend champion
a treacherous friend be the most dangerous enemy and i will say boldly that both religion and virtue have receive more real discredit from hypocrite than the witty profligate or infidel could ever cast upon they nay far as these two in their purity be rightly call the band of civil society and be indeed the great of blessing so when poison and corrupt with fraud pretence and affectation they have become the bad of civil curse and have enable man to perpetrate the most cruel mischief to their own specie
indeed i doubt not but this ridicule will in general be allow my chief apprehension be as many true and just sentiment often come from the mouth of these person lest the whole should be take together and i should be conceive to ridicule all alike
now the reader will be pleased to consider that as neither of these man be fool they could not be suppose to have holden none but wrong principle and to have utter nothing but absurdity what injustice therefore must i have do to their character have i select only what be bad
and how horribly wretched and maim must their argument have appear
upon the whole it be not religion or virtue but the want of they which be here expose
have not thwackum too much neglect virtue and square religion in the composition of their several system and have not both utterly discard all natural goodness of heart they have never be represent as the object of derision in this history in which we will now proceed
this matter then which put an end to the debate mention in the last chapter be no other than a quarrel between master blifil and tom jones the consequence of which have be a bloody nose to the former for though master blifil notwithstanding he be the young be in size above the other s match yet tom be much his superior at the noble art of boxing
tom however cautiously avoid all engagement with that youth for besides that tommy jones be an inoffensive lad amidst all his roguery and really love blifil mr thwackum be always the second of the latter would have be sufficient to deter he
but well say a certain author no man be wise at all hour it be therefore no wonder that a boy be not so
a difference arise at play between the two lad master blifil call tom a beggarly bastard
upon which the latter who be somewhat passionate in his disposition immediately cause that phenomenon in the face of the former which we have above remember
master blifil now with his blood run from his nose and the tear gallop after from his eye appear before his uncle and the tremendous thwackum
in which court an indictment of assault battery and wounding be instantly prefer against tom who in his excuse only plead the provocation which be indeed all the matter that master blifil have omit
it be indeed possible that this circumstance might have escape his memory for in his reply he positively insist that he have make use of no such appellation add heaven forbid such naughty word should ever come out of his mouth tom though against all form of law rejoin in affirmance of the word
upon which master blifil say it be no wonder
those who will tell one fib will hardly stick at another
if i have tell my master such a wicked fib as you have do i should be ashamed to show my face what fib child cry thwackum pretty eagerly
why he tell you that nobody be with he a shooting when he kill the partridge but he know here he burst into a flood of tear yes he know for he confess it to i that black george the gamekeeper be there
nay he say--yes you do--deny it if you can that you would not have conf the truth though master have cut you to piece at this the fire flash from thwackum s eye and he cry out in triumph-- oh
ho
this be your mistaken notion of honour
this be the boy who be not to be whip again but mr allworthy with a more gentle aspect turn towards the lad and say be this true child
how come you to persist so obstinately in a falsehood tom say he scorn a lie as much as any one but he think his honour engage he to act as he do for he have promise the poor fellow to conceal he which he say he think himself far oblige to as the gamekeeper have beg he not to go into the gentleman s manor and have at last go himself in compliance with his persuasion he say this be the whole truth of the matter and he would take his oath of it and conclude with very passionately beg mr allworthy to have compassion on the poor fellow s family especially as he himself only have be guilty and the other have be very difficultly prevail on to do what he do
indeed sir say he it could hardly be call a lie that i tell for the poor fellow be entirely innocent of the whole matter
i should have go alone after the bird nay i do go at first and he only follow i to prevent more mischief
do pray sir let i be punish take my little horse away again but pray sir forgive poor george mr allworthy hesitate a few moment and then dismiss the boy advise they to live more friendly and peaceably together
chapter v
the opinion of the divine and the philosopher concern the two boy with some reason for their opinion and other matter
it be probable that by disclose this secret which have be communicate in the utmost confidence to he young blifil preserve his companion from a good lashing for the offence of the bloody nose would have be of itself sufficient cause for thwackum to have proceed to correction but now this be totally absorb in the consideration of the other matter and with regard to this mr allworthy declare privately he think the boy deserve reward rather than punishment so that thwackum s hand be withhold by a general pardon
thwackum whose meditation be full of birch exclaim against this weak and as he say he would venture to call it wicked lenity
to remit the punishment of such crime be he say to encourage they
he enlarge much on the correction of child and quote many text from solomon and other which be to be find in so many other book shall not be find here
he then apply himself to the vice of lying on which head he be altogether as learn as he have be on the other
square say he have be endeavour to reconcile the behaviour of tom with his idea of perfect virtue but could not
he own there be something which at first sight appear like fortitude in the action but as fortitude be a virtue and falsehood a vice they could by no mean agree or unite together
he add that as this be in some measure to confound virtue and vice it might be worth mr thwackum s consideration whether a large castigation might not be lay on upon the account
as both these learn man concur in censuring jones so be they no less unanimous in applaud master blifil
to bring truth to light be by the parson assert to be the duty of every religious man and by the philosopher this be declare to be highly conformable with the rule of right and the eternal and unalterable fitness of thing
all this however weigh very little with mr allworthy
he could not be prevail on to sign the warrant for the execution of jones
there be something within his own breast with which the invincible fidelity which that youth have preserve correspond much well than it have do with the religion of thwackum or with the virtue of square
he therefore strictly order the former of these gentleman to abstain from lay violent hand on tom for what have past
the pedagogue be oblige to obey those order but not without great reluctance and frequent muttering that the boy would be certainly spoil
towards the gamekeeper the good man behave with more severity
he presently summon that poor fellow before he and after many bitter remonstrance pay he his wage and dismist he from his service for mr allworthy rightly observe that there be a great difference between be guilty of a falsehood to excuse yourself and to excuse another
he likewise urge as the principal motive to his inflexible severity against this man that he have basely suffer tom jones to undergo so heavy a punishment for his sake whereas he ought to have prevent it by make the discovery himself
when this story become public many people differ from square and thwackum in judge the conduct of the two lad on the occasion
master blifil be generally call a sneaking rascal a poor-spirit wretch with other epithet of the like kind whilst tom be honour with the appellation of a brave lad a jolly dog and an honest fellow
indeed his behaviour to black george much ingratiate he with all the servant for though that fellow be before universally dislike yet he be no soon turn away than he be as universally pitied and the friendship and gallantry of tom jones be celebrate by they all with the high applause and they condemn master blifil as openly as they durst without incur the danger of offend his mother
for all this however poor tom smart in the flesh for though thwackum have be inhibit to exercise his arm on the forego account yet as the proverb say it be easy to find a stick c
so be it easy to find a rod and indeed the not be able to find one be the only thing which could have keep thwackum any long time from chastise poor jones
have the bare delight in the sport be the only inducement to the pedagogue it be probable master blifil would likewise have have his share but though mr allworthy have give he frequent order to make no difference between the lad yet be thwackum altogether as kind and gentle to this youth as he be harsh nay even barbarous to the other
to say the truth blifil have greatly gain his master s affection partly by the profound respect he always show his person but much more by the decent reverence with which he receive his doctrine for he have get by heart and frequently repeat his phrase and maintain all his master s religious principle with a zeal which be surprize in one so young and which greatly endear he to the worthy preceptor
tom jones on the other hand be not only deficient in outward token of respect often forget to pull off his hat or to bow at his master s approach but be altogether as unmindful both of his master s precept and example
he be indeed a thoughtless giddy youth with little sobriety in his manner and less in his countenance and would often very impudently and indecently laugh at his companion for his serious behaviour
mr square have the same reason for his preference of the former lad for tom jones show no more regard to the learn discourse which this gentleman would sometimes throw away upon he than to those of thwackum
he once venture to make a jest of the rule of right and at another time say he believe there be no rule in the world capable of make such a man as his father for so mr allworthy suffer himself to be call
master blifil on the contrary have address enough at sixteen to recommend himself at one and the same time to both these opposite
with one he be all religion with the other he be all virtue
and when both be present he be profoundly silent which both interpret in his favour and in their own
nor be blifil content with flatter both these gentleman to their face he take frequent occasion of praise they behind their back to allworthy before whom when they two be alone and his uncle commend any religious or virtuous sentiment for many such come constantly from he he seldom fail to ascribe it to the good instruction he have receive from either thwackum or square for he know his uncle repeat all such compliment to the person for whose use they be mean and he find by experience the great impression which they make on the philosopher as well as on the divine for to say the truth there be no kind of flattery so irresistible as this at second hand
the young gentleman moreover soon perceive how extremely grateful all those panegyric on his instructor be to mr allworthy himself as they so loudly resound the praise of that singular plan of education which he have lay down for this worthy man have observe the imperfect institution of our public school and the many vice which boy be there liable to learn have resolve to educate his nephew as well as the other lad whom he have in a manner adopt in his own house where he think their moral would escape all that danger of be corrupt to which they would be unavoidably expose in any public school or university
have therefore determine to commit these boy to the tuition of a private tutor mr thwackum be recommend to he for that office by a very particular friend of whose understanding mr allworthy have a great opinion and in whose integrity he place much confidence
this thwackum be fellow of a college where he almost entirely reside and have a great reputation for learning religion and sobriety of manner
and these be doubtless the qualification by which mr allworthy s friend have be induce to recommend he though indeed this friend have some obligation to thwackum s family who be the most considerable person in a borough which that gentleman represent in parliament
thwackum at his first arrival be extremely agreeable to allworthy and indeed he perfectly answer the character which have be give of he
upon long acquaintance however and more intimate conversation this worthy man see infirmity in the tutor which he could have wish he to have be without though as those seem greatly overbalance by his good quality they do not incline mr allworthy to part with he nor would they indeed have justify such a proceeding for the reader be greatly mistaken if he conceive that thwackum appear to mr allworthy in the same light as he doth to he in this history and he be as much deceive if he imagine that the most intimate acquaintance which he himself could have have with that divine would have inform he of those thing which we from our inspiration be enable to open and discover
of reader who from such conceit as these condemn the wisdom or penetration of mr allworthy i shall not scruple to say that they make a very bad and ungrateful use of that knowledge which we have communicate to they
these apparent error in the doctrine of thwackum serve greatly to palliate the contrary error in that of square which our good man no less saw and condemn
he think indeed that the different exuberancie of these gentleman would correct their different imperfection and that from both especially with his assistance the two lad would derive sufficient precept of true religion and virtue
if the event happen contrary to his expectation this possibly proceed from some fault in the plan itself which the reader hath my leave to discover if he can for we do not pretend to introduce any infallible character into this history where we hope nothing will be find which hath never yet be see in human nature
to return therefore the reader will not i think wonder that the different behaviour of the two lad above commemorate produce the different effect of which he hath already see some instance and besides this there be another reason for the conduct of the philosopher and the pedagogue but this be matter of great importance we shall reveal it in the next chapter
chapter vi
contain a well reason still for the before-mention opinion
it be to be know then that those two learn personage who have lately make a considerable figure on the theatre of this history have from their first arrival at mr allworthy s house take so great an affection the one to his virtue the other to his religion that they have meditate the close alliance with he
for this purpose they have cast their eye on that fair widow whom though we have not for some time make any mention of she the reader we trust hath not forget
mrs blifil be indeed the object to which they both aspire
it may seem remarkable that of four person whom we have commemorate at mr allworthy s house three of they should fix their inclination on a lady who be never greatly celebrate for her beauty and who be moreover now a little descend into the vale of year but in reality bosom friend and intimate acquaintance have a kind of natural propensity to particular female at the house of a friend--viz to his grandmother mother sister daughter aunt niece or cousin when they be rich and to his wife sister daughter niece cousin mistress or servant-maid if they should be handsome
we would not however have our reader imagine that person of such character as be support by thwackum and square would undertake a matter of this kind which hath be a little censure by some rigid moralist before they have thoroughly examine it and consider whether it be as shakespear phrase it stuff o th conscience or no
thwackum be encourage to the undertaking by reflect that to covet your neighbour s sister be nowhere forbidden and he know it be a rule in the construction of all law that expressum facit cessare tacitum the sense of which be when a lawgiver set down plainly his whole meaning we be prevent from make he mean what we please ourselves as some instance of woman therefore be mention in the divine law which forbid we to covet our neighbour s good and that of a sister omit he conclude it to be lawful
and as to square who be in his person what be call a jolly fellow or a widow s man he easily reconcile his choice to the eternal fitness of thing
now as both of these gentleman be industrious in take every opportunity of recommend themselves to the widow they apprehend one certain method be by give her son the constant preference to the other lad and as they conceive the kindness and affection which mr allworthy show the latter must be highly disagreeable to she they doubt not but the lay hold on all occasion to degrade and vilify he would be highly pleasing to she who as she hat the boy must love all those who do he any hurt
in this thwackum have the advantage for while square could only scarify the poor lad s reputation he could flea his skin and indeed he consider every lash he give he as a compliment pay to his mistress so that he could with the utmost propriety repeat this old flog line castigo te non quod odio habeam se quod amem
i chastise thee not out of hatred but out of love and this indeed he often have in his mouth or rather accord to the old phrase never more properly apply at his finger end
for this reason principally the two gentleman concur as we have see above in their opinion concern the two lad this being indeed almost the only instance of their concur on any point for beside the difference of their principle they have both long ago strongly suspect each other s design and hat one another with no little degree of inveteracy
this mutual animosity be a good deal increase by their alternate success for mrs blifil know what they would be at long before they imagine it or indeed intend she should for they proceed with great caution lest she should be offend and acquaint mr allworthy
but they have no reason for any such fear she be well enough please with a passion of which she intend none should have any fruit but herself
and the only fruit she design for herself be flattery and courtship for which purpose she soothe they by turn and a long time equally
she be indeed rather inclined to favour the parson s principle but square s person be more agreeable to her eye for he be a comely man whereas the pedagogue do in countenance very nearly resemble that gentleman who in the harlot s progress be see correct the lady in bridewell
whether mrs blifil have be surfeit with the sweet of marriage or disgust by its bitter or from what other cause it proceed i will not determine but she could never be bring to listen to any second proposal
however she at last converse with square with such a degree of intimacy that malicious tongue begin to whisper thing of she to which as well for the sake of the lady as that they be highly disagreeable to the rule of right and the fitness of thing we will give no credit and therefore shall not blot our paper with they
the pedagogue tis certain whip on without get a step nearer to his journey s end
indeed he have commit a great error and that square discover much soon than himself
mrs blifil as perhaps the reader may have formerly guess be not over and above pleased with the behaviour of her husband nay to be honest she absolutely hat he till his death at last a little reconcile he to her affection
it will not be therefore greatly wonder at if she have not the most violent regard to the offspring she have by he
and in fact she have so little of this regard that in his infancy she seldom see her son or take any notice of he and hence she acquiesce after a little reluctance in all the favour which mr allworthy shower on the foundling whom the good man call his own boy and in all thing put on an entire equality with master blifil
this acquiescence in mrs blifil be consider by the neighbour and by the family as a mark of her condescension to her brother s humour and she be imagine by all other as well as thwackum and square to hate the foundling in her heart nay the more civility she show he the more they conceive she detest he and the surer scheme she be lay for his ruin for as they think it her interest to hate he it be very difficult for she to persuade they she do not
thwackum be the more confirmed in his opinion as she have more than once slily cause he to whip tom jones when mr allworthy who be an enemy to this exercise be abroad whereas she have never give any such order concern young blifil
and this have likewise impose upon square
in reality though she certainly hat her own son--of which however monstrous it appear i be assure she be not a singular instance--she appear notwithstanding all her outward compliance to be in her heart sufficiently displease with all the favour show by mr allworthy to the foundling
she frequently complain of this behind her brother s back and very sharply censure he for it both to thwackum and square nay she would throw it in the tooth of allworthy himself when a little quarrel or miff as it be vulgarly call arise between they
however when tom grow up and give token of that gallantry of temper which greatly recommend man to woman this disinclination which she have discover to he when a child by degree abate and at last she so evidently demonstrate her affection to he to be much strong than what she bear her own son that it be impossible to mistake she any long
she be so desirous of often see he and discover such satisfaction and delight in his company that before he be eighteen year old he be become a rival to both square and thwackum and what be bad the whole country begin to talk as loudly of her inclination to tom as they have before do of that which she have show to square on which account the philosopher conceive the most implacable hatred for our poor heroe
chapter vii
in which the author himself make his appearance on the stage
though mr allworthy be not of himself hasty to see thing in a disadvantageous light and be a stranger to the public voice which seldom reach to a brother or a husband though it ring in the ear of all the neighbourhood yet be this affection of mrs blifil to tom and the preference which she too visibly give he to her own son of the utmost disadvantage to that youth
for such be the compassion which inhabit mr allworthy s mind that nothing but the steel of justice could ever subdue it
to be unfortunate in any respect be sufficient if there be no demerit to counterpoise it to turn the scale of that good man s pity and to engage his friendship and his benefaction
when therefore he plainly see master blifil be absolutely detest for that he be by his own mother he begin on that account only to look with an eye of compassion upon he and what the effect of compassion be in good and benevolent mind i need not here explain to most of my reader
henceforward he see every appearance of virtue in the youth through the magnifying end and view all his fault with the glass invert so that they become scarce perceptible
and this perhaps the amiable temper of pity may make commendable but the next step the weakness of human nature alone must excuse for he no soon perceive that preference which mrs blifil give to tom than that poor youth however innocent begin to sink in his affection as he rise in her
this it be true would of itself alone never have be able to eradicate jones from his bosom but it be greatly injurious to he and prepare mr allworthy s mind for those impression which afterwards produce the mighty event that will be contain hereafter in this history and to which it must be confest the unfortunate lad by his own wantonness wildness and want of caution too much contribute
in record some instance of these we shall if rightly understand afford a very useful lesson to those well-dispose youth who shall hereafter be our reader for they may here find that goodness of heart and openness of temper though these may give they great comfort within and administer to an honest pride in their own mind will by no mean alas
do their business in the world
prudence and circumspection be necessary even to the good of man
they be indeed as it be a guard to virtue without which she can never be safe
it be not enough that your design nay that your action be intrinsically good you must take care they shall appear so
if your inside be never so beautiful you must preserve a fair outside also
this must be constantly look to or malice and envy will take care to blacken it so that the sagacity and goodness of an allworthy will not be able to see through it and to discern the beauty within
let this my young reader be your constant maxim that no man can be good enough to enable he to neglect the rule of prudence nor will virtue herself look beautiful unless she be bedeck with the outward ornament of decency and decorum
and this precept my worthy disciple if you read with due attention you will i hope find sufficiently enforce by example in the follow page
i ask pardon for this short appearance by way of chorus on the stage
it be in reality for my own sake that while i be discover the rock on which innocence and goodness often split i may not be misunderstood to recommend the very mean to my worthy reader by which i intend to show they they will be undo
and this as i could not prevail on any of my actor to speak i myself be oblige to declare
chapter viii
a childish incident in which however be see a good-natured disposition in tom jones
the reader may remember that mr allworthy give tom jones a little horse as a kind of smart-money for the punishment which he imagine he have suffer innocently
this horse tom keep above half a year and then ride he to a neighbouring fair and sell he
at his return be question by thwackum what he have do with the money for which the horse be sell he frankly declare he would not tell he
oho say thwackum you will not
then i will have it out of your br--h that be the place to which he always apply for information on every doubtful occasion
tom be now mount on the back of a footman and everything prepare for execution when mr allworthy enter the room give the criminal a reprieve and take he with he into another apartment where be alone with tom he put the same question to he which thwackum have before ask he
tom answer he could in duty refuse he nothing but as for that tyrannical rascal he would never make he any other answer than with a cudgel with which he hop soon to be able to pay he for all his barbarity
mr allworthy very severely reprimand the lad for his indecent and disrespectful expression concern his master but much more for his avow an intention of revenge
he threaten he with the entire loss of his favour if he ever hear such another word from his mouth for he say he would never support or befriend a reprobate
by these and the like declaration he extort some compunction from tom in which that youth be not over-sincere for he really meditate some return for all the smarting favour he have receive at the hand of the pedagogue
he be however bring by mr allworthy to express a concern for his resentment against thwackum and then the good man after some wholesome admonition permit he to proceed which he do as follows indeed my dear sir i love and honour you more than all the world i know the great obligation i have to you and should detest myself if i think my heart be capable of ingratitude
could the little horse you give i speak i be sure he could tell you how fond i be of your present for i have more pleasure in feed he than in rid he
indeed sir it go to my heart to part with he nor would i have sell he upon any other account in the world than what i do
you yourself sir i be convince in my case would have do the same for none ever so sensibly feel the misfortune of other
what would you feel dear sir if you think yourself the occasion of they
indeed sir there never be any misery like theirs like whose child say allworthy what do you mean oh sir answer tom your poor gamekeeper with all his large family ever since your discard he have be perish with all the misery of cold and hunger i could not bear to see these poor wretch naked and starve and at the same time know myself to have be the occasion of all their suffering
i could not bear it sir upon my soul i could not here the tear run down his cheek and he thus proceed it be to save they from absolute destruction i part with your dear present notwithstanding all the value i have for it i sell the horse for they and they have every farthing of the money mr allworthy now stand silent for some moment and before he speak the tear start from his eye
he at length dismiss tom with a gentle rebuke advise he for the future to apply to he in case of distress rather than to use extraordinary mean of relieve they himself
this affair be afterwards the subject of much debate between thwackum and square
thwackum hold that this be fly in mr allworthy s face who have intend to punish the fellow for his disobedience
he say in some instance what the world call charity appear to he to be oppose the will of the almighty which have mark some particular person for destruction and that this be in like manner act in opposition to mr allworthy concluding as usual with a hearty recommendation of birch
square argue strongly on the other side in opposition perhaps to thwackum or in compliance with mr allworthy who seem very much to approve what jones have do
as to what he urge on this occasion as i be convince most of my reader will be much abler advocate for poor jones it would be impertinent to relate it
indeed it be not difficult to reconcile to the rule of right an action which it would have be impossible to deduce from the rule of wrong
chapter ix
contain an incident of a more heinous kind with the comment of thwackum and square
it hath be observe by some man of much great reputation for wisdom than myself that misfortune seldom come single
an instance of this may i believe be see in those gentleman who have the misfortune to have any of their roguery detect for here discovery seldom stop till the whole be come out
thus it happen to poor tom who be no soon pardon for sell the horse than he be discover to have some time before sell a fine bible which mr allworthy give he the money arise from which sale he have dispose of in the same manner
this bible master blifil have purchase though he have already such another of his own partly out of respect for the book and partly out of friendship to tom be unwilling that the bible should be sell out of the family at half-price
he therefore deposit the say half-price himself for he be a very prudent lad and so careful of his money that he have lay up almost every penny which he have receive from mr allworthy
some people have be note to be able to read in no book but their own
on the contrary from the time when master blifil be first possess of this bible he never use any other
nay he be see read in it much oftener than he have before be in his own
now as he frequently ask thwackum to explain difficult passage to he that gentleman unfortunately take notice of tom s name which be write in many part of the book
this bring on an inquiry which oblige master blifil to discover the whole matter
thwackum be resolve a crime of this kind which he call sacrilege should not go unpunished
he therefore proceed immediately to castigation and not content with that he acquaint mr allworthy at their next meeting with this monstrous crime as it appear to he inveigh against tom in the most bitter term and liken he to the buyer and seller who be drive out of the temple
square see this matter in a very different light
he say he could not perceive any high crime in sell one book than in sell another
that to sell bibles be strictly lawful by all law both divine and human and consequently there be no unfitness in it
he tell thwackum that his great concern on this occasion bring to his mind the story of a very devout woman who out of pure regard to religion steal tillotson s sermon from a lady of her acquaintance
this story cause a vast quantity of blood to rush into the parson s face which of itself be none of the pale and he be go to reply with great warmth and anger have not mrs blifil who be present at this debate interpose
that lady declare herself absolutely of mr square s side
she argue indeed very learnedly in support of his opinion and conclude with say if tom have be guilty of any fault she must confess her own son appear to be equally culpable for that she could see no difference between the buyer and the seller both of whom be alike to be drive out of the temple
mrs blifil have declare her opinion put an end to the debate
square s triumph would almost have stopt his word have he need they and thwackum who for reason before-mention durst not venture at disoblige the lady be almost choake with indignation
as to mr allworthy he say since the boy have be already punish he would not deliver his sentiment on the occasion and whether he be or be not angry with the lad i must leave to the reader s own conjecture
soon after this an action be bring against the gamekeeper by squire western the gentleman in whose manor the partridge be kill for depredation of the like kind
this be a most unfortunate circumstance for the fellow as it not only of itself threaten his ruin but actually prevent mr allworthy from restore he to his favour for as that gentleman be walk out one evening with master blifil and young jones the latter slily draw he to the habitation of black george where the family of that poor wretch namely his wife and child be find in all the misery with which cold hunger and nakedness can affect human creature for as to the money they have receive from jones former debt have consume almost the whole
such a scene as this could not fail of affect the heart of mr allworthy
he immediately give the mother a couple of guineas with which he bid her cloath her child
the poor woman burst into tear at this goodness and while she be thank he could not refrain from express her gratitude to tom who have she say long preserve both she and her from starve
we have not say she have a morsel to eat nor have these poor child have a rag to put on but what his goodness hath bestow on we for indeed besides the horse and the bible tom have sacrifice a night-gown and other thing to the use of this distressed family
on their return home tom make use of all his eloquence to display the wretchedness of these people and the penitence of black george himself and in this he succeed so well that mr allworthy say he think the man have suffer enough for what be past that he would forgive he and think of some mean of provide for he and his family
jones be so delighted with this news that though it be dark when they return home he could not help go back a mile in a shower of rain to acquaint the poor woman with the glad tiding but like other hasty divulger of news he only bring on himself the trouble of contradict it for the ill fortune of black george make use of the very opportunity of his friend s absence to overturn all again
chapter x
in which master blifil and jones appear in different light
master blifil fall very short of his companion in the amiable quality of mercy but he as greatly exceed he in one of a much high kind namely in justice in which he follow both the precept and example of thwackum and square for though they would both make frequent use of the word mercy yet it be plain that in reality square hold it to be inconsistent with the rule of right and thwackum be for do justice and leave mercy to heaven
the two gentleman do indeed somewhat differ in opinion concern the object of this sublime virtue by which thwackum would probably have destroy one half of mankind and square the other half
master blifil then though he have keep silence in the presence of jones yet when he have well consider the matter could by no mean endure the thought of suffer his uncle to confer favour on the undeserving
he therefore resolve immediately to acquaint he with the fact which we have above slightly hint to the reader
the truth of which be as follow the gamekeeper about a year after he be dismiss from mr allworthy s service and before tom s sell the horse be in want of bread either to fill his own mouth or those of his family as he pass through a field belong to mr western espy a hare sit in her form
this hare he have basely and barbarously knock on the head against the law of the land and no less against the law of sportsman
the higgler to whom the hare be sell be unfortunately take many month after with a quantity of game upon he be oblige to make his peace with the squire by become evidence against some poacher
and now black george be pitch upon by he as be a person already obnoxious to mr western and one of no good fame in the country
he be besides the good sacrifice the higgler could make as he have supply he with no game since and by this mean the witness have an opportunity of screen his well customer for the squire be charm with the power of punish black george whom a single transgression be sufficient to ruin make no further enquiry
have this fact be truly lay before mr allworthy it might probably have do the gamekeeper very little mischief
but there be no zeal blinder than that which be inspire with the love of justice against offender
master blifil have forget the distance of the time
he vary likewise in the manner of the fact and by the hasty addition of the single letter s he considerably alter the story for he say that george have wire hare
these alteration might probably have be set right have not master blifil unluckily insist on a promise of secrecy from mr allworthy before he reveal the matter to he but by that mean the poor gamekeeper be condemn without have an opportunity to defend himself for as the fact of kill the hare and of the action bring be certainly true mr allworthy have no doubt concern the rest
short-live then be the joy of these poor people for mr allworthy the next morning declare he have fresh reason without assign it for his anger and strictly forbad tom to mention george any more though as for his family he say he would endeavour to keep they from starve but as to the fellow himself he would leave he to the law which nothing could keep he from break
tom could by no mean divine what have incense mr allworthy for of master blifil he have not the least suspicion
however as his friendship be to be tired out by no disappointment he now determine to try another method of preserve the poor gamekeeper from ruin
jones be lately grow very intimate with mr western
he have so greatly recommend himself to that gentleman by leap over five-bar gate and by other act of sportsmanship that the squire have declare tom would certainly make a great man if he have but sufficient encouragement
he often wish he have himself a son with such part and one day very solemnly assert at a drinking bout that tom should hunt a pack of hound for a thousand pound of his money with any huntsman in the whole country
by such kind of talent he have so ingratiate himself with the squire that he be a most welcome guest at his table and a favourite companion in his sport everything which the squire hold most dear to wit his gun dog and horse be now as much at the command of jones as if they have be his own
he resolve therefore to make use of this favour on behalf of his friend black george whom he hop to introduce into mr western s family in the same capacity in which he have before serve mr allworthy
the reader if he consider that this fellow be already obnoxious to mr western and if he consider far the weighty business by which that gentleman s displeasure have be incur will perhaps condemn this as a foolish and desperate undertaking but if he should totally condemn young jones on that account he will greatly applaud he for strengthen himself with all imaginable interest on so arduous an occasion
for this purpose then tom apply to mr western s daughter a young lady of about seventeen year of age whom her father next after those necessary implement of sport just before mention love and esteem above all the world
now as she have some influence on the squire so tom have some little influence on she
but this be the intend heroine of this work a lady with whom we ourselves be greatly in love and with whom many of our reader will probably be in love too before we part it be by no mean proper she should make she appearance at the end of a book
book iv
contain the time of a year
chapter i
contain five page of paper
as truth distinguish our writing from those idle romance which be fill with monster the production not of nature but of distemper brain and which have be therefore recommend by an eminent critic to the sole use of the pastry-cook so on the other hand we would avoid any resemblance to that kind of history which a celebrate poet seem to think be no less calculated for the emolument of the brewer as the reading it should be always attend with a tankard of good ale-- while--history with her comrade ale soothe the sad series of her serious tale for as this be the liquor of modern historian nay perhaps their muse if we may believe the opinion of butler who attribute inspiration to ale it ought likewise to be the potation of their reader since every book ought to be read with the same spirit and in the same manner as it be writ
thus the famous author of hurlothrumbo tell a learn bishop that the reason his lordship could not taste the excellence of his piece be that he do not read it with a fiddle in his hand which instrument he himself have always have in his own when he compose it
that our work therefore might be in no danger of be liken to the labour of these historian we have take every occasion of intersperse through the whole sundry simile description and other kind of poetical embellishment
these be indeed design to supply the place of the said ale and to refresh the mind whenever those slumber which in a long work be apt to invade the reader as well as the writer shall begin to creep upon he
without interruption of this kind the good narrative of plain matter of fact must overpower every reader for nothing but the ever last watchfulness which homer have ascribe only to jove himself can be proof against a newspaper of many volume
we shall leave to the reader to determine with what judgment we have choose the several occasion for insert those ornamental part of our work
surely it will be allow that none could be more proper than the present where we be about to introduce a considerable character on the scene no less indeed than the heroine of this heroic historical prosaic poem
here therefore we have think proper to prepare the mind of the reader for her reception by fill it with every pleasing image which we can draw from the face of nature
and for this method we plead many precedent
first this be an art well know to and much practise by our tragick poet who seldom fail to prepare their audience for the reception of their principal character
thus the heroe be always introduce with a flourish of drum and trumpet in order to rouse a martial spirit in the audience and to accommodate their ear to bombast and fustian which mr locke s blind man would not have grossly err in liken to the sound of a trumpet
again when lover be come forth soft music often conduct they on the stage either to soothe the audience with the softness of the tender passion or to lull and prepare they for that gentle slumber in which they will most probably be compose by the ensue scene
and not only the poet but the master of these poet the manager of playhouse seem to be in this secret for besides the aforesaid kettle-drum c which denote the heroe s approach he be generally usher on the stage by a large troop of half a dozen scene-shifter and how necessary these be imagine to his appearance may be conclude from the follow theatrical story king pyrrhus be at dinner at an ale-house border on the theatre when he be summon to go on the stage
the heroe be unwilling to quit his shoulder of mutton and as unwilling to draw on himself the indignation of mr wilks his brother-manager for make the audience wait have bribe these his harbinger to be out of the way
while mr wilks therefore be thunder out where be the carpenter to walk on before king pyrrhus that monarch very quietly eat his mutton and the audience however impatient be oblige to entertain themselves with music in his absence
to be plain i much question whether the politician who hath generally a good nose hath not scent out somewhat of the utility of this practice
i be convinced that awful magistrate my lord-mayor contract a good deal of that reverence which attend he through the year by the several pageant which precede his pomp
nay i must confess that even i myself who be not remarkably liable to be captivate with show have yield not a little to the impression of much precede state
when i have see a man strut in a procession after other whose business be only to walk before he i have conceive a high notion of his dignity than i have feel on see he in a common situation
but there be one instance which come exactly up to my purpose
this be the custom of send on a basket-woman who be to precede the pomp at a coronation and to strew the stage with flower before the great personage begin their procession
the antient would certainly have invoke the goddess flora for this purpose and it would have be no difficulty for their priest or politician to have persuade the people of the real presence of the deity though a plain mortal have personate she and perform her office
but we have no such design of impose on our reader and therefore those who object to the heathen theology may if they please change our goddess into the above-mention basket-woman
our intention in short be to introduce our heroine with the utmost solemnity in our power with an elevation of stile and all other circumstance proper to raise the veneration of our reader indeed we would for certain cause advise those of our male reader who have any heart to read no far be we not well assure that how amiable soever the picture of our heroine will appear as it be really a copy from nature many of our fair countrywoman will be find worthy to satisfy any passion and to answer any idea of female perfection which our pencil will be able to raise
and now without any further preface we proceed to our next chapter
chapter ii
a short hint of what we can do in the sublime and a description of miss sophia western
hush be every ruder breath
may the heathen ruler of the wind confine in iron chain the boisterous limb of noisy boreas and the sharp-pointed nose of bitter-biting eurus
do thou sweet zephyrus rise from thy fragrant bed mount the western sky and lead on those delicious gale the charm of which call forth the lovely flora from her chamber perfume with pearly dew when on the 1 st of june her birth-day the bloom maid in loose attire gently trip it over the verdant mead where every flower rise to do her homage till the whole field become enamel and colour contend with sweet which shall ravish she most
so charming may she now appear
and you the feather chorister of nature whose sweetest note not even handel can excell tune your melodious throat to celebrate her appearance
from love proceed your music and to love it return
awaken therefore that gentle passion in every swain for lo
adorn with all the charm in which nature can array she bedeck with beauty youth sprightliness innocence modesty and tenderness breathe sweetness from her rosy lip and dart brightness from her sparkling eye the lovely sophia come
reader perhaps thou hast see the statue of the venus de medicis
perhaps too thou hast see the gallery of beauty at hampton court
thou may st remember each bright churchill of the galaxy and all the toast of the kit-cat
or if their reign be before thy time at least thou hast see their daughter the no less dazzling beauty of the present age whose name should we here insert we apprehend they would fill the whole volume
now if thou hast see all these be not afraid of the rude answer which lord rochester once give to a man who have see many thing
no
if thou hast see all these without know what beauty be thou hast no eye if without feel its power thou hast no heart
yet be it possible my friend that thou mayest have see all these without be able to form an exact idea of sophia for she do not exactly resemble any of they
she be most like the picture of lady ranelagh and i have hear more still to the famous dutchess of mazarine but most of all she resemble one whose image never can depart from my breast and whom if thou dost remember thou hast then my friend an adequate idea of sophia
but lest this should not have be thy fortune we will endeavour with our utmost skill to describe this paragon though we be sensible that our high ability be very inadequate to the task
sophia then the only daughter of mr western be a middle-sized woman but rather incline to tall
her shape be not only exact but extremely delicate and the nice proportion of her arm promise the true symmetry in her limb
her hair which be black be so luxuriant that it reach she middle before she cut it to comply with the modern fashion and it be now curl so gracefully in her neck that few could believe it to be her own
if envy could find any part of the face which demand less commendation than the rest it might possibly think her forehead might have be high without prejudice to she
her eyebrow be full even and arch beyond the power of art to imitate
her black eye have a lustre in they which all her softness could not extinguish
her nose be exactly regular and her mouth in which be two row of ivory exactly answer sir john suckling s description in those lines her lip be red and one be thin compar d to that be next her chin
some bee have sting it newly
her cheek be of the oval kind and in her right she have a dimple which the least smile discover
her chin have certainly its share in form the beauty of her face but it be difficult to say it be either large or small though perhaps it be rather of the former kind
her complexion have rather more of the lily than of the rose but when exercise or modesty increase her natural colour no vermilion could equal it
then one might indeed cry out with the celebrated dr donne her pure and eloquent blood spoke in her cheek and so distinctly work that one might almost say her body think
her neck be long and finely turn and here if i be not afraid of offend her delicacy i might justly say the high beauty of the famous venus de medicis be outdo
here be whiteness which no lily ivory nor alabaster could match
the fine cambric might indeed be suppose from envy to cover that bosom which be much whiter than itself it be indeed nitor splenden pario marmore purius
a gloss shin beyond the pure brightness of parian marble
such be the outside of sophia nor be this beautiful frame disgrace by an inhabitant unworthy of it
her mind be every way equal to her person nay the latter borrow some charm from the former for when she smile the sweetness of her temper diffuse that glory over her countenance which no regularity of feature can give
but as there be no perfection of the mind which do not discover themselves in that perfect intimacy to which we intend to introduce our reader with this charming young creature so it be needless to mention they here nay it be a kind of tacit affront to our reader s understanding and may also rob he of that pleasure which he will receive in form his own judgment of her character
it may however be proper to say that whatever mental accomplishment she have derive from nature they be somewhat improve and cultivate by art for she have be educate under the care of an aunt who be a lady of great discretion and be thoroughly acquaint with the world have live in her youth about the court whence she have retire some year since into the country
by her conversation and instruction sophia be perfectly well breed though perhaps she want a little of that ease in her behaviour which be to be acquire only by habit and live within what be call the polite circle
but this to say the truth be often too dearly purchase and though it hath charm so inexpressible that the french perhaps among other quality mean to express this when they declare they know not what it be yet its absence be well compensate by innocence nor can good sense and a natural gentility ever stand in need of it
chapter iii
wherein the history go back to commemorate a trifling incident that happen some year since but which trifle as it be have some future consequence
the amiable sophia be now in her eighteenth year when she be introduce into this history
her father as hath be say be fonder of she than of any other human creature
to she therefore tom jones apply in order to engage her interest on the behalf of his friend the gamekeeper
but before we proceed to this business a short recapitulation of some previous matter may be necessary
though the different temper of mr allworthy and of mr western do not admit of a very intimate correspondence yet they live upon what be call a decent footing together by which mean the young people of both family have be acquaint from their infancy and as they be all near of the same age have be frequent playmate together
the gaiety of tom s temper suit well with sophia than the grave and sober disposition of master blifil
and the preference which she give the former of these would often appear so plainly that a lad of a more passionate turn than master blifil be might have show some displeasure at it
as he do not however outwardly express any such disgust it would be an ill office in we to pay a visit to the inmost recess of his mind as some scandalous people search into the most secret affair of their friend and often pry into their closet and cupboard only to discover their poverty and meanness to the world
however as person who suspect they have give other cause of offence be apt to conclude they be offend so sophia impute an action of master blifil to his anger which the superior sagacity of thwackum and square discern to have arise from a much well principle
tom jones when very young have present sophia with a little bird which he have take from the nest have nurse up and teach to sing
of this bird sophia then about thirteen year old be so extremely fond that her chief business be to feed and tend it and her chief pleasure to play with it
by these mean little tommy for so the bird be call be become so tame that it would feed out of the hand of its mistress would perch upon the finger and lie content in her bosom where it seem almost sensible of its own happiness though she always keep a small string about its leg nor would ever trust it with the liberty of fly away
one day when mr allworthy and his whole family din at mr western s master blifil be in the garden with little sophia and observe the extreme fondness that she show for her little bird desire she to trust it for a moment in his hand
sophia presently comply with the young gentleman s request and after some previous caution deliver he her bird of which he be no soon in possession than he slipt the string from its leg and toss it into the air
the foolish animal no soon perceive itself at liberty than forget all the favour it have receive from sophia it fly directly from she and perch on a bough at some distance
sophia see her bird go scream out so loud that tom jones who be at a little distance immediately run to her assistance
he be no soon informed of what have happen than he curse blifil for a pitiful malicious rascal and then immediately strip off his coat he apply himself to climb the tree to which the bird escape
tom have almost recover his little namesake when the branch on which it be perch and that hang over a canal break and the poor lad plump over head and ear into the water
sophia s concern now change its object
and as she apprehend the boy s life be in danger she scream ten time loud than before and indeed master blifil himself now second she with all the vociferation in his power
the company who be sit in a room next the garden be instantly alarm and come all forth but just as they reach the canal tom for the water be luckily pretty shallow in that part arrive safely on shore
thwackum fall violently on poor tom who stand drop and shiver before he when mr allworthy desire he to have patience and turn to master blifil say pray child what be the reason of all this disturbance master blifil answer indeed uncle i be very sorry for what i have do i have be unhappily the occasion of it all
i have miss sophia s bird in my hand and think the poor creature languish for liberty i own i could not forbear give it what it desire for i always think there be something very cruel in confine anything
it seem to be against the law of nature by which everything hath a right to liberty nay it be even unchristian for it be not do what we would be do by but if i have imagine miss sophia would have be so much concerned at it i be sure i never would have do it nay if i have know what would have happen to the bird itself for when master jones who climb up that tree after it fall into the water the bird take a second flight and presently a nasty hawk carry it away poor sophia who now first hear of her little tommy s fate for her concern for jones have prevent her perceive it when it happen shed a shower of tear
these mr allworthy endeavour to assuage promise she a much fine bird but she declare she would never have another
her father chide she for cry so for a foolish bird but could not help tell young blifil if he be a son of his his backside should be well flead
sophia now return to her chamber the two young gentleman be send home and the rest of the company return to their bottle where a conversation ensue on the subject of the bird so curious that we think it deserve a chapter by itself
chapter iv
contain such very deep and grave matter that some reader perhaps may not relish it
square have no soon light his pipe than address himself to allworthy he thus begin sir i cannot help congratulate you on your nephew who at an age when few lad have any idea but of sensible object be arrive at a capacity of distinguish right from wrong
to confine anything seem to i against the law of nature by which everything hath a right to liberty
these be his word and the impression they have make on i be never to be eradicate
can any man have a high notion of the rule of right and the eternal fitness of thing
i cannot help promise myself from such a dawn that the meridian of this youth will be equal to that of either the elder or the young brutus here thwackum hastily interrupt and spill some of his wine and swallow the rest with great eagerness answer from another expression he make use of i hope he will resemble much well man
the law of nature be a jargon of word which mean nothing
i know not of any such law nor of any right which can be derive from it
to do as we would be do by be indeed a christian motive as the boy well express himself and i be glad to find my instruction have bear such good fruit if vanity be a thing fit say square i might indulge some on the same occasion for whence only he can have learn his notion of right or wrong i think be pretty apparent
if there be no law of nature there be no right nor wrong how say the parson do you then banish revelation
be i talk with a deist or an atheist drink about say western
pox of your law of nature
i donot know what you mean either of you by right and wrong
to take away my girl s bird be wrong in my opinion and my neighbour allworthy may do as he please but to encourage boy in such practice be to breed they up to the gallow allworthy answer that he be sorry for what his nephew have do but could not consent to punish he as he act rather from a generous than unworthy motive he say if the boy have steal the bird none would have be more ready to vote for a severe chastisement than himself but it be plain that be not his design and indeed it be as apparent to he that he could have no other view but what he have himself avow
for as to that malicious purpose which sophia suspect it never once enter into the head of mr allworthy he at length conclude with again blame the action as inconsiderate and which he say be pardonable only in a child
square have deliver his opinion so openly that if he be now silent he must submit to have his judgment censure
he say therefore with some warmth that mr allworthy have too much respect to the dirty consideration of property
