that in pass our judgment on great and mighty action all private regard should be lay aside for by adhere to those narrow rule the young brutus have be condemn of ingratitude and the elder of parricide and if they have be hang too for those crime cry thwackum they would have have no more than their desert
a couple of heathenish villain
heaven be praise we have no brutus now-a-day
i wish mr square you would desist from fill the mind of my pupil with such antichristian stuff for the consequence must be while they be under my care its being well scourge out of they again
there be your disciple tom almost spoil already
i overhear he the other day dispute with master blifil that there be no merit in faith without work
i know that be one of your tenet and i suppose he have it from you donot accuse i of spoil he say square
who teach he to laugh at whatever be virtuous and decent and fit and right in the nature of thing
he be your own scholar and i disclaim he
no no master blifil be my boy
young as he be that lad s notion of moral rectitude i defy you ever to eradicate thwackum put on a contemptuous sneer at this and reply ay ay i will venture he with you
he be too well ground for all your philosophical cannot to hurt
no no i have take care to instil such principle into him-- and i have instill principle into he too cry square
what but the sublime idea of virtue could inspire a human mind with the generous thought of give liberty
and i repeat to you again if it be a fit thing to be proud i might claim the honour of have infuse that idea and if pride be not forbid say thwackum i might boast of have teach he that duty which he himself assign as his motive so between you both say the squire the young gentleman hath be teach to rob my daughter of her bird
i find i must take care of my partridge-mew
i shall have some virtuous religious man or other set all my partridge at liberty then slap a gentleman of the law who be present on the back he cry out what say you to this mr counsellor
be not this against law the lawyer with great gravity deliver himself as follows if the case be put of a partridge there can be no doubt but an action would lie for though this be ferae naturae yet be reclaim property vest but be the case of a singing bird though reclaim as it be a thing of base nature it must be consider as nullius in bonis
in this case therefore i conceive the plaintiff must be non-suited and i should disadvise the bringing any such action well say the squire if it be nullus bonus let we drink about and talk a little of the state of the nation or some such discourse that we all understand for i be sure i donot understand a word of this
it may be learn and sense for aught i know but you shall never persuade i into it
pox
you have neither of you mention a word of that poor lad who deserve to be commend to venture break his neck to oblige my girl be a generous-spirited action i have learn enough to see that
d--n i herebe tom s health
i shall love the boy for it the long day i have to live thus be the debate interrupt but it would probably have be soon resume have not mr allworthy presently call for his coach and carry off the two combatant
such be the conclusion of this adventure of the bird and of the dialogue occasion by it which we could not help recount to our reader though it happen some year before that stage or period of time at which our history be now arrive
chapter v
contain matter accommodate to every taste
parva leve capiunt animos--small thing affect light mind be the sentiment of a great master of the passion of love
and certain it be that from this day sophia begin to have some little kindness for tom jones and no little aversion for his companion
many accident from time to time improve both these passion in her breast which without our recounting the reader may well conclude from what we have before hint of the different temper of these lad and how much the one suit with her own inclination more than the other
to say the truth sophia when very young discern that tom though an idle thoughtless rattle rascal be nobody s enemy but his own and that master blifil though a prudent discreet sober young gentleman be at the same time strongly attach to the interest only of one single person and who that single person be the reader will be able to divine without any assistance of our
these two character be not always receive in the world with the different regard which seem severally due to either and which one would imagine mankind from self-interest should show towards they
but perhaps there may be a political reason for it in find one of a truly benevolent disposition man may very reasonably suppose they have find a treasure and be desirous of keep it like all other good thing to themselves
hence they may imagine that to trumpet forth the praise of such a person would in the vulgar phrase be cry roast-meat and call in partaker of what they intend to apply solely to their own use
if this reason do not satisfy the reader i know no other mean of account for the little respect which i have commonly see pay to a character which really do great honour to human nature and be productive of the high good to society
but it be otherwise with sophia
she honour tom jones and scorn master blifil almost as soon as she know the meaning of those two word
sophia have be absent upwards of three year with her aunt during all which time she have seldom see either of these young gentleman
she din however once together with her aunt at mr allworthy s
this be a few day after the adventure of the partridge before commemorate
sophia hear the whole story at table where she say nothing nor indeed could her aunt get many word from she as she return home but her maid when undress she happen to say well miss i suppose you have see young master blifil to-day she answer with much passion i hate the name of master blifil as i do whatever be base and treacherous and i wonder mr allworthy would suffer that old barbarous schoolmaster to punish a poor boy so cruelly for what be only the effect of his good-nature she then recount the story to she maid and conclude with say donot you think he be a boy of noble spirit this young lady be now return to her father who give she the command of his house and place she at the upper end of his table where tom who for his great love of hunting be become a great favourite of the squire often din
young man of open generous disposition be naturally inclined to gallantry which if they have good understanding as be in reality tom s case exert itself in an oblige complacent behaviour to all woman in general
this greatly distinguish tom from the boisterous brutality of mere country squire on the one hand and from the solemn and somewhat sullen deportment of master blifil on the other and he begin now at twenty to have the name of a pretty fellow among all the woman in the neighbourhood
tom behave to sophia with no particularity unless perhaps by show she a high respect than he pay to any other
this distinction her beauty fortune sense and amiable carriage seem to demand but as to design upon her person he have none for which we shall at present suffer the reader to condemn he of stupidity but perhaps we shall be able indifferently well to account for it hereafter
sophia with the high degree of innocence and modesty have a remarkable sprightliness in her temper
this be so greatly increase whenever she be in company with tom that have he not be very young and thoughtless he must have observe it or have not mr western s thought be generally either in the field the stable or the dog-kennel it might have perhaps create some jealousy in he but so far be the good gentleman from entertain any such suspicion that he give tom every opportunity with his daughter which any lover could have wish and this tom innocently improve to well advantage by follow only the dictate of his natural gallantry and good-nature than he might perhaps have do have he have the deep design on the young lady
but indeed it can occasion little wonder that this matter escape the observation of other since poor sophia herself never remark it and her heart be irretrievably lose before she suspect it be in danger
matter be in this situation when tom one afternoon find sophia alone begin after a short apology with a very serious face to acquaint she that he have a favour to ask of she which he hop she goodness would comply with
though neither the young man s behaviour nor indeed his manner of open this business be such as could give she any just cause of suspecting he intend to make love to she yet whether nature whisper something into her ear or from what cause it arise i will not determine certain it be some idea of that kind must have intrude itself for her colour forsook her cheek her limb tremble and her tongue would have falter have tom stop for an answer but he soon relieve she from her perplexity by proceed to inform she of his request which be to solicit her interest on behalf of the gamekeeper whose own ruin and that of a large family must be he say the consequence of mr western s pursue his action against he
sophia presently recover her confusion and with a smile full of sweetness say be this the mighty favour you ask with so much gravity
i will do it with all my heart
i really pity the poor fellow and no long ago than yesterday send a small matter to his wife this small matter be one of her gown some linen and ten shilling in money of which tom have hear and it have in reality put this solicitation into his head
our youth now embolden with his success resolve to push the matter far and venture even to beg her recommendation of he to her father s service protest that he think he one of the honest fellow in the country and extremely well qualified for the place of a gamekeeper which luckily then happen to be vacant
sophia answer well i will undertake this too but i cannot promise you as much success as in the former part which i assure you i will not quit my father without obtain
however i will do what i can for the poor fellow for i sincerely look upon he and his family as object of great compassion
and now mr jones i must ask you a favour a favour madam cry tom if you know the pleasure you have give i in the hope of receive a command from you you would think by mention it you do confer the great favour on i for by this dear hand i would sacrifice my life to oblige you he then snatch her hand and eagerly kiss it which be the first time his lip have ever touch she
the blood which before have forsake her cheek now make her sufficient amend by rush all over her face and neck with such violence that they become all of a scarlet colour
she now first feel a sensation to which she have be before a stranger and which when she have leisure to reflect on it begin to acquaint she with some secret which the reader if he doth not already guess they will know in due time
sophia as soon as she could speak which be not instantly inform he that the favour she have to desire of he be not to lead she father through so many danger in hunting for that from what she have hear she be terribly frighten every time they go out together and expect some day or other to see her father bring home with break limb
she therefore beg he for her sake to be more cautious and as he well know mr western would follow he not to ride so madly nor to take those dangerous leap for the future
tom promise faithfully to obey her command and after thank she for her kind compliance with his request take his leave and depart highly charm with his success
poor sophia be charm too but in a very different way
her sensation however the reader s heart if he or she have any will well represent than i can if i have as many mouth as ever poet wish for to eat i suppose those many dainty with which he be so plentifully provide
it be mr western s custom every afternoon as soon as he be drunk to hear his daughter play on the harpsichord for he be a great lover of music and perhaps have he live in town might have pass for a connoisseur for he always except against the fine composition of mr handel
he never relish any music but what be light and airy and indeed his most favourite tune be old sir simon the king st george he be for england bobbing joan and some other
his daughter though she be a perfect mistress of music and would never willingly have play any but handel s be so devoted to her father s pleasure that she learn all those tune to oblige he
however she would now and then endeavour to lead he into her own taste and when he require the repetition of his ballad would answer with a nay dear sir and would often beg he to suffer she to play something else
this evening however when the gentleman be retire from his bottle she play all his favourite three time over without any solicitation
this so please the good squire that he start from his couch give his daughter a kiss and swear her hand be greatly improve
she take this opportunity to execute her promise to tom in which she succeed so well that the squire declare if she would give he t other bout of old sir simon he would give the gamekeeper his deputation the next morning
sir simon be play again and again till the charm of the music soothe mr western to sleep
in the morning sophia do not fail to remind he of his engagement and his attorney be immediately send for order to stop any further proceeding in the action and to make out the deputation
tom s success in this affair soon begin to ring over the country and various be the censure pass upon it some greatly applaud it as an act of good nature other sneer and say no wonder that one idle fellow should love another young blifil be greatly enrage at it
he have long hat black george in the same proportion as jones delight in he not from any offence which he have ever receive but from his great love to religion and virtue for black george have the reputation of a loose kind of a fellow
blifil therefore represent this as fly in mr allworthy s face and declare with great concern that it be impossible to find any other motive for do good to such a wretch
thwackum and square likewise sing to the same tune
they be now especially the latter become greatly jealous of young jones with the widow for he now approach the age of twenty be really a fine young fellow and that lady by her encouragement to he seem daily more and more to think he so
allworthy be not however move with their malice
he declare himself very well satisfied with what jones have do
he say the perseverance and integrity of his friendship be highly commendable and he wish he could see more frequent instance of that virtue
but fortune who seldom greatly relish such spark as my friend tom perhaps because they do not pay more ardent address to she give now a very different turn to all his action and show they to mr allworthy in a light far less agreeable than that gentleman s goodness have hitherto see they in
chapter vi
an apology for the insensibility of mr jones to all the charm of the lovely sophia in which possibly we may in a considerable degree lower his character in the estimation of those man of wit and gallantry who approve the hero in most of our modern comedy
there be two sort of people who i be afraid have already conceive some contempt for my heroe on account of his behaviour to sophia
the former of these will blame his prudence in neglect an opportunity to possess himself of mr western s fortune and the latter will no less despise he for his backwardness to so fine a girl who seem ready to fly into his arm if he would open they to receive she
now though i shall not perhaps be able absolutely to acquit he of either of these charge for want of prudence admit of no excuse and what i shall produce against the latter charge will i apprehend be scarce satisfactory yet as evidence may sometimes be offer in mitigation i shall set forth the plain matter of fact and leave the whole to the reader s determination
mr jones have somewhat about he which though i think writer be not thoroughly agree in its name doth certainly inhabit some human breast whose use be not so properly to distinguish right from wrong as to prompt and incite they to the former and to restrain and withhold they from the latter
this somewhat may be indeed resemble to the famous trunk-maker in the playhouse for whenever the person who be possess of it doth what be right no ravished or friendly spectator be so eager or so loud in his applause on the contrary when he doth wrong no critic be so apt to hiss and explode he
to give a high idea of the principle i mean as well as one more familiar to the present age it may be consider as sit on its throne in the mind like the lord high chancellor of this kingdom in his court where it preside govern direct judge acquit and condemn accord to merit and justice with a knowledge which nothing escape a penetration which nothing can deceive and an integrity which nothing can corrupt
this active principle may perhaps be say to constitute the most essential barrier between we and our neighbour the brute for if there be some in the human shape who be not under any such dominion i choose rather to consider they as deserter from we to our neighbour among whom they will have the fate of deserter and not be place in the first rank
our heroe whether he derive it from thwackum or square i will not determine be very strongly under the guidance of this principle for though he do not always act rightly yet he never do otherwise without feel and suffer for it
it be this which teach he that to repay the civility and little friendship of hospitality by rob the house where you have receive they be to be the basest and mean of thief
he do not think the baseness of this offence lessen by the height of the injury commit on the contrary if to steal another s plate deserve death and infamy it seem to he difficult to assign a punishment adequate to the rob a man of his whole fortune and of his child into the bargain
this principle therefore prevent he from any thought of make his fortune by such mean for this as i have say be an active principle and doth not content itself with knowledge or belief only
have he be greatly enamoured of sophia he possibly might have think otherwise but give i leave to say there be great difference between run away with a man s daughter from the motive of love and do the same thing from the motive of theft
now though this young gentleman be not insensible of the charm of sophia though he greatly like her beauty and esteem all her other qualification she have make however no deep impression on his heart for which as it render he liable to the charge of stupidity or at least of want of taste we shall now proceed to account
the truth then be his heart be in the possession of another woman
here i question not but the reader will be surprize at our long taciturnity as to this matter and quite at a loss to divine who this woman be since we have hitherto not dropt a hint of any one likely to be a rival to sophia for as to mrs blifil though we have be oblige to mention some suspicion of her affection for tom we have not hitherto give the least latitude for imagine that he have any for she and indeed i be sorry to say it but the youth of both sex be too apt to be deficient in their gratitude for that regard with which person more advanced in year be sometimes so kind to honour they
that the reader may be no long in suspense he will be pleased to remember that we have often mention the family of george seagrim commonly call black george the gamekeeper which consist at present of a wife and five child
the second of these child be a daughter whose name be molly and who be esteem one of the handsome girl in the whole country
congreve well say there be in true beauty something which vulgar soul cannot admire so can no dirt or rag hide this something from those soul which be not of the vulgar stamp
the beauty of this girl make however no impression on tom till she grow towards the age of sixteen when tom who be near three year old begin first to cast the eye of affection upon she
and this affection he have fix on the girl long before he could bring himself to attempt the possession of her person for though his constitution urge he greatly to this his principle no less forcibly restrain he
to debauch a young woman however low her condition be appear to he a very heinous crime and the good-will he bore the father with the compassion he have for his family very strongly corroborate all such sober reflection so that he once resolve to get the well of his inclination and he actually abstain three whole month without ever go to seagrim s house or see his daughter
now though molly be as we have say generally think a very fine girl and in reality she be so yet her beauty be not of the most amiable kind
it have indeed very little of feminine in it and would have become a man at least as well as a woman for to say the truth youth and florid health have a very considerable share in the composition
nor be her mind more effeminate than her person
as this be tall and robust so be that bold and forward
so little have she of modesty that jones have more regard for her virtue than she herself
and as most probably she like tom as well as he like she so when she perceive his backwardness she herself grow proportionably forward and when she see he have entirely desert the house she find mean of throw herself in his way and behave in such a manner that the youth must have have very much or very little of the heroe if her endeavour have prove unsuccessful
in a word she soon triumph over all the virtuous resolution of jones for though she behave at last with all decent reluctance yet i rather chuse to attribute the triumph to she since in fact it be her design which succeed
in the conduct of this matter i say molly so well play her part that jones attribute the conquest entirely to himself and consider the young woman as one who have yield to the violent attack of his passion
he likewise impute she yield to the ungovernable force of her love towards he and this the reader will allow to have be a very natural and probable supposition as we have more than once mention the uncommon comeliness of his person and indeed he be one of the handsome young fellow in the world
as there be some mind whose affection like master blifil s be solely place on one single person whose interest and indulgence alone they consider on every occasion regard the good and ill of all other as merely indifferent any far than as they contribute to the pleasure or advantage of that person so there be a different temper of mind which borrow a degree of virtue even from self-love
such can never receive any kind of satisfaction from another without love the creature to whom that satisfaction be owing and without make its well-being in some sort necessary to their own ease
of this latter specie be our heroe
he consider this poor girl as one whose happiness or misery he have cause to be dependent on himself
her beauty be still the object of desire though great beauty or a fresher object might have be more so but the little abatement which fruition have occasion to this be highly overbalance by the consideration of the affection which she visibly bear he and of the situation into which he have bring she
the former of these create gratitude the latter compassion and both together with his desire for her person raise in he a passion which might without any great violence to the word be call love though perhaps it be at first not very judiciously place
this then be the true reason of that insensibility which he have show to the charm of sophia and that behaviour in she which might have be reasonably enough interpret as an encouragement to his address for as he could not think of abandon his molly poor and destitute as she be so no more could he entertain a notion of betray such a creature as sophia
and surely have he give the least encouragement to any passion for that young lady he must have be absolutely guilty of one or other of those crime either of which would in my opinion have very justly subject he to that fate which at his first introduction into this history i mention to have be generally predict as his certain destiny
chapter vii
be the short chapter in this book
her mother first perceive the alteration in the shape of molly and in order to hide it from her neighbour she foolishly clothe she in that sack which sophia have send she though indeed that young lady have little apprehension that the poor woman would have be weak enough to let any of her daughter wear it in that form
molly be charm with the first opportunity she ever have of show her beauty to advantage for though she could very well bear to contemplate herself in the glass even when dress in rag and though she have in that dress conquer the heart of jones and perhaps of some other yet she think the addition of finery would much improve her charm and extend her conquest
molly therefore have dress herself out in this sack with a new laced cap and some other ornament which tom have give she repair to church with her fan in her hand the very next sunday
the great be deceive if they imagine they have appropriate ambition and vanity to themselves
these noble quality flourish as notably in a country church and churchyard as in the drawing-room or in the closet
scheme have indeed be lay in the vestry which would hardly disgrace the conclave
here be a ministry and here be an opposition
here be plot and circumvention party and faction equal to those which be to be find in court
nor be the woman here less practise in the high feminine art than their fair superior in quality and fortune
here be prude and coquette
here be dress and ogle falsehood envy malice scandal in short everything which be common to the most splendid assembly or politest circle
let those of high life therefore no long despise the ignorance of their inferior nor the vulgar any long rail at the vice of their better
molly have seat herself some time before she be know by her neighbour
and then a whisper run through the whole congregation who be she but when she be discover such sneer gigling tittering and laugh ensue among the woman that mr allworthy be oblige to exert his authority to preserve any decency among they
chapter viii
a battle sing by the muse in the homerican style and which none but the classical reader can taste
mr western have an estate in this parish and as his house stand at little great distance from this church than from his own he very often come to divine service here and both he and the charming sophia happen to be present at this time
sophia be much pleased with the beauty of the girl whom she pity for her simplicity in have dress herself in that manner as she see the envy which it have occasion among her equal
she no soon come home than she send for the gamekeeper and order he to bring his daughter to she say she would provide for she in the family and might possibly place the girl about her own person when her own maid who be now go away have leave she
poor seagrim be thunderstruck at this for he be no stranger to the fault in the shape of his daughter
he answer in a stammering voice that he be afraid molly would be too awkward to wait on her ladyship as she have never be at service no matter for that say sophia she will soon improve
i be pleased with the girl and be resolve to try she black george now repair to his wife on whose prudent counsel he depend to extricate he out of this dilemma but when he come thither he find his house in some confusion
so great envy have this sack occasion that when mr allworthy and the other gentry be go from church the rage which have hitherto be confine burst into an uproar and have vent itself at first in opprobrious word laugh hiss and gesture betook itself at last to certain missile weapon which though from their plastic nature they threaten neither the loss of life or of limb be however sufficiently dreadful to a well-dress lady
molly have too much spirit to bear this treatment tamely
have therefore--but hold as we be diffident of our own ability let we here invite a superior power to our assistance
ye muses then whoever ye be who love to sing battle and principally thou who whilom didst recount the slaughter in those field where hudibras and trulla fight if thou wert not starve with thy friend butler assist i on this great occasion
all thing be not in the power of all
as a vast herd of cow in a rich farmer s yard if while they be milk they hear their calf at a distance lament the robbery which be then commit roar and bellow so roar forth the somersetshire mob an hallaloo make up of almost as many squall scream and other different sound as there be person or indeed passion among they some be inspire by rage other alarm by fear and other have nothing in their head but the love of fun but chiefly envy the sister of satan and his constant companion rush among the crowd and blow up the fury of the woman who no soon come up to molly than they pelt she with dirt and rubbish
molly have endeavour in vain to make a handsome retreat face about and lay hold of ragged bess who advance in the front of the enemy she at one blow fell she to the ground
the whole army of the enemy though near a hundred in number see the fate of their general give back many pace and retire behind a new-dug grave for the churchyard be the field of battle where there be to be a funeral that very evening
molly pursue her victory and catch up a skull which lie on the side of the grave discharge it with such fury that have hit a taylor on the head the two skull send equally forth a hollow sound at their meeting and the taylor take presently measure of his length on the ground where the skull lay side by side and it be doubtful which be the more valuable of the two
molly then take a thigh-bone in her hand fall in among the flying rank and deal her blow with great liberality on either side overthrow the carcass of many a mighty heroe and heroine
recount o muse the name of those who fall on this fatal day
first jemmy tweedle feel on his hinder head the direful bone
he the pleasant bank of sweetly-wind stour have nourish where he first learn the vocal art with which wander up and down at wakes and fair he cheer the rural nymph and swain when upon the green they interweave the sprightly dance while he himself stand fiddle and jump to his own music
how little now avail his fiddle
he thump the verdant floor with his carcass
next old echepole the sowgelder receive a blow in his forehead from our amazonian heroine and immediately fall to the ground
he be a swing fat fellow and fall with almost as much noise as a house
his tobacco-box drop at the same time from his pocket which molly take up as lawful spoil
then kate of the mill tumble unfortunately over a tombstone which catch hold of her ungartered stocking invert the order of nature and give her heel the superiority to her head
betty pippin with young roger her lover fall both to the ground where oh perverse fate
she salute the earth and he the sky
tom freckle the smith s son be the next victim to her rage
he be an ingenious workman and make excellent patten nay the very patten with which he be knock down be his own workmanship
have he be at that time sing psalm in the church he would have avoid a broken head
miss crow the daughter of a farmer john giddish himself a farmer nan slouch esther codling will spray tom bennet the three misses potter whose father keep the sign of the red lion betty chambermaid jack ostler and many other of inferior note lie roll among the graf
not that the strenuous arm of molly reach all these for many of they in their flight overthrew each other
but now fortune fear she have act out of character and have incline too long to the same side especially as it be the right side hastily turn about for now goody brown--whom zekiel brown caress in his arm nor he alone but half the parish besides so famous be she in the field of venus nor indeed less in those of mars
the trophy of both these her husband always bear about on his head and face for if ever human head do by its horn display the amorous glory of a wife zekiel s do nor do his well-scratch face less denote her talent or rather talon of a different kind
no long bear this amazon the shameful flight of her party
she stopt short and call aloud to all who flee speak as follow ye somersetshire man or rather ye somersetshire woman be ye not ashamed thus to fly from a single woman
but if no other will oppose she i myself and joan top here will have the honour of the victory have thus say she fly at molly seagrim and easily wrench the thigh-bone from her hand at the same time claw off her cap from her head
then lay hold of the hair of molly with her left hand she attack she so furiously in the face with the right that the blood soon begin to trickle from her nose
molly be not idle this while
she soon remove the clout from the head of goody brown and then fasten on her hair with one hand with the other she cause another bloody stream to issue forth from the nostril of the enemy
when each of the combatant have bear off sufficient spoil of hair from the head of her antagonist the next rage be against the garment
in this attack they exert so much violence that in a very few minute they be both naked to the middle
it be lucky for the woman that the seat of fistycuff war be not the same with they as among man but though they may seem a little to deviate from their sex when they go forth to battle yet i have observe they never so far forget as to assail the bosom of each other where a few blow would be fatal to most of they
this i know some derive from their being of a more bloody inclination than the male
on which account they apply to the nose as to the part whence blood may most easily be draw but this seem a far-fetched as well as ill-natured supposition
goody brown have great advantage of molly in this particular for the former have indeed no breast her bosom if it may be so call as well in colour as in many other property exactly resemble an antient piece of parchment upon which any one might have drum a considerable while without do she any great damage
molly beside her present unhappy condition be differently form in those part and might perhaps have tempt the envy of brown to give she a fatal blow have not the lucky arrival of tom jones at this instant put an immediate end to the bloody scene
this accident be luckily owe to mr square for he master blifil and jones have mount their horse after church to take the air and have ride about a quarter of a mile when square change his mind not idly but for a reason which we shall unfold as soon as we have leisure desire the young gentleman to ride with he another way than they have at first purpose
this motion be comply with bring they of necessity back again to the churchyard
master blifil who ride first see such a mob assemble and two woman in the posture in which we leave the combatant stopt his horse to enquire what be the matter
a country fellow scratch his head answer he i donot know measter un t i an t please your honour here hath be a vight i think between goody brown and moll seagrim who who cry tom but without wait for an answer have discover the feature of his molly through all the discomposure in which they now be he hastily alight turn his horse loose and leap over the wall run to she
she now first burst into tear tell he how barbarously she have be treat
upon which forget the sex of goody brown or perhaps not know it in his rage--for in reality she have no feminine appearance but a petticoat which he might not observe--he give she a lash or two with his horsewhip and then fly at the mob who be all accuse by moll he deal his blow so profusely on all side that unless i would again invoke the muse which the good-natured reader may think a little too hard upon she as she hath so lately be violently sweat it would be impossible for i to recount the horse-whipping of that day
have scour the whole coast of the enemy as well as any of homer s hero ever do or as don quixote or any knight-errant in the world could have do he return to molly whom he find in a condition which must give both i and my reader pain be it to be describe here
tom rave like a madman beat his breast tear his hair stamp on the ground and vow the utmost vengeance on all who have be concern
he then pull off his coat and button it round she put his hat upon her head wipe the blood from her face as well as he could with his handkerchief and call out to the servant to ride as fast as possible for a side-saddle or a pillion that he might carry her safe home
master blifil object to the send away the servant as they have only one with they but as square second the order of jones he be oblige to comply
the servant return in a very short time with the pillion and molly have collect her rag as well as she could be place behind he
in which manner she be carry home square blifil and jones attend
here jones have receive his coat give she a sly kiss and whisper she that he would return in the evening quit his molly and ride on after his companion

contain matter of no very peaceable colour

but you would have your own headstrong way yes you would you villain black george be in the main a peaceable kind of fellow and nothing choleric nor rash yet do he bear about he something of what the antient call the irascible and which his wife if she have be endow with much wisdom would have fear
he have long experience that when the storm grow very high argument be but wind which serve rather to increase than to abate it
he be therefore seldom unprovide with a small switch a remedy of wonderful force as he have often essay and which the word villain serve as a hint for his applying
no soon therefore have this symptom appear than he have immediate recourse to the say remedy which though as it be usual in all very efficacious medicine it at first seem to heighten and inflame the disease soon produce a total calm and restore the patient to perfect ease and tranquillity
this be however a kind of horse-medicine which require a very robust constitution to digest and be therefore proper only for the vulgar unless in one single instance viz where superiority of birth break out in which case we should not think it very improperly apply by any husband whatever if the application be not in itself so base that like certain application of the physical kind which need not be mention it so much degrade and contaminate the hand employ in it that no gentleman should endure the thought of anything so low and detestable
the whole family be soon reduce to a state of perfect quiet for the virtue of this medicine like that of electricity be often communicate through one person to many other who be not touch by the instrument
to say the truth as they both operate by friction it may be doubt whether there be not something analogous between they of which mr freke would do well to enquire before he publish the next edition of his book
a council be now call in which after many debate molly still persist that she would not go to service it be at length resolve that goody seagrim herself should wait on miss western and endeavour to procure the place for her eld daughter who declare great readiness to accept it but fortune who seem to have be an enemy of this little family afterwards put a stop to her promotion

chapter xi
the narrow escape of molly seagrim with some observation for which we have be force to dive pretty deep into nature
tom jones have ride one of mr western s horse that morning in the chase so that have no horse of his own in the squire s stable he be oblige to go home on foot this he do so expeditiously that he run upwards of three mile within the half-hour
just as he arrive at mr allworthy s outward gate he meet the constable and company with molly in their possession whom they be conduct to that house where the inferior sort of people may learn one good lesson viz respect and deference to their superior since it must show they the wide distinction fortune intend between those person who be to be correct for their fault and those who be not which lesson if they do not learn i be afraid they very rarely learn any other good lesson or improve their moral at the house of correction
a lawyer may perhaps think mr allworthy exceed his authority a little in this instance
and to say the truth i question as here be no regular information before he whether his conduct be strictly regular
however as his intention be truly upright he ought to be excuse in foro conscientiae since so many arbitrary act be daily commit by magistrate who have not this excuse to plead for themselves
tom be no soon inform by the constable whither they be proceed indeed he pretty well guess it of himself than he catch molly in his arm and embrace she tenderly before they all swear he would murder the first man who offer to lay hold of she
he bid she dry her eye and be comfort for wherever she go he would accompany she
then turn to the constable who stand tremble with his hat off he desire he in a very mild voice to return with he for a moment only to his father for so he now call allworthy for he durst he say be assure that when he have alledge what he have to say in her favour the girl would be discharge
the constable who i make no doubt would have surrender his prisoner have tom demand she very readily consent to this request
so back they all go into mr allworthy s hall where tom desire they to stay till his return and then go himself in pursuit of the good man
as soon as he be find tom throw himself at his foot and have beg a patient hearing confess himself to be the father of the child of which molly be then big
he entreat he to have compassion on the poor girl and to consider if there be any guilt in the case it lie principally at his door
if there be any guilt in the case answer allworthy warmly be you then so profligate and abandon a libertine to doubt whether the break the law of god and man the corrupting and ruin a poor girl be guilt
i own indeed it doth lie principally upon you and so heavy it be that you ought to expect it should crush you whatever may be my fate say tom let i succeed in my intercession for the poor girl
i confess i have corrupt she
but whether she shall be ruin depend on you
for heaven s sake sir revoke your warrant and do not send she to a place which must unavoidably prove her destruction allworthy bid he immediately call a servant
tom answer there be no occasion for he have luckily meet they at the gate and rely upon his goodness have bring they all back into his hall where they now wait his final resolution which upon his knee he besought he might be in favour of the girl that she might be permit to go home to her parent and not be expose to a great degree of shame and scorn than must necessarily fall upon she
i know say he that be too much
i know i be the wicked occasion of it
i will endeavour to make amend if possible and if you shall have hereaft the goodness to forgive i i hope i shall deserve it allworthy hesitate some time and at last say well i will discharge my mittimus you may send the constable to i he be instantly call discharge and so be the girl
it will be believe that mr allworthy fail not to read tom a very severe lecture on this occasion but it be unnecessary to insert it here as we have faithfully transcribe what he say to jenny jones in the first book most of which may be apply to the man equally with the woman
so sensible an effect have these reproof on the young man who be no hardened sinner that he retire to his own room where he pass the evening alone in much melancholy contemplation
allworthy be sufficiently offend by this transgression of jones for notwithstanding the assertion of mr western it be certain this worthy man have never indulge himself in any loose pleasure with woman and greatly condemn the vice of incontinence in other
indeed there be much reason to imagine that there be not the least truth in what mr western affirm especially as he lay the scene of those impurity at the university where mr allworthy have never be
in fact the good squire be a little too apt to indulge that kind of pleasantry which be generally call rhodomontade but which may with as much propriety be express by a much short word and perhaps we too often supply the use of this little monosyllable by other since very much of what frequently pass in the world for wit and humour should in the strict purity of language receive that short appellation which in conformity to the well-breed law of custom i here suppress
but whatever detestation mr allworthy have to this or to any other vice he be not so blinded by it but that he could discern any virtue in the guilty person as clearly indeed as if there have be no mixture of vice in the same character
while he be angry therefore with the incontinence of jones he be no less pleased with the honour and honesty of his self-accusation
he begin now to form in his mind the same opinion of this young fellow which we hope our reader may have conceive
and in balance his fault with his perfection the latter seem rather to preponderate
it be to no purpose therefore that thwackum who be immediately charge by mr blifil with the story unbend all his rancour against poor tom
allworthy give a patient hearing to their invective and then answer coldly that young man of tom s complexion be too generally addicted to this vice but he believe that youth be sincerely affect with what he have say to he on the occasion and he hop he would not transgress again so that as the day of whipping be at an end the tutor have no other vent but his own mouth for his gall the usual poor resource of impotent revenge
but square who be a less violent be a much more artful man and as he hat jones more perhaps than thwackum himself do so he contrive to do he more mischief in the mind of mr allworthy
the reader must remember the several little incident of the partridge the horse and the bible which be recount in the second book
by all which jones have rather improve than injure the affection which mr allworthy be incline to entertain for he
the same i believe must have happen to he with every other person who hath any idea of friendship generosity and greatness of spirit that be to say who hath any trace of goodness in his mind
square himself be not unacquainted with the true impression which those several instance of goodness have make on the excellent heart of allworthy for the philosopher very well know what virtue be though he be not always perhaps steady in its pursuit but as for thwackum from what reason i will not determine no such thought ever enter into his head he see jones in a bad light and he imagine allworthy see he in the same but that he be resolve from pride and stubbornness of spirit not to give up the boy whom he have once cherish since by so do he must tacitly acknowledge that his former opinion of he have be wrong
square therefore embrace this opportunity of injure jones in the tender part by give a very bad turn to all these before-mention occurrence
i be sorry sir say he to own i have be deceive as well as yourself
i could not i confess help be pleased with what i ascribe to the motive of friendship though it be carry to an excess and all excess be faulty and vicious but in this i make allowance for youth
little do i suspect that the sacrifice of truth which we both imagine to have be make to friendship be in reality a prostitution of it to a depraved and debauch appetite
you now plainly see whence all the seem generosity of this young man to the family of the gamekeeper proceed
he support the father in order to corrupt the daughter and preserve the family from starve to bring one of they to shame and ruin
this be friendship
this be generosity
as sir richard steele say glutton who give high price for delicacy be very worthy to be call generous in short i be resolve from this instance never to give way to the weakness of human nature more nor to think anything virtue which doth not exactly quadrate with the unerring rule of right the goodness of allworthy have prevent those consideration from occur to himself yet be they too plausible to be absolutely and hastily reject when lay before his eye by another
indeed what square have say sink very deeply into his mind and the uneasiness which it there create be very visible to the other though the good man would not acknowledge this but make a very slight answer and forcibly drive off the discourse to some other subject
it be well perhaps for poor tom that no such suggestion have be make before he be pardon for they certainly stamp in the mind of allworthy the first bad impression concern jones
chapter xii
contain much clear matter but which flow from the same fountain with those in the precede chapter
the reader will be please i believe to return with i to sophia
she pass the night after we see she last in no very agreeable manner
sleep befriend she but little and dream less
in the morning when mrs honour her maid attend she at the usual hour she be find already up and drest
person who live two or three mile distance in the country be consider as next-door neighbour and transaction at the one house fly with incredible celerity to the other
mrs honour therefore have hear the whole story of molly s shame which she be of a very communicative temper have no soon enter the apartment of her mistress than she begin to relate in the follow manner la ma am what doth your la ship think
the girl that your la ship see at church on sunday whom you think so handsome though you would not have think she so handsome neither if you have see she nearer but to be sure she hath be carry before the justice for be big with child
she seem to i to look like a confident slut and to be sure she hath lay the child to young mr jones
and all the parish say mr allworthy be so angry with young mr jones that he willnot see he
to be sure one cannot help pity the poor young man and yet he doth not deserve much pity neither for demean himself with such kind of trumpery
yet he be so pretty a gentleman i should be sorry to have he turn out of door
i dare to swear the wench be as willing as he for she be always a forward kind of body
and when wench be so come young man be not so much to be blame neither for to be sure they do no more than what be natural
indeed it be beneath they to meddle with such dirty draggle-tail and whatever happen to they it be good enough for they
and yet to be sure the vile baggage be most in fault
i wish with all my heart they be well to be whip at the cart s tail for it be pity they should be the ruin of a pretty young gentleman and nobody can deny but that mr jones be one of the most handsome young man that ever---- she be run on thus when sophia with a more peevish voice than she have ever speak to she in before cry prithee why dost thou trouble i with all this stuff
what concern have i in what mr jones doth
i suppose you be all alike
and you seem to i to be angry it be not your own case i ma am answer mrs honour i be sorry your ladyship should have such an opinion of i
i be sure nobody can say any such thing of i
all the young fellow in the world may go to the divil for i
because i say he be a handsome man
everybody say it as well as i
to be sure i never think as it be any harm to say a young man be handsome but to be sure i shall never think he so any more now for handsome be that handsome doe
a beggar wench stop thy torrent of impertinence cry sophia and see whether my father want i at breakfast mrs honour then fling out of the room mutter much to herself of which marry come up i assure you be all that could be plainly distinguish
whether mrs honour really deserve that suspicion of which her mistress give she a hint be a matter which we cannot indulge our reader s curiosity by resolve
we will however make he amend in disclose what pass in the mind of sophia
the reader will be pleased to recollect that a secret affection for mr jones have insensibly steal into the bosom of this young lady
that it have there grow to a pretty great height before she herself have discover it
when she first begin to perceive its symptom the sensation be so sweet and pleasing that she have not resolution sufficient to check or repel they and thus she go on cherish a passion of which she never once consider the consequence
this incident relate to molly first open her eye
she now first perceive the weakness of which she have be guilty and though it cause the utmost perturbation in her mind yet it have the effect of other nauseous physic and for the time expel her distemper
its operation indeed be most wonderfully quick and in the short interval while her maid be absent so entirely remove all symptom that when mrs honour return with a summon from her father she be become perfectly easy and have bring herself to a thorough indifference for mr jones
the disease of the mind do in almost every particular imitate those of the body
for which reason we hope that learn faculty for whom we have so profound a respect will pardon we the violent hand we have be necessitate to lay on several word and phrase which of right belong to they and without which our description must have be often unintelligible
now there be no one circumstance in which the distemper of the mind bear a more exact analogy to those which be call bodily than that aptness which both have to a relapse
this be plain in the violent disease of ambition and avarice
i have know ambition when cure at court by frequent disappointment which be the only physic for it to break out again in a contest for foreman of the grand jury at an assize and have hear of a man who have so far conquer avarice as to give away many a sixpence that comfort himself at last on his deathbe by make a crafty and advantageous bargain concern his ensue funeral with an undertaker who have marry his only child
in the affair of love which out of strict conformity with the stoic philosophy we shall here treat as a disease this proneness to relapse be no less conspicuous
thus it happen to poor sophia upon whom the very next time she see young jones all the former symptom return and from that time cold and hot fit alternately seize her heart
the situation of this young lady be now very different from what it have ever be before
that passion which have formerly be so exquisitely delicious become now a scorpion in her bosom
she resist it therefore with her utmost force and summon every argument her reason which be surprisingly strong for her age could suggest to subdue and expel it
in this she so far succeed that she begin to hope from time and absence a perfect cure
she resolve therefore to avoid tom jones as much as possible for which purpose she begin to conceive a design of visit her aunt to which she make no doubt of obtain her father s consent
but fortune who have other design in her head put an immediate stop to any such proceeding by introduce an accident which will be relate in the next chapter
chapter xiii
a dreadful accident which befel sophia
the gallant behaviour of jones and the more dreadful consequence of that behaviour to the young lady with a short digression in favour of the female sex
mr western grow every day fonder and fonder of sophia insomuch that his beloved dog themselves almost give place to she in his affection but as he could not prevail on himself to abandon these he contrive very cunningly to enjoy their company together with that of his daughter by insist on she rid a hunting with he
sophia to whom her father s word be a law readily comply with his desire though she have not the least delight in a sport which be of too rough and masculine a nature to suit with her disposition
she have however another motive beside her obedience to accompany the old gentleman in the chase for by her presence she hop in some measure to restrain his impetuosity and to prevent he from so frequently expose his neck to the utmost hazard
the strong objection be that which would have formerly be an inducement to she namely the frequent meeting with young jones whom she have determine to avoid but as the end of the hunting season now approach she hop by a short absence with her aunt to reason herself entirely out of her unfortunate passion and have not any doubt of be able to meet he in the field the subsequent season without the least danger
on the second day of her hunting as she be return from the chase and be arrive within a little distance from mr western s house her horse whose mettlesome spirit require a well rider fall suddenly to prance and caper in such a manner that she be in the most imminent peril of fall
tom jones who be at a little distance behind see this and immediately gallop up to her assistance
as soon as he come up he leap from his own horse and catch hold of her by the bridle
the unruly beast presently rear himself an end on his hind leg and throw his lovely burthen from his back and jones catch she in his arm
she be so affected with the fright that she be not immediately able to satisfy jones who be very sollicitous to know whether she have receive any hurt
she soon after however recover her spirit assure he she be safe and thank he for the care he have take of she
jones answer if i have preserve you madam i be sufficiently repay for i promise you i would have secure you from the least harm at the expense of a much great misfortune to myself than i have suffer on this occasion what misfortune reply sophia eagerly i hope you have come to no mischief be not concern madam answer jones
heaven be praise you have escape so well consider the danger you be in
if i have break my arm i consider it as a trifle in comparison of what i fear upon your account sophia then scream out break your arm
heaven forbid i be afraid i have madam say jones but i beg you will suffer i first to take care of you
i have a right hand yet at your service to help you into the next field whence we have but a very little walk to your father s house sophia see his left arm dangle by his side while he be use the other to lead she no long doubt of the truth
she now grow much pale than her fear for herself have make she before
all her limb be seize with a trembling insomuch that jones could scarce support she and as her thought be in no less agitation she could not refrain from give jones a look so full of tenderness that it almost argue a strong sensation in her mind than even gratitude and pity united can raise in the gentle female bosom without the assistance of a third more powerful passion
mr western who be advance at some distance when this accident happen be now return as be the rest of the horseman
sophia immediately acquaint they with what have befall jones and beg they to take care of he
upon which western who have be much alarm by meet his daughter s horse without its rider and be now overjoyed to find her unhurt cry out i be glad it be no bad
if tom hath break his arm we will get a joiner to mend un again the squire alight from his horse and proceed to his house on foot with his daughter and jones
an impartial spectator who have meet they on the way would on view their several countenance have conclude sophia alone to have be the object of compassion for as to jones he exult in have probably save the life of the young lady at the price only of a broken bone and mr western though he be not unconcerned at the accident which have befall jones be however delight in a much high degree with the fortunate escape of his daughter
the generosity of sophia s temper construe this behaviour of jones into great bravery and it make a deep impression on her heart for certain it be that there be no one quality which so generally recommend man to woman as this proceed if we believe the common opinion from that natural timidity of the sex which be say mr osborne so great that a woman be the most cowardly of all the creature god ever made a sentiment more remarkable for its bluntness than for its truth
aristotle in his politic doth they i believe more justice when he say the modesty and fortitude of man differ from those virtue in woman for the fortitude which become a woman would be cowardice in a man and the modesty which become a man would be pertness in a woman nor be there perhaps more of truth in the opinion of those who derive the partiality which woman be incline to show to the brave from this excess of their fear
mr bayle i think in his article of helen impute this and with great probability to their violent love of glory for the truth of which we have the authority of he who of all other see farth into human nature and who introduce the heroine of his odyssey the great pattern of matrimonial love and constancy assign the glory of her husband as the only source of her affection towards he the english reader will not find this in the poem for the sentiment be entirely leave out in the translation
however this be certain it be that the accident operate very strongly on sophia and indeed after much enquiry into the matter i be inclined to believe that at this very time the charming sophia make no less impression on the heart of jones to say truth he have for some time become sensible of the irresistible power of her charm
chapter xiv
the arrival of a surgeon his operation and a long dialogue between sophia and her maid
when they arrive at mr western s hall sophia who have totter along with much difficulty sink down in her chair but by the assistance of hartshorn and water she be prevent from faint away and have pretty well recover her spirit when the surgeon who be send for to jones appear
mr western who impute these symptom in his daughter to her fall advise she to be presently blood by way of prevention
in this opinion he be second by the surgeon who give so many reason for bleeding and quote so many case where person have miscarry for want of it that the squire become very importunate and indeed insist peremptorily that his daughter should be blood
sophia soon yield to the command of her father though entirely contrary to her own inclination for she suspect i believe less danger from the fright than either the squire or the surgeon
she then stretch out her beautiful arm and the operator begin to prepare for his work
while the servant be busy in provide material the surgeon who impute the backwardness which have appear in sophia to her fear begin to comfort she with assurance that there be not the least danger for no accident he say could ever happen in bleeding but from the monstrous ignorance of pretender to surgery which he pretty plainly insinuated be not at present to be apprehend
sophia declare she be not under the least apprehension add if you open an artery i promise you iwill forgive you will you cry western d--n i if i will
if he do thee the least mischief d--n i if i donot ha the heart s blood o un out the surgeon assent to bleed she upon these condition and then proceed to his operation which he perform with as much dexterity as he have promise and with as much quickness for he take but little blood from she say it be much safe to bleed again and again than to take away too much at once
sophia when her arm be bind up retire for she be not willing nor be it perhaps strictly decent to be present at the operation on jones
indeed one objection which she have to bleed though she do not make it be the delay which it would occasion to set the break bone
for western when sophia be concern have no consideration but for she and as for jones himself he sit like patience on a monument smile at grief to say the truth when he see the blood spring from the lovely arm of sophia he scarce think of what have happen to himself
the surgeon now order his patient to be stript to his shirt and then entirely bar the arm he begin to stretch and examine it in such a manner that the torture he put he to cause jones to make several wry face which the surgeon observe greatly wonder at cry what be the matter sir
i be sure it be impossible i should hurt you and then hold forth the broken arm he begin a long and very learn lecture of anatomy in which simple and double fracture be most accurately consider and the several way in which jones might have break his arm be discuss with proper annotation show how many of these would have be well and how many bad than the present case
have at length finish his laboured harangue with which the audience though it have greatly raise their attention and admiration be not much edify as they really understand not a single syllable of all he have say he proceed to business which he be more expeditious in finishing than he have be in begin
jones be then order into a bed which mr western compel he to accept at his own house and sentence of water-gruel be pass upon he
among the good company which have attend in the hall during the bone-setting mrs honour be one who be summon to her mistress as soon as it be over and ask by she how the young gentleman do presently launch into extravagant praise on the magnanimity as she call it of his behaviour which she say be so charming in so pretty a creature she then burst forth into much warm encomium on the beauty of his person enumerate many particular and end with the whiteness of his skin
this discourse have an effect on sophia s countenance which would not perhaps have escape the observance of the sagacious waiting-woman have she once look her mistress in the face all the time she be speak but as a look-glass which be most commodiously place opposite to she give she an opportunity of survey those feature in which of all other she take most delight so she have not once remove her eye from that amiable object during her whole speech
mrs honour be so intirely wrap up in the subject on which she exercise her tongue and the object before her eye that she give her mistress time to conquer her confusion which have do she smile on her maid and tell she she be certainly in love with this young fellow i in love madam answer she upon my word ma am i assure you ma am upon my soul ma am i be not why if you be cry her mistress i see no reason that you should be ashamed of it for he be certainly a pretty fellow yes ma am answer the other that he be the most handsome man i ever see in my life
yes to be sure that he be and as your ladyship say i donot know why i should be ashamed of love he though he be my better
to be sure gentlefolk be but flesh and blood no more than us servant
besides as for mr jones thof squire allworthy hath make a gentleman of he he be not so good as myself by birth for thof i be a poor body i be an honest person s child and my father and mother be marry which be more than some people can say as high as they hold their head
marry come up
i assure you my dirty cousin
thof his skin be so white and to be sure it be the most whitest that ever be see i be a christian as well as he and nobody can say that i be base bear my grandfather be a clergyman and would have be very angry i believe to have think any of his family should have take up with molly seagrim s dirty leaving this be the second person of low condition whom we have record in this history to have spring from the clergy
it be to be hop such instance will in future age when some provision be make for the family of the inferior clergy appear strange than they can be think at present
perhaps sophia might have suffer she maid to run on in this manner from want sufficient spirit to stop her tongue which the reader may probably conjecture be no very easy task for certainly there be some passage in her speech which be far from be agreeable to the lady
however she now check the torrent as there seem no end of its flowing
i wonder say she at your assurance in daring to talk thus of one of my father s friend
as to the wench i order you never to mention her name to i
and with regard to the young gentleman s birth those who can say nothing more to his disadvantage may as well be silent on that head as i desire you will be for the future i be sorry i have offend your ladyship answer mrs honour
i be sure i hate molly seagrim as much as your ladyship can and as for abuse squire jones i can call all the servant in the house to witness that whenever any talk hath be about bastard i have always take his part for which of you say i to the footman would not be a bastard if he could to be make a gentleman of
and say i i be sure he be a very fine gentleman and he hath one of the white hand in the world for to be sure so he hath and say i one of the sweetest temperedest well natured man in the world he be and say i all the servant and neighbour all round the country love he
and to be sure i could tell your ladyship something but that i be afraid it would offend you what could you tell i honour say sophia
nay ma am to be sure he mean nothing by it therefore i would not have your ladyship be offend prithee tell i say sophia i will know it this instant why ma am answer mrs honour he come into the room one day last week when i be at work and there lie your ladyship s muff on a chair and to be sure he put his hand into it that very muff your ladyship give i but yesterday
la
say i mr jones you will stretch my lady s muff and spoil it but he still keep his hand in it and then he kiss it--to be sure i hardly ever see such a kiss in my life as he give it i suppose he do not know it be mine reply sophia
your ladyship shall hear ma am
he kiss it again and again and say it be the prettiest muff in the world
la
sir say i you have see it a hundred time
yes mrs honour cry he but who can see anything beautiful in the presence of your lady but herself nay thatbe not all neither but i hope your ladyship willnot be offend for to be sure he mean nothing
one day as your ladyship be play on the harpsichord to my master mr jones be sit in the next room and methought he look melancholy
la
say i mr jones whatbe the matter
a penny for your thought say i
why hussy say he start up from a dream what can i be think of when that angel your mistress be play
and then squeeze i by the hand oh
mrs honour say he how happy will that man be and then he sigh
upon my troth his breath be as sweet as a nosegay but to be sure he mean no harm by it
so i hope your ladyship will not mention a word for he give i a crown never to mention it and make i swear upon a book but i believe indeed it be not the bible till something of a more beautiful red than vermilion be find out i shall say nothing of sophia s colour on this occasion
ho--nour say she i--if you will not mention this any more to i--nor to anybody else i will not betray you--i mean i will not be angry but i be afraid of your tongue
why my girl will you give it such liberties nay ma am answer she to be sure i would soon cut out my tongue than offend your ladyship
to be sure i shall never mention a word that your ladyship would not have i why i would not have you mention this any more say sophia for it may come to my father s ear and he would be angry with mr jones though i really believe as you say he mean nothing
i should be very angry myself if i imagined-- nay ma am say honour i protest i believe he mean nothing
i think he talk as if he be out of his sens nay he say he believe he be beside himself when he have speak the word
ay sir say i i believe so too
yes say he honour but i ask your ladyship s pardon i could tear my tongue out for offend you go on say sophia you may mention anything you have not tell i before yes honour say he this be some time afterwards when he give i the crown i be neither such a coxcomb or such a villain as to think of she in any other delight but as my goddess as such i will always worship and adore she while i have breath this be all ma am i will be swear to the good of my remembrance
i be in a passion with he myself till i find he mean no harm indeed honour say sophia i believe you have a real affection for i
i be provoke the other day when i give you warning but if you have a desire to stay with i you shall to be sure ma am answer mrs honour i shall never desire to part with your ladyship
to be sure i almost cry my eye out when you give i warning
it would be very ungrateful in i to desire to leave your ladyship because as why i should never get so good a place again
i be sure i would live and die with your ladyship for as poor mr jones say happy be the man---- here the dinner bell interrupt a conversation which have work such an effect on sophia that she be perhaps more obliged to she bleed in the morning than she at the time have apprehend she should be
as to the present situation of her mind i shall adhere to a rule of horace by not attempt to describe it from despair of success
most of my reader will suggest it easily to themselves and the few who cannot would not understand the picture or at least would deny it to be natural if ever so well draw
book v
contain a portion of time somewhat long than half a year
chapter i
of the serious in writing and for what purpose it be introduce
peradventure there may be no part in this prodigious work which will give the reader less pleasure in the perusing than those which have give the author the great pain in composing
among these probably may be reckon those initial essay which we have prefix to the historical matter contain in every book and which we have determine to be essentially necessary to this kind of writing of which we have set ourselves at the head
for this our determination we do not hold ourselves strictly bind to assign any reason it be abundantly sufficient that we have lay it down as a rule necessary to be observe in all prosai-comi-epic writing
who ever demand the reason of that nice unity of time or place which be now establish to be so essential to dramatic poetry
what critic hath be ever ask why a play may not contain two day as well as one
or why the audience provide they travel like elector without any expense may not be waft fifty mile as well as five
hath any commentator well account for the limitation which an antient critic hath set to the drama which he will have contain neither more nor less than five act
or hath any one live attempt to explain what the modern judge of our theatre mean by that word low by which they have happily succeed in banish all humour from the stage and have make the theatre as dull as a drawing-room
upon all these occasion the world seem to have embrace a maxim of our law viz cuicunque in arte sua perito credendum est for it seem perhaps difficult to conceive that any one should have have enough of impudence to lay down dogmatical rule in any art or science without the least foundation
in such case therefore we be apt to conclude there be sound and good reason at the bottom though we be unfortunately not able to see so far
now in reality the world have pay too great a compliment to critic and have imagine they man of much great profundity than they really be
from this complacence the critic have be embolden to assume a dictatorial power and have so far succeed that they be now become the master and have the assurance to give law to those author from whose predecessor they originally receive they
the critic rightly consider be no more than the clerk whose office it be to transcribe the rule and law lay down by those great judge whose vast strength of genius hath place they in the light of legislator in the several science over which they preside
this office be all which the critic of old aspire to nor do they ever dare to advance a sentence without support it by the authority of the judge from whence it be borrow
but in process of time and in age of ignorance the clerk begin to invade the power and assume the dignity of his master
the law of writing be no long found on the practice of the author but on the dictate of the critic
the clerk become the legislator and those very peremptorily give law whose business it be at first only to transcribe they
hence arise an obvious and perhaps an unavoidable error for these critic be man of shallow capacity very easily mistook mere form for substance
they act as a judge would who should adhere to the lifeless letter of law and reject the spirit
little circumstance which be perhaps accidental in a great author be by these critic consider to constitute his chief merit and transmit as essential to be observe by all his successor
to these encroachment time and ignorance the two great supporter of imposture give authority and thus many rule for good writing have be establish which have not the least foundation in truth or nature and which commonly serve for no other purpose than to curb and restrain genius in the same manner as it would have restrain the dancing-master have the many excellent treatise on that art lay it down as an essential rule that every man must dance in chain
to avoid therefore all imputation of lay down a rule for posterity found only on the authority of ipse dixit for which to say the truth we have not the profound veneration--we shall here waive the privilege above contend for and proceed to lay before the reader the reason which have induce we to intersperse these several digressive essay in the course of this work
and here we shall of necessity be lead to open a new vein of knowledge which if it hath be discover hath not to our remembrance be work on by any antient or modern writer
this vein be no other than that of contrast which run through all the work of the creation and may probably have a large share in constitute in we the idea of all beauty as well natural as artificial for what demonstrate the beauty and excellence of anything but its reverse
thus the beauty of day and that of summer be set off by the horror of night and winter
and i believe if it be possible for a man to have see only the two former he would have a very imperfect idea of their beauty
but to avoid too serious an air can it be doubt but that the fine woman in the world would lose all benefit of her charm in the eye of a man who have never see one of another cast
the lady themselves seem so sensible of this that they be all industrious to procure foil nay they will become foil to themselves for i have observe at bath particularly that they endeavour to appear as ugly as possible in the morning in order to set off that beauty which they intend to show you in the evening
most artist have this secret in practice though some perhaps have not much study the theory
the jeweller know that the fine brilliant require a foil and the painter by the contrast of his figure often acquire great applause
a great genius among we will illustrate this matter fully
i cannot indeed range he under any general head of common artist as he hath a title to be place among those inventa qui vitam excoluere per arte who by invent art have life improve
i mean here the inventor of that most exquisite entertainment call the english pantomime
this entertainment consist of two part which the inventor distinguish by the name of the serious and the comic
the serious exhibit a certain number of heathen god and hero who be certainly the bad and dull company into which an audience be ever introduce and which be a secret know to few be actually intend so to be in order to contrast the comic part of the entertainment and to display the trick of harlequin to the well advantage
this be perhaps no very civil use of such personage but the contrivance be nevertheless ingenious enough and have its effect
and this will now plainly appear if instead of serious and comic we supply the word duller and dull for the comic be certainly dull than anything before show on the stage and could be set off only by that superlative degree of dulness which compose the serious
