so intolerably serious indeed be these god and hero that harlequin though the english gentleman of that name be not at all relate to the french family for he be of a much more serious disposition be always welcome on the stage as he relieve the audience from bad company
judicious writer have always practise this art of contrast with great success
i have be surprize that horace should cavil at this art in homer but indeed he contradict himself in the very next line indignor quandoque bonus dormitat homerus verum opere in longo fas est obrepere somnum i grieve if e er great homer chance to sleep yet slumber on long work have right to creep
for we be not here to understand as perhaps some have that an author actually fall asleep while he be write
it be true that reader be too apt to be so overtaken but if the work be as long as any of oldmixon the author himself be too well entertain to be subject to the least drowsiness
he be as mr pope observe sleepless himself to give his reader sleep
to say the truth these soporific part be so many scene of serious artfully interweave in order to contrast and set off the rest and this be the true meaning of a late facetious writer who tell the public that whenever he be dull they might be assure there be a design in it
in this light then or rather in this darkness i would have the reader to consider these initial essay
and after this warning if he shall be of opinion that he can find enough of serious in other part of this history he may pass over these in which we profess to be laboriously dull and begin the follow book at the second chapter
chapter ii
in which mr jones receive many friendly visit during his confinement with some fine touch of the passion of love scarce visible to the naked eye
tom jones have many visitor during his confinement though some perhaps be not very agreeable to he
mr allworthy see he almost every day but though he pity tom s suffering and greatly approve the gallant behaviour which have occasion they yet he think this be a favourable opportunity to bring he to a sober sense of his indiscreet conduct and that wholesome advice for that purpose could never be apply at a more proper season than at the present when the mind be soften by pain and sickness and alarm by danger and when its attention be unembarrasse with those turbulent passion which engage we in the pursuit of pleasure
at all season therefore when the good man be alone with the youth especially when the latter be totally at ease he take occasion to remind he of his former miscarriage but in the mild and tender manner and only in order to introduce the caution which he prescribe for his future behaviour on which alone he assure he would depend his own felicity and the kindness which he might yet promise himself to receive at the hand of his father by adoption unless he should hereafter forfeit his good opinion for as to what have past he say it should be all forgive and forget
he therefore advise he to make a good use of this accident that so in the end it might prove a visitation for his own good thwackum be likewise pretty assiduous in his visit and he too consider a sick-bed to be a convenient scene for lecture
his stile however be more severe than mr allworthy s he tell his pupil that he ought to look on his broken limb as a judgment from heaven on his sin
that it would become he to be daily on his knee pour forth thanksgiving that he have break his arm only and not his neck which latter he say be very probably reserve for some future occasion and that perhaps not very remote
for his part he say he have often wonder some judgment have not overtake he before but it might be perceive by this that divine punishment though slow be always sure hence likewise he advise he to foresee with equal certainty the great evil which be yet behind and which be as sure as this of overtake he in his state of reprobacy
these be say he to be avert only by such a thorough and sincere repentance as be not to be expect or hop for from one so abandon in his youth and whose mind i be afraid be totally corrupted
it be my duty however to exhort you to this repentance though i too well know all exhortation will be vain and fruitless
but liberavi animam meam i can accuse my own conscience of no neglect though it be at the same time with the utmost concern i see you travel on to certain misery in this world and to as certain damnation in the next square talk in a very different strain he say such accident as a broken bone be below the consideration of a wise man
that it be abundantly sufficient to reconcile the mind to any of these mischance to reflect that they be liable to befal the wise of mankind and be undoubtedly for the good of the whole he say it be a mere abuse of word to call those thing evil in which there be no moral unfitness that pain which be the bad consequence of such accident be the most contemptible thing in the world with more of the like sentence extract out of the second book of tully s tusculan question and from the great lord shaftesbury
in pronounce these he be one day so eager that he unfortunately bite his tongue and in such a manner that it not only put an end to his discourse but create much emotion in he and cause he to mutter an oath or two but what be bad of all this accident give thwackum who be present and who hold all such doctrine to be heathenish and atheistical an opportunity to clap a judgment on his back
now this be do with so malicious a sneer that it totally unhinge if i may so say the temper of the philosopher which the bite of his tongue have somewhat ruffle and as he be disabled from vent his wrath at his lip he have possibly find a more violent method of revenge himself have not the surgeon who be then luckily in the room contrary to his own interest interpose and preserve the peace
mr blifil visit his friend jones but seldom and never alone
this worthy young man however profess much regard for he and as great concern at his misfortune but cautiously avoid any intimacy lest as he frequently hint it might contaminate the sobriety of his own character for which purpose he have constantly in his mouth that proverb in which solomon speak against evil communication
not that he be so bitter as thwackum for he always express some hope of tom s reformation which he say the unparalleled goodness show by his uncle on this occasion must certainly effect in one not absolutely abandon but conclude if mr jones ever offend hereafter i shall not be able to say a syllable in his favour as to squire western he be seldom out of the sick-room unless when he be engage either in the field or over his bottle
nay he would sometimes retire hither to take his beer and it be not without difficulty that he be prevent from force jones to take his beer too for no quack ever hold his nostrum to be a more general panacea than he do this which he say have more virtue in it than be in all the physic in an apothecary s shop
he be however by much entreaty prevail on to forbear the application of this medicine but from serenade his patient every hunting morning with the horn under his window it be impossible to withhold he nor do he ever lie aside that hallow with which he enter into all company when he visit jones without any regard to the sick person s be at that time either awake or asleep
this boisterous behaviour as it mean no harm so happily it effect none and be abundantly compensate to jones as soon as he be able to sit up by the company of sophia whom the squire then bring to visit he nor be it indeed long before jones be able to attend her to the harpsichord where she would kindly condescend for hour together to charm he with the most delicious music unless when the squire think proper to interrupt she by insist on old sir simon or some other of his favourite piece
notwithstanding the nice guard which sophia endeavour to set on her behaviour she could not avoid let some appearance now and then slip forth for love may again be liken to a disease in this that when it be deny a vent in one part it will certainly break out in another
what her lip therefore conceal her eye her blush and many little involuntary action betray
one day when sophia be play on the harpsichord and jones be attend the squire come into the room cry there tom i have have a battle for thee below-stair with thick parson thwackum
he hath be a tell allworthy before my face that the broken bone be a judgment upon thee
d--n it say i how can that be
do he not come by it in defence of a young woman
a judgment indeed
pox if he never doth anything bad he will go to heaven soon than all the parson in the country
he hath more reason to glory in it than to be ashamed of it indeed sir say jones i have no reason for either but if it preserve miss western i shall always think it the happy accident of my life and to gu say the squire to zet allworthy against thee vor it
d--n un if the parson have unt his petticuoat on i should have lend un o flick for i love thee dearly my boy and d--n i if there be anything in my power which i willnot do for thee
sha t take thy choice of all the horse in my stable to-morrow morning except only the chevalier and miss slouch jones thank he but decline accept the offer
nay add the squire sha t ha the sorrel mare that sophy ride
she cost i fifty guineas and come six year old this grass if she have cost i a thousand cry jones passionately i would have give she to the dog pooh
pooh answer western what
because she break thy arm
shouldst forget and forgive
i think hadst be more a man than to bear malice against a dumb creature here sophia interpose and put an end to the conversation by desire her father s leave to play to he a request which he never refuse
the countenance of sophia have undergo more than one change during the foregoing speech and probably she impute the passionate resentment which jones have express against the mare to a different motive from that from which her father have derive it
her spirit be at this time in a visible flutter and she play so intolerably ill that have not western soon fall asleep he must have remark it
jones however who be sufficiently awake and be not without an ear any more than without eye make some observation which be join to all which the reader may remember to have pass formerly give he pretty strong assurance when he come to reflect on the whole that all be not well in the tender bosom of sophia an opinion which many young gentleman will i doubt not extremely wonder at his not have be well confirm in long ago
to confess the truth he have rather too much diffidence in himself and be not forward enough in see the advance of a young lady a misfortune which can be cure only by that early town education which be at present so generally in fashion
when these thought have fully take possession of jones they occasion a perturbation in his mind which in a constitution less pure and firm than his might have be at such a season attend with very dangerous consequence
he be truly sensible of the great worth of sophia
he extremely like her person no less admire her accomplishment and tenderly love her goodness
in reality as he have never once entertain any thought of possess she nor have ever give the least voluntary indulgence to his inclination he have a much strong passion for she than he himself be acquaint with
his heart now bring forth the full secret at the same time that it assure he the adorable object return his affection
chapter iii
which all who have no heart will think to contain much ado about nothing
the reader will perhaps imagine the sensation which now arise in jones to have be so sweet and delicious that they would rather tend to produce a chearful serenity in the mind than any of those dangerous effect which we have mention but in fact sensation of this kind however delicious be at their first recognition of a very tumultuous nature and have very little of the opiate in they
they be moreover in the present case embitter with certain circumstance which be mix with sweet ingredient tend altogether to compose a draught that might be term bitter-sweet than which as nothing can be more disagreeable to the palate so nothing in the metaphorical sense can be so injurious to the mind
for first though he have sufficient foundation to flatter himself in what he have observe in sophia he be not yet free from doubt of misconstruing compassion or at good esteem into a warm regard
he be far from a sanguine assurance that sophia have any such affection towards he as might promise his inclination that harvest which if they be encourage and nurse they would finally grow up to require
besides if he could hope to find no bar to his happiness from the daughter he think himself certain of meet an effectual bar in the father who though he be a country squire in his diversion be perfectly a man of the world in whatever regard his fortune have the most violent affection for his only daughter and have often signify in his cup the pleasure he propose in see she married to one of the rich man in the county
jones be not so vain and senseles a coxcomb as to expect from any regard which western have profess for he that he would ever be induce to lay aside these view of advance his daughter
he well know that fortune be generally the principal if not the sole consideration which operate on the good of parent in these matter for friendship make we warmly espouse the interest of other but it be very cold to the gratification of their passion
indeed to feel the happiness which may result from this it be necessary we should possess the passion ourselves
as he have therefore no hope of obtain her father s consent so he think to endeavour to succeed without it and by such mean to frustrate the great point of mr western s life be to make a very ill use of his hospitality and a very ungrateful return to the many little favour receive however roughly at his hand
if he see such a consequence with horror and disdain how much more be he shock with what regard mr allworthy to whom as he have more than filial obligation so have he for he more than filial piety
he know the nature of that good man to be so averse to any baseness or treachery that the least attempt of such a kind would make the sight of the guilty person for ever odious to his eye and his name a detestable sound in his ear
the appearance of such unsurmountable difficulty be sufficient to have inspire he with despair however ardent his wish have be but even these be contruole by compassion for another woman
the idea of lovely molly now intrude itself before he
he have swear eternal constancy in her arm and she have as often vow never to out-live his desert she
he now see she in all the most shocking posture of death nay he consider all the misery of prostitution to which she would be liable and of which he would be doubly the occasion first by seducing and then by desert she for he well know the hatred which all her neighbour and even her own sister bear she and how ready they would all be to tear she to piece
indeed he have expose she to more envy than shame or rather to the latter by mean of the former for many woman abuse she for be a whore while they envy she her lover and her finery and would have be themselves glad to have purchase these at the same rate
the ruin therefore of the poor girl must he foresaw unavoidably attend his desert she and this think sting he to the soul
poverty and distress seem to he to give none a right of aggravate those misfortune
the meanness of her condition do not represent her misery as of little consequence in his eye nor do it appear to justify or even to palliate his guilt in bring that misery upon she
but why do i mention justification
his own heart would not suffer he to destroy a human creature who he think love he and have to that love sacrifice her innocence
his own good heart plead her cause not as a cold venal advocate but as one interested in the event and which must itself deeply share in all the agony its owner bring on another
when this powerful advocate have sufficiently raise the pity of jones by paint poor molly in all the circumstance of wretchedness it artfully call in the assistance of another passion and represent the girl in all the amiable colour of youth health and beauty as one greatly the object of desire and much more so at least to a good mind from be at the same time the object of compassion
amidst these thought poor jones pass a long sleepless night and in the morning the result of the whole be to abide by molly and to think no more of sophia
in this virtuous resolution he continue all the next day till the evening cherish the idea of molly and drive sophia from his thought but in the fatal evening a very trifling accident set all his passion again on float and work so total a change in his mind that we think it decent to communicate it in a fresh chapter
chapter iv
a little chapter in which be contain a little incident
among other visitant who pay their compliment to the young gentleman in his confinement mrs honour be one
the reader perhaps when he reflect on some expression which have formerly dropt from she may conceive that she herself have a very particular affection for mr jones but in reality it be no such thing
tom be a handsome young fellow and for that specie of man mrs honour have some regard but this be perfectly indiscriminate for have be cross in the love which she bear a certain nobleman s footman who have basely desert she after a promise of marriage she have so securely keep together the broken remain of her heart that no man have ever since be able to possess himself of any single fragment
she view all handsome man with that equal regard and benevolence which a sober and virtuous mind bear to all the good
she might indeed be call a lover of man as socrates be a lover of mankind prefer one to another for corporeal as he for mental qualification but never carry this preference so far as to cause any perturbation in the philosophical serenity of her temper
the day after mr jones have that conflict with himself which we have see in the precede chapter mrs honour come into his room and find he alone begin in the following manner la sir where do you think i have be
i warrant you you would not guess in fifty year but if you do guess to be sure i must not tell you neither nay if it be something which you must not tell i say jones i shall have the curiosity to enquire and i know you will not be so barbarous to refuse i i donot know cry she why i should refuse you neither for that matter for to be sure you willnot mention it any more
and for that matter if you know where i have be unless you know what i have be about it would not signify much
nay i donot see why it should be keep a secret for my part for to be sure she be the good lady in the world upon this jones begin to beg earnestly to be let into this secret and faithfully promise not to divulge it
she then proceed thus why you must know sir my young lady send i to enquire after molly seagrim and to see whether the wench want anything to be sure i do not care to go methink but servant must do what they be ordered how could you undervalue yourself so mr jones so my lady bid i go and carry she some linen and other thing
she be too good
if such forward slut be send to bridewell it would be well for they
i tell my lady say i madam your la ship be encourage idleness and be my sophia so good say jones
my sophia
i assure you marry come up answer honour
and yet if you know all--indeed if i be as mr jones i should look a little high than such trumpery as molly seagrim what do you mean by these word reply jones if i know all i mean what i mean say honour
donot you remember put your hand in my lady s muff once
i vow i could almost find in my heart to tell if i be certain my lady would never come to the hearing on t jones then make several solemn protestation
and honour proceeded-- then to be sure my lady give i that muff and afterwards upon hear what you have done then you tell she what i have do interrupt jones
if i do sir answer she you need not be angry with i
manybe the man would have give his head to have have my lady tell if they have known for to be sure the big lord in the land might be proud--but i protest i have a great mind not to tell you jones fall to entreaty and soon prevail on she to go on thus
you must know then sir that my lady have give this muff to i but about a day or two after i have tell she the story she quarrel with her new muff and to be sure it be the prettiest that ever be see
honour say she this be an odious muff it be too big for i i cannot wear it till i can get another you must let i have my old one again and you may have this in the room on t--for shebe a good lady and scorn to give a thing and take a thing i promise you that
so to be sure i fetch it she back again and i believe she hath wear it upon her arm almost ever since and i warrant hath give it many a kiss when nobody hath see she here the conversation be interrupt by mr western himself who come to summon jones to the harpsichord whither the poor young fellow go all pale and trembling
this western observe but on see mrs honour impute it to a wrong cause and have give jones a hearty curse between jest and earnest he bid he beat abroad and not poach up the game in his warren
sophia look this evening with more than usual beauty and we may believe it be no small addition to her charm in the eye of mr jones that she now happen to have on her right arm this very muff
she be play one of her father s favourite tune and he be lean on her chair when the muff fall over her finger and put she out
this so disconcert the squire that he snatch the muff from she and with a hearty curse throw it into the fire
sophia instantly start up and with the utmost eagerness recover it from the flame
though this incident will probably appear of little consequence to many of our reader yet trifle as it be it have so violent an effect on poor jones that we think it our duty to relate it
in reality there be many little circumstance too often omit by injudicious historian from which event of the utmost importance arise
the world may indeed be consider as a vast machine in which the great wheel be originally set in motion by those which be very minute and almost imperceptible to any but the strong eye
thus not all the charm of the incomparable sophia not all the dazzle brightness and languish softness of her eye the harmony of her voice and of her person not all her wit good-humour greatness of mind or sweetness of disposition have be able so absolutely to conquer and enslave the heart of poor jones as this little incident of the muff
thus the poet sweetly sing of troy-- captique dolis lachrymisque coacti quos neque tydides nec larissaeus achilles non anni domuere decem non mille carinae what diomede or thetis great son a thousand ship nor ten year siege have do false tear and fawn word the city win
the citadel of jones be now take by surprize
all those consideration of honour and prudence which our heroe have lately with so much military wisdom place as guard over the avenue of his heart run away from their post and the god of love march in in triumph
chapter v
a very long chapter contain a very great incident
but though this victorious deity easily expel his avowed enemy from the heart of jones he find it more difficult to supplant the garrison which he himself have place there
to lay aside all allegory the concern for what must become of poor molly greatly disturb and perplex the mind of the worthy youth
the superior merit of sophia totally eclipse or rather extinguish all the beauty of the poor girl but compassion instead of contempt succeed to love
he be convince the girl have place all her affection and all her prospect of future happiness in he only
for this he have he know give sufficient occasion by the utmost profusion of tenderness towards she a tenderness which he have take every mean to persuade she he would always maintain
she on her side have assure he of her firm belief in his promise and have with the most solemn vow declare that on his fulfilling or break these promise it depend whether she should be the happy or most miserable of womankind
and to be the author of this high degree of misery to a human being be a thought on which he could not bear to ruminate a single moment
he consider this poor girl as have sacrifice to he everything in her little power as have be at her own expense the object of his pleasure as sigh and languish for he even at that very instant
shall then say he my recovery for which she hath so ardently wish shall my presence which she hath so eagerly expect instead of give she that joy with which she hath flatter herself cast she at once down into misery and despair
can i be such a villain
here when the genius of poor molly seem triumphant the love of sophia towards he which now appear no long dubious rush upon his mind and bear away every obstacle before it
at length it occur to he that he might possibly be able to make molly amend another way namely by give she a sum of money
this nevertheless he almost despair of she accept when he recollect the frequent and vehement assurance he have receive from she that the world put in balance with he would make she no amend for his loss
however her extreme poverty and chiefly her egregious vanity somewhat of which hath be already hint to the reader give he some little hope that notwithstanding all her avow tenderness she might in time be bring to content herself with a fortune superior to her expectation and which might indulge her vanity by set she above all her equal
he resolve therefore to take the first opportunity of make a proposal of this kind
one day accordingly when his arm be so well recover that he could walk easily with it sling in a sash he steal forth at a season when the squire be engage in his field exercise and visit his fair one
her mother and sister whom he find take their tea inform he first that molly be not at home but afterwards the eld sister acquaint he with a malicious smile that she be above stair a-bed
tom have no objection to this situation of his mistress and immediately ascend the ladder which lead towards her bed-chamber but when he come to the top he to his great surprize find the door fast nor could he for some time obtain any answer from within for molly as she herself afterwards inform he be fast asleep
the extreme of grief and joy have be remark to produce very similar effect and when either of these rush on we by surprize it be apt to create such a total perturbation and confusion that we be often thereby deprive of the use of all our faculty
it cannot therefore be wonder at that the unexpected sight of mr jones should so strongly operate on the mind of molly and should overwhelm she with such confusion that for some minute she be unable to express the great rapture with which the reader will suppose she be affect on this occasion
as for jones he be so entirely possess and as it be enchant by the presence of his beloved object that he for a while forget sophia and consequently the principal purpose of his visit
this however soon recur to his memory and after the first transport of their meeting be over he find mean by degree to introduce a discourse on the fatal consequence which must attend their amour if mr allworthy who have strictly forbid he ever see she more should discover that he still carry on this commerce
such a discovery which his enemy give he reason to think would be unavoidable must he say end in his ruin and consequently in her
since therefore their hard fate have determine that they must separate he advise she to bear it with resolution and swear he would never omit any opportunity through the course of his life of show she the sincerity of his affection by provide for she in a manner beyond her utmost expectation or even beyond her wish if ever that should be in his power conclude at last that she might soon find some man who would marry she and who would make her much happy than she could be by lead a disreputable life with he
molly remain a few moment in silence and then burst into a flood of tear she begin to upbraid he in the following word and this be your love for i to forsake i in this manner now you have ruin i
how often when i have tell you that all man be false and perjury alike and grow tired of we as soon as ever they have have their wicked will of we how often have you swear you would never forsake i
and can you be such a perjury man after all
what signify all the rich in the world to i without you now you have gain my heart so you have--you have--
why do you mention another man to i
i can never love any other man as long as i live
all other man be nothing to i
if the great squire in all the country would come a suiting to i to-morrow i would not give my company to he
no i shall always hate and despise the whole sex for your sake she be proceed thus when an accident put a stop to her tongue before it have run out half its career
the room or rather garret in which molly lay be up one pair of stair that be to say at the top of the house be of a sloping figure resemble the great delta of the greeks
the english reader may perhaps form a well idea of it by be tell that it be impossible to stand upright anywhere but in the middle
now as this room want the conveniency of a closet molly have to supply that defect nail up an old rug against the rafter of the house which enclose a little hole where her good apparel such as the remain of that sack which we have formerly mention some cap and other thing with which she have lately provide herself be hang up and secure from the dust
this enclose place exactly front the foot of the bed to which indeed the rug hang so near that it serve in a manner to supply the want of curtain
now whether molly in the agony of her rage push this rug with her foot or jones might touch it or whether the pin or nail give way of its own accord i be not certain but as molly pronounce those last word which be record above the wicked rug get loose from its fastening and discover everything hide behind it where among other female utensil appeared-- with shame i write it and with sorrow will it be read the philosopher square in a posture for the place would not near admit his stand upright as ridiculous as can possibly be conceive
the posture indeed in which he stand be not greatly unlike that of a soldier who be tie neck and heel or rather resemble the attitude in which we often see fellow in the public street of london who be not suffer but deserve punishment by so stand
he have a nightcap belong to molly on his head and his two large eye the moment the rug fall star directly at jones so that when the idea of philosophy be add to the figure now discover it would have be very difficult for any spectator to have refrain from immoderate laughter
i question not but the surprize of the reader will be here equal to that of jones as the suspicion which must arise from the appearance of this wise and grave man in such a place may seem so inconsistent with that character which he hath doubtless maintain hitherto in the opinion of every one
but to confess the truth this inconsistency be rather imaginary than real
philosopher be compose of flesh and blood as well as other human creature and however sublimate and refine the theory of these may be a little practical frailty be as incident to they as to other mortal
it be indeed in theory only and not in practice as we have before hint that consist the difference for though such great being think much well and more wisely they always act exactly like other man
they know very well how to subdue all appetite and passion and to despise both pain and pleasure and this knowledge afford much delightful contemplation and be easily acquire but the practice would be vexatious and troublesome and therefore the same wisdom which teach they to know this teach they to avoid carry it into execution
mr square happen to be at church on that sunday when as the reader may be pleased to remember the appearance of molly in her sack have cause all that disturbance
here he first observe she and be so pleased with her beauty that he prevail with the young gentleman to change their intended ride that evening that he might pass by the habitation of molly and by that mean might obtain a second chance of see she
this reason however as he do not at that time mention to any so neither do we think proper to communicate it then to the reader
among other particular which constitute the unfitness of thing in mr square s opinion danger and difficulty be two
the difficulty therefore which he apprehend there might be in corrupt this young wench and the danger which would accrue to his character on the discovery be such strong dissuasive that it be probable he at first intend to have content himself with the pleasing idea which the sight of beauty furnish we with
these the grave man after a full meal of serious meditation often allow themselves by way of dessert for which purpose certain book and picture find their way into the most private recess of their study and a certain liquorish part of natural philosophy be often the principal subject of their conversation
but when the philosopher hear a day or two afterwards that the fortress of virtue have already be subdue he begin to give a large scope to his desire
his appetite be not of that squeamish kind which cannot feed on a dainty because another hath taste it
in short he like the girl the well for the want of that chastity which if she have possess it must have be a bar to his pleasure he pursue and obtain she
the reader will be mistake if he think molly give square the preference to her young lover on the contrary have she be confine to the choice of one only tom jones would undoubtedly have be of the two the victorious person
nor be it solely the consideration that two be well than one though this have its proper weight to which mr square owe his success the absence of jones during his confinement be an unlucky circumstance and in that interval some well-choose present from the philosopher so soften and unguarde the girl s heart that a favourable opportunity become irresistible and square triumph over the poor remain of virtue which subsist in the bosom of molly
it be now about a fortnight since this conquest when jones pay the above-mention visit to his mistress at a time when she and square be in bed together
this be the true reason why the mother deny she as we have see for as the old woman share in the profit arise from the iniquity of her daughter she encourage and protect she in it to the utmost of her power but such be the envy and hatred which the eld sister bear towards molly that notwithstanding she have some part of the booty she would willingly have part with this to ruin her sister and spoil her trade
hence she have acquaint jones with she be above-stair in bed in hope that he might have catch she in square s arm
this however molly find mean to prevent as the door be fasten which give she an opportunity of convey her lover behind that rug or blanket where he now be unhappily discover
square no soon make his appearance than molly fling herself back in her bed cry out she be undo and abandon herself to despair
this poor girl who be yet but a novice in her business have not arrive to that perfection of assurance which help off a town lady in any extremity and either prompt she with an excuse or else inspire she to brazen out the matter with her husband who from love of quiet or out of fear of his reputation--and sometimes perhaps from fear of the gallant who like mr constant in the play wear a sword--be glad to shut his eye and content to put his horn in his pocket
molly on the contrary be silence by this evidence and very fairly give up a cause which she have hitherto maintain with so many tear and with such solemn and vehement protestation of the pure love and constancy
as to the gentleman behind the arra he be not in much less consternation
he stand for a while motionless and seem equally at a loss what to say or whither to direct his eye
jones though perhaps the most astonished of the three first find his tongue and be immediately recover from those uneasy sensation which molly by her upbraiding have occasion he burst into a loud laughter and then salute mr square advance to take he by the hand and to relieve he from his place of confinement
square be now arrive in the middle of the room in which part only he could stand upright look at jones with a very grave countenance and say to he well sir i see you enjoy this mighty discovery and i dare swear take great delight in the thought of expose i but if you will consider the matter fairly you will find you be yourself only to blame
i be not guilty of corrupt innocence
i have do nothing for which that part of the world which judge of matter by the rule of right will condemn i
fitness be govern by the nature of thing and not by custom form or municipal law
nothing be indeed unfit which be not unnatural well reason old boy answer jones but why dost thou think that i should desire to expose thee
i promise thee i be never well pleased with thee in my life and unless thou hast a mind to discover it thyself this affair may remain a profound secret for i nay mr jones reply square i would not be think to undervalue reputation
good fame be a species of the kalon and it be by no mean fitting to neglect it
besides to murder one s own reputation be a kind of suicide a detestable and odious vice
if you think proper therefore to conceal any infirmity of mine for such i may have since no man be perfectly perfect i promise you i will not betray myself
thing may be fitting to be do which be not fitting to be boast of for by the perverse judgment of the world that often become the subject of censure which be in truth not only innocent but laudable right cry jones what can be more innocent than the indulgence of a natural appetite
or what more laudable than the propagation of our species to be serious with you answer square i profess they always appear so to i and yet say jones you be of a different opinion when my affair with this girl be first discover why i must confess say square as the matter be misrepresent to i by that parson thwackum i might condemn the corruption of innocence it be that sir it be that--and that-- for you must know mr jones in the consideration of fitness very minute circumstance sir very minute circumstance cause great alteration well cry jones be that as it will it shall be your own fault as i have promise you if you ever hear any more of this adventure
behave kindly to the girl and i will never open my lip concern the matter to any one
and molly do you be faithful to your friend and i will not only forgive your infidelity to i but will do you all the service i can so say he take a hasty leave and slip down the ladder retire with much expedition
square be rejoice to find this adventure be likely to have no bad conclusion and as for molly be recover from her confusion she begin at first to upbraid square with have be the occasion of her loss of jones but that gentleman soon find the mean of mitigate her anger partly by caress and partly by a small nostrum from his purse of wonderful and approve efficacy in purge off the ill humour of the mind and in restore it to a good temper
she then pour forth a vast profusion of tenderness towards her new lover turn all she have say to jones and jones himself into ridicule and vow though he once have the possession of her person that none but square have ever be master of her heart
chapter vi
by compare which with the former the reader may possibly correct some abuse which he hath formerly be guilty of in the application of the word love
the infidelity of molly which jones have now discover would perhaps have vindicate a much great degree of resentment than he express on the occasion and if he have abandon she directly from that moment very few i believe would have blame he
certain however it be that he see she in the light of compassion and though his love to she be not of that kind which could give he any great uneasiness at her inconstancy yet be he not a little shocked on reflect that he have himself originally corrupt her innocence for to this corruption he impute all the vice into which she appear now so likely to plunge herself
this consideration give he no little uneasiness till betty the eld sister be so kind some time afterwards entirely to cure he by a hint that one will barnes and not himself have be the first seducer of molly and that the little child which he have hitherto so certainly conclude to be his own might very probably have an equal title at least to claim barnes for its father
jones eagerly pursue this scent when he have first receive it and in a very short time be sufficiently assure that the girl have tell he truth not only by the confession of the fellow but at last by that of molly herself
this will barnes be a country gallant and have acquire as many trophy of this kind as any ensign or attorney s clerk in the kingdom
he have indeed reduce several woman to a state of utter profligacy have break the heart of some and have the honour of occasion the violent death of one poor girl who have either drown herself or what be rather more probable have be drown by he
among other of his conquest this fellow have triumph over the heart of betty seagrim
he have make love to she long before molly be grow to be a fit object of that pastime but have afterwards desert she and apply to her sister with whom he have almost immediate success
now will have in reality the sole possession of molly s affection while jones and square be almost equally sacrifice to her interest and to her pride
hence have grow that implacable hatred which we have before see rag in the mind of betty though we do not think it necessary to assign this cause soon as envy itself alone be adequate to all the effect we have mention
jones be become perfectly easy by possession of this secret with regard to molly but as to sophia he be far from be in a state of tranquillity nay indeed he be under the most violent perturbation his heart be now if i may use the metaphor entirely evacuate and sophia take absolute possession of it
he love she with an unbounded passion and plainly see the tender sentiment she have for he yet could not this assurance lessen his despair of obtain the consent of her father nor the horror which attend his pursuit of she by any base or treacherous method
the injury which he must thus do to mr western and the concern which would accrue to mr allworthy be circumstance that torment he all day and haunt he on his pillow at night
his life be a constant struggle between honour and inclination which alternately triumph over each other in his mind
he often resolve in the absence of sophia to leave her father s house and to see her no more and as often in her presence forget all those resolution and determine to pursue she at the hazard of his life and at the forfeiture of what be much dearer to he
this conflict begin soon to produce very strong and visible effect for he lose all his usual sprightliness and gaiety of temper and become not only melancholy when alone but dejected and absent in company nay if ever he put on a forced mirth to comply with mr western s humour the constraint appear so plain that he seem to have be give the strong evidence of what he endeavour to conceal by such ostentation
it may perhaps be a question whether the art which he use to conceal his passion or the mean which honest nature employ to reveal it betray he most for while art make he more than ever reserve to sophia and forbad he to address any of his discourse to she nay to avoid meet her eye with the utmost caution nature be no less busy in counterplot he
hence at the approach of the young lady he grow pale and if this be sudden start
if his eye accidentally meet her the blood rush into his cheek and his countenance become all over scarlet
if common civility ever oblige he to speak to she as to drink her health at table his tongue be sure to falter
if he touch she his hand nay his whole frame tremble
and if any discourse tend however remotely to raise the idea of love an involuntary sigh seldom fail to steal from his bosom
most of which accident nature be wonderfully industrious to throw daily in his way
all these symptom escape the notice of the squire but not so of sophia
she soon perceive these agitation of mind in jones and be at no loss to discover the cause for indeed she recognize it in her own breast
and this recognition be i suppose that sympathy which hath be so often note in lover and which will sufficiently account for she be so much quick-sighted than her father
but to say the truth there be a more simple and plain method of accounting for that prodigious superiority of penetration which we must observe in some man over the rest of the human specie and one which will serve not only in the case of lover but of all other
from whence be it that the knave be generally so quick-sighted to those symptom and operation of knavery which often dupe an honest man of a much well understanding
there surely be no general sympathy among knave nor have they like freemason any common sign of communication
in reality it be only because they have the same thing in their head and their thought be turn the same way
thus that sophia see and that western do not see the plain symptom of love in jones can be no wonder when we consider that the idea of love never enter into the head of the father whereas the daughter at present think of nothing else
when sophia be well satisfied of the violent passion which torment poor jones and no less certain that she herself be its object she have not the least difficulty in discover the true cause of his present behaviour
this highly endear he to she and raise in her mind two of the good affection which any lover can wish to raise in a mistress--these be esteem and pity--for sure the most outrageously rigid among her sex will excuse she pity a man whom she see miserable on her own account nor can they blame she for esteem one who visibly from the most honourable motif endeavour to smother a flame in his own bosom which like the famous spartan theft be prey upon and consume his very vital
thus his backwardness his shun she his coldness and his silence be the forward the most diligent the warm and most eloquent advocate and wrought so violently on her sensible and tender heart that she soon feel for he all those gentle sensation which be consistent with a virtuous and elevated female mind
in short all which esteem gratitude and pity can inspire in such towards an agreeable man--indeed all which the nice delicacy can allow
in a word she be in love with he to distraction
one day this young couple accidentally meet in the garden at the end of the two walk which be both bound by that canal in which jones have formerly risque drown to retrieve the little bird that sophia have there lose
this place have be of late much frequent by sophia
here she use to ruminate with a mixture of pain and pleasure on an incident which however trifle in itself have possibly sow the first seed of that affection which be now arrive to such maturity in her heart
here then this young couple meet
they be almost close together before either of they know anything of the other s approach
a bystander would have discover sufficient mark of confusion in the countenance of each but they feel too much themselves to make any observation
as soon as jones have a little recover his first surprize he accost the young lady with some of the ordinary form of salutation which she in the same manner return and their conversation begin as usual on the delicious beauty of the morning
hence they past to the beauty of the place on which jones launch forth very high encomium
when they come to the tree whence he have formerly tumble into the canal sophia could not help remind he of that accident and say i fancy mr jones you have some little shuddering when you see that water i assure you madam answer jones the concern you feel at the loss of your little bird will always appear to i the high circumstance in that adventure
poor little tommy
there be the branch he stand upon
how could the little wretch have the folly to fly away from that state of happiness in which i have the honour to place he
his fate be a just punishment for his ingratitude upon my word mr jones say she your gallantry very narrowly escape as severe a fate
sure the remembrance must affect you indeed madam answer he if i have any reason to reflect with sorrow on it it be perhaps that the water have not be a little deep by which i might have escape many bitter heart-ache that fortune seem to have in store for i fie mr jones reply sophia i be sure you cannot be in earnest now
this affected contempt of life be only an excess of your complacence to i
you would endeavour to lessen the obligation of have twice venture it for my sake
beware the third time she speak these last word with a smile and a softness inexpressible
jones answer with a sigh he fear it be already too late for caution and then look tenderly and stedfastly on she he cry oh miss western
can you desire i to live
can you wish i so ill sophia look down on the ground answer with some hesitation indeed mr jones i do not wish you ill oh i know too well that heavenly temper cry jones that divine goodness which be beyond every other charm nay now answer she i understand you not
i can stay no long i--i would not be understand cry he nay i cannot be understand
i know not what i say
meet you here so unexpectedly i have be unguarde for heaven s sake pardon i if i have say anything to offend you
i do not mean it
indeed i would rather have die--nay the very thought would kill i you surprize i answer she
how can you possibly think you have offend me fear madam say he easily run into madness and there be no degree of fear like that which i feel of offend you
how can i speak then
nay donot look angrily at i one frown will destroy i
i mean nothing
blame my eye or blame those beauty
what be i say
pardon i if i have say too much
my heart overflow
i have struggle with my love to the utmost and have endeavour to conceal a fever which prey on my vital and will i hope soon make it impossible for i ever to offend you more mr jones now fall a trembling as if he have be shake with the fit of an ague
sophia who be in a situation not very different from his answer in these word mr jones i will not affect to misunderstand you indeed i understand you too well but for heaven s sake if you have any affection for i let i make the good of my way into the house
i wish i may be able to support myself thither jones who be hardly able to support himself offer she his arm which she condescend to accept but beg he would not mention a word more to she of this nature at present
he promise he would not insist only on her forgiveness of what love without the leave of his will have force from he this she tell he he know how to obtain by his future behaviour and thus this young pair totter and tremble along the lover not once dare to squeeze the hand of his mistress though it be lock in his
sophia immediately retire to her chamber where mrs honour and the hartshorn be summon to her assistance
as to poor jones the only relief to his distempered mind be an unwelcome piece of news which as it open a scene of different nature from those in which the reader hath lately be conversant will be communicate to he in the next chapter
chapter vii
in which mr allworthy appear on a sick-bed
mr western be become so fond of jones that he be unwilling to part with he though his arm have be long since cure and jones either from the love of sport or from some other reason be easily persuade to continue at his house which he do sometimes for a fortnight together without pay a single visit at mr allworthy s nay without ever hear from thence
mr allworthy have be for some day indispose with a cold which have be attend with a little fever
this he have however neglect as it be usual with he to do all manner of disorder which do not confine he to his bed or prevent his several faculty from perform their ordinary functions a conduct which we would by no mean be think to approve or recommend to imitation for surely the gentleman of the aesculapian art be in the right in advise that the moment the disease have enter at one door the physician should be introduce at the other what else be mean by that old adage venienti occurrite morbo oppose a distemper at its first approach thus the doctor and the disease meet in fair and equal conflict whereas by give time to the latter we often suffer he to fortify and entrench himself like a french army so that the learn gentleman find it very difficult and sometimes impossible to come at the enemy
nay sometimes by gain time the disease apply to the french military politic and corrupt nature over to his side and then all the power of physic must arrive too late
agreeable to these observation be i remember the complaint of the great doctor misaubin who use very pathetically to lament the late application which be make to his skill say bygar i believe my pation take i for de undertaker for dey never send for i till de physicion have kill dem mr allworthy s distemper by mean of this neglect gain such ground that when the increase of his fever oblige he to send for assistance the doctor at his first arrival shake his head wish he have be send for soon and intimate that he think he in very imminent danger
mr allworthy who have settle all his affair in this world and be as well prepared as it be possible for human nature to be for the other receive this information with the utmost calmness and unconcern
he could indeed whenever he lay himself down to rest say with cato in the tragical poem-- let guilt or fear disturb man s rest cato know neither of they indifferent in his choice to sleep or die
in reality he could say this with ten time more reason and confidence than cato or any other proud fellow among the antient or modern hero for he be not only devoid of fear but might be consider as a faithful labourer when at the end of harvest he be summon to receive his reward at the hand of a bountiful master
the good man give immediate order for all his family to be summon round he
none of these be then abroad but mrs blifil who have be some time in london and mr jones whom the reader hath just part from at mr western s and who receive this summon just as sophia have leave he
the news of mr allworthy s danger for the servant tell he he be die drive all thought of love out of his head
he hurry instantly into the chariot which be send for he and order the coachman to drive with all imaginable haste nor do the idea of sophia i believe once occur to he on the way
and now the whole family namely mr blifil mr jones mr thwackum mr square and some of the servant for such be mr allworthy s order be all assemble round his bed the good man sit up in it and be begin to speak when blifil fall to blubber and begin to express very loud and bitter lamentation
upon this mr allworthy shake he by the hand and say do not sorrow thus my dear nephew at the most ordinary of all human occurrence
when misfortune befal our friend we be justly grieve for those be accident which might often have be avoid and which may seem to render the lot of one man more peculiarly unhappy than that of other but death be certainly unavoidable and be that common lot in which alone the fortune of all man agree nor be the time when this happen to we very material
if the wise of man hath compare life to a span surely we may be allow to consider it as a day
it be my fate to leave it in the evening but those who be take away early have only lose a few hour at the good little worth lamenting and much oftener hour of labour and fatigue of pain and sorrow
one of the roman poet i remember liken our leave life to our departure from a feast a thought which hath often occur to i when i have see man struggle to protract an entertainment and to enjoy the company of their friend a few moment long
alas
how short be the most protract of such enjoyment
how immaterial the difference between he who retire the soon and he who stay the late
this be see life in the good view and this unwillingness to quit our friend be the most amiable motive from which we can derive the fear of death and yet the long enjoyment which we can hope for of this kind be of so trivial a duration that it be to a wise man truly contemptible
few man i own think in this manner for indeed few man think of death till they be in its jaw
however gigantic and terrible an object this may appear when it approach they they be nevertheless incapable of see it at any distance nay though they have be ever so much alarmed and frighten when they have apprehend themselves in danger of die they be no soon clear from this apprehension than even the fear of it be erase from their mind
but alas
he who escape from death be not pardon he be only reprieve and reprieve to a short day
grieve therefore no more my dear child on this occasion an event which may happen every hour which every element nay almost every particle of matter that surround we be capable of produce and which must and will most unavoidably reach we all at last ought neither to occasion our surprize nor our lamentation
my physician have acquaint i which i take very kindly of he that i be in danger of leave you all very shortly i have determine to say a few word to you at this our parting before my distemper which i find grow very fast upon i put it out of my power
but i shall waste my strength too much
i intend to speak concern my will which though i have settle long ago i think proper to mention such head of it as concern any of you that i may have the comfort of perceive you be all satisfied with the provision i have there make for you
nephew blifil i leave you the heir to my whole estate except only 500 a-year which be to revert to you after the death of your mother and except one other estate of 500 a-year and the sum of 6000 which i have bestow in the following manner the estate of 500 a-year i have give to you mr jones and as i know the inconvenience which attend the want of ready money i have add 1000 in specie
in this i know not whether i have exceed or fall short of your expectation
perhaps you will think i have give you too little and the world will be as ready to condemn i for give you too much but the latter censure i despise and as to the former unless you should entertain that common error which i have often hear in my life plead as an excuse for a total want of charity namely that instead of raise gratitude by voluntary act of bounty we be apt to raise demand which of all other be the most boundless and most difficult to satisfy pardon i the bare mention of this i will not suspect any such thing jones fling himself at his benefactor s foot and take eagerly hold of his hand assure he his goodness to he both now and all other time have so infinitely exceed not only his merit but his hope that no word could express his sense of it
and i assure you sir say he your present generosity hath leave i no other concern than for the present melancholy occasion
oh my friend my father here his word choake he and he turn away to hide a tear which be start from his eye
allworthy then gently squeeze his hand and proceed thus i be convince my child that you have much goodness generosity and honour in your temper if you will add prudence and religion to these you must be happy for the three former quality i admit make you worthy of happiness but they be the latter only which will put you in possession of it
one thousand pound i have give to you mr thwackum a sum i be convince which greatly exceed your desire as well as your want
however you will receive it as a memorial of my friendship and whatever superfluity may redound to you that piety which you so rigidly maintain will instruct you how to dispose of they
a like sum mr square i have bequeath to you
this i hope will enable you to pursue your profession with well success than hitherto
i have often observe with concern that distress be more apt to excite contempt than commiseration especially among man of business with whom poverty be understand to indicate want of ability
but the little i have be able to leave you will extricate you from those difficulty with which you have formerly struggle and then i doubt not but you will meet with sufficient prosperity to supply what a man of your philosophical temper will require
i find myself grow faint so i shall refer you to my will for my disposition of the residue
my servant will there find some token to remember i by and there be a few charity which i trust my executor will see faithfully perform
bless you all
i be set out a little before you here a footman come hastily into the room and say there be an attorney from salisbury who have a particular message which he say he must communicate to mr allworthy himself that he seem in a violent hurry and protest he have so much business to do that if he could cut himself into four quarter all would not be sufficient
go child say allworthy to blifil see what the gentleman want
i be not able to do any business now nor can he have any with i in which you be not at present more concerned than myself
besides i really be--i be incapable of see any one at present or of any long attention he then salute they all say perhaps he should be able to see they again but he should be now glad to compose himself a little find that he have too much exhausted his spirit in discourse
some of the company shed tear at their parting and even the philosopher square wipe his eye albeit unused to the melt mood
as to mrs wilkins she dropt her pearl as fast as the arabian tree their medicinal gum for this be a ceremonial which that gentlewoman never omit on a proper occasion
after this mr allworthy again lay himself down on his pillow and endeavour to compose himself to rest
chapter viii
contain matter rather natural than pleasing
besides grief for her master there be another source for that briny stream which so plentifully rise above the two mountainous cheek-bone of the housekeeper
she be no soon retire than she begin to mutter to herself in the follow pleasant strain sure master might have make some difference methink between i and the other servant
i suppose he hath leave i mourning but i fackins
if that be all the devil shall wear it for he for i
iwould have his worship know i be no beggar
i have save five hundred pound in his service and after all to be use in this manner it be a fine encouragement to servant to be honest and to be sure if i have take a little something now and then other have take ten time as much and now we be all put in a lump together
if so be that it be so the legacy may go to the devil with he that give it
no i willnot give it up neither because that will please some folk
no iwill buy the gay gown i can get and dance over the old curmudgeon s grave in it
this be my reward for take his part so often when all the country have cry shame of he for breed up his bastard in that manner but he be go now where he must pay for all
it would have become he well to have repent of his sin on his deathbed than to glory in they and give away his estate out of his own family to a misbegotten child
find in his bed forsooth
a pretty story
ay ay those that hide know where to find
lord forgive he
i warrant he hath many more bastard to answer for if the truth be know
one comfort be they will all be know where he be a go now the servant will find some token to remember i by those be the very word i shall never forget they if i be to live a thousand year
ay ay i shall remember you for huddle i among the servant
one would have think he might have mention my name as well as that of square but he be a gentleman forsooth though he have not cloth on his back when he come hither first
marry come up with such gentleman
though he hath live here this many year i donot believe there be arrow a servant in the house ever see the colour of his money
the devil shall wait upon such a gentleman for i much more of the like kind she mutter to herself but this taste shall suffice to the reader
neither thwackum nor square be much well satisfied with their legacy
though they breathe not their resentment so loud yet from the discontent which appear in their countenance as well as from the follow dialogue we collect that no great pleasure reign in their mind
about an hour after they have leave the sick-room square meet thwackum in the hall and accost he thus well sir have you hear any news of your friend since we part from him if you mean mr allworthy answer thwackum i think you might rather give he the appellation of your friend for he seem to i to have deserve that title the title be as good on your side reply square for his bounty such as it be hath be equal to both i should not have mention it first cry thwackum but since you begin i must inform you i be of a different opinion
there be a wide distinction between voluntary favour and reward
the duty i have do in his family and the care i have take in the education of his two boy be service for which some man might have expect a great return
i would not have you imagine i be therefore dissatisfied for st paul hath teach i to be content with the little i have
have the modicum be less i should have know my duty
but though the scripture oblige i to remain contented it doth not enjoin i to shut my eye to my own merit nor restrain i from see when i be injure by an unjust comparison since you provoke i return square that injury be do to i nor do i ever imagine mr allworthy have hold my friendship so light as to put i in balance with one who receive his wage
i know to what it be owe it proceed from those narrow principle which you have be so long endeavour to infuse into he in contempt of everything which be great and noble
the beauty and loveliness of friendship be too strong for dim eye nor can it be perceive by any other medium than that unerre rule of right which you have so often endeavour to ridicule that you have pervert your friend s understanding i wish cry thwackum in a rage i wish for the sake of his soul your damnable doctrine have not pervert his faith
it be to this i impute his present behaviour so unbecome a christian
who but an atheist could think of leave the world without have first make up his account
without confess his sin and receive that absolution which he know he have one in the house duly authorize to give he
he will feel the want of these necessary when it be too late when he be arrive at that place where there be wail and gnashing of tooth
it be then he will find in what mighty stead that heathen goddess that virtue which you and all other deist of the age adore will stand he
he will then summon his priest when there be none to be find and will lament the want of that absolution without which no sinner can be safe if it be so material say square why donot you present it he of your own accord it hath no virtue cry thwackum but to those who have sufficient grace to require it
but why do i talk thus to a heathen and an unbeliever
it be you that teach he this lesson for which you have be well reward in this world as i doubt not your disciple will soon be in the other i know not what you mean by reward say square but if you hint at that pitiful memorial of our friendship which he hath think fit to bequeath i i despise it and nothing but the unfortunate situation of my circumstance should prevail on i to accept it the physician now arrive and begin to inquire of the two disputant how we all do above-stair
in a miserable way answer thwackum
it be no more than i expect cry the doctor but pray what symptom have appear since i leave you no good one i be afraid reply thwackum after what past at our departure i think there be little hope the bodily physician perhaps misunderstood the curer of soul and before they come to an explanation mr blifil come to they with a most melancholy countenance and acquaint they that he bring sad news that his mother be dead at salisbury that she have be seize on the road home with the gout in her head and stomach which have carry she off in a few hour
good-lack-a-day say the doctor
one cannot answer for event but i wish i have be at hand to have be call in
the gout be a distemper which it be difficult to treat yet i have be remarkably successful in it thwackum and square both condole with mr blifil for the loss of his mother which the one advise he to bear like a man and the other like a christian
the young gentleman say he know very well we be all mortal and he would endeavour to submit to his loss as well as he could
that he could not however help complain a little against the peculiar severity of his fate which bring the news of so great a calamity to he by surprize and that at a time when he hourly expect the severe blow he be capable of feeling from the malice of fortune
he say the present occasion would put to the test those excellent rudiment which he have learn from mr thwackum and mr square and it would be entirely owe to they if he be enable to survive such misfortune
it be now debate whether mr allworthy should be inform of the death of his sister
this the doctor violently oppose in which i believe the whole college would agree with he but mr blifil say he have receive such positive and repeat order from his uncle never to keep any secret from he for fear of the disquietude which it might give he that he durst not think of disobedience whatever might be the consequence
he say for his part consider the religious and philosophic temper of his uncle he could not agree with the doctor in his apprehension
he be therefore resolve to communicate it to he for if his uncle recover as he heartily pray he might he know he would never forgive an endeavour to keep a secret of this kind from he
the physician be force to submit to these resolution which the two other learn gentleman very highly commend
so together move mr blifil and the doctor toward the sick-room where the physician first enter and approach the bed in order to feel his patient s pulse which he have no soon do than he declare he be much well that the last application have succeed to a miracle and have bring the fever to intermit so that he say there appear now to be as little danger as he have before apprehend there be hope
to say the truth mr allworthy s situation have never be so bad as the great caution of the doctor have represent it but as a wise general never despise his enemy however inferior that enemy s force may be so neither doth a wise physician ever despise a distemper however inconsiderable
as the former preserve the same strict discipline place the same guard and employ the same scout though the enemy be never so weak so the latter maintain the same gravity of countenance and shake his head with the same significant air let the distemper be never so trifling
and both among many other good one may assign this solid reason for their conduct that by these mean the great glory redound to they if they gain the victory and the less disgrace if by any unlucky accident they should happen to be conquer
mr allworthy have no soon lift up his eye and thank heaven for these hope of his recovery than mr blifil draw near with a very dejected aspect and have apply his handkerchief to his eye either to wipe away his tear or to do as ovid somewhere express himself on another occasion si nullus erit tamen excute nullum if there be none then wipe away that none he communicate to his uncle what the reader hath be just before acquaint with
allworthy receive the news with concern with patience and with resignation
he dropt a tender tear then compose his countenance and at last cry the lord s will be do in everything he now enquire for the messenger but blifil tell he it have be impossible to detain he a moment for he appear by the great hurry he be in to have some business of importance on his hand that he complain of be hurry and drive and tear out of his life and repeat many time that if he could divide himself into four quarter he know how to dispose of every one
allworthy then desire blifil to take care of the funeral
he say he would have his sister deposit in his own chapel and as to the particular he leave they to his own discretion only mention the person whom he would have employ on this occasion
chapter ix
which among other thing may serve as a comment on that saying of aeschines that drunkenness show the mind of a man as a mirrour reflect his person the reader may perhaps wonder at hear nothing of mr jones in the last chapter
in fact his behaviour be so different from that of the person there mention that we choose not to confound his name with theirs
when the good man have end his speech jones be the last who desert the room
thence he retire to his own apartment to give vent to his concern but the restlessness of his mind would not suffer he to remain long there he slip softly therefore to allworthy s chamber-door where he listen a considerable time without hear any kind of motion within unless a violent snoring which at last his fear misrepresent as groan
this so alarm he that he could not forbear enter the room where he find the good man in the bed in a sweet compose sleep and his nurse snore in the above mention hearty manner at the bed s foot
he immediately take the only method of silence this thorough bass whose music he fear might disturb mr allworthy and then sit down by the nurse he remain motionless till blifil and the doctor come in together and wake the sick man in order that the doctor might feel his pulse and that the other might communicate to he that piece of news which have jones be apprize of it would have have great difficulty of find its way to mr allworthy s ear at such a season
when he first hear blifil tell his uncle this story jones could hardly contain the wrath which kindle in he at the other s indiscretion especially as the doctor shake his head and declare his unwillingness to have the matter mention to his patient
but as his passion do not so far deprive he of all use of his understanding as to hide from he the consequence which any violent expression towards blifil might have on the sick this apprehension still his rage at the present and he grow afterwards so satisfied with find that this news have in fact produce no mischief that he suffer his anger to die in his own bosom without ever mention it to blifil
the physician din that day at mr allworthy s and have after dinner visit his patient he return to the company and tell they that he have now the satisfaction to say with assurance that his patient be out of all danger that he have bring his fever to a perfect intermission and doubt not by throw in the bark to prevent its return
this account so please jones and throw he into such immoderate excess of rapture that he might be truly say to be drunk with joy--an intoxication which greatly forward the effect of wine and as he be very free too with the bottle on this occasion for he drink many bumper to the doctor s health as well as to other toast he become very soon literally drunk
jones have naturally violent animal spirit these be set on float and augment by the spirit of wine produce most extravagant effect
he kiss the doctor and embrace he with the most passionate endearment swear that next to mr allworthy himself he love he of all man live
doctor add he you deserve a statue to be erect to you at the public expense for have preserve a man who be not only the darling of all good man who know he but a blessing to society the glory of his country and an honour to human nature
d--n i if i donot love he well than my own soul more shame for you cry thwackum
though i think you have reason to love he for he hath provide very well for you
and perhaps it might have be well for some folk that he have not live to see just reason of revoke his gift jones now look on thwackum with inconceivable disdain answer and doth thy mean soul imagine that any such consideration could weigh with i
no let the earth open and swallow her own dirt if i have million of acre i would say it rather than swallow up my dear glorious friend quis desiderio sit pudor aut modus tam chari capitis what modesty or measure can set bound to our desire of so dear a friend the word desiderium here cannot be easily translate
it include our desire of enjoy our friend again and the grief which attend that desire
the doctor now interpose and prevent the effect of a wrath which be kindle between jones and thwackum after which the former give a loose to mirth sing two or three amorous song and fall into every frantic disorder which unbridled joy be apt to inspire but so far be he from any disposition to quarrel that he be ten time well humour if possible than when he be sober
to say truth nothing be more erroneous than the common observation that man who be ill-natured and quarrelsome when they be drunk be very worthy person when they be sober for drink in reality doth not reverse nature or create passion in man which do not exist in they before
it take away the guard of reason and consequently force we to produce those symptom which many when sober have art enough to conceal
it heighten and inflame our passion generally indeed that passion which be uppermost in our mind so that the angry temper the amorous the generous the good-humour the avaricious and all other disposition of man be in their cup heighten and expose
and yet as no nation produce so many drunken quarrel especially among the low people as england for indeed with they to drink and to fight together be almost synonymous term i would not methink have it thence conclude that the english be the worst-natured people alive
perhaps the love of glory only be at the bottom of this so that the fair conclusion seem to be that our countryman have more of that love and more of bravery than any other plebeian
and this the rather as there be seldom anything ungenerous unfair or ill-natured exercise on these occasion nay it be common for the combatant to express good-will for each other even at the time of the conflict and as their drunken mirth generally end in a battle so do most of their battle end in friendship
but to return to our history
though jones have show no design of give offence yet mr blifil be highly offended at a behaviour which be so inconsistent with the sober and prudent reserve of his own temper
he bear it too with the great impatience as it appear to he very indecent at this season when as he say the house be a house of mourning on the account of his dear mother and if it have please heaven to give he some prospect of mr allworthy s recovery it would become they well to express the exultation of their heart in thanksgiving than in drunkenness and riot which be properer method to encrease the divine wrath than to avert it thwackum who have swallow more liquor than jones but without any ill effect on his brain second the pious harangue of blifil but square for reason which the reader may probably guess be totally silent
wine have not so totally overpowered jones as to prevent his recollect mr blifil s loss the moment it be mention
