as no person therefore be more ready to confess and condemn his own error he offer to shake mr blifil by the hand and beg his pardon say his excessive joy for mr allworthy s recovery have drive every other thought out of his mind blifil scornfully reject his hand and with much indignation answer it be little to be wonder at if tragical spectacle make no impression on the blind but for his part he have the misfortune to know who his parent be and consequently must be affect with their loss jones who notwithstanding his good humour have some mixture of the irascible in his constitution leap hastily from his chair and catch hold of blifil s collar cry out d--n you for a rascal do you insult i with the misfortune of my birth he accompany these word with such rough action that they soon get the well of mr blifil s peaceful temper and a scuffle immediately ensue which might have produce mischief have it not be prevent by the interposition of thwackum and the physician for the philosophy of square render he superior to all emotion and he very calmly smoake his pipe as be his custom in all broil unless when he apprehend some danger of have it break in his mouth
the combatant be now prevent from execute present vengeance on each other betook themselves to the common resource of disappointed rage and vent their wrath in threat and defiance
in this kind of conflict fortune which in the personal attack seem to incline to jones be now altogether as favourable to his enemy
a truce nevertheless be at length agree on by the mediation of the neutral party and the whole company again sit down at the table where jones be prevail on to ask pardon and blifil to give it peace be restore and everything seem in statu quo
but though the quarrel be in all appearance perfectly reconcile the good humour which have be interrupt by it be by no means restore
all merriment be now at an end and the subsequent discourse consist only of grave relation of matter of fact and of as grave observation upon they a species of conversation in which though there be much of dignity and instruction there be but little entertainment
as we presume therefore to convey only this last to the reader we shall pass by whatever be say till the rest of the company have by degree drop off leave only square and the physician together at which time the conversation be a little heighten by some comment on what have happen between the two young gentleman both of whom the doctor declare to be no well than scoundrel to which appellation the philosopher very sagaciously shake his head agree
chapter x
show the truth of many observation of ovid and of other more grave writer who have prove beyond contradiction that wine be often the forerunner of incontinency
jones retire from the company in which we have see he engage into the field where he intend to cool himself by a walk in the open air before he attend mr allworthy

it be now a pleasant evening in the latter end of june when our heroe be walk in a most delicious grove where the gentle breeze fan the leaf together with the sweet trilling of a murmuring stream and the melodious note of nightingale form altogether the most enchanting harmony

some of my reader may be incline to think this event unnatural
however the fact be true and perhaps may be sufficiently account for by suggest that jones probably think one woman well than none and molly as probably imagine two man to be well than one
besides the before-mention motive assign to the present behaviour of jones the reader will be likewise pleased to recollect in his favour that he be not at this time perfect master of that wonderful power of reason which so well enable grave and wise man to subdue their unruly passion and to decline any of these prohibit amusement
wine now have totally subdue this power in jones
he be indeed in a condition in which if reason have interpose though only to advise she might have receive the answer which one cleostratus give many year ago to a silly fellow who ask he if he be not ashamed to be drunk
be not you say cleostratus ashamed to admonish a drunken man to say the truth in a court of justice drunkenness must not be an excuse yet in a court of conscience it be greatly so and therefore aristotle who commend the law of pittacus by which drunken man receive double punishment for their crime allow there be more of policy than justice in that law
now if there be any transgression pardonable from drunkenness they be certainly such as mr jones be at present guilty of on which head i could pour forth a vast profusion of learn if i imagine it would either entertain my reader or teach he anything more than he know already
for his sake therefore i shall keep my learning to myself and return to my history
it hath be observe that fortune seldom doth thing by half
to say truth there be no end to her freak whenever she be disposed to gratify or displease

to which blifil answer he be certain he have see a fellow and wench retire together among the bush which he doubt not be with some wicked purpose as to the name of jones he think proper to conceal it and why he do so must be leave to the judgment of the sagacious reader for we never chuse to assign motif to the action of man when there be any possibility of our being mistaken
the parson who be not only strictly chaste in his own person but a great enemy to the opposite vice in all other fire at this information
he desire mr blifil to conduct he immediately to the place which as he approach he breathe forth vengeance mix with lamentation nor do he refrain from cast some oblique reflection on mr allworthy insinuate that the wickedness of the country be principally owe to the encouragement he have give to vice by have exert such kindness to a bastard and by have mitigate that just and wholesome rigour of the law which allot a very severe punishment to loose wench
the way through which our hunter be to pass in pursuit of their game be so beset with briar that it greatly obstruct their walk and cause besides such a rustling that jones have sufficient warning of their arrival before they could surprize he nay indeed so incapable be thwackum of conceal his indignation and such vengeance do he mutter forth every step he take that this alone must have abundantly satisfied jones that he be to use the language of sportsman find sit
chapter xi
in which a simile in mr pope s period of a mile introduce as bloody a battle as can possibly be fight without the assistance of steel or cold iron
as in the season of rut an uncouth phrase by which the vulgar denote that gentle dalliance which in the well-woode forest of hampshire pass between lover of the ferine kind if while the lofty-crest stag meditate the amorous sport a couple of puppy or any other beast of hostile note should wander so near the temple of venus ferina that the fair hind should shrink from the place touch with that somewhat either of fear or frolic of nicety or skittishness with which nature hath bedeck all female or hath at least instruct they how to put it on lest through the indelicacy of male the samean mystery should be pry into by unhallowed eye for at the celebration of these rite the female priestess cry out with she in virgil who be then probably hard at work on such celebration procul o procul este profani proclamat vate totoque absistite luco far hence be soul profane the sibyl cry d and from the grove abstain dryden
this be an ambiguous phrase and may mean either a forest well cloathe with wood or well stript of it

the relation of the master and scholar be indelible as indeed all other relation be for they all derive their original from heaven
i would have you think yourself therefore as much oblige to obey i now as when i teach you your first rudiment i believe you would cry jones but that will not happen unless you have the same birchen argument to convince i then i must tell you plainly say thwackum i be resolve to discover the wicked wretch and i must tell you plainly return jones i be resolve you shall not thwackum then offer to advance and jones lay hold of his arm which mr blifil endeavour to rescue declare he would not see his old master insult jones now find himself engage with two think it necessary to rid himself of one of his antagonist as soon as possible

this parson have be a champion in his youth and have win much honour by his fist both at school and at the university
he have now indeed for a great number of year decline the practice of that noble art yet be his courage full as strong as his faith and his body no less strong than either
he be moreover as the reader may perhaps have conceive somewhat irascible in his nature
when he look back therefore and see his friend stretch out on the ground and find himself at the same time so roughly handle by one who have formerly be only passive in all conflict between they a circumstance which highly aggravate the whole his patience at length give way he throw himself into a posture of offence and collect all his force attack jones in the front with as much impetuosity as he have formerly attack he in the rear
our heroe receive the enemy s attack with the most undaunted intrepidity and his bosom resound with the blow
this he presently return with no less violence aim likewise at the parson s breast but he dexterously drive down the fist of jones so that it reach only his belly where two pound of beef and as many of pudding be then deposit and whence consequently no hollow sound could proceed
many lusty blow much more pleasant as well as easy to have see than to read or describe be give on both side at last a violent fall in which jones have throw his knee into thwackum s breast so weaken the latter that victory have be no long dubious have not blifil who have now recover his strength again renew the fight and by engage with jones give the parson a moment s time to shake his ear and to regain his breath
and now both together attack our heroe whose blow do not retain that force with which they have fall at first so weakened be he by his combat with thwackum for though the pedagogue choose rather to play solo on the human instrument and have be lately use to those only yet he still retain enough of his antient knowledge to perform his part very well in a duet
the victory accord to modern custom be like to be decide by number when on a sudden a fourth pair of fist appear in the battle and immediately pay their compliment to the parson and the owner of they at the same time cry out be not you ashamed and be d--n d to you to fall two of you upon one the battle which be of the kind that for distinction s sake be call royal now rag with the utmost violence during a few minute till blifil be a second time lay sprawl by jones thwackum condescend to apply for quarter to his new antagonist who be now find to be mr western himself for in the heat of the action none of the combatant have recognize he
in fact that honest squire happen in his afternoon s walk with some company to pass through the field where the bloody battle be fight and have conclude from see three man engage that two of they must be on a side he hasten from his companion and with more gallantry than policy espouse the cause of the weak party
by which generous proceeding he very probably prevent mr jones from become a victim to the wrath of thwackum and to the pious friendship which blifil bear his old master for besides the disadvantage of such odd jones have not yet sufficiently recover the former strength of his broken arm
this reinforcement however soon put an end to the action and jones with his ally obtain the victory

in which be see a more moving spectacle than all the blood in the body of thwackum and blifil and of twenty other such be capable of produce

an effect however widely different for so soft and balmy be it that have all his former blow be stab it would for some minute have prevent his feeling their smart
the company now move backwards and soon arrive where thwackum have get mr blifil again on his leg
here we cannot suppress a pious wish that all quarrel be to be decide by those weapon only with which nature know what be proper for we hath supply we and that cold iron be to be use in dig no bowel but those of the earth
then would war the pastime of monarchs be almost inoffensive and battle between great army might be fight at the particular desire of several lady of quality who together with the king themselves might be actual spectator of the conflict
then might the field be this moment well strew with human carcass and the next the dead man or infinitely the great part of they might get up like mr bayes s troop and march off either at the sound of a drum or fiddle as should be previously agree on
i would avoid if possible treat this matter ludicrously lest grave man and politician whom i know to be offend at a jest may cry pish at it but in reality might not a battle be as well decide by the great number of broken head bloody nose and black eye as by the great heap of mangle and murder human body
might not town be contend for in the same manner
indeed this may be think too detrimental a scheme to the french interest since they would thus lose the advantage they have over other nation in the superiority of their engineer but when i consider the gallantry and generosity of that people i be persuade they would never decline put themselves upon a par with their adversary or as the phrase be make themselves his match
but such reformation be rather to be wish than hop for i shall content myself therefore with this short hint and return to my narrative

book vi
contain about three weeks
chapter i
of love
in our last book we have be oblige to deal pretty much with the passion of love and in our succeed book shall be force to handle this subject still more largely
it may not therefore in this place be improper to apply ourselves to the examination of that modern doctrine by which certain philosopher among many other wonderful discovery pretend to have find out that there be no such passion in the human breast
whether these philosopher be the same with that surprising sect who be honourably mention by the late dr swift as have by the mere force of genius alone without the least assistance of any kind of learning or even read discover that profound and invaluable secret that there be no god or whether they be not rather the same with those who some year since very much alarm the world by show that there be no such thing as virtue or goodness really exist in human nature and who deduce our good action from pride i will not here presume to determine
in reality i be inclined to suspect that all these several finder of truth be the very identical man who be by other call the finder of gold
the method use in both these search after truth and after gold be indeed one and the same viz the searching rummaging and examine into a nasty place indeed in the former instance into the nasty of all place a bad mind
but though in this particular and perhaps in their success the truth-finder and the gold-finder may very properly be compare together yet in modesty surely there can be no comparison between the two for who ever hear of a gold-finder that have the impudence or folly to assert from the ill success of his search that there be no such thing as gold in the world
whereas the truth-finder have rake out that jake his own mind and be there capable of trace no ray of divinity nor anything virtuous or good or lovely or loving very fairly honestly and logically conclude that no such thing exist in the whole creation
to avoid however all contention if possible with these philosopher if they will be call so and to show our own disposition to accommodate matter peaceably between we we shall here make they some concession which may possibly put an end to the dispute
first we will grant that many mind and perhaps those of the philosopher be entirely free from the least trace of such a passion
secondly that what be commonly call love namely the desire of satisfy a voracious appetite with a certain quantity of delicate white human flesh be by no mean that passion for which i here contend
this be indeed more properly hunger and as no glutton be ashamed to apply the word love to his appetite and to say he love such and such dish so may the lover of this kind with equal propriety say he hunger after such and such woman
thirdly i will grant which i believe will be a most acceptable concession that this love for which i be an advocate though it satisfy itself in a much more delicate manner doth nevertheless seek its own satisfaction as much as the gross of all our appetite
and lastly that this love when it operate towards one of a different sex be very apt towards its complete gratification to call in the aid of that hunger which i have mention above and which it be so far from abate that it heighten all its delight to a degree scarce imaginable by those who have never be susceptible of any other emotion than what have proceed from appetite alone
in return to all these concession i desire of the philosopher to grant that there be in some i believe in many human breast a kind and benevolent disposition which be gratify by contribute to the happiness of other
that in this gratification alone as in friendship in parental and filial affection as indeed in general philanthropy there be a great and exquisite delight
that if we will not call such disposition love we have no name for it
that though the pleasure arise from such pure love may be heighten and sweeten by the assistance of amorous desire yet the former can subsist alone nor be they destroy by the intervention of the latter
lastly that esteem and gratitude be the proper motif to love as youth and beauty be to desire and therefore though such desire may naturally cease when age or sickness overtake its object yet these can have no effect on love nor ever shake or remove from a good mind that sensation or passion which hath gratitude and esteem for its basis
to deny the existence of a passion of which we often see manif instance seem to be very strange and absurd and can indeed proceed only from that self-admonition which we have mention above but how unfair be this
doth the man who recognize in his own heart no trace of avarice or ambition conclude therefore that there be no such passion in human nature
why will we not modestly observe the same rule in judging of the good as well as the evil of other
or why in any case will we as shakespear phrase it put the world in our own person predominant vanity be i be afraid too much concerned here
this be one instance of that adulation which we bestow on our own mind and this almost universally
for there be scarce any man how much soever he may despise the character of a flatterer but will condescend in the mean manner to flatter himself
to those therefore i apply for the truth of the above observation whose own mind can bear testimony to what i have advance
examine your heart my good reader and resolve whether you do believe these matter with i
if you do you may now proceed to their exemplification in the follow page if you do not you have i assure you already read more than you have understand and it would be wise to pursue your business or your pleasure such as they be than to throw away any more of your time in read what you can neither taste nor comprehend
to treat of the effect of love to you must be as absurd as to discourse on colour to a man bear blind since possibly your idea of love may be as absurd as that which we be tell such blind man once entertain of the colour scarlet that colour seem to he to be very much like the sound of a trumpet and love probably may in your opinion very greatly resemble a dish of soup or a surloin of roast-beef
chapter ii
the character of mrs western
her great learning and knowledge of the world and an instance of the deep penetration which she derive from those advantage
the reader hath see mr western his sister and daughter with young jones and the parson go together to mr western s house where the great part of the company spend the evening with much joy and festivity
sophia be indeed the only grave person for as to jones though love have now get entire possession of his heart yet the pleasing reflection on mr allworthy s recovery and the presence of his mistress join to some tender look which she now and then could not refrain from give he so elevate our heroe that he join the mirth of the other three who be perhaps as good-humour people as any in the world
sophia retain the same gravity of countenance the next morning at breakfast whence she retire likewise early than usual leave she father and aunt together
the squire take no notice of this change in his daughter s disposition
to say the truth though he be somewhat of a politician and have be twice a candidate in the country interest at an election he be a man of no great observation
his sister be a lady of a different turn
she have live about the court and have see the world
hence she have acquire all that knowledge which the say world usually communicate and be a perfect mistress of manner custom ceremony and fashion
nor do her erudition stop here
she have considerably improve her mind by study she have not only read all the modern play opera oratorio poem and romance--in all which she be a critic but have go through rapin s history of england eachard s roman history and many french mémoire pour servir à l histoire to these she have add most of the political pamphlet and journal publish within the last twenty year
from which she have attain a very competent skill in politic and could discourse very learnedly on the affair of europe
she be moreover excellently well skilled in the doctrine of amour and know well than anybody who and who be together a knowledge which she the more easily attain as her pursuit of it be never divert by any affair of her own for either she have no inclination or they have never be solicit which last be indeed very probable for her masculine person which be near six foot high add to her manner and learning possibly prevent the other sex from regard she notwithstanding her petticoat in the light of a woman
however as she have consider the matter scientifically she perfectly well know though she have never practise they all the art which fine lady use when they desire to give encouragement or to conceal liking with all the long appendage of smile ogle glance c as they be at present practise in the beau-monde
to sum the whole no specie of disguise or affectation have escape her notice but as to the plain simple working of honest nature as she have never see any such she could know but little of they
by mean of this wonderful sagacity mrs western have now as she think make a discovery of something in the mind of sophia
the first hint of this she take from the behaviour of the young lady in the field of battle and the suspicion which she then conceive be greatly corroborate by some observation which she have make that evening and the next morning
however be greatly cautious to avoid be find in a mistake she carry the secret a whole fortnight in her bosom give only some oblique hint by simper wink nod and now and then drop an obscure word which indeed sufficiently alarm sophia but do not at all affect her brother
be at length however thoroughly satisfied of the truth of her observation she take an opportunity one morning when she be alone with her brother to interrupt one of his whistle in the follow manner pray brother have you not observe something very extraordinary in my niece lately no not i answer western be anything the matter with the girl i think there be reply she and something of much consequence too why she doth not complain of anything cry western and she hath have the small-pox brother return she girl be liable to other distemper besides the small-pox and sometimes possibly to much bad here western interrupt she with much earnestness and beg she if anything ail his daughter to acquaint he immediately add she know he love she more than his own soul and that he would send to the world s end for the good physician to she nay nay answer she smile the distemper be not so terrible but i believe brother you be convince i know the world and i promise you i be never more deceive in my life if my niece be not most desperately in love how
in love cry western in a passion in love without acquaint i
iwill disinherit she iwill turn she out of door stark naked without a farthing
be all my kindness vor ur and vondness o ur come to this to fall in love without ask i leave but you will not answer mrs western turn this daughter whom you love well than your own soul out of door before you know whether you shall approve her choice
suppose she should have fix on the very person whom you yourself would wish i hope you would not be angry then no no cry western that would make a difference
if she marry the man i would ha she she may love whom she please i shallnot trouble my head about that that be speak answer the sister like a sensible man but i believe the very person she hath choose would be the very person you would choose for she
i will disclaim all knowledge of the world if it be not so and i believe brother you will allow i have some why lookee sister say western i do believe you have as much as any woman and to be sure those be woman s matter
you know i donot love to hear you talk about politic they belong to we and petticoat should not meddle but come who be the man marry say she you may find he out yourself if you please
you who be so great a politician can be at no great loss
the judgment which can penetrate into the cabinet of prince and discover the secret spring which move the great state wheel in all the political machine of europe must surely with very little difficulty find out what pass in the rude uninformed mind of a girl sister cry the squire i have often warn d you not to talk the court gibberish to i
i tell you i donot understand the lingo but i can read a journal or the london evening post perhaps indeed there may be now and tan a verse which i cannot make much of because half the letter be leave out yet i know very well what be mean by that and that our affair donot go so well as they should do because of bribery and corruption i pity your country ignorance from my heart cry the lady do you answer western and i pity your town learning i have rather be anything than a courtier and a presbyterian and a hanoverian too as some people i believe be if you mean i answer she you know i be a woman brother and it signify nothing what i be
besides-- i do know you be a woman cry the squire and itbe well for thee that art one if hadst be a man i promise thee i have lend thee a flick long ago ay there say she in that flick lie all your fancied superiority
your body and not your brain be strong than our
believe i it be well for you that you be able to beat we or such be the superiority of our understanding we should make all of you what the brave and wise and witty and polite be already--our slave i be glad i know your mind answer the squire
but wewill talk more of this matter another time
at present do tell i what man be it you mean about my daughter hold a moment say she while i digest that sovereign contempt i have for your sex or else i ought to be angry too with you
there--i have make a shift to gulp it down
and now good politic sir what think you of mr blifil
do she not faint away on see he lie breathless on the ground
do she not after he be recover turn pale again the moment we come up to that part of the field where he stand
and pray what else should be the occasion of all her melancholy that night at supper the next morning and indeed ever since fore george cry the squire now you mind i on t i remember it all
it be certainly so and i be glad on t with all my heart
i know sophy be a good girl and would not fall in love to make i angry
i be never more rejoice in my life for nothing can lie so handy together as our two estate
i have this matter in my head some time ago for certainly the two estate be in a manner join together in matrimony already and it would be a thousand pity to part they
it be true indeed there be large estate in the kingdom but not in this county and i have rather bate something than marry my daughter among stranger and foreigner
besides most o zuch great estate be in the hand of lord and i heate the very name of themmun
well but sister what would you advise i to do for i tell you woman know these matter well than we do oh your humble servant sir answer the lady we be oblige to you for allow we a capacity in anything
since you be pleased then most politic sir to ask my advice i think you may propose the match to allworthy yourself
there be no indecorum in the proposalbe come from the parent of either side
king alcinous in mr pope s odyssey offer his daughter to ulysses
i need not caution so politic a person not to say that your daughter be in love that would indeed be against all rule well say the squire i will propose it but i shall certainly lend un a flick if he should refuse i fear not cry mrs western the match be too advantageous to be refuse i donot know that answer the squire allworthy be a queer b--ch and money hath no effect o un brother say the lady your politic astonish i
be you really to be impose on by profession
do you think mr allworthy hath more contempt for money than other man because he profess more
such credulity would well become one of we weak woman than that wise sex which heaven hath form for politician
indeed brother you would make a fine plenipo to negotiate with the french
they would soon persuade you that they take town out of mere defensive principle sister answer the squire with much scorn let your friend at court answer for the town take as you be a woman i shall lay no blame upon you for i suppose they be wise than to trust woman with secret he accompany this with so sarcastical a laugh that mrs western could bear no long
she have be all this time fret in a tender part for she be indeed very deeply skilled in these matter and very violent in they and therefore burst forth in a rage declare she brother to be both a clown and a blockhead and that she would stay no long in his house
the squire though perhaps he have never read machiavel be however in many point a perfect politician
he strongly hold all those wise tenet which be so well inculcated in that politico-peripatetic school of exchange-alley
he know the just value and only use of money viz to lay it up
he be likewise well skilled in the exact value of reversion expectation c and have often consider the amount of his sister s fortune and the chance which he or his posterity have of inherit it
this he be infinitely too wise to sacrifice to a trifling resentment
when he find therefore he have carry matter too far he begin to think of reconcile they which be no very difficult task as the lady have great affection for her brother and still great for her niece and though too susceptible of an affront offer to her skill in politic on which she much value herself be a woman of a very extraordinary good and sweet disposition
have first therefore lay violent hand on the horse for whose escape from the stable no place but the window be leave open he next apply himself to his sister soften and soothe she by unsaying all he have say and by assertion directly contrary to those which have incense she
lastly he summon the eloquence of sophia to his assistance who besides a most graceful and win address have the advantage of be hear with great favour and partiality by her aunt
the result of the whole be a kind smile from mrs western who say brother you be absolutely a perfect croat but as those have their use in the army of the empress queen so you likewise have some good in you
i will therefore once more sign a treaty of peace with you and see that you do not infringe it on your side at least as you be so excellent a politician i may expect you will keep your league like the french till your interest call upon you to break they chapter iii
contain two defiance to the critic
the squire have settle matter with his sister as we have see in the last chapter be so greatly impatient to communicate the proposal to allworthy that mrs western have the utmost difficulty to prevent he from visit that gentleman in his sickness for this purpose
mr allworthy have be engage to dine with mr western at the time when he be take ill
he be therefore no soon discharge out of the custody of physic but he think as be usual with he on all occasion both the high and the low of fulfil his engagement
in the interval between the time of the dialogue in the last chapter and this day of public entertainment sophia have from certain obscure hint throw out by her aunt collect some apprehension that the sagacious lady suspect her passion for jones
she now resolve to take this opportunity of wipe out all such suspicion and for that purpose to put an entire constraint on her behaviour
first she endeavour to conceal a throb melancholy heart with the utmost sprightliness in her countenance and the high gaiety in her manner
secondly she address her whole discourse to mr blifil and take not the least notice of poor jones the whole day
the squire be so delighted with this conduct of his daughter that he scarce eat any dinner and spend almost his whole time in watch opportunity of convey sign of his approbation by wink and nod to his sister who be not at first altogether so pleased with what she see as be her brother
in short sophia so greatly overact her part that her aunt be at first stagger and begin to suspect some affectation in her niece but as she be herself a woman of great art so she soon attribute this to extreme art in sophia
she remember the many hint she have give her niece concern she be in love and imagine the young lady have take this way to rally she out of her opinion by an overact civility a notion that be greatly corroborate by the excessive gaiety with which the whole be accompany
we cannot here avoid remark that this conjecture would have be well found have sophia live ten year in the air of grosvenor square where young lady do learn a wonderful knack of rallying and play with that passion which be a mighty serious thing in wood and grove an hundred mile distant from london
to say the truth in discover the deceit of other it matter much that our own art be wind up if i may use the expression in the same key with theirs for very artful man sometimes miscarry by fancy other wiser or in other word great knave than they really be
as this observation be pretty deep i will illustrate it by the follow short story
three countryman be pursue a wiltshire thief through brentford
the simple of they see the wiltshire house write under a sign advise his companion to enter it for there most probably they would find their countryman
the second who be wise laugh at this simplicity but the third who be wise still answer let we go in however for he may think we should not suspect he of go amongst his own countryman they accordingly go in and search the house and by that mean miss overtake the thief who be at that time but a little way before they and who as they all know but have never once reflect could not read
the reader will pardon a digression in which so invaluable a secret be communicate since every gamester will agree how necessary it be to know exactly the play of another in order to countermine he
this will moreover afford a reason why the wise man as be often see be the bubble of the weak and why many simple and innocent character be so generally misunderstood and misrepresent but what be most material this will account for the deceit which sophia put on her politic aunt
dinner be end and the company retire into the garden mr western who be thoroughly convinced of the certainty of what his sister have tell he take mr allworthy aside and very bluntly propose a match between sophia and young mr blifil
mr allworthy be not one of those man whose heart flutter at any unexpected and sudden tiding of worldly profit
his mind be indeed temper with that philosophy which become a man and a christian
he affect no absolute superiority to all pleasure and pain to all joy and grief but be not at the same time to be discompose and ruffle by every accidental blast by every smile or frown of fortune
he receive therefore mr western s proposal without any visible emotion or without any alteration of countenance
he say the alliance be such as he sincerely wish then launch forth into a very just encomium on the young lady s merit acknowledge the offer to be advantageous in point of fortune and after thank mr western for the good opinion he have profess of his nephew conclude that if the young people like each other he should be very desirous to complete the affair
western be a little disappointed at mr allworthy s answer which be not so warm as he expect
he treat the doubt whether the young people might like one another with great contempt say that parent be the good judge of proper match for their child that for his part he should insist on the most resign obedience from his daughter and if any young fellow could refuse such a bed-fellow he be his humble servant and hop there be no harm do allworthy endeavour to soften this resentment by many eulogium on sophia declare he have no doubt but that mr blifil would very gladly receive the offer but all be ineffectual he could obtain no other answer from the squire but-- i say no more--i humbly hope therebe no harm do--thatbe all which word he repeat at least a hundred time before they part
allworthy be too well acquainted with his neighbour to be offend at this behaviour and though he be so averse to the rigour which some parent exercise on their child in the article of marriage that he have resolve never to force his nephew s inclination he be nevertheless much pleased with the prospect of this union for the whole country resound the praise of sophia and he have himself greatly admire the uncommon endowment of both her mind and person
to which i believe we may add the consideration of her vast fortune which though he be too sober to be intoxicate with it he be too sensible to despise
and here in defiance of all the bark critic in the world i must and will introduce a digression concern true wisdom of which mr allworthy be in reality as great a pattern as he be of goodness
true wisdom then notwithstanding all which mr hogarth s poor poet may have writ against rich and in spite of all which any rich well-feed divine may have preach against pleasure consist not in the contempt of either of these
a man may have as much wisdom in the possession of an affluent fortune as any beggar in the street or may enjoy a handsome wife or a hearty friend and still remain as wise as any sour popish recluse who bury all his social faculty and starve his belly while he well lash his back
to say truth the wise man be the likeli to possess all worldly blessing in an eminent degree for as that moderation which wisdom prescribe be the sure way to useful wealth so can it alone qualify we to taste many pleasure
the wise man gratify every appetite and every passion while the fool sacrifice all the rest to pall and satiate one
it may be object that very wise man have be notoriously avaricious
i answer not wise in that instance
it may likewise be say that the wise man have be in their youth immoderately fond of pleasure
i answer they be not wise then
wisdom in short whose lesson have be represent as so hard to learn by those who never be at her school only teach we to extend a simple maxim universally know and follow even in the low life a little farth than that life carry it
and this be not to buy at too dear a price
now whoever take this maxim abroad with he into the grand market of the world and constantly apply it to honour to rich to pleasure and to every other commodity which that market afford be i will venture to affirm a wise man and must be so acknowledge in the worldly sense of the word for he make the good of bargain since in reality he purchase everything at the price only of a little trouble and carry home all the good thing i have mention while he keep his health his innocence and his reputation the common price which be pay for they by other entire and to himself
from this moderation likewise he learn two other lesson which complete his character
first never to be intoxicate when he hath make the good bargain nor deject when the market be empty or when its commodity be too dear for his purchase
but i must remember on what subject i be write and not trespass too far on the patience of a good-natured critic
here therefore i put an end to the chapter
chapter iv
contain sundry curious matter
as soon as mr allworthy return home he take mr blifil apart and after some preface communicate to he the proposal which have be make by mr western and at the same time inform he how agreeable this match would be to himself
the charm of sophia have not make the least impression on blifil not that his heart be pre-engaged neither be he totally insensible of beauty or have any aversion to woman but his appetite be by nature so moderate that he be able by philosophy or by study or by some other method easily to subdue they and as to that passion which we have treat of in the first chapter of this book he have not the least tincture of it in his whole composition
but though he be so entirely free from that mixed passion of which we there treat and of which the virtue and beauty of sophia form so notable an object yet be he altogether as well furnish with some other passion that promise themselves very full gratification in the young lady s fortune
such be avarice and ambition which divide the dominion of his mind between they
he have more than once consider the possession of this fortune as a very desirable thing and have entertain some distant view concern it but his own youth and that of the young lady and indeed principally a reflection that mr western might marry again and have more child have restrain he from too hasty or eager a pursuit
this last and most material objection be now in great measure remove as the proposal come from mr western himself
blifil therefore after a very short hesitation answer mr allworthy that matrimony be a subject on which he have not yet think but that he be so sensible of his friendly and fatherly care that he should in all thing submit himself to his pleasure
allworthy be naturally a man of spirit and his present gravity arise from true wisdom and philosophy not from any original phlegm in his disposition for he have possess much fire in his youth and have marry a beautiful woman for love
he be not therefore greatly pleased with this cold answer of his nephew nor could he help launch forth into the praise of sophia and express some wonder that the heart of a young man could be impregnable to the force of such charm unless it be guard by some prior affection
blifil assure he he have no such guard and then proceed to discourse so wisely and religiously on love and marriage that he would have stopt the mouth of a parent much less devoutly inclined than be his uncle
in the end the good man be satisfied that his nephew far from have any objection to sophia have that esteem for she which in sober and virtuous mind be the sure foundation of friendship and love
and as he doubt not but the lover would in a little time become altogether as agreeable to his mistress he foresaw great happiness arise to all party by so proper and desirable an union
with mr blifil s consent therefore he write the next morning to mr western acquaint he that his nephew have very thankfully and gladly receive the proposal and would be ready to wait on the young lady whenever she should be pleased to accept his visit
western be much pleased with this letter and immediately return an answer in which without have mention a word to his daughter he appoint that very afternoon for open the scene of courtship
as soon as he have dispatch this messenger he go in quest of his sister whom he find read and expound the gazette to parson supple
to this exposition he be oblige to attend near a quarter of an hour though with great violence to his natural impetuosity before he be suffer to speak
at length however he find an opportunity of acquaint the lady that he have business of great consequence to impart to she to which she answer brother i be entirely at your service
thing look so well in the north that i be never in a well humour the parson then withdraw western acquaint she with all which have pass and desire she to communicate the affair to sophia which she readily and chearfully undertake though perhaps her brother be a little oblige to that agreeable northern aspect which have so delight she that he hear no comment on his proceeding for they be certainly somewhat too hasty and violent

it would be bad politic indeed add she to protract a siege when the enemy s army be at hand and in danger of relieve it

chapter vi
contain a dialogue between sophia and mrs honour which may a little relieve those tender affection which the foregoing scene may have raise in the mind of a good-natured reader

i be irretrievably undo heaven forbid answer the waiting-woman but if i cannot be of any service to you pray tell i madam--it will be some comfort to i to know--pray dear ma am tell i whatbe the matter my father cry sophia be go to marry i to a man i both despise and hate o dear ma am answer the other who be this wicked man
for to be sure he be very bad or your la ship would not despise he his name be poison to my tongue reply sophia thou wilt know it too soon indeed to confess the truth she know it already and therefore be not very inquisitive as to that point
she then proceed thus i donot pretend to give your la ship advice whereof your la ship know much well than i can pretend to be but a servant but i-fackin
no father in england should marry i against my consent
and to be sure the squire be so good that if he do but know your la ship despise and hat the young man to be sure he would not desire you to marry he
and if your la ship would but give i leave to tell my master so
to be sure it would be more properer to come from your own mouth but as your la ship doth not care to foul your tongue with his nasty name-- you be mistaken honour say sophia my father be determine before he ever think fit to mention it to i more shame for he cry honour you be to go to bed to he and not master and thof a man may be a very proper man yet every woman maynot think he handsome alike
i be sure my master would never act in this manner of his own head
i wish some people would trouble themselves only with what belong to they they would not i believe like to be serve so if it be their own case for though i be a maid i can easily believe as how all man be not equally agreeable
and what signify your la ship have so great a fortune if you cannot please yourself with the man you think most handsome
well i say nothing but to be sure it be a pity some folk have not be well bear nay as for that matter i should not mind it myself but then there be not so much money and what of that
your la ship hath money enough for both and where can your la ship bestow your fortune well
for to be sure every one must allow that he be the most handsome charmingest fine tall properest man in the world what do you mean by run on in this manner to i cry sophia with a very grave countenance
have i ever give any encouragement for these liberties nay ma am i ask pardon i mean no harm answer she but to be sure the poor gentleman hath run in my head ever since i see he this morning
to be sure if your la ship have but see he just now you must have pity he
poor gentleman
i wish some misfortune hath not happen to he for he hath be walk about with his arm across and look so melancholy all this morning i vow and protest it make i almost cry to see he to see whom say sophia
poor mr jones answer honour
see he
why where do you see he cry sophia
by the canal ma am say honour
there he hath be walk all this morning and at last there he lay himself down i believe he lie there still
to be sure if it have not be for my modesty be a maid as i be i should have go and speak to he

there
no no what should he do there
he be go before this time to be sure
