besides i want you for something else
go fetch i my hat and glove
i shall walk with my aunt in the grove before dinner honour do immediately as she be bid and sophia put her hat on when look in the glass she fancy the ribbon with which her hat be tie do not become she and so send she maid back again for a ribbon of a different colour and then give mrs honour repeat charge not to leave her work on any account as she say it be in violent haste and must be finish that very day she mutter something more about go to the grove and then sally out the contrary way and walk as fast as her tender trembling limb could carry she directly towards the canal
jones have be there as mrs honour have tell she he have indeed spend two hour there that morning in melancholy contemplation on his sophia and have go out from the garden at one door the moment she enter it at another
so that those unlucky minute which have be spend in change the ribbon have prevent the lover from meeting at this time a most unfortunate accident from which my fair reader will not fail to draw a very wholesome lesson
and here i strictly forbid all male critic to intermeddle with a circumstance which i have recount only for the sake of the lady and upon which they only be at liberty to comment
chapter vii
a picture of formal courtship in miniature as it always ought to be draw and a scene of a tenderer kind paint at full length
it be well remark by one and perhaps by more that misfortune do not come single
this wise maxim be now verify by sophia who be not only disappointed of see the man she love but have the vexation of be oblige to dress herself out in order to receive a visit from the man she hat
that afternoon mr western for the first time acquaint his daughter with his intention tell she he know very well that she have hear it before from her aunt
sophia look very grave upon this nor could she prevent a few pearl from steal into her eye
come come say western none of your maidenish air i know all i assure you sister hath tell i all be it possible say sophia that my aunt can have betray i already ay ay say western betray you
ay
why you betray yourself yesterday at dinner
you show your fancy very plainly i think
but you young girl never know what you would be at
so you cry because i be go to marry you to the man you be in love with
your mother i remember whimper and whine just in the same manner but it be all over within twenty-four hour after we be marry mr blifil be a brisk young man and will soon put an end to your squeamishness
come chear up chear up i expect un every minute sophia be now convinced that her aunt have behave honourably to she and she determine to go through that disagreeable afternoon with as much resolution as possible and without give the least suspicion in the world to her father
mr blifil soon arrive and mr western soon after withdraw leave the young couple together
here a long silence of near a quarter of an hour ensue for the gentleman who be to begin the conversation have all the unbecoming modesty which consist in bashfulness
he often attempt to speak and as often suppress his word just at the very point of utterance
at last out they break in a torrent of far-fetched and high-strained compliment which be answer on her side by downcast look half bow and civil monosyllable
blifil from his inexperience in the way of woman and from his conceit of himself take this behaviour for a modest assent to his courtship and when to shorten a scene which she could no long support sophia rise up and leave the room he impute that too merely to bashfulness and comfort himself that he should soon have enough of her company
he be indeed perfectly well satisfied with his prospect of success for as to that entire and absolute possession of the heart of his mistress which romantic lover require the very idea of it never enter his head
her fortune and her person be the sole object of his wish of which he make no doubt soon to obtain the absolute property as mr western s mind be so earnestly bent on the match and as he well know the strict obedience which sophia be always ready to pay to her father s will and the great still which her father would exact if there be occasion
this authority therefore together with the charm which he fancy in his own person and conversation could not fail he think of succeed with a young lady whose inclination be he doubt not entirely disengaged
of jones he certainly have not even the least jealousy and i have often think it wonderful that he have not
perhaps he imagine the character which jones bear all over the country how justly let the reader determine of be one of the wild fellow in england might render he odious to a lady of the most exemplary modesty
perhaps his suspicion might be lay asleep by the behaviour of sophia and of jones himself when they be all in company together
lastly and indeed principally he be well assured there be not another self in the case
he fancy that he know jones to the bottom and have in reality a great contempt for his understanding for not be more attached to his own interest
he have no apprehension that jones be in love with sophia and as for any lucrative motif he imagine they would sway very little with so silly a fellow
blifil moreover think the affair of molly seagrim still go on and indeed believe it would end in marriage for jones really love he from his childhood and have keep no secret from he till his behaviour on the sickness of mr allworthy have entirely alienate his heart and it be by mean of the quarrel which have ensue on this occasion and which be not yet reconcile that mr blifil know nothing of the alteration which have happen in the affection which jones have formerly bear towards molly
from these reason therefore mr blifil see no bar to his success with sophia
he conclude her behaviour be like that of all other young lady on a first visit from a lover and it have indeed entirely answer his expectation
mr western take care to way-lie the lover at his exit from his mistress
he find he so elevated with his success so enamour with his daughter and so satisfied with her reception of he that the old gentleman begin to caper and dance about his hall and by many other antic action to express the extravagance of his joy for he have not the least command over any of his passion and that which have at any time the ascendant in his mind hurry he to the wild excess
as soon as blifil be depart which be not till after many hearty kiss and embrace bestow on he by western the good squire go instantly in quest of his daughter whom he no soon find than he pour forth the most extravagant rapture bid her chuse what clothe and jewel she please and declare that he have no other use for fortune but to make she happy
he then caress she again and again with the utmost profusion of fondness call she by the most endear name and protest she be his only joy on earth
sophia perceive her father in this fit of affection which she do not absolutely know the reason of for fit of fondness be not unusual to he though this be rather more violent than ordinary think she should never have a well opportunity of disclose herself than at present as far at least as regard mr blifil and she too well foresaw the necessity which she should soon be under of come to a full explanation
after have thank the squire therefore for all his profession of kindness she add with a look full of inexpressible softness and be it possible my papa can be so good to place all his joy in his sophy s happiness which western have confirm by a great oath and a kiss she then lay hold of his hand and fall on her knee after many warm and passionate declaration of affection and duty she beg he not to make she the most miserable creature on earth by force she to marry a man whom she detest
this i entreat of you dear sir say she for your sake as well as my own since you be so very kind to tell i your happiness depend on mine how
what say western star wildly
oh
sir continue she not only your poor sophy s happiness her very life her being depend upon your grant she request
i cannot live with mr blifil
to force i into this marriage would be kill i you cannot live with mr blifil say western
no upon my soul i cannot answer sophia
then die and be d--d cry he spurn she from he
oh
sir cry sophia catch hold of the skirt of his coat take pity on i i beseech you
donot look and say such cruel--can you be unmove while you see your sophy in this dreadful condition
can the good of father break my heart
will he kill i by the most painful cruel linger death pooh
pooh cry the squire all stuff and nonsense all maidenish trick
kill you indeed
will marriage kill you oh
sir answer sophia such a marriage be bad than death
he be not even indifferent i hate and det he if you det un never so much cry western you shall ha un this he bind by an oath too shocking to repeat and after many violent asseveration conclude in these word i be resolve upon the match and unless you consent to it i will not give you a groat not a single farthing no though i see you expire with famine in the street i would not relieve you with a morsel of bread
this be my fix resolution and so i leave you to consider on it he then break from she with such violence that her face dash against the floor and he burst directly out of the room leave poor sophia prostrate on the ground
when western come into the hall he there find jones who see his friend look wild pale and almost breathless could not forbear enquire the reason of all these melancholy appearance
upon which the squire immediately acquaint he with the whole matter conclude with bitter denunciation against sophia and very pathetic lamentation of the misery of all father who be so unfortunate to have daughter
jones to whom all the resolution which have be take in favour of blifil be yet a secret be at first almost strike dead with this relation but recover his spirit a little mere despair as he afterwards say inspire he to mention a matter to mr western which seem to require more impudence than a human forehead be ever gift with
he desire leave to go to sophia that he might endeavour to obtain her concurrence with her father s inclination
if the squire have be as quicksighte as he be remarkable for the contrary passion might at present very well have blind he
he thank jones for offer to undertake the office and say go go prithee try what canst do and then swear many execrable oath that he would turn she out of door unless she consent to the match
chapter viii
the meeting between jones and sophia
jones depart instantly in quest of sophia whom he find just rise from the ground where her father have leave she with the tear trickle from her eye and the blood run from her lip
he presently run to she and with a voice full at once of tenderness and terrour cry o my sophia what mean this dreadful sight she look softly at he for a moment before she speak and then say mr jones for heaven s sake how come you here leave i i beseech you this moment do not say he impose so harsh a command upon i--my heart bleed fast than those lip
o sophia how easily could i drain my vein to preserve one drop of that dear blood i have too many obligation to you already answer she for sure you mean they such here she look at he tenderly almost a minute and then burst into an agony cry oh mr jones why do you save my life
my death would have be happy for we both happi for we both cry he
could rack or wheel kill i so painfully as sophia s--i cannot bear the dreadful sound
do i live but for she both his voice and look be full of inexpressible tenderness when he speak these word and at the same time he lay gently hold on her hand which she do not withdraw from he to say the truth she hardly know what she do or suffer
a few moment now pass in silence between these lover while his eye be eagerly fix on sophia and her decline towards the ground at last she recover strength enough to desire he again to leave she for that her certain ruin would be the consequence of their be find together add oh mr jones you know not you know not what hath pass this cruel afternoon i know all my sophia answer he your cruel father hath tell i all and he himself hath send i hither to you my father send you to i reply she sure you dream would to heaven cry he it be but a dream
oh sophia your father hath send i to you to be an advocate for my odious rival to solicit you in his favour
i take any mean to get access to you
o speak to i sophia
comfort my bleed heart
sure no one ever love ever doate like i
do not unkindly withhold this dear this soft this gentle hand--one moment perhaps tear you for ever from i--nothing less than this cruel occasion could i believe have ever conquer the respect and awe with which you have inspire i she stand a moment silent and cover with confusion then lift up her eye gently towards he she cry what would mr jones have i say o do but promise cry he that you never will give yourself to blifil name not answer she the detest sound
be assure i never will give he what be in my power to withhold from he now then cry he while you be so perfectly kind go a little far and add that i may hope alas say she mr jones whither will you drive i
what hope have i to bestow
you know my father s intention but i know answer he your compliance with they cannot be compel what say she must be the dreadful consequence of my disobedience
my own ruin be my least concern
i cannot bear the thought of be the cause of my father s misery he be himself the cause cry jones by exact a power over you which nature hath not give he
think on the misery which i be to suffer if i be to lose you and see on which side pity will turn the balance think of it reply she can you imagine i do not feel the ruin which i must bring on you should i comply with your desire
it be that thought which give i resolution to bid you fly from i for ever and avoid your own destruction i fear no destruction cry he but the loss of sophia
if you would save i from the most bitter agony recall that cruel sentence
indeed i can never part with you indeed i cannot the lover now stand both silent and trembling sophia be unable to withdraw her hand from jones and he almost as unable to hold it when the scene which i believe some of my reader will think have last long enough be interrupt by one of so different a nature that we shall reserve the relation of it for a different chapter
chapter ix
be of a much more tempestuous kind than the former
before we proceed with what now happen to our lover it may be proper to recount what have past in the hall during their tender interview
soon after jones have leave mr western in the manner above mention his sister come to he and be presently inform of all that have pass between her brother and sophia relate to blifil
this behaviour in her niece the good lady construe to be an absolute breach of the condition on which she have engage to keep her love for mr jones a secret
she consider herself therefore at full liberty to reveal all she know to the squire which she immediately do in the most explicit term and without any ceremony or preface
the idea of a marriage between jones and his daughter have never once enter into the squire s head either in the warm minute of his affection towards that young man or from suspicion or on any other occasion
he do indeed consider a parity of fortune and circumstance to be physically as necessary an ingredient in marriage as difference of sex or any other essential and have no more apprehension of his daughter s fall in love with a poor man than with any animal of a different specie
he become therefore like one thunderstruck at his sister s relation
he be at first incapable of make any answer have be almost deprive of his breath by the violence of the surprize
this however soon return and as be usual in other case after an intermission with redouble force and fury
the first use he make of the power of speech after his recovery from the sudden effect of his astonishment be to discharge a round volley of oath and imprecation
after which he proceed hastily to the apartment where he expect to find the lover and murmur or rather indeed roar forth intention of revenge every step he go
as when two dove or two wood-pigeon or as when strephon and phyllis for that come near to the mark be retire into some pleasant solitary grove to enjoy the delightful conversation of love that bashful boy who cannot speak in public and be never a good companion to more than two at a time here while every object be serene should hoarse thunder burst suddenly through the shatter cloud and rumble roll along the sky the frightened maid start from the mossy bank or verdant turf the pale livery of death succeed the red regimental in which love have before dr her cheek fear shake her whole frame and her lover scarce support her trembling tottering limb
or as when two gentleman stranger to the wondrous wit of the place be crack a bottle together at some inn or tavern at salisbury if the great dowdy who act the part of a madman as well as some of his setter-on do that of a fool should rattle his chain and dreadfully hum forth the grumble catch along the gallery the fright stranger stand aghast scar at the horrid sound they seek some place of shelter from the approach danger and if the well-bar window do admit their exit would venture their neck to escape the threaten fury now come upon they
so tremble poor sophia so turn she pale at the noise of her father who in a voice most dreadful to hear come on swearing cursing and vow the destruction of jones
to say the truth i believe the youth himself would from some prudent consideration have prefer another place of abode at this time have his terror on sophia s account give he liberty to reflect a moment on what any otherway concern himself than as his love make he partake whatever affect she
and now the squire have burst open the door behold an object which instantly suspend all his fury against jones this be the ghastly appearance of sophia who have faint away in her lover s arm
this tragical sight mr western no soon beheld than all his rage forsook he he roar for help with his utmost violence run first to his daughter then back to the door call for water and then back again to sophia never consider in whose arm she then be nor perhaps once recollect that there be such a person in the world as jones for indeed i believe the present circumstance of his daughter be now the sole consideration which employ his thought
mrs western and a great number of servant soon come to the assistance of sophia with water cordial and everything necessary on those occasion
these be apply with such success that sophia in a very few minute begin to recover and all the symptom of life to return
upon which she be presently lead off by her own maid and mrs western nor do that good lady depart without leave some wholesome admonition with her brother on the dreadful effect of his passion or as she please to call it madness
the squire perhaps do not understand this good advice as it be deliver in obscure hint shrug and note of admiration at least if he do understand it he profit very little by it for no soon be he cure of his immediate fear for his daughter than he relapse into his former frenzy which must have produce an immediate battle with jones have not parson supple who be a very strong man be present and by mere force restrain the squire from act of hostility
the moment sophia be depart jones advance in a very suppliant manner to mr western whom the parson hold in his arm and beg he to be pacify for that while he continue in such a passion it would be impossible to give he any satisfaction
i wull have satisfaction o thee answer the squire so doff thy clothe
at unt half a man and iwill lick thee as well as wast ever lick in thy life he then bespatter the youth with abundance of that language which pass between country gentleman who embrace opposite side of the question with frequent application to he to salute that part which be generally introduce into all controversy that arise among the low order of the english gentry at horse-race cock-match and other public place
allusion to this part be likewise often make for the sake of the jest
and here i believe the wit be generally misunderstood
in reality it lie in desire another to kiss your a-- for have just before threaten to kick his for i have observe very accurately that no one ever desire you to kick that which belong to himself nor offer to kiss this part in another
it may likewise seem surprize that in the many thousand kind invitation of this sort which every one who hath converse with country gentleman must have hear no one i believe hath ever see a single instance where the desire hath be comply with a great instance of their want of politeness for in town nothing can be more common than for the fine gentleman to perform this ceremony every day to their superior without have that favour once request of they
to all such wit jones very calmly answer sir this usage may perhaps cancel every other obligation you have confer on i but there be one you can never cancel nor will i be provoke by your abuse to lift my hand against the father of sophia at these word the squire grow still more outrageous than before so that the parson beg jones to retire say you behold sir how he waxeth wrath at your abode here therefore let i pray you not to tarry any long
his anger be too much kindle for you to commune with he at present
you have well therefore conclude your visit and refer what matter you have to urge in your behalf to some other opportunity jones accept this advice with thank and immediately depart
the squire now regain the liberty of his hand and so much temper as to express some satisfaction in the restraint which have be lay upon he declare that he should certainly have beat his brain out and add it would have vex one confoundedly to have be hang for such a rascal the parson now begin to triumph in the success of his peace-make endeavour and proceed to read a lecture against anger which might perhaps rather have tend to raise than to quiet that passion in some hasty mind
this lecture he enrich with many valuable quotation from the antient particularly from seneca who hath indeed so well handle this passion that none but a very angry man can read he without great pleasure and profit
the doctor conclude this harangue with the famous story of alexander and clitus but as i find that enter in my common-place under title drunkenness i shall not insert it here
the squire take no notice of this story nor perhaps of anything he say for he interrupt he before he have finish by call for a tankard of beer observe which be perhaps as true as any observation on this fever of the mind that anger make a man dry
no soon have the squire swallow a large draught than he renew the discourse on jones and declare a resolution of go the next morning early to acquaint mr allworthy
his friend would have dissuade he from this from the mere motive of good-nature but his dissuasion have no other effect than to produce a large volley of oath and curse which greatly shock the pious ear of supple but he do not dare to remonstrate against a privilege which the squire claim as a freeborn englishman
to say truth the parson submit to please his palate at the squire s table at the expense of suffer now and then this violence to his ear
he content himself with think he do not promote this evil practice and that the squire would not swear an oath the less if he never enter within his gate
however though he be not guilty of ill manner by rebuke a gentleman in his own house he pay he off obliquely in the pulpit which have not indeed the good effect of work a reformation in the squire himself yet it so far operate on his conscience that he put the law very severely in execution against other and the magistrate be the only person in the parish who could swear with impunity
chapter x
in which mr western visit mr allworthy
mr allworthy be now retire from breakfast with his nephew well satisfied with the report of the young gentleman s successful visit to sophia for he greatly desire the match more on account of the young lady s character than of her riches when mr western break abruptly in upon they and without any ceremony begin as follows there you have do a fine piece of work truly
you have bring up your bastard to a fine purpose not that i believe you have have any hand in it neither that is as a man may say designedly but there be a fine kettle-of-fish make on t up at our house what can be the matter mr western say allworthy
o matter enow of all conscience my daughter hath fall in love with your bastard thatbe all but i willnot ge she a hapeny not the twentieth part of a brass varden
i always think what would come o breed up a bastard like a gentleman and let un come about to vok s house
itbe well vor un i could not get at un ihave a lick d un ihave a spoil d his caterwauling ihave a teach the son of a whore to meddle with meat for his master
he shallnot ever have a morsel of meat of mine or a varden to buy it if she will ha un one smock shall be her portion
iwould soon ge my esteate to the zinking fund that it may be send to hanover to corrupt our nation with i be heartily sorry cry allworthy
pox o your sorrow say western it will do i abundance of good when i have lose my only child my poor sophy that be the joy of my heart and all the hope and comfort of my age but i be resolve i will turn she out o door she shall beg and starve and rot in the street
not one hapeny not a hapeny shall she ever hae o mine
the son of a bitch be always good at find a hare sitting an be rot to n i little think what puss he be look after but it shall be the bad he ever vound in his life
she shall be no well than carrion the skin o er be all he shall ha and zu you may tell un i be in amazement cry allworthy at what you tell i after what pass between my nephew and the young lady no long ago than yesterday yes sir answer western it be after what pass between your nephew and she that the whole matter come out
mr blifil there be no soon go than the son of a whore come lurch about the house
little do i think when i use to love he for a sportsman that he be all the while a poaching after my daughter why truly say allworthy i could wish you have not give he so many opportunity with she and you will do i the justice to acknowledge that i have always be averse to his staying so much at your house though i own i have no suspicion of this kind why zound cry western who could have think it
what the devil have she to do wi n
he do not come there a courting to she he come there a hunting with i but be it possible say allworthy that you should never discern any symptom of love between they when you have see they so often together never in my life as i hope to be save cry western i never so much as zeed he kiss she in all my life and so far from court she he use rather to be more silent when she be in company than at any other time and as for the girl she be always less civil to n than to any young man that come to the house
as to that matter i be not more easy to be deceive than another i would not have you think i be neighbour allworthy could scarce refrain laughter at this but he resolve to do a violence to himself for he perfectly well know mankind and have too much good-breeding and good-nature to offend the squire in his present circumstance
he then ask western what he would have he do upon this occasion
to which the other answer that he would have he keep the rascal away from his house and that he would go and lock up the wench for he be resolve to make she marry mr blifil in spite of her tooth he then shake blifil by the hand and swear he would have no other son-in-law
presently after which he take his leave say his house be in such disorder that it be necessary for he to make haste home to take care his daughter do not give he the slip and as for jones he swear if he catch he at his house he would qualify he to run for the gelding plate
when allworthy and blifil be again leave together a long silence ensue between they all which interval the young gentleman fill up with sigh which proceed partly from disappointment but more from hatred for the success of jones be much more grievous to he than the loss of sophia
at length his uncle ask he what he be determined to do and he answer in the follow words alas
sir can it be a question what step a lover will take when reason and passion point different way
i be afraid it be too certain he will in that dilemma always follow the latter
reason dictate to i to quit all thought of a woman who place her affection on another my passion bid i hope she may in time change her inclination in my favour
here however i conceive an objection may be raise which if it could not fully be answer would totally deter i from any further pursuit
i mean the injustice of endeavour to supplant another in a heart of which he seem already in possession but the determined resolution of mr western show that in this case i shall by so do promote the happiness of every party not only that of the parent who will thus be preserve from the high degree of misery but of both the other who must be undo by this match
the lady i be sure will be undo in every sense for besides the loss of most part of her own fortune she will be not only married to a beggar but the little fortune which her father cannot withhold from she will be squander on that wench with whom i know he yet converse
nay that be a trifle for i know he to be one of the bad man in the world for have my dear uncle know what i have hitherto endeavour to conceal he must have long since abandon so profligate a wretch how say allworthy hath he do anything bad than i already know
tell i i beseech you no reply blifil it be now past and perhaps he may have repent of it i command you on your duty say allworthy to tell i what you mean you know sir say blifil i never disobey you but i be sorry i mention it since it may now look like revenge whereas i thank heaven no such motive ever enter my heart and if you oblige i to discover it i must be his petitioner to you for your forgiveness i will have no condition answer allworthy i think i have show tenderness enough towards he and more perhaps than you ought to thank i for more indeed i fear than he deserve cry blifil for in the very day of your utmost danger when myself and all the family be in tear he fill the house with riot and debauchery
he drank and sung and roar and when i give he a gentle hint of the indecency of his action he fall into a violent passion swear many oath call i rascal and strike i how cry allworthy do he dare to strike you i be sure cry blifil i have forgive he that long ago
i wish i could so easily forget his ingratitude to the good of benefactor and yet even that i hope you will forgive he since he must have certainly be possess with the devil for that very evening as mr thwackum and myself be take the air in the field and exult in the good symptom which then first begin to discover themselves we unluckily see he engage with a wench in a manner not fit to be mention
mr thwackum with more boldness than prudence advance to rebuke he when i be sorry to say it he fall upon the worthy man and beat he so outrageously that i wish he may have yet recover the bruise
nor be i without my share of the effect of his malice while i endeavour to protect my tutor but that i have long forgive nay i prevail with mr thwackum to forgive he too and not to inform you of a secret which i fear might be fatal to he
and now sir since i have unadvisedly drop a hint of this matter and your command have oblige i to discover the whole let i intercede with you for he o child say allworthy i know not whether i should blame or applaud your goodness in conceal such villany a moment but where be mr thwackum
not that i want any confirmation of what you say but i will examine all the evidence of this matter to justify to the world the example i be resolve to make of such a monster thwackum be now send for and presently appear
he corroborate every circumstance which the other have depose nay he produce the record upon his breast where the handwriting of mr jones remain very legible in black and blue
he conclude with declare to mr allworthy that he should have long since inform he of this matter have not mr blifil by the most earnest interposition prevent he
he be say he an excellent youth though such forgiveness of enemy be carry the matter too far in reality blifil have take some pain to prevail with the parson and to prevent the discovery at that time for which he have many reason
he know that the mind of man be apt to be soften and relax from their usual severity by sickness
besides he imagine that if the story be tell when the fact be so recent and the physician about the house who might have unravel the real truth he should never be able to give it the malicious turn which he intend
again he resolve to hoard up this business till the indiscretion of jones should afford some additional complaint for he think the joint weight of many fact fall upon he together would be the most likely to crush he and he watch therefore some such opportunity as that with which fortune have now kindly present he
lastly by prevail with thwackum to conceal the matter for a time he know he should confirm an opinion of his friendship to jones which he have greatly labour to establish in mr allworthy
chapter xi
a short chapter but which contain sufficient matter to affect the good-natured reader
it be mr allworthy s custom never to punish any one not even to turn away a servant in a passion
he resolve therefore to delay pass sentence on jones till the afternoon
the poor young man attend at dinner as usual but his heart be too much load to suffer he to eat
his grief too be a good deal aggravate by the unkind look of mr allworthy whence he conclude that western have discover the whole affair between he and sophia but as to mr blifil s story he have not the least apprehension for of much the great part he be entirely innocent and for the residue as he have forgive and forget it himself so he suspect no remembrance on the other side
when dinner be over and the servant depart mr allworthy begin to harangue
he set forth in a long speech the many iniquity of which jones have be guilty particularly those which this day have bring to light and conclude by tell he that unless he could clear himself of the charge he be resolve to banish he his sight for ever many disadvantage attend poor jones in make his defence nay indeed he hardly know his accusation for as mr allworthy in recount the drunkenness c while he lie ill out of modesty sunk everything that relate particularly to himself which indeed principally constitute the crime jones could not deny the charge
his heart be besides almost break already and his spirit be so sunk that he could say nothing for himself but acknowledge the whole and like a criminal in despair throw himself upon mercy conclude that though he must own himself guilty of many folly and inadvertency he hop he have do nothing to deserve what would be to he the great punishment in the world allworthy answer that he have forgive he too often already in compassion to his youth and in hope of his amendment that he now find he be an abandon reprobate and such as it would be criminal in any one to support and encourage
nay say mr allworthy to he your audacious attempt to steal away the young lady call upon i to justify my own character in punish you
the world who have already censure the regard i have show for you may think with some colour at least of justice that i connive at so base and barbarous an action--an action of which you must have know my abhorrence and which have you have any concern for my ease and honour as well as for my friendship you would never have think of undertake
fie upon it young man
indeed there be scarce any punishment equal to your crime and i can scarce think myself justifiable in what i be now go to bestow on you
however as i have educate you like a child of my own i will not turn you naked into the world
when you open this paper therefore you will find something which may enable you with industry to get an honest livelihood but if you employ it to bad purpose i shall not think myself oblige to supply you far be resolve from this day forward to converse no more with you on any account
i cannot avoid say there be no part of your conduct which i resent more than your ill-treatment of that good young man mean blifil who hath behave with so much tenderness and honour towards you these last word be a dose almost too bitter to be swallow
a flood of tear now gush from the eye of jones and every faculty of speech and motion seem to have desert he
it be some time before he be able to obey allworthy s peremptory command of depart which he at length do have first kiss his hand with a passion difficult to be affect and as difficult to be describe
the reader must be very weak if when he consider the light in which jones then appear to mr allworthy he should blame the rigour of his sentence
and yet all the neighbourhood either from this weakness or from some bad motive condemn this justice and severity as the high cruelty
nay the very person who have before censure the good man for the kindness and tenderness show to a bastard his own accord to the general opinion now cry out as loudly against turn his own child out of door
the woman especially be unanimous in take the part of jones and raise more story on the occasion than i have room in this chapter to set down
one thing must not be omit that in their censure on this occasion none ever mention the sum contain in the paper which allworthy give jones which be no less than five hundred pound but all agree that he be send away penniless and some say naked from the house of his inhuman father
chapter xii
contain love-letter c
jones be command to leave the house immediately and tell that his clothe and everything else should be send to he whithersoever he should order they
he accordingly set out and walk above a mile not regard and indeed scarce knowing whither he go
at length a little brook obstruct his passage he throw himself down by the side of it nor could he help mutter with some little indignation sure my father will not deny i this place to rest in here he presently fall into the most violent agony tear his hair from his head and use most other action which generally accompany fit of madness rage and despair
when he have in this manner vent the first emotion of passion he begin to come a little to himself
his grief now take another turn and discharge itself in a gentle way till he become at last cool enough to reason with his passion and to consider what step be proper to be take in his deplorable condition
and now the great doubt be how to act with regard to sophia
the thought of leave she almost rent his heart asunder but the consideration of reduce she to ruin and beggary still rack he if possible more and if the violent desire of possess her person could have induce he to listen one moment to this alternative still he be by no means certain of her resolution to indulge his wish at so high an expense
the resentment of mr allworthy and the injury he must do to his quiet argue strongly against this latter and lastly the apparent impossibility of his success even if he would sacrifice all these consideration to it come to his assistance and thus honour at last back with despair with gratitude to his benefactor and with real love to his mistress get the well of burn desire and he resolve rather to quit sophia than pursue she to her ruin
it be difficult for any who have not feel it to conceive the glowing warmth which fill his breast on the first contemplation of this victory over his passion
pride flatter he so agreeably that his mind perhaps enjoy perfect happiness but this be only momentary sophia soon return to his imagination and allay the joy of his triumph with no less bitter pang than a good-natured general must feel when he survey the bleed heap at the price of whose blood he hath purchase his laurel for thousand of tender idea lie murder before our conqueror
be resolve however to pursue the path of this giant honour as the gigantic poet lee call it he determine to write a farewel letter to sophia and accordingly proceed to a house not far off where be furnish with proper material he write as follows madam when you reflect on the situation in which i write i be sure your good-nature will pardon any inconsistency or absurdity which my letter contain for everything here flow from a heart so full that no language can express its dictate
i have resolve madam to obey your command in fly for ever from your dear your lovely sight
cruel indeed those command be but it be a cruelty which proceed from fortune not from my sophia
fortune hath make it necessary necessary to your preservation to forget there ever be such a wretch as i be
believe i i would not hint all my suffering to you if i imagine they could possibly escape your ear
i know the goodness and tenderness of your heart and would avoid give you any of those pain which you always feel for the miserable
o let nothing which you shall hear of my hard fortune cause a moment s concern for after the loss of you everything be to i a trifle
o sophia
it be hard to leave you it be hard still to desire you to forget i yet the sincere love oblige i to both
pardon my conceiving that any remembrance of i can give you disquiet but if i be so gloriously wretched sacrifice i every way to your relief
think i never love you or think truly how little i deserve you and learn to scorn i for a presumption which can never be too severely punished i be unable to say more may guardian angel protect you for ever he be now search his pocket for his wax but find none nor indeed anything else therein for in truth he have in his frantic disposition toss everything from he and amongst the rest his pocket-book which he have receive from mr allworthy which he have never open and which now first occur to his memory
the house supply he with a wafer for his present purpose with which have seal his letter he return hastily towards the brook side in order to search for the thing which he have there lose
in his way he meet his old friend black george who heartily condole with he on his misfortune for this have already reach his ear and indeed those of all the neighbourhood
jones acquaint the gamekeeper with his loss and he as readily go back with he to the brook where they search every tuft of grass in the meadow as well where jones have not be as where he have be but all to no purpose for they find nothing for indeed though the thing be then in the meadow they omit to search the only place where they be deposit to wit in the pocket of the say george for he have just before find they and be luckily apprize of their value have very carefully put they up for his own use
the gamekeeper have exert as much diligence in quest of the lost good as if he have hop to find they desire mr jones to recollect if he have be in no other place for sure say he if you have lose they here so lately the thing must have be here still for this be a very unlikely place for any one to pass by and indeed it be by great accident that he himself have pass through that field in order to lay wire for hare with which he be to supply a poulterer at bath the next morning
jones now give over all hope of recover his loss and almost all thought concern it and turn to black george ask he earnestly if he would do he the great favour in the world
george answer with some hesitation sir you know you may command i whatever be in my power and i heartily wish it be in my power to do you any service in fact the question stagger he for he have by sell game amass a pretty good sum of money in mr western s service and be afraid that jones want to borrow some small matter of he but he be presently relieve from his anxiety by be desire to convey a letter to sophia which with great pleasure he promise to do
and indeed i believe there be few favour which he would not have gladly confer on mr jones for he bear as much gratitude towards he as he could and be as honest as man who love money well than any other thing in the universe generally be
mrs honour be agree by both to be the proper mean by which this letter should pass to sophia
they then separate the gamekeeper return home to mr western s and jones walk to an alehouse at half a mile s distance to wait for his messenger s return
george no soon come home to his master s house than he meet with mrs honour to whom have first sound she with a few previous question he deliver the letter for her mistress and receive at the same time another from she for mr jones which honour tell he she have carry all that day in her bosom and begin to despair of find any mean of deliver it
the gamekeeper return hastily and joyfully to jones who have receive sophia s letter from he instantly withdraw and eagerly break it open read as follows sir it be impossible to express what i have feel since i see you
your submitting on my account to such cruel insult from my father lay i under an obligation i shall ever own
as you know his temper i beg you will for my sake avoid he
i wish i have any comfort to send you but believe this that nothing but the last violence shall ever give my hand or heart where you would be sorry to see they bestow jones read this letter a hundred time over and kiss it a hundred time as often
his passion now bring all tender desire back into his mind
he repent that he have writ to sophia in the manner we have see above but he repent more that he have make use of the interval of his messenger s absence to write and dispatch a letter to mr allworthy in which he have faithfully promise and bind himself to quit all thought of his love
however when his cool reflection return he plainly perceive that his case be neither mend nor alter by sophia s billet unless to give he some little glimpse of hope from her constancy of some favourable accident hereafter
he therefore resume his resolution and take leave of black george set forward to a town about five mile distant whither he have desire mr allworthy unless he please to revoke his sentence to send his thing after he
chapter xiii
the behaviour of sophia on the present occasion which none of her sex will blame who be capable of behave in the same manner
and the discussion of a knotty point in the court of conscience
sophia have pass the last twenty-four hour in no very desirable manner

in comment on which text mrs western have display her eloquence during several hour

forget he indeed

i detest all mankind nay to be sure ma am answer honour your la ship hath have enough to give you a surfeit of they

what dost thou mean why to be sure ma am my master no soon tell squire allworthy about mr jones have offer to make love to your la ship than the squire strip he stark naked and turn he out of door ha say sophia i have be the cursed wretched cause of his destruction

his conscience however immediately start at this suggestion and begin to upbraid he with ingratitude to his benefactor
to this his avarice answer that his conscience should have consider the matter before when he deprive poor jones of his 500
that have quietly acquiesce in what be of so much great importance it be absurd if not downright hypocrisy to affect any qualm at this trifle
in return to which conscience like a good lawyer attempt to distinguish between an absolute breach of trust as here where the good be deliver and a bare concealment of what be find as in the former case
avarice presently treat this with ridicule call it a distinction without a difference and absolutely insist that when once all pretension of honour and virtue be give up in any one instance that there be no precedent for resort to they upon a second occasion
in short poor conscience have certainly be defeat in the argument have not fear stept in to her assistance and very strenuously urge that the real distinction between the two action do not lie in the different degree of honour but of safety for that the secrete the 500 be a matter of very little hazard whereas the detaining the sixteen guineas be liable to the utmost danger of discovery
by this friendly aid of fear conscience obtain a compleat victory in the mind of black george and after make he a few compliment on his honesty force he to deliver the money to jones

book vii
contain three day
chapter i
a comparison between the world and the stage
the world hath be often compare to the theatre and many grave writer as well as the poet have consider human life as a great drama resemble in almost every particular those scenical representation which thespis be first report to have invent and which have be since receive with so much approbation and delight in all polite country
this thought hath be carry so far and be become so general that some word proper to the theatre and which be at first metaphorically apply to the world be now indiscriminately and literally speak of both thus stage and scene be by common use grow as familiar to we when we speak of life in general as when we confine ourselves to dramatic performance and when transaction behind the curtain be mention st james s be more likely to occur to our thought than drury-lane
it may seem easy enough to account for all this by reflect that the theatrical stage be nothing more than a representation or as aristotle call it an imitation of what really exist and hence perhaps we might fairly pay a very high compliment to those who by their writing or action have be so capable of imitate life as to have their picture in a manner confound with or mistaken for the original
but in reality we be not so fond of pay compliment to these people whom we use as child frequently do the instrument of their amusement and have much more pleasure in hiss and buffet they than in admire their excellence
there be many other reason which have induce we to see this analogy between the world and the stage
some have consider the large part of mankind in the light of actor as personate character no more their own and to which in fact they have no well title than the player hath to be in earnest think the king or emperor whom he represent
thus the hypocrite may be say to be a player and indeed the greeks call they both by one and the same name
the brevity of life hath likewise give occasion to this comparison
so the immortal shakespear-- lifebe a poor player that strut and fret his hour upon the stage and then be hear no more
for which hackneyed quotation i will make the reader amend by a very noble one which few i believe have read
it be take from a poem call the deity publish about nine year ago and long since bury in oblivion a proof that good book no more than good man do always survive the bad
from thee all human action take their spring the rise of empire and the fall of king
see the vast theatre of time display d while o er the scene succeed hero tread
with pomp the shin image succeed what leader triumph and what monarch bleed
perform the part thy providence assign d their pride their passion to thy end inclin d awhile they glitter in the face of day then at thy nod the phantom pass away no trace leave of all the busy scene but that remembrance says-- the thing have be the deity
in all these however and in every other similitude of life to the theatre the resemblance hath be always take from the stage only
none as i remember have at all consider the audience at this great drama
but as nature often exhibit some of her good performance to a very full house so will the behaviour of her spectator no less admit the above-mention comparison than that of her actor
in this vast theatre of time be seat the friend and the critic here be clap and shout hiss and groan in short everything which be ever see or hear at the theatre-royal
let we examine this in one example for instance in the behaviour of the great audience on that scene which nature be pleased to exhibit in the twelfth chapter of the precede book where she introduce black george run away with the 500 from his friend and benefactor
those who sit in the world s upper gallery treat that incident i be well convince with their usual vociferation and every term of scurrilous reproach be most probably vent on that occasion
if we have descend to the next order of spectator we should have find an equal degree of abhorrence though less of noise and scurrility yet here the good woman give black george to the devil and many of they expect every minute that the cloven-foot gentleman would fetch his own
the pit as usual be no doubt divide those who delight in heroic virtue and perfect character object to the produce such instance of villany without punish they very severely for the sake of example
some of the author s friend cry look e gentleman the man be a villain but it be nature for all that and all the young critic of the age the clerk apprentice c call it low and fall a groaning
as for the box they behave with their accustomed politeness
most of they be attend to something else
some of those few who regard the scene at all declare he be a bad kind of man while other refuse to give their opinion till they have hear that of the good judge
now we who be admit behind the scene of this great theatre of nature and no author ought to write anything besides dictionary and spelling-book who hath not this privilege can censure the action without conceive any absolute detestation of the person whom perhaps nature may not have design to act an ill part in all her drama for in this instance life most exactly resemble the stage since it be often the same person who represent the villain and the heroe and he who engage your admiration to-day will probably attract your contempt to-morrow
as garrick whom i regard in tragedy to be the great genius the world hath ever produce sometimes condescend to play the fool so do scipio the great and laelius the wise accord to horace many year ago nay cicero report they to have be incredibly childish these it be true play the fool like my friend garrick in jest only but several eminent character have in numberless instance of their life play the fool egregiously in earnest so far as to render it a matter of some doubt whether their wisdom or folly be predominant or whether they be well intitle to the applause or censure the admiration or contempt the love or hatred of mankind
those person indeed who have pass any time behind the scene of this great theatre and be thoroughly acquaint not only with the several disguise which be there put on but also with the fantastic and capricious behaviour of the passions who be the manager and director of this theatre for as to reason the patentee he be know to be a very idle fellow and seldom to exert himself may most probably have learn to understand the famous nil admirari of horace or in the english phrase to stare at nothing
a single bad act no more constitute a villain in life than a single bad part on the stage
the passion like the manager of a playhouse often force man upon part without consult their judgment and sometimes without any regard to their talent
thus the man as well as the player may condemn what he himself act nay it be common to see vice sit as awkwardly on some man as the character of iago would on the honest face of mr william mills
upon the whole then the man of candour and of true understanding be never hasty to condemn
he can censure an imperfection or even a vice without rage against the guilty party
in a word they be the same folly the same childishness the same ill-breeding and the same ill-nature which raise all the clamour and uproar both in life and on the stage
the bad of man generally have the word rogue and villain most in their mouth as the low of all wretch be the aptest to cry out low in the pit
chapter ii
contain a conversation which mr jones have with himself
jones receive his effect from mr allworthy s early in the morning with the following answer to his letter sir i be command by my uncle to acquaint you that as he do not proceed to those measure he have take with you without the great deliberation and after the full evidence of your unworthiness so will it be always out of your power to cause the least alteration in his resolution
he express great surprize at your presumption in say you have resign all pretension to a young lady to whom it be impossible you should ever have have any her birth and fortune have make she so infinitely your superior
lastly i be command to tell you that the only instance of your compliance with my uncle s inclination which he require be your immediately quit this country
i cannot conclude this without offer you my advice as a christian that you would seriously think of amend your life
that you may be assist with grace so to do will be always the prayer of your humble servant w
blifil many contend passion be raise in our heroe s mind by this letter but the tender prevail at last over the indignant and irascible and a flood of tear come seasonably to his assistance and possibly prevent his misfortune from either turn his head or burst his heart
he grow however soon ashamed of indulge this remedy and start up he cry well then i will give mr allworthy the only instance he require of my obedience
i will go this moment--but whither why let fortune direct since there be no other who think it of any consequence what become of this wretched person it shall be a matter of equal indifference to myself
shall i alone regard what no other--ha
have i not reason to think there be another one whose value be above that of the whole world i may i must imagine my sophia be not indifferent to what become of i
shall i then leave this only friend--and such a friend
shall i not stay with her where--how can i stay with she
have i any hope of ever see she though she be as desirous as myself without expose she to the wrath of her father and to what purpose
can i think of solicit such a creature to consent to her own ruin
shall i indulge any passion of mine at such a price
shall i lurk about this country like a thief with such intentions no i disdain i detest the thought
farewel sophia farewel most lovely most beloved-- here passion stop his mouth and find a vent at his eye
and now have take a resolution to leave the country he begin to debate with himself whither he should go
the world as milton phrase it lie all before he and jones no more than adam have any man to whom he might resort for comfort or assistance
all his acquaintance be the acquaintance of mr allworthy and he have no reason to expect any countenance from they as that gentleman have withdraw his favour from he
man of great and good character should indeed be very cautious how they discard their dependent for the consequence to the unhappy sufferer be be discard by all other
what course of life to pursue or to what business to apply himself be a second consideration and here the prospect be all a melancholy void
every profession and every trade require length of time and what be bad money for matter be so constituted that nothing out of nothing be not a truer maxim in physics than in politic and every man who be greatly destitute of money be on that account entirely exclude from all mean of acquire it
at last the ocean that hospitable friend to the wretched open she capacious arm to receive he and he instantly resolve to accept she kind invitation
to express myself less figuratively he determine to go to sea
this think indeed no soon suggest itself than he eagerly embrace it and have presently hire horse he set out for bristol to put it in execution
but before we attend he on this expedition we shall resort awhile to mr western s and see what far happen to the charming sophia
chapter iii
contain several dialogue
the morning in which mr jones depart mrs western summon sophia into her apartment and have first acquaint she that she have obtain her liberty of her father she proceed to read she a long lecture on the subject of matrimony which she treat not as a romantic scheme of happiness arise from love as it hath be describe by the poet nor do she mention any of those purpose for which we be teach by divine to regard it as institute by sacred authority she consider it rather as a fund in which prudent woman deposit their fortune to the good advantage in order to receive a large interest for they than they could have elsewhere
when mrs western have finish sophia answer that she be very incapable of argue with a lady of her aunt s superior knowledge and experience especially on a subject which she have so very little consider as this of matrimony argue with i child reply the other i do not indeed expect it
i should have see the world to very little purpose truly if i be to argue with one of your year
i have take this trouble in order to instruct you
the antient philosopher such as socrates alcibiades and other do not use to argue with their scholar
you be to consider i child as socrates not ask your opinion but only inform you of mine from which last word the reader may possibly imagine that this lady have read no more of the philosophy of socrates than she have of that of alcibiades and indeed we cannot resolve his curiosity as to this point
madam cry sophia i have never presume to controvert any opinion of your and this subject as i say i have never yet think of and perhaps never may indeed sophy reply the aunt this dissimulation with i be very foolish
the french shall as soon persuade i that they take foreign town in defence only of their own country as you can impose on i to believe you have never yet think seriously of matrimony
how can you child affect to deny that you have consider of contract an alliance when you so well know i be acquaint with the party with whom you desire to contract it an alliance as unnatural and contrary to your interest as a separate league with the french would be to the interest of the dutch
but however if you have not hitherto consider of this matter i promise you it be now high time for my brother be resolve immediately to conclude the treaty with mr blifil and indeed i be a sort of guarantee in the affair and have promise your concurrence indeed madam cry sophia this be the only instance in which i must disobey both yourself and my father
for this be a match which require very little consideration in i to refuse if i be not as great a philosopher as socrates himself return mrs western you would overcome my patience
what objection can you have to the young gentleman a very solid objection in my opinion say sophia-- i hate he will you never learn a proper use of word answer the aunt
indeed child you should consult bailey s dictionary
it be impossible you should hate a man from whom you have receive no injury
by hatred therefore you mean no more than dislike which be no sufficient objection against your marrying of he
i have know many couple who have entirely dislike each other lead very comfortable genteel life
believe i child i know these thing well than you
you will allow i i think to have see the world in which i have not an acquaintance who would not rather be think to dislike her husband than to like he
the contrary be such out-of-fashion romantic nonsense that the very imagination of it be shocking indeed madam reply sophia i shall never marry a man i dislike
if i promise my father never to consent to any marriage contrary to his inclination i think i may hope he will never force i into that state contrary to my own inclination cry the aunt with some warmth
inclination
i be astonish at your assurance
a young woman of your age and unmarried to talk of inclination
but whatever your inclination may be my brother be resolve nay since you talk of inclination i shall advise he to hasten the treaty
inclination sophia then fling herself upon her knee and tear begin to trickle from her shin eye
she entreat her aunt to have mercy upon she and not to resent so cruelly her unwillingness to make herself miserable often urge that she alone be concern and that her happiness only be at stake as a bailiff when well authorize by his writ have possess himself of the person of some unhappy debtor view all his tear without concern in vain the wretched captive attempt to raise compassion in vain the tender wife bereft of her companion the little prattling boy or fright girl be mention as inducement to reluctance
the noble bumtrap blind and deaf to every circumstance of distress greatly rise above all the motif to humanity and into the hand of the gaoler resolve to deliver his miserable prey
not less blind to the tear or less deaf to every entreaty of sophia be the politic aunt nor less determined be she to deliver over the trembling maid into the arm of the gaoler blifil
she answer with great impetuosity so far madam from your being concern alone your concern be the least or surely the least important

no
it be a match between two kingdom rather than between two person
the same happen in great family such as ours
the alliance between the family be the principal matter
you ought to have a great regard for the honour of your family than for your own person and if the example of a princess cannot inspire you with these noble thought you cannot surely complain at be use no bad than all princess be use i hope madam cry sophia with a little elevation of voice i shall never do anything to dishonour my family but as for mr blifil whatever may be the consequence i be resolve against he and no force shall prevail in his favour western who have be within hearing during the great part of the precede dialogue have now exhaust all his patience he therefore enter the room in a violent passion crying d--n i then if shatunt ha un d--n i if shatunt thatbe all--thatbe all d--n i if shatunt mrs western have collect a sufficient quantity of wrath for the use of sophia but she now transfer it all to the squire

have i not take infinite pain to show you that the law of nature hath enjoin a duty on child to their parent
have i not tell you what plato say on that subject a subject on which you be so notoriously ignorant when you come first under my care that i verily believe you do not know the relation between a daughter and a father tis a lie answer western

more than gothic ignorance answer the lady

remember that--i be no rat
i be a true englishman and not of your hanover breed that have eat up the nation thou art one of those wise man cry she whose nonsensical principle have undo the nation by weaken the hand of our government at home and by discourage our friend and encourage our enemy abroad ho

he be indeed a great master of this kind of vociferation and have a holla proper for most occasion in life
woman who like mrs western know the world and have apply themselves to philosophy and politic would have immediately avail themselves of the present disposition of mr western s mind by throw in a few artful compliment to his understanding at the expense of his absent adversary but poor sophia be all simplicity
by which word we do not intend to insinuate to the reader that she be silly which be generally understand as a synonymous term with simple for she be indeed a most sensible girl and her understanding be of the first rate but she want all that useful art which female convert to so many good purpose in life and which as it rather arise from the heart than from the head be often the property of the silliest of woman
chapter iv
a picture of a country gentlewoman take from the life
mr western have finish his holla and take a little breath begin to lament in very pathetic term the unfortunate condition of man who be say he always whipt in by the humour of some d--n d b-- or other
i think i be hard run enough by your mother for one man but after give she a dodge herebe another b-- follow i upon the foil but curse my jacket if i will be run down in this manner by any o um sophia never have a single dispute with her father till this unlucky affair of blifil on any account except in defence of her mother whom she have love most tenderly though she lose she in the eleventh year of her age
the squire to whom that poor woman have be a faithful upper-servant all the time of their marriage have return that behaviour by make what the world call a good husband
he very seldom swear at she perhaps not above once a week and never beat she she have not the least occasion for jealousy and be perfect mistress of her time for she be never interrupt by her husband who be engage all the morning in his field exercise and all the evening with bottle companion
she scarce indeed ever see he but at meal where she have the pleasure of carve those dish which she have before attend at the dressing
from these meal she retire about five minute after the other servant have only stay to drink the king over the water such be it seem mr western s order for it be a maxim with he that woman should come in with the first dish and go out after the first glass
obedience to these order be perhaps no difficult task for the conversation if it may be call so be seldom such as could entertain a lady
it consist chiefly of hallowing singing relation of sporting adventure b--d--y and abuse of woman and of the government
these however be the only season when mr western see his wife for when he repair to her bed he be generally so drunk that he could not see and in the sporting season he always rise from she before it be light
thus be she perfect mistress of her time and have besides a coach and four usually at her command though unhappily indeed the badness of the neighbourhood and of the road make this of little use for none who have set much value on their neck would have pass through the one or who have set any value on their hour would have visit the other
now to deal honestly with the reader she do not make all the return expect to so much indulgence for she have be marry against her will by a fond father the match have be rather advantageous on her side for the squire s estate be upward of 3000 a year and her fortune no more than a bare 8000
hence perhaps she have contract a little gloominess of temper for she be rather a good servant than a good wife nor have she always the gratitude to return the extraordinary degree of roar mirth with which the squire receive she even with a good-humour smile
she would moreover sometimes interfere with matter which do not concern she as the violent drinking of her husband which in the gentle term she would take some of the few opportunity he give she of remonstrate against
and once in her life she very earnestly entreat he to carry she for two month to london which he peremptorily deny nay be angry with his wife for the request ever after be well assure that all the husband in london be cuckold
for this last and many other good reason western at length heartily hat his wife and as he never conceal this hatred before her death so he never forget it afterwards but when anything in the least sour he as a bad scenting day or a distemper among his hound or any other such misfortune he constantly vent his spleen by invective against the deceased say if my wife be alive now she would be glad of this these invective he be especially desirous of throw forth before sophia for as he love she more than he do any other so he be really jealous that she have love her mother well than he
and this jealousy sophia seldom fail of heighten on these occasion for he be not content with violate her ear with the abuse of her mother but endeavour to force an explicit approbation of all this abuse with which desire he never could prevail upon she by any promise or threat to comply
hence some of my reader will perhaps wonder that the squire have not hat sophia as much as he have hat her mother but i must inform they that hatred be not the effect of love even through the medium of jealousy
it be indeed very possible for jealous person to kill the object of their jealousy but not to hate they
which sentiment be a pretty hard morsel and bear something of the air of a paradox we shall leave the reader to chew the cud upon it to the end of the chapter

ay certainly

he receive the sound with much the same action as a man receive a bullet in his head

nay who hath actually put she into they

chapter vi
contain great variety of matter
the squire overtake his sister just as she be step into the coach and partly by force and partly by solicitation prevail upon she to order her horse back into their quarter
he succeed in this attempt without much difficulty for the lady be as we have already hint of a most placable disposition and greatly love she brother though she despise his part or rather his little knowledge of the world
poor sophia who have first set on foot this reconciliation be now make the sacrifice to it
they both concur in their censure on her conduct jointly declare war against she and directly proceed to counsel how to carry it on in the most vigorous manner
for this purpose mrs western propose not only an immediate conclusion of the treaty with allworthy but as immediately to carry it into execution say that there be no other way to succeed with her niece but by violent method which she be convince sophia have not sufficient resolution to resist
by violent say she i mean rather hasty measure for as to confinement or absolute force no such thing must or can be attempt
our plan must be concert for a surprize and not for a storm these matter be resolve on when mr blifil come to pay a visit to his mistress
the squire no soon hear of his arrival than he stept aside by his sister s advice to give his daughter order for the proper reception of her lover which he do with the most bitter execration and denunciation of judgment on her refusal
the impetuosity of the squire bear down all before he and sophia as her aunt very wisely foresaw be not able to resist he
she agree therefore to see blifil though she have scarce spirit or strength sufficient to utter her assent
indeed to give a peremptory denial to a father whom she so tenderly love be no easy task
have this circumstance be out of the case much less resolution than what she be really mistress of would perhaps have serve she but it be no unusual thing to ascribe those action entirely to fear which be in a great measure produce by love
in pursuance therefore of her father s peremptory command sophia now admit mr blifil s visit
scene like this when paint at large afford as we have observe very little entertainment to the reader
here therefore we shall strictly adhere to a rule of horace by which writer be direct to pass over all those matter which they despair of place in a shining light a rule we conceive of excellent use as well to the historian as to the poet and which if follow must at least have this good effect that many a great evil for so all great book be call would thus be reduce to a small one
it be possible the great art use by blifil at this interview would have prevail on sophia to have make another man in his circumstance her confident and to have reveal the whole secret of her heart to he but she have contract so ill an opinion of this young gentleman that she be resolve to place no confidence in he for simplicity when set on its guard be often a match for cunning
her behaviour to he therefore be entirely force and indeed such as be generally prescribe to virgin upon the second formal visit from one who be appoint for their husband
but though blifil declare himself to the squire perfectly satisfied with his reception yet that gentleman who in company with his sister have overhear all be not so well pleased
he resolve in pursuance of the advice of the sage lady to push matter as forward as possible and address himself to his intended son-in-law in the hunting phrase he cry after a loud holla follow she boy follow she run in run in thatbe it honey
dead dead dead
never be bashful nor stand shall i shall i
allworthy and i can finish all matter between we this afternoon and let we ha the wedding to-morrow blifil have convey the utmost satisfaction into his countenance answer as there be nothing sir in this world which i so eagerly desire as an alliance with your family except my union with the most amiable and deserve sophia you may easily imagine how impatient i must be to see myself in possession of my two high wish
if i have not therefore importune you on this head you will impute it only to my fear of offend the lady by endeavour to hurry on so bless an event fast than a strict compliance with all the rule of decency and decorum will permit
but if by your interest sir she might be induce to dispense with any formalities-- formalities
with a pox answer the squire
pooh all stuff and nonsense
