and to say the truth blifil have pass sentence against sophia for however pleased he have declare himself to western with his reception he be by no mean satisfied unless it be that he be convince of the hatred and scorn of his mistress and this have produce no less reciprocal hatred and scorn in he
it may perhaps be ask why then do he not put an immediate end to all further courtship
i answer for that very reason as well as for several other equally good which we shall now proceed to open to the reader
though mr blifil be not of the complexion of jones nor ready to eat every woman he see yet he be far from be destitute of that appetite which be say to be the common property of all animal
with this he have likewise that distinguish taste which serve to direct man in their choice of the object or food of their several appetite and this teach he to consider sophia as a most delicious morsel indeed to regard she with the same desire which an ortolan inspire into the soul of an epicure
now the agony which affect the mind of sophia rather augmented than impair her beauty for she tear add brightness to her eye and her breast rise higher with her sigh
indeed no one hath see beauty in its high lustre who hath never see it in distress
blifil therefore look on this human ortolan with great desire than when he view she last nor be his desire at all lessen by the aversion which he discover in she to himself
on the contrary this serve rather to heighten the pleasure he propose in rifle her charm as it add triumph to lust nay he have some further view from obtain the absolute possession of her person which we detest too much even to mention and revenge itself be not without its share in the gratification which he promise himself
the rival poor jones and supplant he in her affection add another spur to his pursuit and promise another additional rapture to his enjoyment
besides all these view which to some scrupulous person may seem to savour too much of malevolence he have one prospect which few reader will regard with any great abhorrence
and this be the estate of mr western which be all to be settle on his daughter and her issue for so extravagant be the affection of that fond parent that provide his child would but consent to be miserable with the husband he choose he care not at what price he purchase he
for these reason mr blifil be so desirous of the match that he intend to deceive sophia by pretend love to she and to deceive her father and his own uncle by pretend he be belove by she
in do this he avail himself of the piety of thwackum who hold that if the end propose be religious as surely matrimony be it matter not how wicked be the mean
as to other occasion he use to apply the philosophy of square which teach that the end be immaterial so that the mean be fair and consistent with moral rectitude
to say truth there be few occurrence in life on which he could not draw advantage from the precept of one or other of those great master
little deceit be indeed necessary to be practise on mr western who think the inclination of his daughter of as little consequence as blifil himself conceive they to be but as the sentiment of mr allworthy be of a very different kind so it be absolutely necessary to impose on he
in this however blifil be so well assist by western that he succeed without difficulty for as mr allworthy have be assure by her father that sophia have a proper affection for blifil and that all which he have suspect concern jones be entirely false blifil have nothing more to do than to confirm these assertion which he do with such equivocation that he preserve a salvo for his conscience and have the satisfaction of convey a lie to his uncle without the guilt of tell one
when he be examine touch the inclination of sophia by allworthy who say he would on no account be accessary to force a young lady into a marriage contrary to her own will he answer that the real sentiment of young lady be very difficult to be understand that her behaviour to he be full as forward as he wish it and that if he could believe her father she have all the affection for he which any lover could desire
as for jones say he whom i be loth to call villain though his behaviour to you sir sufficiently justify the appellation his own vanity or perhaps some wicked view might make he boast of a falsehood for if there have be any reality in miss western s love to he the greatness of her fortune would never have suffer he to desert she as you be well informed he hath
lastly sir i promise you i would not myself for any consideration no not for the whole world consent to marry this young lady if i be not persuade she have all the passion for i which i desire she should have this excellent method of convey a falsehood with the heart only without make the tongue guilty of an untruth by the mean of equivocation and imposture hath quiet the conscience of many a notable deceiver and yet when we consider that it be omniscience on which these endeavour to impose it may possibly seem capable of afford only a very superficial comfort and that this artful and refined distinction between communicate a lie and tell one be hardly worth the pain it cost they
allworthy be pretty well satisfied with what mr western and mr blifil tell he and the treaty be now at the end of two day conclude
nothing then remain previous to the office of the priest but the office of the lawyer which threaten to take up so much time that western offer to bind himself by all manner of covenant rather than defer the happiness of the young couple
indeed he be so very earnest and pressing that an indifferent person might have conclude he be more a principal in this match than he really be but this eagerness be natural to he on all occasion and he conduct every scheme he undertake in such a manner as if the success of that alone be sufficient to constitute the whole happiness of his life
the joint importunity of both father and son-in-law would probably have prevail on mr allworthy who brook but ill any delay of give happiness to other have not sophia herself prevent it and take measure to put a final end to the whole treaty and to rob both church and law of those taxis which these wise body have think proper to receive from the propagation of the human specie in a lawful manner
of which in the next chapter
chapter vii
a strange resolution of sophia and a more strange stratagem of mrs honour

to be sure i tremble every inch of i
dear ma am consider that to be deny christian burial and to have your corpse bury in the highway and a stake drive through you as farmer halfpenny be serve at ox cross and to be sure his ghost hath walk there ever since for several people have see he
to be sure it can be nothing but the devil which can put such wicked thought into the head of anybody for certainly it be less wicked to hurt all the world than one s own dear self and so i have hear say by more parson than one
if your la ship hath such a violent aversion and hat the young gentleman so very bad that you cannot bear to think of go into bed to he for to be sure there may be such antipathy in nature and one have liever touch a toad than the flesh of some people sophia have be too much wrapt in contemplation to pay any great attention to the forego excellent discourse of she maid interrupt she therefore without make any answer to it she say honour i be come to a resolution
i be determined to leave my father s house this very night and if you have the friendship for i which you have often profess you will keep i company that i will ma am to the world s end answer honour but i beg your la ship to consider the consequence before you undertake any rash action
where can your la ship possibly go there be reply sophia a lady of quality in london a relation of mine who spend several month with my aunt in the country during all which time she treat i with great kindness and express so much pleasure in my company that she earnestly desire my aunt to suffer i to go with she to london
as she be a woman of very great note i shall easily find she out and i make no doubt of be very well and kindly receive by she i would not have your la ship too confident of that cry honour for the first lady i live with use to invite people very earnestly to her house but if she hear afterwards they be come she use to get out of the way
besides though this lady would be very glad to see your la ship as to be sure anybody would be glad to see your la ship yet when she hear your la ship be run away from my master-- you be mistaken honour say sophia she look upon the authority of a father in a much low light than i do for she press i violently to go to london with she and when i refuse to go without my father s consent she laugh i to scorn call i silly country girl and say i should make a pure love wife since i could be so dutiful a daughter
so i have no doubt but she will both receive i and protect i too till my father find i out of his power can be bring to some reason well but ma am answer honour how doth your la ship think of make your escape
where will you get any horse or conveyance
for as for your own horse as all the servant know a little how matter stand between my master and your la ship robin will be hang before he will suffer it to go out of the stable without my master s express order i intend to escape say sophia by walk out of the door when they be open
i thank heaven my leg be very able to carry i
they have support i many a long evening yes to be sure cry honour i will follow your la ship through the world but your la ship have almost as good be alone for i should not be able to defend you if any robber or other villain should meet with you
nay i should be in as horrible a fright as your la ship for to be certain they would ravish we both
besides ma am consider how cold the night be now we shall be freeze to death a good brisk pace answer sophia will preserve we from the cold and if you cannot defend i from a villain honour i will defend you for i will take a pistol with i
there be two always charge in the hall dear ma am you frighten i more and more cry honour sure your la ship would not venture to fire it off
i have rather run any chance than your la ship should do that why so say sophia smile would not you honour fire a pistol at any one who should attack your virtue to be sure ma am cry honour one s virtue be a dear thing especially to we poor servant for it be our livelihood as a body may say yet i mortally hate fire-arm for so many accident happen by they well well say sophia i believe i may ensure your virtue at a very cheap rate without carry any arm with we for i intend to take horse at the very first town we come to and we shall hardly be attack in our way thither
look ee honour i be resolve to go and if you will attend i i promise you i will reward you to the very utmost of my power this last argument have a strong effect on honour than all the precede
and since she see her mistress so determined she desist from any further dissuasion
they then enter into a debate on way and mean of execute their project
here a very stubborn difficulty occur and this be the removal of their effect which be much more easily get over by the mistress than by the maid for when a lady hath once take a resolution to run to a lover or to run from he all obstacle be consider as trifle
but honour be inspire by no such motive she have no rapture to expect nor any terror to shun and besides the real value of her clothe in which consist a great part of her fortune she have a capricious fondness for several gown and other thing either because they become she or because they be give she by such a particular person because she have buy they lately or because she have have they long or for some other reason equally good so that she could not endure the thought of leave the poor thing behind she expose to the mercy of western who she doubt not would in his rage make they suffer martyrdom
the ingenious mrs honour have apply all her oratory to dissuade her mistress from her purpose when she find she positively determine at last start the following expedient to remove her clothe viz to get herself turn out of door that very evening
sophia highly approve this method but doubt how it might be bring about
o ma am cry honour your la ship may trust that to i we servant very well know how to obtain this favour of our master and mistress though sometimes indeed where they owe we more wage than they can readily pay they will put up with all our affront and will hardly take any warning we can give they but the squire be none of those and since your la ship be resolve upon set out to-night i warrant i get discharge this afternoon it be then resolve that she should pack up some linen and a night-gown for sophia with her own thing and as for all her other clothe the young lady abandon they with no more remorse than the sailor feel when he throw over the good of other in order to save his own life
chapter viii
contain scene of altercation of no very uncommon kind
mrs honour have scarce soon part from her young lady than something for i would not like the old woman in quevedo injure the devil by any false accusation and possibly he might have no hand in it but something i say suggest itself to she that by sacrifice sophia and all her secret to mr western she might probably make her fortune
many consideration urge this discovery
the fair prospect of a handsome reward for so great and acceptable a service to the squire tempt her avarice and again the danger of the enterprize she have undertake the uncertainty of its success night cold robber ravisher all alarm her fear
so forcibly do all these operate upon she that she be almost determined to go directly to the squire and to lay open the whole affair
she be however too upright a judge to decree on one side before she have hear the other
and here first a journey to london appear very strongly in support of sophia
she eagerly long to see a place in which she fancy charm short only of those which a raptured saint imago in heaven
in the next place as she know sophia to have much more generosity than her master so her fidelity promise she a great reward than she could gain by treachery
she then cross-examine all the article which have raise her fear on the other side and find on fairly sift the matter that there be very little in they
and now both scale be reduce to a pretty even balance her love to her mistress be throw into the scale of her integrity make that rather preponderate when a circumstance strike upon her imagination which might have have a dangerous effect have its whole weight be fairly put into the other scale
this be the length of time which must intervene before sophia would be able to fulfil her promise for though she be intitle to her mother s fortune at the death of her father and to the sum of 3000 leave she by an uncle when she come of age yet these be distant day and many accident might prevent the intend generosity of the young lady whereas the reward she might expect from mr western be immediate
but while she be pursue this thought the good genius of sophia or that which preside over the integrity of mrs honour or perhaps mere chance send an accident in her way which at once preserve her fidelity and even facilitate the intended business
mrs western s maid claim great superiority over mrs honour on several account
first her birth be high for her great-grandmother by the mother s side be a cousin not far remove to an irish peer
secondly her wage be great
and lastly she have be at london and have of consequence see more of the world
she have always behave therefore to mrs honour with that reserve and have always exact of she those mark of distinction which every order of female preserve and require in conversation with those of an inferior order
now as honour do not at all time agree with this doctrine but would frequently break in upon the respect which the other demand mrs western s maid be not at all pleased with her company indeed she earnestly long to return home to the house of her mistress where she domineer at will over all the other servant
she have be greatly therefore disappoint in the morning when mrs western have change her mind on the very point of departure and have be in what be vulgarly call a glouting humour ever since
in this humour which be none of the sweetest she come into the room where honour be debate with herself in the manner we have above related
honour no soon see she than she address she in the follow oblige phrase soh madam i find we be to have the pleasure of your company long which i be afraid the quarrel between my master and your lady would have rob we of i donot know madam answer the other what you mean by we and we
i assure you i do not look on any of the servant in this house to be proper company for i
i be company i hope for their better every day in the week
i do not speak on your account mrs honour for you be a civilized young woman and when you have see a little more of the world i should not be ashamed to walk with you in st james s park hoity toity cry honour madam be in her air i protest
mrs honour forsooth
sure madam you might call i by my sir-name for though my lady call i honour i have a sir-name as well as other folk
ashamed to walk with i quotha
marry as good as yourself i hope since you make such a return to my civility say the other i must acquaint you mrs honour that you be not so good as i
in the country indeed one be oblige to take up with all kind of trumpery but in town i visit none but the woman of woman of quality
indeed mrs honour there be some difference i hope between you and i i hope so too answer honour there be some difference in our age and--i think in our person upon speak which last word she strut by mrs western s maid with the most provoking air of contempt turn up her nose toss her head and violently brush the hoop of her competitor with her own
the other lady put on one of her most malicious sneer and say creature
you be below my anger and it be beneath i to give ill word to such an audacious saucy trollop but hussy i must tell you your breeding show the meanness of your birth as well as of your education and both very properly qualify you to be the mean serve-woman of a country girl don t abuse my lady cry honour i willnot take that of you shebe as much well than your as she be young and ten thousand time more handsomer here ill luck or rather good luck send mrs western to see she maid in tear which begin to flow plentifully at her approach and of which be ask the reason by her mistress she presently acquaint she that her tear be occasion by the rude treatment of that creature there--mean honour
and madam continue she i could have despise all she say to i but she hath have the audacity to affront your ladyship and to call you ugly--yes madam she call you ugly old cat to my face
i could not bear to hear your ladyship call ugly why do you repeat her impudence so often say mrs western
and then turn to mrs honour she ask she how she have the assurance to mention her name with disrespect disrespect madam answer honour i never mention your name at all i say somebody be not as handsome as my mistress and to be sure you know that as well as i hussy reply the lady i will make such a saucy trollop as yourself know that i be not a proper subject of your discourse
and if my brother doth not discharge you this moment i will never sleep in his house again
i will find he out and have you discharge this moment discharge cry honour and suppose i be there be more place in the world than one
thank heaven good servant need not want place and if you turn away all who do not think you handsome you will want servant very soon let i tell you that mrs western speak or rather thunder in answer but as she be hardly articulate we cannot be very certain of the identical word we shall therefore omit insert a speech which at well would not greatly redound to her honour
she then depart in search of her brother with a countenance so full of rage that she resemble one of the fury rather than a human creature
the two chambermaid be again leave alone begin a second bout at altercation which soon produce a combat of a more active kind
in this the victory belong to the lady of inferior rank but not without some loss of blood of hair and of lawn and muslin
chapter ix
the wise demeanour of mr western in the character of a magistrate
a hint to justice of peace concern the necessary qualification of a clerk with extraordinary instance of paternal madness and filial affection
logicians sometimes prove too much by an argument and politician often overreach themselves in a scheme
thus have it like to have happen to mrs honour who instead of recover the rest of her clothe have like to have stop even those she have on she back from escape for the squire no soon hear of she have abuse his sister than he swear twenty oath he would send she to bridewell
mrs western be a very good-natured woman and ordinarily of a forgiving temper
she have lately remit the trespass of a stage-coachman who have overturn her post-chaise into a ditch nay she have even break the law in refuse to prosecute a highwayman who have rob she not only of a sum of money but of her ear-ring at the same time d--ne she and say such handsome b--s as you donot want jewel to set they off and be d--n d to you but now so uncertain be our temper and so much do we at different time differ from ourselves she would hear of no mitigation nor could all the affect penitence of honour nor all the entreaty of sophia for her own servant prevail with she to desist from earnestly desire her brother to execute justiceship for it be indeed a syllable more than justice on the wench
but luckily the clerk have a qualification which no clerk to a justice of peace ought ever to be without namely some understanding in the law of this realm
he therefore whisper in the ear of the justice that he would exceed his authority by commit the girl to bridewell as there have be no attempt to break the peace for i be afraid sir say he you cannot legally commit any one to bridewell only for ill-breeding in matter of high importance particularly in case relate to the game the justice be not always attentive to these admonition of his clerk for indeed in execute the law under that head many justice of peace suppose they have a large discretionary power by virtue of which under the notion of search for and take away engine for the destruction of the game they often commit trespass and sometimes felony at their pleasure
but this offence be not of quite so high a nature nor so dangerous to the society
here therefore the justice behave with some attention to the advice of his clerk for in fact he have already have two information exhibit against he in the king s bench and have no curiosity to try a third
the squire therefore put on a most wise and significant countenance after a preface of several hum and hah tell his sister that upon more mature deliberation he be of opinion that as there be no breaking up of the peace such as the law say he call break open a door or break a hedge or break a head or any such sort of breaking the matter do not amount to a felonious kind of a thing nor trespass nor damage and therefore there be no punishment in the law for it mrs western say she know the law much well that she have know servant very severely punish for affront their master and then name a certain justice of the peace in london who she say would commit a servant to bridewell at any time when a master or mistress desire it like enough cry the squire it may be so in london but the law be different in the country here follow a very learn dispute between the brother and sister concern the law which we would insert if we imagine many of our reader could understand it
this be however at length refer by both party to the clerk who decide it in favour of the magistrate and mrs western be in the end oblige to content herself with the satisfaction of have honour turn away to which sophia herself very readily and cheerfully consent
thus fortune after have divert herself accord to custom with two or three frolick at last dispose all matter to the advantage of our heroine who indeed succeed admirably well in her deceit consider it be the first she have ever practise
and to say the truth i have often conclude that the honest part of mankind would be much too hard for the knavish if they could bring themselves to incur the guilt or think it worth their while to take the trouble
honour act her part to the utmost perfection
she no soon see herself secure from all danger of bridewell a word which have raise most horrible idea in her mind than she resume those air which she terror before have a little abate and lay down her place with as much affectation of content and indeed of contempt as be ever practise at the resignation of place of much great importance
if the reader please therefore we chuse rather to say she resign--which hath indeed be always hold a synonymous expression with be turn out or turn away
mr western order she to be very expeditious in pack for his sister declare she would not sleep another night under the same roof with so impudent a slut
to work therefore she go and that so earnestly that everything be ready early in the evening when have receive her wage away pack bag and baggage to the great satisfaction of every one but of none more than of sophia who have appoint she maid to meet she at a certain place not far from the house exactly at the dreadful and ghostly hour of twelve begin to prepare for her own departure
but first she be oblige to give two painful audience the one to her aunt and the other to her father
in these mrs western herself begin to talk to she in a more peremptory stile than before but her father treat she in so violent and outrageous a manner that he frighten she into an affected compliance with his will which so highly please the good squire that he change his frown into smile and his menace into promise he vow his whole soul be wrapt in her that her consent for so he construe the word you know sir i must not nor can refuse to obey any absolute command of your have make he the happy of mankind
he then give she a large bank-bill to dispose of in any trinket she please and kiss and embrace she in the fond manner while tear of joy trickle from those eye which a few moment before have dart fire and rage against the dear object of all his affection
instance of this behaviour in parent be so common that the reader i doubt not will be very little astonish at the whole conduct of mr western
if he should i own i be not able to account for it since that he love his daughter most tenderly be i think beyond dispute
so indeed have many other who have render their child most completely miserable by the same conduct which though it be almost universal in parent hath always appear to i to be the most unaccountable of all the absurdity which ever enter into the brain of that strange prodigious creature man
the latter part of mr western s behaviour have so strong an effect on the tender heart of sophia that it suggest a thought to she which not all the sophistry of her politic aunt nor all the menace of her father have ever once bring into her head
she reverence her father so piously and love he so passionately that she have scarce ever feel more pleasing sensation than what arise from the share she frequently have of contribute to his amusement and sometimes perhaps to high gratification for he never could contain the delight of hear she commend which he have the satisfaction of hear almost every day of her life
the idea therefore of the immense happiness she should convey to her father by her consent to this match make a strong impression on her mind
again the extreme piety of such an act of obedience work very forcibly as she have a very deep sense of religion
lastly when she reflect how much she herself be to suffer be indeed to become little less than a sacrifice or a martyr to filial love and duty she feel an agreeable tickling in a certain little passion which though it bear no immediate affinity either to religion or virtue be often so kind as to lend great assistance in execute the purpose of both
sophia be charm with the contemplation of so heroic an action and begin to compliment herself with much premature flattery when cupid who lie hid in her muff suddenly creep out and like punchinello in a puppet-show kick all out before he
in truth for we scorn to deceive our reader or to vindicate the character of our heroine by ascribe her action to supernatural impulse the thought of her beloved jones and some hope however distant in which he be very particularly concern immediately destroy all which filial love piety and pride have with their joint endeavour be labour to bring about
but before we proceed any far with sophia we must now look back to mr jones
chapter x
contain several matter natural enough perhaps but low
the reader will be pleased to remember that we leave mr jones in the beginning of this book on his road to bristol be determined to seek his fortune at sea or rather indeed to fly away from his fortune on shore
it happen a thing not very unusual that the guide who undertake to conduct he on his way be unluckily unacquainted with the road so that have miss his right track and be ashamed to ask information he ramble about backwards and forwards till night come on and it begin to grow dark
jones suspect what have happen acquaint the guide with his apprehension but he insist on it that they be in the right road and add it would be very strange if he should not know the road to bristol though in reality it would have be much strange if he have know it have never past through it in his life before
jones have not such implicit faith in his guide but that on their arrival at a village he inquire of the first fellow he see whether they be in the road to bristol
whence do you come cry the fellow
no matter say jones a little hastily i want to know if this be the road to bristol the road to bristol cry the fellow scratch his head why measter i believe you will hardly get to bristol this way to-night prithee friend then answer jones do tell we which be the way why measter cry the fellow you must be come out of your road the lord know whither for thick way goeth to glocester well and which way go to bristol say jones
why you be go away from bristol answer the fellow
then say jones we must go back again ay you must say the fellow
well and when we come back to the top of the hill which way must we take why you must keep the strait road but i remember there be two road one to the right and the other to the left why you must keep the right-hand road and then gu strait vorward only remember to turn vurst to your right and then to your leave again and then to your right and that bring you to the squire s and then you must keep strait vorward and turn to the left another fellow now come up and ask which way the gentleman be go of which be inform by jones he first scratch his head and then lean upon a pole he have in his hand begin to tell he that he must keep the right-hand road for about a mile or a mile and a half or such a matter and then he must turn short to the left which would bring he round by measter jin bearnes s but which be mr john bearnes s say jones
o lord cry the fellow why donot you know measter jin bearnes
whence then do you come these two fellow have almost conquer the patience of jones when a plain well-look man who be indeed a quaker accost he thus friend i perceive thou hast lose thy way and if thou wilt take my advice thou wilt not attempt to find it to-night
it be almost dark and the road be difficult to hit besides there have be several robbery commit lately between this and bristol
here be a very creditable good house just by where thou may st find good entertainment for thyself and thy cattle till morning jones after a little persuasion agree to stay in this place till the morning and be conduct by his friend to the public-house
the landlord who be a very civil fellow tell jones he hop he would excuse the badness of his accommodation for that his wife be go from home and have lock up almost everything and carry the key along with she indeed the fact be that a favourite daughter of hers be just married and go that morning home with her husband and that she and her mother together have almost stript the poor man of all his good as well as money for though he have several child this daughter only who be the mother s favourite be the object of her consideration and to the humour of this one child she would with pleasure have sacrifice all the rest and her husband into the bargain
though jones be very unfit for any kind of company and would have prefer be alone yet he could not resist the importunity of the honest quaker who be the more desirous of sit with he from have remark the melancholy which appear both in his countenance and behaviour and which the poor quaker think his conversation might in some measure relieve
after they have past some time together in such a manner that my honest friend might have think himself at one of his silent meeting the quaker begin to be move by some spirit or other probably that of curiosity and say friend i perceive some sad disaster hath befall thee but pray be of comfort
perhaps thou hast lose a friend
if so thou must consider we be all mortal
and why shouldst thou grieve when thou know thy grief will do thy friend no good
we be all bear to affliction
i myself have my sorrow as well as thee and most probably great sorrow
though i have a clear estate of 100 a year which be as much as i want and i have a conscience i thank the lord void of offence my constitution be sound and strong and there be no man can demand a debt of i nor accuse i of an injury yet friend i should be concern to think thee as miserable as myself here the quaker end with a deep sigh and jones presently answer i be very sorry sir for your unhappiness whatever be the occasion of it ah
friend reply the quaker one only daughter be the occasion one who be my great delight upon earth and who within this week be run away from i and be married against my consent
i have provide she a proper match a sober man and one of substance but she forsooth would chuse for herself and away she be go with a young fellow not worth a groat
if she have be dead as i suppose thy friend be i should have be happy that be very strange sir say jones
why would it not be well for she to be dead than to be a beggar reply the quaker for as i tell you the fellow be not worth a groat and surely she cannot expect that i shall ever give she a shilling
no as she hath marry for love let she live on love if she can let she carry her love to market and see whether any one will change it into silver or even into halfpence you know your own concern good sir say jones
it must have be continue the quaker a long premeditate scheme to cheat i for they have know one another from their infancy and i always preach to she against love and tell she a thousand time over it be all folly and wickedness
nay the cunning slut pretend to hearken to i and to despise all wantonness of the flesh and yet at last break out at a window two pair of stair for i begin indeed a little to suspect she and have lock she up carefully intend the very next morning to have marry she up to my liking
but she disappoint i within a few hour and escape away to the lover of her own chusing who lose no time for they be married and bedded and all within an hour
but it shall be the bad hour s work for they both that ever they do for they may starve or beg or steal together for i
i will never give either of they a farthing here jones start up cry i really must be excuse i wish you would leave i come come friend say the quaker donot give way to concern
you see there be other people miserable besides yourself i see there be madman and fool and villain in the world cry jones
but let i give you a piece of advice send for your daughter and son-in-law home and donot be yourself the only cause of misery to one you pretend to love send for she and her husband home cry the quaker loudly i would soon send for the two great enemy i have in the world well go home yourself or where you please say jones for i will sit no long in such company nay friend answer the quaker i scorn to impose my company on any one he then offer to pull money from his pocket but jones push he with some violence out of the room
the subject of the quaker s discourse have so deeply affected jones that he star very wildly all the time he be speak
this the quaker have observe and this add to the rest of his behaviour inspire honest broadbrim with a conceit that his companion be in reality out of his sens
instead of resent the affront therefore the quaker be move with compassion for his unhappy circumstance and have communicate his opinion to the landlord he desire he to take great care of his guest and to treat he with the high civility
indeed say the landlord i shall use no such civility towards he for it seem for all his laced waistcoat there he be no more a gentleman than myself but a poor parish bastard breed up at a great squire s about thirty mile off and now turn out of door not for any good to be sure
i shall get he out of my house as soon as possible
if i do lose my reckoning the first loss be always the good
it be not above a year ago that i lose a silver spoon what dost thou talk of a parish bastard robin answer the quaker
thou must certainly be mistake in thy man not at all reply robin the guide who know he very well tell it i for indeed the guide have no soon take his place at the kitchen fire than he acquaint the whole company with all he know or have ever hear concern jones
the quaker be no soon assure by this fellow of the birth and low fortune of jones than all compassion for he vanish and the honest plain man go home fire with no less indignation than a duke would have feel at receive an affront from such a person
the landlord himself conceive an equal disdain for his guest so that when jones ring the bell in order to retire to bed he be acquaint that he could have no bed there
besides disdain of the mean condition of his guest robin entertain violent suspicion of his intention which be he suppose to watch some favourable opportunity of rob the house
in reality he might have be very well ease of these apprehension by the prudent precaution of his wife and daughter who have already remove everything which be not fix to the freehold but he be by nature suspicious and have be more particularly so since the loss of his spoon
in short the dread of be rob totally absorb the comfortable consideration that he have nothing to lose
jones be assure that he could have no bed very contentedly betook himself to a great chair make with rush when sleep which have lately shun his company in much well apartment generously pay he a visit in his humble cell
as for the landlord he be prevent by his fear from retire to rest
he return therefore to the kitchen fire whence he could survey the only door which open into the parlour or rather hole where jones be seat and as for the window to that room it be impossible for any creature large than a cat to have make his escape through it
chapter xi
the adventure of a company of soldier
the landlord have take his seat directly opposite to the door of the parlour determined to keep guard there the whole night
the guide and another fellow remain long on duty with he though they neither know his suspicion nor have any of their own
the true cause of their watching do indeed at length put an end to it for this be no other than the strength and goodness of the beer of which have tipple a very large quantity they grow at first very noisy and vociferous and afterwards fall both asleep
but it be not in the power of liquor to compose the fear of robin
he continue still wake in his chair with his eye fix stedfastly on the door which lead into the apartment of mr jones till a violent thundering at his outward gate call he from his seat and oblige he to open it which he have no soon do than his kitchen be immediately full of gentleman in red coat who all rush upon he in as tumultuous a manner as if they intend to take his little castle by storm
the landlord be now force from his post to furnish his numerous guest with beer which they call for with great eagerness and upon his second or third return from the cellar he see mr jones stand before the fire in the midst of the soldier for it may easily be believe that the arrival of so much good company should put an end to any sleep unless that from which we be to be awaken only by the last trumpet
the company have now pretty well satisfied their thirst nothing remain but to pay the reckoning a circumstance often productive of much mischief and discontent among the inferior rank of gentry who be apt to find great difficulty in assess the sum with exact regard to distributive justice which direct that every man shall pay accord to the quantity which he drink
this difficulty occur upon the present occasion and it be the great as some gentleman have in their extreme hurry march off after their first draught and have entirely forget to contribute anything towards the said reckoning
a violent dispute now arise in which every word may be say to have be depose upon oath for the oath be at least equal to all the other word speak
in this controversy the whole company speak together and every man seem wholly bent to extenuate the sum which fall to his share so that the most probable conclusion which could be foresee be that a large portion of the reckoning would fall to the landlord s share to pay or what be much the same thing would remain unpaid
all this while mr jones be engage in conversation with the serjeant for that officer be entirely unconcerned in the present dispute be privilege by immemorial custom from all contribution
the dispute now grow so very warm that it seem to draw towards a military decision when jones step forward silence all their clamour at once by declare that he would pay the whole reckoning which indeed amount to no more than three shilling and fourpence
this declaration procure jones the thank and applause of the whole company
the term honourable noble and worthy gentleman resound through the room nay my landlord himself begin to have a well opinion of he and almost to disbelieve the account which the guide have give
the serjeant have inform mr jones that they be march against the rebel and expect to be command by the glorious duke of cumberland
by which the reader may perceive a circumstance which we have not think necessary to communicate before that this be the very time when the late rebellion be at the high and indeed the bandit be now march into england intend as it be think to fight the king s force and to attempt push forward to the metropolis
jones have some heroic ingredient in his composition and be a hearty well-wisher to the glorious cause of liberty and of the protestant religion
it be no wonder therefore that in circumstance which would have warrant a much more romantic and wild undertaking it should occur to he to serve as a volunteer in this expedition
our command officer have say all in his power to encourage and promote this good disposition from the first moment he have be acquaint with it
he now proclaim the noble resolution aloud which be receive with great pleasure by the whole company who all cry out god bless king george and your honour and then add with many oath we will stand by you both to the last drop of our blood the gentleman who have be all night tipple at the alehouse be prevail on by some argument which a corporal have put into his hand to undertake the same expedition
and now the portmanteau belong to mr jones be put up in the baggage-cart the force be about to move forwards when the guide step up to jones say sir i hope you will consider that the horse have be keep out all night and we have travel a great way out of our way jones be surprize at the impudence of this demand and acquaint the soldier with the merit of his cause who be all unanimous in condemn the guide for his endeavour to put upon a gentleman
some say he ought to be tie neck and heel other that he deserve to run the gantlope and the serjeant shake his cane at he and wish he have he under his command swear heartily he would make an example of he
jones content himself however with a negative punishment and walk off with his new comrade leave the guide to the poor revenge of cursing and revile he in which latter the landlord join say ay ay he be a pure one i warrant you
a pretty gentleman indeed to go for a soldier
he shall wear a laced wastecoat truly
it be an old proverb and a true one all be not gold that glister
i be glad my house be well rid of he all that day the serjeant and the young soldier march together and the former who be an arch fellow tell the latter many entertaining story of his campaign though in reality he have never make any for he be but lately come into the service and have by his own dexterity so well ingratiate himself with his officer that he have promote himself to a halberd chiefly indeed by his merit in recruiting in which he be most excellently well skilled
much mirth and festivity pass among the soldier during their march
in which the many occurrence that have pass at their last quarter be remember and every one with great freedom make what joke he please on his officer some of which be of the coarser kind and very near border on scandal
this bring to our heroe s mind the custom which he have read of among the greeks and romans of indulge on certain festival and solemn occasion the liberty to slave of use an uncontrouled freedom of speech towards their master
our little army which consist of two company of foot be now arrive at the place where they be to halt that evening
the serjeant then acquaint his lieutenant who be the commanding officer that they have pick up two fellow in that day s march one of which he say be as fine a man as ever he see mean the tippler for that he be near six foot well proportion and strongly limbe and the other mean jones would do well enough for the rear rank
the new soldier be now produce before the officer who have examine the six-foot man he be first produce come next to survey jones at the first sight of whom the lieutenant could not help show some surprize for besides that he be very well dress and be naturally genteel he have a remarkable air of dignity in his look which be rarely see among the vulgar and be indeed not inseparably annex to the feature of their superior
sir say the lieutenant my serjeant inform i that you be desirous of enlist in the company i have at present under my command if so sir we shall very gladly receive a gentleman who promise to do much honour to the company by bear arm in it jones answer that he have not mention anything of enlist himself that he be most zealously attach to the glorious cause for which they be go to fight and be very desirous of serve as a volunteer conclude with some compliment to the lieutenant and express the great satisfaction he should have in be under his command
the lieutenant return his civility commend his resolution shake he by the hand and invite he to dine with himself and the rest of the officer
chapter xii
the adventure of a company of officer
the lieutenant whom we mention in the precede chapter and who command this party be now near sixty year of age
he have enter very young into the army and have serve in the capacity of an ensign at the battle of tanniere here he have receive two wound and have so well distinguish himself that he be by the duke of marlborough advance to be a lieutenant immediately after that battle
in this commission he have continue ever since viz near forty year during which time he have see vast number prefer over his head and have now the mortification to be command by boy whose father be at nurse when he first enter into the service
nor be this ill success in his profession solely owe to his have no friend among the man in power
he have the misfortune to incur the displeasure of his colonel who for many year continue in the command of this regiment
nor do he owe the implacable ill-will which this man bear he to any neglect or deficiency as an officer nor indeed to any fault in himself but solely to the indiscretion of his wife who be a very beautiful woman and who though she be remarkably fond of her husband would not purchase his preferment at the expense of certain favour which the colonel require of she
the poor lieutenant be more peculiarly unhappy in this that while he feel the effect of the enmity of his colonel he neither know nor suspect that he really bear he any for he could not suspect an ill-will for which he be not conscious of give any cause and his wife fear what her husband s nice regard to his honour might have occasion content herself with preserve her virtue without enjoy the triumph of her conquest
this unfortunate officer for so i think he may be call have many good quality besides his merit in his profession for he be a religious honest good-natured man and have behave so well in his command that he be highly esteemed and belove not only by the soldier of his own company but by the whole regiment
the other officer who march with he be a french lieutenant who have be long enough out of france to forget his own language but not long enough in england to learn ours so that he really speak no language at all and could barely make himself understand on the most ordinary occasion
there be likewise two ensign both very young fellow one of whom have be breed under an attorney and the other be son to the wife of a nobleman s butler
as soon as dinner be end jones inform the company of the merriment which have pass among the soldier upon their march and yet say he notwithstanding all their vociferation i dare swear they will behave more like grecians than trojans when they come to the enemy grecians and trojans say one of the ensign who the devil be they
i have hear of all the troop in europe but never of any such as these donot pretend to more ignorance than you have mr northerton say the worthy lieutenant
i suppose you have hear of the greeks and trojans though perhaps you never read pope s homer who i remember now the gentleman mention it compare the march of the trojans to the cackling of geese and greatly commend the silence of the grecians
and upon my honour there be great justice in the cadet s observation begar i remember dem ver well say the french lieutenant i ave read they at school in dans madam daciere des greek des trojan dey fight for von woman--ouy ouy i ave read all dat d--n homo with all my heart say northerton i have the mark of he on my a-- yet
there s thomas of our regiment always carry a homo in his pocket d--n i if ever i come at it if i donot burn it
and therebe corderius another d--n d son of a whore that hath get i many a flogging then you have be at school mr northerton say the lieutenant
ay d--n i have i answer he the devil take my father for send i thither
the old put want to make a parson of i but d--n i think i to myself iwill nick you there old cull the devil a smack of your nonsense shall you ever get into i
therebe jemmy oliver of our regiment he narrowly escape be a pimp too and that would have be a thousand pity for d--n i if he be not one of the prettiest fellow in the whole world but he go far than i with the old cull for jimmey can neither write nor read you give your friend a very good character say the lieutenant and a very deserved one i dare say
but prithee northerton leave off that foolish as well as wicked custom of swearing for you be deceive i promise you if you think there be wit or politeness in it
i wish too you would take my advice and desist from abuse the clergy
scandalous name and reflection cast on any body of man must be always unjustifiable but especially so when throw on so sacred a function for to abuse the body be to abuse the function itself and i leave to you to judge how inconsistent such behaviour be in man who be go to fight in defence of the protestant religion mr adderly which be the name of the other ensign have sit hitherto kick his heel and hum a tune without seem to listen to the discourse he now answer o monsieur on ne parle pas de la religion dan la guerre well say jack cry northerton if la religion be the only matter the parson should fight their own battle for i i donot know gentleman say jones what may be your opinion but i think no man can engage in a nobler cause than that of his religion and i have observe in the little i have read of history that no soldier have fight so bravely as those who have be inspire with a religious zeal for my own part though i love my king and country i hope as well as any man in it yet the protestant interest be no small motive to my become a volunteer in the cause northerton now wink on adderly and whisper to he slily smoke the prig adderly smoke he then turn to jones say to he i be very glad sir you have choose our regiment to be a volunteer in for if our parson should at any time take a cup too much i find you can supply his place
i presume sir you have be at the university may i crave the favour to know what college sir answer jones so far from have be at the university i have even have the advantage of yourself for i be never at school i presume cry the ensign only upon the information of your great learning oh
sir answer jones it be as possible for a man to know something without have be at school as it be to have be at school and to know nothing well say young volunteer cry the lieutenant
upon my word northerton you have well let he alone for he will be too hard for you northerton do not very well relish the sarcasm of jones but he think the provocation be scarce sufficient to justify a blow or a rascal or scoundrel which be the only repartee that suggest themselves
he be therefore silent at present but resolve to take the first opportunity of return the jest by abuse
it now come to the turn of mr jones to give a toast as it be call who could not refrain from mention his dear sophia
this he do the more readily as he imagine it utterly impossible that any one present should guess the person he mean
but the lieutenant who be the toast-master be not content with sophia only
he say he must have her sir-name upon which jones hesitate a little and presently after name miss sophia western
ensign northerton declare he would not drink her health in the same round with his own toast unless somebody would vouch for she
i know one sophy western say he that be lie with by half the young fellow at bath and perhaps this be the same woman jones very solemnly assure he of the contrary assert that the young lady he name be one of great fashion and fortune
ay ay say the ensign and so she be d--n i it be the same woman and iwill hold half a dozen of burgundy tom french of our regiment bring she into company with we at any tavern in bridges-street he then proceed to describe her person exactly for he have see she with her aunt and conclude with say that her father have a great estate in somersetshire the tenderness of lover can ill brook the least jest with the name of their mistress
however jones though he have enough of the lover and of the heroe too in his disposition do not resent these slander as hastily as perhaps he ought to have do
to say the truth have see but little of this kind of wit he do not readily understand it and for a long time imagine mr northerton have really mistaken his charmer for some other
but now turn to the ensign with a stern aspect he say pray sir chuse some other subject for your wit for i promise you i will bear no jesting with this lady s character jesting cry the other d--n i if ever i be more in earnest in my life
tom french of our regiment have both she and her aunt at bath then i must tell you in earnest cry jones that you be one of the most impudent rascal upon earth he have no soon speak these word than the ensign together with a volley of curse discharge a bottle full at the head of jones which hit he a little above the right temple bring he instantly to the ground
the conqueror perceive the enemy to lie motionless before he and blood begin to flow pretty plentifully from his wound begin now to think of quit the field of battle where no more honour be to be get but the lieutenant interpose by step before the door and thus cut off his retreat
northerton be very importunate with the lieutenant for his liberty urge the ill consequence of his stay ask he what he could have do less
zounds say he i be but in jest with the fellow
i never hear any harm of miss western in my life have not you say the lieutenant then you richly deserve to be hang as well for make such jest as for use such a weapon you be my prisoner sir nor shall you stir from hence till a proper guard come to secure you such an ascendant have our lieutenant over this ensign that all that fervency of courage which have level our poor heroe with the floor would scarce have animate the say ensign to have draw his sword against the lieutenant have he then have one dangle at his side but all the sword be hang up in the room be at the very beginning of the fray secure by the french officer
so that mr northerton be oblige to attend the final issue of this affair
the french gentleman and mr adderly at the desire of their commanding officer have raise up the body of jones but as they could perceive but little if any sign of life in he they again let he fall adderly damn he for have blood his wastecoat and the frenchman declare begar i no tush the engliseman de mort i have hear de englise ley law what you call hang up de man dat tush he last when the good lieutenant apply himself to the door he apply himself likewise to the bell and the drawer immediately attend he dispatch he for a file of musqueteer and a surgeon
these command together with the drawer s report of what he have himself see not only produce the soldier but presently draw up the landlord of the house his wife and servant and indeed every one else who happen at that time to be in the inn
to describe every particular and to relate the whole conversation of the ensue scene be not within my power unless i have forty pen and could at once write with they all together as the company now speak
the reader must therefore content himself with the most remarkable incident and perhaps he may very well excuse the rest
the first thing do be secure the body of northerton who be deliver into the custody of six man with a corporal at their head be by they conduct from a place which he be very willing to leave but it be unluckily to a place whither he be very unwilling to go
to say the truth so whimsical be the desire of ambition the very moment this youth have attain the above-mention honour he would have be well content to have retire to some corner of the world where the fame of it should never have reach his ear
it surprize we and so perhaps it may the reader that the lieutenant a worthy and good man should have apply his chief care rather to secure the offender than to preserve the life of the wound person
we mention this observation not with any view of pretend to account for so odd a behaviour but lest some critic should hereafter plume himself on discover it
we would have these gentleman know we can see what be odd in character as well as themselves but it be our business to relate fact as they be which when we have do it be the part of the learn and sagacious reader to consult that original book of nature whence every passage in our work be transcribe though we quote not always the particular page for its authority
the company which now arrive be of a different disposition
they suspend their curiosity concern the person of the ensign till they should see he hereafter in a more engaging attitude
at present their whole concern and attention be employ about the bloody object on the floor which be place upright in a chair soon begin to discover some symptom of life and motion
these be no soon perceive by the company for jones be at first generally conclude to be dead than they all fall at once to prescribe for he for as none of the physical order be present every one there take that office upon he
bleeding be the unanimous voice of the whole room but unluckily there be no operator at hand every one then cry call the barber but none stir a step
several cordial be likewise prescribe in the same ineffective manner till the landlord order up a tankard of strong beer with a toast which he say be the good cordial in england
the person principally assistant on this occasion indeed the only one who do any service or seem likely to do any be the landlady she cut off some of her hair and apply it to the wound to stop the blood she fall to chafe the youth s temple with her hand and have expr great contempt for her husband s prescription of beer she despatch one of her maid to her own closet for a bottle of brandy of which as soon as it be bring she prevail on jones who be just return to his sens to drink a very large and plentiful draught
soon afterwards arrive the surgeon who have view the wound have shake his head and blame everything which be do order his patient instantly to bed in which place we think proper to leave he some time to his repose and shall here therefore put an end to this chapter
chapter xiii
contain the great address of the landlady the great learning of a surgeon and the solid skill in casuistry of the worthy lieutenant
when the wounded man be carry to his bed and the house begin again to clear up from the hurry which this accident have occasion the landlady thus address the command officer i be afraid sir say she this young man do not behave himself as well as he should do to your honour and if he have be kill i suppose he have but his desart to be sure when gentleman admit inferior parson into their company they oft to keep their distance but as my first husband use to say few of them know how to do it
for my own part i be sure i should not have suffer any fellow to include themselves into gentleman s company but i thoft he have be an officer himself till the serjeant tell i he be but a recruit landlady answer the lieutenant you mistake the whole matter
the young man behave himself extremely well and be i believe a much well gentleman than the ensign who abuse he
if the young fellow dy the man who strike he will have most reason to be sorry for it for the regiment will get rid of a very troublesome fellow who be a scandal to the army and if he escape from the hand of justice blame i madam thatbe all ay
ay
good lack-a-day say the landlady who could have thoft it
ay ay ay i be satisfied your honour will see justice do and to be sure it oft to be to every one
gentleman oft not to kill poor folk without answer for it
a poor man hath a soul to be save as well as his better indeed madam say the lieutenant you do the volunteer wrong i dare swear he be more of a gentleman than the officer ay cry the landlady why look you there now well my first husband be a wise man he use to say you cannot always know the inside by the outside
nay that might have be well enough too for i never saw d he till he be all over blood
who would have thoft it
mayhap some young gentleman cross in love
good lack-a-day if he should die what a concern it will be to his parent
why sure the devil must possess the wicked wretch to do such an act
to be sure he be a scandal to the army as your honour say for most of the gentleman of the army that ever i see be quite different sort of people and look as if they would scorn to spill any christian blood as much as any man i mean that is in a civil way as my first husband use to say
to be sure when they come into the war there must be bloodshe but that they be not to be blame for
the more of our enemy they kill there the well and i wish with all my heart they could kill every mother s son of they o fie madam say the lieutenant smile all be rather too bloody-minded a wish not at all sir answer she i be not at all bloody-minded only to our enemy and there be no harm in that
to be sure it be natural for we to wish our enemy dead that the war may be at an end and our taxis be lower for it be a dreadful thing to pay as we do
why now there be above forty shilling for window-light and yet we have stopt up all we could we have almost blind the house i be sure
say i to the exciseman say i i think you oft to favour we i be sure we be very good friend to the government and so we be for sartain for we pay a mint of money to um
and yet i often think to myself the government doth not imagine itself more obliged to we than to those that donot pay um a farthing
ay ay it be the way of the world she be proceed in this manner when the surgeon enter the room
the lieutenant immediately ask how his patient do
but he resolve he only by say well i believe than he would have be by this time if i have not be call and even as it be perhaps it would have be lucky if i could have be call soon i hope sir say the lieutenant the skull be not fractured hum cry the surgeon fracture be not always the most dangerous symptom
contusion and laceration be often attend with bad phaenomena and with more fatal consequence than fracture
people who know nothing of the matter conclude if the skull be not fractured all be well whereas i have rather see a man s skull break all to piece than some contusion i have meet with i hope say the lieutenant there be no such symptom here symptom answer the surgeon be not always regular nor constant
i have know very unfavourable symptom in the morning change to favourable one at noon and return to unfavourable again at night
of wound indeed it be rightly and truly say nemo repente fuit turpissimus
i be once i remember call to a patient who have receive a violent contusion in his tibia by which the exterior cutis be lacerate so that there be a profuse sanguinary discharge and the interior membrane be so divellicated that the os or bone very plainly appear through the aperture of the vulnus or wound
some febrile symptom intervene at the same time for the pulse be exuberant and indicate much phlebotomy i apprehend an immediate mortification
to prevent which i presently make a large orifice in the vein of the left arm whence i draw twenty ounce of blood which i expect to have find extremely sizy and glutinous or indeed coagulate as it be in pleuretic complaint but to my surprize it appear rosy and florid and its consistency differ little from the blood of those in perfect health
i then apply a fomentation to the part which highly answer the intention and after three or four time dress the wound begin to discharge a thick pus or matter by which mean the cohesion--but perhaps i do not make myself perfectly well understood no really answer the lieutenant i cannot say i understand a syllable well sir say the surgeon then i shall not tire your patience in short within six week my patient be able to walk upon his leg as perfectly as he could have do before he receive the contusion i wish sir say the lieutenant you would be so kind only to inform i whether the wound this young gentleman hath have the misfortune to receive be likely to prove mortal sir answer the surgeon to say whether a wound will prove mortal or not at first dressing would be very weak and foolish presumption we be all mortal and symptom often occur in a cure which the great of our profession could never foresee but do you think he in danger say the other in danger
ay surely cry the doctor who be there among we who in the most perfect health can be say not to be in danger
can a man therefore with so bad a wound as this be say to be out of danger
all i can say at present be that it be well i be call as i be and perhaps it would have be well if i have be call soon
i will see he again early in the morning and in the meantime let he be keep extremely quiet and drink liberally of water-gruel won t you allow he sack-whey say the landlady ay ay sack-whey cry the doctor if you will provide it be very small and a little chicken broth too add she yes yes chicken broth say the doctor be very good mayn t i make he some jelly too say the landlady ay ay answer the doctor jelly be very good for wound for they promote cohesion and indeed it be lucky she have not name soup or high sauce for the doctor would have comply rather than have lose the custom of the house
the doctor be no soon go than the landlady begin to trumpet forth his fame to the lieutenant who have not from their short acquaintance conceive quite so favourable an opinion of his physical ability as the good woman and all the neighbourhood entertain and perhaps very rightly for though i be afraid the doctor be a little of a coxcomb he might be nevertheless very much of a surgeon
the lieutenant have collect from the learn discourse of the surgeon that mr jones be in great danger give order for keep mr northerton under a very strict guard design in the morning to attend he to a justice of peace and to commit the conducting the troop to gloucester to the french lieutenant who though he could neither read write nor speak any language be however a good officer
in the evening our commander send a message to mr jones that if a visit would not be troublesome he would wait on he
this civility be very kindly and thankfully receive by jones and the lieutenant accordingly go up to his room where he find the wounded man much well than he expect nay jones assure his friend that if he have not receive express order to the contrary from the surgeon he should have get up long ago for he appear to himself to be as well as ever and feel no other inconvenience from his wound but an extreme soreness on that side of his head
i should be very glad quoth the lieutenant if you be as well as you fancy yourself for then you could be able to do yourself justice immediately for when a matter cannot be make up as in case of a blow the soon you take he out the well but i be afraid you think yourself well than you be and he would have too much advantage over you iwill try however answer jones if you please and will be so kind to lend i a sword for i have none here of my own my sword be heartily at your service my dear boy cry the lieutenant kiss he you be a brave lad and i love your spirit but i fear your strength for such a blow and so much loss of blood must have very much weaken you and though you feel no want of strength in your bed yet you most probably would after a thrust or two
i cannot consent to your take he out tonight but i hope you will be able to come up with we before we get many day march advance and i give you my honour you shall have satisfaction or the man who hath injure you shallnot stay in our regiment i wish say jones it be possible to decide this matter to-night now you have mention it to i i shall not be able to rest oh never think of it return the other a few day will make no difference
the wound of honour be not like those in your body they suffer nothing by the delay of cure
it will be altogether as well for you to receive satisfaction a week hence as now but suppose say jones i should grow bad and die of the consequence of my present wound then your honour answer the lieutenant will require no reparation at all
i myself will do justice to your character and testify to the world your intention to have act properly if you have recover still reply jones i be concern at the delay
i be almost afraid to mention it to you who be a soldier but though i have be a very wild young fellow still in my most serious moment and at the bottom i be really a christian so be i too i assure you say the officer and so zealous a one that i be pleased with you at dinner for take up the cause of your religion and i be a little offended with you now young gentleman that you should express a fear of declare your faith before any one but how terrible must it be cry jones to any one who be really a christian to cherish malice in his breast in opposition to the command of he who hath expressly forbid it
how can i bear to do this on a sick-bed
or how shall i make up my account with such an article as this in my bosom against i why i believe there be such a command cry the lieutenant but a man of honour cannot keep it
and you must be a man of honour if you will be in the army
i remember i once put the case to our chaplain over a bowl of punch and he confess there be much difficulty in it but he say he hop there might be a latitude grant to soldier in this one instance and to be sure it be our duty to hope so for who would bear to live without his honour
no no my dear boy be a good christian as long as you live but be a man of honour too and never put up an affront not all the book nor all the parson in the world shall ever persuade i to that
i love my religion very well but i love my honour more
there must be some mistake in the wording the text or in the translation or in the understanding it or somewhere or other
but however that be a man must run the risque for he must preserve his honour
so compose yourself to-night and i promise you you shall have an opportunity of do yourself justice here he give jones a hearty buss shake he by the hand and take his leave
but though the lieutenant s reasoning be very satisfactory to himself it be not entirely so to his friend
jones therefore have revolve this matter much in his thought at last come to a resolution which the reader will find in the next chapter
chapter xiv
a most dreadful chapter indeed and which few reader ought to venture upon in an evening especially when alone
jones swallow a large mess of chicken or rather cock broth with a very good appetite as indeed he would have do the cock it be make of with a pound of bacon into the bargain and now find in himself no deficiency of either health or spirit he resolve to get up and seek his enemy
but first he send for the serjeant who be his first acquaintance among these military gentleman
unluckily that worthy officer have in a literal sense take his fill of liquor have be some time retire to his bolster where he be snore so loud that it be not easy to convey a noise in at his ear capable of drown that which issue from his nostril
however as jones persist in his desire of see he a vociferous drawer at length find mean to disturb his slumber and to acquaint he with the message
of which the serjeant be no soon make sensible than he arise from his bed and have his clothe already on immediately attend
jones do not think fit to acquaint the serjeant with his design though he might have do it with great safety for the halberdier be himself a man of honour and have kill his man
he would therefore have faithfully keep this secret or indeed any other which no reward be publish for discover
but as jones know not those virtue in so short an acquaintance his caution be perhaps prudent and commendable enough
he begin therefore by acquaint the serjeant that as he be now enter into the army he be ashamed of be without what be perhaps the most necessary implement of a soldier namely a sword add that he should be infinitely oblige to he if he could procure one
for which say he i will give you any reasonable price nor do i insist upon its be silver-hilte only a good blade and such as may become a soldier s thigh the serjeant who well know what have happen and have hear that jones be in a very dangerous condition immediately conclude from such a message at such a time of night and from a man in such a situation that he be light-headed
now as he have his wit to use that word in its common signification always ready he bethink himself of make his advantage of this humour in the sick man
sir say he i believe i can fit you
i have a most excellent piece of stuff by i
it be not indeed silver-hilte which as you say doth not become a soldier but the handle be decent enough and the blade one of the good in europe
it be a blade that--a blade that--in short i will fetch it you this instant and you shall see it and handle it
i be glad to see your honour so well with all my heart be instantly return with the sword he deliver it to jones who take it and draw it and then tell the serjeant it would do very well and bid he name his price
the serjeant now begin to harangue in praise of his good
he say nay he swear very heartily that the blade be take from a french officer of very high rank at the battle of dettingen
i take it myself say he from his side after i have knock he o the head
the hilt be a golden one
that i sell to one of our fine gentleman for there be some of they an t please your honour who value the hilt of a sword more than the blade here the other stop he and beg he to name a price
the serjeant who think jones absolutely out of his sens and very near his end be afraid lest he should injure his family by ask too little
however after a moment s hesitation he content himself with name twenty guineas and swear he would not sell it for less to his own brother
twenty guineas say jones in the utmost surprize sure you think i be mad or that i never see a sword in my life
twenty guineas indeed
i do not imagine you would endeavour to impose upon i
here take the sword--no now i think on t i will keep it myself and show it your officer in the morning acquaint he at the same time what a price you ask i for it the serjeant as we have say have always his wit in sensu praedicto about he and now plainly see that jones be not in the condition he have apprehend he to be he now therefore counterfeit as great surprize as the other have show and say i be certain sir i have not ask you so much out of the way
besides you be to consider it be the only sword i have and i must run the risque of my officer s displeasure by go without one myself
and truly put all this together i donot think twenty shilling be so much out of the way twenty shilling cry jones why you just now ask i twenty guineas how cry the serjeant sure your honour must have mistake i or else i mistake myself--and indeed i be but half awake
twenty guineas indeed
no wonder your honour fly into such a passion
i say twenty guineas too
no no i mean twenty shilling i assure you
and when your honour come to consider everything i hope you will not think that so extravagant a price
it be indeed true you may buy a weapon which look as well for less money
but---- here jones interrupt he say i will be so far from make any word with you that i will give you a shilling more than your demand he then give he a guinea bid he return to his bed and wish he a good march add he hop to overtake they before the division reach worcester
the serjeant very civilly take his leave fully satisfied with his merchandize and not a little pleased with his dexterous recovery from that false step into which his opinion of the sick man s light-headedness have betray he
as soon as the serjeant be depart jones rise from his bed and dress himself entirely put on even his coat which as its colour be white show very visibly the stream of blood which have flow down it and now have grasp his new-purchase sword in his hand he be go to issue forth when the thought of what he be about to undertake lay suddenly hold of he and he begin to reflect that in a few minute he might possibly deprive a human being of life or might lose his own
very well say he and in what cause do i venture my life
why in that of my honour
and who be this human be
a rascal who hath injure and insult i without provocation
but be not revenge forbid by heaven
yes but it be enjoin by the world
well but shall i obey the world in opposition to the express command of heaven
