shall i incur the divine displeasure rather than be call--ha--coward--scoundrel i ll think no more i be resolve and must fight he the clock have now strike twelve and every one in the house be in their bed except the centinel who stand to guard northerton when jones softly open his door issue forth in pursuit of his enemy of whose place of confinement he have receive a perfect description from the drawer
it be not easy to conceive a much more tremendous figure than he now exhibit
he have on as we have say a light-colour coat cover with stream of blood
his face which miss that very blood as well as twenty ounce more draw from he by the surgeon be pallid
round his head be a quantity of bandage not unlike a turban
in the right hand he carry a sword and in the leave a candle
so that the bloody banquo be not worthy to be compare to he
in fact i believe a more dreadful apparition be never raise in a church-yard nor in the imagination of any good people meet in a winter evening over a christmas fire in somersetshire
when the centinel first see our heroe approach his hair begin gently to lift up his grenadi cap and in the same instant his knee fall to blow with each other
presently his whole body be seize with bad than an ague fit
he then fire his piece and fall flat on his face
whether fear or courage be the occasion of his firing or whether he take aim at the object of his terror i cannot say
if he do however he have the good fortune to miss his man
jones see the fellow fall guess the cause of his fright at which he could not forbear smile not in the least reflect on the danger from which he have just escape
he then pass by the fellow who still continue in the posture in which he fall and enter the room where northerton as he have hear be confine
here in a solitary situation he find--an empty quart pot stand on the table on which some beer be spill it look as if the room have lately be inhabit but at present it be entirely vacant
jones then apprehend it might lead to some other apartment but upon search all round it he could perceive no other door than that at which he enter and where the centinel have be post
he then proceed to call northerton several time by his name but no one answer nor do this serve to any other purpose than to confirm the centinel in his terror who be now convince that the volunteer be dead of his wound and that his ghost be come in search of the murderer he now lie in all the agony of horror and i wish with all my heart some of those actor who be hereafter to represent a man fright out of his wit have see he that they might be teach to copy nature instead of perform several antic trick and gesture for the entertainment and applause of the gallery
perceive the bird be fly at least despair to find he and rightly apprehend that the report of the firelock would alarm the whole house our heroe now blow out his candle and gently steal back again to his chamber and to his bed whither he would not have be able to have get undiscovered have any other person be on the same staircase save only one gentleman who be confine to his bed by the gout for before he could reach the door to his chamber the hall where the centinel have be post be half full of people some in their shirt and other not half drest all very earnestly enquiring of each other what be the matter
the soldier be now find lie in the same place and posture in which we just now leave he
several immediately apply themselves to raise he and some conclude he dead but they presently see their mistake for he not only struggle with those who lay their hand on he but fall a roaring like a bull
in reality he imagine so many spirit or devil be handle he for his imagination be possess with the horror of an apparition convert every object he see or feel into nothing but ghost and spectre
at length he be overpower by number and get upon his leg when candle be bring and see two or three of his comrade present he come a little to himself but when they ask he what be the matter
he answer i be a dead man thatbe all i be a dead man i cannot recover it i have see he what hast thou see jack say one of the soldier
why i have see the young volunteer that be kill yesterday he then imprecate the most heavy curse on himself if he have not see the volunteer all over blood vomit fire out of his mouth and nostril pass by he into the chamber where ensign northerton be and then seize the ensign by the throat fly away with he in a clap of thunder
this relation meet with a gracious reception from the audience
all the woman present believe it firmly and pray heaven to defend they from murder
amongst the man too many have faith in the story but other turn it into derision and ridicule and a serjeant who be present answer very coolly young man you will hear more of this for go to sleep and dream on your post the soldier reply you may punish i if you please but i be as broad awake as i be now and the devil carry i away as he hath the ensign if i do not see the dead man as i tell you with eye as big and as fiery as two large flambeaux the commander of the force and the commander of the house be now both arrive for the former be awake at the time and hear the centinel fire his piece think it his duty to rise immediately though he have no great apprehension of any mischief whereas the apprehension of the latter be much great lest her spoon and tankard should be upon the march without have receive any such order from she
our poor centinel to whom the sight of this officer be not much more welcome than the apparition as he think it which he have see before again relate the dreadful story and with many addition of blood and fire but he have the misfortune to gain no credit with either of the last-mention person for the officer though a very religious man be free from all terror of this kind besides have so lately leave jones in the condition we have see he have no suspicion of his be dead
as for the landlady though not over religious she have no kind of aversion to the doctrine of spirit but there be a circumstance in the tale which she well know to be false as we shall inform the reader presently
but whether northerton be carry away in thunder or fire or in whatever other manner he be go it be now certain that his body be no long in custody
upon this occasion the lieutenant form a conclusion not very different from what the serjeant be just mention to have make before and immediately order the centinel to be take prisoner
so that by a strange reverse of fortune though not very uncommon in a military life the guard become the guard
chapter xv
the conclusion of the foregoing adventure
besides the suspicion of sleep the lieutenant harbour another and bad doubt against the poor centinel and this be that of treachery for as he believe not one syllable of the apparition so he imagine the whole to be an invention form only to impose upon he and that the fellow have in reality be bribe by northerton to let he escape
and this he imagine the rather as the fright appear to he the more unnatural in one who have the character of as brave and bold a man as any in the regiment have be in several action have receive several wound and in a word have behave himself always like a good and valiant soldier
that the reader therefore may not conceive the least ill opinion of such a person we shall not delay a moment in rescue his character from the imputation of this guilt
mr northerton then as we have before observe be fully satisfied with the glory which he have obtain from this action
he have perhaps see or hear or guess that envy be apt to attend fame
not that i would here insinuate that he be heathenishly inclined to believe in or to worship the goddess nemesis for in fact i be convinced he never hear of her name
he be besides of an active disposition and have a great antipathy to those close quarter in the castle of gloucester for which a justice of peace might possibly give he a billet
nor be he moreover free from some uneasy meditation on a certain wooden edifice which i forbear to name in conformity to the opinion of mankind who i think rather ought to honour than to be ashamed of this building as it be or at least might be make of more benefit to society than almost any other public erection
in a word to hint at no more reason for his conduct mr northerton be desirous of depart that evening and nothing remain for he but to contrive the quomodo which appear to be a matter of some difficulty
now this young gentleman though somewhat crooked in his moral be perfectly straight in his person which be extremely strong and well make
his face too be account handsome by the generality of woman for it be broad and ruddy with tolerably good tooth
such charm do not fail make an impression on my landlady who have no little relish for this kind of beauty
she have indeed a real compassion for the young man and hear from the surgeon that affair be like to go ill with the volunteer she suspect they might hereafter wear no benign aspect with the ensign
have obtain therefore leave to make he a visit and find he in a very melancholy mood which she considerably heighten by tell he there be scarce any hope of the volunteer s life she proceed to throw forth some hint which the other readily and eagerly take up they soon come to a right understanding and it be at length agree that the ensign should at a certain signal ascend the chimney which communicate very soon with that of the kitchen he might there again let himself down for which she would give he an opportunity by keep the coast clear
but lest our reader of a different complexion should take this occasion of too hastily condemn all compassion as a folly and pernicious to society we think proper to mention another particular which might possibly have some little share in this action
the ensign happen to be at this time possess of the sum of fifty pound which do indeed belong to the whole company for the captain have quarrel with his lieutenant have entrust the payment of his company to the ensign
this money however he think proper to deposit in my landlady s hand possibly by way of bail or security that he would hereafter appear and answer to the charge against he but whatever be the condition certain it be that she have the money and the ensign his liberty
the reader may perhaps expect from the compassionate temper of this good woman that when she see the poor centinel take prisoner for a fact of which she know he innocent she should immediately have interpose in his behalf but whether it be that she have already exhaust all her compassion in the above-mention instance or that the feature of this fellow though not very different from those of the ensign could not raise it i will not determine but far from be an advocate for the present prisoner she urge his guilt to his officer declare with uplifted eye and hand that she would not have have any concern in the escape of a murderer for all the world
everything be now once more quiet and most of the company return again to their bed but the landlady either from the natural activity of her disposition or from her fear for her plate have no propensity to sleep prevail with the officer as they be to march within little more than an hour to spend that time with she over a bowl of punch
jones have lie awake all this while and have hear great part of the hurry and bustle that have pass of which he have now some curiosity to know the particular
he therefore apply to his bell which he ring at least twenty time without any effect for my landlady be in such high mirth with her company that no clapper could be hear there but her own and the drawer and chambermaid who be sit together in the kitchen for neither durst he sit up nor she lie in bed alone the more they hear the bell ring the more they be frightened and as it be nail down in their place
at last at a lucky interval of chat the sound reach the ear of our good landlady who presently send forth her summon which both her servant instantly obey
joe say the mistress donot you hear the gentleman s bell ring
why donot you go up it be not my business answer the drawer to wait upon the chamber--it be betty chambermaid s if you come to that answer the maid it be not my business to wait upon gentleman
i have do it indeed sometimes but the devil fetch i if ever i do again since you make your preamble about it the bell still ring violently their mistress fall into a passion and swore if the drawer do not go up immediately she would turn he away that very morning
if you do madam say he i cannot help it
i willnot do another servant s business she then apply herself to the maid and endeavour to prevail by gentle mean but all in vain betty be as inflexible as joe
both insist it be not their business and they would not do it
the lieutenant then fall a laughing and say come i will put an end to this contention and then turn to the servant commend they for their resolution in not give up the point but add he be sure if one would consent to go the other would
to which proposal they both agree in an instant and accordingly go up very lovingly and close together
when they be go the lieutenant appease the wrath of the landlady by satisfy she why they be both so unwilling to go alone
they return soon after and acquaint their mistress that the sick gentleman be so far from be dead that he speak as heartily as if he be well and that he give his service to the captain and should be very glad of the favour of see he before he march
the good lieutenant immediately comply with his desire and sit down by his bed-side acquaint he with the scene which have happen below conclude with his intention to make an example of the centinel
upon this jones relate to he the whole truth and earnestly beg he not to punish the poor soldier who i be confident say he be as innocent of the ensign s escape as he be of forge any lie or of endeavour to impose on you the lieutenant hesitate a few moment and then answer why as you have clear the fellow of one part of the charge so it will be impossible to prove the other because he be not the only centinel
but i have a good mind to punish the rascal for be a coward
yet who know what effect the terror of such an apprehension may have
and to say the truth he hath always behave well against an enemy
come it be a good thing to see any sign of religion in these fellow so i promise you he shall be set at liberty when we march
but hark the general beat
my dear boy give i another buss
donot discompose nor hurry yourself but remember the christian doctrine of patience and i warrant you will soon be able to do yourself justice and to take an honourable revenge on the fellow who hath injure you the lieutenant then depart and jones endeavour to compose himself to rest
book viii
contain about two day
chapter i
a wonderful long chapter concern the marvellous be much the long of all our introductory chapter
as we be now enter upon a book in which the course of our history will oblige we to relate some matter of a more strange and surprize kind than any which have hitherto occur it may not be amiss in the prolegomenous or introductory chapter to say something of that specie of writing which be call the marvellous
to this we shall as well for the sake of ourselves as of other endeavour to set some certain bound and indeed nothing can be more necessary as critic of different complexion be here apt to run into very different extreme for while some be with m
dacier ready to allow that the same thing which be impossible may be yet probable other have so little historic or poetic faith that they believe nothing to be either possible or probable the like to which hath not occur to their own observation
by this word here and in most other part of our work we mean every reader in the world
it be happy for m
dacier that he be not an irishman
first then i think it may very reasonably be require of every writer that he keep within the bound of possibility and still remember that what it be not possible for man to perform it be scarce possible for man to believe he do perform
this conviction perhaps give birth to many story of the antient heathen deity for most of they be of poetical original
the poet be desirous to indulge a wanton and extravagant imagination take refuge in that power of the extent of which his reader be no judge or rather which they imagine to be infinite and consequently they could not be shock at any prodigy relate of it
this hath be strongly urge in defence of homer s miracle and it be perhaps a defence not as mr pope would have it because ulysses tell a set of foolish lie to the phaeacians who be a very dull nation but because the poet himself write to heathens to whom poetical fable be article of faith
for my own part i must confess so compassionate be my temper i wish polypheme have confine himself to his milk diet and preserve his eye nor could ulysses be much more concerned than myself when his companion be turn into swine by circe who show i think afterwards too much regard for man s flesh to be suppose capable of convert it into bacon
i wish likewise with all my heart that homer could have know the rule prescribe by horace to introduce supernatural agent as seldom as possible
we should not then have see his god come on trivial errand and often behave themselves so as not only to forfeit all title to respect but to become the object of scorn and derision
a conduct which must have shock the credulity of a pious and sagacious heathen and which could never have be defend unless by agree with a supposition to which i have be sometimes almost inclined that this most glorious poet as he certainly be have an intent to burlesque the superstitious faith of his own age and country
but i have rest too long on a doctrine which can be of no use to a christian writer for as he cannot introduce into his work any of that heavenly host which make a part of his creed so it be horrid puerility to search the heathen theology for any of those deity who have be long since dethrone from their immortality
lord shaftesbury observe that nothing be more cold than the invocation of a muse by a modern he might have add that nothing can be more absurd
a modern may with much more elegance invoke a ballad as some have think homer do or a mug of ale with the author of hudibras which latter may perhaps have inspire much more poetry as well as prose than all the liquor of hippocrene or helicon
the only supernatural agent which can in any manner be allow to we modern be ghost but of these i would advise an author to be extremely sparing
these be indeed like arsenic and other dangerous drug in physic to be use with the utmost caution nor would i advise the introduction of they at all in those work or by those author to which or to whom a horse-laugh in the reader would be any great prejudice or mortification
as for elf and fairy and other such mummery i purposely omit the mention of they as i should be very unwilling to confine within any bound those surprizing imagination for whose vast capacity the limit of human nature be too narrow whose work be to be consider as a new creation and who have consequently just right to do what they will with their own
man therefore be the high subject unless on very extraordinary occasion indeed which present itself to the pen of our historian or of our poet and in relate his action great care be to be take that we do not exceed the capacity of the agent we describe
nor be possibility alone sufficient to justify we we must keep likewise within the rule of probability
it be i think the opinion of aristotle or if not it be the opinion of some wise man whose authority will be as weighty when it be as old that it be no excuse for a poet who relate what be incredible that the thing relate be really matter of fact this may perhaps be allow true with regard to poetry but it may be think impracticable to extend it to the historian for he be oblige to record matter as he find they though they may be of so extraordinary a nature as will require no small degree of historical faith to swallow they
such be the successless armament of xerxes describe by herodotus or the successful expedition of alexander relate by arrian
such of later year be the victory of agincourt obtain by harry the fifth or that of narva win by charles the twelfth of sweden
all which instance the more we reflect on they appear still the more astonishing
such fact however as they occur in the thread of the story nay indeed as they constitute the essential part of it the historian be not only justifiable in recording as they really happen but indeed would be unpardonable should he omit or alter they
but there be other fact not of such consequence nor so necessary which though ever so well attest may nevertheless be sacrifice to oblivion in complacence to the scepticism of a reader
such be that memorable story of the ghost of george villiers which might with more propriety have be make a present of to dr drelincourt to have keep the ghost of mrs veale company at the head of his discourse upon death than have be introduce into so solemn a work as the history of the rebellion
to say the truth if the historian will confine himself to what really happen and utterly reject any circumstance which though never so well attest he must be well assured be false he will sometimes fall into the marvellous but never into the incredible
he will often raise the wonder and surprize of his reader but never that incredulous hatred mention by horace
it be by fall into fiction therefore that we generally offend against this rule of deserting probability which the historian seldom if ever quit till he forsake his character and commence a writer of romance
in this however those historian who relate public transaction have the advantage of we who confine ourselves to scene of private life
the credit of the former be by common notoriety support for a long time and public record with the concurrent testimony of many author bear evidence to their truth in future age
thus a trajan and an antoninus a nero and a caligula have all meet with the belief of posterity and no one doubt but that man so very good and so very bad be once the master of mankind
but we who deal in private character who search into the most retire recess and draw forth example of virtue and vice from hole and corner of the world be in a more dangerous situation
as we have no public notoriety no concurrent testimony no record to support and corroborate what we deliver it become we to keep within the limit not only of possibility but of probability too and this more especially in paint what be greatly good and amiable
knavery and folly though never so exorbitant will more easily meet with assent for ill-nature add great support and strength to faith
thus we may perhaps with little danger relate the history of fisher who have long owe his bread to the generosity of mr derby and have one morning receive a considerable bounty from his hand yet in order to possess himself of what remain in his friend s scrutore conceal himself in a public office of the temple through which there be a passage into mr derby s chamber
here he overhear mr derby for many hour solace himself at an entertainment which he that evening give his friend and to which fisher have be invite
during all this time no tender no grateful reflection arise to restrain his purpose but when the poor gentleman have let his company out through the office fisher come suddenly from his lurking-place and walk softly behind his friend into his chamber discharge a pistol-ball into his head
this may be believe when the bone of fisher be as rotten as his heart
nay perhaps it will be credit that the villain go two day afterwards with some young lady to the play of hamlet and with an unaltered countenance hear one of the lady who little suspect how near she be to the person cry out good god
if the man that murder mr derby be now present manifest in this a more seared and callous conscience than even nero himself of whom we be tell by suetonius that the consciousness of his guilt after the death of his mother become immediately intolerable and so continue nor could all the congratulation of the soldier of the senate and the people allay the horror of his conscience but now on the other hand should i tell my reader that i have know a man whose penetrate genius have enable he to raise a large fortune in a way where no beginning be chaulke out to he that he have do this with the most perfect preservation of his integrity and not only without the least injustice or injury to any one individual person but with the high advantage to trade and a vast increase of the public revenue that he have expend one part of the income of this fortune in discover a taste superior to most by work where the high dignity be unite with the pure simplicity and another part in display a degree of goodness superior to all man by act of charity to object whose only recommendation be their merit or their want that he be most industrious in search after merit in distress most eager to relieve it and then as careful perhaps too careful to conceal what he have do that his house his furniture his garden his table his private hospitality and his public beneficence all denote the mind from which they flow and be all intrinsically rich and noble without tinsel or external ostentation that he fill every relation in life with the most adequate virtue that he be most piously religious to his creator most zealously loyal to his sovereign a most tender husband to his wife a kind relation a munificent patron a warm and firm friend a knowing and a chearful companion indulgent to his servant hospitable to his neighbour charitable to the poor and benevolent to all mankind
should i add to these the epithet of wise brave elegant and indeed every other amiable epithet in our language i might surely say quis credet
nemo hercule
nemo vel duo vel nemo and yet i know a man who be all i have here describe
but a single instance and i really know not such another be not sufficient to justify we while we be write to thousand who never hear of the person nor of anything like he
such rarae ave should be remit to the epitaph writer or to some poet who may condescend to hitch he in a distich or to slide he into a rhime with an air of carelessness and neglect without give any offence to the reader
in the last place the action should be such as may not only be within the compass of human agency and which human agent may probably be suppose to do but they should be likely for the very actor and character themselves to have perform for what may be only wonderful and surprize in one man may become improbable or indeed impossible when relate of another
this last requisite be what the dramatic critic call conversation of character and it require a very extraordinary degree of judgment and a most exact knowledge of human nature
it be admirably remark by a most excellent writer that zeal can no more hurry a man to act in direct opposition to itself than a rapid stream can carry a boat against its own current
i will venture to say that for a man to act in direct contradiction to the dictate of his nature be if not impossible as improbable and as miraculous as anything which can well be conceive
should the good part of the story of m
antoninus be ascribe to nero or should the bad incident of nero s life be impute to antoninus what would be more shocking to belief than either instance
whereas both these be relate of their proper agent constitute the truly marvellous
our modern author of comedy have fall almost universally into the error here hint at their hero generally be notorious rogue and their heroine abandon jade during the first four act but in the fifth the former become very worthy gentleman and the latter woman of virtue and discretion nor be the writer often so kind as to give himself the least trouble to reconcile or account for this monstrous change and incongruity
there be indeed no other reason to be assign for it than because the play be draw to a conclusion as if it be no less natural in a rogue to repent in the last act of a play than in the last of his life which we perceive to be generally the case at tyburn a place which might indeed close the scene of some comedy with much propriety as the hero in these be most commonly eminent for those very talent which not only bring man to the gallow but enable they to make an heroic figure when they be there
within these few restriction i think every writer may be permit to deal as much in the wonderful as he please nay if he thus keep within the rule of credibility the more he can surprize the reader the more he will engage his attention and the more he will charm he
as a genius of the high rank observe in his fifth chapter of the bathos the great art of all poetry be to mix truth with fiction in order to join the credible with the surprizing for though every good author will confine himself within the bound of probability it be by no mean necessary that his character or his incident should be trite common or vulgar such as happen in every street or in every house or which may be meet with in the home article of a newspaper
nor must he be inhibit from show many person and thing which may possibly have never fall within the knowledge of great part of his reader
if the writer strictly observe the rule above-mention he hath discharge his part and be then intitle to some faith from his reader who be indeed guilty of critical infidelity if he disbelieve he
for want of a portion of such faith i remember the character of a young lady of quality which be condemn on the stage for be unnatural by the unanimous voice of a very large assembly of clerk and apprentice though it have the previous suffrage of many lady of the first rank one of whom very eminent for her understanding declare it be the picture of half the young people of her acquaintance
chapter ii
in which the landlady pay a visit to mr jones
when jones have take leave of his friend the lieutenant he endeavour to close his eye but all in vain his spirit be too lively and wakeful to be lull to sleep
so have amuse or rather torment himself with the thought of his sophia till it be open daylight he call for some tea upon which occasion my landlady herself vouchsafe to pay he a visit
this be indeed the first time she have see he or at least have take any notice of he but as the lieutenant have assure she that he be certainly some young gentleman of fashion she now determine to show he all the respect in her power for to speak truly this be one of those house where gentleman to use the language of advertisement meet with civil treatment for their money
she have no soon begin to make his tea than she likewise begin to discourse la
sir say she i think it be great pity that such a pretty young gentleman should under-value himself so as to go about with these soldier fellow
they call themselves gentleman i warrant you but as my first husband use to say they should remember it be we that pay they
and to be sure it be very hard upon we to be oblige to pay they and to keep um too as we publican be
i have twenty of um last night besides officer nay for matter o that i have rather have the soldier than officer for nothing be ever good enough for those spark and i be sure if you be to see the bill la
sir it be nothing
i have have less trouble i warrant you with a good squire s family where we take forty or fifty shilling of a night besides horse
and yet i warrant i there be narrow a one of those officer fellow but look upon himself to be as good as arrow a squire of 500 a year
to be sure it doth i good to hear their man run about after um cry your honour and your honour
marry come up with such honour and an ordinary at a shill a head
then therebe such swearing among um to be sure it frighten i out o my wit i think nothing can ever prosper with such wicked people

i think indeed how well the rest would secure he they all hang together for if you have be in danger of death which i be glad to see you be not it would have be all as one to such wicked people
they would have let the murderer go
laud have mercy upon um i would not have such a sin to answer for for the whole world
but though you be likely with the blessing to recover there be laa for he yet and if you will employ lawyer small i darest be swear hewill make the fellow fly the country for he though perhaps hewill have flee the country before for it be here to-day and go to-morrow with such chap
i hope however you will learn more wit for the future and return back to your friend i warrant they be all miserable for your loss and if they be but to know what have happen--la my seeming
i would not for the world they should
come come we know very well what all the matter be but if one willnot another will so pretty a gentleman need never want a lady
i be sure if i be you i would see the fine she that ever wear a head hang before i would go for a soldier for her nay donot blush so for indeed he do to a violent degree
why you think sir i know nothing of the matter i warrant you about madam sophia how say jones start up do you know my sophia do i
ay marry cry the landlady manybe the time hath she lain in this house with her aunt i suppose say jones
why there it be now cry the landlady
ay ay ay i know the old lady very well
and a sweet young creature be madam sophia thatbe the truth on t a sweet creature cry jones o heaven angels be paint fair to look like she
therebe in she all that we believe of heav n amazing brightness purity and truth eternal joy and everlaste love
and could i ever have imagine that you have know my sophia i wish say the landlady you know half so much of she

what a delicious neck she hath
her lovely limb have stretch themselves in that very bed you now lie in here cry jones hath sophia ever lay here ay ay here there in that very bed say the landlady where i wish you have she this moment and she may wish so too for anything i know to the contrary for she hath mention your name to i ha cry he do she ever mention her poor jones
you flatter i now i can never believe so much why then answer she as i hope to be save and may the devil fetch i if i speak a syllable more than the truth i have hear she mention mr jones but in a civil and modest way i confess yet i could perceive she think a great deal more than she say o my dear woman cry jones her thought of i i shall never be worthy of

i who would undergo all the plague and misery which any daemon ever invent for mankind to procure she any good nay torture itself could not be misery to i do i but know that she be happy why look you there now say the landlady i tell she you be a constant lovier but pray madam tell i when or where you know anything of i for i never be here before nor do i remember ever to have see you nor be it possible you should answer she for you be a little thing when i have you in my lap at the squire s how the squire s say jones what do you know that great and good mr allworthy then yes marry do i say she who in the country doth not the fame of his goodness indeed answer jones must have extend far than this but heaven only can know he--can know that benevolence which it copy from itself and send upon earth as its own pattern
mankind be as ignorant of such divine goodness as they be unworthy of it but none so unworthy of it as myself
i who be raise by he to such a height take in as you must well know a poor base-bear child adopt by he and treat as his own son to dare by my folly to disoblige he to draw his vengeance upon i
yes i deserve it all for i will never be so ungrateful as ever to think he hath do an act of injustice by i
no i deserve to be turn out of door as i be
and now madam say he i believe you will not blame i for turn soldier especially with such a fortune as this in my pocket at which word he shake a purse which have but very little in it and which still appear to the landlady to have less
my good landlady be accord to vulgar phrase strike all of a heap by this relation
she answer coldly that to be sure people be the good judge what be most proper for their circumstance
but hark say she i think i hear somebody call
come
come
the devil s in all our volk nobody hath any ear
i must go down-stair if you want any more breakfast the maid will come up
come at which word without take any leave she fling out of the room for the low sort of people be very tenacious of respect and though they be content to give this gratis to person of quality yet they never confer it on those of their own order without take care to be well pay for their pain
chapter iii
in which the surgeon make his second appearance
before we proceed any far that the reader may not be mistake in imagine the landlady know more than she do nor surprize that she know so much it may be necessary to inform he that the lieutenant have acquaint she that the name of sophia have be the occasion of the quarrel and as for the rest of her knowledge the sagacious reader will observe how she come by it in the precede scene
great curiosity be indeed mix with her virtue and she never willingly suffer any one to depart from her house without enquire as much as possible into their name family and fortune
she be no soon go than jones instead of animadvert on her behaviour reflect that he be in the same bed which he be inform have hold his dear sophia
this occasion a thousand fond and tender thought which we would dwell long upon do we not consider that such kind of lover will make a very inconsiderable part of our reader
in this situation the surgeon find he when he come to dress his wound
the doctor perceive upon examination that his pulse be disorder and hear that he have not sleep declare that he be in great danger for he apprehend a fever be come on which he would have prevent by bleed but jones would not submit declare he would lose no more blood and doctor say he if you will be so kind only to dress my head i have no doubt of be well in a day or two i wish answer the surgeon i could assure your being well in a month or two
well indeed
no no people be not so soon well of such contusion but sir i be not at this time of day to be instruct in my operation by a patient and i insist on make a revulsion before i dress you jones persist obstinately in his refusal and the doctor at last yield tell he at the same time that he would not be answerable for the ill consequence and hop he would do he the justice to acknowledge that he have give he a contrary advice which the patient promise he would
the doctor retire into the kitchen where address himself to the landlady he complain bitterly of the undutiful behaviour of his patient who would not be blood though he be in a fever
it be an eat fever then say the landlady for he hath devour two swing butter toast this morning for breakfast very likely say the doctor i have know people eat in a fever and it be very easily account for because the acidity occasion by the febrile matter may stimulate the nerve of the diaphragm and thereby occasion a craving which will not be easily distinguishable from a natural appetite but the aliment will not be concrete nor assimilate into chyle and so will corrode the vascular orifice and thus will aggravate the febrific symptom
indeed i think the gentleman in a very dangerous way and if he be not blood i be afraid will die every man must die some time or other answer the good woman it be no business of mine
i hope doctor you would not have i hold he while you bleed he
but hark ee a word in your ear i would advise you before you proceed too far to take care who be to be your paymaster paymaster say the doctor star why ihave a gentleman under my hand have i not i imagine so as well as you say the landlady but as my first husband use to say everything be not what it look to be
he be an arrant scrub i assure you
however take no notice that i mention anything to you of the matter but i think people in business oft always to let one another know such thing and have i suffer such a fellow as this cry the doctor in a passion to instruct i
shall i hear my practice insult by one who will not pay i
i be glad i have make this discovery in time
i will see now whether he will be blood or no he then immediately go upstairs and fling open the door of the chamber with much violence awake poor jones from a very sound nap into which he be fall and what be still bad from a delicious dream concern sophia
will you be blood or no cry the doctor in a rage
i have tell you my resolution already answer jones and i wish with all my heart you have take my answer for you have awake i out of the sweetest sleep which i ever have in my life ay ay cry the doctor many a man hath doze away his life
sleep be not always good no more than food but remember i demand of you for the last time will you be blooded i answer you for the last time say jones i will not then i wash my hand of you cry the doctor and i desire you to pay i for the trouble i have have already
two journey at 5 s
each two dressing at 5 s
more and half a crown for phlebotomy i hope say jones you donot intend to leave i in this condition indeed but i shall say the other
then say jones you have use i rascally and i will not pay you a farthing very well cry the doctor the first loss be the good
what a pox do my landlady mean by send for i to such vagabond at which word he fling out of the room and his patient turn himself about soon recover his sleep but his dream be unfortunately go
chapter iv
in which be introduce one of the pleasant barber that be ever record in history the barber of bagdad or he in don quixote not excepted
the clock have now strike five when jones awake from a nap of seven hour so much refresh and in such perfect health and spirit that he resolve to get up and dress himself for which purpose he unlock his portmanteau and take out clean linen and a suit of cloath but first he slipt on a frock and go down into the kitchen to bespeak something that might pacify certain tumult he find rise within his stomach
meet the landlady he accost she with great civility and ask what he could have for dinner for dinner say she it be an odd time a day to think about dinner
there be nothing drest in the house and the fire be almost out well but say he i must have something to eat and it be almost indifferent to i what for to tell you the truth i be never more hungry in my life then say she i believe there be a piece of cold buttock and carrot which will fit you nothe well answer jones but i should be oblige to you if you would let it be fry to which the landlady consent and say smile she be glad to see he so well recover for the sweetness of our heroe s temper be almost irresistible besides she be really no ill-humour woman at the bottom but she love money so much that she hat everything which have the semblance of poverty
jones now return in order to dress himself while his dinner be prepare and be accord to his order attend by the barber
this barber who go by the name of little benjamin be a fellow of great oddity and humour which have frequently let he into small inconveniencie such as slap in the face kick in the breech broken bone c
for every one doth not understand a jest and those who do be often displeased with be themselves the subject of it
this vice be however incurable in he and though he have often smart for it yet if ever he conceive a joke he be certain to be deliver of it without the least respect of person time or place
he have a great many other particularity in his character which i shall not mention as the reader will himself very easily perceive they on his farth acquaintance with this extraordinary person
jones be impatient to be drest for a reason which may be easily imagine think the shaver be very tedious in prepare his sud and beg he to make haste to which the other answer with much gravity for he never discompose his muscle on any account festina lente be a proverb which i learn long before i ever touch a razor i find friend you be a scholar reply jones
a poor one say the barber non omnia possumus omnes again say jones i fancy you be good at cap verse excuse i sir say the barber non tanto i dignor honore and then proceed to his operation sir say he since i have deal in sud i could never discover more than two reason for shaving the one be to get a beard and the other to get rid of one
i conjecture sir it may not be long since you shave from the former of these motif
upon my word you have have good success for one may say of your beard that it be tondenti gravior i conjecture say jones that thou art a very comical fellow you mistake i widely sir say the barber i be too much addicted to the study of philosophy hinc illae lacrymae sir thatbe my misfortune
too much learning hath be my ruin indeed say jones i confess friend you have more learning than generally belong to your trade but i cannot see how it can have injure you alas
sir answer the shaver my father disinherit i for it
he be a dancing-master and because i could read before i could dance he take an aversion to i and leave every farthing among his other children will you please to have your temple--o la
i ask your pardon i fancy there be hiatus in manuscriptis
i hear you be go to the war but i find it be a mistake why do you conclude so say jones
sure sir answer the barber you be too wise a man to carry a broken head thither for that would be carry coal to newcastle upon my word cry jones thou art a very odd fellow and i like thy humour extremely i shall be very glad if thou wilt come to i after dinner and drink a glass with i i long to be well acquaint with thee o dear sir say the barber i can do you twenty time as great a favour if you will accept of it what be that my friend cry jones
why i will drink a bottle with you if you please for i dearly love good-nature and as you have find i out to be a comical fellow so i have no skill in physiognomy if you be not one of the well-natured gentleman in the universe jones now walk downstairs neatly dr and perhaps the fair adonis be not a lovelier figure and yet he have no charm for my landlady for as that good woman do not resemble venus at all in her person so neither do she in her taste
happy have it be for nanny the chambermaid if she have see with the eye of her mistress for that poor girl fall so violently in love with jones in five minute that her passion afterwards cost she many a sigh
this nanny be extremely pretty and altogether as coy for she have refuse a drawer and one or two young farmer in the neighbourhood but the bright eye of our heroe thaw all her ice in a moment
when jones return to the kitchen his cloth be not yet lay nor indeed be there any occasion it should his dinner remain in statu quo as do the fire which be to dress it
this disappointment might have put many a philosophical temper into a passion but it have no such effect on jones
he only give the landlady a gentle rebuke say since it be so difficult to get it heated he would eat the beef cold but now the good woman whether move by compassion or by shame or by whatever other motive i cannot tell first give she servant a round scold for disobey the order which she have never give and then bid the drawer lie a napkin in the sun she set about the matter in good earnest and soon accomplish it
this sun into which jones be now conduct be truly name as lucus a non lucendo for it be an apartment into which the sun have scarce ever look
it be indeed the bad room in the house and happy be it for jones that it be so
however he be now too hungry to find any fault but have once satisfy his appetite he order the drawer to carry a bottle of wine into a well room and express some resentment at have be show into a dungeon
the drawer have obey his command he be after some time attend by the barber who would not indeed have suffer he to wait so long for his company have he not be listen in the kitchen to the landlady who be entertain a circle that she have gather round she with the history of poor jones part of which she have extract from his own lip and the other part be her own ingenious composition for she say he be a poor parish boy take into the house of squire allworthy where he be breed up as an apprentice and now turn out of door for his misdeed particularly for make love to his young mistress and probably for rob the house for how else should he come by the little money he hath and this say she be your gentleman forsooth a servant of squire allworthy say the barber whatbe his name why he tell i his name be jones say she perhaps he go by a wrong name
nay and he tell i too that the squire have maintain he as his own son thof he have quarrel with he now and if his name be jones he tell you the truth say the barber for i have relation who live in that country nay and some people say he be his son why doth he not go by the name of his father i cannot tell that say the barber many people s son donot go by the name of their father nay say the landlady if i think he be a gentleman s son thof he be a bye-blow i should behave to he in another guess manner for many of these bye-blow come to be great man and as my poor first husband use to say never affront any customer thatbe a gentleman chapter v
a dialogue between mr jones and the barber
this conversation pass partly while jones be at dinner in his dungeon and partly while he be expect the barber in the parlour
and as soon as it be end mr benjamin as we have say attend he and be very kindly desire to sit down
jones then fill out a glass of wine drink his health by the appellation of doctissime tonsorum
ago tibi gratia domine say the barber and then look very steadfastly at jones he say with great gravity and with a seem surprize as if he have recollect a face he have see before sir may i crave the favour to know if your name be not jones to which the other answer that it be proh deum atque hominum fidem say the barber how strangely thing come to pass
mr jones i be your most obedient servant
i find you do not know i which indeed be no wonder since you never see i but once and then you be very young
pray sir how doth the good squire allworthy
how doth ille optimus omnium patronus i find say jones you do indeed know i but i have not the like happiness of recollect you i do not wonder at that cry benjamin but i be surprize i do not know you soon for you be not in the least alter
and pray sir may i without offence enquire whither you be travel this way fill the glass mr barber say jones and ask no more question nay sir answer benjamin i would not be troublesome and i hope you donot think i a man of an impertinent curiosity for that be a vice which nobody can lay to my charge but i ask pardon for when a gentleman of your figure travel without his servant we may suppose he to be as we say in casu incognito and perhaps i ought not to have mention your name i own say jones i do not expect to have be so well know in this country as i find i be yet for particular reason i shall be oblige to you if you will not mention my name to any other person till i be go from hence pauca verba answer the barber and i wish no other here know you but myself for some people have tongue but i promise you i can keep a secret
my enemy will allow i that virtue and yet that be not the characteristic of your profession mr barber answer jones
alas
sir reply benjamin non si male nunc et olim sic erit
i be not bear nor breed a barber i assure you
i have spend most of my time among gentleman and though i say it i understand something of gentility
and if you have think i as worthy of your confidence as you have some other people i should have show you i could have keep a secret well
i should not have degrade your name in a public kitchen for indeed sir some people have not use you well for besides make a public proclamation of what you tell they of a quarrel between yourself and squire allworthy they add lie of their own thing which i know to be lie you surprize i greatly cry jones
upon my word sir answer benjamin i tell the truth and i need not tell you my landlady be the person
i be sure it move i to hear the story and i hope it be all false for i have a great respect for you i do assure you i have and have have ever since the good-nature you show to black george which be talk of all over the country and i receive more than one letter about it
indeed it make you belove by everybody
you will pardon i therefore for it be real concern at what i hear make i ask many question for i have no impertinent curiosity about i but i love good-nature and thence become amoris abundantia erga te every profession of friendship easily gain credit with the miserable it be no wonder therefore if jones who besides his be miserable be extremely open-hearted very readily believe all the profession of benjamin and receive he into his bosom
the scrap of latin some of which benjamin apply properly enough though it do not savour of profound literature seem yet to indicate something superior to a common barber and so indeed do his whole behaviour
jones therefore believe the truth of what he have say as to his original and education and at length after much entreaty he say since you have hear my friend so much of my affair and seem so desirous to know the truth if you will have patience to hear it i will inform you of the whole patience cry benjamin that i will if the chapter be never so long and i be very much oblige to you for the honour you do i jones now begin and relate the whole history forget only a circumstance or two namely everything which pass on that day in which he have fight with thwackum and end with his resolution to go to sea till the rebellion in the north have make he change his purpose and have bring he to the place where he then be
little benjamin who have be all attention never once interrupt the narrative but when it be end he could not help observe that there must be surely something more invent by his enemy and tell mr allworthy against he or so good a man would never have dismiss one he have love so tenderly in such a manner
to which jones answer he doubt not but such villanous art have be make use of to destroy he and surely it be scarce possible for any one to have avoid make the same remark with the barber who have not indeed hear from jones one single circumstance upon which he be condemn for his action be not now place in those injurious light in which they have be misrepresent to allworthy nor could he mention those many false accusation which have be from time to time prefer against he to allworthy for with none of these he be himself acquaint
he have likewise as we have observe omit many material fact in his present relation
upon the whole indeed everything now appear in such favourable colour to jones that malice itself would have find it no easy matter to fix any blame upon he
not that jones desire to conceal or to disguise the truth nay he would have be more unwilling to have suffer any censure to fall on mr allworthy for punish he than on his own action for deserve it but in reality so it happen and so it always will happen for let a man be never so honest the account of his own conduct will in spite of himself be so very favourable that his vice will come purify through his lip and like foul liquor well strained will leave all their foulness behind
for though the fact themselves may appear yet so different will be the motif circumstance and consequence when a man tell his own story and when his enemy tell it that we scarce can recognise the fact to be one and the same
though the barber have drink down this story with greedy ear he be not yet satisfied
there be a circumstance behind which his curiosity cold as it be most eagerly long for
jones have mention the fact of his amour and of his be the rival of blifil but have cautiously conceal the name of the young lady
the barber therefore after some hesitation and many hum and hah at last begged leave to crave the name of the lady who appear to be the principal cause of all this mischief
jones pause a moment and then say since i have trust you with so much and since i be afraid her name be become too publick already on this occasion i will not conceal it from you
her name be sophia western proh deum atque hominum fidem
squire western hath a daughter grow a woman ay and such a woman cry jones that the world cannot match
no eye ever see anything so beautiful but that be her least excellence
such sense
such goodness
oh i could praise she for ever and yet should omit half her virtues mr western a daughter grow up cry the barber i remember the father a boy well tempus edax rerum the wine be now at an end the barber press very eagerly to be his bottle but jones absolutely refuse say he have already drank more than he ought and that he now choose to retire to his room where he wish he could procure himself a book a book cry benjamin what book would you have
latin or english
i have some curious book in both language such as erasmi colloquia ovid de tristibus gradus ad parnassum and in english i have several of the good book though some of they be a little torn but i have a great part of stowe s chronicle the sixth volume of pope s homer the third volume of the spectator the second volume of echard s roman history the craftsman robinson crusoe thomas a kempis and two volume of tom brown s works those last cry jones be book i never see so if you please lend i one of those volume the barber assure he he would be highly entertain for he look upon the author to have be one of the great wit that ever the nation produce
he then step to his house which be hard by and immediately return after which the barber have receive very strict injunction of secrecy from jones and have swear inviolably to maintain it they separate the barber go home and jones retire to his chamber
chapter vi
in which more of the talent of mr benjamin will appear as well as who this extraordinary person be
in the morning jones grow a little uneasy at the desertion of his surgeon as he apprehend some inconvenience or even danger might attend the not dress his wound he enquire of the drawer what other surgeon be to be meet with in that neighbourhood
the drawer tell he there be one not far off but he have know he often refuse to be concern after another have be send before he but sir say he if you will take my advice there be not a man in the kingdom can do your business well than the barber who be with you last night
we look upon he to be one of the able man at a cut in all this neighbourhood
for though he hath not be she above three month he hath do several great cure the drawer be presently dispatch for little benjamin who be acquaint in what capacity he be want prepare himself accordingly and attend but with so different an air and aspect from that which he wear when his basin be under his arm that he could scarce be know to be the same person
so tonsor say jones i find you have more trade than one how come you not to inform i of this last night a surgeon answer benjamin with great gravity be a profession not a trade
the reason why i do not acquaint you last night that i profess this art be that i then conclude you be under the hand of another gentleman and i never love to interfere with my brother in their business
ars omnibus communis
but now sir if you please i will inspect your head and when i see into your skull i will give my opinion of your case jones have no great faith in this new professor however he suffer he to open the bandage and to look at his wound which as soon as he have do benjamin begin to groan and shake his head violently
upon which jones in a peevish manner bid he not play the fool but tell he in what condition he find he
shall i answer you as a surgeon or a friend say benjamin
as a friend and seriously say jones
why then upon my soul cry benjamin it would require a great deal of art to keep you from be well after a very few dressing and if you will suffer i to apply some salve of mine i will answer for the success jones give his consent and the plaister be apply accordingly
there sir cry benjamin now i will if you please resume my former self but a man be oblige to keep up some dignity in his countenance whilst he be perform these operation or the world will not submit to be handle by he
you cannot imagine sir of how much consequence a grave aspect be to a grave character
a barber may make you laugh but a surgeon ought rather to make you cry mr barber or mr surgeon or mr barber-surgeon say jones
o dear sir answer benjamin interrupt he infandum regina jubes renovare dolorem
you recall to my mind that cruel separation of the united fraternity so much to the prejudice of both body as all separation must be accord to the old adage vis unita fortior which to be sure there be not want some of one or of the other fraternity who be able to construe
what a blow be this to i who unite both in my own person well by whatever name you please to be call continue jones you certainly be one of the oddest most comical fellow i ever meet with and must have something very surprize in your story which you must confess i have a right to hear i do confess it answer benjamin and will very readily acquaint you with it when you have sufficient leisure for i promise you it will require a good deal of time jones tell he he could never be more at leisure than at present
well then say benjamin i will obey you but first i will fasten the door that none may interrupt we he do so and then advance with a solemn air to jones say i must begin by tell you sir that you yourself have be the great enemy i ever have jones be a little startled at this sudden declaration
i your enemy sir say he with much amazement and some sternness in his look
nay be not angry say benjamin for i promise you i be not
you be perfectly innocent of have intend i any wrong for you be then an infant but i shall i believe unriddle all this the moment i mention my name
do you never hear sir of one partridge who have the honour of be repute your father and the misfortune of be ruin by that honour i have indeed hear of that partridge say jones and have always believe myself to be his son well sir answer benjamin i be that partridge but i here absolve you from all filial duty for i do assure you you be no son of mine how reply jones and be it possible that a false suspicion should have draw all the ill consequence upon you with which i be too well acquaint it be possible cry benjamin for it be so but though it be natural enough for man to hate even the innocent cause of their suffering yet i be of a different temper
i have love you ever since i hear of your behaviour to black george as i tell you and i be convince from this extraordinary meeting that you be bear to make i amend for all i have suffer on that account
besides i dream the night before i see you that i stumble over a stool without hurt myself which plainly show i something good be towards i and last night i dream again that i ride behind you on a milk-white mare which be a very excellent dream and betoken much good fortune which i be resolve to pursue unless you have the cruelty to deny i i should be very glad mr partridge answer jones to have it in my power to make you amend for your suffering on my account though at present i see no likelihood of it however i assure you i will deny you nothing which be in my power to grant it be in your power sure enough reply benjamin for i desire nothing more than leave to attend you in this expedition
nay i have so entirely set my heart upon it that if you should refuse i you will kill both a barber and a surgeon in one breath jones answer smile that he should be very sorry to be the occasion of so much mischief to the public
he then advance many prudential reason in order to dissuade benjamin whom we shall hereafter call partridge from his purpose but all be in vain
partridge rely strongly on his dream of the milk-white mare
besides sir say he i promise you i have as good an inclination to the cause as any man can possibly have and go i will whether you admit i to go in your company or not jones who be as much pleased with partridge as partridge could be with he and who have not consult his own inclination but the good of the other in desire he to stay behind when he find his friend so resolute at last give his consent but then recollect himself he say perhaps mr partridge you think i shall be able to support you but i really be not and then take out his purse he tell out nine guineas which he declare be his whole fortune
partridge answer that his dependence be only on his future favour for he be thoroughly convinced he would shortly have enough in his power
at present sir say he i believe i be rather the rich man of the two but all i have be at your service and at your disposal
i insist upon your take the whole and i beg only to attend you in the quality of your servant nil desperandum est teucro duce et auspice teucro but to this generous proposal concern the money jones would by no mean submit
it be resolve to set out the next morning when a difficulty arise concern the baggage for the portmanteau of mr jones be too large to be carry without a horse
if i may presume to give my advice say partridge this portmanteau with everything in it except a few shirt should be leave behind
those i shall be easily able to carry for you and the rest of your cloath will remain very safe lock up in my house this method be no soon propose than agree to and then the barber depart in order to prepare everything for his intended expedition
chapter vii
contain well reason than any which have yet appear for the conduct of partridge an apology for the weakness of jones and some further anecdote concern my landlady
though partridge be one of the most superstitious of man he would hardly perhaps have desire to accompany jones on his expedition merely from the omen of the joint-stool and white mare if his prospect have be no well than to have share the plunder gain in the field of battle
in fact when partridge come to ruminate on the relation he have hear from jones he could not reconcile to himself that mr allworthy should turn his son for so he most firmly believe he to be out of door for any reason which he have hear assign
he conclude therefore that the whole be a fiction and that jones of whom he have often from his correspondent hear the wild character have in reality run away from his father
it come into his head therefore that if he could prevail with the young gentleman to return back to his father he should by that mean render a service to allworthy which would obliterate all his former anger nay indeed he conceive that very anger be counterfeit and that allworthy have sacrifice he to his own reputation
and this suspicion indeed he well account for from the tender behaviour of that excellent man to the foundling child from his great severity to partridge who know himself to be innocent could not conceive that any other should think he guilty lastly from the allowance which he have privately receive long after the annuity have be publickly take from he and which he look upon as a kind of smart-money or rather by way of atonement for injustice for it be very uncommon i believe for man to ascribe the benefaction they receive to pure charity when they can possibly impute they to any other motive
if he could by any mean therefore persuade the young gentleman to return home he doubt not but that he should again be receive into the favour of allworthy and well reward for his pain nay and should be again restore to his native country a restoration which ulysses himself never wish more heartily than poor partridge
as for jones he be well satisfied with the truth of what the other have assert and believe that partridge have no other inducement but love to he and zeal for the cause a blameable want of caution and diffidence in the veracity of other in which he be highly worthy of censure
to say the truth there be but two way by which man become possess of this excellent quality
the one be from long experience and the other be from nature which last i presume be often mean by genius or great natural part and it be infinitely the well of the two not only as we be master of it much early in life but as it be much more infallible and conclusive for a man who hath be impose on by ever so many may still hope to find other more honest whereas he who receive certain necessary admonition from within that this be impossible must have very little understanding indeed if he ever render himself liable to be once deceive
as jones have not this gift from nature he be too young to have gain it by experience for at the diffident wisdom which be to be acquire this way we seldom arrive till very late in life which be perhaps the reason why some old man be apt to despise the understanding of all those who be a little young than themselves
jones spend most part of the day in the company of a new acquaintance
this be no other than the landlord of the house or rather the husband of the landlady
he have but lately make his descent downstair after a long fit of the gout in which distemper he be generally confine to his room during one half of the year and during the rest he walk about the house smoake his pipe and drink his bottle with his friend without concern himself in the least with any kind of business
he have be breed as they call it a gentleman that be breed up to do nothing and have spend a very small fortune which he inherit from an industrious farmer his uncle in hunting horse-racing and cock-fighting and have be marry by my landlady for certain purpose which he have long since desist from answer for which she hat he heartily
but as he be a surly kind of fellow so she content herself with frequently upbraid he by disadvantageous comparison with her first husband whose praise she have eternally in her mouth and as she be for the most part mistress of the profit so she be satisfied to take upon herself the care and government of the family and after a long successless struggle to suffer her husband to be master of himself
in the evening when jones retire to his room a small dispute arise between this fond couple concern him what say the wife you have be tipple with the gentleman i see yes answer the husband we have crack a bottle together and a very gentlemanlike man he be and hath a very pretty notion of horse-flesh
indeed he be young and hath not see much of the world for i believe he hath be at very few horse-race oho
he be one of your order be he reply the landlady he must be a gentleman to be sure if he be a horse-racer
the devil fetch such gentry
i be sure i wish i have never see any of they
i have reason to love horse-racer truly that you have say the husband for i be one you know yes answer she you be a pure one indeed
as my first husband use to say i may put all the good i have ever get by you in my eye and see never the bad d--n your first husband cry he
donot d--n a well man than yourself answer the wife if he have be alive you durst not have do it then you think say he i have not so much courage as yourself for you have d--n d he often in my hearing if i do say she i have repent of it manybe the good time and oft
and if he be so good to forgive i a word speak in haste or so it doth not become such a one as you to twitter i
he be a husband to i he be and if ever i do make use of an ill word or so in a passion i never call he rascal i should have tell a lie if i have call he rascal much more she say but not in his hearing for have light his pipe he stagger off as fast as he could
we shall therefore transcribe no more of her speech as it approach still near and nearer to a subject too indelicate to find any place in this history
early in the morning partridge appear at the bedside of jones ready equipped for the journey with his knapsack at his back
this be his own workmanship for besides his other trade he be no indifferent taylor
he have already put up his whole stock of linen in it consist of four shirt to which he now add eight for mr jones and then pack up the portmanteau he be depart with it towards his own house but be stopt in his way by the landlady who refuse to suffer any removal till after the payment of the reckoning
the landlady be as we have say absolute governess in these region it be therefore necessary to comply with her rule so the bill be presently writ out which amount to a much large sum than might have be expect from the entertainment which jones have meet with
but here we be oblige to disclose some maxim which publican hold to be the grand mystery of their trade
the first be if they have anything good in their house which indeed very seldom happen to produce it only to person who travel with great equipage
2 dly to charge the same for the very bad provision as if they be the good
and lastly if any of their guest call but for little to make they pay a double price for everything they have so that the amount by the head may be much the same
the bill be make and discharge jones set forward with partridge carry his knapsack nor do the landlady condescend to wish he a good journey for this be it seem an inn frequent by people of fashion and i know not whence it be but all those who get their livelihood by people of fashion contract as much insolence to the rest of mankind as if they really belong to that rank themselves
chapter viii
jones arrive at gloucester and go to the bell the character of that house and of a petty-fogger which he there meet with
mr jones and partridge or little benjamin which epithet of little be perhaps give he ironically he be in reality near six foot high have leave their last quarter in the manner before describe travel on to gloucester without meet any adventure worth relate
be arrive here they choose for their house of entertainment the sign of the bell an excellent house indeed and which i do most seriously recommend to every reader who shall visit this antient city
the master of it be brother to the great preacher whitefield but be absolutely untainted with the pernicious principle of methodism or of any other heretical sect
he be indeed a very honest plain man and in my opinion not likely to create any disturbance either in church or state
his wife hath i believe have much pretension to beauty and be still a very fine woman
her person and deportment might have make a shin figure in the polite assembly but though she must be conscious of this and many other perfection she seem perfectly content with and resign to that state of life to which she be call and this resignation be entirely owe to the prudence and wisdom of her temper for she be at present as free from any methodistical notion as her husband i say at present for she freely confess that her brother s document make at first some impression upon she and that she have put herself to the expense of a long hood in order to attend the extraordinary emotion of the spirit but have find during an experiment of three week no emotion she say worth a farthing she very wisely lay by her hood and abandon the sect
to be concise she be a very friendly good-natured woman and so industrious to oblige that the guest must be of a very morose disposition who be not extremely well satisfied in her house
mrs whitefield happen to be in the yard when jones and his attendant march in
her sagacity soon discover in the air of our heroe something which distinguish he from the vulgar
she order she servant therefore immediately to show he into a room and presently afterwards invite he to dinner with herself which invitation he very thankfully accept for indeed much less agreeable company than that of mrs whitefield and a much bad entertainment than she have provide would have be welcome after so long fasting and so long a walk
besides mr jones and the good governess of the mansion there sit down at table an attorney of salisbury indeed the very same who have bring the news of mrs blifil s death to mr allworthy and whose name which i think we do not before mention be dowle there be likewise present another person who stile himself a lawyer and who live somewhere near linlinch in somersetshire
this fellow i say stile himself a lawyer but be indeed a most vile petty-fogger without sense or knowledge of any kind one of those who may be term train-bearer to the law a sort of supernumerary in the profession who be the hackney of attorney and will ride more mile for half-a-crown than a postboy
during the time of dinner the somersetshire lawyer recollect the face of jones which he have see at mr allworthy s for he have often visit in that gentleman s kitchen
he therefore take occasion to enquire after the good family there with that familiarity which would have become an intimate friend or acquaintance of mr allworthy and indeed he do all in his power to insinuate himself to be such though he have never have the honour of speak to any person in that family high than the butler
jones answer all his question with much civility though he never remember to have see the petty-fogger before and though he conclude from the outward appearance and behaviour of the man that he usurp a freedom with his better to which he be by no mean intitle
as the conversation of fellow of this kind be of all other the most detestable to man of any sense the cloth be no soon removed than mr jones withdraw and a little barbarously leave poor mrs whitefield to do a penance which i have often hear mr timothy harris and other publican of good taste lament as the severe lot annex to their calling namely that of be oblige to keep company with their guest
jones have no soon quit the room than the petty-fogger in a whispering tone ask mrs whitefield if she know who that fine spark be she answer she have never see the gentleman before the gentleman indeed reply the petty-fogger a pretty gentleman truly
why hebe the bastard of a fellow who be hang for horse-stealing
he be dropt at squire allworthy s door where one of the servant find he in a box so full of rain-water that he would certainly have be drown have he not be reserve for another fate ay ay you need not mention it i protest we understand what that fate be very well cry dowle with a most facetious grin well continue the other the squire order he to be take in for he be a timbersome man everybody know and be afraid of draw himself into a scrape and there the bastard be breed up and fed and cloathifie all to the world like any gentleman and there he get one of the servant-maid with child and persuade she to swear it to the squire himself and afterwards he break the arm of one mr thwackum a clergyman only because he reprimand he for follow whore and afterwards he snapt a pistol at mr blifil behind his back and once when squire allworthy be sick he get a drum and beat it all over the house to prevent he from sleep and twenty other prank he hath play for all which about four or five day ago just before i leave the country the squire strip he stark naked and turn he out of door and very justly too i protest cry dowle i would turn my own son out of door if he be guilty of half as much
and pray what be the name of this pretty gentleman the name o un answer petty-fogger why he be call thomas jones jones answer dowle a little eagerly what mr jones that live at mr allworthy s
be that the gentleman that din with us the very same say the other
