i have hear of the gentleman cry dowling often but i never hear any ill character of he and i be sure say mrs whitefield if half what this gentleman hath say be true mr jones hath the most deceitful countenance i ever see for sure his look promise something very different and i must say for the little i have see of he he be as civil a well-breed man as you would wish to converse with petty-fogger calling to mind that he have not be swear as he usually be before he give his evidence now bind what he have declare with so many oath and imprecation that the landlady s ear be shocked and she put a stop to his swearing by assure he of her belief
upon which he say i hope madam you imagine i would scorn to tell such thing of any man unless i know they to be true
what interest have i in take away the reputation of a man who never injure i
i promise you every syllable of what i have say be fact and the whole country know it as mrs whitefield have no reason to suspect that the petty-fogger have any motive or temptation to abuse jones the reader cannot blame she for believe what he so confidently affirm with many oath
she accordingly give up her skill in physiognomy and hence-forwards conceive so ill an opinion of her guest that she heartily wish he out of her house
this dislike be now far increase by a report which mr whitefield make from the kitchen where partridge have inform the company that though he carry the knapsack and content himself with stay among servant while tom jones as he call he be regale in the parlour he be not his servant but only a friend and companion and as good a gentleman as mr jones himself dowling sit all this while silent bite his finger make face grinning and look wonderfully arch at last he open his lip and protest that the gentleman look like another sort of man
he then call for his bill with the utmost haste declare he must be at hereford that evening lament his great hurry of business and wish he could divide himself into twenty piece in order to be at once in twenty place
the petty-fogger now likewise depart and then jones desire the favour of mrs whitefield s company to drink tea with he but she refuse and with a manner so different from that with which she have receive he at dinner that it a little surprized he
and now he soon perceive her behaviour totally change for instead of that natural affability which we have before celebrate she wear a constrain severity on her countenance which be so disagreeable to mr jones that he resolve however late to quit the house that evening
he do indeed account somewhat unfairly for this sudden change for besides some hard and unjust surmise concern female fickleness and mutability he begin to suspect that he owe this want of civility to his want of horse a sort of animal which as they dirty no sheet be think in inn to pay well for their bed than their rider and be therefore consider as the more desirable company but mrs whitefield to do her justice have a much more liberal way of think
she be perfectly well-breed and could be very civil to a gentleman though he walk on foot
in reality she look on our heroe as a sorry scoundrel and therefore treat he as such for which not even jones himself have he know as much as the reader could have blame she nay on the contrary he must have approve she conduct and have esteem she the more for the disrespect show towards himself
this be indeed a most aggravating circumstance which attend deprive man unjustly of their reputation for a man who be conscious of have an ill character cannot justly be angry with those who neglect and slight he but ought rather to despise such as affect his conversation unless where a perfect intimacy must have convince they that their friend s character hath be falsely and injuriously asperse
this be not however the case of jones for as he be a perfect stranger to the truth so he be with good reason offend at the treatment he receive
he therefore pay his reckoning and depart highly against the will of mr partridge who have remonstrate much against it to no purpose at last condescend to take up his knapsack and to attend his friend
chapter ix
contain several dialogue between jones and partridge concern love cold hunger and other matter with the lucky and narrow escape of partridge as he be on the very brink of make a fatal discovery to his friend
the shadow begin now to descend large from the high mountain the feather creation have betake themselves to their rest
now the high order of mortal be sit down to their dinner and the low order to their supper
in a word the clock strike five just as mr jones take his leave of gloucester an hour at which as it be now mid-winter the dirty finger of night would have draw her sable curtain over the universe have not the moon forbid she who now with a face as broad and as red as those of some jolly mortal who like she turn night into day begin to rise from her bed where she have slumber away the day in order to sit up all night
jones have not travel far before he pay his compliment to that beautiful planet and turn to his companion ask he if he have ever beheld so delicious an evening
partridge make no ready answer to his question he proceed to comment on the beauty of the moon and repeat some passage from milton who hath certainly excel all other poet in his description of the heavenly luminary
he then tell partridge the story from the spectator of two lover who have agree to entertain themselves when they be at a great distance from each other by repair at a certain fix hour to look at the moon thus please themselves with the thought that they be both employ in contemplate the same object at the same time
those lover add he must have have soul truly capable of feel all the tenderness of the sublimest of all human passion very probably cry partridge but i envy they more if they have body incapable of feel cold for i be almost frozen to death and be very much afraid i shall lose a piece of my nose before we get to another house of entertainment
nay truly we may well expect some judgment should happen to we for our folly in run away so by night from one of the most excellent inn i ever set my foot into
i be sure i never see more good thing in my life and the great lord in the land cannot live well in his own house than he may there
and to forsake such a house and go a rambling about the country the lord know whither per devia rura viarum i say nothing for my part but some people might not have charity enough to conclude we be in our sober sens fie upon it mr partridge say jones have a well heart consider you be go to face an enemy and be you afraid of face a little cold
i wish indeed we have a guide to advise which of these road we should take may i be so bold say partridge to offer my advice
interdum stultus opportuna loquitur why which of they cry jones would you recommend truly neither of they answer partridge
the only road we can be certain of find be the road we come
a good hearty pace will bring we back to gloucester in an hour but if we go forward the lord harry know when we shall arrive at any place for i see at least fifty mile before i and no house in all the way you see indeed a very fair prospect say jones which receive great additional beauty from the extreme lustre of the moon
however i will keep the left-hand track as that seem to lead directly to those hill which we be inform lie not far from worcester
and here if you be inclined to quit i you may and return back again but for my part i be resolve to go forward it be unkind in you sir say partridge to suspect i of any such intention
what i have advise hath be as much on your account as on my own but since you be determined to go on i be as much determine to follow
i prae sequar te they now travel some mile without speak to each other during which suspense of discourse jones often sigh and benjamin groan as bitterly though from a very different reason
at length jones make a full stop and turn about cry who know partridge but the lovely creature in the universe may have her eye now fix on that very moon which i behold at this instant very likely sir answer partridge and if my eye be fix on a good surloin of roast beef the devil might take the moon and her horn into the bargain do ever tramontane make such an answer cry jones
prithee partridge wast thou ever susceptible of love in thy life or hath time wear away all the trace of it from thy memory alack-a-day cry partridge well would it have be for i if i have never know what love be
infandum regina jubes renovare dolorem
i be sure i have taste all the tenderness and sublimity and bitterness of the passion be your mistress unkind then say jones
very unkind indeed sir answer partridge for she marry i and make one of the most confound wife in the world
however heaven be praise shebe go and if i believe she be in the moon accord to a book i once read which teach that to be the receptacle of depart spirit i would never look at it for fear of see she but i wish sir that the moon be a look-glass for your sake and that miss sophia western be now place before it my dear partridge cry jones what a thought be there
a thought which i be certain could never have enter into any mind but that of a lover
o partridge
could i hope once again to see that face but alas
all those golden dream be vanish for ever and my only refuge from future misery be to forget the object of all my former happiness and do you really despair of ever see miss western again answer partridge if you will follow my advice i will engage you shall not only see she but have she in your arm ha
do not awaken a thought of that nature cry jones i have struggle sufficiently to conquer all such wish already nay answer partridge if you do not wish to have your mistress in your arm you be a most extraordinary lover indeed well well say jones let we avoid this subject but pray what be your advice to give it you in the military phrase then say partridge as we be soldier to the right about let we return the way we come we may yet reach gloucester to-night though late whereas if we proceed we be likely for aught i see to ramble about for ever without come either to house or home i have already tell you my resolution be to go on answer jones but i would have you go back
i be oblige to you for your company hither and i beg you to accept a guinea as a small instance of my gratitude
nay it would be cruel in i to suffer you to go any far for to deal plainly with you my chief end and desire be a glorious death in the service of my king and country as for your money reply partridge i beg sir you will put it up i will receive none of you at this time for at present i be i believe the rich man of the two
and as your resolution be to go on so mine be to follow you if you do
nay now my presence appear absolutely necessary to take care of you since your intention be so desperate for i promise you my view be much more prudent as you be resolve to fall in battle if you can so i be resolve as firmly to come to no hurt if i can help it
and indeed i have the comfort to think there will be but little danger for a popish priest tell i the other day the business would soon be over and he believe without a battle a popish priest cry jones i have hear be not always to be believe when he speak in behalf of his religion yes but so far answer the other from speak in behalf of his religion he assure i the catholicks do not expect to be any gainer by the change for that prince charles be as good a protestant as any in england and that nothing but regard to right make he and the rest of the popish party to be jacobites i believe he to be as much a protestant as i believe he hath any right say jones and i make no doubt of our success but not without a battle
so that i be not so sanguine as your friend the popish priest nay to be sure sir answer partridge all the prophecy i have ever read speak of a great deal of blood to be spill in the quarrel and the miller with three thumb who be now alive be to hold the horse of three king up to his knee in blood
lord have mercy upon we all and send well time with what stuff and nonsense hast thou fill thy head answer jones this too i suppose come from the popish priest
monster and prodigy be the proper argument to support monstrous and absurd doctrine
the cause of king george be the cause of liberty and true religion
in other word it be the cause of common sense my boy and i warrant you will succeed though briarius himself be to rise again with his hundred thumb and to turn miller partridge make no reply to this
he be indeed cast into the utmost confusion by this declaration of jones
for to inform the reader of a secret which he have no proper opportunity of reveal before partridge be in truth a jacobite and have conclude that jones be of the same party and be now proceed to join the rebel
an opinion which be not without foundation
for the tall long-sided dame mention by hudibra--that many-eye many-tongue many-mouthed many-eared monster of virgil have relate the story of the quarrel between jones and the officer with the usual regard to truth
she have indeed change the name of sophia into that of the pretender and have report that drink his health be the cause for which jones be knock down
this partridge have hear and most firmly believe
tis no wonder therefore that he have thence entertain the above-mention opinion of jones and which he have almost discover to he before he find out his own mistake
and at this the reader will be the less inclined to wonder if he please to recollect the doubtful phrase in which jones first communicate his resolution to mr partridge and indeed have the word be less ambiguous partridge might very well have construe they as he do be persuade as he be that the whole nation be of the same inclination in their heart nor do it stagger he that jones have travel in the company of soldier for he have the same opinion of the army which he have of the rest of the people
but however well affect he might be to james or charles he be still much more attach to little benjamin than to either for which reason he no soon discover the principle of his fellow-traveller than he think proper to conceal and outwardly give up his own to the man on whom he depend for the make his fortune since he by no mean believe the affair of jones to be so desperate as they really be with mr allworthy for as he have keep a constant correspondence with some of his neighbour since he leave that country he have hear much indeed more than be true of the great affection mr allworthy bear this young man who as partridge have be instruct be to be that gentleman s heir and whom as we have say he do not in the least doubt to be his son
he imagine therefore that whatever quarrel be between they it would be certainly make up at the return of mr jones an event from which he promise great advantage if he could take this opportunity of ingratiate himself with that young gentleman and if he could by any mean be instrumental in procure his return he doubt not as we have before say but it would as highly advance he in the favour of mr allworthy
we have already observe that he be a very good-natured fellow and he hath himself declare the violent attachment he have to the person and character of jones but possibly the view which i have just before mention might likewise have some little share in prompt he to undertake this expedition at least in urge he to continue it after he have discover that his master and himself like some prudent father and son though they travel together in great friendship have embrace opposite party
i be lead into this conjecture by have remark that though love friendship esteem and such like have very powerful operation in the human mind interest however be an ingredient seldom omit by wise man when they would work other to their own purpose
this be indeed a most excellent medicine and like ward s pill fly at once to the particular part of the body on which you desire to operate whether it be the tongue the hand or any other member where it scarce ever fail of immediately produce the desire effect
chapter x
in which our traveller meet with a very extraordinary adventure
just as jones and his friend come to the end of their dialogue in the precede chapter they arrive at the bottom of a very steep hill
here jones stopt short and direct his eye upwards stand for a while silent
at length he call to his companion and say partridge i wish i be at the top of this hill it must certainly afford a most charming prospect especially by this light for the solemn gloom which the moon cast on all object be beyond expression beautiful especially to an imagination which be desirous of cultivate melancholy idea very probably answer partridge but if the top of the hill be proper to produce melancholy thought i suppose the bottom be the likeli to produce merry one and these i take to be much the well of the two
i protest you have make my blood run cold with the very mentioning the top of that mountain which seem to i to be one of the high in the world
no no if we look for anything let it be for a place under ground to screen ourselves from the frost do so say jones let it be but within hearing of this place and i will hallow to you at my return back surely sir you be not mad say partridge indeed i be answer jones if ascend this hill be madness but as you complain so much of the cold already i would have you stay below
i will certainly return to you within an hour pardon me sir cry partridge i have determine to follow you wherever you go indeed he be now afraid to stay behind for though he be coward enough in all respect yet his chief fear be that of ghost with which the present time of night and the wildness of the place extremely well suit
at this instant partridge espy a glimmering light through some tree which seem very near to they
he immediately cry out in a rapture oh sir
heaven hath at last hear my prayer and hath bring we to a house perhaps it may be an inn
let i beseech you sir if you have any compassion either for i or yourself do not despise the goodness of providence but let we go directly to yon light
whether it be a public-house or no i be sure if they be christians that dwell there they will not refuse a little house-room to person in our miserable condition jones at length yield to the earnest supplication of partridge and both together make directly towards the place whence the light issue
they soon arrive at the door of this house or cottage for it might be call either without much impropriety
here jones knock several time without receive any answer from within at which partridge whose head be full of nothing but of ghost devil witch and such like begin to tremble cry lord have mercy upon we
surely the people must be all dead
i can see no light neither now and yet i be certain i see a candle burning but a moment before well
i have hear of such thing what hast thou hear of say jones
the people be either fast asleep or probably as this be a lonely place be afraid to open their door he then begin to vociferate pretty loudly and at last an old woman open an upper casement ask who they be and what they want
jones answer they be traveller who have lose their way and have see a light in the window have be lead thither in hope of find some fire to warm themselves
whoever you be cry the woman you have no business here nor shall i open the door to any one at this time of night partridge whom the sound of a human voice have recover from his fright fall to the most earnest supplication to be admit for a few minute to the fire say he be almost dead with the cold to which fear have indeed contribute equally with the frost
he assure she that the gentleman who speak to she be one of the great squire in the country and make use of every argument save one which jones afterwards effectually add and this be the promise of half-a-crown a bribe too great to be resist by such a person especially as the genteel appearance of jones which the light of the moon plainly discover to she together with his affable behaviour have entirely subdue those apprehension of thief which she have at first conceive
she agree therefore at last to let they in where partridge to his infinite joy find a good fire ready for his reception
the poor fellow however have no soon warm himself than those thought which be always uppermost in his mind begin a little to disturb his brain
there be no article of his creed in which he have a strong faith than he have in witchcraft nor can the reader conceive a figure more adapt to inspire this idea than the old woman who now stand before he
she answer exactly to that picture draw by otway in his orphan
indeed if this woman have live in the reign of james the first her appearance alone would have hang she almost without any evidence
many circumstance likewise conspire to confirm partridge in his opinion
her living as he then imagine by herself in so lonely a place and in a house the outside of which seem much too good for she but its inside be furnish in the most neat and elegant manner
to say the truth jones himself be not a little surprized at what he see for besides the extraordinary neatness of the room it be adorn with a great number of nicknack and curiosity which might have engage the attention of a virtuoso
while jones be admire these thing and partridge sit tremble with the firm belief that he be in the house of a witch the old woman say i hope gentleman you will make what haste you can for i expect my master presently and i would not for double the money he should find you here then you have a master cry jones
indeed you will excuse i good woman but i be surprize to see all those fine thing in your house ah sir say she if the twentieth part of these thing be mine i should think myself a rich woman
but pray sir do not stay much long for i look for he in every minute why sure he would not be angry with you say jones for do a common act of charity alack-a-day sir say she he be a strange man not at all like other people
he keep no company with anybody and seldom walk out but by night for he doth not care to be see and all the country people be as much afraid of meet he for his dress be enough to frighten those who be not use to it
they call he the man of the hill for there he walk by night and the country people be not i believe more afraid of the devil himself
he would be terribly angry if he find you here pray sir say partridge donot let we offend the gentleman i be ready to walk and be never warm in my life
do pray sir let we go
here be pistol over the chimney who know whether they be charge or no or what he may do with them fear nothing partridge cry jones i will secure thee from danger nay for matter o that he never doth any mischief say the woman but to be sure it be necessary he should keep some arm for his own safety for his house hath be beset more than once and it be not many night ago that we think we hear thief about it for my own part i have often wonder that he be not murder by some villain or other as he walk out by himself at such hour but then as i say the people be afraid of he and besides they think i suppose he hath nothing about he worth take i should imagine by this collection of rarity cry jones that your master have be a traveller yes sir answer she he hath be a very great one there be few gentleman that know more of all matter than he
i fancy he hath be crost in love or whatever it be i know not but i have live with he above these thirty year and in all that time he hath hardly speak to six live people she then again solicit their departure in which she be back by partridge but jones purposely protract the time for his curiosity be greatly raise to see this extraordinary person
though the old woman therefore conclude every one of her answer with desire he to be go and partridge proceed so far as to pull he by the sleeve he still continue to invent new question till the old woman with an affright countenance declare she hear her master s signal and at the same instant more than one voice be hear without the door cry d--n your blood show we your money this instant
your money you villain or we will blow your brain about your ear o good heaven cry the old woman some villain to be sure have attack my master
o la
what shall i do
what shall i do how cry jones how are these pistol loaded o good sir there be nothing in they indeed
o pray donot murder we gentleman for in reality she now have the same opinion of those within as she have of those without
jones make she no answer but snatch an old broad sword which hang in the room he instantly sally out where he find the old gentleman struggle with two ruffian and beg for mercy
jones ask no question but fall so briskly to work with his broad sword that the fellow immediately quit their hold and without offer to attack our heroe betook themselves to their heel and make their escape for he do not attempt to pursue they be content with have deliver the old gentleman and indeed he conclude he have pretty well do their business for both of they as they run off cry out with bitter oath that they be dead man
jones presently run to lift up the old gentleman who have be throw down in the scuffle express at the same time great concern lest he should have receive any harm from the villain
the old man star a moment at jones and then cry no sir no i have very little harm i thank you
lord have mercy upon me i see sir say jones you be not free from apprehension even of those who have have the happiness to be your deliverer nor can i blame any suspicion which you may have but indeed you have no real occasion for any here be none but your friend present
have mist our way this cold night we take the liberty of warm ourselves at your fire whence we be just depart when we hear you call for assistance which i must say providence alone seem to have send you providence indeed cry the old gentleman if it be so so it be i assure you cry jones
here be your own sword sir i have use it in your defence and i now return it into your hand the old man have receive the sword which be stain with the blood of his enemy look stedfastly at jones during some moment and then with a sigh cry out you will pardon i young gentleman i be not always of a suspicious temper nor be i a friend to ingratitude be thankful then cry jones to that providence to which you owe your deliverance as to my part i have only discharge the common duty of humanity and what i would have do for any fellow-creature in your situation let i look at you a little long cry the old gentleman
you be a human creature then
well perhaps you be
come pray walk into my little hutt
you have be my deliverer indeed the old woman be distract between the fear which she have of her master and for he and partridge be if possible in a great fright
the former of these however when she hear her master speak kindly to jones and perceive what have happen come again to herself but partridge no soon see the gentleman than the strangeness of his dress infuse great terror into that poor fellow than he have before feel either from the strange description which he have hear or from the uproar which have happen at the door
to say the truth it be an appearance which might have affect a more constant mind than that of mr partridge
this person be of the tall size with a long beard as white as snow
his body be cloathe with the skin of an ass make something into the form of a coat
he wear likewise boot on his leg and a cap on his head both compose of the skin of some other animal
as soon as the old gentleman come into his house the old woman begin her congratulation on his happy escape from the ruffian
yes cry he i have escape indeed thank to my preserver o the blessing on he answer she he be a good gentleman i warrant he
i be afraid your worship would have be angry with i for let he in and to be certain i should not have do it have not i see by the moon-light that he be a gentleman and almost frozen to death
and to be certain it must have be some good angel that send he hither and tempt i to do it i be afraid sir say the old gentleman to jones that i have nothing in this house which you can either eat or drink unless you will accept a dram of brandy of which i can give you some most excellent and which i have have by i these thirty year jones decline this offer in a very civil and proper speech and then the other ask he whither he be travel when he mist his way say i must own myself surprize to see such a person as you appear to be journey on foot at this time of night
i suppose sir you be a gentleman of these part for you do not look like one who be use to travel far without horse appearance cry jones be often deceitful man sometimes look what they be not
i assure you i be not of this country and whither i be travel in reality i scarce know myself whoever you be or whithersoever you be go answer the old man i have obligation to you which i can never return i once more reply jones affirm that you have none for there can be no merit in have hazard that in your service on which i set no value and nothing be so contemptible in my eye as life i be sorry young gentleman answer the stranger that you have any reason to be so unhappy at your year indeed i be sir answer jones the most unhappy of mankind perhap you have have a friend or a mistress reply the other
how could you cry jones mention two word sufficient to drive i to distraction either of they be enough to drive any man to distraction answer the old man
i enquire no far sir perhaps my curiosity hath lead i too far already indeed sir cry jones i cannot censure a passion which i feel at this instant in the high degree
you will pardon i when i assure you that everything which i have see or hear since i first enter this house hath conspire to raise the great curiosity in i
something very extraordinary must have determine you to this course of life and i have reason to fear your own history be not without misfortune here the old gentleman again sigh and remain silent for some minute at last look earnestly on jones he say i have read that a good countenance be a letter of recommendation if so none ever can be more strongly recommend than yourself
if i do not feel some yearning towards you from another consideration i must be the most ungrateful monster upon earth and i be really concerned it be no otherwise in my power than by word to convince you of my gratitude jones after a moment s hesitation answer that it be in his power by word to gratify he extremely
i have conf a curiosity say he sir need i say how much oblige i should be to you if you would condescend to gratify it
will you suffer i therefore to beg unless any consideration restrain you that you would be pleased to acquaint i what motif have induce you thus to withdraw from the society of mankind and to betake yourself to a course of life to which it sufficiently appear you be not bear i scarce think myself at liberty to refuse you anything after what hath happen reply the old man
if you desire therefore to hear the story of an unhappy man i will relate it to you
indeed you judge rightly in think there be commonly something extraordinary in the fortune of those who fly from society for however it may seem a paradox or even a contradiction certain it be that great philanthropy chiefly incline we to avoid and det mankind not on account so much of their private and selfish vice but for those of a relative kind such as envy malice treachery cruelty with every other specie of malevolence
these be the vice which true philanthropy abhor and which rather than see and converse with she avoid society itself
however without a compliment to you you do not appear to i one of those whom i should shun or det nay i must say in what little hath dropt from you there appear some parity in our fortune i hope however yours will conclude more successfully here some compliment pass between our heroe and his host and then the latter be go to begin his history when partridge interrupt he
his apprehension have now pretty well leave he but some effect of his terror remain he therefore remind the gentleman of that excellent brandy which he have mention
this be presently bring and partridge swallow a large bumper
the gentleman then without any farth preface begin as you may read in the next chapter
chapter xi
in which the man of the hill begin to relate his history
i be bear in a village of somersetshire call mark in the year 1657
my father be one of those whom they call gentleman farmer
he have a little estate of about 300 a year of his own and rent another estate of near the same value
he be prudent and industrious and so good a husbandman that he might have lead a very easy and comfortable life have not an arrant vixen of a wife sour his domestic quiet
but though this circumstance perhaps make he miserable it do not make he poor for he confine she almost entirely at home and rather choose to bear eternal upbraiding in his own house than to injure his fortune by indulge she in the extravagancy she desire abroad
by this xanthippe so be the wife of socrates call say partridge by this xanthippe he have two son of which i be the young
he design to give we both good education but my elder brother who unhappily for he be the favourite of my mother utterly neglect his learning insomuch that after have be five or six year at school with little or no improvement my father be tell by his master that it would be to no purpose to keep he long there at last comply with my mother in take he home from the hand of that tyrant as she call his master though indeed he give the lad much less correction than his idleness deserve but much more it seem than the young gentleman like who constantly complain to his mother of his severe treatment and she as constantly give he a hearing yes yes cry partridge i have see such mother i have be abuse myself by they and very unjustly such parent deserve correction as much as their child jones chid the pedagogue for his interruption and then the stranger proceed
my brother now at the age of fifteen bade adieu to all learning and to everything else but to his dog and gun with which latter he become so expert that though perhaps you may think it incredible he could not only hit a standing mark with great certainty but hath actually shoot a crow as it be fly in the air
he be likewise excellent at find a hare sitting and be soon repute one of the good sportsman in the country a reputation which both he and his mother enjoy as much as if he have be think the fine scholar
the situation of my brother make i at first think my lot the hard in be continue at school but i soon change my opinion for as i advance pretty fast in learn my labour become easy and my exercise so delightful that holiday be my most unpleasant time for my mother who never love i now apprehend that i have the great share of my father s affection and finding or at least thinking that i be more take notice of by some gentleman of learning and particularly by the parson of the parish than my brother she now hat my sight and make home so disagreeable to i that what be call by school-boy black monday be to i the whitest in the whole year
have at length go through the school at taunton i be thence remove to exeter college in oxford where i remain four year at the end of which an accident take i off entirely from my study and hence i may truly date the rise of all which happen to i afterwards in life
there be at the same college with myself one sir george gresham a young fellow who be intitle to a very considerable fortune which he be not by the will of his father to come into full possession of till he arrive at the age of twenty-five
however the liberality of his guardian give he little cause to regret the abundant caution of his father for they allow he five hundred pound a year while he remain at the university where he keep his horse and his whore and live as wicked and as profligate a life as he could have do have he be never so entirely master of his fortune for besides the five hundred a year which he receive from his guardian he find mean to spend a thousand more
he be above the age of twenty-one and have no difficulty in gain what credit he please
this young fellow among many other tolerable bad quality have one very diabolical
he have a great delight in destroy and ruin the youth of inferior fortune by draw they into expense which they could not afford so well as himself and the well and worthy and soberer any young man be the great pleasure and triumph have he in his destruction
thus act the character which be record of the devil and go about seek whom he might devour
it be my misfortune to fall into an acquaintance and intimacy with this gentleman
my reputation of diligence in my study make i a desirable object of his mischievous intention and my own inclination make it sufficiently easy for he to effect his purpose for though i have apply myself with much industry to book in which i take great delight there be other pleasure in which i be capable of take much great for i be high-mettle have a violent flow of animal spirit be a little ambitious and extremely amorous
i have not long contract an intimacy with sir george before i become a partaker of all his pleasure and when i be once enter on that scene neither my inclination nor my spirit would suffer i to play an under part
i be second to none of the company in any act of debauchery nay i soon distinguish myself so notably in all riot and disorder that my name generally stand first in the roll of delinquent and instead of be lament as the unfortunate pupil of sir george i be now accuse as the person who have mislead and debauch that hopeful young gentleman for though he be the ringleader and promoter of all the mischief he be never so consider
i fall at last under the censure of the vice-chancellor and very narrowly escape expulsion
you will easily believe sir that such a life as i be now describe must be incompatible with my further progress in learn and that in proportion as i addict myself more and more to loose pleasure i must grow more and more remiss in application to my study
this be truly the consequence but this be not all
my expense now greatly exceed not only my former income but those addition which i extort from my poor generous father on pretence of sum be necessary for prepare for my approach degree of batchelor of art
these demand however grow at last so frequent and exorbitant that my father by slow degree open his ear to the account which he receive from many quarter of my present behaviour and which my mother fail not to echo very faithfully and loudly add ay this be the fine gentleman the scholar who doth so much honour to his family and be to be the making of it
i think what all this learning would come to
he be to be the ruin of we all i find after his eld brother hath be deny necessary for his sake to perfect his education forsooth for which he be to pay we such interest i think what the interest would come to with much more of the same kind but i have i believe satisfy you with this taste
my father therefore begin now to return remonstrance instead of money to my demand which bring my affair perhaps a little soon to a crisis but have he remit i his whole income you will imagine it could have suffice a very short time to support one who keep pace with the expense of sir george gresham
it be more than possible that the distress i be now in for money and the impracticability of go on in this manner might have restore i at once to my sens and to my study have i open my eye before i become involved in debt from which i see no hope of ever extricate myself
this be indeed the great art of sir george and by which he accomplish the ruin of many whom he afterwards laugh at as fool and coxcomb for vie as he call it with a man of his fortune
to bring this about he would now and then advance a little money himself in order to support the credit of the unfortunate youth with other people till by mean of that very credit he be irretrievably undo
my mind be by these mean grow as desperate as my fortune there be scarce a wickedness which i do not meditate in order for my relief
self-murder itself become the subject of my serious deliberation and i have certainly resolve on it have not a more shameful though perhaps less sinful think expel it from my head here he hesitate a moment and then cry out i protest so many year have not wash away the shame of this act and i shall blush while i relate it jones desire he to pass over anything that might give he pain in the relation but partridge eagerly cry out oh pray sir let we hear this i have rather hear this than all the rest as i hope to be save i will never mention a word of it jones be go to rebuke he but the stranger prevent it by proceed thus i have a chum a very prudent frugal young lad who though he have no very large allowance have by his parsimony heap up upwards of forty guineas which i know he keep in his escritore
i take therefore an opportunity of purloin his key from his breech-pocket while he be asleep and thus make myself master of all his riches after which i again convey his key into his pocket and counterfeit sleep--though i never once close my eye lie in bed till after he arise and go to prayer--an exercise to which i have long be unaccustomed
timorous thief by extreme caution often subject themselves to discovery which those of a bold kind escape
thus it happen to i for have i boldly break open his escritore i have perhaps escape even his suspicion but as it be plain that the person who rob he have possess himself of his key he have no doubt when he first miss his money but that his chum be certainly the thief
now as he be of a fearful disposition and much my inferior in strength and i believe in courage he do not dare to confront i with my guilt for fear of bad bodily consequence which might happen to he
he repair therefore immediately to the vice-chancellor and upon swear to the robbery and to the circumstance of it very easily obtain a warrant against one who have now so bad a character through the whole university
luckily for i i lay out of the college the next evening for that day i attend a young lady in a chaise to witney where we staid all night and in our return the next morning to oxford i meet one of my crony who acquaint i with sufficient news concern myself to make i turn my horse another way pray sir do he mention anything of the warrant say partridge
but jones beg the gentleman to proceed without regard any impertinent question which he do as follows have now abandon all thought of return to oxford the next thing which offer itself be a journey to london
i impart this intention to my female companion who at first remonstrate against it but upon produce my wealth she immediately consent
we then strike across the country into the great cirencester road and make such haste that we spend the next evening save one in london
when you consider the place where i now be and the company with whom i be you will i fancy conceive that a very short time bring i to an end of that sum of which i have so iniquitously possess myself
i be now reduce to a much high degree of distress than before the necessary of life begin to be number among my want and what make my case still the more grievous be that my paramour of whom i be now grow immoderately fond share the same distress with myself
to see a woman you love in distress to be unable to relieve she and at the same time to reflect that you have bring she into this situation be perhaps a curse of which no imagination can represent the horror to those who have not feel it i believe it from my soul cry jones and i pity you from the bottom of my heart he then take two or three disorderly turn about the room and at last beg pardon and fling himself into his chair cry i thank heaven i have escape that this circumstance continue the gentleman so severely aggravate the horror of my present situation that they become absolutely intolerable
i could with less pain endure the rag in my own natural unsatisfie appetite even hunger or thirst than i could submit to leave ungratified the most whimsical desire of a woman on whom i so extravagantly doate that though i know she have be the mistress of half my acquaintance i firmly intend to marry she
but the good creature be unwilling to consent to an action which the world might think so much to my disadvantage
and as possibly she compassionate the daily anxiety which she must have perceive i suffer on her account she resolve to put an end to my distress
she soon indeed find mean to relieve i from my troublesome and perplexed situation for while i be distract with various invention to supply she with pleasure she very kindly--betray i to one of her former lover at oxford by whose care and diligence i be immediately apprehend and commit to gaol
here i first begin seriously to reflect on the miscarriage of my former life on the error i have be guilty of on the misfortune which i have bring on myself and on the grief which i must have occasion to one of the good of father
when i add to all these the perfidy of my mistress such be the horror of my mind that life instead of be long desirable grow the object of my abhorrence and i could have gladly embrace death as my dear friend if it have offer itself to my choice unattended by shame
the time of the assize soon come and i be remove by habeas corpus to oxford where i expect certain conviction and condemnation but to my great surprize none appear against i and i be at the end of the session discharge for want of prosecution
in short my chum have leave oxford and whether from indolence or from what other motive i be ignorant have decline concern himself any far in the affair perhaps cry partridge he do not care to have your blood upon his hand and he be in the right on t
if any person be to be hang upon my evidence i should never be able to lie alone afterwards for fear of see his ghost i shall shortly doubt partridge say jones whether thou art more brave or wise you may laugh at i sir if you please answer partridge but if you will hear a very short story which i can tell and which be most certainly true perhaps you may change your opinion
in the parish where i be born-- here jones would have silence he but the stranger intercede that he might be permit to tell his story and in the meantime promise to recollect the remainder of his own
partridge then proceed thus in the parish where i be bear there live a farmer whose name be bridle and he have a son name francis a good hopeful young fellow i be at the grammar-school with he where i remember he be get into ovid s epistles and he could construe you three line together sometimes without look into a dictionary
besides all this he be a very good lad never miss church o sundays and be reckon one of the good psalm-singer in the whole parish
he would indeed now and then take a cup too much and that be the only fault he have well but come to the ghost cry jones
never fear sir i shall come to he soon enough answer partridge
you must know then that farmer bridle lose a mare a sorrel one to the good of my remembrance and so it fall out that this young francis shortly afterward be at a fair at hindon and as i think it be on-- i cannot remember the day and be as he be what should he happen to meet but a man upon his father s mare
frank call out presently stop thief and it be in the middle of the fair it be impossible you know for the man to make his escape
so they apprehend he and carry he before the justice i remember it be justice willoughby of noyle a very worthy good gentleman and he commit he to prison and bind frank in a recognisance i think they call it--a hard word compound of re and cognosco but it differ in its meaning from the use of the simple as many other compound do
well at last down come my lord justice page to hold the assize and so the fellow be have up and frank be have up for a witness
to be sure i shall never forget the face of the judge when he begin to ask he what he have to say against the prisoner
he make poor frank tremble and shake in his shoe
well you fellow say my lord what have you to say
donot stand hum and hawing but speak out but however he soon turn altogether as civil to frank and begin to thunder at the fellow and when he ask he if he have anything to say for himself the fellow say he have find the horse
ay answer the judge thou art a lucky fellow i have travel the circuit these forty year and never find a horse in my life but iwill tell thee what friend thou wast more lucky than thou didst know of for thou didst not only find a horse but a halter too i promise thee to be sure i shall never forget the word
upon which everybody fall a laughing as how could they help it
nay and twenty other jest he make which i cannot remember now
there be something about his skill in horse-flesh which make all the folk laugh
to be certain the judge must have be a very brave man as well as a man of much learning
it be indeed charming sport to hear trial upon life and death
one thing i own i think a little hard that the prisoner s counsel be not suffer to speak for he though he desire only to be hear one very short word but my lord would not hearken to he though he suffer a counsellor to talk against he for above half-an-hour
i think it hard i own that there should be so many of they my lord and the court and the jury and the counsellor and the witness all upon one poor man and he too in chain
well the fellow be hang as to be sure it could be no otherwise and poor frank could never be easy about it
he never be in the dark alone but he fancy he see the fellow s spirit well and be this thy story cry jones
no no answer partridge
o lord have mercy upon i
i be just now come to the matter for one night come from the alehouse in a long narrow dark lane there he run directly up against he and the spirit be all in white and fall upon frank and frank who be a sturdy lad fall upon the spirit again and there they have a tussel together and poor frank be dreadfully beat indeed he make a shift at last to crawl home but what with the beating and what with the fright he lie ill above a fortnight and all this be most certainly true and the whole parish will bear witness to it the stranger smile at this story and jones burst into a loud fit of laughter upon which partridge cry ay you may laugh sir and so do some other particularly a squire who be think to be no well than an atheist who forsooth because there be a calf with a white face find dead in the same lane the next morning would fain have it that the battle be between frank and that as if a calf would set upon a man
besides frank tell i he know it to be a spirit and could swear to he in any court in christendom and he have not drank above a quart or two or such a matter of liquor at the time
lud have mercy upon we and keep we all from dip our hand in blood i say well sir say jones to the stranger mr partridge hath finish his story and i hope will give you no future interruption if you will be so kind to proceed he then resume his narration but as he hath take breath for a while we think proper to give it to our reader and shall therefore put an end to this chapter
chapter xii
in which the man of the hill continue his history
i have now regain my liberty say the stranger but i have lose my reputation for there be a wide difference between the case of a man who be barely acquit of a crime in a court of justice and of he who be acquit in his own heart and in the opinion of the people
i be conscious of my guilt and ashame to look any one in the face so resolve to leave oxford the next morning before the daylight discover i to the eye of any beholder
when i have get clear of the city it first enter into my head to return home to my father and endeavour to obtain his forgiveness but as i have no reason to doubt his knowledge of all which have past and as i be well assured of his great aversion to all act of dishonesty i could entertain no hope of be receive by he especially since i be too certain of all the good office in the power of my mother nay have my father s pardon be as sure as i conceive his resentment to be i yet question whether i could have have the assurance to behold he or whether i could upon any term have submit to live and converse with those who i be convince know i to have be guilty of so base an action
i hasten therefore back to london the good retirement of either grief or shame unless for person of a very public character for here you have the advantage of solitude without its disadvantage since you may be alone and in company at the same time and while you walk or sit unobserved noise hurry and a constant succession of object entertain the mind and prevent the spirit from prey on themselves or rather on grief or shame which be the most unwholesome diet in the world and on which though there be many who never taste either but in public there be some who can feed very plentifully and very fatally when alone
but as there be scarce any human good without its concomitant evil so there be people who find an inconvenience in this unobserving temper of mankind i mean person who have no money for as you be not put out of countenance so neither be you cloathe or feed by those who do not know you
and a man may be as easily starve in leadenhall-market as in the desert of arabia
it be at present my fortune to be destitute of that great evil as it be apprehend to be by several writer who i suppose be overburthene with it namely money with submission sir say partridge i do not remember any writer who have call it malorum but irritamenta malorum
effodiuntur ope irritamenta malorum well sir continue the stranger whether it be an evil or only the cause of evil i be entirely void of it and at the same time of friend and as i think of acquaintance when one evening as i be pass through the inner temple very hungry and very miserable i hear a voice on a sudden hail i with great familiarity by my christian name and upon turn about i presently recollect the person who so salute i to have be my fellow-collegiate one who have leave the university above a year and long before any of my misfortune have befall i
this gentleman whose name be watson shake i heartily by the hand and express great joy at meet i propose our immediately drink a bottle together
i first decline the proposal and pretend business but as he be very earnest and pressing hunger at last overcame my pride and i fairly confess to he i have no money in my pocket yet not without frame a lie for an excuse and impute it to my have change my breech that morning
mr watson answer i think jack you and i have be too old acquaintance for you to mention such a matter he then take i by the arm and be pull i along but i give he very little trouble for my own inclination pull i much strong than he could do
we then go into the friars which you know be the scene of all mirth and jollity
here when we arrive at the tavern mr watson apply himself to the drawer only without take the least notice of the cook for he have no suspicion but that i have din long since
however as the case be really otherwise i forge another falsehood and tell my companion i have be at the further end of the city on business of consequence and have snapt up a mutton-chop in haste so that i be again hungry and wish he would add a beef-steak to his bottle some people cry partridge ought to have good memory or do you find just money enough in your breech to pay for the mutton-chop your observation be right answer the stranger and i believe such blunder be inseparable from all deal in untruth but to proceed--i begin now to feel myself extremely happy
the meat and wine soon revive my spirit to a high pitch and i enjoy much pleasure in the conversation of my old acquaintance the rather as i think he entirely ignorant of what have happen at the university since his leave it
but he do not suffer i to remain long in this agreeable delusion for take a bumper in one hand and hold i by the other here my boy cry he herebe wish you joy of your be so honourably acquit of that affair lay to your charge i be thunderstruck with confusion at those word which watson observe proceed thus nay never be ashamed man thou hast be acquit and no one now dare call thee guilty but prithee do tell i who be thy friend--i hope thou didst really rob he
for rat i if it be not a meritorious action to strip such a sneaking pitiful rascal and instead of the two hundred guinea i wish you have take as many thousand
come come my boy donot be shy of confess to i you be not now bring before one of the pimp
d--n i if i donot honour you for it for as i hope for salvation i would have make no manner of scruple of do the same thing this declaration a little relieved my abashment and as wine have now somewhat open my heart i very freely acknowledge the robbery but acquaint he that he have be misinformed as to the sum take which be little more than a fifth part of what he have mention
i be sorry for it with all my heart quoth he and i wish thee well success another time
though if you will take my advice you shall have no occasion to run any such risque
here say he take some dice out of his pocket herebe the stuff
here be the implement here be the little doctor which cure the distemper of the purse
follow but my counsel and i will show you a way to empty the pocket of a queer cull without any danger of the nubbe cheat nubbe cheat cry partridge pray sir what be that why that sir say the stranger be a cannot phrase for the gallow for as gamester differ little from highwayman in their moral so do they very much resemble they in their language
we have now each drank our bottle when mr watson say the board be sit and that he must attend earnestly press i at the same time to go with he and try my fortune
i answer he know that be at present out of my power as i have inform he of the emptiness of my pocket
to say the truth i doubt not from his many strong expression of friendship but that he would offer to lend i a small sum for that purpose but he answer never mind that man e en boldly run a levant partridge be go to inquire the meaning of that word but jones stop his mouth but be circumspect as to the man
i will tip you the proper person which may be necessary as you do not know the town nor can distinguish a rum cull from a queer one the bill be now bring when watson pay his share and be depart
i remind he not without blushing of my have no money
he answer that signify nothing score it behind the door or make a bold brush and take no notice or--stay say he i will go down-stair first and then do you take up my money and score the whole reckoning at the bar and i will wait for you at the corner i express some dislike at this and hint my expectation that he would have deposit the whole but he swear he have not another sixpence in his pocket
he then go down and i be prevail on to take up the money and follow he which i do close enough to hear he tell the drawer the reckoning be upon the table
the drawer past by i up-stair but i make such haste into the street that i hear nothing of his disappointment nor do i mention a syllable at the bar accord to my instruction
we now go directly to the gaming-table where mr watson to my surprize pull out a large sum of money and place it before he as do many other all of they no doubt consider their own heap as so many decoy bird which be to intice and draw over the heap of their neighbour
here it would be tedious to relate all the freak which fortune or rather the dice play in this her temple
mountain of gold be in a few moment reduce to nothing at one part of the table and rise as suddenly in another
the rich grow in a moment poor and the poor as suddenly become rich so that it seem a philosopher could nowhere have so well instruct his pupil in the contempt of rich at least he could nowhere have well inculcate the incertainty of their duration
for my own part after have considerably improve my small estate i at last entirely demolish it
mr watson too after much variety of luck rise from the table in some heat and declare he have lose a cool hundred and would play no long
then come up to i he ask i to return with he to the tavern but i positively refuse say i would not bring myself a second time into such a dilemma and especially as he have lose all his money and be now in my own condition
pooh say he i have just borrow a couple of guineas of a friend and one of they be at your service he immediately put one of they into my hand and i no long resist his inclination
i be at first a little shocked at return to the same house whence we have depart in so unhandsome a manner but when the drawer with very civil address tell we he believe we have forget to pay our reckoning i become perfectly easy and very readily give he a guinea bid he pay himself and acquiesce in the unjust charge which have be lay on my memory
mr watson now bespoke the most extravagant supper he could well think of and though he have content himself with simple claret before nothing now but the most precious burgundy would serve his purpose
our company be soon encrease by the addition of several gentleman from the gaming-table most of whom as i afterwards find come not to the tavern to drink but in the way of business for the true gamester pretend to be ill and refuse their glass while they ply heartily two young fellow who be to be afterwards pillage as indeed they be without mercy
of this plunder i have the good fortune to be a sharer though i be not yet let into the secret
there be one remarkable accident attend this tavern play for the money by degree totally disappear so that though at the beginning the table be half cover with gold yet before the play end which it do not till the next day be sunday at noon there be scarce a single guinea to be see on the table and this be the stranger as every person present except myself declare he have lose and what be become of the money unless the devil himself carry it away be difficult to determine most certainly he do say partridge for evil spirit can carry away anything without be see though there be never so many folk in the room and i should not have be surprize if he have carry away all the company of a set of wicked wretch who be at play in sermon time
and i could tell you a true story if i would where the devil take a man out of bed from another man s wife and carry he away through the keyhole of the door
ihave see the very house where it be do and nobody hath live in it these thirty year though jones be a little offend by the impertinence of partridge he could not however avoid smile at his simplicity
the stranger do the same and then proceed with his story as will be see in the next chapter
chapter xiii
in which the forego story be far continued
my fellow-collegiate have now enter i in a new scene of life
i soon become acquaint with the whole fraternity of sharper and be let into their secret i mean into the knowledge of those gross cheat which be proper to impose upon the raw and unexperienced for there be some trick of a fine kind which be know only to a few of the gang who be at the head of their profession a degree of honour beyond my expectation for drink to which i be immoderately addicted and the natural warmth of my passion prevent i from arrive at any great success in an art which require as much coolness as the most austere school of philosophy
mr watson with whom i now live in the close amity have unluckily the former fail to a very great excess so that instead of make a fortune by his profession as some other do he be alternately rich and poor and be often oblige to surrender to his cool friend over a bottle which they never taste that plunder that he have take from cull at the public table
however we both make a shift to pick up an uncomfortable livelihood and for two year i continue of the calling during which time i taste all the variety of fortune sometimes flourish in affluence and at other be oblige to struggle with almost incredible difficulty
to-day wallowing in luxury and to-morrow reduce to the coarse and most homely fare
my fine clothe be often on my back in the evening and at the pawn-shop the next morning
one night as i be return pennyless from the gaming-table i observe a very great disturbance and a large mob gather together in the street
as i be in no danger from pickpocket i venture into the croud where upon enquiry i find that a man have be rob and very ill use by some ruffian
the wounded man appear very bloody and seem scarce able to support himself on his leg
as i have not therefore be deprive of my humanity by my present life and conversation though they have leave i very little of either honesty or shame i immediately offer my assistance to the unhappy person who thankfully accept it and put himself under my conduct beg i to convey he to some tavern where he might send for a surgeon be as he say faint with loss of blood
he seem indeed highly pleased at find one who appear in the dress of a gentleman for as to all the rest of the company present their outside be such that he could not wisely place any confidence in they
i take the poor man by the arm and lead he to the tavern where we keep our rendezvous as it happen to be the near at hand
a surgeon happen luckily to be in the house immediately attend and apply himself to dress his wound which i have the pleasure to hear be not likely to be mortal
the surgeon have very expeditiously and dextrously finish his business begin to enquire in what part of the town the wound man lodge who answer that he be come to town that very morning that his horse be at an inn in piccadilly and that he have no other lodging and very little or no acquaintance in town this surgeon whose name i have forget though i remember it begin with an r have the first character in his profession and be serjeant-surgeon to the king
he have moreover many good quality and be a very generous good-natured man and ready to do any service to his fellow-creature
he offer his patient the use of his chariot to carry he to his inn and at the same time whisper in his ear that if he want any money he would furnish he the poor man be not now capable of return thank for this generous offer for have have his eye for some time stedfastly on i he throw himself back in his chair cry oh my son
my son and then faint away
many of the people present imagine this accident have happen through his loss of blood but i who at the same time begin to recollect the feature of my father be now confirm in my suspicion and satisfy that it be he himself who appear before i
i presently run to he raise he in my arm and kiss his cold lip with the utmost eagerness
here i must draw a curtain over a scene which i cannot describe for though i do not lose my being as my father for a while do my sens be however so overpowered with affright and surprize that i be a stranger to what pass during some minute and indeed till my father have again recover from his swoon and i find myself in his arm both tenderly embrace each other while the tear trickle a-pace down the cheek of each of we
most of those present seem affect by this scene which we who might be consider as the actor in it be desirous of remove from the eye of all spectator as fast as we could my father therefore accept the kind offer of the surgeon s chariot and i attend he in it to his inn
when we be alone together he gently upbraid i with have neglect to write to he during so long a time but entirely omit the mention of that crime which have occasion it
he then inform i of my mother s death and insist on my return home with he say that he have long suffer the great anxiety on my account that he know not whether he have most fear my death or wish it since he have so many more dreadful apprehension for i
at last he say a neighbouring gentleman who have just recover a son from the same place inform he where i be and that to reclaim i from this course of life be the sole cause of his journey to london he thank heaven he have succeed so far as to find i out by mean of an accident which have like to have prove fatal to he and have the pleasure to think he partly owe his preservation to my humanity with which he profest himself to be more delighted than he should have be with my filial piety if i have know that the object of all my care be my own father
vice have not so deprave my heart as to excite in it an insensibility of so much paternal affection though so unworthily bestow
i presently promise to obey his command in my return home with he as soon as he be able to travel which indeed he be in a very few day by the assistance of that excellent surgeon who have undertake his cure
the day precede my father s journey before which time i scarce ever leave he i go to take my leave of some of my most intimate acquaintance particularly of mr watson who dissuade i from bury myself as he call it out of a simple compliance with the fond desire of a foolish old fellow
such sollicitation however have no effect and i once more see my own home
my father now greatly sollicite i to think of marriage but my inclination be utterly averse to any such thought
i have taste of love already and perhaps you know the extravagant excess of that most tender and most violent passion here the old gentleman pause and look earnestly at jones whose countenance within a minute s space display the extremity of both red and white
upon which the old man without make any observation renew his narrative
be now provide with all the necessary of life i betook myself once again to study and that with a more inordinate application than i have ever do formerly
the book which now employ my time solely be those as well antient as modern which treat of true philosophy a word which be by many thought to be the subject only of farce and ridicule
i now read over the work of aristotle and plato with the rest of those inestimable treasure which antient greece have bequeath to the world
these author though they instruct i in no science by which man may promise to themselves to acquire the least rich or worldly power teach i however the art of despise the high acquisition of both
they elevate the mind and steel and harden it against the capricious invasion of fortune
they not only instruct in the knowledge of wisdom but confirm man in her habit and demonstrate plainly that this must be our guide if we propose ever to arrive at the great worldly happiness or to defend ourselves with any tolerable security against the misery which everywhere surround and invest we
to this i add another study compare to which all the philosophy teach by the wise heathen be little well than a dream and be indeed as full of vanity as the silly jester ever pleased to represent it
this be that divine wisdom which be alone to be find in the holy scriptures for they impart to we the knowledge and assurance of thing much more worthy our attention than all which this world can offer to our acceptance of thing which heaven itself hath condescend to reveal to we and to the small knowledge of which the high human wit unassisted could never ascend
i begin now to think all the time i have spend with the good heathen writer be little more than labour lose for however pleasant and delightful their lesson may be or however adequate to the right regulation of our conduct with respect to this world only yet when compare with the glory reveal in scripture their high document will appear as trifling and of as little consequence as the rule by which child regulate their childish little game and pastime
true it be that philosophy make we wise but christianity make we well man
philosophy elevate and steel the mind christianity soften and sweeten it
the former make we the object of human admiration the latter of divine love
that insure we a temporal but this an eternal happiness but i be afraid i tire you with my rhapsody not at all cry partridge lud forbid we should be tired with good thing i have spend continue the stranger about four year in the most delightful manner to myself totally give up to contemplation and entirely unembarrassed with the affair of the world when i lose the good of father and one whom i so entirely love that my grief at his loss exceed all description
i now abandon my book and give myself up for a whole month to the effect of melancholy and despair
time however the good physician of the mind at length bring i relief ay ay tempus edax rerum say partridge i then continue the strange betook myself again to my former study which i may say perfect my cure for philosophy and religion may be call the exercise of the mind and when this be disorder they be as wholesome as exercise can be to a distempered body
they do indeed produce similar effect with exercise for they strengthen and confirm the mind till man become in the noble strain of horace-- fortis et in seipso totus tere atque rotundus externi ne quid valeat per laeve morari in quem manca ruit semper fortuna firm in himself who on himself rely polish d and round who run his proper course and break misfortune with superior force mr francis
here jones smile at some conceit which intrude itself into his imagination but the stranger i believe perceive it not and proceed thus my circumstance be now greatly alter by the death of that good of man for my brother who be now become master of the house differ so widely from i in his inclination and our pursuit in life have be so very various that we be the bad of company to each other but what make our live together still more disagreeable be the little harmony which could subsist between the few who resort to i and the numerous train of sportsman who often attend my brother from the field to the table for such fellow besides the noise and nonsense with which they persecute the ear of sober man endeavour always to attack they with affront and contempt
this be so much the case that neither i myself nor my friend could ever sit down to a meal with they without be treat with derision because we be unacquainted with the phrase of sportsman
for man of true learning and almost universal knowledge always compassionate the ignorance of other but fellow who excel in some little low contemptible art be always certain to despise those who be unacquainted with that art
in short we soon separate and i go by the advice of a physician to drink the bath water for my violent affliction add to a sedentary life have throw i into a kind of paralytic disorder for which those water be account an almost certain cure
the second day after my arrival as i be walk by the river the sun shine so intensely hot though it be early in the year that i retire to the shelter of some willow and sit down by the river side
here i have not be seat long before i hear a person on the other side of the willow sigh and bemoan himself bitterly
on a sudden have utter a most impious oath he cry i be resolve to bear it no long and directly throw himself into the water
i immediately start and run towards the place call at the same time as loudly as i could for assistance
an angler happen luckily to be a-fishing a little below i though some very high sedge have hide he from my sight
he immediately come up and both of we together not without some hazard of our life draw the body to the shore
at first we perceive no sign of life remain but have hold the body up by the heel for we soon have assistance enough it discharge a vast quantity of water at the mouth and at length begin to discover some symptom of breathing and a little afterwards to move both its hand and its leg
an apothecary who happen to be present among other advise that the body which seem now to have pretty well empty itself of water and which begin to have many convulsive motion should be directly take up and carry into a warm bed
this be accordingly perform the apothecary and myself attend
as we be go towards an inn for we know not the man s lodging luckily a woman meet we who after some violent screaming tell we that the gentleman lodge at her house
when i have see the man safely deposit there i leave he to the care of the apothecary who i suppose use all the right method with he for the next morning i hear he have perfectly recover his sens
i then go to visit he intend to search out as well as i could the cause of his have attempt so desperate an act and to prevent as far as i be able his pursue such wicked intention for the future
i be no soon admit into his chamber than we both instantly know each other for who should this person be but my good friend mr watson
here i will not trouble you with what past at our first interview for i would avoid prolixity as much as possible pray let we hear all cry partridge i want mightily to know what bring he to bath you shall hear everything material answer the stranger and then proceed to relate what we shall proceed to write after we have give a short breathing time to both ourselves and the reader
chapter xiv
in which the man of the hill conclude his history
mr watson continue the strange very freely acquaint i that the unhappy situation of his circumstance occasion by a tide of ill luck have in a manner force he to a resolution of destroy himself
i now begin to argue very seriously with he in opposition to this heathenish or indeed diabolical principle of the lawfulness of self-murder and say everything which occur to i on the subject but to my great concern it seem to have very little effect on he
he seem not at all to repent of what he have do and give i reason to fear he would soon make a second attempt of the like horrible kind
when i have finish my discourse instead of endeavour to answer my argument he look i stedfastly in the face and with a smile say you be strangely alter my good friend since i remember you
i question whether any of our bishop could make a well argument against suicide than you have entertain i with but unless you can find somebody who will lend i a cool hundred i must either hang or drown or starve and in my opinion the last death be the most terrible of the three i answer he very gravely that i be indeed alter since i have see he last
that i have find leisure to look into my folly and to repent of they
i then advise he to pursue the same step and at last conclude with an assurance that i myself would lend he a hundred pound if it would be of any service to his affair and he would not put it into the power of a die to deprive he of it
mr watson who seem almost compose in slumber by the former part of my discourse be rouse by the latter
he seize my hand eagerly give i a thousand thank and declare i be a friend indeed add that he hop i have a well opinion of he than to imagine he have profit so little by experience as to put any confidence in those damned dice which have so often deceive he
no no cry he let i but once handsomely be set up again and if ever fortune make a break merchant of i afterwards i will forgive she i very well understand the language of set up and break merchant
i therefore say to he with a very grave face mr watson you must endeavour to find out some business or employment by which you may procure yourself a livelihood and i promise you could i see any probability of be repay hereafter i would advance a much large sum than what you have mention to equip you in any fair and honourable call but as to gaming besides the baseness and wickedness of make it a profession you be really to my own knowledge unfit for it and it will end in your certain ruin
