why now thatbe strange answer he neither you nor any of my friend would ever allow i to know anything of the matter and yet i believe i be as good a hand at every game as any of you all and i heartily wish i be to play with you only for your whole fortune i should desire no well sport and i would let you name your game into the bargain but come my dear boy have you the hundred in your pocket i answer i have only a bill for 50 which i deliver he and promise to bring he the rest next morning and after give he a little more advice take my leave
i be indeed well than my word for i return to he that very afternoon
when i enter the room i find he sit up in his bed at card with a notorious gamester
this sight you will imagine shock i not a little to which i may add the mortification of see my bill deliver by he to his antagonist and thirty guineas only give in exchange for it
the other gamester presently quit the room and then watson declare he be ashamed to see i but say he i find luck run so damnably against i that i will resolve to leave off play for ever
i have think of the kind proposal you make i ever since and i promise you there shall be no fault in i if i do not put it in execution though i have no great faith in his promise i produce he the remainder of the hundred in consequence of my own for which he give i a note which be all i ever expect to see in return for my money
we be prevent from any further discourse at present by the arrival of the apothecary who with much joy in his countenance and without even ask his patient how he do proclaim there be great news arrive in a letter to himself which he say would shortly be public that the duke of monmouth be land in the west with a vast army of dutch and that another vast fleet hover over the coast of norfolk and be to make a descent there in order to favour the duke s enterprize with a diversion on that side this apothecary be one of the great politician of his time
he be more delighted with the most paultry packet than with the good patient and the high joy he be capable of he receive from have a piece of news in his possession an hour or two soon than any other person in the town
his advice however be seldom authentic for he would swallow almost anything as a truth--a humour which many make use of to impose upon he
thus it happen with what he at present communicate for it be know within a short time afterwards that the duke be really land but that his army consist only of a few attendant and as to the diversion in norfolk it be entirely false
the apothecary staid no long in the room than while he acquaint we with his news and then without say a syllable to his patient on any other subject depart to spread his advice all over the town
event of this nature in the public be generally apt to eclipse all private concern
our discourse therefore now become entirely political for my own part i have be for some time very seriously affect with the danger to which the protestant religion be so visibly expose under a popish prince and think the apprehension of it alone sufficient to justify that insurrection for no real security can ever be find against the persecuting spirit of popery when arm with power except the deprive it of that power as woeful experience presently show
you know how king james behave after get the well of this attempt how little he value either his royal word or coronation oath or the liberty and right of his people
but all have not the sense to foresee this at first and therefore the duke of monmouth be weakly support yet all could feel when the evil come upon they and therefore all unite at last to drive out that king against whose exclusion a great party among we have so warmly contend during the reign of his brother and for whom they now fight with such zeal and affection what you say interrupt jones be very true and it have often strike i as the most wonderful thing i ever read of in history that so soon after this convincing experience which bring our whole nation to join so unanimously in expel king james for the preservation of our religion and liberty there should be a party among we mad enough to desire the place his family again on the throne you be not in earnest answer the old man there can be no such party
as bad an opinion as i have of mankind i cannot believe they infatuate to such a degree
there may be some hot-head papists lead by their priest to engage in this desperate cause and think it a holy war but that protestant that be member of the church of england should be such apostate such felos de se i cannot believe it no no young man unacquainted as i be with what have past in the world for these last thirty year i cannot be so impose upon as to credit so foolish a tale but i see you have a mind to sport with my ignorance can it be possible reply jones that you have live so much out of the world as not to know that during that time there have be two rebellion in favour of the son of king james one of which be now actually rag in the very heart of the kingdom at these word the old gentleman start up and in a most solemn tone of voice conjure jones by his maker to tell he if what he say be really true which the other as solemnly affirm he walk several turn about the room in a profound silence then cry then laugh and at last fall down on his knee and bless god in a loud thanksgive prayer for have deliver he from all society with human nature which could be capable of such monstrous extravagance
after which be remind by jones that he have break off his story he resume it again in this manner as mankind in the day i be speak of be not yet arrive at that pitch of madness which i find they be capable of now and which to be sure i have only escape by live alone and at a distance from the contagion there be a considerable rise in favour of monmouth and my principle strongly incline i to take the same part i determine to join he and mr watson from different motif concur in the same resolution for the spirit of a gamester will carry a man as far upon such an occasion as the spirit of patriotism we soon provide ourselves with all necessary and go to the duke at bridgewater
the unfortunate event of this enterprize you be i conclude as well acquainted with as myself
i escape together with mr watson from the battle at sedgemore in which action i receive a slight wound
we ride near forty mile together on the exeter road and then abandon our horse scramble as well as we could through the field and bye-road till we arrive at a little wild hut on a common where a poor old woman take all the care of we she could and dress my wound with salve which quickly heal it pray sir where be the wound say partridge
the stranger satisfy he it be in his arm and then continue his narrative
here sir say he mr watson leave i the next morning in order as he pretend to get we some provision from the town of collumpton but--can i relate it or can you believe it this mr watson this friend this base barbarous treacherous villain betray i to a party of horse belong to king james and at his return deliver i into their hand
the soldier be six in number have now seize i and be conduct i to taunton gaol but neither my present situation nor the apprehension of what might happen to i be half so irksome to my mind as the company of my false friend who have surrender himself be likewise consider as a prisoner though he be well treat as be to make his peace at my expense
he at first endeavour to excuse his treachery but when he receive nothing but scorn and upbraid from i he soon change his note abuse i as the most atrocious and malicious rebel and lay all his own guilt to my charge who as he declare have solicit and even threaten he to make he take up arm against his gracious as well as lawful sovereign
this false evidence for in reality he have be much the forwarder of the two sting i to the quick and raise an indignation scarce conceivable by those who have not feel it
however fortune at length take pity on i for as we be get a little beyond wellington in a narrow lane my guard receive a false alarm that near fifty of the enemy be at hand upon which they shift for themselves and leave i and my betrayer to do the same
that villain immediately run from i and i be glad he do or i should have certainly endeavour though i have no arm to have execute vengeance on his baseness
i be now once more at liberty and immediately withdraw from the highway into the field i travel on scarce know which way i go and make it my chief care to avoid all public road and all town--nay even the most homely house for i imagine every human creature whom i see desirous of betray i
at last after ramble several day about the country during which the field afford i the same bed and the same food which nature bestow on our savage brother of the creation i at length arrive at this place where the solitude and wildness of the country invite i to fix my abode
the first person with whom i take up my habitation be the mother of this old woman with whom i remain conceal till the news of the glorious revolution put an end to all my apprehension of danger and give i an opportunity of once more visit my own home and of enquire a little into my affair which i soon settle as agreeably to my brother as to myself have resign everything to he for which he pay i the sum of a thousand pound and settle on i an annuity for life
his behaviour in this last instance as in all other be selfish and ungenerous
i could not look on he as my friend nor indeed do he desire that i should so i presently take my leave of he as well as of my other acquaintance and from that day to this my history be little well than a blank and be it possible sir say jones that you can have reside here from that day to this o no sir answer the gentleman i have be a great traveller and there be few part of europe with which i be not acquaint i have not sir cry jones the assurance to ask it of you now indeed it would be cruel after so much breath as you have already spend but you will give i leave to wish for some further opportunity of hear the excellent observation which a man of your sense and knowledge of the world must have make in so long a course of travel indeed young gentleman answer the stranger i will endeavour to satisfy your curiosity on this head likewise as far as i be able jones attempt fresh apology but be prevent and while he and partridge sit with greedy and impatient ear the stranger proceed as in the next chapter
the rest of this paragraph and the two follow paragraph in the first edition be as follow for my own part i have be for some time very seriously affect with the danger to which the protestant religion be so visibly expose that nothing but the immediate interposition of providence seem capable of preserve it for king james have indeed declare war against the protestant cause
he have bring known papist into the army and attempt to bring they into the church and into the university
popish priest swarm through the nation appear publicly in their habit and boast that they should shortly walk in procession through the street
our own clergy be forbid to preach against popery and bishop be order to supend those who do and to do the business at once an illegal ecclesiastical commission be erect little inferior to an inquisition of which probably it be intend to be the ringleader
thus as our duty to the king can never be call more than our second duty he have discharge we from this by make it incompatible with our preserve the first which be surely to heaven
besides this he have dissolve his subject from their allegiance by break his coronation oath to which their allegiance be annex for he have imprison bishop because they would not give up their religion and turn out judge because they would not absolutely surrender the law into his hand nay he seize this himself and when he claim a dispense power he declare himself in fact as absolute as any tyrant ever be or can be
i have recapitulate these matter in full lest some of they should have be omit in history and i think nothing less than such provocation as i have here mention nothing less than certain and imminent danger to their religion and liberty can justify or even mitigate the dreadful sin of rebellion in any people i promise you sir say jones all these fact and more i have read in history but i will tell you a fact which be not yet record and of which i suppose you be ignorant
there be actually now a rebellion on foot in this kingdom in favour of the son of that very king james a profess papist more bigoted if possible than his father and this carry on by protestants against a king who hath never in one single instance make the least invasion on our liberty prodigious indeed answer the stranger
you tell i what would be incredible of a nation which do not deserve the character that virgil give of a woman varium et mutabile semper
surely this be to be unworthy of the care which providence seem to have take of we in the preservation of our religion against the powerful design and constant machination of popery a preservation so strange and unaccountable that i almost think we may appeal to it as to a miracle for the proof of its holiness
prodigious indeed
a protestant rebellion in favour of a popish prince
the folly of mankind be as wonderful as their knavery--but to conclude my story i resolve to take arm in defence of my country of my religion and my liberty and mr
watson join in the same resolution
we soon provide ourselves with an necessary and join the duke at bridgewater the unfortunate event of this enterprise you be perhaps well acquaint with than myself
i escape together with mr
watson from the battle at sedgemore
chapter xv
a brief history of europe and a curious discourse between mr jones and the man of the hill
in italy the landlord be very silent
in france they be more talkative but yet civil
in germany and holland they be generally very impertinent
and as for their honesty i believe it be pretty equal in all those country
the laquais à louange be sure to lose no opportunity of cheat you and as for the postilion i think they be pretty much alike all the world over
these sir be the observation on man which i make in my travel for these be the only man i ever converse with
my design when i go abroad be to divert myself by see the wondrous variety of prospect beast bird fish insect and vegetable with which god have be pleased to enrich the several part of this globe a variety which as it must give great pleasure to a contemplative beholder so doth it admirably display the power and wisdom and goodness of the creator
indeed to say the truth there be but one work in his whole creation that doth he any dishonour and with that i have long since avoid hold any conversation you will pardon i cry jones but i have always imagine that there be in this very work you mention as great variety as in all the rest for besides the difference of inclination custom and climate have i be tell introduce the utmost diversity into human nature very little indeed answer the other those who travel in order to acquaint themselves with the different manner of man might spare themselves much pain by go to a carnival at venice for there they will see at once all which they can discover in the several court of europe
the same hypocrisy the same fraud in short the same folly and vice dress in different habit
in spain these be equip with much gravity and in italy with vast splendor
in france a knave be dress like a fop and in the northern country like a sloven
but human nature be everywhere the same everywhere the object of detestation and scorn
as for my own part i past through all these nation as you perhaps may have do through a croud at a shew-jostling to get by they hold my nose with one hand and defend my pocket with the other without speak a word to any of they while i be press on to see what i want to see which however entertain it might be in itself scarce make i amend for the trouble the company give i do not you find some of the nation among which you travel less troublesome to you than other say jones
o yes reply the old man the turks be much more tolerable to i than the christians for they be man of profound taciturnity and never disturb a stranger with question
now and then indeed they bestow a short curse upon he or spit in his face as he walk the street but then they have do with he and a man may live an age in their country without hear a dozen word from they
but of all the people i ever see heaven defend i from the french
with their damned prate and civility and do the honour of their nation to stranger as they be pleased to call it but indeed set forth their own vanity they be so troublesome that i have infinitely rather pass my life with the hottentots than set my foot in paris again
they be a nasty people but their nastiness be mostly without whereas in france and some other nation that i willnot name it be all within and make they stink much more to my reason than that of hottentots do to my nose
thus sir i have end the history of my life for as to all that series of year during which i have live retire here it afford no variety to entertain you and may be almost consider as one day the retirement have be so compleat that i could hardly have enjoy a more absolute solitude in the desert of the thebais than here in the midst of this populous kingdom
as i have no estate i be plague with no tenant or steward my annuity be pay i pretty regularly as indeed it ought to be for it be much less than what i might have expect in return for what i give up
visit i admit none and the old woman who keep my house know that her place entirely depend upon she save i all the trouble of buy the thing that i want keep off all sollicitation or business from i and hold her tongue whenever i be within hearing
as my walk be all by night i be pretty secure in this wild unfrequented place from meet any company
some few person i have meet by chance and send they home heartily fright as from the oddness of my dress and figure they take i for a ghost or a hobgoblin
but what have happen to-night show that even here i cannot be safe from the villany of man for without your assistance i have not only be rob but very probably murder the rest of this paragraph be omit in the third edition jones thank the stranger for the trouble he have take in relate his story and then express some wonder how he could possibly endure a life of such solitude in which say he you may well complain of the want of variety
indeed i be astonish how you have fill up or rather kill so much of your time i be not at all surprize answer the other that to one whose affection and thought be fix on the world my hour should appear to have want employment in this place but there be one single act for which the whole life of man be infinitely too short what time can suffice for the contemplation and worship of that glorious immortal and eternal being among the work of whose stupendous creation not only this globe but even those numberless luminary which we may here behold spangle all the sky though they should many of they be sun light different system of world may possibly appear but as a few atom oppose to the whole earth which we inhabit
can a man who by divine meditation be admit as it be into the conversation of this ineffable incomprehensible majesty think day or year or age too long for the continuance of so ravish an honour
shall the trifling amusement the palling pleasure the silly business of the world roll away our hour too swiftly from we and shall the pace of time seem sluggish to a mind exercise in study so high so important and so glorious
as no time be sufficient so no place be improper for this great concern
on what object can we cast our eye which may not inspire we with idea of his power of his wisdom and of his goodness
it be not necessary that the rise sun should dart his fiery glory over the eastern horizon nor that the boisterous wind should rush from their cavern and shake the lofty forest nor that the open cloud should pour their deluge on the plain it be not necessary i say that any of these should proclaim his majesty there be not an insect not a vegetable of so low an order in the creation as not to be honour with bear mark of the attribute of its great creator mark not only of his power but of his wisdom and goodness
man alone the king of this globe the last and great work of the supreme being below the sun man alone hath basely dishonour his own nature and by dishonesty cruelty ingratitude and treachery hath call his maker s goodness in question by puzzle we to account how a benevolent being should form so foolish and so vile an animal
yet this be the being from whose conversation you think i suppose that i have be unfortunately restrain and without whose blessed society life in your opinion must be tedious and insipid in the former part of what you say reply jones i most heartily and readily concur but i believe as well as hope that the abhorrence which you express for mankind in the conclusion be much too general
indeed you here fall into an error which in my little experience i have observe to be a very common one by take the character of mankind from the bad and base among they whereas indeed as an excellent writer observe nothing should be esteem as characteristical of a specie but what be to be find among the good and most perfect individual of that specie
this error i believe be generally commit by those who from want of proper caution in the choice of their friend and acquaintance have suffer injury from bad and worthless man two or three instance of which be very unjustly charge on all human nature i think i have experience enough of it answer the other my first mistress and my first friend betray i in the basest manner and in matter which threaten to be of the bad of consequence--even to bring i to a shameful death but you will pardon i cry jones if i desire you to reflect who that mistress and who that friend be
what well my good sir could be expect in love derive from the stew or in friendship first produce and nourish at the gaming-table
to take the character of woman from the former instance or of man from the latter would be as unjust as to assert that air be a nauseous and unwholesome element because we find it so in a jake
i have live but a short time in the world and yet have know man worthy of the high friendship and woman of the high love alas
young man answer the stranger you have live you confess but a very short time in the world i be somewhat old than you when i be of the same opinion you might have remain so still reply jones if you have not be unfortunate i will venture to say incautious in the placing your affection
if there be indeed much more wickedness in the world than there be it would not prove such general assertion against human nature since much of this arrive by mere accident and many a man who commit evil be not totally bad and corrupt in his heart
in truth none seem to have any title to assert human nature to be necessarily and universally evil but those whose own mind afford they one instance of this natural depravity which be not i be convince your case and such say the stranger will be always the most backward to assert any such thing
knave will no more endeavour to persuade we of the baseness of mankind than a highwayman will inform you that there be thief on the road
this would indeed be a method to put you on your guard and to defeat their own purpose
for which reason though knave as i remember be very apt to abuse particular person yet they never cast any reflection on human nature in general the old gentleman speak this so warmly that as jones despair of make a convert and be unwilling to offend he return no answer
the day now begin to send forth its first stream of light when jones make an apology to the stranger for have staid so long and perhaps detain he from his rest
the stranger answer he never want rest less than at present for that day and night be indifferent season to he and that he commonly make use of the former for the time of his repose and of the latter for his walk and lucubration
however say he it be now a most lovely morning and if you can bear any long to be without your own rest or food i will gladly entertain you with the sight of some very fine prospect which i believe you have not yet see jones very readily embrace this offer and they immediately set forward together from the cottage
as for partridge he have fall into a profound repose just as the stranger have finish his story for his curiosity be satisfied and the subsequent discourse be not forcible enough in its operation to conjure down the charm of sleep
jones therefore leave he to enjoy his nap and as the reader may perhaps be at this season glad of the same favour we will here put an end to the eighth book of our history
book ix
contain twelve hours
chapter i
of those who lawfully may and of those who may not write such history as this
among other good us for which i have think proper to institute these several introductory chapter i have consider they as a kind of mark or stamp which may hereafter enable a very indifferent reader to distinguish what be true and genuine in this historic kind of writing from what be false and counterfeit
indeed it seem likely that some such mark may shortly become necessary since the favourable reception which two or three author have lately procure for their work of this nature from the public will probably serve as an encouragement to many other to undertake the like
thus a swarm of foolish novel and monstrous romance will be produce either to the great impoverishing of bookseller or to the great loss of time and depravation of moral in the reader nay often to the spreading of scandal and calumny and to the prejudice of the character of many worthy and honest people
i question not but the ingenious author of the spectator be principally induce to prefix greek and latin motto to every paper from the same consideration of guard against the pursuit of those scribbler who have no talent of a writer but what be teach by the writing-master be yet nowise afraid nor ashamed to assume the same title with the great genius than their good brother in the fable be of bray in the lion s skin
by the device therefore of his motto it become impracticable for any man to presume to imitate the spectators without understanding at least one sentence in the learn language
in the same manner i have now secure myself from the imitation of those who be utterly incapable of any degree of reflection and whose learning be not equal to an essay
i would not be here understand to insinuate that the great merit of such historical production can ever lie in these introductory chapter but in fact those part which contain mere narrative only afford much more encouragement to the pen of an imitator than those which be compose of observation and reflection
here i mean such imitator as rowe be of shakespear or as horace hint some of the romans be of cato by bare foot and sour face
to invent good story and to tell they well be possibly very rare talent and yet i have observe few person who have scruple to aim at both and if we examine the romance and novel with which the world abound i think we may fairly conclude that most of the author would not have attempt to show their tooth if the expression may be allow i in any other way of writing nor could indeed have string together a dozen sentence on any other subject whatever
scribimus indocti doctique passim each desperate blockhead dare to write verse be the trade of every live wight francis
may be more truly say of the historian and biographer than of any other specie of writing for all the art and science even criticism itself require some little degree of learn and knowledge
poetry indeed may perhaps be think an exception but then it demand number or something like number whereas to the composition of novel and romance nothing be necessary but paper pen and ink with the manual capacity of use they
this i conceive their production show to be the opinion of the author themselves and this must be the opinion of their reader if indeed there be any such
hence we be to derive that universal contempt which the world who always denominate the whole from the majority have cast on all historical writer who do not draw their material from record
and it be the apprehension of this contempt that hath make we so cautiously avoid the term romance a name with which we might otherwise have be well enough content
though as we have good authority for all our character no less indeed than the vast authentic doomsday-book of nature as be elsewhere hint our labour have sufficient title to the name of history
certainly they deserve some distinction from those work which one of the witty of man regard only as proceed from a pruritus or indeed rather from a looseness of the brain
but besides the dishonour which be thus cast on one of the most useful as well as entertaining of all kind of writing there be just reason to apprehend that by encourage such author we shall propagate much dishonour of another kind i mean to the character of many good and valuable member of society for the dull writer no more than the dull companion be always inoffensive
they have both enough of language to be indecent and abusive
and surely if the opinion just above cite be true we cannot wonder that work so nastily derive should be nasty themselves or have a tendency to make other so
to prevent therefore for the future such intemperate abuse of leisure of letter and of the liberty of the press especially as the world seem at present to be more than usually threaten with they i shall here venture to mention some qualification every one of which be in a pretty high degree necessary to this order of historian
the first be genius without a full vein of which no study say horace can avail we
by genius i would understand that power or rather those power of the mind which be capable of penetrate into all thing within our reach and knowledge and of distinguish their essential difference
these be no other than invention and judgment and they be both call by the collective name of genius as they be of those gift of nature which we bring with we into the world
concern each of which many seem to have fall into very great error for by invention i believe be generally understand a creative faculty which would indeed prove most romance writer to have the high pretension to it whereas by invention be really mean no more and so the word signify than discovery or find out or to explain it at large a quick and sagacious penetration into the true essence of all the object of our contemplation
this i think can rarely exist without the concomitancy of judgment for how we can be say to have discover the true essence of two thing without discern their difference seem to i hard to conceive
now this last be the undisputed province of judgment and yet some few man of wit have agree with all the dull fellow in the world in represent these two to have be seldom or never the property of one and the same person
but though they should be so they be not sufficient for our purpose without a good share of learning for which i could again cite the authority of horace and of many other if any be necessary to prove that tool be of no service to a workman when they be not sharpen by art or when he want rule to direct he in his work or hath no matter to work upon
all these us be supply by learn for nature can only furnish we with capacity or as i have choose to illustrate it with the tool of our profession learning must fit they for use must direct they in it and lastly must contribute part at least of the material
a competent knowledge of history and of the belle-lettre be here absolutely necessary and without this share of knowledge at least to affect the character of an historian be as vain as to endeavour at build a house without timber or mortar or brick or stone
homer and milton who though they add the ornament of number to their work be both historian of our order be master of all the learning of their time
again there be another sort of knowledge beyond the power of learn to bestow and this be to be have by conversation
so necessary be this to the understanding the character of man that none be more ignorant of they than those learn pedant whose life have be entirely consume in college and among book for however exquisitely human nature may have be describe by writer the true practical system can be learn only in the world
indeed the like happen in every other kind of knowledge
neither physic nor law be to be practically know from book
nay the farmer the planter the gardener must perfect by experience what he hath acquire the rudiment of by read
how accurately soever the ingenious mr miller may have describe the plant he himself would advise his disciple to see it in the garden
as we must perceive that after the nice stroke of a shakespear or a jonson of a wycherly or an otway some touch of nature will escape the reader which the judicious action of a garrick of a cibber or a clive can convey to he so on the real stage the character show himself in a strong and bold light than he can be describe
and if this be the case in those fine and nervous description which great author themselves have take from life how much more strongly will it hold when the writer himself take his line not from nature but from book
such character be only the faint copy of a copy and can have neither the justness nor spirit of an original
there be a peculiar propriety in mention this great actor and these two most justly celebrate actress in this place as they have all form themselves on the study of nature only and not on the imitation of their predecessor
hence they have be able to excel all who have go before they a degree of merit which the servile herd of imitator can never possibly arrive at
now this conversation in our historian must be universal that be with all rank and degree of man for the knowledge of what be call high life will not instruct he in low nor e converso will his be acquaint with the inferior part of mankind teach he the manner of the superior
and though it may be think that the knowledge of either may sufficiently enable he to describe at least that in which he hath be conversant yet he will even here fall greatly short of perfection for the folly of either rank do in reality illustrate each other
for instance the affectation of high life appear more glaring and ridiculous from the simplicity of the low and again the rudeness and barbarity of this latter strike with much strong idea of absurdity when contrast with and oppose to the politeness which controul the former
besides to say the truth the manner of our historian will be improve by both these conversation for in the one he will easily find example of plainness honesty and sincerity in the other of refinement elegance and a liberality of spirit which last quality i myself have scarce ever see in man of low birth and education
nor will all the quality i have hitherto give my historian avail he unless he have what be generally mean by a good heart and be capable of feeling
the author who will make i weep say horace must first weep himself
in reality no man can paint a distress well which he doth not feel while he be paint it nor do i doubt but that the most pathetic and affect scene have be writ with tear
in the same manner it be with the ridiculous
i be convinced i never make my reader laugh heartily but where i have laugh before he unless it should happen at any time that instead of laugh with i he should be incline to laugh at i
perhaps this may have be the case at some passage in this chapter from which apprehension i will here put an end to it
chapter ii
contain a very surprizing adventure indeed which mr jones meet with in his walk with the man of the hill
aurora now first open her casement anglice the day begin to break when jones walk forth in company with the stranger and mount mazard hill of which they have no soon gain the summit than one of the most noble prospect in the world present itself to their view and which we would likewise present to the reader but for two reason first we despair of make those who have see this prospect admire our description secondly we very much doubt whether those who have not see it would understand it
jones stand for some minute fix in one posture and direct his eye towards the south upon which the old gentleman ask what he be look at with so much attention
alas
sir answer he with a sigh i be endeavour to trace out my own journey hither
good heaven
what a distance be gloucester from we
what a vast track of land must be between i and my own home ay ay young gentleman cry the other and by your sighing from what you love well than your own home or i be mistaken
i perceive now the object of your contemplation be not within your sight and yet i fancy you have a pleasure in look that way jones answer with a smile i find old friend you have not yet forget the sensation of your youth
i own my thought be employ as you have guess they now walk to that part of the hill which look to the north-west and which hang over a vast and extensive wood
here they be no soon arrive than they hear at a distance the most violent scream of a woman proceed from the wood below they
jones listen a moment and then without say a word to his companion for indeed the occasion seem sufficiently press run or rather slid down the hill and without the least apprehension or concern for his own safety make directly to the thicket whence the sound have issue
he have not enter far into the wood before he behold a most shocking sight indeed a woman stript half naked under the hand of a ruffian who have put his garter round her neck and be endeavour to draw she up to a tree
jones ask no question at this interval but fall instantly upon the villain and make such good use of his trusty oaken stick that he lay he sprawl on the ground before he could defend himself indeed almost before he know he be attack nor do he cease the prosecution of his blow till the woman herself beg he to forbear say she believe he have sufficiently do his business
the poor wretch then fall upon her knee to jones and give he a thousand thank for her deliverance
he presently lift she up and tell she he be highly pleased with the extraordinary accident which have send he thither for her relief where it be so improbable she should find any add that heaven seem to have design he as the happy instrument of her protection
nay answer she i could almost conceive you to be some good angel and to say the truth you look more like an angel than a man in my eye indeed he be a charming figure and if a very fine person and a most comely set of feature adorn with youth health strength freshness spirit and good-nature can make a man resemble an angel he certainly have that resemblance
the redeem captive have not altogether so much of the human-angelic specie she seem to be at least of the middle age nor have her face much appearance of beauty but her cloath be tear from all the upper part of her body her breast which be well form and extremely white attract the eye of her deliverer and for a few moment they stand silent and gaze at each other till the ruffian on the ground begin to move jones take the garter which have be intend for another purpose and bind both his hand behind he
and now on contemplate his face he discover greatly to his surprize and perhaps not a little to his satisfaction this very person to be no other than ensign northerton
nor have the ensign forget his former antagonist whom he know the moment he come to himself
his surprize be equal to that of jones but i conceive his pleasure be rather less on this occasion
jones help northerton upon his leg and then look he stedfastly in the face i fancy sir say he you do not expect to meet i any more in this world and i confess i have as little expectation to find you here
however fortune i see hath bring we once more together and hath give i satisfaction for the injury i have receive even without my own knowledge it be very much like a man of honour indeed answer northerton to take satisfaction by knock a man down behind his back
neither be i capable of give you satisfaction here as i have no sword but if you dare behave like a gentleman let we go where i can furnish myself with one and i will do by you as a man of honour ought doth it become such a villain as you be cry jones to contaminate the name of honour by assume it
but i shall waste no time in discourse with you
justice require satisfaction of you now and shall have it then turn to the woman he ask she if she be near her home or if not whether she be acquaint with any house in the neighbourhood where she might procure herself some decent cloath in order to proceed to a justice of the peace
she answer she be an entire stranger in that part of the world
jones then recollect himself say he have a friend near who would direct they indeed he wonder at his not follow but in fact the good man of the hill when our heroe depart sit himself down on the brow where though he have a gun in his hand he with great patience and unconcern have attend the issue
jones then step without the wood perceive the old man sit as we have just describe he he presently exert his utmost agility and with surprizing expedition ascend the hill
the old man advise he to carry the woman to upton which he say be the near town and there he would be sure of furnish she with all manner of conveniencie
jones have receive his direction to the place take his leave of the man of the hill and desire he to direct partridge the same way return hastily to the wood
our heroe at his departure to make this enquiry of his friend have consider that as the ruffian s hand be tie behind he he be incapable of execute any wicked purpose on the poor woman
besides he know he should not be beyond the reach of her voice and could return soon enough to prevent any mischief
he have moreover declare to the villain that if he attempt the least insult he would be himself immediately the executioner of vengeance on he
but jones unluckily forget that though the hand of northerton be tie his leg be at liberty nor do he lay the least injunction on the prisoner that he should not make what use of these he please
northerton therefore have give no parole of that kind think he might without any breach of honour depart not be oblige as he imagine by any rule to wait for a formal discharge
he therefore take up his leg which be at liberty and walk off through the wood which favour his retreat nor do the woman whose eye be perhaps rather turn toward her deliverer once think of his escape or give herself any concern or trouble to prevent it
jones therefore at his return find the woman alone
he would have spend some time in search for northerton but she would not permit he earnestly entreat that he would accompany she to the town whither they have be direct
as to the fellow s escape say she it give i no uneasiness for philosophy and christianity both preach up forgiveness of injury
but for you sir i be concern at the trouble i give you nay indeed my nakedness may well make i ashamed to look you in the face and if it be not for the sake of your protection i should wish to go alone jones offer she his coat but i know not for what reason she absolutely refuse the most earnest solicitation to accept it
he then beg she to forget both the cause of her confusion
with regard to the former say he i have do no more than my duty in protect you and as for the latter i will entirely remove it by walk before you all the way for i would not have my eye offend you and i could not answer for my power of resist the attractive charm of so much beauty thus our heroe and the redeem lady walk in the same manner as orpheus and eurydice march heretofore but though i cannot believe that jones be designedly tempt by his fair one to look behind he yet as she frequently want his assistance to help she over stile and have besides many trip and other accident he be often oblige to turn about
however he have well fortune than what attend poor orpheus for he bring his companion or rather follower safe into the famous town of upton
chapter iii
the arrival of mr jones with his lady at the inn with a very full description of the battle of upton
though the reader we doubt not be very eager to know who this lady be and how she fall into the hand of mr northerton we must beg he to suspend his curiosity for a short time as we be oblige for some very good reason which hereafter perhaps he may guess to delay his satisfaction a little long
mr jones and his fair companion no soon enter the town than they go directly to that inn which in their eye present the fairest appearance to the street
here jones have order a servant to show a room above stair be ascend when the dishevel fair hastily follow be lay hold on by the master of the house who cry heyday where be that beggar wench go
stay below stair i desire you but jones at that instant thunder from above let the lady come up in so authoritative a voice that the good man instantly withdraw his hand and the lady make the good of her way to the chamber
here jones wish her joy of her safe arrival and then depart in order as he promise to send the landlady up with some cloath
the poor woman thank he heartily for all his kindness and say she hop she should see he again soon to thank he a thousand time more
during this short conversation she cover her white bosom as well as she could possibly with her arm for jones could not avoid steal a sly peep or two though he take all imaginable care to avoid give any offence
our traveller have happen to take up their residence at a house of exceed good repute whither irish lady of strict virtue and many northern lass of the same predicament be accustom to resort in their way to bath
the landlady therefore would by no mean have admit any conversation of a disreputable kind to pass under her roof
indeed so foul and contagious be all such proceeding that they contaminate the very innocent scene where they be commit and give the name of a bad house or of a house of ill repute to all those where they be suffer to be carry on
not that i would intimate that such strict chastity as be preserve in the temple of vesta can possibly be maintain at a public inn
my good landlady do not hope for such a blessing nor would any of the lady i have speak of or indeed any other of the most rigid note have expect or insist on any such thing
but to exclude all vulgar concubinage and to drive all whore in rag from within the wall be within the power of every one
this my landlady very strictly adhere to and this her virtuous guest who do not travel in rag would very reasonably have expect of she
now it require no very blameable degree of suspicion to imagine that mr jones and his ragged companion have certain purpose in their intention which though tolerate in some christian country connive at in other and practise in all be however as expressly forbid as murder or any other horrid vice by that religion which be universally believe in those country
the landlady therefore have no soon receive an intimation of the entrance of the above-say person than she begin to meditate the most expeditious mean for their expulsion
in order to this she have provide herself with a long and deadly instrument with which in time of peace the chambermaid be willnot to demolish the labour of the industrious spider
in vulgar phrase she have take up the broomstick and be just about to sally from the kitchen when jones accost she with a demand of a gown and other vestment to cover the half-naked woman upstairs
nothing can be more provoking to the human temper nor more dangerous to that cardinal virtue patience than solicitation of extraordinary office of kindness on behalf of those very person with whom we be highly incensed
for this reason shakespear hath artfully introduce his desdemona soliciting favour for cassio of her husband as the mean of inflame not only his jealousy but his rage to the high pitch of madness and we find the unfortunate moor less able to command his passion on this occasion than even when he behold his value present to his wife in the hand of his suppose rival
in fact we regard these effort as insult on our understanding and to such the pride of man be very difficultly bring to submit
my landlady though a very good-temper woman have i suppose some of this pride in her composition for jones have scarce end his request when she fall upon he with a certain weapon which though it be neither long nor sharp nor hard nor indeed threaten from its appearance with either death or wound hath be however hold in great dread and abhorrence by many wise man--nay by many brave one insomuch that some who have dare to look into the mouth of a loaded cannon have not dare to look into a mouth where this weapon be brandish and rather than run the hazard of its execution have content themselves with make a most pitiful and sneak figure in the eye of all their acquaintance
to confess the truth i be afraid mr jones be one of these for though he be attack and violently belabour with the aforesaid weapon he could not be provoke to make any resistance but in a most cowardly manner apply with many entreaty to his antagonist to desist from pursue she blow in plain english he only beg she with the utmost earnestness to hear he but before he could obtain his request my landlord himself enter into the fray and embrace that side of the cause which seem to stand very little in need of assistance
there be a sort of hero who be suppose to be determine in their chusing or avoid a conflict by the character and behaviour of the person whom they be to engage
these be say to know their man and jones i believe know his woman for though he have be so submissive to she he be no soon attack by her husband than he demonstrate an immediate spirit of resentment and enjoin he silence under a very severe penalty no less than that i think of be convert into fuel for his own fire
the husband with great indignation but with a mixture of pity answer you must pray first to be make able
i believe i be a well man than yourself ay every way that i be and presently proceed to discharge half-a-dozen whore at the lady above stair the last of which have scarce issue from his lip when a swing blow from the cudgel that jones carry in his hand assault he over the shoulder
it be a question whether the landlord or the landlady be the most expeditious in return this blow
my landlord whose hand be empty fall to with his fist and the good wife uplift her broom and aim at the head of jones have probably put an immediate end to the fray and to jones likewise have not the descent of this broom be prevent--not by the miraculous intervention of any heathen deity but by a very natural though fortunate accident viz by the arrival of partridge who enter the house at that instant for fear have cause he to run every step from the hill and who see the danger which threaten his master or companion which you chuse to call he prevent so sad a catastrophe by catch hold of the landlady s arm as it be brandish aloft in the air
the landlady soon perceive the impediment which prevent her blow and be unable to rescue her arm from the hand of partridge she let fall the broom and then leave jones to the discipline of her husband she fall with the utmost fury on that poor fellow who have already give some intimation of himself by cry zounds
do you intend to kill my friend partridge though not much addict to battle would not however stand still when his friend be attack nor be he much displeased with that part of the combat which fall to his share he therefore return my landlady s blow as soon as he receive they and now the fight be obstinately maintain on all part and it seem doubtful to which side fortune would incline when the naked lady who have listen at the top of the stair to the dialogue which precede the engagement descend suddenly from above and without weigh the unfair inequality of two to one fall upon the poor woman who be box with partridge nor do that great champion desist but rather redouble his fury when he find fresh succour be arrive to his assistance
victory must now have fall to the side of the traveller for the brave troop must yield to number have not susan the chambermaid come luckily to support her mistress
this susan be as two-hand a wench accord to the phrase as any in the country and would i believe have beat the famed thalestris herself or any of her subject amazons for her form be robust and man-like and every way make for such encounter
as her hand and arm be form to give blow with great mischief to an enemy so be her face as well contrive to receive blow without any great injury to herself her nose be already flat to her face her lip be so large that no swelling could be perceive in they and moreover they be so hard that a fist could hardly make any impression on they
lastly her cheek-bone stand out as if nature have intend they for two bastion to defend her eye in those encounter for which she seem so well calculate and to which she be most wonderfully well inclined
this fair creature enter the field of battle immediately file to that wing where her mistress maintain so unequal a fight with one of either sex
here she presently challenge partridge to single combat
he accept the challenge and a most desperate fight begin between they
now the dog of war be let loose begin to lick their bloody lip now victory with golden wing hang hover in the air now fortune take her scale from her shelf begin to weigh the fate of tom jones his female companion and partridge against the landlord his wife and maid all which hang in exact balance before she when a good-natured accident put suddenly an end to the bloody fray with which half of the combatant have already sufficiently feast
this accident be the arrival of a coach and four upon which my landlord and landlady immediately desist from fight and at their entreaty obtain the same favour of their antagonist but susan be not so kind to partridge for that amazonian fair have overthrow and bestrid her enemy be now cuff he lustily with both her hand without any regard to his request of a cessation of arm or to those loud exclamation of murder which he roar forth
no soon however have jones quit the landlord than he fly to the rescue of his defeat companion from whom he with much difficulty draw off the enrage chambermaid but partridge be not immediately sensible of his deliverance for he still lie flat on the floor guard his face with his hand nor do he cease roar till jones have force he to look up and to perceive that the battle be at an end
the landlord who have no visible hurt and the landlady hide she well-scratch face with her handkerchief run both hastily to the door to attend the coach from which a young lady and she maid now alight
these the landlady presently usher into that room where mr jones have at first deposit his fair prize as it be the good apartment in the house
hither they be oblige to pass through the field of battle which they do with the utmost haste cover their face with their handkerchief as desirous to avoid the notice of any one
indeed their caution be quite unnecessary for the poor unfortunate helen the fatal cause of all the bloodshed be entirely take up in endeavour to conceal her own face and jones be no less occupy in rescue partridge from the fury of susan which be happily effect the poor fellow immediately depart to the pump to wash his face and to stop that bloody torrent which susan have plentifully set a-flow from his nostril
chapter iv
in which the arrival of a man of war put a final end to hostility and cause the conclusion of a firm and last peace between all party
a serjeant and a file of musqueteer with a deserter in their custody arrive about this time
the serjeant presently enquire for the principal magistrate of the town and be inform by my landlord that he himself be vest in that office
he then demand his billet together with a mug of beer and complain it be cold spread himself before the kitchen fire
mr jones be at this time comfort the poor distressed lady who sit down at a table in the kitchen and lean her head upon her arm be bemoan she misfortune but lest my fair reader should be in pain concern a particular circumstance i think proper here to acquaint they that before she have quit the room above stair she have so well cover herself with a pillowbeer which she there find that she regard to decency be not in the least violate by the presence of so many man as be now in the room
one of the soldier now go up to the serjeant and whisper something in his ear upon which he stedfastly fix his eye on the lady and have look at she for near a minute he come up to she say i ask pardon madam but i be certain i be not deceive you can be no other person than captain waters s lady the poor woman who in her present distress have very little regard the face of any person present no soon look at the serjeant than she presently recollect he and call he by his name answer that she be indeed the unhappy person he imagine she to be but add i wonder any one should know i in this disguise to which the serjeant reply he be very much surprize to see her ladyship in such a dress and be afraid some accident have happen to she an accident hath happen to i indeed say she and i be highly obliged to this gentleman point to jones that it be not a fatal one or that i be now live to mention it whatever the gentleman hath do cry the serjeant i be sure the captain will make he amend for it and if i can be of any service your ladyship may command i and i shall think myself very happy to have it in my power to serve your ladyship and so indeed may any one for i know the captain will well reward they for it the landlady who hear from the stair all that past between the serjeant and mrs waters come hastily down and run directly up to she begin to ask pardon for the offence she have commit beg that all might be impute to ignorance of her quality for lud
madam say she how should i have imagine that a lady of your fashion would appear in such a dress
i be sure madam if i have once suspect that your ladyship be your ladyship i would soon have burn my tongue out than have say what i have say and i hope your ladyship will accept of a gown till you can get your own cloath prithee woman say mrs waters cease your impertinence how can you imagine i should concern myself about anything which come from the lip of such low creature as yourself
but i be surprize at your assurance in thinking after what be past that i will condescend to put on any of your dirty thing
i would have you know creature i have a spirit above that here jones interfere and beg mrs waters to forgive the landlady and to accept her gown for i must confess cry he our appearance be a little suspicious when first we come in and i be well assure all this good woman do be as she profess out of regard to the reputation of her house yes upon my truly be it say she the gentleman speak very much like a gentleman and i see very plainly be so and to be certain the house be well known to be a house of as good reputation as any on the road and though i say it be frequent by gentry of the good quality both irish and english
i defy anybody to say black be my eye for that matter
and as i be say if i have know your ladyship to be your ladyship i would as soon have burn my finger as have affront your ladyship but truly where gentry come and spend their money i be not willing that they should be scandalize by a set of poor shabby vermin that wherever they go leave more lice than money behind they such folk never raise my compassion for to be certain it be foolish to have any for they and if our justice do as they ought they would be all whipt out of the kingdom for to be certain it be what be most fitting for they
but as for your ladyship i be heartily sorry your ladyship hath have a misfortune and if your ladyship will do i the honour to wear my cloath till you can get some of your ladyship s own to be certain the good i have be at your ladyship s service whether cold shame or the persuasion of mr jones prevail most on mrs waters i will not determine but she suffer herself to be pacify by this speech of my landlady and retire with that good woman in order to apparel herself in a decent manner
my landlord be likewise begin his oration to jones but be presently interrupt by that generous youth who shake he heartily by the hand and assure he of entire forgiveness say if you be satisfied my worthy friend i promise you i be and indeed in one sense the landlord have the well reason to be satisfy for he have receive a bellyfull of drubbing whereas jones have scarce feel a single blow
partridge who have be all this time wash his bloody nose at the pump return into the kitchen at the instant when his master and the landlord be shake hand with each other
as he be of a peaceable disposition he be pleased with those symptom of reconciliation and though his face bear some mark of susan s fist and many more of her nail he rather choose to be content with his fortune in the last battle than to endeavour at better it in another
the heroic susan be likewise well content with her victory though it have cost she a black eye which partridge have give she at the first onset
between these two therefore a league be strike and those hand which have be the instrument of war become now the mediator of peace
matter be thus restore to a perfect calm at which the serjeant though it may seem so contrary to the principle of his profession testify his approbation
why now thatbe friendly say he d--n i i hate to see two people bear ill-will to one another after they have have a tussel
the only way when friend quarrel be to see it out fairly in a friendly manner as a man may call it either with a fist or sword or pistol accord as they like and then let it be all over for my own part d--n i if ever i love my friend well than when i be fight with he
to bear malice be more like a frenchman than an englishman he then propose a libation as a necessary part of the ceremony at all treaty of this kind
perhaps the reader may here conclude that he be well verse in antient history but this though highly probable as he cite no authority to support the custom i will not affirm with any confidence
most likely indeed it be that he found his opinion on very good authority since he confirm it with many violent oath
jones no soon hear the proposal than immediately agree with the learn serjeant he order a bowl or rather a large mug fill with the liquor use on these occasion to be bring in and then begin the ceremony himself
he place his right hand in that of the landlord and seize the bowl with his left utter the usual word and then make his libation
after which the same be observe by all present
indeed there be very little need of be particular in describe the whole form as it differ so little from those libation of which so much be record in antient author and their modern transcriber
the principal difference lie in two instance for first the present company pour the liquor only down their throat and secondly the serjeant who officiate as priest drank the last but he preserve i believe the antient form in swallow much the large draught of the whole company and in be the only person present who contribute nothing towards the libation besides his good office in assist at the performance
the good people now range themselves round the kitchen fire where good humour seem to maintain an absolute dominion and partridge not only forget his shameful defeat but convert hunger into thirst and soon become extremely facetious
we must however quit this agreeable assembly for a while and attend mr jones to mrs waters s apartment where the dinner which he have bespoke be now on the table
indeed it take no long time in prepare have be all dr three day before and require nothing more from the cook than to warm it over again
chapter v
an apology for all hero who have good stomach with a description of a battle of the amorous kind
hero notwithstanding the high idea which by the mean of flatterer they may entertain of themselves or the world may conceive of they have certainly more of mortal than divine about they
however elevate their mind may be their body at least which be much the major part of most be liable to the bad infirmity and subject to the vilest office of human nature
among these latter the act of eating which hath by several wise man be consider as extremely mean and derogatory from the philosophic dignity must be in some measure perform by the great prince heroe or philosopher upon earth nay sometimes nature hath be so frolicsome as to exact of these dignified character a much more exorbitant share of this office than she hath oblige those of the low order to perform
to say the truth as no know inhabitant of this globe be really more than man so none need be ashamed of submit to what the necessity of man demand but when those great personage i have just mention condescend to aim at confine such low office to themselves--as when by hoarding or destroy they seem desirous to prevent any other from eat--then they surely become very low and despicable
now after this short preface we think it no disparagement to our heroe to mention the immoderate ardour with which he lay about he at this season
indeed it may be doubt whether ulysses who by the way seem to have have the good stomach of all the hero in that eat poem of the odyssey ever make a well meal
three pound at least of that flesh which formerly have contribute to the composition of an ox be now honour with become part of the individual mr jones
this particular we think ourselves oblige to mention as it may account for our heroe s temporary neglect of his fair companion who eat but very little and be indeed employ in consideration of a very different nature which pass unobserve by jones till he have entirely satisfied that appetite which a fast of twenty-four hour have procure he but his dinner be no soon end than his attention to other matter revive with these matter therefore we shall now proceed to acquaint the reader
mr jones of whose personal accomplishment we have hitherto say very little be in reality one of the handsome young fellow in the world
his face besides be the picture of health have in it the most apparent mark of sweetness and good-nature
these quality be indeed so characteristical in his countenance that while the spirit and sensibility in his eye though they must have be perceive by an accurate observer might have escape the notice of the less discerning so strongly be this good-nature paint in his look that it be remark by almost every one who see he
it be perhaps as much owe to this as to a very fine complexion that his face have a delicacy in it almost inexpressible and which might have give he an air rather too effeminate have it not be join to a most masculine person and mien which latter have as much in they of the hercules as the former have of the adonis
he be besides active genteel gay and good-humour and have a flow of animal spirit which enliven every conversation where he be present
when the reader hath duly reflect on these many charm which all center in our heroe and consider at the same time the fresh obligation which mrs waters have to he it will be a mark more of prudery than candour to entertain a bad opinion of she because she conceive a very good opinion of he
but whatever censure may be pass upon she it be my business to relate matter of fact with veracity
mrs waters have in truth not only a good opinion of our heroe but a very great affection for he
to speak out boldly at once she be in love accord to the present universally-receive sense of that phrase by which love be apply indiscriminately to the desirable object of all our passion appetite and sens and be understand to be that preference which we give to one kind of food rather than to another
but though the love to these several object may possibly be one and the same in all case its operation however must be allow to be different for how much soever we may be in love with an excellent surloin of beef or bottle of burgundy with a damask rise or cremona fiddle yet do we never smile nor ogle nor dress nor flatter nor endeavour by any other art or trick to gain the affection of the say beef c
sigh indeed we sometimes may but it be generally in the absence not in the presence of the beloved object
for otherwise we might possibly complain of their ingratitude and deafness with the same reason as pasiphae doth of her bull whom she endeavour to engage by all the coquetry practise with good success in the drawing-room on the much more sensible as well as tender heart of the fine gentleman there
the contrary happen in that love which operate between person of the same specie but of different sex
here we be no soon in love than it become our principal care to engage the affection of the object belove
for what other purpose indeed be our youth instruct in all the art of render themselves agreeable
if it be not with a view to this love i question whether any of those trade which deal in set off and adorn the human person would procure a livelihood
nay those great polisher of our manner who be by some thought to teach what principally distinguish we from the brute creation even dancing-masters themselves might possibly find no place in society
in short all the grace which young lady and young gentleman too learn from other and the many improvement which by the help of a look-glass they add of their own be in reality those very spicula et face amoris so often mention by ovid or as they be sometimes call in our own language the whole artillery of love
now mrs waters and our heroe have no soon sit down together than the former begin to play this artillery upon the latter
but here as we be about to attempt a description hitherto unassayed either in prose or verse we think proper to invoke the assistance of certain aërial being who will we doubt not come kindly to our aid on this occasion
say then ye graces
you that inhabit the heavenly mansion of seraphina s countenance for you be truly divine be always in her presence and well know all the art of charming say what be the weapon now use to captivate the heart of mr jones first from two lovely blue eye whose bright orb flash lightning at their discharge fly forth two pointed ogle but happily for our heroe hit only a vast piece of beef which he be then convey into his plate and harmless spend their force
the fair warrior perceive their miscarriage and immediately from her fair bosom draw forth a deadly sigh
a sigh which none could have hear unmove and which be sufficient at once to have sweep off a dozen beau so soft so sweet so tender that the insinuate air must have find its subtle way to the heart of our heroe have it not luckily be drive from his ear by the coarse bubble of some bottled ale which at that time he be pour forth
many other weapon do she assay but the god of eat if there be any such deity for i do not confidently assert it preserve his votary or perhaps it may not be dignu vindice nodus and the present security of jones may be account for by natural mean for as love frequently preserve from the attack of hunger so may hunger possibly in some case defend we against love
the fair one enrage at her frequent disappointment determine on a short cessation of arm
which interval she employ in make ready every engine of amorous warfare for the renewing of the attack when dinner should be over
no soon then be the cloth remove than she again begin her operation
first have plant her right eye sidewise against mr jones she shoot from its corner a most penetrate glance which though great part of its force be spend before it reach our heroe do not vent itself absolutely without effect
this the fair one perceiving hastily withdraw her eye and level they downwards as if she be concerned for what she have do though by this mean she design only to draw he from his guard and indeed to open his eye through which she intend to surprize his heart
and now gently lift up those two bright orb which have already begin to make an impression on poor jones she discharge a volley of small charm at once from her whole countenance in a smile
not a smile of mirth nor of joy but a smile of affection which most lady have always ready at their command and which serve they to show at once their good-humour their pretty dimple and their white tooth
this smile our heroe receive full in his eye and be immediately stagger with its force
he then begin to see the design of the enemy and indeed to feel their success
a parley now be set on foot between the party during which the artful fair so slily and imperceptibly carry on her attack that she have almost subdue the heart of our heroe before she again repair to act of hostility
to confess the truth i be afraid mr jones maintain a kind of dutch defence and treacherously deliver up the garrison without duly weigh his allegiance to the fair sophia
in short no soon have the amorous parley end and the lady have unmask the royal battery by carelessly let her handkerchief drop from her neck than the heart of mr jones be entirely take and the fair conqueror enjoy the usual fruit of her victory here the graces think proper to end their description and here we think proper to end the chapter
chapter vi
a friendly conversation in the kitchen which have a very common though not very friendly conclusion
while our lover be entertain themselves in the manner which be partly describe in the forego chapter they be likewise furnish out an entertainment for their good friend in the kitchen
and this in a double sense by afford they matter for their conversation and at the same time drink to enliven their spirit
there be now assemble round the kitchen fire besides my landlord and landlady who occasionally go backward and forward mr partridge the serjeant and the coachman who drive the young lady and she maid
partridge have acquaint the company with what he have learn from the man of the hill concern the situation in which mrs waters have be find by jones the serjeant proceed to that part of her history which be know to he
he say she be the wife of mr waters who be a captain in their regiment and have often be with he at quarter
some folk say he use indeed to doubt whether they be lawfully married in a church or no
but for my part thatbe no business of mine i must own if i be put to my corporal oath i believe she be little well than one of we and i fancy the captain may go to heaven when the sun shin upon a rainy day
but if he do that be neither here nor there for he willnot want company
and the lady to give the devil his due be a very good sort of lady and love the cloth and be always desirous to do strict justice to it for she hath beg off many a poor soldier and by her good-will would never have any of they punish
but yet to be sure ensign northerton and she be very well acquainted together at our last quarter that be the very right and truth of the matter
but the captain he know nothing about it and as long as there be enough for he too what do it signify
he love she not a bit the bad and i be certain would run any man through the body that be to abuse she therefore i willnot abuse she for my part
i only repeat what other folk say and to be certain what everybody say there must be some truth in ay ay a great deal of truth i warrant you cry partridge veritas odium parit all a parcel of scandalous stuff answer the mistress of the house
i be sure now she be drest she look like a very good sort of lady and she behave herself like one for she give i a guinea for the use of my cloath a very good lady indeed cry the landlord and if you have not be a little too hasty you would not have quarrel with she as you do at first you need mention that with my truly answer she if it have not be for your nonsense nothing have happen
you must be meddle with what do not belong to you and throw in your fool s discourse well well answer he whatbe past cannot be mend so therebe an end of the matter yes cry she for this once but will it be mend ever the more hereafter
this be not the first time i have suffer for your numscull s pate
i wish you would always hold your tongue in the house and meddle only in matter without door which concern you
donot you remember what happen about seven year ago nay my dear return he donot rip up old story
come come allbe well and i be sorry for what i have do the landlady be go to reply but be prevent by the peace-make serjeant sorely to the displeasure of partridge who be a great lover of what be call fun and a great promoter of those harmless quarrel which tend rather to the production of comical than tragical incident
the serjeant ask partridge whither he and his master be travel
none of your magister answer partridge i be no man s servant i assure you for though i have have misfortune in the world i write gentleman after my name and as poor and simple as i may appear now i have teach grammar-school in my time se hei mihi
non sum quod fui no offence i hope sir say the serjeant where then if i may venture to be so bold may you and your friend be travelling you have now denominate we right say partridge
amici sumus and i promise you my friend be one of the great gentleman in the kingdom at which word both landlord and landlady prick up their ear
he be the heir of squire allworthy what the squire who doth so much good all over the country cry my landlady
even he answer partridge then i warrant say she hewill have a swing great estate hereafter most certainly answer partridge well reply the landlady i think the first moment i see he he look like a good sort of gentleman but my husband here to be sure be wise than anybody i own my dear cry he it be a mistake a mistake indeed answer she but when do you ever know i to make such mistakes but how come it sir cry the landlord that such a great gentleman walk about the country afoot i donot know return partridge great gentleman have humour sometimes
he hath now a dozen horse and servant at gloucester and nothing would serve he but last night it be very hot weather he must cool himself with a walk to yon high hill whither i likewise walk with he to bear he company but if ever you catch i there again for i be never so frightened in all my life
we meet with the strange man there i ll be hang cry the landlord if it be not the man of the hill as they call he if indeed he be a man but i know several people who believe it be the devil that live there nay nay like enough say partridge and now you put i in the head of it i verily and sincerely believe it be the devil though i could not perceive his cloven foot but perhaps he might have the power give he to hide that since evil spirit can appear in what shape they please and pray sir say the serjeant no offence i hope but pray what sort of a gentleman be the devil
for i have hear some of our officer say there be no such person and that it be only a trick of the parson to prevent their being break for if it be publickly know that there be no devil the parson would be of no more use than we be in time of peace those officer say partridge be very great scholar i suppose not much of schollard neither answer the serjeant they have not half your learning sir i believe and to be sure i think there must be a devil notwithstanding what they say though one of they be a captain for methought think i to myself if there be no devil how can wicked people be send to he
