and i have read all that upon a book some of your officer quoth the landlord will find there be a devil to their shame i believe
i donot question but hewill pay off some old score upon my account
here be one quarter upon i half a year who have the conscience to take up one of my good bed though he hardly spend a shilling a day in the house and suffer his man to roast cabbage at the kitchen fire because i would not give they a dinner on a sunday
every good christian must desire there should be a devil for the punishment of such wretch harkee landlord say the serjeant donot abuse the cloth for i willnot take it d--n the cloth answer the landlord i have suffer enough by they bear witness gentleman say the serjeant he curse the king and thatbe high treason i curse the king
you villain say the landlord
yes you do cry the serjeant you curse the cloth and thatbe curse the king
itbe all one and the same for every man who curse the cloth would curse the king if he durst so for matter o that itbe all one and the same thing excuse i there mr serjeant quoth partridge thatbe a non sequitur none of your outlandish linguo answer the serjeant leap from his seat i will not sit still and hear the cloth abuse you mistake i friend cry partridge
i do not mean to abuse the cloth i only say your conclusion be a non sequitur you be another cry the serjeant an you come to that
no more a sequitur than yourself
you be a pack of rascal and iwill prove it for i will fight the good man of you all for twenty pound this challenge effectually silence partridge whose stomach for drubbing do not so soon return after the hearty meal which he have lately be treat with but the coachman whose bone be less sore and whose appetite for fighting be somewhat sharp do not so easily brook the affront of which he conceive some part at least fall to his share
he start therefore from his seat and advance to the serjeant swore he look on himself to be as good a man as any in the army and offer to box for a guinea
the military man accept the combat but refuse the wager upon which both immediately stript and engage till the driver of horse be so well maul by the leader of man that he be oblige to exhaust his small remainder of breath in beg for quarter
this word which the serjeant unhappily mistake for an affront be a term in logic and mean that the conclusion do not follow from the premise
the young lady be now desirous to depart and have give order for her coach to be prepare but all in vain for the coachman be disabled from perform his office for that evening
an antient heathen would perhaps have impute this disability to the god of drink no less than to the god of war for in reality both the combatant have sacrifice as well to the former deity as to the latter
to speak plainly they be both dead drunk nor be partridge in a much well situation
as for my landlord drinking be his trade and the liquor have no more effect on he than it have on any other vessel in his house
the mistress of the inn be summon to attend mr jones and his companion at their tea give a full relation of the latter part of the foregoing scene and at the same time express great concern for the young lady who she say be under the utmost uneasiness at be prevent from pursue her journey
she be a sweet pretty creature add she and i be certain i have see her face before
i fancy she be in love and run away from her friend
who know but some young gentleman or other may be expect she with a heart as heavy as her own jones fetch a heavy sigh at those word of which though mrs waters observe it she take no notice while the landlady continue in the room but after the departure of that good woman she could not forbear give our heroe certain hint on her suspect some very dangerous rival in his affection
the aukward behaviour of mr jones on this occasion convince she of the truth without his give she a direct answer to any of her question but she be not nice enough in her amour to be greatly concerned at the discovery
the beauty of jones highly charm her eye but as she could not see his heart she give herself no concern about it
she could feast heartily at the table of love without reflect that some other already have be or hereafter might be feast with the same repast
a sentiment which if it deal but little in refinement deal however much in substance and be less capricious and perhaps less ill-natured and selfish than the desire of those female who can be content enough to abstain from the possession of their lover provide they be sufficiently satisfied that no one else possess they
chapter vii
contain a full account of mrs waters and by what mean she come into that distressful situation from which she be rescue by jones
though nature hath by no mean mix up an equal share either of curiosity or vanity in every human composition there be perhaps no individual to whom she hath not allot such a proportion of both as require much art and pain too to subdue and keep under a conquest however absolutely necessary to every one who would in any degree deserve the character of wisdom or good breeding
as jones therefore might very justly be call a well-breed man he have stifle all that curiosity which the extraordinary manner in which he have find mrs waters must be suppose to have occasion
he have indeed at first throw out some few hint to the lady but when he perceive she industriously avoid any explanation he be content to remain in ignorance the rather as he be not without suspicion that there be some circumstance which must have raise her blush have she relate the whole truth
now since it be possible that some of our reader may not so easily acquiesce under the same ignorance and as we be very desirous to satisfy they all we have take uncommon pain to inform ourselves of the real fact with the relation of which we shall conclude this book
this lady then have live some year with one captain waters who be a captain in the same regiment to which mr northerton belong
she past for that gentleman s wife and go by his name and yet as the serjeant say there be some doubt concern the reality of their marriage which we shall not at present take upon we to resolve
mrs waters i be sorry to say it have for some time contract an intimacy with the above-mention ensign which do no great credit to her reputation
that she have a remarkable fondness for that young fellow be most certain but whether she indulge this to any very criminal length be not so extremely clear unless we will suppose that woman never grant every favour to a man but one without grant he that one also
the division of the regiment to which captain waters belong have two day precede the march of that company to which mr northerton be the ensign so that the former have reach worcester the very day after the unfortunate re-encounter between jones and northerton which we have before record
now it have be agree between mrs waters and the captain that she would accompany he in his march as far as worcester where they be to take their leave of each other and she be thence to return to bath where she be to stay till the end of the winter s campaign against the rebel
with this agreement mr northerton be make acquainted
to say the truth the lady have make he an assignation at this very place and promise to stay at worcester till his division come thither with what view and for what purpose must be leave to the reader s divination for though we be oblige to relate fact we be not oblige to do a violence to our nature by any comment to the disadvantage of the lovely part of the creation
northerton no soon obtain a release from his captivity as we have see than he haste away to overtake mrs waters which as he be a very active nimble fellow he do at the last-mention city some few hour after captain waters have leave she
at his first arrival he make no scruple of acquaint she with the unfortunate accident which he make appear very unfortunate indeed for he totally extract every particle of what could be call fault at least in a court of honour though he leave some circumstance which might be questionable in a court of law
woman to their glory be it speak be more generally capable of that violent and apparently disintereste passion of love which seek only the good of its object than man
mrs waters therefore be no soon apprize of the danger to which her lover be expose than she lose every consideration besides that of his safety and this be a matter equally agreeable to the gentleman it become the immediate subject of debate between they
after much consultation on this matter it be at length agree that the ensign should go across the country to hereford whence he might find some conveyance to one of the sea-port in wales and thence might make his escape abroad
in all which expedition mrs waters declare she would bear he company and for which she be able to furnish he with money a very material article to mr northerton she have then in her pocket three bank-note to the amount of 90 besides some cash and a diamond ring of pretty considerable value on her finger
all which she with the utmost confidence reveal to this wicked man little suspecting she should by these mean inspire he with a design of rob she
now as they must by take horse from worcester have furnish any pursuer with the mean of hereafter discover their route the ensign propose and the lady presently agree to make their first stage on foot for which purpose the hardness of the frost be very seasonable
the main part of the lady s baggage be already at bath and she have nothing with she at present besides a very small quantity of linen which the gallant undertake to carry in his own pocket
all thing therefore be settle in the evening they arise early the next morning and at five o clock depart from worcester it be then above two hour before day but the moon which be then at the full give they all the light she be capable of affording
mrs waters be not of that delicate race of woman who be oblige to the invention of vehicle for the capacity of remove themselves from one place to another and with whom consequently a coach be reckon among the necessary of life
her limb be indeed full of strength and agility and as her mind be no less animate with spirit she be perfectly able to keep pace with her nimble lover
have travel on for some mile in a high road which northerton say he be inform lead to hereford they come at the break of day to the side of a large wood where he suddenly stop and affect to meditate a moment with himself express some apprehension from travel any long in so public a way
upon which he easily persuade his fair companion to strike with he into a path which seem to lead directly through the wood and which at length bring they both to the bottom of mazard hill
whether the execrable scheme which he now attempt to execute be the effect of previous deliberation or whether it now first come into his head i cannot determine
but be arrive in this lonely place where it be very improbable he should meet with any interruption he suddenly slip his garter from his leg and lay violent hand on the poor woman endeavour to perpetrate that dreadful and detestable fact which we have before commemorate and which the providential appearance of jones do so fortunately prevent
happy be it for mrs waters that she be not of the weak order of female for no soon do she perceive by his tie a knot in his garter and by his declaration what his hellish intention be than she stand stoutly to her defence and so strongly struggle with her enemy scream all the while for assistance that she delay the execution of the villain s purpose several minute by which mean mr jones come to her relief at that very instant when her strength fail and she be totally overpower and deliver she from the ruffian s hand with no other loss than that of her cloath which be tear from her back and of the diamond ring which during the contention either drop from her finger or be wrench from it by northerton
thus reader we have give thee the fruit of a very painful enquiry which for thy satisfaction we have make into this matter
and here we have open to thee a scene of folly as well as villany which we could scarce have believe a human creature capable of be guilty of have we not remember that this fellow be at that time firmly persuade that he have already commit a murder and have forfeit his life to the law
as he conclude therefore that his only safety lie in flight he think the possessing himself of this poor woman s money and ring would make he amend for the additional burthen he be to lay on his conscience
and here reader we must strictly caution thee that thou dost not take any occasion from the misbehaviour of such a wretch as this to reflect on so worthy and honourable a body of man as be the officer of our army in general
thou wilt be pleased to consider that this fellow as we have already inform thee have neither the birth nor education of a gentleman nor be a proper person to be enrol among the number of such
if therefore his baseness can justly reflect on any besides himself it must be only on those who give he his commission
book x
in which the history go forward about twelve hour
chapter i
contain instruction very necessary to be peruse by modern critic
reader it be impossible we should know what sort of person thou wilt be for perhaps thou may st be as learn in human nature as shakespear himself be and perhaps thou may st be no wise than some of his editor
now lest this latter should be the case we think proper before we go any far together to give thee a few wholesome admonition that thou may st not as grossly misunderstand and misrepresent we as some of the said editor have misunderstood and misrepresent their author
first then we warn thee not too hastily to condemn any of the incident in this our history as impertinent and foreign to our main design because thou dost not immediately conceive in what manner such incident may conduce to that design
this work may indeed be consider as a great creation of our own and for a little reptile of a critic to presume to find fault with any of its part without know the manner in which the whole be connect and before he come to the final catastrophe be a most presumptuous absurdity
the allusion and metaphor we have here make use of we must acknowledge to be infinitely too great for our occasion but there be indeed no other which be at all adequate to express the difference between an author of the first rate and a critic of the low
another caution we would give thee my good reptile be that thou dost not find out too near a resemblance between certain character here introduce as for instance between the landlady who appear in the seventh book and she in the ninth
thou art to know friend that there be certain characteristic in which most individual of every profession and occupation agree
to be able to preserve these characteristic and at the same time to diversify their operation be one talent of a good writer
again to mark the nice distinction between two person actuate by the same vice or folly be another and as this last talent be find in very few writer so be the true discernment of it find in as few reader though i believe the observation of this form a very principal pleasure in those who be capable of the discovery every person for instance can distinguish between sir epicure mammon and sir fopling flutter but to note the difference between sir fopling flutter and sir courtly nice require a more exquisite judgment for want of which vulgar spectator of play very often do great injustice in the theatre where i have sometimes know a poet in danger of be convict as a thief upon much bad evidence than the resemblance of hand hath be hold to be in the law
in reality i apprehend every amorous widow on the stage would run the hazard of be condemn as a servile imitation of dido but that happily very few of our play-house critic understand enough of latin to read virgil
in the next place we must admonish thee my worthy friend for perhaps thy heart may be well than thy head not to condemn a character as a bad one because it be not perfectly a good one
if thou dost delight in these model of perfection there be book enow write to gratify thy taste but as we have not in the course of our conversation ever happen to meet with any such person we have not choose to introduce any such here
to say the truth i a little question whether mere man ever arrive at this consummate degree of excellence as well as whether there hath ever exist a monster bad enough to verify that nulla virtute redemptum a vitiis whose vice be not allay with a single virtue in juvenal nor do i indeed conceive the good purpose serve by insert character of such angelic perfection or such diabolical depravity in any work of invention since from contemplate either the mind of man be more likely to be overwhelm with sorrow and shame than to draw any good us from such pattern for in the former instance he may be both concerned and ashamed to see a pattern of excellence in his nature which he may reasonably despair of ever arrive at and in contemplate the latter he may be no less affect with those uneasy sensation at see the nature of which he be a partaker degrade into so odious and detestable a creature
in fact if there be enough of goodness in a character to engage the admiration and affection of a well-dispose mind though there should appear some of those little blemish quas humana parum cavit natura they will raise our compassion rather than our abhorrence
indeed nothing can be of more moral use than the imperfection which be see in example of this kind since such form a kind of surprize more apt to affect and dwell upon our mind than the fault of very vicious and wicked person
the foible and vice of man in whom there be great mixture of good become more glaring object from the virtue which contrast they and shew their deformity and when we find such vice attend with their evil consequence to our favourite character we be not only teach to shun they for our own sake but to hate they for the mischief they have already bring on those we love
and now my friend have give you these few admonition we will if you please once more set forward with our history
chapter ii
contain the arrival of an irish gentleman with very extraordinary adventure which ensue at the inn
now the little trembling hare which the dread of all her numerous enemy and chiefly of that cunning cruel carnivorous animal man have confine all the day to her lurking-place sport wantonly o er the lawn now on some hollow tree the owl shrill chorister of the night hoot forth note which might charm the ear of some modern connoisseur in music now in the imagination of the half-drunk clown as he stagger through the churchyard or rather charnelyard to his home fear paint the bloody hobgoblin now thief and ruffian be awake and honest watchman fast asleep in plain english it be now midnight and the company at the inn as well those who have be already mention in this history as some other who arrive in the evening be all in bed
only susan chambermaid be now stir she be oblige to wash the kitchen before she retire to the arm of the fond expect hostler
in this posture be affair at the inn when a gentleman arrive there post
he immediately alight from his horse and come up to susan enquire of she in a very abrupt and confused manner be almost out of breath with eagerness whether there be any lady in the house
the hour of night and the behaviour of the man who star very wildly all the time a little surprized susan so that she hesitate before she make any answer upon which the gentleman with redouble eagerness beg she to give he a true information say he have lose his wife and be come in pursuit of she
upon my shoul cry he i have be near catch she already in two or three place if i have not find she go just as i come up with she
if she be in the house do carry i up in the dark and show she to i and if she be go away before i do tell i which way i shall go after she to meet she and upon my shoul i will make you the rich poor woman in the nation he then pull out a handful of guineas a sight which would have bribe person of much great consequence than this poor wench to much bad purpose
susan from the account she have receive of mrs waters make not the least doubt but that she be the very identical stray whom the right owner pursue
as she conclude therefore with great appearance of reason that she never could get money in an honester way than by restore a wife to her husband she make no scruple of assure the gentleman that the lady he want be then in the house and be presently afterwards prevail upon by very liberal promise and some earnest pay into her hand to conduct he to the bedchamber of mrs waters
it hath be a custom long establish in the polite world and that upon very solid and substantial reason that a husband shall never enter his wife s apartment without first knock at the door
the many excellent us of this custom need scarce be hint to a reader who hath any knowledge of the world for by this mean the lady hath time to adjust herself or to remove any disagreeable object out of the way for there be some situation in which nice and delicate woman would not be discover by their husband
to say the truth there be several ceremony institute among the polished part of mankind which though they may to coarser judgment appear as matter of mere form be find to have much of substance in they by the more discerning and lucky would it have be have the custom above mention be observe by our gentleman in the present instance
knock indeed he do at the door but not with one of those gentle rap which be usual on such occasion
on the contrary when he find the door lock he fly at it with such violence that the lock immediately give way the door burst open and he fall headlong into the room
he have no soon recover his leg than forth from the bed upon his leg likewise appear--with shame and sorrow be we oblige to proceed--our heroe himself who with a menacing voice demand of the gentleman who he be and what he mean by dare to burst open his chamber in that outrageous manner
the gentleman at first thought he have commit a mistake and be go to ask pardon and retreat when on a sudden as the moon shine very bright he cast his eye on stay gown petticoat cap ribbon stocking garter shoe clog c all which lie in a disorder manner on the floor
all these operate on the natural jealousy of his temper so enrage he that he lose all power of speech and without return any answer to jones he endeavour to approach the bed
jones immediately interpose a fierce contention arise which soon proceed to blow on both side
and now mrs waters for we must confess she be in the same bed be i suppose awaken from her sleep and see two man fight in her bedchamber begin to scream in the most violent manner cry out murder
robbery
and more frequently rape
which last some perhaps may wonder she should mention who do not consider that these word of exclamation be use by lady in a fright as fa la la ra da c be in music only as the vehicle of sound and without any fix idea
next to the lady s chamber be deposit the body of an irish gentleman who arrive too late at the inn to have be mention before
this gentleman be one of those whom the irish call a calabalaro or cavalier
he be a young brother of a good family and have no fortune at home be oblige to look abroad in order to get one for which purpose he be proceed to the bath to try his luck with card and the woman
this young fellow lie in bed read one of mrs behn s novel for he have be instruct by a friend that he would find no more effectual method of recommend himself to the lady than the improve his understanding and fill his mind with good literature
he no soon therefore hear the violent uproar in the next room than he leap from his bolster and take his sword in one hand and the candle which burn by he in the other he go directly to mrs waters s chamber
if the sight of another man in his shirt at first add some shock to the decency of the lady it make she presently amend by considerably abate her fear for no soon have the calabalaro enter the room than he cry out mr fitzpatrick what the devil be the maning of this upon which the other immediately answer o mr maclachlan
i be rejoice you be here this villain hath debauch my wife and be get into bed with she what wife cry maclachlan do not i know mrs fitzpatrick very well and donot i see that the lady whom the gentleman who stand here in his shirt be lie in bed with be none of she fitzpatrick now perceive as well by the glimpse he have of the lady as by her voice which might have be distinguish at a great distance than he now stand from she that he have make a very unfortunate mistake begin to ask many pardon of the lady and then turn to jones he say i would have you take notice i do not ask your pardon for you have bate i for which i be resolve to have your blood in the morning jones treat this menace with much contempt and mr maclachlan answer indeed mr fitzpatrick you may be ashamed of your own self to disturb people at this time of night if all the people in the inn be not asleep you would have awaken they as you have i
the gentleman have serve you very rightly
upon my conscience though i have no wife if you have treat she so i would have cut your throat jones be so confound with his fear for his lady s reputation that he know neither what to say or do but the invention of woman be as hath be observe much ready than that of man
she recollect that there be a communication between her chamber and that of mr jones rely therefore on his honour and her own assurance she answer i know not what you mean villain
i be wife to none of you

rape and now the landlady come into the room mrs waters fall upon she with the utmost virulence say she think herself in a sober inn and not in a bawdy-house but that a set of villain have break into her room with an intent upon she honour if not upon her life and both she say be equally dear to she the landlady now begin to roar as loudly as the poor woman in bed have do before
she cry she be undo and that the reputation of her house which be never blow upon before be utterly destroy then turn to the man she cry what in the devil s name be the reason of all this disturbance in the lady s room fitzpatrick hang down his head repeat that he have commit a mistake for which he heartily ask pardon and then retire with his countryman
jones who be too ingenious to have miss the hint give he by his fair one boldly assert that he have run to her assistance upon hear the door break open with what design he could not conceive unless of rob the lady which if they intend he say he have the good fortune to prevent i never have a robbery commit in my house since i have keep it cry the landlady i would have you to know sir i harbour no highwayman here i scorn the word thof i say it
none but honest good gentlefolk be welcome to my house and i thank good luck i have always have enow of such customer indeed as many as i could entertain
here hath be my lord-- and then she repeat over a catalogue of name and title many of which we might perhaps be guilty of a breach of privilege by insert
jones after much patience at length interrupt she by make an apology to mrs waters for have appear before she in his shirt assure she that nothing but a concern for her safety could have prevail on he to do it the reader may inform himself of her answer and indeed of her whole behaviour to the end of the scene by consider the situation which she affect it be that of a modest lady who be awaken out of her sleep by three strange man in her chamber
this be the part which she undertake to perform and indeed she execute it so well that none of our theatrical actress could exceed she in any of their performance either on or off the stage
and hence i think we may very fairly draw an argument to prove how extremely natural virtue be to the fair sex for though there be not perhaps one in ten thousand who be capable of make a good actress and even among these we rarely see two who be equally able to personate the same character yet this of virtue they can all admirably well put on and as well those individual who have it not as those who possess it can all act it to the utmost degree of perfection
when the man be all depart mrs waters recover from her fear recover likewise from her anger and speak in much gentler accent to the landlady who do not so readily quit her concern for the reputation of the house in favour of which she begin again to number the many great person who have sleep under her roof but the lady stopt her short and have absolutely acquit she of have have any share in the past disturbance beg to be leave to her repose which she say she hop to enjoy unmoleste during the remainder of the night
upon which the landlady after much civility and many courtsie take she leave
chapter iii
a dialogue between the landlady and susan the chamber-maid proper to be read by all inn-keeper and their servant with the arrival and affable behaviour of a beautiful young lady which may teach person of condition how they may acquire the love of the whole world
the landlady remember that susan have be the only person out of bed when the door be burst open resort presently to she to enquire into the first occasion of the disturbance as well as who the strange gentleman be and when and how he arrive
susan relate the whole story which the reader know already vary the truth only in some circumstance as she see convenient and totally conceal the money which she have receive
but whereas her mistress have in the preface to her enquiry speak much in compassion for the fright which the lady have be in concern any intended depredation on her virtue susan could not help endeavour to quiet the concern which her mistress seem to be under on that account by swear heartily she see jones leap out from her bed
the landlady fall into a violent rage at these word
a likely story truly cry she that a woman should cry out and endeavour to expose herself if that be the case
i desire to know what well proof any lady can give of her virtue than she cry out which i believe twenty people can witness for she she do
i beg madam you would spread no such scandal of any of my guest for it will not only reflect on they but upon the house and i be sure no vagabond nor wicked beggarly people come here well say susan then i must not believe my own eye no indeed must you not always answer her mistress i would not have believe my own eye against such good gentlefolk
i have not have a well supper order this half-year than they order last night and so easy and good-humour be they that they find no fault with my worcestershire perry which i sell they for champagne and to be sure it be as well taste and as wholesome as the good champagne in the kingdom otherwise i would scorn to give it them and they drink i two bottle
no no i will never believe any harm of such sober good sort of people susan be thus silence her mistress proceed to other matter
and so you tell i continue she that the strange gentleman come post and there be a footman without with the horse why then he be certainly some of your great gentlefolk too
why do not you ask he whether hewould have any supper
i think he be in the other gentleman s room go up and ask whether he call
perhaps hewill order something when he find anybody stir in the house to dress it
now donot commit any of your usual blunder by tell he the fire s out and the fowl alive
and if he should order mutton donot blab out that we have none
the butcher i know kill a sheep just before i go to bed and he never refuse to cut it up warm when i desire it
go remember therebe all sort of mutton and fowl go open the door with gentlemen d ye call

donot forget his honour
go if you donot mind all these matter well youwill never come to anything susan depart and soon return with an account that the two gentleman be get both into the same bed
two gentleman say the landlady in the same bed
thatbe impossible they be two arrant scrub i warrant they and i believe young squire allworthy guess right that the fellow intend to rob her ladyship for if he have break open the lady s door with any of the wicked design of a gentleman he would never have sneak away to another room to save the expense of a supper and a bed to himself
they be certainly thief and their search after a wife be nothing but a pretence in these censure my landlady do mr fitzpatrick great injustice for he be really bear a gentleman though not worth a groat and though perhaps he have some few blemish in his heart as well as in his head yet be a sneaking or a niggardly fellow be not one of they
in reality he be so generous a man that whereas he have receive a very handsome fortune with his wife he have now spend every penny of it except some little pittance which be settle upon she and in order to possess himself of this he have use she with such cruelty that together with his jealousy which be of the bitter kind it have force the poor woman to run away from he
this gentleman then be well tired with his long journey from chester in one day with which and some good dry blow he have receive in the scuffle his bone be so sore that add to the soreness of his mind it have quite deprive he of any appetite for eating
and be now so violently disappointed in the woman whom at the maid s instance he have mistake for his wife it never once enter into his head that she might nevertheless be in the house though he have err in the first person he have attack
he therefore yield to the dissuasion of his friend from search any far after she that night and accept the kind offer of part of his bed
the footman and post-boy be in a different disposition
they be more ready to order than the landlady be to provide however after be pretty well satisfied by they of the real truth of the case and that mr fitzpatrick be no thief she be at length prevail on to set some cold meat before they which they be devour with great greediness when partridge come into the kitchen
he have be first awake by the hurry which we have before see and while he be endeavour to compose himself again on his pillow a screech-owl have give he such a serenade at his window that he leap in a most horrible affright from his bed and huddle on his cloath with great expedition run down to the protection of the company whom he hear talk below in the kitchen
his arrival detain my landlady from return to her rest for she be just about to leave the other two guest to the care of susan but the friend of young squire allworthy be not to be so neglect especially as he call for a pint of wine to be mull
she immediately obey by put the same quantity of perry to the fire for this readily answer to the name of every kind of wine
the irish footman be retire to bed and the post-boy be go to follow but partridge invite he to stay and partake of his wine which the lad very thankfully accept
the schoolmaster be indeed afraid to return to bed by himself and as he do not know how soon he might lose the company of my landlady he be resolve to secure that of the boy in whose presence he apprehend no danger from the devil or any of his adherent
and now arrive another post-boy at the gate upon which susan be order out return introduce two young woman in rid habit one of which be so very richly laced that partridge and the post-boy instantly start from their chair and my landlady fall to her courtsie and her ladyship with great eagerness
the lady in the rich habit say with a smile of great condescension if you will give i leave madam i will warm myself a few minute at your kitchen fire for it be really very cold but i must insist on disturb no one from his seat this be speak on account of partridge who have retreat to the other end of the room strike with the utmost awe and astonishment at the splendor of the lady s dress
indeed she have a much well title to respect than this for she be one of the most beautiful creature in the world
the lady earnestly desire partridge to return to his seat but could not prevail

chapter v
show who the amiable lady and her unamiable maid be
as in the month of june the damask rise which chance hath plant among the lily with their candid hue mix his vermilion or as some playsome heifer in the pleasant month of may diffuse she odoriferous breath over the flowery meadow or as in the blooming month of april the gentle constant dove perch on some fair bough sit meditate on her mate so look a hundred charm and breathing as many sweet her thought be fix on her tommy with a heart as good and innocent as her face be beautiful sophia for it be she herself lie recline her lovely head on her hand when she maid enter the room and run directly to the bed cry madam--madam--who doth your ladyship think be in the house sophia start up cry i hope my father hath not overtake we no madam it be one worth a hundred father mr jones himself be here at this very instant mr jones say sophia it be impossible

never a barrel the well herring cry he noscitur a socio be a true saying
it must be confess indeed that the lady in the fine garment be the civiller of the two but i warrant neither of they be a bit well than they should be
a couple of bath trull iwill answer for they your quality donot ride about at this time o night without servant sbodlikin and thatbe true cry the landlady you have certainly hit upon the very matter for quality donot come into a house without bespeak a supper whether they eat or no while they be thus discourse mrs honour return and discharge her commission by bid the landlady immediately wake mr jones and tell he a lady want to speak with he
the landlady refer she to partridge say he be the squire s friend but for her part she never call man-folk especially gentleman and then walk sullenly out of the kitchen
honour apply herself to partridge but he refuse for my friend cry he go to bed very late and he would be very angry to be disturb so soon mrs honour insist still to have he call say she be sure instead of be angry that he would be to the high degree delight when he know the occasion another time perhaps he might cry partridge but non omnia possumus omne
one woman be enough at once for a reasonable man what do you mean by one woman fellow cry honour
none of your fellow answer partridge
he then proceed to inform she plainly that jones be in bed with a wench and make use of an expression too indelicate to be here insert which so enrage mrs honour that she call he jackanape and return in a violent hurry to her mistress whom she acquaint with the success of her errand and with the account she have receive which if possible she exaggerate be as angry with jones as if he have pronounce all the word that come from the mouth of partridge
she discharge a torrent of abuse on the master and advise her mistress to quit all thought of a man who have never show himself deserve of she
she then rip up the story of molly seagrim and give the most malicious turn to his formerly quit sophia herself which i must confess the present incident not a little countenance
the spirit of sophia be too much dissipate by concern to enable she to stop the torrent of she maid
at last however she interrupt she say i never can believe this some villain hath belie he
you say you have it from his friend but surely it be not the office of a friend to betray such secret i suppose cry honour the fellow be his pimp for i never see so ill-look a villain
besides such profligate rake as mr jones be never ashamed of these matter to say the truth this behaviour of partridge be a little inexcusable but he have not sleep off the effect of the dose which he swallow the evening before which have in the morning receive the addition of above a pint of wine or indeed rather of malt spirit for the perry be by no mean pure
now that part of his head which nature design for the reservoir of drink be very shallow a small quantity of liquor overflow it and open the sluice of his heart so that all the secret there deposit run out
these sluice be indeed naturally very ill-secure
to give the well-natured turn we can to his disposition he be a very honest man for as he be the most inquisitive of mortal and eternally pry into the secret of other so he very faithfully pay they by communicate in return everything within his knowledge
while sophia torment with anxiety know not what to believe nor what resolution to take susan arrive with the sack-whey
mrs honour immediately advise her mistress in a whisper to pump this wench who probably could inform she of the truth
sophia approve it and begin as follow come hither child now answer i truly what i be go to ask you and i promise you i will very well reward you
be there a young gentleman in this house a handsome young gentleman that---- here sophia blush and be confound
a young gentleman cry honour that come hither in company with that saucy rascal who be now in the kitchen susan answer there be do you know anything of any lady continue sophia any lady
i donot ask you whether she be handsome or no perhaps she be not thatbe nothing to the purpose but do you know of any lady la madam cry honour you will make a very bad examiner
hark ee child say she be not that very young gentleman now in bed with some nasty trull or other here susan smile and be silent
answer the question child say sophia and herebe a guinea for you a guinea
madam cry susan la whatbe a guinea
if my mistress should know it i shall certainly lose my place that very instant herebe another for you say sophia and i promise you faithfully your mistress shall never know it susan after a very short hesitation take the money and tell the whole story conclude with say if you have any great curiosity madam i can steal softly into his room and see whether he be in his own bed or no she accordingly do this by sophia s desire and return with an answer in the negative
sophia now tremble and turn pale
mrs honour beg she to be comfort and not to think any more of so worthless a fellow
why there say susan i hope madam your ladyship willnot be offend but pray madam be not your ladyship s name madam sophia western how be it possible you should know i answer sophia
why that man that the gentlewoman speak of who be in the kitchen tell about you last night
but i hope your ladyship be not angry with i indeed child say she i be not pray tell i all and i promise you iwill reward you why madam continue susan that man tell we all in the kitchen that madam sophia western--indeed i donot know how to bring it out here she stopt till have receive encouragement from sophia and be vehemently press by mrs honour she proceed thus he tell we madam though to be sure it be all a lie that your ladyship be die for love of the young squire and that he be go to the war to get rid of you
i think to myself then he be a false-hearted wretch but now to see such a fine rich beautiful lady as you be forsake for such an ordinary woman for to be sure so she be and another man s wife into the bargain
it be such a strange unnatural thing in a manner sophia give she a third guinea and tell she she would certainly be her friend if she mention nothing of what have pass nor inform any one who she be dismiss the girl with order to the post-boy to get the horse ready immediately
be now leave alone with she maid she tell her trusty waiting-woman that she never be more easy than at present
i be now convince say she he be not only a villain but a low despicable wretch
i can forgive all rather than his expose my name in so barbarous a manner
that render he the object of my contempt
yes honour i be now easy i be indeed i be very easy and then she burst into a violent flood of tear
after a short interval spend by sophia chiefly in cry and assure she maid that she be perfectly easy susan arrive with an account that the horse be ready when a very extraordinary thought suggest itself to our young heroine by which mr jones would be acquaint with she have be at the inn in a way which if any spark of affection for she remain in he would be at least some punishment for his fault
the reader will be pleased to remember a little muff which hath have the honour of be more than once remember already in this history
this muff ever since the departure of mr jones have be the constant companion of sophia by day and her bedfellow by night and this muff she have at this very instant upon her arm whence she take it off with great indignation and have writ her name with her pencil upon a piece of paper which she pin to it she bribe the maid to convey it into the empty bed of mr jones in which if he do not find it she charge she to take some method of convey it before his eye in the morning
then have pay for what mrs honour have eat in which bill be include an account for what she herself might have eat she mount her horse and once more assure her companion that she be perfectly easy continue her journey
chapter vi
containing among other thing the ingenuity of partridge the madness of jones and the folly of fitzpatrick
it be now past five in the morning and other company begin to rise and come to the kitchen among whom be the serjeant and the coachman who be thoroughly reconcile make a libation or in the english phrase drink a hearty cup together
in this drinking nothing more remarkable happen than the behaviour of partridge who when the serjeant drink a health to king george repeat only the word king nor could he be bring to utter more for though he be go to fight against his own cause yet he could not be prevail upon to drink against it
mr jones be now return to his own bed but from whence he return we must beg to be excuse from relate summon partridge from this agreeable company who after a ceremonious preface have obtain leave to offer his advice deliver himself as follows it be sir an old saying and a true one that a wise man may sometimes learn counsel from a fool i wish therefore i might be so bold as to offer you my advice which be to return home again and leave these horrida bella these bloody war to fellow who be content to swallow gunpowder because they have nothing else to eat
now everybody know your honour want for nothing at home when thatbe the case why should any man travel abroad partridge cry jones thou art certainly a coward i wish therefore thou wouldst return home thyself and trouble i no more i ask your honour s pardon cry partridge i speak on your account more than my own for as to i heaven know my circumstance be bad enough and i be so far from be afraid that i value a pistol or a blunderbuss or any such thing no more than a pop-gun
every man must die once and what signify the manner how
besides perhaps i may come off with the loss only of an arm or a leg
i assure you sir i be never less afraid in my life and so if your honour be resolve to go on i be resolve to follow you
but in that case i wish i might give my opinion
to be sure it be a scandalous way of travel for a great gentleman like you to walk afoot
now here be two or three good horse in the stable which the landlord will certainly make no scruple of trust you with but if he should i can easily contrive to take they and let the bad come to the bad the king would certainly pardon you as you be go to fight in his cause now as the honesty of partridge be equal to his understanding and both deal only in small matter he would never have attempt a roguery of this kind have he not imagine it altogether safe for he be one of those who have more consideration of the gallow than of the fitness of thing but in reality he think he might have commit this felony without any danger for besides that he doubt not but the name of mr allworthy would sufficiently quiet the landlord he conceive they should be altogether safe whatever turn affair might take as jones he imagine would have friend enough on one side and as his friend would as well secure he on the other
when mr jones find that partridge be in earnest in this proposal he very severely rebuke he and that in such bitter term that the other attempt to laugh it off and presently turn the discourse to other matter say he believe they be then in a bawdy house and that he have with much ado prevent two wench from disturb his honour in the middle of the night
heyday say he i believe they get into your chamber whether i would or no for here lie the muff of one of they on the ground indeed as jones return to his bed in the dark he have never perceive the muff on the quilt and in leap into his bed he have tumble it on the floor
this partridge now take up and be go to put into his pocket when jones desire to see it
the muff be so very remarkable that our heroe might possibly have recollect it without the information annex
but his memory be not put to that hard office for at the same instant he see and read the word sophia western upon the paper which be pin to it
his look now grow frantic in a moment and he eagerly cry out oh heavens
how come this muff here i know no more than your honour cry partridge but i see it upon the arm of one of the woman who would have disturb you if i would have suffer they where be they cry jones jump out of bed and lay hold of his cloath
many mile off i believe by this time say partridge
and now jones upon further enquiry be sufficiently assure that the bearer of this muff be no other than the lovely sophia herself
the behaviour of jones on this occasion his thought his look his word his action be such as beggar all description
after many bitter execration on partridge and not few on himself he order the poor fellow who be frightened out of his wit to run down and hire he horse at any rate and a very few minute afterwards have shuffle on his clothe he hasten down-stair to execute the order himself which he have just before give
but before we proceed to what pass on his arrival in the kitchen it will be necessary to recur to what have there happen since partridge have first leave it on his master s summon
the serjeant be just march off with his party when the two irish gentleman arise and come downstairs both complain that they have be so often wake by the noise in the inn that they have never once be able to close their eye all night
the coach which have bring the young lady and her maid and which perhaps the reader may have hitherto conclude be her own be indeed a return coach belong to mr king of bath one of the worthy and honest man that ever deal in horse-flesh and whose coach we heartily recommend to all our reader who travel that road
by which mean they may perhaps have the pleasure of rid in the very coach and be drive by the very coachman that be record in this history
the coachman have but two passenger and hear mr maclachlan be go to bath offer to carry he thither at a very moderate price
he be induce to this by the report of the hostler who say that the horse which mr maclachlan have hire from worcester would be much more pleased with return to his friend there than to prosecute a long journey for that the say horse be rather a two-legged than a four-legged animal
mr maclachlan immediately close with the proposal of the coachman and at the same time persuade his friend fitzpatrick to accept of the fourth place in the coach
this conveyance the soreness of his bone make more agreeable to he than a horse and be well assure of meet with his wife at bath he think a little delay would be of no consequence
maclachlan who be much the sharp man of the two no soon hear that this lady come from chester with the other circumstance which he learn from the hostler than it come into his head that she might possibly be his friend s wife and presently acquaint he with this suspicion which have never once occur to fitzpatrick himself
to say the truth he be one of those composition which nature make up in too great a hurry and forget to put any brain into their head
now it happen to this sort of man as to bad hound who never hit off a fault themselves but no soon doth a dog of sagacity open his mouth than they immediately do the same and without the guidance of any scent run directly forwards as fast as they be able
in the same manner the very moment mr maclachlan have mention his apprehension mr fitzpatrick instantly concur and fly directly up-stair to surprize his wife before he know where she be and unluckily as fortune love to play trick with those gentleman who put themselves entirely under her conduct run his head against several door and post to no purpose
much kinder be she to i when she suggest that simile of the hound just before insert since the poor wife may on these occasion be so justly compare to a hunted hare
like that little wretched animal she prick up her ear to listen after the voice of her pursuer like she fly away tremble when she hear it and like she be generally overtake and destroy in the end
this be not however the case at present for after a long fruitless search mr fitzpatrick return to the kitchen where as if this have be a real chace enter a gentleman hallow as hunter do when the hound be at a fault
he be just alight from his horse and have many attendant at his heel
here reader it may be necessary to acquaint thee with some matter which if thou dost know already thou art wise than i take thee to be
and this information thou shalt receive in the next chapter
chapter vii
in which be conclude the adventure that happen at the inn at upton
in the first place then this gentleman just arrive be no other person than squire western himself who be come hither in pursuit of his daughter and have he fortunately be two hour early he have not only find she but his niece into the bargain for such be the wife of mr fitzpatrick who have run away with she five year before out of the custody of that sage lady madam western
now this lady have depart from the inn much about the same time with sophia for have be wake by the voice of her husband she have send up for the landlady and be by she apprized of the matter have bribe the good woman at an extravagant price to furnish she with horse for her escape
such prevalence have money in this family and though the mistress would have turn away her maid for a corrupt hussy if she have know as much as the reader yet she be no more proof against corruption herself than poor susan have be
mr western and his nephew be not know to one another nor indeed would the former have take any notice of the latter if he have know he for this be a steal match and consequently an unnatural one in the opinion of the good squire he have from the time of she commit it abandon the poor young creature who be then no more than eighteen as a monster and have never since suffer she to be name in his presence
the kitchen be now a scene of universal confusion western enquiring after his daughter and fitzpatrick as eagerly after his wife when jones enter the room unfortunately have sophia s muff in his hand
as soon as western see jones he set up the same holla as be use by sportsman when their game be in view
he then immediately run up and lay hold of jones cry we have get the dog fox i warrant the bitch be not far off the jargon which follow for some minute where many speak different thing at the same time as it would be very difficult to describe so would it be no less unpleasant to read
jones have at length shake mr western off and some of the company have interfere between they our heroe protest his innocence as to know anything of the lady when parson supple step up and say it be folly to deny it for why the mark of guilt be in thy hand
i will myself asseverate and bind it by an oath that the muff thou bearest in thy hand belongeth unto madam sophia for i have frequently observe she of later day to bear it about she my daughter s muff cry the squire in a rage
hath he get my daughter s muff
bear witness the good be find upon he
iwill have he before a justice of peace this instant
where be my daughter villain sir say jones i beg you would be pacify
the muff i acknowledge be the young lady s but upon my honour i have never see she at these word western lose all patience and grow inarticulate with rage
some of the servant have acquaint fitzpatrick who mr western be
the good irishman therefore think he have now an opportunity to do an act of service to his uncle and by that mean might possibly obtain his favour stept up to jones and cry out upon my conscience sir you may be ashamed of deny your have see the gentleman s daughter before my face when you know i find you there upon the bed together then turn to western he offer to conduct he immediately to the room where his daughter be which offer be accept he the squire the parson and some other ascend directly to mrs waters s chamber which they enter with no less violence than mr fitzpatrick have do before
the poor lady start from her sleep with as much amazement as terror and behold at her bedside a figure which might very well be suppose to have escape out of bedlam
such wildness and confusion be in the look of mr western who no soon see the lady than he start back shew sufficiently by his manner before he speak that this be not the person seek after
so much more tenderly do woman value their reputation than their person that though the latter seem now in more danger than before yet as the former be secure the lady scream not with such violence as she have do on the other occasion
however she no soon find herself alone than she abandon all thought of further repose and as she have sufficient reason to be dissatisfied with her present lodging she dress herself with all possible expedition
mr western now proceed to search the whole house but to as little purpose as he have disturb poor mrs waters
he then return disconsolate into the kitchen where he find jones in the custody of his servant
this violent uproar have raise all the people in the house though it be yet scarcely daylight
among these be a grave gentleman who have the honour to be in the commission of the peace for the county of worcester
of which mr western be no soon informed than he offer to lay his complaint before he
the justice decline execute his office as he say he have no clerk present nor no book about justice business and that he could not carry all the law in his head about steal away daughter and such sort of thing
here mr fitzpatrick offer to lend he his assistance inform the company that he have be himself breed to the law
and indeed he have serve three year as clerk to an attorney in the north of ireland when chuse a genteeler walk in life he quit his master come over to england and set up that business which require no apprenticeship namely that of a gentleman in which he have succeed as hath be already partly mention mr fitzpatrick declare that the law concern daughter be out of the present case that steal a muff be undoubtedly felony and the good be find upon the person be sufficient evidence of the fact
the magistrate upon the encouragement of so learn a coadjutor and upon the violent intercession of the squire be at length prevail upon to seat himself in the chair of justice where be place upon view the muff which jones still hold in his hand and upon the parson s swear it to be the property of mr western he desire mr fitzpatrick to draw up a commitment which he say he would sign
jones now desire to be hear which be at last with difficulty grant he
he then produce the evidence of mr partridge as to the find it but what be still more susan depose that sophia herself have deliver the muff to she and have order she to convey it into the chamber where mr jones have find it
whether a natural love of justice or the extraordinary comeliness of jones have work on susan to make the discovery i will not determine but such be the effect of her evidence that the magistrate throw himself back in his chair declare that the matter be now altogether as clear on the side of the prisoner as it have before be against he with which the parson concur say the lord forbid he should be instrumental in commit an innocent person to durance
the justice then arise acquit the prisoner and break up the court
mr western now give every one present a hearty curse and immediately order his horse depart in pursuit of his daughter without take the least notice of his nephew fitzpatrick or return any answer to his claim of kindred notwithstanding all the obligation he have just receive from that gentleman
in the violence moreover of his hurry and of his passion he luckily forget to demand the muff of jones i say luckily for he would have die on the spot rather than have part with it
jones likewise with his friend partridge set forward the moment he have pay his reckoning in quest of his lovely sophia whom he now resolve never more to abandon the pursuit of
nor could he bring himself even to take leave of mrs waters of whom he detest the very thought as she have be though not designedly the occasion of his miss the happy interview with sophia to whom he now vow eternal constancy
as for mrs waters she take the opportunity of the coach which be go to bath for which place she set out in company with the two irish gentleman the landlady kindly lend she her cloath in return for which she be content only to receive about double their value as a recompence for the loan
upon the road she be perfectly reconcile to mr fitzpatrick who be a very handsome fellow and indeed do all she could to console he in the absence of his wife
thus end the many odd adventure which mr jones encounter at his inn at upton where they talk to this day of the beauty and lovely behaviour of the charming sophia by the name of the somersetshire angel
chapter viii
in which the history go backward
before we proceed any far in our history it may be proper to look a little back in order to account for the extraordinary appearance of sophia and her father at the inn at upton
the reader may be pleased to remember that in the ninth chapter of the seventh book of our history we leave sophia after a long debate between love and duty decide the cause as it usually i believe happen in favour of the former
this debate have arise as we have there show from a visit which her father have just before make she in order to force her consent to a marriage with blifil and which he have understand to be fully imply in her acknowledgment that she neither must nor could refuse any absolute command of his now from this visit the squire retire to his evening potation overjoy at the success he have gain with his daughter and as he be of a social disposition and willing to have partaker in his happiness the beer be order to flow very liberally into the kitchen so that before eleven in the evening there be not a single person sober in the house except only mrs western herself and the charming sophia
early in the morning a messenger be despatch to summon mr blifil for though the squire imagine that young gentleman have be much less acquainted than he really be with the former aversion of his daughter as he have not however yet receive her consent he long impatiently to communicate it to he not doubt but that the intend bride herself would confirm it with her lip
as to the wedding it have the evening before be fix by the male party to be celebrate on the next morning save one
breakfast be now set forth in the parlour where mr blifil attend and where the squire and his sister likewise be assemble and now sophia be order to be call

then would i draw the picture of the poor serve-man who with pale countenance star eye chatter tooth faultere tongue and trembling limb e en such a man so faint so spiritless so dull so dead in look so woe-begone drew priam s curtain in the dead of night and would have tell he half his troy be burn d enter the room and declare--that madam sophia be not to be find
not to be find cry the squire start from his chair zound and d--nation
