where la
brother say mrs western with true political coldness you be always throw yourself into such violent passion for nothing
my niece i suppose be only walk out into the garden
i protest you be grow so unreasonable that it be impossible to live in the house with you nay nay answer the squire return as suddenly to himself as he have go from himself if that be all the matter it signify not much but upon my soul my mind misgave i when the fellow say she be not to be find he then give order for the bell to be ring in the garden and sit himself contentedly down
no two thing could be more the reverse of each other than be the brother and sister in most instance particularly in this that as the brother never foresaw anything at a distance but be most sagacious in immediately see everything the moment it have happen so the sister eternally foresee at a distance but be not so quick-sighted to object before her eye
of both these the reader may have observe example and indeed both their several talent be excessive for as the sister often foresaw what never come to pass so the brother often see much more than be actually the truth
this be not however the case at present
the same report be bring from the garden as before have be bring from the chamber that madam sophia be not to be find
the squire himself now sally forth and begin to roar forth the name of sophia as loudly and in as hoarse a voice as whilome do hercules that of hylas and as the poet tell we that the whole shore echo back the name of that beautiful youth so do the house the garden and all the neighbouring field resound nothing but the name of sophia in the hoarse voice of the man and in the shrill pipe of the woman while echo seem so pleased to repeat the beloved sound that if there be really such a person i believe ovid hath belie she sex
nothing reign for a long time but confusion till at last the squire have sufficiently spend his breath return to the parlour where he find mrs western and mr blifil and throw himself with the utmost dejection in his countenance into a great chair
here mrs western begin to apply the follow consolation brother i be sorry for what hath happen and that my niece should have behave herself in a manner so unbecome her family but it be all your own doing and you have nobody to thank but yourself
you know she hath be educate always in a manner directly contrary to my advice and now you see the consequence
have i not a thousand time argue with you about give my niece her own will
but you know i never could prevail upon you and when i have take so much pain to eradicate her headstrong opinion and to rectify your error in policy you know she be take out of my hand so that i have nothing to answer for
have i be trust entirely with the care of her education no such accident as this have ever befall you so that you must comfort yourself by think it be all your own doing and indeed what else could be expect from such indulgence zounds
sister answer he you be enough to make one mad
have i indulge she
have i give she she will it be no long ago than last night that i threaten if she disobey i to confine she to her chamber upon bread and water as long as she lived you would provoke the patience of job do ever mortal hear the like reply she
brother if i have not the patience of fifty job you would make i forget all decency and decorum
why would you interfere
do i not beg you do i not intreat you to leave the whole conduct to i
you have defeat all the operation of the campaign by one false step
would any man in his sens have provoke a daughter by such threat as these
how often have i tell you that english woman be not to be treat like ciracessian slave
we have the protection of the world we be to be win by gentle mean only and not to be hector and bully and beat into compliance
i thank heaven no salique law govern here
brother you have a roughness in your manner which no woman but myself would bear
i do not wonder my niece be frightened and terrify into take this measure and to speak honestly i think my niece will be justify to the world for what she hath do
i repeat it to you again brother you must comfort yourself by rememb re that it be all your own fault
how often have i advised-- here western rise hastily from his chair and vent two or three horrid imprecation run out of the room
possibly circassian
when he be depart his sister express more bitterness if possible against he than she have do while he be present for the truth of which she appeal to mr blifil who with great complacence acquiesce entirely in all she say but excuse all the fault of mr western as they must be consider he say to have proceed from the too inordinate fondness of a father which must be allow the name of an amiable weakness so much the more inexcuseable answer the lady for whom doth he ruin by his fondness but his own child to which blifil immediately agree
mrs western then begin to express great confusion on the account of mr blifil and of the usage which he have receive from a family to which he intend so much honour
on this subject she treat the folly of her niece with great severity but conclude with throw the whole on her brother who she say be inexcuseable to have proceed so far without well assurance of his daughter s consent but he be say she always of a violent headstrong temper and i can scarce forgive myself for all the advice i have throw away upon he after much of this kind of conversation which perhaps would not greatly entertain the reader be it here particularly related mr blifil take his leave and return home not highly pleased with his disappointment which however the philosophy which he have acquire from square and the religion infuse into he by thwackum together with somewhat else teach he to bear rather well than more passionate lover bear these kind of evil
chapter ix
the escape of sophia
it be now time to look after sophia whom the reader if he love she half so well as i do will rejoice to find escape from the clutch of her passionate father and from those of her dispassionate lover
twelve time do the iron register of time beat on the sonorous bell-metal summon the ghost to rise and walk their nightly round in plainer language it be twelve o clock and all the family as we have say lie bury in drink and sleep except only mrs western who be deeply engage in read a political pamphlet and except our heroine who now softly steal down-stair and have unbarred and unlocked one of the house-door sally forth and hasten to the place of appointment
notwithstanding the many pretty art which lady sometimes practise to display their fear on every little occasion almost as many as the other sex use to conceal theirs certainly there be a degree of courage which not only become a woman but be often necessary to enable she to discharge her duty
it be indeed the idea of fierceness and not of bravery which destroy the female character for who can read the story of the justly celebrated arria without conceiving as high an opinion of her gentleness and tenderness as of her fortitude
at the same time perhaps many a woman who shriek at a mouse or a rat may be capable of poison a husband or what be bad of drive he to poison himself
sophia with all the gentleness which a woman can have have all the spirit which she ought to have
when therefore she come to the place of appointment and instead of meet she maid as be agree see a man ride directly up to she she neither scream out nor faint away not that her pulse then beat with its usual regularity for she be at first under some surprize and apprehension but these be relieve almost as soon as raise when the man pull off his hat ask she in a very submissive manner if her ladyship do not expect to meet another lady and then proceed to inform she that he be send to conduct she to that lady
sophia could have no possible suspicion of any falsehood in this account she therefore mount resolutely behind the fellow who convey she safe to a town about five mile distant where she have the satisfaction of find the good mrs honour for as the soul of the waiting-woman be wrapt up in those very habiliment which use to enwrap her body she could by no mean bring herself to trust they out of her sight
upon these therefore she keep guard in person while she detach the aforesaid fellow after her mistress have give he all proper instruction
they now debate what course to take in order to avoid the pursuit of mr western who they know would send after they in a few hour
the london road have such charm for honour that she be desirous of go on directly allege that as sophia could not be miss till eight or nine the next morning her pursuer would not be able to overtake she even though they know which way she have go
but sophia have too much at stake to venture anything to chance nor do she dare trust too much to her tender limb in a contest which be to be decide only by swiftness
she resolve therefore to travel across the country for at least twenty or thirty mile and then to take the direct road to london
so have hire horse to go twenty mile one way when she intend to go twenty mile the other she set forward with the same guide behind whom she have ride from her father s house the guide have now take up behind he in the room of sophia a much heavy as well as much less lovely burden be indeed a huge portmanteau well stuff with those outside ornament by mean of which the fair honour hop to gain many conquest and finally to make her fortune in london city
when they have go about two hundred pace from the inn on the london road sophia ride up to the guide and with a voice much full of honey than be ever that of plato though his mouth be suppose to have be a bee-hive beg he to take the first turning which lead towards bristol
reader i be not superstitious nor any great believer of modern miracle
i do not therefore deliver the following as a certain truth for indeed i can scarce credit it myself but the fidelity of an historian oblige i to relate what hath be confidently assert
the horse then on which the guide ride be report to have be so charm by sophia s voice that he make a full stop and express an unwillingness to proceed any far
perhaps however the fact may be true and less miraculous than it hath be represent since the natural cause seem adequate to the effect for as the guide at that moment desist from a constant application of his armed right heel for like hudibra he wear but one spur it be more than possible that this omission alone might occasion the beast to stop especially as this be very frequent with he at other time
but if the voice of sophia have really an effect on the horse it have very little on the rider
he answer somewhat surlily that measter have order he to go a different way and that he should lose his place if he go any other than that he be order sophia find all her persuasion have no effect begin now to add irresistible charm to her voice charm which accord to the proverb make the old mare trot instead of stand still charm
to which modern age have attribute all that irresistible force which the antient impute to perfect oratory
in a word she promise she would reward he to his utmost expectation
the lad be not totally deaf to these promise but he dislike their be indefinite for though perhaps he have never hear that word yet that in fact be his objection
he say gentlevolks do not consider the case of poor volk that he have like to have be turn away the other day for rid about the country with a gentleman from squire allworthy s who do not reward he as he should have do with whom say sophia eagerly
with a gentleman from squire allworthy s repeat the lad the squire s son i think they call un whither
which way do he go say sophia why a little o one side o bristol about twenty mile off answer the lad guide i say sophia to the same place and iwill give thee a guinea or two if one be not sufficient to be certain say the boy it be honestly worth two when your ladyship consider what a risk i run but however if your ladyship will promise i the two guineas iwill e en venture to be certain it be a sinful thing to ride about my measter s horse but one comfort be i can only be turn away and two guineas will partly make i amend the bargain be thus strike the lad turn aside into the bristol road and sophia set forward in pursuit of jones highly contrary to the remonstrance of mrs honour who have much more desire to see london than to see mr jones for indeed she be not his friend with her mistress as he have be guilty of some neglect in certain pecuniary civility which be by custom due to the waiting-gentlewoman in all love affair and more especially in those of a clandestine kind
this we impute rather to the carelessness of his temper than to any want of generosity but perhaps she derive it from the latter motive
certain it be that she hat he very bitterly on that account and resolve to take every opportunity of injure he with her mistress
it be therefore highly unlucky for she that she have go to the very same town and inn whence jones have start and still more unlucky be she in have stumble on the same guide and on this accidental discovery which sophia have make
our traveller arrive at hambrook at the break of day where honour be against she will charge to enquire the route which mr jones have take
of this indeed the guide himself could have inform they but sophia i know not for what reason never ask he the question
this be the village where jones meet the quaker
when mrs honour have make her report from the landlord sophia with much difficulty procure some indifferent horse which bring she to the inn where jones have be confine rather by the misfortune of meeting with a surgeon than by have meet with a broken head
here honour be again charge with a commission of enquiry have no soon apply herself to the landlady and have describe the person of mr jones than that sagacious woman begin in the vulgar phrase to smell a rat
when sophia therefore enter the room instead of answer the maid the landlady address herself to the mistress begin the follow speech good lack-a-day
why there now who would have think it
i protest the lovely couple that ever eye behold
i-fackins madam it be no wonder the squire run on so about your ladyship
he tell i indeed you be the fine lady in the world and to be sure so you be
mercy on he poor heart
i bepitie he so i do when he use to hug his pillow and call it his dear madam sophia
i do all i could to dissuade he from go to the war i tell he there be man enow that be good for nothing else but to be kill that have not the love of such fine lady sure say sophia the good woman be distract no no cry the landlady i be not distract
what doth your ladyship think i donot know then
i assure you he tell i all what saucy fellow cry honour tell you anything of my lady no saucy fellow answer the landlady but the young gentleman you enquire after and a very pretty young gentleman he be and he love madam sophia western to the bottom of his soul he love my lady
iwould have you to know woman she be meat for his master nay honour say sophia interrupt she donot be angry with the good woman she intend no harm no marry donot i answer the landlady embolden by the soft accent of sophia and then launch into a long narrative too tedious to be here set down in which some passage dropt that give a little offence to sophia and much more to her waiting-woman who hence take occasion to abuse poor jones to her mistress the moment they be alone together say that he must be a very pitiful fellow and could have no love for a lady whose name he would thus prostitute in an ale-house sophia do not see his behaviour in so very disadvantageous a light and be perhaps more pleased with the violent rapture of his love which the landlady exaggerate as much as she have do every other circumstance than she be offend with the rest and indeed she impute the whole to the extravagance or rather ebullience of his passion and to the openness of his heart
this incident however be afterwards revive in her mind and place in the most odious colour by honour serve to heighten and give credit to those unlucky occurrence at upton and assist the waiting-woman in her endeavour to make her mistress depart from that inn without see jones
the landlady find sophia intend to stay no long than till she horse be ready and that without either eat or drinking soon withdraw when honour begin to take her mistress to task for indeed she use great freedom and after a long harangue in which she remind she of her intention to go to london and give frequent hint of the impropriety of pursue a young fellow she at last conclude with this serious exhortation for heaven s sake madam consider what you be about and whither you be go this advice to a lady who have already ride near forty mile and in no very agreeable season may seem foolish enough
it may be suppose she have well consider and resolve this already nay mrs honour by the hint she throw out seem to think so and this i doubt not be the opinion of many reader who have i make no doubt be long since well convinced of the purpose of our heroine and have heartily condemn she for it as a wanton baggage
but in reality this be not the case
sophia have be lately so distract between hope and fear her duty and love to her father her hatred to blifil her compassion and why should we not confess the truth her love for jones which last the behaviour of her father of her aunt of every one else and more particularly of jones himself have blow into a flame that her mind be in that confused state which may be truly say to make we ignorant of what we do or whither we go or rather indeed indifferent as to the consequence of either
the prudent and sage advice of she maid produce however some cool reflection and she at length determine to go to gloucester and thence to proceed directly to london
but unluckily a few mile before she enter that town she meet the hack-attorney who as be before mention have din there with mr jones
this fellow be well know to mrs honour stopt and speak to she of which sophia at that time take little notice more than to enquire who he be
but have have a more particular account from honour of this man afterwards at gloucester and hearing of the great expedition he usually make in travelling for which as hath be before observe he be particularly famous recollect likewise that she have overhear mrs honour inform he that they be go to gloucester she begin to fear lest her father might by this fellow s mean be able to trace she to that city wherefore if she should there strike into the london road she apprehend he would certainly be able to overtake she
she therefore alter her resolution and have hire horse to go a week s journey a way which she do not intend to travel she again set forward after a light refreshment contrary to the desire and earnest entreaty of she maid and to the no less vehement remonstrance of mrs whitefield who from good breeding or perhaps from good nature for the poor young lady appear much fatigue press she very heartily to stay that evening at gloucester
have refresh herself only with some tea and with lie about two hour on the bed while her horse be get ready she resolutely leave mrs whitefield s about eleven at night and strike directly into the worcester road within less than four hour arrive at that very inn where we last see she
have thus trace our heroine very particularly back from her departure till her arrival at upton we shall in a very few word bring her father to the same place who have receive the first scent from the post-boy who conduct his daughter to hambrook very easily trace she afterwards to gloucester whence he pursue she to upton as he have learn mr jones have take that route for partridge to use the squire s expression leave everywhere a strong scent behind he and he doubt not in the least but sophia travel or as he phrase it run the same way
he use indeed a very coarse expression which need not be here insert as fox-hunter who alone will understand it will easily suggest it to themselves
book xi
contain about three day
chapter i
a crust for the critic
in our last initial chapter we may be suppose to have treat that formidable set of man who be call critic with more freedom than become we since they exact and indeed generally receive great condescension from author
we shall in this therefore give the reason of our conduct to this august body and here we shall perhaps place they in a light in which they have not hitherto be see
this word critic be of greek derivation and signify judgment
hence i presume some person who have not understand the original and have see the english translation of the primitive have conclude that it mean judgment in the legal sense in which it be frequently use as equivalent to condemnation
i be the rather inclined to be of that opinion as the great number of critic hath of late year be find amongst the lawyer
many of these gentleman from despair perhaps of ever rise to the bench in westminster-hall have place themselves on the bench at the playhouse where they have exert their judicial capacity and have give judgment i e condemn without mercy
the gentleman would perhaps be well enough please if we be to leave they thus compare to one of the most important and honourable office in the commonwealth and if we intend to apply to their favour we would do so but as we design to deal very sincerely and plainly too with they we must remind they of another officer of justice of a much low rank to whom as they not only pronounce but execute their own judgment they bear likewise some remote resemblance
but in reality there be another light in which these modern critic may with great justice and propriety be see and this be that of a common slanderer
if a person who prys into the character of other with no other design but to discover their fault and to publish they to the world deserve the title of a slanderer of the reputation of man why should not a critic who read with the same malevolent view be as properly stile the slanderer of the reputation of book
vice hath not i believe a more abject slave society produce not a more odious vermin nor can the devil receive a guest more worthy of he nor possibly more welcome to he than a slanderer
the world i be afraid regard not this monster with half the abhorrence which he deserve and i be more afraid to assign the reason of this criminal lenity show towards he yet it be certain that the thief look innocent in the comparison nay the murderer himself can seldom stand in competition with his guilt for slander be a more cruel weapon than a sword as the wound which the former give be always incurable
one method indeed there be of killing and that the basest and most execrable of all which bear an exact analogy to the vice here disclaim against and that be poison a means of revenge so base and yet so horrible that it be once wisely distinguish by our law from all other murder in the peculiar severity of the punishment
besides the dreadful mischief do by slander and the baseness of the mean by which they be effect there be other circumstance that highly aggravate its atrocious quality for it often proceed from no provocation and seldom promise itself any reward unless some black and infernal mind may propose a reward in the thought of have procure the ruin and misery of another
shakespear hath nobly touch this vice when he says-- who steal my purse steal trash t be something nothing twa mine tis his and hath be slave to thousand but he that filch from i my good name robs i of that which not enriches him but makes i poor indeed with all this my good reader will doubtless agree but much of it will probably seem too severe when apply to the slanderer of book
but let it here be consider that both proceed from the same wicked disposition of mind and be alike void of the excuse of temptation
nor shall we conclude the injury do this way to be very slight when we consider a book as the author s offspring and indeed as the child of his brain
the reader who hath suffer his muse to continue hitherto in a virgin state can have but a very inadequate idea of this kind of paternal fondness
to such we may parody the tender exclamation of macduff alas
thou hast write no book but the author whose muse hath bring forth will feel the pathetic strain perhaps will accompany i with tear especially if his darling be already no more while i mention the uneasiness with which the big muse bear about her burden the painful labour with which she produce it and lastly the care the fondness with which the tender father nourish his favourite till it be bring to maturity and produce into the world
nor be there any paternal fondness which seem less to savour of absolute instinct and which may so well be reconcile to worldly wisdom as this
these child may most truly be call the rich of their father and many of they have with true filial piety feed their parent in his old age so that not only the affection but the interest of the author may be highly injure by these slanderer whose poisonous breath bring his book to an untimely end
lastly the slander of a book be in truth the slander of the author for as no one can call another bastard without call the mother a whore so neither can any one give the name of sad stuff horrid nonsense c to a book without call the author a blockhead which though in a moral sense it be a preferable appellation to that of villain be perhaps rather more injurious to his worldly interest
now however ludicrous all this may appear to some other i doubt not will feel and acknowledge the truth of it nay may perhaps think i have not treat the subject with decent solemnity but surely a man may speak truth with a smile countenance
in reality to depreciate a book maliciously or even wantonly be at least a very ill-natured office and a morose snarl critic may i believe be suspect to be a bad man
i will therefore endeavour in the remain part of this chapter to explain the mark of this character and to show what criticism i here intend to obviate for i can never be understand unless by the very person here mean to insinuate that there be no proper judge of writing or to endeavour to exclude from the commonwealth of literature any of those noble critic to whose labour the learned world be so greatly indebted
such be aristotle horace and longinus among the antient dacier and bossu among the french and some perhaps among we who have certainly be duly authorise to execute at least a judicial authority in foro literario
but without ascertain all the proper qualification of a critic which i have touch on elsewhere i think i may very boldly object to the censure of any one past upon work which he hath not himself read
such censurer as these whether they speak from their own guess or suspicion or from the report and opinion of other may properly be say to slander the reputation of the book they condemn
such may likewise be suspect of deserve this character who without assign any particular fault condemn the whole in general defamatory term such as vile dull d--d stuff c and particularly by the use of the monosyllable low a word which become the mouth of no critic who be not right honourable
again though there may be some fault justly assign in the work yet if those be not in the most essential part or if they be compensate by great beauty it will savour rather of the malice of a slanderer than of the judgment of a true critic to pass a severe sentence upon the whole merely on account of some vicious part
this be directly contrary to the sentiment of horace verum ubi plura nitent in carmine non ego paucis offendor maculi quas aut incuria fudit aut humana parum cavit natura---- but where the beauty more in number shine i be not angry when a casual line that with some trivial fault unequal flow a careless hand or human frailty shows mr francis
for as martial say aliter non fit avite liber
no book can be otherwise compose
all beauty of character as well as of countenance and indeed of everything human be to be try in this manner
cruel indeed would it be if such a work as this history which hath employ some thousand of hour in the composing should be liable to be condemn because some particular chapter or perhaps chapter may be obnoxious to very just and sensible objection
and yet nothing be more common than the most rigorous sentence upon book support by such objection which if they be rightly take and that they be not always do by no mean go to the merit of the whole
in the theatre especially a single expression which doth not coincide with the taste of the audience or with any individual critic of that audience be sure to be hiss and one scene which should be disapprove would hazard the whole piece
to write within such severe rule as these be as impossible as to live up to some splenetic opinion and if we judge accord to the sentiment of some critic and of some christians no author will be save in this world and no man in the next
chapter ii
the adventure which sophia meet with after she leave upton
our history just before it be oblige to turn about and travel backwards have mention the departure of sophia and she maid from the inn we shall now therefore pursue the step of that lovely creature and leave her unworthy lover a little long to bemoan his ill-luck or rather his ill-conduct
sophia have direct her guide to travel through bye-road across the country they now pass the severn and have scarce get a mile from the inn when the young lady look behind she see several horse come after on full speed
this greatly alarm her fear and she call to the guide to put on as fast as possible
he immediately obey she and away they ride a full gallop
but the fast they go the fast be they follow and as the horse behind be somewhat swifter than those before so the former be at length overtaken
a happy circumstance for poor sophia whose fear join to her fatigue have almost overpower her spirit but she be now instantly relieve by a female voice that greet she in the soft manner and with the utmost civility
this greet sophia as soon as she could recover her breath with like civility and with the high satisfaction to herself return
the traveller who join sophia and who have give she such terror consist like her own company of two female and a guide
the two party proceed three full mile together before any one offer again to open their mouth when our heroine have pretty well get the well of her fear but yet be somewhat surprize that the other still continue to attend she as she pursue no great road and have already pass through several turning accost the strange lady in a most obliging tone and say she be very happy to find they be both travel the same way the other who like a ghost only want to be speak to readily answer that the happiness be entirely her that she be a perfect stranger in that country and be so overjoyed at meet a companion of her own sex that she have perhaps be guilty of an impertinence which require great apology in keep pace with she more civility pass between these two lady for mrs honour have now give place to the fine habit of the stranger and have fall into the rear
but though sophia have great curiosity to know why the other lady continue to travel on through the same bye-road with herself nay though this give she some uneasiness yet fear or modesty or some other consideration restrain she from ask the question
the strange lady now labour under a difficulty which appear almost below the dignity of history to mention
her bonnet have be blow from her head not less than five time within the last mile nor could she come at any ribbon or handkerchief to tie it under her chin
when sophia be inform of this she immediately supply she with a handkerchief for this purpose which while she be pull from her pocket she perhaps too much neglect the management of her horse for the beast now unluckily make a false step fall upon his fore-leg and throw his fair rider from his back
though sophia come head foremost to the ground she happily receive not the least damage and the same circumstance which have perhaps contribute to her fall now preserve she from confusion for the lane which they be then pass be narrow and very much overgrown with tree so that the moon could here afford very little light and be moreover at present so obscure in a cloud that it be almost perfectly dark
by these mean the young lady s modesty which be extremely delicate escape as free from injury as her limb and she be once more reinstate in her saddle have receive no other harm than a little fright by her fall
daylight at length appear in its full lustre and now the two lady who be rid over a common side by side look stedfastly at each other at the same moment both their eye become fix both their horse stopt and both speak together with equal joy pronounce the one the name of sophia the other that of harriet
this unexpected encounter surprize the lady much more than i believe it will the sagacious reader who must have imagine that the strange lady could be no other than mrs fitzpatrick the cousin of miss western whom we before mention to have sally from the inn a few minute after she
so great be the surprize and joy which these two cousin conceive at this meeting for they have formerly be most intimate acquaintance and friend and have long live together with their aunt western that it be impossible to recount half the congratulation which pass between they before either ask a very natural question of the other namely whither she be go
this at last however come first from mrs fitzpatrick but easy and natural as the question may seem sophia find it difficult to give it a very ready and certain answer
she beg her cousin therefore to suspend all curiosity till they arrive at some inn which i suppose say she can hardly be far distant and believe i harriet i suspend as much curiosity on my side for indeed i believe our astonishment be pretty equal the conversation which pass between these lady on the road be i apprehend little worth relate and less certainly be that between the two waiting-woman for they likewise begin to pay their compliment to each other
as for the guide they be debar from the pleasure of discourse the one be place in the van and the other oblige to bring up the rear
in this posture they travel many hour till they come into a wide and well-beat road which as they turn to the right soon bring they to a very fair promising inn where they all alight but so fatigue be sophia that as she have sit her horse during the last five or six mile with great difficulty so be she now incapable of dismount from he without assistance
this the landlord who have hold of her horse presently perceive offer to lift she in his arm from her saddle and she too readily accept the tender of his service
indeed fortune seem to have resolve to put sophia to the blush that day and the second malicious attempt succeed well than the first for my landlord have no soon receive the young lady in his arm than his foot which the gout have lately very severely handle give way and down he tumble but at the same time with no less dexterity than gallantry contrive to throw himself under his charming burden so that he alone receive any bruise from the fall for the great injury which happen to sophia be a violent shock give to her modesty by an immoderate grin which at she rise from the ground she observe in the countenance of most of the bye-stander
this make her suspect what have really happen and what we shall not here relate for the indulgence of those reader who be capable of laugh at the offence give to a young lady s delicacy
accident of this kind we have never regard in a comical light nor will we scruple to say that he must have a very inadequate idea of the modesty of a beautiful young woman who would wish to sacrifice it to so paltry a satisfaction as can arise from laughter
this fright and shock join to the violent fatigue which both her mind and body have undergo almost overcome the excellent constitution of sophia and she have scarce strength sufficient to totter into the inn lean on the arm of she maid
here she be no soon seat than she call for a glass of water but mrs honour very judiciously in my opinion change it into a glass of wine
mrs fitzpatrick hear from mrs honour that sophia have not be in bed during the two last night and observe she to look very pale and wan with her fatigue earnestly entreat she to refresh herself with some sleep
she be yet a stranger to her history or her apprehension but have she know both she would have give the same advice for rest be visibly necessary for she and their long journey through bye-road so entirely remove all danger of pursuit that she be herself perfectly easy on that account
sophia be easily prevail on to follow the counsel of her friend which be heartily second by her maid
mrs fitzpatrick likewise offer to bear her cousin company which sophia with much complacence accept
the mistress be no soon in bed than the maid prepare to follow she example
she begin to make many apology to her sister abigail for leave she alone in so horrid a place as an inn but the other stopt her short be as well inclined to a nap as herself and desire the honour of be her bedfellow
sophia s maid agree to give she a share of her bed but put in her claim to all the honour
so after many courtsie and compliment to bed together go the waiting-woman as their mistress have do before they
it be usual with my landlord as indeed it be with the whole fraternity to enquire particularly of all coachman footman postboy and other into the name of all his guest what their estate be and where it lay
it cannot therefore be wonder at that the many particular circumstance which attend our traveller and especially their retire all to sleep at so extraordinary and unusual an hour as ten in the morning should excite his curiosity
as soon therefore as the guide enter the kitchen he begin to examine who the lady be and whence they come but the guide though they faithfully relate all they know give he very little satisfaction
on the contrary they rather enflame his curiosity than extinguish it
this landlord have the character among all his neighbour of be a very sagacious fellow
he be think to see far and deep into thing than any man in the parish the parson himself not excepted
perhaps his look have contribute not a little to procure he this reputation for there be in this something wonderfully wise and significant especially when he have a pipe in his mouth which indeed he seldom be without
his behaviour likewise greatly assist in promote the opinion of his wisdom
in his deportment he be solemn if not sullen and when he speak which be seldom he always deliver himself in a slow voice and though his sentence be short they be still interrupt with many hum and ha s ay ays and other expletive so that though he accompany his word with certain explanatory gesture such as shake or nod the head or point with his fore-finger he generally leave his hearer to understand more than he express nay he commonly give they a hint that he know much more than he think proper to disclose
this last circumstance alone may indeed very well account for his character of wisdom since man be strangely inclined to worship what they do not understand
a grand secret upon which several imposer on mankind have totally rely for the success of their fraud
this polite person now take his wife aside ask she what she think of the lady lately arrive think of they say the wife why what should i think of they i know answer he what i think
the guide tell strange story
one pretend to be come from gloucester and the other from upton and neither of they for what i can find can tell whither they be go
but what people ever travel across the country from upton hither especially to london
and one of the maid-servant before she alight from her horse ask if this be not the london road
now i have put all these circumstance together and whom do you think i have find they out to be nay answer she you know i never pretend to guess at your discovery it be a good girl reply he chuck she under the chin i must own you have always submit to my knowledge of these matter
why then depend upon it mind what i say--depend upon it they be certainly some of the rebel lady who they say travel with the young chevalier and have take a roundabout way to escape the duke s army husband quoth the wife you have certainly hit it for one of they be dress as fine as any princess and to be sure she look for all the world like one but yet when i consider one thing when you consider cry the landlord contemptuously---- come pray letus hear what you consider why it be answer the wife that she be too humble to be any very great lady for while our betty be warm the bed she call she nothing but child and my dear and sweetheart and when betty offer to pull off her shoe and stocking she would not suffer she say she would not give she the trouble pugh answer the husband that be nothing
dost think because you have see some great lady rude and uncivil to person below they that none of they know how to behave themselves when they come before their inferior
i think i know people of fashion when i see they--i think i do
do not she call for a glass of water when she come in
another sort of woman would have call for a dram you know they would
if she be not a woman of very great quality sell i for a fool and i believe those who buy i will have a bad bargain
now would a woman of her quality travel without a footman unless upon some such extraordinary occasion nay to be sure husband cry she you know these matter well than i or most folk i think i do know something say he
to be sure answer the wife the poor little heart look so piteous when she sit down in the chair i protest i could not help have a compassion for she almost as much as if she have be a poor body
but whatbe to be do husband
if an she be a rebel i suppose you intend to betray she up to the court
well shebe a sweet-temper good-humour lady be she what she will and i shall hardly refrain from cry when i hear she be hang or behead pooh answer the husband but as to whatbe to be do it be not so easy a matter to determine
i hope before she go away we shall have the news of a battle for if the chevalier should get the well she may gain we interest at court and make our fortune without betray she why thatbe true reply the wife and i heartily hope she will have it in her power
certainly shebe a sweet good lady it would go horribly against i to have she come to any harm pooh cry the landlord woman be always so tender-hearted
why you would not harbour rebel would you no certainly answer the wife and as for betray she come what will on t nobody can blame we
it be what anybody would do in our case while our politic landlord who have not we see undeservedly the reputation of great wisdom among his neighbour be engage in debate this matter with himself for he pay little attention to the opinion of his wife news arrive that the rebel have give the duke the slip and have get a day s march towards london and soon after arrive a famous jacobite squire who with great joy in his countenance shake the landlord by the hand say allbe our own boy ten thousand honest frenchman be land in suffolk
old england for ever
ten thousand french my brave lad
i be go to tap away directly this news determine the opinion of the wise man and he resolve to make his court to the young lady when she arise for he have now he say discover that she be no other than madam jenny cameron herself
chapter iii
a very short chapter in which however be a sun a moon a star and an angel
the sun for he keep very good hour at this time of the year have be some time retire to rest when sophia arise greatly refresh by her sleep which short as it be nothing but her extreme fatigue could have occasion for though she have tell she maid and perhaps herself too that she be perfectly easy when she leave upton yet it be certain her mind be a little affected with that malady which be attend with all the restless symptom of a fever and be perhaps the very distemper which physician mean if they mean anything by the fever on the spirit
mrs fitzpatrick likewise leave her bed at the same time and have summon she maid immediately dress herself
she be really a very pretty woman and have she be in any other company but that of sophia might have be think beautiful but when mrs honour of her own accord attend for her mistress would not suffer she to be waked and have equip our heroine the charm of mrs fitzpatrick who have perform the office of the morning-star and have precede great glory share the fate of that star and be totally eclipse the moment those glory shine forth
perhaps sophia never look more beautiful than she do at this instant
we ought not therefore to condemn the maid of the inn for her hyperbole who when she descend after have light the fire declare and ratify it with an oath that if ever there be an angel upon earth she be now above-stair
sophia have acquaint her cousin with her design to go to london and mrs fitzpatrick have agree to accompany she for the arrival of her husband at upton have put an end to her design of go to bath or to her aunt western
they have therefore no soon finish their tea than sophia propose to set out the moon then shin extremely bright and as for the frost she defy it nor have she any of those apprehension which many young lady would have feel at travel by night for she have as we have before observe some little degree of natural courage and this her present sensation which border somewhat on despair greatly encrease
besides as she have already travel twice with safety by the light of the moon she be the well embolden to trust to it a third time
the disposition of mrs fitzpatrick be more timorous for though the great terror have conquer the less and the presence of her husband have drive she away at so unseasonable an hour from upton yet be now arrive at a place where she think herself safe from his pursuit these less terror of i know not what operate so strongly that she earnestly entreat her cousin to stay till the next morning and not expose herself to the danger of travel by night

chapter iv
the history of mrs fitzpatrick

this observation young as you be you could scarce avoid make from my aunt who be free or reserve with all people just as they have more or less of this merit
and this merit i believe it be which principally recommend mr fitzpatrick to her favour
in which he so well succeed that he be always one of her private party

this i understand as an attempt to lessen if possible that disinclination which my interest might be suppose to give i towards the match and i know not but in some measure it have that effect for as i be well content with my own fortune and of all people the least a slave to interested view so i could not be violently the enemy of a man with whose behaviour to i i be greatly please and the more so as i be the only object of such respect for he behave at the same time to many woman of quality without any respect at all

chapter v
in which the history of mrs fitzpatrick be continue
we remain at bath no long than a fortnight after our wedding for as to any reconciliation with my aunt there be no hope and of my fortune not one farthing could be touch till i be of age of which i now want more than two year

good heaven
how be it possible for a man to maintain a constant lie in his appearance abroad and in company and to content himself with shewing disagreeable truth only at home

you will easily conceive my dear graveairs i ask your pardon i really forget myself that when a woman make an imprudent match in the sense of the world that is when she be not an arrant prostitute to pecuniary interest she must necessarily have some inclination and affection for her man

perhaps you will wonder i do not make this discovery long before but woman will suggest a thousand excuse to themselves for the folly of those they like besides give i leave to tell you it require a most penetrate eye to discern a fool through the disguise of gaiety and good breeding
