indeed she be to i a welcome guest
a few day after her arrival perceive i in very low spirit without enquire the cause which indeed she very well know the young lady fall to compassionate my case
she say though politeness have prevent i from complain to my husband s relation of his behaviour yet they all be very sensible of it and feel great concern upon that account but none more than herself and after some more general discourse on this head which i own i could not forbear countenancing at last after much previous precaution and enjoin concealment she communicate to i as a profound secret--that my husband keep a mistress
you will certainly imagine i hear this news with the utmost insensibility--upon my word if you do your imagination will mislead you
contempt have not so keep down my anger to my husband but that hatred rise again on this occasion
what can be the reason of this
be we so abominably selfish that we can be concern at other have possession even of what we despise
or be we not rather abominably vain and be not this the great injury do to our vanity
what think you sophia i donot know indeed answer sophia i have never trouble myself with any of these deep contemplation but i think the lady do very ill in communicating to you such a secret and yet my dear this conduct be natural reply mrs fitzpatrick and when you have see and read as much as myself you will acknowledge it to be so i be sorry to hear it be natural return sophia for i want neither read nor experience to convince i that it be very dishonourable and very ill-natured nay it be surely as ill-breed to tell a husband or wife of the fault of each other as to tell they of their own well continue mrs fitzpatrick my husband at last return and if i be thoroughly acquaint with my own thought i hat he now more than ever but i despise he rather less for certainly nothing so much weaken our contempt as an injury do to our pride or our vanity
he now assume a carriage to i so very different from what he have lately worn and so nearly resemble his behaviour the first week of our marriage that have i now have any spark of love remain he might possibly have rekindle my fondness for he
but though hatred may succeed to contempt and may perhaps get the well of it love i believe cannot
the truth be the passion of love be too restless to remain contented without the gratification which it receive from its object and one can no more be inclined to love without love than we can have eye without see
when a husband therefore cease to be the object of this passion it be most probable some other man--i say my dear if your husband grow indifferent to you--if you once come to despise he--i say--that be--if you have the passion of love in you--lud
i have bewilder myself so--but one be apt in these abstract consideration to lose the concatenation of idea as mr locke says in short the truth be--in short i scarce know what it be but as i be say my husband return and his behaviour at first greatly surprize i but he soon acquaint i with the motive and teach i to account for it
in a word then he have spend and lose all the ready money of my fortune and as he could mortgage his own estate no deep he be now desirous to supply himself with cash for his extravagance by sell a little estate of mine which he could not do without my assistance and to obtain this favour be the whole and sole motive of all the fondness which he now put on
with this i peremptorily refuse to comply
i tell he and i tell he truly that have i be possess of the indies at our first marriage he might have command it all for it have be a constant maxim with i that where a woman dispose of her heart she should always deposit her fortune but as he have be so kind long ago to restore the former into my possession i be resolve likewise to retain what little remain of the latter
i will not describe to you the passion into which these word and the resolute air in which they be speak throw he nor will i trouble you with the whole scene which succeed between we
out come you may be well assure the story of the mistress and out it do come with all the embellishment which anger and disdain could bestow upon it
mr fitzpatrick seem a little thunderstruck with this and more confuse than i have see he though his idea be always confused enough heaven know
he do not however endeavour to exculpate himself but take a method which almost equally confound i
what be this but recrimination
he affect to be jealous he may for aught i know be incline enough to jealousy in his natural temper nay he must have have it from nature or the devil must have put it into his head for i defy all the world to cast a just aspersion on my character nay the most scandalous tongue have never dare censure my reputation
my fame i thank heaven hath be always as spotless as my life and let falsehood itself accuse that if it dare
no my dear graveair however provoke however ill-treat however injure in my love i have firmly resolve never to give the least room for censure on this account and yet my dear there be some people so malicious some tongue so venomous that no innocence can escape they
the most undesigned word the most accidental look the least familiarity the most innocent freedom will be misconstrue and magnify into i know not what by some people
but i despise my dear graveairs i despise all such slander
no such malice i assure you ever give i an uneasy moment
no no i promise you i be above all that but where be i
o let i see i tell you my husband be jealous--and of whom i pray why of whom but the lieutenant i mention to you before
he be oblige to resort above a year and more back to find any object for this unaccountable passion if indeed he really feel any such and be not an arrant counterfeit in order to abuse i
but i have tired you already with too many particular
i will now bring my story to a very speedy conclusion
in short then after many scene very unworthy to be repeat in which my cousin engage so heartily on my side that mr fitzpatrick at last turn she out of door when he find i be neither to be soothe nor bully into compliance he take a very violent method indeed
perhaps you will conclude he beat i but this though he hath approach very near to it he never actually do
he confine i to my room without suffer i to have either pen ink paper or book and a servant every day make my bed and bring i my food
when i have remain a week under this imprisonment he make i a visit and with the voice of a schoolmaster or what be often much the same of a tyrant ask i if i would yet comply i answer very stoutly that i would die first then so you shall and be d--nd cry he for you shall never go alive out of this room here i remain a fortnight long and to say the truth my constancy be almost subdue and i begin to think of submission when one day in the absence of my husband who be go abroad for some short time by the great good fortune in the world an accident happened i--at a time when i begin to give way to the utmost despair----everything would be excusable at such a time--at that very time i received----but it would take up an hour to tell you all particulars in one word then for i will not tire you with circumstance gold the common key to all padlock open my door and set i at liberty
i now make haste to dublin where i immediately procure a passage to england and be proceed to bath in order to throw myself into the protection of my aunt or of your father or of any relation who would afford it i
my husband overtake i last night at the inn where i lay and which you leave a few minute before i but i have the good luck to escape he and to follow you
and thus my dear end my history a tragical one i be sure it be to myself but perhaps i ought rather to apologize to you for its dullness sophia heave a deep sigh and answer indeed harriet i pity you from my soul but what could you expect
why why would you marry an irishman upon my word reply her cousin your censure be unjust
there be among the irish man of as much worth and honour as any among the english nay to speak the truth generosity of spirit be rather more common among they
i have know some example there too of good husband and i believe these be not very plenty in england
ask i rather what i could expect when i marry a fool and i will tell you a solemn truth i do not know he to be so can no man say sophia in a very low and altered voice do you think make a bad husband who be not a fool that answer the other be too general a negative but none i believe be so likely as a fool to prove so
among my acquaintance the silly fellow be the bad husband and i will venture to assert as a fact that a man of sense rarely behave very ill to a wife who deserve very well chapter viii
a dreadful alarm in the inn with the arrival of an unexpected friend of mrs fitzpatrick
sophia now at the desire of her cousin relate--not what follow but what hath go before in this history for which reason the reader will i suppose excuse i for not repeat it over again
one remark however i cannot forbear make on her narrative namely that she make no more mention of jones from the beginning to the end than if there have be no such person alive
this i will neither endeavour to account for nor to excuse
indeed if this may be call a kind of dishonesty it seem the more inexcusable from the apparent openness and explicit sincerity of the other lady but so it be
just as sophia arrive at the conclusion of her story there arrive in the room where the two lady be sit a noise not unlike in loudness to that of a pack of hound just let out from their kennel nor in shrillness to cat when caterwaul or to screech owl or indeed more like for what animal can resemble a human voice to those sound which in the pleasant mansion of that gate which seem to derive its name from a duplicity of tongue issue from the mouth and sometimes from the nostril of those fair river nymph clepe of old the naïade in the vulgar tongue translate oyster-wench for when instead of the antient libation of milk and honey and oil the rich distillation from the juniper-berry or perhaps from malt hath by the early devotion of their votary be pour forth in great abundance should any daring tongue with unhallowed license prophane i e depreciate the delicate fat milton oyster the plaice sound and firm the flounder as much alive as when in the water the shrimp as big as a prawn the fine cod alive but a few hour ago or any other of the various treasure which those water-deity who fish the sea and river have commit to the care of the nymph the angry naïades lift up their immortal voice and the prophane wretch be strike deaf for his impiety
such be the noise which now burst from one of the room below and soon the thunder which long have rattle at a distance begin to approach near and nearer till have ascend by degree upstairs it at last enter the apartment where the lady be
in short to drop all metaphor and figure mrs honour have scold violently below-stair and continue the same all the way up come in to her mistress in a most outrageous passion cry out what doth your ladyship think
would you imagine that this impudent villain the master of this house hath have the impudence to tell i nay to stand it out to my face that your ladyship be that nasty stink wh--re jenny cameron they call she that run about the country with the pretender
nay the lying saucy villain have the assurance to tell i that your ladyship have own yourself to be so but i have claw the rascal i have leave the mark of my nail in his impudent face
my lady
say i you saucy scoundrel my lady be meat for no pretender
she be a young lady of as good fashion and family and fortune as any in somersetshire
do you never hear of the great squire western sirrah
she be his only daughter she is---- and heiress to all his great estate
my lady to be call a nasty scotch wh--re by such a varlet to be sure i wish i have knock his brain out with the punch-bowl the principal uneasiness with which sophia be affect on this occasion honour have herself cause by have in her passion discover who she be
however as this mistake of the landlord sufficiently account for those passage which sophia have before mistaken she acquire some ease on that account nor could she upon the whole forbear smiling
this enrage honour and she cry indeed madam i do not think your ladyship would have make a laugh matter of it
to be call whore by such an impudent low rascal
your ladyship may be angry with i for aught i know for take your part since proffered service they say stink but to be sure i could never bear to hear a lady of mine call whore nor will i bear it
i be sure your ladyship be as virtuous a lady as ever sit foot on english ground and i will claw any villain s eye out who dare for to offer to presume for to say the least word to the contrary
nobody ever could say the least ill of the character of any lady that ever i wait upon hinc illae lachrymae in plain truth honour have as much love for her mistress as most servant have that be to say--but besides this her pride oblige she to support the character of the lady she wait on for she think her own be in a very close manner connect with it
in proportion as the character of her mistress be raise her likewise as she conceive be raise with it and on the contrary she think the one could not be lower without the other
on this subject reader i must stop a moment to tell thee a story
the famous nell gwynn step one day from a house where she have make a short visit into her coach see a great mob assemble and her footman all bloody and dirty the fellow be ask by his mistress the reason of his be in that condition answer i have be fight madam with an impudent rascal who call your ladyship a wh--re you blockhead reply mrs gwynn at this rate you must fight every day of your life why you fool all the world know it do they cry the fellow in a muttering voice after he have shut the coach-door they shallnot call i a whore s footman for all that thus the passion of mrs honour appear natural enough even if it be to be no otherwise account for but in reality there be another cause of her anger for which we must beg leave to remind our reader of a circumstance mention in the above simile
there be indeed certain liquor which be apply to our passion or to fire produce effect the very reverse of those produce by water as they serve to kindle and inflame rather than to extinguish
among these the generous liquor call punch be one
it be not therefore without reason that the learn dr cheney use to call drinking punch pour liquid fire down your throat
now mrs honour have unluckily pour so much of this liquid fire down her throat that the smoke of it begin to ascend into her pericranium and blind the eye of reason which be there suppose to keep she residence while the fire itself from the stomach easily reach the heart and there inflame the noble passion of pride
so that upon the whole we shall cease to wonder at the violent rage of the waiting-woman though at first sight we must confess the cause seem inadequate to the effect
sophia and her cousin both do all in their power to extinguish these flame which have roar so loudly all over the house
they at length prevail or to carry the metaphor one step far the fire have consume all the fuel which the language afford to wit every reproachful term in it at last go out of its own accord
but though tranquillity be restore above-stair it be not so below where my landlady highly resent the injury do to the beauty of her husband by the flesh-spade of mrs honour call aloud for revenge and justice
as to the poor man who have principally suffer in the engagement he be perfectly quiet
perhaps the blood which he lose might have cool his anger for the enemy have not only apply her nail to his cheek but likewise her fist to his nostril which lament the blow with tear of blood in great abundance
to this we may add reflection on his mistake but indeed nothing so effectually silence his resentment as the manner in which he now discover his error for as to the behaviour of mrs honour it have the more confirm he in his opinion but he be now assure by a person of great figure and who be attend by a great equipage that one of the lady be a woman of fashion and his intimate acquaintance
by the order of this person the landlord now ascend and acquaint our fair traveller that a great gentleman below desire to do they the honour of wait on they
sophia turn pale and tremble at this message though the reader will conclude it be too civil notwithstanding the landlord s blunder to have come from her father but fear hath the common fault of a justice of peace and be apt to conclude hastily from every slight circumstance without examine the evidence on both side
to ease the reader s curiosity therefore rather than his apprehension we proceed to inform he that an irish peer have arrive very late that evening at the inn in his way to london
this nobleman have sally from his supper at the hurricane before commemorate have see the attendant of mrs fitzpatrick and upon a short enquiry be inform that her lady with whom he be very particularly acquaint be above
this information he have no soon receive than he address himself to the landlord pacify he and send he upstairs with compliment rather civill than those which be deliver
it may perhaps be wonder at that the waiting-woman herself be not the messenger employ on this occasion but we be sorry to say she be not at present qualified for that or indeed for any other office
the rum for so the landlord choose to call the distillation from malt have basely take the advantage of the fatigue which the poor woman have undergo and have make terrible depredation on her noble faculty at a time when they be very unable to resist the attack
we shall not describe this tragical scene too fully but we think ourselves oblige by that historic integrity which we profess shortly to hint a matter which we would otherwise have be glad to have spar
many historian indeed for want of this integrity or of diligence to say no bad often leave the reader to find out these little circumstance in the dark and sometimes to his great confusion and perplexity
sophia be very soon ease of her causeless fright by the entry of the noble peer who be not only an intimate acquaintance of mrs fitzpatrick but in reality a very particular friend of that lady
to say truth it be by his assistance that she have be enable to escape from her husband for this nobleman have the same gallant disposition with those renowned knight of whom we read in heroic story and have deliver many an imprison nymph from durance
he be indeed as bitter an enemy to the savage authority too often exercise by husband and father over the young and lovely of the other sex as ever knight-errant be to the barbarous power of enchanter nay to say truth i have often suspect that those very enchanter with which romance everywhere abound be in reality no other than the husband of those day and matrimony itself be perhaps the enchant castle in which the nymph be say to be confine
this nobleman have an estate in the neighbourhood of fitzpatrick and have be for some time acquaint with the lady
no soon therefore do he hear of her confinement than he earnestly apply himself to procure her liberty which he presently effect not by storm the castle accord to the example of antient hero but by corrupt the governor in conformity with the modern art of war in which craft be hold to be preferable to valour and gold be find to be more irresistible than either lead or steel
this circumstance however as the lady do not think it material enough to relate to her friend we would not at that time impart it to the reader
we rather choose to leave he a while under a supposition that she have find or coin or by some very extraordinary perhaps supernatural mean have possess herself of the money with which she have bribe her keeper than to interrupt her narrative by give a hint of what seem to she of too little importance to be mention
the peer after a short conversation could not forbear express some surprize at meet the lady in that place nor could he refrain from tell she he imagine she have be go to bath
mrs fitzpatrick very freely answer that she have be prevent in her purpose by the arrival of a person she need not mention
in short say she i be overtake by my husband for i need not affect to conceal what the world know too well already
i have the good fortune to escape in a most surprizing manner and be now go to london with this young lady who be a near relation of mine and who hath escape from as great a tyrant as my own his lordship conclude that this tyrant be likewise a husband make a speech full of compliment to both the lady and as full of invective against his own sex nor indeed do he avoid some oblique glance at the matrimonial institution itself and at the unjust power give by it to man over the more sensible and more meritorious part of the specie
he end his oration with an offer of his protection and of his coach and six which be instantly accept by mrs fitzpatrick and at last upon her persuasion by sophia
matter be thus adjust his lordship take his leave and the lady retire to rest where mrs fitzpatrick entertain her cousin with many high encomium on the character of the noble peer and enlarge very particularly on his great fondness for his wife say she believe he be almost the only person of high rank who be entirely constant to the marriage bed
indeed add she my dear sophy that be a very rare virtue amongst man of condition
never expect it when you marry for believe i if you do you will certainly be deceive a gentle sigh stole from sophia at these word which perhaps contribute to form a dream of no very pleasant kind but as she never reveal this dream to any one so the reader cannot expect to see it relate here
chapter ix
the morning introduce in some pretty writing
a stagecoach
the civility of chambermaid
the heroic temper of sophia
her generosity
the return to it
the departure of the company and their arrival at london with some remark for the use of traveller
those member of society who be bear to furnish the blessing of life now begin to light their candle in order to pursue their daily labour for the use of those who be bear to enjoy these blessing
the sturdy hind now attend the levee of his fellow-labourer the ox the cunning artificer the diligent mechanic spring from their hard mattress and now the bonny housemaid begin to repair the disorder drum-room while the riotous author of that disorder in broken interrupted slumber tumble and toss as if the hardness of down disquiet their repose
in simple phrase the clock have no soon strike seven than the lady be ready for their journey and at their desire his lordship and his equipage be prepare to attend they
and now a matter of some difficulty arise and this be how his lordship himself should be convey for though in stage-coach where passenger be properly consider as so much luggage the ingenious coachman stow half a dozen with perfect ease into the place of four for well he contrive that the fat hostess or well-feed alderman may take up no more room than the slim miss or taper master it be the nature of gut when well squeeze to give way and to lie in a narrow compass yet in these vehicle which be call for distinction s sake gentleman s coach though they be often large than the other this method of packing be never attempt
his lordship would have put a short end to the difficulty by very gallantly desire to mount his horse but mrs fitzpatrick would by no mean consent to it
it be therefore conclude that the abigails should by turn relieve each other on one of his lordship s horse which be presently equip with a side-saddle for that purpose
everything be settle at the inn the lady discharge their former guide and sophia make a present to the landlord partly to repair the bruise which he have receive under herself and partly on account of what he have suffer under the hand of her enraged waiting-woman
and now sophia first discover a loss which give she some uneasiness and this be of the hundred-pound bank-bill which she father have give she at their last meeting and which within a very inconsiderable trifle be all the treasure she be at present worth
she search everywhere and shake and tumble all her thing to no purpose the bill be not to be find and she be at last fully persuade that she have lose it from her pocket when she have the misfortune of tumble from her horse in the dark lane as before record a fact that seem the more probable as she now recollect some discomposure in her pocket which have happen at that time and the great difficulty with which she have draw forth her handkerchief the very instant before her fall in order to relieve the distress of mrs fitzpatrick
misfortune of this kind whatever inconveniencie they may be attend with be incapable of subdue a mind in which there be any strength without the assistance of avarice
sophia therefore though nothing could be worse time than this accident at such a season immediately get the well of her concern and with her wonte serenity and cheerfulness of countenance return to her company
his lordship conduct the lady into the vehicle as he do likewise mrs honour who after many civility and more dear madam at last yield to the well-breed importunity of her sister abigail and submit to be compliment with the first ride in the coach in which indeed she would afterwards have be content to have pursue her whole journey have not her mistress after several fruitless intimation at length force she to take her turn on horseback
the coach now have receive its company begin to move forwards attend by many servant and lead by two captain who have before ride with his lordship and who would have be dismiss from the vehicle upon a much less worthy occasion than be this of accommodate two lady
in this they act only as gentleman but they be ready at any time to have perform the office of a footman or indeed would have condescend low for the honour of his lordship s company and for the convenience of his table
my landlord be so pleased with the present he have receive from sophia that he rather rejoice in than regret his bruise or his scratch
the reader will perhaps be curious to know the quantum of this present but we cannot satisfy his curiosity
whatever it be it satisfy the landlord for his bodily hurt but he lament he have not know before how little the lady value her money for to be sure say he one might have charge every article double and she would have make no cavil at the reckoning his wife however be far from draw this conclusion whether she really feel any injury do to her husband more than he do himself i will not say certain it be she be much less satisfied with the generosity of sophia
indeed cry she my dear the lady know well how to dispose of her money than you imagine
she might very well think we should not put up such a business without some satisfaction and the law would have cost she an infinite deal more than this poor little matter which i wonder you would take you be always so bloodily wise quoth the husband it would have cost she more would it
dost fancy i donot know that as well as thee
but would any of that more or so much have come into our pocket
indeed if son tom the lawyer have be alive i could have be glad to have put such a pretty business into his hand
he would have get a good picking out of it but i have no relation now who be a lawyer and why should i go to law for the benefit of stranger nay to be sure answer she you must know well i believe i do reply he
i fancy when money be to be get i can smell it out as well as another
everybody let i tell you would not have talk people out of this
mind that i say everybody would not have cajole this out of she mind that the wife then join in the applause of her husband s sagacity and thus end the short dialogue between they on this occasion
we will therefore take our leave of these good people and attend his lordship and his fair companion who make such good expedition that they perform a journey of ninety mile in two day and on the second evening arrive in london without have encounter any one adventure on the road worthy the dignity of this history to relate
our pen therefore shall imitate the expedition which it describe and our history shall keep pace with the traveller who be its subject
good writer will indeed do well to imitate the ingenious traveller in this instance who always proportion his stay at any place to the beauty elegancie and curiosity which it afford
at eshur at stowe at wilton at eastbury and at prior s park day be too short for the ravished imagination while we admire the wondrous power of art in improve nature
in some of these art chiefly engage our admiration in other nature and art contend for our applause but in the last the former seem to triumph
here nature appear in her rich attire and art dress with the modest simplicity attend her benignant mistress
here nature indeed pour forth the choice treasure which she hath lavish on this world and here human nature present you with an object which can be exceed only in the other
the same taste the same imagination which luxuriously riot in these elegant scene can be amuse with object of far inferior note
the wood the river the lawn of devon and of dorset attract the eye of the ingenious traveller and retard his pace which delay he afterwards compensate by swiftly scour over the gloomy heath of bagshot or that pleasant plain which extend itself westward from stockbridge where no other object than one single tree only in sixteen mile present itself to the view unless the cloud in compassion to our tired spirit kindly open their variegate mansion to our prospect
not so travel the money-meditate tradesman the sagacious justice the dignified doctor the warm-clothe grazier with all the numerous offspring of wealth and dulness
on they jog with equal pace through the verdant meadow or over the barren heath their horse measure four mile and a half per hour with the utmost exactness the eye of the beast and of his master be alike direct forwards and employ in contemplate the same object in the same manner
with equal rapture the good rider survey the proud boast of the architect and those fair building with which some unknown name hath adorn the rich cloathing town where heap of brick be pile up as a kind of monument to show that heap of money have be pile there before
and now reader as we be in haste to attend our heroine we will leave to thy sagacity to apply all this to the boeotian writer and to those author who be their opposite
this thou wilt be abundantly able to perform without our aid
bestir thyself therefore on this occasion for though we will always lend thee proper assistance in difficult place as we do not like some other expect thee to use the art of divination to discover our meaning yet we shall not indulge thy laziness where nothing but thy own attention be require for thou art highly mistaken if thou dost imagine that we intend when we begin this great work to leave thy sagacity nothing to do or that without sometimes exercise this talent thou wilt be able to travel through our page with any pleasure or profit to thyself
chapter x
contain a hint or two concern virtue and a few more concern suspicion
our company be arrive at london be set down at his lordship s house where while they refresh themselves after the fatigue of their journey servant be despatch to provide a lodging for the two lady for as her ladyship be not then in town mrs fitzpatrick would by no mean consent to accept a bed in the mansion of the peer
some reader will perhaps condemn this extraordinary delicacy as i may call it of virtue as too nice and scrupulous but we must make allowance for her situation which must be own to have be very ticklish and when we consider the malice of censorious tongue we must allow if it be a fault the fault be an excess on the right side and which every woman who be in the self-same situation will do well to imitate
the most formal appearance of virtue when it be only an appearance may perhaps in very abstracted consideration seem to be rather less commendable than virtue itself without this formality but it will however be always more commend and this i believe will be grant by all that it be necessary unless in some very particular case for every woman to support either the one or the other
a lodging be prepare sophia accompany her cousin for that evening but resolve early in the morning to enquire after the lady into whose protection as we have formerly mention she have determine to throw herself when she quit her father s house
and this she be the more eager in do from some observation she have make during her journey in the coach
now as we would by no mean fix the odious character of suspicion on sophia we be almost afraid to open to our reader the conceit which fill her mind concern mrs fitzpatrick of whom she certainly entertain at present some doubt which as they be very apt to enter into the bosom of the bad of people we think proper not to mention more plainly till we have first suggest a word or two to our reader touch suspicion in general
of this there have always appear to i to be two degree
the first of these i chuse to derive from the heart as the extreme velocity of its discernment seem to denote some previous inward impulse and the rather as this superlative degree often form its own object see what be not and always more than really exist
this be that quick-sighted penetration whose hawk s eye no symptom of evil can escape which observe not only upon the action but upon the word and look of man and as it proceed from the heart of the observer so it dive into the heart of the observe and there espy evil as it be in the first embryo nay sometimes before it can be say to be conceive
an admirable faculty if it be infallible but as this degree of perfection be not even claim by more than one mortal being so from the fallibility of such acute discernment have arise many sad mischief and most grievous heart-ache to innocence and virtue
i cannot help therefore regard this vast quick-sightedness into evil as a vicious excess and as a very pernicious evil in itself
and i be the more inclined to this opinion as i be afraid it always proceed from a bad heart for the reason i have above mention and for one more namely because i never know it the property of a good one
now from this degree of suspicion i entirely and absolutely acquit sophia
a second degree of this quality seem to arise from the head
this be indeed no other than the faculty of see what be before your eye and of draw conclusion from what you see
the former of these be unavoidable by those who have any eye and the latter be perhaps no less certain and necessary a consequence of our have any brain
this be altogether as bitter an enemy to guilt as the former be to innocence nor can i see it in an unamiable light even though through human fallibility it should be sometimes mistake
for instance if a husband should accidentally surprize his wife in the lap or in the embrace of some of those pretty young gentleman who profess the art of cuckold-making i should not highly i think blame he for conclude something more than what he see from the familiarity which he really have see and which we be at least favourable enough to when we call they innocent freedom
the reader will easily suggest great plenty of instance to himself i shall add but one more which however unchristian it may be think by some i cannot help esteem to be strictly justifiable and this be a suspicion that a man be capable of do what he hath do already and that it be possible for one who hath be a villain once to act the same part again
and to confess the truth of this degree of suspicion i believe sophia be guilty
from this degree of suspicion she have in fact conceive an opinion that her cousin be really not well than she should be
the case it seem be this mrs fitzpatrick wisely consider that the virtue of a young lady be in the world in the same situation with a poor hare which be certain whenever it venture abroad to meet its enemy for it can hardly meet any other
no soon therefore be she determined to take the first opportunity of quit the protection of her husband than she resolve to cast herself under the protection of some other man and whom could she so properly choose to be her guardian as a person of quality of fortune of honour and who besides a gallant disposition which incline man to knight-errantry that is to be the champion of lady in distress have often declare a violent attachment to herself and have already give she all the instance of it in his power
but as the law hath foolishly omit this office of vice-husband or guardian to an eloped lady and as malice be apt to denominate he by a more disagreeable appellation it be conclude that his lordship should perform all such kind office to the lady in secret and without publickly assume the character of her protector
nay to prevent any other person from see he in this light it be agree that the lady should proceed directly to bath and that his lordship should first go to london and thence should go down to that place by the advice of his physician
now all this sophia very plainly understand not from the lip or behaviour of mrs fitzpatrick but from the peer who be infinitely less expert at retain a secret than be the good lady and perhaps the exact secrecy which mrs fitzpatrick have observe on this head in her narrative serve not a little to heighten those suspicion which be now rise in the mind of her cousin
sophia very easily find out the lady she seek for indeed there be not a chairman in town to whom her house be not perfectly well know and as she receive in return of her first message a most pressing invitation she immediately accept it
mrs fitzpatrick indeed do not desire her cousin to stay with she with more earnestness than civility require
whether she have discern and resent the suspicion above-mention or from what other motive it arise i cannot say but certain it be she be full as desirous of part with sophia as sophia herself could be of go
the young lady when she come to take leave of her cousin could not avoid give she a short hint of advice
she beg she for heaven s sake to take care of herself and to consider in how dangerous a situation she stand add she hop some method would be find of reconcile she to her husband
you must remember my dear say she the maxim which my aunt western hath so often repeat to we both that whenever the matrimonial alliance be break and war declare between husband and wife she can hardly make a disadvantageous peace for herself on any condition
these be my aunt s very word and she hath have a great deal of experience in the world mrs fitzpatrick answer with a contemptuous smile never fear i child take care of yourself for you be young than i
i will come and visit you in a few day but dear sophy let i give you one piece of advice leave the character of graveairs in the country for believe i it will sit very awkwardly upon you in this town thus the two cousin part and sophia repair directly to lady bellaston where she find a most hearty as well as a most polite welcome
the lady have take a great fancy to she when she have see she formerly with her aunt western
she be indeed extremely glad to see she and be no soon acquaint with the reason which induce she to leave the squire and to fly to london than she highly applaud her sense and resolution and after express the high satisfaction in the opinion which sophia have declare she entertain of her ladyship by chuse her house for an asylum she promise she all the protection which it be in her power to give
as we have now bring sophia into safe hand the reader will i apprehend be content to deposit she there a while and to look a little after other personage and particularly poor jones whom we have leave long enough to do penance for his past offence which as be the nature of vice bring sufficient punishment upon he themselves
book xii
contain the same individual time with the former
chapter i
show what be to be deem plagiarism in a modern author and what be to be consider as lawful prize
the learned reader must have observe that in the course of this mighty work i have often translate passage out of the good antient author without quote the original or without take the least notice of the book from whence they be borrow
this conduct in writing be place in a very proper light by the ingenious abbé bannier in his preface to his mythology a work of great erudition and of equal judgment
it will be easy say he for the reader to observe that i have frequently have great regard to he than to my own reputation for an author certainly pay he a considerable compliment when for his sake he suppress learn quotation that come in his way and which would have cost he but the bare trouble of transcribe to fill up a work with these scrap may indeed be consider as a downright cheat on the learn world who be by such mean impose upon to buy a second time in fragment and by retail what they have already in gross if not in their memory upon their shelf and it be still more cruel upon the illiterate who be draw in to pay for what be of no manner of use to they
a writer who intermix great quantity of greek and latin with his work deal by the lady and fine gentleman in the same paultry manner with which they be treat by the auctioneer who often endeavour so to confound and mix up their lot that in order to purchase the commodity you want you be oblige at the same time to purchase that which will do you no service
and yet as there be no conduct so fair and disinterested but that it may be misunderstood by ignorance and misrepresent by malice i have be sometimes tempt to preserve my own reputation at the expense of my reader and to transcribe the original or at least to quote chapter and verse whenever i have make use either of the thought or expression of another
i be indeed in some doubt that i have often suffer by the contrary method and that by suppress the original author s name i have be rather suspect of plagiarism than repute to act from the amiable motive assign by that justly celebrated frenchman
now to obviate all such imputation for the future i do here confess and justify the fact
the antient may be consider as a rich common where every person who hath the small tenement in parnassus hath a free right to fatten his muse
or to place it in a clear light we modern be to the antient what the poor be to the rich
by the poor here i mean that large and venerable body which in english we call the mob
now whoever hath have the honour to be admit to any degree of intimacy with this mob must well know that it be one of their establish maxim to plunder and pillage their rich neighbour without any reluctance and that this be hold to be neither sin nor shame among they
and so constantly do they abide and act by this maxim that in every parish almost in the kingdom there be a kind of confederacy ever carry on against a certain person of opulence call the squire whose property be consider as free-booty by all his poor neighbour who as they conclude that there be no manner of guilt in such depredation look upon it as a point of honour and moral obligation to conceal and to preserve each other from punishment on all such occasion
in like manner be the antient such as homer virgil horace cicero and the rest to be esteem among us writer as so many wealthy squire from whom we the poor of parnassus claim an immemorial custom of take whatever we can come at
this liberty i demand and this i be as ready to allow again to my poor neighbour in their turn
all i profess and all i require of my brother be to maintain the same strict honesty among ourselves which the mob show to one another
to steal from one another be indeed highly criminal and indecent for this may be strictly stile defraud the poor sometimes perhaps those who be poor than ourselves or to set it under the most opprobrious colour rob the spittal
since therefore upon the strict examination my own conscience cannot lay any such pitiful theft to my charge i be content to plead guilty to the former accusation nor shall i ever scruple to take to myself any passage which i shall find in an antient author to my purpose without set down the name of the author from whence it be take
nay i absolutely claim a property in all such sentiment the moment they be transcribe into my writing and i expect all reader henceforward to regard they as purely and entirely my own
this claim however i desire to be allow i only on condition that i preserve strict honesty towards my poor brother from whom if ever i borrow any of that little of which they be possess i shall never fail to put their mark upon it that it may be at all time ready to be restore to the right owner
the omission of this be highly blameable in one mr moore who have formerly borrow some line of pope and company take the liberty to transcribe six of they into his play of the rival modes
mr pope however very luckily find they in the said play and lay violent hand on his own property transfer it back again into his own work and for a further punishment imprison the say moore in the loathsome dungeon of the dunciad where his unhappy memory now remain and eternally will remain as a proper punishment for such his unjust dealing in the poetical trade
chapter ii
in which though the squire doth not find his daughter something be find which put an end to his pursuit
the history now return to the inn at upton whence we shall first trace the footstep of squire western for as he will soon arrive at an end of his journey we shall have then full leisure to attend our heroe
the reader may be pleased to remember that the say squire depart from the inn in great fury and in that fury he pursue his daughter
the hostler have inform he that she have cross the severn he likewise past that river with his equipage and ride full speed vow the utmost vengeance against poor sophia if he should but overtake she
he have not go far before he arrive at a crossway
here he call a short council of war in which after hear different opinion he at last give the direction of his pursuit to fortune and strike directly into the worcester road
in this road he proceed about two mile when he begin to bemoan himself most bitterly frequently cry out what pity be it
sure never be so unlucky a dog as myself and then burst forth a volley of oath and execration
the parson attempt to administer comfort to he on this occasion
sorrow not sir say he like those without hope
howbeit we have not yet be able to overtake young madam we may account it some good fortune that we have hitherto trace her course aright
peradventure she will soon be fatigate with her journey and will tarry in some inn in order to renovate her corporeal function and in that case in all moral certainty you will very briefly be compos voti pogh
d--n the slut answer the squire i be lament the loss of so fine a morning for hunting
it be confound hard to lose one of the well scenting day in all appearance which hath be this season and especially after so long a frost whether fortune who now and then show some compassion in her wanton trick might not take pity of the squire and as she have determine not to let he overtake his daughter might not resolve to make he amend some other way i will not assert but he have hardly utter the word just before commemorate and two or three oath at their heel when a pack of hound begin to open their melodious throat at a small distance from they which the squire s horse and his rider both perceiving both immediately prick up their ear and the squire cry shebe go shebe go
damn i if she be not go instantly clap spur to the beast who little need it have indeed the same inclination with his master and now the whole company cross into a corn-field ride directly towards the hound with much hallowing and whooping while the poor parson blessing himself bring up the rear
thus fable report that the fair grimalkin whom venus at the desire of a passionate lover convert from a cat into a fine woman no soon perceive a mouse than mindful of her former sport and still retain her pristine nature she leap from the bed of her husband to pursue the little animal
what be we to understand by this
not that the bride be displeased with the embrace of her amorous bridegroom for though some have remark that cat be subject to ingratitude yet woman and cat too will be pleased and purr on certain occasion
the truth be as the sagacious sir roger l estrange observe in his deep reflection that if we shut nature out at the door she will come in at the window and that puss though a madam will be a mouser still in the same manner we be not to arraign the squire of any want of love for his daughter for in reality he have a great deal we be only to consider that he be a squire and a sportsman and then we may apply the fable to he and the judicious reflection likewise
the hound run very hard as it be call and the squire pursue over hedge and ditch with all his usual vociferation and alacrity and with all his usual pleasure nor do the thought of sophia ever once intrude themselves to allay the satisfaction he enjoy in the chace which he say be one of the fine he ever see and which he swear be very well worth go fifty mile for
as the squire forget his daughter the servant we may easily believe forget their mistress and the parson after have express much astonishment in latin to himself at length likewise abandon all farth thought of the young lady and jog on at a distance behind begin to meditate a portion of doctrine for the ensue sunday
the squire who own the hound be highly pleased with the arrival of his brother squire and sportsman for all man approve merit in their own way and no man be more expert in the field than mr western nor do any other well know how to encourage the dog with his voice and to animate the hunt with his holla
sportsman in the warmth of a chace be too much engage to attend to any manner of ceremony nay even to the office of humanity for if any of they meet with an accident by tumble into a ditch or into a river the rest pass on regardless and generally leave he to his fate during this time therefore the two squire though often close to each other interchange not a single word
the master of the hunt however often see and approve the great judgment of the stranger in draw the dog when they be at a fault and hence conceive a very high opinion of his understanding as the number of his attendant inspire no small reverence to his quality
as soon therefore as the sport be end by the death of the little animal which have occasion it the two squire meet and in all squire-like greeting salute each other
the conversation be entertain enough and what we may perhaps relate in an appendix or on some other occasion but as it nowise concern this history we cannot prevail on ourselves to give it a place here
it conclude with a second chace and that with an invitation to dinner
this be accept be follow by a hearty bout of drinking which end in as hearty a nap on the part of squire western
our squire be by no mean a match either for his host or for parson supple at his cup that evening for which the violent fatigue of mind as well as body that he have undergo may very well account without the least derogation from his honour
he be indeed accord to the vulgar phrase whistle drunk for before he have swallow the third bottle he become so entirely overpowered that though he be not carry off to bed till long after the parson consider he as absent and have acquaint the other squire with all relate to sophia he obtain his promise of second those argument which he intend to urge the next morning for mr western s return
no soon therefore have the good squire shake off his evening and begin to call for his morning draught and to summon his horse in order to renew his pursuit than mr supple begin his dissuasive which the host so strongly seconded that they at length prevail and mr western agree to return home be principally move by one argument viz that he know not which way to go and might probably be rid far from his daughter instead of towards she
he then take leave of his brother sportsman and express great joy that the frost be break which might perhaps be no small motive to his hastening home set forwards or rather backwards for somersetshire but not before he have first despatch part of his retinue in quest of his daughter after whom he likewise send a volley of the most bitter execration which he could invent
chapter iii
the departure of jones from upton with what pass between he and partridge on the road
at length we be once more come to our heroe and to say truth we have be oblige to part with he so long that consider the condition in which we leave he i apprehend many of our reader have conclude we intend to abandon he for ever he be at present in that situation in which prudent people usually desist from enquire any far after their friend lest they should be shock by hear such friend have hang themselves
but in reality if we have not all the virtue i will boldly say neither have we all the vice of a prudent character and though it be not easy to conceive circumstance much more miserable than those of poor jones at present we shall return to he and attend upon he with the same diligence as if he be wanton in the bright beam of fortune
mr jones then and his companion partridge leave the inn a few minute after the departure of squire western and pursue the same road on foot for the hostler tell they that no horse be by any mean to be at that time procure at upton
on they march with heavy heart for though their disquiet proceed from very different reason yet displeased they be both and if jones sigh bitterly partridge grunt altogether as sadly at every step
when they come to the cross-road where the squire have stopt to take counsel jones stopt likewise and turn to partridge ask his opinion which track they should pursue
ah sir answer partridge i wish your honour would follow my advice why should i not reply jones for it be now indifferent to i whither i go or what become of i my advice then say partridge be that you immediately face about and return home for who that hath such a home to return to as your honour would travel thus about the country like a vagabond
i ask pardon se vox ea sola reperta est alas cry jones i have no home to return to but if my friend my father would receive i could i bear the country from which sophia be fly
cruel sophia
cruel
no let i blame myself no let i blame thee
d--nation seize thee--fool--blockhead
thou hast undo i and i will tear thy soul from thy body at which word he lay violent hand on the collar of poor partridge and shake he more heartily than an ague-fit or his own fear have ever do before
partridge fall tremble on his knee and beg for mercy vow he have mean no harm--when jones after star wildly on he for a moment quit his hold and discharge a rage on himself that have it fall on the other would certainly have put an end to his being which indeed the very apprehension of it have almost effect
we would bestow some pain here in minutely describe all the mad prank which jones play on this occasion could we be well assure that the reader would take the same pain in peruse they but as we be apprehensive that after all the labour which we should employ in paint this scene the say reader would be very apt to skip it entirely over we have save ourselves that trouble
to say the truth we have from this reason alone often do great violence to the luxuriance of our genius and have leave many excellent description out of our work which would otherwise have be in it
and this suspicion to be honest arise as be generally the case from our own wicked heart for we have ourselves be very often most horridly give to jumping as we have run through the page of voluminous historian
suffice it then simply to say that jones after have play the part of a madman for many minute come by degree to himself which no soon happen than turn to partridge he very earnestly beg his pardon for the attack he have make on he in the violence of his passion but conclude by desire he never to mention his return again for he be resolve never to see that country any more
partridge easily forgave and faithfully promise to obey the injunction now lay upon he
and then jones very briskly cry out since it be absolutely impossible for i to pursue any far the step of my angel--i will pursue those of glory
come on my brave lad now for the army it be a glorious cause and i would willingly sacrifice my life in it even though it be worth my preserving and so say he immediately strike into the different road from that which the squire have take and by mere chance pursue the very same through which sophia have before pass
our traveller now march a full mile without speak a syllable to each other though jones indeed mutter many thing to himself
as to partridge he be profoundly silent for he be not perhaps perfectly recover from his former fright besides he have apprehension of provoke his friend to a second fit of wrath especially as he now begin to entertain a conceit which may not perhaps create any great wonder in the reader
in short he begin now to suspect that jones be absolutely out of his sens
at length jones be weary of soliloquy address himself to his companion and blame he for his taciturnity for which the poor man very honestly account from his fear of give offence
and now this fear be pretty well remove by the most absolute promise of indemnity partridge again take the bridle from his tongue which perhaps rejoice no less at regain its liberty than a young colt when the bridle be slipt from his neck and he be turn loose into the pasture
as partridge be inhibit from that topic which would have first suggest itself he fall upon that which be next uppermost in his mind namely the man of the hill
certainly sir say he that could never be a man who dress himself and live after such a strange manner and so unlike other folk
besides his diet as the old woman tell i be chiefly upon herb which be a fitter food for a horse than a christian nay landlord at upton say that the neighbour thereabout have very fearful notion about he
it run strangely in my head that it must have be some spirit who perhaps might be send to forewarn we and who know but all that matter which he tell we of his go to fight and of his be take prisoner and of the great danger he be in of be hang might be intend as a warning to we consider what we be go about
besides i dream of nothing all last night but of fight and methought the blood run out of my nose as liquor out of a tap
indeed sir infandum regina jubes renovare dolorem thy story partridge answer jones be almost as ill apply as thy latin
nothing can be more likely to happen than death to man who go into battle
perhaps we shall both fall in it--and what then what then reply partridge why then there be an end of we be there not
when i be go all be over with i
what matter the cause to i or who get the victory if i be kill
i shall never enjoy any advantage from it
what be all the ringing of bell and bonfire to one that be six foot under ground
there will be an end of poor partridge and an end of poor partridge cry jones there must be one time or other
if you love latin i will repeat you some fine line out of horace which would inspire courage into a coward
dulce et decorum est pro patria mori mors et fugacem persequitur virum nec parcit imbellis juventae poplitibus timidoque tergo i wish you would construe they cry partridge for horace be a hard author and i cannot understand as you repeat they i will repeat you a bad imitation or rather paraphrase of my own say jones for i be but an indifferent poet who would not die in his dear country s cause
since if base fear his dastard step withdraw from death he cannot fly one common grave receives at last the coward and the brave thatbe very certain cry partridge
ay sure mor omnibus communis but there be a great difference between die in one s bed a great many year hence like a good christian with all our friend cry about we and be shoot to-day or to-morrow like a mad dog or perhaps hack in twenty piece with the sword and that too before we have repent of all our sin
o lord have mercy upon we
to be sure the soldier be a wicked kind of people
i never love to have anything to do with they
i could hardly bring myself ever to look upon they as christians
there be nothing but curse and swearing among they
i wish your honour would repent i heartily wish you would repent before it be too late and not think of go among them evil communication corrupt good manner
that be my principal reason
for as for that matter i be no more afraid than another man not i as to matter of that
i know all human flesh must die but yet a man may live many year for all that
why i be a middle-aged man now and yet i may live a great number of year
i have read of several who have live to be above a hundred and some a great deal above a hundred
not that i hope i mean that i promise myself to live to any such age as that neither but if it be only to eighty or ninety
heaven be praise that be a great way off yet and i be not afraid of die then no more than another man but surely to tempt death before a man s time be come seem to i downright wickedness and presumption
besides if it be to do any good indeed but let the cause be what it will what mighty matter of good can two people do
and for my part i understand nothing of it
i never fire off a gun above ten time in my life and then it be not charge with bullet
and for the sword i never learn to fence and know nothing of the matter
and then there be those cannon which certainly it must be think the high presumption to go in the way of and nobody but a madman--i ask pardon upon my soul i mean no harm i beg i may not throw your honour into another passion be under no apprehension partridge cry jones i be now so well convinced of thy cowardice that thou couldst not provoke i on any account your honour answer he may call i coward or anything else you please
if love to sleep in a whole skin make a man a coward non immune ab illis malis sumus
i never read in my grammar that a man cannot be a good man without fight
vir bonus est quis
qui consulta patrum qui lege juraque servat
not a word of fight and i be sure the scripture be so much against it that a man shall never persuade i he be a good christian while he shed christian blood chapter iv
the adventure of a beggar-man
just as partridge have utter that good and pious doctrine with which the last chapter conclude they arrive at another cross-way when a lame fellow in rag ask they for alm upon which partridge give he a severe rebuke say every parish ought to keep their own poor jones then fall a-laugh and ask partridge if he be not ashamed with so much charity in his mouth to have no charity in his heart
your religion say he serve you only for an excuse for your fault but be no incentive to your virtue

master cry the fellow after thank he i have a curious thing here in my pocket which i find about two mile off if your worship will please to buy it
i should not venture to pull it out to every one but as you be so good a gentleman and so kind to the poor you willnot suspect a man of be a thief only because he be poor he then pull out a little gilt pocket-book and deliver it into the hand of jones
jones presently open it and guess reader what he feel see in the first page the word sophia western write by her own fair hand

it be indeed the very bill which western have give his daughter the night before her departure and a jew would have jump to purchase it at five shilling less than 100
the eye of partridge sparkle at this news which jones now proclaim aloud and so do though with somewhat a different aspect those of the poor fellow who have find the book and who i hope from a principle of honesty have never open it but we should not deal honestly by the reader if we omit to inform he of a circumstance which may be here a little material viz
that the fellow could not read

the pocket-book be a late present from mrs western to her niece it have cost five-and-twenty shilling have be buy of a celebrated toyman but the real value of the silver which it contain in its clasp be about eighteen-pence and that price the said toyman as it be altogether as good as when it first issue from his shop would now have give for it
a prudent person would however have take proper advantage of the ignorance of this fellow and would not have offer more than a shilling or perhaps sixpence for it nay some perhaps would have give nothing and leave the fellow to his action of trover which some learn serjeant may doubt whether he could under these circumstance have maintain

at all which the guide expr some sign of astonishment to partridge who more than once shake his head and cry poor gentleman
orandum est ut sit men sana in corpore sano at length they arrive at the very spot where sophia unhappily dropt the pocket-book and where the fellow have as happily find it

and will restore it she nay your worship answer the fellow may do as you please as to that if you will but give i my share that is one-half of the money your honour may keep the rest yourself if you have know the value of these matter as well as other people chapter v
contain more adventure which mr jones and his companion meet on the road

he here therefore stopt to consider which of these road he should pursue when on a sudden they hear the noise of a drum that seem at no great distance

for heaven s sake sir donot affront they if they should come and perhaps they may do we no harm but would it not be the wise way to creep into some of yonder bush till they be go by
what can two unarmed man do perhaps against fifty thousand
certainly nobody but a madman i hope your honour be not offend but certainly no man who hath mens sana in corpore sano here jones interrupt this torrent of eloquence which fear have inspire say that by the drum he perceive they be near some town he then make directly towards the place whence the noise proceed bid partridge take courage for that he would lead he into no danger and add it be impossible the rebel should be so near partridge be a little comfort with this last assurance and though he would more gladly have go the contrary way he follow his leader his heart beat time but not after the manner of hero to the music of the drum which cease not till they have traverse the common and be come into a narrow lane
and now partridge who keep even pace with jones discover something paint fly in the air a very few yard before he which fancy to be the colour of the enemy he fall a bellowing oh lord sir here they be there be the crown and coffin
oh lord
i never see anything so terrible and we be within gun-shot of they already jones no soon look up than he plainly perceive what it be which partridge have thus mistake
partridge say he i fancy you will be able to engage this whole army yourself for by the colour i guess what the drum be which we hear before and which beat up for recruit to a puppet-show a puppet-show answer partridge with most eager transport
and be it really no more than that
i love a puppet-show of all the pastime upon earth
do good sir let we tarry and see it
besides i be quite famished to death for it be now almost dark and i have not eat a morsel since three o clock in the morning they now arrive at an inn or indeed an ale-house where jones be prevail upon to stop the rather as he have no long any assurance of be in the road he desire
they walk both directly into the kitchen where jones begin to enquire if no lady have pass that way in the morning and partridge as eagerly examine into the state of their provision and indeed his enquiry meet with the well success for jones could not hear news of sophia but partridge to his great satisfaction find good reason to expect very shortly the agreeable sight of an excellent smoaking dish of egg and bacon
in strong and healthy constitution love hath a very different effect from what it cause in the puny part of the specie
in the latter it generally destroy all that appetite which tend towards the conservation of the individual but in the former though it often induce forgetfulness and a neglect of food as well as of everything else yet place a good piece of well-powder buttock before a hungry lover and he seldom fail very handsomely to play his part
thus it happen in the present case for though jones perhaps want a prompter and might have travel much far have he be alone with an empty stomach yet no soon do he sit down to the bacon and egg than he fall to as heartily and voraciously as partridge himself
before our traveller have finish their dinner night come on and as the moon be now past the full it be extremely dark
partridge therefore prevail on jones to stay and see the puppet-show which be just go to begin and to which they be very eagerly invite by the master of the say show who declare that his figure be the fine which the world have ever produce and that they have give great satisfaction to all the quality in every town in england
the puppet-show be perform with great regularity and decency
it be call the fine and serious part of the provoked husband and it be indeed a very grave and solemn entertainment without any low wit or humour or jest or to do it no more than justice without anything which could provoke a laugh
the audience be all highly pleased
a grave matron tell the master she would bring her two daughter the next night as he do not show any stuff and an attorney s clerk and an exciseman both declare that the character of lord and lady townley be well preserve and highly in nature
partridge likewise concur with this opinion
the master be so highly elated with these encomium that he could not refrain from add some more of his own
he say the present age be not improve in anything so much as in their puppet-show which by throw out punch and his wife joan and such idle trumpery be at last bring to be a rational entertainment
i remember say he when i first take to the business there be a great deal of low stuff that do very well to make folk laugh but be never calculate to improve the moral of young people which certainly ought to be principally aim at in every puppet-show for why may not good and instructive lesson be convey this way as well as any other
my figure be as big as the life and they represent the life in every particular and i question not but people rise from my little drama as much improve as they do from the great i would by no mean degrade the ingenuity of your profession answer jones but i should have be glad to have see my old acquaintance master punch for all that and so far from improve i think by leave out he and his merry wife joan you have spoil your puppet-show the dancer of wire conceive an immediate and high contempt for jones from these word
and with much disdain in his countenance he reply very probably sir that may be your opinion but i have the satisfaction to know the good judge differ from you and it be impossible to please every taste
i confess indeed some of the quality at bath two or three year ago want mightily to bring punch again upon the stage
i believe i lose some money for not agree to it but let other do as they will a little matter shall never bribe i to degrade my own profession nor will i ever willingly consent to the spoil the decency and regularity of my stage by introduce any such low stuff upon it right friend cry the clerk you be very right
always avoid what be low
there be several of my acquaintance in london who be resolve to drive everything which be low from the stage nothing can be more proper cry the exciseman pull his pipe from his mouth
i remember add he for i then live with my lord i be in the footman s gallery the night when this play of the provoked husband be act first
there be a great deal of low stuff in it about a country gentleman come up to town to stand for parliament-man and there they bring a parcel of his servant upon the stage his coachman i remember particularly but the gentleman in our gallery could not bear anything so low and they damn it
i observe friend you have leave all that matter out and you be to be commend for it nay gentleman cry jones i can never maintain my opinion against so many indeed if the generality of his audience dislike he the learn gentleman who conduct the show might have do very right in dismiss punch from his service the master of the show then begin a second harangue and say much of the great force of example and how much the inferior part of mankind would be deter from vice by observe how odious it be in their superior when he be unluckily interrupt by an incident which though perhaps we might have omit it at another time we cannot help relate at present but not in this chapter
chapter vi
from which it may be infer that the good thing be liable to be misunderstood and misinterpret
a violent uproar now arise in the entry where my landlady be well cuff she maid both with her fist and tongue
she have indeed miss the wench from her employment and after a little search have find she on the puppet-show stage in company with the merry andrew and in a situation not very proper to be describe
though grace for that be her name have forfeit all title to modesty yet have she not impudence enough to deny a fact in which she be actually surprize she therefore take another turn and attempt to mitigate the offence
why do you beat i in this manner mistress cry the wench
if you donot like my doing you may turn i away
if i be a w--e for the other have liberally bestow that appellation on she my better be so as well as i
what be the fine lady in the puppet-show just now
i suppose she do not lie all night out from her husband for nothing the landlady now burst into the kitchen and fall foul on both her husband and the poor puppet-mover
here husband say she you see the consequence of harbour these people in your house
if one doth draw a little drink the more for they one be hardly make amend for the litter they make and then to have one s house make a bawdy-house of by such lousy vermin
in short i desire you would be go to-morrow morning for i will tolerate no more such doing
it be only the way to teach our servant idleness and nonsense for to be sure nothing well can be learn by such idle show as these
i remember when puppet-show be make of good scripture story as jephthah s rash vow and such good thing and when wicked people be carry away by the devil
there be some sense in those matter but as the parson tell we last sunday nobody believe in the devil now-a-day and here you bring about a parcel of puppet drest up like lord and lady only to turn the head of poor country wench and when their head be once turn topsy-turvy no wonder everything else be so virgil i think tell we that when the mob be assemble in a riotous and tumultuous manner and all sort of missile weapon fly about if a man of gravity and authority appear amongst they the tumult be presently appease and the mob which when collect into one body may be well compare to an ass erect their long ear at the grave man s discourse
on the contrary when a set of grave man and philosopher be dispute when wisdom herself may in a manner be consider as present and administer argument to the disputant should a tumult arise among the mob or should one scold who be herself equal in noise to a mighty mob appear among the say philosopher their dispute cease in a moment wisdom no long perform her ministerial office and the attention of every one be immediately attract by the scold alone
thus the uproar aforesaid and the arrival of the landlady silence the master of the puppet-show and put a speedy and final end to that grave and solemn harangue of which we have give the reader a sufficient taste already
nothing indeed could have happen so very inopportune as this accident the most wanton malice of fortune could not have contrive such another stratagem to confound the poor fellow while he be so triumphantly descant on the good moral inculcate by his exhibition
his mouth be now as effectually stopt as that of quack must be if in the midst of a declamation on the great virtue of his pill and powder the corpse of one of his martyr should be bring forth and deposit before the stage as a testimony of his skill
instead therefore of answer my landlady the puppet-show man run out to punish his merry andrew and now the moon begin to put forth her silver light as the poet call it though she look at that time more like a piece of copper jones call for his reckoning and order partridge whom my landlady have just awake from a profound nap to prepare for his journey but partridge have lately carry two point as my reader hath see before be embolden to attempt a third which be to prevail with jones to take up a lodging that evening in the house where he then be
he introduce this with an affected surprize at the intention which mr jones declare of remove and after urge many excellent argument against it he at last insist strongly that it could be to no manner of purpose whatever for that unless jones know which way the lady be go every step he take might very possibly lead he the far from she for you find sir say he by all the people in the house that she be not go this way
how much well therefore would it be to stay till the morning when we may expect to meet with somebody to enquire of this last argument have indeed some effect on jones and while he be weigh it the landlord throw all the rhetoric of which he be master into the same scale
sure sir say he your servant give you most excellent advice for who would travel by night at this time of the year he then begin in the usual stile to trumpet forth the excellent accommodation which his house afford and my landlady likewise open on the occasion----but not to detain the reader with what be common to every host and hostess it be sufficient to tell he jones be at last prevail on to stay and refresh himself with a few hour rest which indeed he very much want for he have hardly shut his eye since he have leave the inn where the accident of the break head have happen
as soon as jones have take a resolution to proceed no far that night he presently retire to rest with his two bedfellow the pocket-book and the muff but partridge who at several time have refresh himself with several nap be more inclined to eat than to sleep and more to drink than to either
and now the storm which grace have raise be at an end and my landlady be again reconcile to the puppet-man who on his side forgave the indecent reflection which the good woman in her passion have cast on his performance a face of perfect peace and tranquillity reign in the kitchen where sit assemble round the fire the landlord and landlady of the house the master of the puppet-show the attorney s clerk the exciseman and the ingenious mr partridge in which company past the agreeable conversation which will be find in the next chapter
chapter vii
contain a remark or two of our own and many more of the good company assemble in the kitchen
though the pride of partridge do not submit to acknowledge himself a servant yet he condescend in most particular to imitate the manner of that rank
