one instance of this be his greatly magnify the fortune of his companion as he call jones such be a general custom with all servant among stranger as none of they would willingly be think the attendant on a beggar for the high the situation of the master be the high consequently be that of the man in his own opinion the truth of which observation appear from the behaviour of all the footman of the nobility
but though title and fortune communicate a splendor all around they and the footman of man of quality and of estate think themselves entitle to a part of that respect which be pay to the quality and estate of their master it be clearly otherwise with regard to virtue and understanding
these advantage be strictly personal and swallow themselves all the respect which be pay to they
to say the truth this be so very little that they cannot well afford to let any other partake with they
as these therefore reflect no honour on the domestic so neither be he at all dishonour by the most deplorable want of both in his master
indeed it be otherwise in the want of what be call virtue in a mistress the consequence of which we have before see for in this dishonour there be a kind of contagion which like that of poverty communicate itself to all who approach it
now for these reason we be not to wonder that servant i mean among the man only should have so great regard for the reputation of the wealth of their master and little or none at all for their character in other point and that though they would be ashamed to be the footman of a beggar they be not so to attend upon a rogue or a blockhead and do consequently make no scruple to spread the fame of the iniquity and folly of their say master as far as possible and this often with great humour and merriment
in reality a footman be often a wit as well as a beau at the expence of the gentleman whose livery he wear
after partridge therefore have enlarge greatly on the vast fortune to which mr jones be heir he very freely communicate an apprehension which he have begin to conceive the day before and for which as we hint at that very time the behaviour of jones seem to have furnish a sufficient foundation
in short he be now pretty well confirm in an opinion that his master be out of his wit with which opinion he very bluntly acquaint the good company round the fire
with this sentiment the puppet-show man immediately coincide
i own say he the gentleman surprize i very much when he talk so absurdly about puppet-show
it be indeed hardly to be conceive that any man in his sens should be so much mistaken what you say now account very well for all his monstrous notion
poor gentleman
i be heartily concerned for he indeed he hath a strange wildness about his eye which i take notice of before though i do not mention it the landlord agree with this last assertion and likewise claim the sagacity of have observe it
and certainly add he it must be so for no one but a madman would have think of leave so good a house to ramble about the country at that time of night the exciseman pull his pipe from his mouth say he think the gentleman look and talk a little wildly and then turn to partridge if he be a madman say he he should not be suffer to travel thus about the country for possibly he may do some mischief
it be a pity he be not secure and send home to his relation now some conceit of this kind be likewise lurk in the mind of partridge for as he be now persuade that jones have run away from mr allworthy he promise himself the high reward if he could by any mean convey he back
but fear of jones of whose fierceness and strength he have see and indeed feel some instance have however represent any such scheme as impossible to be execute and have discourage he from apply himself to form any regular plan for the purpose
but no soon do he hear the sentiment of the exciseman than he embrace that opportunity of declare his own and express a hearty wish that such a matter could be bring about
could be bring about say the exciseman why there be nothing easy ah
sir answer partridge you donot know what a devil of a fellow he be
he can take i up with one hand and throw i out at window and he would too if he do but imagine-- pogh say the exciseman i believe i be as good a man as he
besides here be five of we i donot know what five cry the landlady my husband shall have nothing to do in it
nor shall any violent hand be lay upon anybody in my house
the young gentleman be as pretty a young gentleman as ever i see in my life and i believe he be no more mad than any of we
what do you tell of his have a wild look with his eye
they be the prettiest eye i ever see and he hath the prettiest look with they and a very modest civil young man he be
i be sure i have bepitie he heartily ever since the gentleman there in the corner tell we he be crost in love
certainly that be enough to make any man especially such a sweet young gentleman as he be to look a little otherwise than he do before
lady indeed
what the devil would the lady have well than such a handsome man with a great estate
i suppose she be one of your quality folk one of your townly lady that we see last night in the puppet-show who donot know what they would be at the attorney s clerk likewise declare he would have no concern in the business without the advice of counsel
suppose say he an action of false imprisonment should be bring against we what defence could we make
who know what may be sufficient evidence of madness to a jury
but i only speak upon my own account for it donot look well for a lawyer to be concern in these matter unless it be as a lawyer
jury be always less favourable to we than to other people
i donot therefore dissuade you mr thomson to the exciseman nor the gentleman nor anybody else the exciseman shake his head at this speech and the puppet-show man say madness be sometimes a difficult matter for a jury to decide for i remember say he i be once present at a tryal of madness where twenty witness swear that the person be as mad as a march hare and twenty other that he be as much in his sens as any man in england and indeed it be the opinion of most people that it be only a trick of his relation to rob the poor man of his right very likely cry the landlady
i myself know a poor gentleman who be keep in a mad-house all his life by his family and they enjoy his estate but it do they no good for though the law give it they it be the right of another pogh cry the clerk with great contempt who hath any right but what the law give they
if the law give i the good estate in the country i should never trouble myself much who have the right if it be so say partridge felix quem faciunt aliena pericula cautum my landlord who have be call out by the arrival of a horseman at the gate now return into the kitchen and with an affrighted countenance cry out what do you think gentleman
the rebel have give the duke the slip and be get almost to london
it be certainly true for a man on horseback just now tell i so i be glad of it with all my heart cry partridge then there will be no fighting in these part i be glad cry the clerk for a well reason for i would always have right take place ay but answer the landlord i have hear some people say this man hath no right i will prove the contrary in a moment cry the clerk if my father die seize of a right do you mind i seize of a right i say doth not that right descend to his son and doth not one right descend as well as another but how can he have any right to make we papishe say the landlord
never fear that cry partridge
as to the matter of right the gentleman there hath prove it as clear as the sun and as to the matter of religion it be quite out of the case
the papist themselves donot expect any such thing
a popish priest whom i know very well and who be a very honest man tell i upon his word and honour they have no such design and another priest of my acquaintance say the landlady hath tell i the same thing but my husband be always so afraid of papishe
i know a great many papishe that be very honest sort of people and spend their money very freely and it be always a maxim with i that one man s money be as good as another s very true mistress say the puppet-show man i donot care what religion come provide the presbyterians be not uppermost for they be enemy to puppet-show and so you would sacrifice your religion to your interest cry the exciseman and be desirous to see popery bring in be you not i truly answer the other i hate popery as much as any man but yet it be a comfort to one that one should be able to live under it which i could not do among presbyterians
to be sure every man value his livelihood first that must be grant and i warrant if you would confess the truth you be more afraid of lose your place than anything else but never fear friend there will be an excise under another government as well as under this why certainly reply the exciseman i should be a very ill man if i do not honour the king whose bread i eat
that be no more than natural as a man may say for what signify it to i that there would be an excise-office under another government since my friend would be out and i could expect no well than to follow they
no no friend i shall never be bubble out of my religion in hope only of keep my place under another government for i should certainly be no well and very probably might be bad why that be what i say cry the landlord whenever folk say who know what may happen
odsook
should not i be a blockhead to lend my money to i know not who because mayhap he may return it again
i be sure it be safe in my own bureau and there i will keep it the attorney s clerk have take a great fancy to the sagacity of partridge
whether this proceed from the great discernment which the former have into man as well as thing or whether it arise from the sympathy between their mind for they be both truly jacobites in principle they now shake hand heartily and drink bumper of strong beer to health which we think proper to bury in oblivion
these health be afterwards pledge by all present and even by my landlord himself though reluctantly but he could not withstand the menace of the clerk who swear he would never set his foot within his house again if he refuse
the bumper which be swallow on this occasion soon put an end to the conversation
here therefore we will put an end to the chapter
chapter viii
in which fortune seem to have be in a well humour with jones than we have hitherto see she
as there be no wholesomer so perhaps there be few strong sleep potion than fatigue
of this jones might be say to have take a very large dose and it operate very forcibly upon he
he have already sleep nine hour and might perhaps have sleep long have he not be awaken by a most violent noise at his chamber-door where the sound of many heavy blow be accompany with many exclamation of murder
jones presently leapt from his bed where he find the master of the puppet-show belabour the back and rib of his poor merry-andrew without either mercy or moderation
jones instantly interpose on behalf of the suffer party and pin the insulting conqueror up to the wall for the puppet-show man be no more able to contend with jones than the poor party-colour jester have be to contend with this puppet-man
but though the merry-andrew be a little fellow and not very strong he have nevertheless some choler about he
he therefore no soon find himself deliver from the enemy than he begin to attack he with the only weapon at which he be his equal
from this he first discharge a volley of general abusive word and thence proceed to some particular accusations-- d--n your bl--d you rascal say he i have not only support you for to i you owe all the money you get but i have save you from the gallow
do you not want to rob the lady of her fine riding-habit no long ago than yesterday in the back-lane here
can you deny that you wish to have she alone in a wood to strip she--to strip one of the prettiest lady that ever be see in the world
and here you have fall upon i and have almost murder i for do no harm to a girl as willing as myself only because she like i well than you jones no soon hear this than he quit the master lay on he at the same time the most violent injunction of forbearance from any further insult on the merry-andrew and then take the poor wretch with he into his own apartment he soon learn tiding of his sophia whom the fellow as he be attend his master with his drum the day before have see pass by
he easily prevail with the lad to show he the exact place and then have summon partridge he depart with the utmost expedition
it be almost eight of the clock before all matter could be get ready for his departure for partridge be not in any haste nor could the reckoning be presently adjust and when both these be settle and over jones would not quit the place before he have perfectly reconcile all difference between the master and the man
when this be happily accomplish he set forwards and be by the trusty merry-andrew conduct to the spot by which sophia have past and then have handsomely reward his conductor he again push on with the utmost eagerness be highly delighted with the extraordinary manner in which he receive his intelligence
of this partridge be no soon acquaint than he with great earnestness begin to prophesy and assure jones that he would certainly have good success in the end for he say two such accident could never have happen to direct he after his mistress if providence have not design to bring they together at last and this be the first time that jones lend any attention to the superstitious doctrine of his companion
they have not go above two mile when a violent storm of rain overtake they and as they happen to be at the same time in sight of an ale-house partridge with much earnest entreaty prevail with jones to enter and weather the storm
hunger be an enemy if indeed it may be call one which partake more of the english than of the french disposition for though you subdue this never so often it will always rally again in time and so it do with partridge who be no soon arrive within the kitchen than he begin to ask the same question which he have ask the night before
the consequence of this be an excellent cold chine be produce upon the table upon which not only partridge but jones himself make a very hearty breakfast though the latter begin to grow again uneasy as the people of the house could give he no fresh information concern sophia
their meal be over jones be again prepare to sally notwithstanding the violence of the storm still continue but partridge beg heartily for another mug and at last cast his eye on a lad at the fire who have enter into the kitchen and who at that instant be look as earnestly at he he turn suddenly to jones and cry master give i your hand a single mug shallnot serve the turn this bout
why herebe more news of madam sophia come to town
the boy there stand by the fire be the very lad that ride before she
i can swear to my own plaister on his face heaven bless you sir cry the boy it be your own plaister sure enough i shall have always reason to remember your goodness for it hath almost cure i at these word jones start from his chair and bid the boy follow he immediately depart from the kitchen into a private apartment for so delicate be he with regard to sophia that he never willingly mention her name in the presence of many people and though he have as it be from the overflowings of his heart give sophia as a toast among the officer where he think it be impossible she should be know yet even there the reader may remember how difficultly he be prevail upon to mention her surname
hard therefore be it and perhaps in the opinion of many sagacious reader very absurd and monstrous that he should principally owe his present misfortune to the suppose want of that delicacy with which he so abound for in reality sophia be much more offended at the freedom which she think and not without good reason he have take with her name and character than at any freedom in which under his present circumstance he have indulge himself with the person of another woman and to say truth i believe honour could never have prevail on she to leave upton without her see jones have it not be for those two strong instance of a levity in his behaviour so void of respect and indeed so highly inconsistent with any degree of love and tenderness in great and delicate mind
but so matter fall out and so i must relate they and if any reader be shock at their appear unnatural i cannot help it
i must remind such person that i be not write a system but a history and i be not oblige to reconcile every matter to the received notion concern truth and nature
but if this be never so easy to do perhaps it might be more prudent in i to avoid it
for instance as the fact at present before we now stand without any comment of mine upon it though it may at first sight offend some reader yet upon more mature consideration it must please all for wise and good man may consider what happen to jones at upton as a just punishment for his wickedness with regard to woman of which it be indeed the immediate consequence and silly and bad person may comfort themselves in their vice by flatter their own heart that the character of man be rather owe to accident than to virtue
now perhaps the reflection which we should be here incline to draw would alike contradict both these conclusion and would show that these incident contribute only to confirm the great useful and uncommon doctrine which it be the purpose of this whole work to inculcate and which we must not fill up our page by frequently repeat as an ordinary parson fill his sermon by repeat his text at the end of every paragraph
we be content that it must appear however unhappily sophia have err in her opinion of jones she have sufficient reason for her opinion since i believe every other young lady would in her situation have err in the same manner
nay have she follow she lover at this very time and have enter this very alehouse the moment he be depart from it she would have find the landlord as well acquaint with her name and person as the wench at upton have appear to be
for while jones be examine his boy in whisper in an inner room partridge who have no such delicacy in his disposition be in the kitchen very openly catechise the other guide who have attend mrs fitzpatrick by which mean the landlord whose ear be open on all such occasion become perfectly well acquainted with the tumble of sophia from her horse c with the mistake concern jenny cameron with the many consequence of the punch and in short with almost everything which have happen at the inn whence we despatch our lady in a coach-and-six when we last take our leaf of they
chapter ix
contain little more than a few odd observation
jones have be absent a full half-hour when he return into the kitchen in a hurry desire the landlord to let he know that instant what be to pay
and now the concern which partridge feel at be oblige to quit the warm chimney-corner and a cup of excellent liquor be somewhat compensate by hear that he be to proceed no far on foot for jones by golden argument have prevail with the boy to attend he back to the inn whither he have before conduct sophia but to this however the lad consent upon condition that the other guide would wait for he at the alehouse because as the landlord at upton be an intimate acquaintance of the landlord at gloucester it might some time or other come to the ear of the latter that his horse have be let to more than one person and so the boy might be bring to account for money which he wisely intend to put in his own pocket
we be oblige to mention this circumstance trifle as it may seem since it retard mr jones a considerable time in his setting out for the honesty of this latter boy be somewhat high--that be somewhat high-price and would indeed have cost jones very dear have not partridge who as we have say be a very cunning fellow artfully throw in half-a-crown to be spend at that very alehouse while the boy be wait for his companion
this half-crown the landlord no soon get scent of than he open after it with such vehement and persuasive outcry that the boy be soon overcome and consent to take half-a-crown more for his stay
here we cannot help observe that as there be so much of policy in the low life great man often overvalue themselves on those refinement in imposture in which they be frequently excel by some of the low of the human specie
the horse be now produce jones directly leapt into the side-saddle on which his dear sophia have rid
the lad indeed very civilly offer he the use of his but he choose the side-saddle probably because it be soft
partridge however though full as effeminate as jones could not bear the thought of degrade his manhood he therefore accept the boy s offer and now jones being mount on the side-saddle of his sophia the boy on that of mrs honour and partridge bestride the third horse they set forwards on their journey and within four hour arrive at the inn where the reader hath already spend so much time
partridge be in very high spirit during the whole way and often mention to jones the many good omen of his future success which have lately befriend he and which the reader without be the least superstitious must allow to have be particularly fortunate
partridge be moreover well pleased with the present pursuit of his companion than he have be with his pursuit of glory and from these very omen which assure the pedagogue of success he likewise first acquire a clear idea of the amour between jones and sophia to which he have before give very little attention as he have originally take a wrong scent concern the reason of jones s departure and as to what happen at upton he be too much frightened just before and after his leave that place to draw any other conclusion from thence than that poor jones be a downright madman a conceit which be not at all disagreeable to the opinion he before have of his extraordinary wildness of which he think his behaviour on their quit gloucester so well justify all the account he have formerly receive
he be now however pretty well satisfied with his present expedition and henceforth begin to conceive much worthy sentiment of his friend s understanding
the clock have just strike three when they arrive and jones immediately bespoke post-horse but unluckily there be not a horse to be procure in the whole place which the reader will not wonder at when he consider the hurry in which the whole nation and especially this part of it be at this time engage when express be pass and repasse every hour of the day and night
jones endeavour all he could to prevail with his former guide to escorte he to coventry but he be inexorable
while he be argue with the boy in the inn-yard a person come up to he and salute he by his name enquire how all the good family do in somersetshire and now jones cast his eye upon this person presently discover he to be mr dowling the lawyer with whom he have din at gloucester and with much courtesy return the salutation
dowle very earnestly press mr jones to go no far that night and back his solicitation with many unanswerable argument such as that it be almost dark that the road be very dirty and that he would be able to travel much well by day-light with many other equally good some of which jones have probably suggest to himself before but as they be then ineffectual so they be still and he continue resolute in his design even though he should be oblige to set out on foot
when the good attorney find he could not prevail on jones to stay he as strenuously apply himself to persuade the guide to accompany he
he urge many motif to induce he to undertake this short journey and at last conclude with say do you think the gentleman willnot very well reward you for your trouble two to one be odd at every other thing as well as at foot-ball
but the advantage which this united force hath in persuasion or entreaty must have be visible to a curious observer for he must have often see that when a father a master a wife or any other person in authority have stoutly adhere to a denial against all the reason which a single man could produce they have afterwards yield to the repetition of the same sentiment by a second or third person who hath undertake the cause without attempt to advance anything new in its behalf
and hence perhaps proceed the phrase of second an argument or a motion and the great consequence this be of in all assembly of public debate
hence likewise probably it be that in our court of law we often hear a learn gentleman generally a serjeant repeat for an hour together what another learn gentleman who speak just before he have be say
instead of account for this we shall proceed in our usual manner to exemplify it in the conduct of the lad above mention who submit to the persuasion of mr dowling and promise once more to admit jones into his side-saddle but insist on first give the poor creature a good bait say they have travel a great way and be rid very hard
indeed this caution of the boy be needless for jones notwithstanding his hurry and impatience would have order this of himself for he by no mean agree with the opinion of those who consider animal as mere machine and when they bury their spur in the belly of their horse imagine the spur and the horse to have an equal capacity of feel pain
while the beast be eat their corn or rather be suppose to eat it for as the boy be take care of himself in the kitchen the ostler take great care that his corn should not be consume in the stable mr jones at the earnest desire of mr dowling accompany that gentleman into his room where they sit down together over a bottle of wine
chapter x
in which mr jones and mr dowling drink a bottle together
mr dowling pour out a glass of wine name the health of the good squire allworthy add if you please sir we will likewise remember his nephew and heir the young squire come sir here s mr blifil to you a very pretty young gentleman and who i dare swear will hereafter make a very considerable figure in his country
i have a borough for he myself in my eye sir answer jones i be convinced you donot intend to affront i so i shall not resent it but i promise you you have join two person very improperly together for one be the glory of the human specie and the other be a rascal who dishonour the name of man dowle star at this
he say he think both the gentleman have a very unexceptionable character
as for squire allworthy himself say he i never have the happiness to see he but all the world talk of his goodness
and indeed as to the young gentleman i never see he but once when i carry he the news of the loss of his mother and then i be so hurried and drive and tear with the multiplicity of business that i have hardly time to converse with he but he look so like a very honest gentleman and behave himself so prettily that i protest i never be more delighted with any gentleman since i be bear i donot wonder answer jones that he should impose upon you in so short an acquaintance for he hath the cunning of the devil himself and you may live with he many year without discover he
i be breed up with he from my infancy and we be hardly ever asunder but it be very lately only that i have discover half the villany which be in he
i own i never greatly like he
i think he want that generosity of spirit which be the sure foundation of all that be great and noble in human nature
i see a selfishness in he long ago which i despise but it be lately very lately that i have find he capable of the basest and black design for indeed i have at last find out that he hath take an advantage of the openness of my own temper and hath concert the deep project by a long train of wicked artifice to work my ruin which at last he hath effect ay
ay cry dowle i protest then it be a pity such a person should inherit the great estate of your uncle allworthy alas sir cry jones you do i an honour to which i have no title
it be true indeed his goodness once allow i the liberty of call he by a much near name but as this be only a voluntary act of goodness i can complain of no injustice when he think proper to deprive i of this honour since the loss cannot be more unmerited than the gift originally be
i assure you sir i be no relation of mr allworthy and if the world who be incapable of set a true value on his virtue should think in his behaviour to i he hath deal hardly by a relation they do an injustice to the good of man for i--but i ask your pardon i shall trouble you with no particular relate to myself only as you seem to think i a relation of mr allworthy i think proper to set you right in a matter that might draw some censure upon he which i promise you i would rather lose my life than give occasion to i protest sir cry dowling you talk very much like a man of honour but instead of give i any trouble i protest it would give i great pleasure to know how you come to be think a relation of mr allworthy s if you be not
your horse willnot be ready this half-hour and as you have sufficient opportunity i wish you would tell i how all that happen for i protest it seem very surprizing that you should pass for a relation of a gentleman without be so jones who in the compliance of his disposition though not in his prudence a little resemble his lovely sophia be easily prevail on to satisfy mr dowling s curiosity by relate the history of his birth and education which he do like othello
even from his boyish year to th very moment he be bad to tell the which to hear dowle like desdemona do seriously incline he swear twa strange twa pass strange twas pitiful twa wonderous pitiful
mr dowling be indeed very greatly affect with this relation for he have not divest himself of humanity by be an attorney
indeed nothing be more unjust than to carry our prejudice against a profession into private life and to borrow our idea of a man from our opinion of his calling
habit it be true lessen the horror of those action which the profession make necessary and consequently habitual but in all other instance nature work in man of all profession alike nay perhaps even more strongly with those who give she as it be a holiday when they be follow their ordinary business
a butcher i make no doubt would feel compunction at the slaughter of a fine horse and though a surgeon can feel no pain in cut off a limb i have know he compassionate a man in a fit of the gout
the common hangman who hath stretch the neck of hundred be know to have tremble at his first operation on a head and the very professor of human blood-shedding who in their trade of war butcher thousand not only of their fellow-professor but often of woman and child without remorse even these i say in time of peace when drum and trumpet be lay aside often lay aside all their ferocity and become very gentle member of civil society
in the same manner an attorney may feel all the misery and distress of his fellow-creature provide he happen not to be concerned against they
jones as the reader know be yet unacquainted with the very black colour in which he have be represent to mr allworthy and as to other matter he do not shew they in the most disadvantageous light for though he be unwilling to cast any blame on his former friend and patron yet he be not very desirous of heap too much upon himself
dowle therefore observe and not without reason that very ill office must have be do he by somebody for certainly cry he the squire would never have disinherit you only for a few fault which any young gentleman might have commit
indeed i cannot properly say disinherit for to be sure by law you cannot claim as heir
thatbe certain that nobody need go to counsel for
yet when a gentleman have in a manner adopt you thus as his own son you might reasonably have expect some very considerable part if not the whole nay if you have expect the whole i should not have blame you for certainly all man be for get as much as they can and they be not to be blame on that account indeed you wrong i say jones i should have be content with very little i never have any view upon mr allworthy s fortune nay i believe i may truly say i never once consider what he could or might give i
this i solemnly declare if he have do a prejudice to his nephew in my favour i would have undo it again
i have rather enjoy my own mind than the fortune of another man
what be the poor pride arise from a magnificent house a numerous equipage a splendid table and from all the other advantage or appearance of fortune compare to the warm solid content the swelling satisfaction the thrilling transport and the exulting triumph which a good mind enjoy in the contemplation of a generous virtuous noble benevolent action
i envy not blifil in the prospect of his wealth nor shall i envy he in the possession of it
i would not think myself a rascal half an hour to exchange situation
i believe indeed mr blifil suspect i of the view you mention and i suppose these suspicion as they arise from the baseness of his own heart so they occasion his baseness to i
but i thank heaven i know i feel--i feel my innocence my friend and i would not part with that feeling for the world
for as long as i know i have never do nor even design an injury to any be whatever pone i pigris ubi nulla campis arbor aestiva recreatur aura quod latus mundi nebulae malusque jupiter urget
pone sub curru nimium propinqui solis in terra dominibus negata dulce ridentem lalagen amabo dulce loquentem place i where never summer breeze unbinds the glebe or warm the tree where ever-lower cloud appear and angry jove deform th inclement year
place i beneath the burn ray where roll the rapid car of day love and the nymph shall charm my toil the nymph who sweetly speak and sweetly smile
mr francis
he then fill a bumper of wine and drink it off to the health of his dear lalage and fill dowling s glass likewise up to the brim insist on his pledge he
why then herebe miss lalage s health with all my heart cry dowle
i have hear she toast often i protest though i never see she but they say shebe extremely handsome though the latin be not the only part of this speech which dowle do not perfectly understand yet there be somewhat in it that make a very strong impression upon he
and though he endeavour by wink nodding sneer and grinning to hide the impression from jones for we be as often ashamed of think right as of think wrong it be certain he secretly approve as much of his sentiment as he understand and really feel a very strong impulse of compassion for he
but we may possibly take some other opportunity of comment upon this especially if we should happen to meet mr dowle any more in the course of our history
at present we be oblige to take our leave of that gentleman a little abruptly in imitation of mr jones who be no soon inform by partridge that his horse be ready than he deposit his reckoning wish his companion a good night mount and set forward towards coventry though the night be dark and it just then begin to rain very hard
chapter xi
the disaster which befel jones on his departure for coventry with the sage remark of partridge
no road can be plain than that from the place where they now be to coventry and though neither jones nor partridge nor the guide have ever travel it before it would have be almost impossible to have miss their way have it not be for the two reason mention in the conclusion of the last chapter
these two circumstance however happen both unfortunately to intervene our traveller deviate into a much less frequent track and after rid full six mile instead of arrive at the stately spire of coventry they find themselves still in a very dirty lane where they see no symptom of approach the suburb of a large city
jones now declare that they must certainly have lose their way but this the guide insist upon be impossible a word which in common conversation be often use to signify not only improbable but often what be really very likely and sometimes what hath certainly happen an hyperbolical violence like that which be so frequently offer to the word infinite and eternal by the former of which it be usual to express a distance of half a yard and by the latter a duration of five minute
and thus it be as usual to assert the impossibility of lose what be already actually lose
this be in fact the case at present for notwithstanding all the confident assertion of the lad to the contrary it be certain they be no more in the right road to coventry than the fraudulent griping cruel canting miser be in the right road to heaven
it be not perhaps easy for a reader who hath never be in those circumstance to imagine the horror with which darkness rain and wind fill person who have lose their way in the night and who consequently have not the pleasant prospect of warm fire dry cloath and other refreshment to support their mind in struggle with the inclemency of the weather
a very imperfect idea of this horror will however serve sufficiently to account for the conceit which now fill the head of partridge and which we shall presently be oblige to open
jones grow more and more positive that they be out of their road and the boy himself at last acknowledge he believe they be not in the right road to coventry though he affirm at the same time it be impossible they should have mist the way
but partridge be of a different opinion
he say when they first set out he imagine some mischief or other would happen did not you observe sir say he to jones that old woman who stand at the door just as you be take horse
i wish you have give she a small matter with all my heart for she say then you might repent it and at that very instant it begin to rain and the wind hath continue rise ever since
whatever some people may think i be very certain it be in the power of witch to raise the wind whenever they please
i have see it happen very often in my time and if ever i see a witch in all my life that old woman be certainly one
i think so to myself at that very time and if i have have any halfpence in my pocket i would have give she some for to be sure it be always good to be charitable to those sort of people for fear what may happen and many a person hath lose his cattle by save a halfpenny jones though he be horridly vex at the delay which this mistake be likely to occasion in his journey could not help smile at the superstition of his friend whom an accident now greatly confirm in his opinion
this be a tumble from his horse by which however he receive no other injury than what the dirt confer on his cloath
partridge have no soon recover his leg than he appeal to his fall as conclusive evidence of all he have assert but jones finding he be unhurt answer with a smile this witch of your partridge be a most ungrateful jade and doth not i find distinguish she friend from other in her resentment
if the old lady have be angry with i for neglect she i donot see why she should tumble you from your horse after all the respect you have express for she it be ill jesting cry partridge with people who have power to do these thing for they be often very malicious
i remember a farrier who provoke one of they by ask she when the time she have bargain with the devil for would be out and within three month from that very day one of his good cow be drown
nor be she satisfied with that for a little time afterwards he lose a barrel of well-drink for the old witch pull out the spigot and let it run all over the cellar the very first evening he have tap it to make merry with some of his neighbour
in short nothing ever thrive with he afterwards for she worry the poor man so that he take to drink and in a year or two his stock be seize and he and his family be now come to the parish the guide and perhaps his horse too be both so attentive to this discourse that either through want of care or by the malice of the witch they be now both sprawl in the dirt
partridge entirely impute this fall as he have do his own to the same cause
he tell mr jones it would certainly be his turn next and earnestly entreat he to return back and find out the old woman and pacify she
we shall very soon add he reach the inn for though we have seem to go forward i be very certain we be in the identical place in which we be an hour ago and i dare swear if it be daylight we might now see the inn we set out from instead of return any answer to this sage advice jones be entirely attentive to what have happen to the boy who receive no other hurt than what have before befall partridge and which his cloath very easily bore as they have be for many year inure to the like
he soon regain his side-saddle and by the hearty curse and blow which he bestow on his horse quickly satisfy mr jones that no harm be do
chapter xii
relate that mr jones continue his journey contrary to the advice of partridge with what happen on that occasion
they now discover a light at some distance to the great pleasure of jones and to the no small terror of partridge who firmly believe himself to be bewitch and that this light be a jack-with-a-lantern or somewhat more mischievous
but how be these fear increase when as they approach near to this light or light as they now appear they hear a confused sound of human voice of singing laughing and hallowing together with a strange noise that seem to proceed from some instrument but could hardly be allow the name of music
indeed to favour a little the opinion of partridge it might very well be call music bewitch
it be impossible to conceive a much great degree of horror than what now seize on partridge the contagion of which have reach the post-boy who have be very attentive to many thing that the other have utter
he now therefore join in petition jones to return say he firmly believe what partridge have just before say that though the horse seem to go on they have not move a step forwards during at least the last half-hour
jones could not help smile in the midst of his vexation at the fear of these poor fellow
either we advance say he towards the light or the light have advance towards we for we be now at a very little distance from they but how can either of you be afraid of a set of people who appear only to be merry-making merry-making sir cry partridge who could be merry-making at this time of night and in such a place and such weather
they can be nothing but ghost or witch or some evil spirit or other thatbe certain let they be what they will cry jones i be resolve to go up to they and enquire the way to coventry
all witch partridge be not such ill-natured hag as that we have the misfortune to meet with last o lord sir cry partridge there be no know what humour they will be in to be sure it be always good to be civil to they but what if we should meet with something bad than witch with evil spirit themselves pray sir be advise pray sir do
if you have read so many terrible account as i have of these matter you would not be so fool-hardy the lord know whither we have get already or whither we be go for sure such darkness be never see upon earth and i question whether it can be dark in the other world jones put forwards as fast as he could notwithstanding all these hint and caution and poor partridge be oblige to follow for though he hardly dare to advance he dare still less to stay behind by himself
at length they arrive at the place whence the light and different noise have issue
this jones perceive to be no other than a barn where a great number of man and woman be assemble and divert themselves with much apparent jollity
jones no soon appear before the great door of the barn which be open than a masculine and very rough voice from within demand who be there to which jones gently answer a friend and immediately ask the road to coventry
if you be a friend cry another of the man in the barn you have well alight till the storm be over for indeed it be now more violent than ever you be very welcome to put up your horse for there be sufficient room for he at the end of the barn you be very obliging return jones and i will accept your offer for a few minute whilst the rain continue and here be two more who will be glad of the same favour this be accord with more good-will than it be accept for partridge would rather have submit to the utmost inclemency of the weather than have trust to the clemency of those whom he take for hobgoblin and the poor post-boy be now infect with the same apprehension but they be both oblige to follow the example of jones the one because he durst not leave his horse and the other because he fear nothing so much as be leave by himself
have this history be writ in the day of superstition i should have have too much compassion for the reader to have leave he so long in suspense whether beelzebub or satan be about actually to appear in person with all his hellish retinue but as these doctrine be at present very unfortunate and have but few if any believer i have not be much aware of convey any such terror
to say truth the whole furniture of the infernal region hath long be appropriate by the manager of playhouse who seem lately to have lay they by as rubbish capable only of affect the upper gallery a place in which few of our reader ever sit
however though we do not suspect raise any great terror on this occasion we have reason to fear some other apprehension may here arise in our reader into which we would not willingly betray he i mean that we be go to take a voyage into fairy-land and introduce a set of being into our history which scarce any one be ever childish enough to believe though many have be foolish enough to spend their time in writing and read their adventure
to prevent therefore any such suspicion so prejudicial to the credit of an historian who profess to draw his material from nature only we shall now proceed to acquaint the reader who these people be whose sudden appearance have strike such terror into partridge have more than half frighten the post-boy and have a little surprized even mr jones himself
the people then assemble in this barn be no other than a company of egyptians or as they be vulgarly call gypsy and they be now celebrate the wedding of one of their society
it be impossible to conceive a happy set of people than appear here to be meet together
the utmost mirth indeed shew itself in every countenance nor be their ball totally void of all order and decorum
perhaps it have more than a country assembly be sometimes conduct with for these people be subject to a formal government and law of their own and all pay obedience to one great magistrate whom they call their king
greater plenty likewise be nowhere to be see than what flourish in this barn
here be indeed no nicety nor elegance nor do the keen appetite of the guest require any
here be good store of bacon fowl and mutton to which every one present provide well sauce himself than the good and dear french cook can prepare
aeneas be not describe under more consternation in the temple of juno dum stupet obtutuque haeret defixus in uno than be our heroe at what he see in this barn
while he be look everywhere round he with astonishment a venerable person approach he with many friendly salutation rather of too hearty a kind to be call courtly
this be no other than the king of the gypsy himself
he be very little distinguished in dress from his subject nor have he any regalia of majesty to support his dignity and yet there seem as mr jones say to be somewhat in his air which denote authority and inspire the beholder with an idea of awe and respect though all this be perhaps imaginary in jones and the truth may be that such idea be incident to power and almost inseparable from it
there be somewhat in the open countenance and courteous behaviour of jones which be accompany with much comeliness of person greatly recommend he at first sight to every beholder
these be perhaps a little heighten in the present instance by that profound respect which he pay to the king of the gypsy the moment he be acquaint with his dignity and which be the sweet to his gypseian majesty as he be not use to receive such homage from any but his own subject
the king order a table to be spread with the choicest of their provision for his accommodation and have place himself at his right hand his majesty begin to discourse with our heroe in the follow manner i doubt not sir but you have often see some of my people who be what you call de party detache for dey go about everywhere but i fancy you imagine not we be so considrable body as we be and may be you will be surprize more when you hear de gypsy be as orderly and well govern people as any upon face of de earth
i have honour as i say to be deir king and no monarch can do boast of more dutiful subject ne no more affectionate
how far i deserve deir good-will i no say but dis i can say dat i never design anyte but to do dem good
i sall no do boast of dat neider for what can i do oderwise dan consider of de good of dose poor people who go about all day to give i always de good of what dey get
dey love and honour i darefore because i do love and take care of dem dat be all i know no oder reason
about a tousand or two tousand year ago i cannot tell to a year or two as can neider write nor read dere be a great what you call--a volution among de gypsy for dere be de lord gypsy in dose day and dese lord do quarrel vid one anoder about de place but de king of de gypsy do demolish dem all and make all his subject equal vid each oder and since dat time dey have agree very well for dey no tink of be king and may be it be well for dem as dey be for i assure you it be ver troublesome ting to be king and always to do justice i have often wish to be de private gypsy when i have be force to punish my dear friend and relation for dough we never put to death our punishment be ver severe
dey make de gypsy ashamed of demselve and dat be ver terrible punishment i ave scarce ever know de gypsy so punish do harm any more the king then proceed to express some wonder that there be no such punishment as shame in other government
upon which jones assure he to the contrary for that there be many crime for which shame be inflict by the english law and that it be indeed one consequence of all punishment
dat be ver strange say the king for i know and hear good deal of your people dough i no live among dem and i have often hear dat sham be de consequence and de cause too of many of your reward
be your reward and punishment den de same ting while his majesty be thus discourse with jones a sudden uproar arise in the barn and as it seem upon this occasion the courtesy of these people have by degree remove all the apprehension of partridge and he be prevail upon not only to stuff himself with their food but to taste some of their liquor which by degree entirely expel all fear from his composition and in its stead introduce much more agreeable sensation
a young female gypsy more remarkable for her wit than her beauty have decoy the honest fellow aside pretend to tell his fortune
now when they be alone together in a remote part of the barn whether it proceed from the strong liquor which be never so apt to inflame inordinate desire as after moderate fatigue or whether the fair gypsy herself throw aside the delicacy and decency of her sex and tempt the youth partridge with express solicitation but they be discover in a very improper manner by the husband of the gypsy who from jealousy it seem have keep a watchful eye over his wife and have dog she to the place where he find she in the arm of her gallant
to the great confusion of jones partridge be now hurry before the king who hear the accusation and likewise the culprit s defence which be indeed very trifling for the poor fellow be confound by the plain evidence which appear against he and have very little to say for himself
his majesty then turn towards jones say sir you have hear what dey say what punishment do you tink your man deserve jones answer he be sorry for what have happen and that partridge should make the husband all the amend in his power he say he have very little money about he at that time and put his hand into his pocket offer the fellow a guinea
to which he immediately answer he hop his honour would not think of give he less than five this sum after some altercation be reduce to two and jones have stipulate for the full forgiveness of both partridge and the wife be go to pay the money when his majesty restrain his hand turn to the witness and ask he at what time he have discover the criminal to which he answer that he have be desire by the husband to watch the motion of his wife from her first speak to the stranger and that he have never lose sight of she afterwards till the crime have be commit the king then ask if the husband be with he all that time in his lurking-place to which he answer in the affirmative
his egyptian majesty then address himself to the husband as follow i be sorry to see any gypsy dat have no more honour dan to sell de honour of his wife for money
if you have de love for your wife you would have prevent dis matter and not endeavour to make she de whore dat you might discover she
i do order dat you have no money give you for you deserve punishment not reward i do order derefore dat you be de infamous gypsy and do wear pair of horn upon your forehead for one month and dat your wife be call de whore and point at all dat time for you be de infamous gypsy but she be no less de infamous whore the gypsy immediately proceed to execute the sentence and leave jones and partridge alone with his majesty
jones greatly applaud the justice of the sentence upon which the king turn to he say i believe you be surprize for i suppose you have ver bad opinion of my people i suppose you tink we all de tieve i must confess sir say jones i have not hear so favourable an account of they as they seem to deserve i vil tell you say the king how the difference be between you and we
my people rob your people and your people rob one anoder jones afterwards proceed very gravely to sing forth the happiness of those subject who live under such a magistrate
indeed their happiness appear to have be so compleat that we be aware lest some advocate for arbitrary power should hereafter quote the case of those people as an instance of the great advantage which attend that government above all other
and here we will make a concession which would not perhaps have be expect from we that no limited form of government be capable of rise to the same degree of perfection or of produce the same benefit to society with this
mankind have never be so happy as when the great part of the then know world be under the dominion of a single master and this state of their felicity continue during the reign of five successive prince this be the true aera of the golden age and the only golden age which ever have any existence unless in the warm imagination of the poet from the expulsion from eden down to this day
nerva trajan adrian and the two antonini
in reality i know but of one solid objection to absolute monarchy
the only defect in which excellent constitution seem to be the difficulty of find any man adequate to the office of an absolute monarch for this indispensably require three quality very difficult as it appear from history to be find in princely nature first a sufficient quantity of moderation in the prince to be content with all the power which be possible for he to have
2 ndly enough of wisdom to know his own happiness
and 3 rdly goodness sufficient to support the happiness of other when not only compatible with but instrumental to his own
now if an absolute monarch with all these great and rare qualification should be allow capable of confer the great good on society it must be surely grant on the contrary that absolute power vest in the hand of one who be deficient in they all be likely to be attend with no less a degree of evil
in short our own religion furnish we with adequate idea of the blessing as well as curse which may attend absolute power
the picture of heaven and of hell will place a very lively image of both before our eye for though the prince of the latter can have no power but what he originally derive from the omnipotent sovereign in the former yet it plainly appear from scripture that absolute power in his infernal dominion be grant to their diabolical ruler
this be indeed the only absolute power which can by scripture be derive from heaven
if therefore the several tyranny upon earth can prove any title to a divine authority it must be derive from this original grant to the prince of darkness and these subordinate deputation must consequently come immediately from he whose stamp they so expressly bear
to conclude as the example of all age shew we that mankind in general desire power only to do harm and when they obtain it use it for no other purpose it be not consonant with even the least degree of prudence to hazard an alteration where our hope be poorly keep in countenance by only two or three exception out of a thousand instance to alarm our fear
in this case it will be much wise to submit to a few inconveniencie arise from the dispassionate deafness of law than to remedy they by apply to the passionate open ear of a tyrant
nor can the example of the gypsy though possibly they may have long be happy under this form of government be here urge since we must remember the very material respect in which they differ from all other people and to which perhaps this their happiness be entirely owing namely that they have no false honours among they and that they look on shame as the most grievous punishment in the world
chapter xiii
a dialogue between jones and partridge
the honest lover of liberty will we doubt not pardon that long digression into which we be lead at the close of the last chapter to prevent our history from be apply to the use of the most pernicious doctrine which priestcraft have ever the wickedness or the impudence to preach
we will now proceed with mr jones who when the storm be over take leave of his egyptian majesty after many thank for his courteous behaviour and kind entertainment and set out for coventry to which place for it be still dark a gypsy be order to conduct he
jones have by reason of his deviation travel eleven mile instead of six and most of those through very execrable road where no expedition could have be make in quest of a midwife do not arrive at coventry till near twelve
nor could he possibly get again into the saddle till past two for post-horse be now not easy to get nor be the hostler or post-boy in half so great a hurry as himself but choose rather to imitate the tranquil disposition of partridge who be deny the nourishment of sleep take all opportunity to supply its place with every other kind of nourishment and be never well pleased than when he arrive at an inn nor ever more dissatisfied than when he be again force to leave it
jones now travel post we will follow he therefore accord to our custom and to the rule of longinus in the same manner
from coventry he arrive at daventry from daventry at stratford and from stratford at dunstable whither he come the next day a little after noon and within a few hour after sophia have leave it and though he be oblige to stay here long than he wish while a smith with great deliberation shoe the post-horse he be to ride he doubt not but to overtake his sophia before she should set out from st albans at which place he conclude and very reasonably that his lordship would stop and dine
and have he be right in this conjecture he most probably would have overtake his angel at the aforesaid place but unluckily my lord have appoint a dinner to be prepare for he at his own house in london and in order to enable he to reach that place in proper time he have order a relay of horse to meet he at st albans
when jones therefore arrive there he be inform that the coach-and-six have set out two hour before
if fresh post-horse have be now ready as they be not it seem so apparently impossible to overtake the coach before it reach london that partridge think he have now a proper opportunity to remind his friend of a matter which he seem entirely to have forget what this be the reader will guess when we inform he that jones have eat nothing more than one poached egg since he have leave the alehouse where he have first meet the guide return from sophia for with the gypsy he have feast only his understanding
the landlord so entirely agree with the opinion of mr partridge that he no soon hear the latter desire his friend to stay and dine than he very readily put in his word and retract his promise before give of furnish the horse immediately he assure mr jones he would lose no time in bespeak a dinner which he say could be get ready soon than it be possible to get the horse up from grass and to prepare they for their journey by a feed of corn
jones be at length prevail on chiefly by the latter argument of the landlord and now a joint of mutton be put down to the fire
while this be prepare partridge be admit into the same apartment with his friend or master begin to harangue in the follow manner
certainly sir if ever man deserve a young lady you deserve young madam western for what a vast quantity of love must a man have to be able to live upon it without any other food as you do
i be positive i have eat thirty time as much within these last twenty-four hour as your honour and yet i be almost famish for nothing make a man so hungry as travel especially in this cold raw weather
and yet i cannot tell how it be but your honour be seemingly in perfect good health and you never look well nor fresher in your life
it must be certainly love that you live upon and a very rich diet too partridge answer jones
but do not fortune send i an excellent dainty yesterday
dost thou imagine i cannot live more than twenty-four hour on this dear pocket-book undoubtedly cry partridge there be enough in that pocket-book to purchase many a good meal
fortune send it to your honour very opportunely for present use as your honour s money must be almost out by this time what do you mean answer jones i hope you donot imagine that i should be dishonest enough even if it belong to any other person besides miss western---- dishonest reply partridge heaven forbid i should wrong your honour so much
but wherebe the dishonesty in borrow a little for present spending since you will be so well able to pay the lady hereafter
no indeed i would have your honour pay it again as soon as it be convenient by all mean but where can be the harm in make use of it now you want it
indeed if it belong to a poor body it would be another thing but so great a lady to be sure can never want it especially now as she be along with a lord who it cannot be doubt will let she have whatever she hath need of
besides if she should want a little she cannot want the whole therefore i would give she a little but i would be hang before i mention the having find it at first and before i get some money of my own for london i have hear be the very bad of place to be in without money
indeed if i have not know to whom it belong i might have think it be the devil s money and have be afraid to use it but as you know otherwise and come honestly by it it would be an affront to fortune to part with it all again at the very time when you want it most you can hardly expect she should ever do you such another good turn for fortuna nunquam perpetuo est bona
you will do as you please notwithstanding all i say but for my part i would be hang before i mention a word of the matter by what i can see partridge cry jones hang be a matter non longe alienum a scaevolae studiis you should say alienus say partridge i remember the passage it be an example under communis alienus immunis variis casibus serviunt if you do remember it cry jones i find you donot understand it but i tell thee friend in plain english that he who find another s property and wilfully detain it from the know owner deserve in foro conscientiae to be hang no less than if he have steal it
and as for this very identical bill which be the property of my angel and be once in her dear possession i will not deliver it into any hand but her own upon any consideration whatever no though i be as hungry as thou art and have no other mean to satisfy my craving appetite this i hope to do before i sleep but if it should happen otherwise i charge thee if thou would st not incur my displeasure for ever not to shock i any more by the bare mention of such detestable baseness i should not have mention it now cry partridge if it have appear so to i for ibe sure i scorn any wickedness as much as another but perhaps you know well and yet i might have imagine that i should not have live so many year and have teach school so long without be able to distinguish between fas et nefas but it seem we be all to live and learn
i remember my old schoolmaster who be a prodigious great scholar use often to say polly matete cry town be my daskalon
the english of which he tell we be that a child may sometimes teach his grandmother to suck egg
i have live to a fine purpose truly if i be to be teach my grammar at this time of day
perhaps young gentleman you may change your opinion if you live to my year for i remember i think myself as wise when i be a stripling of one or two and twenty as i be now
i be sure i always teach alienus and my master read it so before i there be not many instance in which partridge could provoke jones nor be there many in which partridge himself could have be hurry out of his respect
unluckily however they have both hit on one of these
we have already see partridge could not bear to have his learning attack nor could jones bear some passage or other in the foregoing speech
and now look upon his companion with a contemptuous and disdainful air a thing not usual with he he cry partridge i see thou art a conceited old fool and i wish thou art not likewise an old rogue
indeed if i be as well convince of the latter as i be of the former thou should st travel no far in my company the sage pedagogue be content with the vent which he have already give to his indignation and as the vulgar phrase be immediately draw in his horn
he say he be sorry he have utter anything which might give offence for that he have never intend it but nemo omnibus horis sapit
as jones have the vice of a warm disposition he be entirely free from those of a cold one and if his friend must have confest his temper to have be a little too easily ruffle his enemy must at the same time have conf that it as soon subside nor do it at all resemble the sea whose swelling be more violent and dangerous after a storm be over than while the storm itself subsist
he instantly accept the submission of partridge shake he by the hand and with the most benign aspect imaginable say twenty kind thing and at the same time very severely condemn himself though not half so severely as he will most probably be condemn by many of our good reader
partridge be now highly comfort as his fear of have offend be at once abolish and his pride completely satisfied by jones have own himself in the wrong which submission he instantly apply to what have principally nettle he and repeat in a muttering voice to be sure sir your knowledge may be superior to mine in some thing but as to the grammar i think i may challenge any man live
i think at least i have that at my finger s end if anything could add to the satisfaction which the poor man now enjoy he receive this addition by the arrival of an excellent shoulder of mutton that at this instant come smoake to the table
on which have both plentifully feast they again mount their horse and set forward for london
chapter xiv
what happen to mr jones in his journey from st albans
they be get about two mile beyond barnet and it be now the dusk of the evening when a genteel-look man but upon a very shabby horse ride up to jones and ask he whether he be go to london to which jones answer in the affirmative
the gentleman reply i should be oblige to you sir if you will accept of my company for it be very late and i be a stranger to the road jones readily comply with the request and on they travel together hold that sort of discourse which be usual on such occasion
of this indeed robbery be the principal topic upon which subject the stranger express great apprehension but jones declare he have very little to lose and consequently as little to fear
here partridge could not forbear put in his word
your honour say he may think it a little but i be sure if i have a hundred-pound bank-note in my pocket as you have i should be very sorry to lose it but for my part i never be less afraid in my life for we be four of we and if we all stand by one another the good man in england cannot rob we
suppose he should have a pistol he can kill but one of we and a man can die but once thatbe my comfort a man can die but once besides the reliance on superior number a kind of valour which hath raise a certain nation among the modern to a high pitch of glory there be another reason for the extraordinary courage which partridge now discover for he have at present as much of that quality as be in the power of liquor to bestow
our company be now arrive within a mile of highgate when the stranger turn short upon jones and pull out a pistol demand that little bank-note which partridge have mention
jones be at first somewhat shocked at this unexpected demand however he presently recollect himself and tell the highwayman all the money he have in his pocket be entirely at his service and so say he pull out upwards of three guinea and offer to deliver it but the other answer with an oath that would not do
jones answer coolly he be very sorry for it and return the money into his pocket
the highwayman then threaten if he do not deliver the bank-note that moment he must shoot he hold his pistol at the same time very near to his breast
jones instantly catch hold of the fellow s hand which tremble so that he could scarce hold the pistol in it and turn the muzzle from he
a struggle then ensue in which the former wrest the pistol from the hand of his antagonist and both come from their horse on the ground together the highwayman upon his back and the victorious jones upon he
the poor fellow now begin to implore mercy of the conqueror for to say the truth he be in strength by no mean a match for jones
indeed sir say he i could have have no intention to shoot you for you will find the pistol be not load
this be the first robbery i ever attempt and i have be drive by distress to this at this instant at about a hundred and fifty yard distance lay another person on the ground roar for mercy in a much loud voice than the highwayman
this be no other than partridge himself who endeavour to make his escape from the engagement have be throw from his horse and lie flat on his face not dare to look up and expect every minute to be shoot
in this posture he lay till the guide who be no otherwise concern than for his horse have secure the stumble beast come up to he and tell he his master have get the well of the highwayman
partridge leapt up at this news and run back to the place where jones stand with his sword draw in his hand to guard the poor fellow which partridge no soon see than he cry out kill the villain sir run he through the body kill he this instant luckily however for the poor wretch he have fall into more merciful hand for jones have examine the pistol and find it to be really unload begin to believe all the man have tell he before partridge come up namely that he be a novice in the trade and that he have be drive to it by the distress he mention the great indeed imaginable that of five hungry child and a wife lie in of the sixth in the utmost want and misery
the truth of all which the highwayman most vehemently assert and offer to convince mr jones of it if he would take the trouble to go to his house which be not above two mile off say that he desire no favour but upon condition of prove all he have all alledge jones at first pretend that he would take the fellow at his word and go with he declare that his fate should depend entirely on the truth of his story
upon this the poor fellow immediately express so much alacrity that jones be perfectly satisfied with his veracity and begin now to entertain sentiment of compassion for he
he return the fellow his empty pistol advise he to think of honester mean of relieve his distress and give he a couple of guineas for the immediate support of his wife and his family add he wish he have more for his sake for the hundred pound that have be mention be not his own our reader will probably be divide in their opinion concern this action some may applaud it perhaps as an act of extraordinary humanity while those of a more saturnine temper will consider it as a want of regard to that justice which every man owe his country
partridge certainly see it in that light for he testify much dissatisfaction on the occasion quote an old proverb and say he should not wonder if the rogue attack they again before they reach london
the highwayman be full of expression of thankfulness and gratitude
he actually dropt tear or pretend so to do
he vow he would immediately return home and would never afterwards commit such a transgression whether he keep his word or no perhaps may appear hereafter
our traveller have remount their horse arrive in town without encounter any new mishap
on the road much pleasant discourse pass between jones and partridge on the subject of their last adventure in which jones expr a great compassion for those highwayman who be by unavoidable distress drive as it be to such illegal course as generally bring they to a shameful death i mean say he those only whose high guilt extend no far than to robbery and who be never guilty of cruelty nor insult to any person which be a circumstance that i must say to the honour of our country distinguish the robber of england from those of all other nation for murder be amongst those almost inseparably incident to robbery no doubt answer partridge it be well to take away one s money than one s life and yet it be very hard upon honest man that they cannot travel about their business without be in danger of these villain
and to be sure it would be well that all rogue be hang out of the way than that one honest man should suffer
for my own part indeed i should not care to have the blood of any of they on my own hand but it be very proper for the law to hang they all
what right hath any man to take sixpence from i unless i give it he
be there any honesty in such a man no surely cry jones no more than there be in he who take the horse out of another man s stable or who apply to his own use the money which he find when he know the right owner these hint stopt the mouth of partridge nor do he open it again till jones have throw some sarcastical joke on his cowardice he offer to excuse himself on the inequality of fire-arm say a thousand naked man be nothing to one pistol for though it be true it will kill but one at a single discharge yet who can tell but that one may be himself book xiii
contain the space of twelve day
chapter i
an invocation
come bright love of fame inspire my glowing breast not thee i will call who over swell tide of blood and tear dost bear the heroe on to glory while sigh of million waft his spread sail but thee fair gentle maid whom mnesis happy nymph first on the bank of hebrus do produce
thee whom maeonia educate whom mantua charm and who on that fair hill which overlook the proud metropolis of britain sat st with thy milton sweetly tune the heroic lyre fill my ravished fancy with the hope of charming age yet to come
foretel i that some tender maid whose grandmother be yet unborn hereafter when under the fictitious name of sophia she read the real worth which once exist in my charlotte shall from her sympathetic breast send forth the heaving sigh
do thou teach i not only to foresee but to enjoy nay even to feed on future praise
comfort i by a solemn assurance that when the little parlour in which i sit at this instant shall be reduce to a bad furnish box i shall be read with honour by those who never know nor see i and whom i shall neither know nor see
and thou much plumper dame whom no airy form nor phantom of imagination cloathe whom the well-season beef and pudde richly stain with plum delight thee i call of whom in a treckschuyte in some dutch canal the fat ufrow gelt impregnate by a jolly merchant of amsterdam be deliver in grub-street school didst thou suck in the element of thy erudition
here hast thou in thy maturer age teach poetry to tickle not the fancy but the pride of the patron
comedy from thee learn a grave and solemn air while tragedy storm aloud and rend th affrighted theatre with its thunder
to soothe thy weary limb in slumber alderman history tell his tedious tale and again to awaken thee monsieur romance perform his surprize trick of dexterity
nor less thy well-feed bookseller obey thy influence
by thy advice the heavy unread folio lump which long have doze on the dusty shelf piecemeale into number run nimbly through the nation
instruct by thee some book like quack impose on the world by promise wonder while other turn beau and trust all their merit to a gild outside
come thou jolly substance with thy shin face keep back thy inspiration but hold forth thy tempting reward thy shin chink heap thy quickly convertible bank-bill big with unseen rich thy often-vary stock the warm the comfortable house and lastly a fair portion of that bounteous mother whose flow breast yield redundant sustenance for all her numerous offspring do not some too greedily and wantonly drive their brother from the teat
come thou and if i be too tasteless of thy valuable treasure warm my heart with the transporting thought of convey they to other
tell i that through thy bounty the pratling babe whose innocent play hath often be interrupt by my labour may one time be amply reward for they
and now this ill-yoked pair this lean shadow and this fat substance have prompt i to write whose assistance shall i invoke to direct my pen
first genius thou gift of heaven without whose aid in vain we struggle against the stream of nature
thou who dost sow the generous seed which art nourishe and bring to perfection
do thou kindly take i by the hand and lead i through all the maze the winding labyrinth of nature
initiate i into all those mystery which profane eye never behold
teach i which to thee be no difficult task to know mankind well than they know themselves
remove that mist which dim the intellect of mortal and cause they to adore man for their art or to detest they for their cunning in deceive other when they be in reality the object only of ridicule for deceive themselves
strip off the thin disguise of wisdom from self-conceit of plenty from avarice and of glory from ambition
come thou that hast inspire thy aristophanes thy lucian thy cervantes thy rabelais thy molière thy shakespear thy swift thy marivaux fill my page with humour till mankind learn the good-nature to laugh only at the folly of other and the humility to grieve at their own
and thou almost the constant attendant on true genius humanity bring all thy tender sensation
if thou hast already dispose of they all between thy allen and thy lyttleton steal they a little while from their bosom
not without these the tender scene be paint
from these alone proceed the noble disinterested friendship the melting love the generous sentiment the ardent gratitude the soft compassion the candid opinion and all those strong energy of a good mind which fill the moistened eye with tear the glowing cheek with blood and swell the heart with tide of grief joy and benevolence
and thou o learning
