for without thy assistance nothing pure nothing correct can genius produce do thou guide my pen
thee in thy favourite field where the limpid gently-roll thames wash thy etonian bank in early youth i have worship
to thee at thy birchen altar with true spartan devotion i have sacrifice my blood
come then and from thy vast luxuriant store in long antiquity pile up pour forth the rich profusion
open thy maeonian and thy mantuan coffer with whatever else include thy philosophic thy poetic and thy historical treasure whether with greek or roman character thou hast choose to inscribe the ponderous chest give i a while that key to all thy treasure which to thy warburton thou hast entrust
lastly come experience long conversant with the wise the good the learn and the polite
nor with they only but with every kind of character from the minister at his levee to the bailiff in his spunge-house from the dutchess at her drum to the landlady behind her bar
from thee only can the manner of mankind be know to which the recluse pedant however great his part or extensive his learning may be hath ever be a stranger
come all these and more if possible for arduous be the task i have undertake and without all your assistance will i find be too heavy for i to support
but if you all smile on my labour i hope still to bring they to a happy conclusion
chapter ii
what befel mr jones on his arrival in london
the learn dr misaubin use to say that the proper direction to he be to dr misaubin in the world intimate that there be few people in it to whom his great reputation be not know
and perhaps upon a very nice examination into the matter we shall find that this circumstance bear no inconsiderable part among the many blessing of grandeur
the great happiness of be know to posterity with the hope of which we so delight ourselves in the precede chapter be the portion of few
to have the several element which compose our name as sydenham express it repeat a thousand year hence be a gift beyond the power of title and wealth and be scarce to be purchase unless by the sword and the pen
but to avoid the scandalous imputation while we yet live of be one whom nobody know a scandal by the bye as old as the day of homer will always be the envy portion of those who have a legal title either to honour or estate
see the 2 d odyssey ver
175
from that figure therefore which the irish peer who bring sophia to town hath already make in this history the reader will conclude doubtless it must have be an easy matter to have discover his house in london without know the particular street or square which he inhabit since he must have be one whom everybody know
to say the truth so it would have be to any of those tradesman who be accustomed to attend the region of the great for the door of the great be generally no less easy to find than it be difficult to get entrance into they
but jones as well as partridge be an entire stranger in london and as he happen to arrive first in a quarter of the town the inhabitant of which have very little intercourse with the householder of hanover or grosvenor-square for he enter through gray s-inn-lane so he ramble about some time before he could even find his way to those happy mansion where fortune segregate from the vulgar those magnanimous hero the descendant of antient britons saxons or danes whose ancestor be bear in well day by sundry kind of merit have entail rich and honour on their posterity
jones be at length arrive at those terrestrial elysian field would now soon have discover his lordship s mansion but the peer unluckily quit his former house when he go for ireland and as he be just enter into a new one the fame of his equipage have not yet sufficiently blaze in the neighbourhood so that after a successless enquiry till the clock have strike eleven jones at last yield to the advice of partridge and retreat to the bull and gate in holborn that be the inn where he have first alight and where he retire to enjoy that kind of repose which usually attend person in his circumstance
early in the morning he again set forth in pursuit of sophia and many a weary step he take to no well purpose than before
at last whether it be that fortune relent or whether it be no long in her power to disappoint he he come into the very street which be honour by his lordship s residence and be direct to the house he give one gentle rap at the door
the porter who from the modesty of the knock have conceive no high idea of the person approach conceive but little well from the appearance of mr jones who be drest in a suit of fustian and have by his side the weapon formerly purchase of the serjeant of which though the blade might be compose of well-temper steel the handle be compose only of brass and that none of the bright
when jones therefore enquire after the young lady who have come to town with his lordship this fellow answer surlily that there be no lady there jones then desire to see the master of the house but be inform that his lordship would see nobody that morning
and upon grow more press the porter say he have positive order to let no person in but if you think proper say he to leave your name i will acquaint his lordship and if you call another time you shall know when he will see you jones now declare that he have very particular business with the young lady and could not depart without see she upon which the porter with no very agreeable voice or aspect affirm that there be no young lady in that house and consequently none could he see add sure you be the strange man i ever meet with for you will not take an answer i have often think that by the particular description of cerberus the porter of hell in the 6 th aeneid virgil might possibly intend to satirize the porter of the great man in his time the picture at least resemble those who have the honour to attend at the door of our great man
the porter in his lodge answer exactly to cerberus in his den and like he must be appease by a sop before access can be gain to his master
perhaps jones might have see he in that light and have recollect the passage where the sibyl in order to procure an entrance for aeneas present the keeper of the stygian avenue with such a sop
jones in like manner now begin to offer a bribe to the human cerberus which a footman overhearing instantly advanced and declare if mr jones would give he the sum propose he would conduct he to the lady jones instantly agree and be forthwith conduct to the lodging of mrs fitzpatrick by the very fellow who have attend the lady thither the day before
nothing more aggravate ill success than the near approach to good
the gamester who lose his party at piquet by a single point lament his bad luck ten time as much as he who never come within a prospect of the game
so in a lottery the proprietor of the next number to that which win the great prize be apt to account themselves much more unfortunate than their fellow-sufferer
in short these kind of hairbreadth missing of happiness look like the insult of fortune who may be consider as thus play trick with we and wantonly divert herself at our expense
jones who more than once already have experience this frolicsome disposition of the heathen goddess be now again doom to be tantalize in the like manner for he arrive at the door of mrs fitzpatrick about ten minute after the departure of sophia
he now address himself to the waiting-woman belong to mrs fitzpatrick who tell he the disagreeable news that the lady be go but could not tell he whither and the same answer he afterwards receive from mrs fitzpatrick herself
for as that lady make no doubt but that mr jones be a person detach from her uncle western in pursuit of his daughter so she be too generous to betray she
though jones have never see mrs fitzpatrick yet he have hear that a cousin of sophia be married to a gentleman of that name
this however in the present tumult of his mind never once recur to his memory but when the footman who have conduct he from his lordship s acquaint he with the great intimacy between the lady and with their call each other cousin he then recollect the story of the marriage which he have formerly hear and as he be presently convince that this be the same woman he become more surprize at the answer which he have receive and very earnestly desire leave to wait on the lady herself but she as positively refuse he that honour
jones who though he have never see a court be well breed than most who frequent it be incapable of any rude or abrupt behaviour to a lady
when he have receive therefore a peremptory denial he retire for the present say to the waiting-woman that if this be an improper hour to wait on her lady he would return in the afternoon and that he then hop to have the honour of see she the civility with which he utter this add to the great comeliness of his person make an impression on the waiting-woman and she could not help answer perhaps sir you may and indeed she afterwards say everything to her mistress which she think most likely to prevail on she to admit a visit from the handsome young gentleman for so she call he
jones very shrewdly suspect that sophia herself be now with her cousin and be deny to he which he impute to her resentment of what have happen at upton
have therefore dispatch partridge to procure he lodging he remain all day in the street watch the door where he think his angel lie conceal but no person do he see issue forth except a servant of the house and in the evening he return to pay his visit to mrs fitzpatrick which that good lady at last condescend to admit
there be a certain air of natural gentility which it be neither in the power of dress to give nor to conceal
mr jones as hath be before hint be possess of this in a very eminent degree
he meet therefore with a reception from the lady somewhat different from what his apparel seem to demand and after he have pay she his proper respect be desire to sit down
the reader will not i believe be desirous of know all the particular of this conversation which end very little to the satisfaction of poor jones
for though mrs fitzpatrick soon discover the lover as all woman have the eye of hawk in those matter yet she still think it be such a lover as a generous friend of the lady should not betray she to
in short she suspect this be the very mr blifil from whom sophia have fly and all the answer which she artfully draw from jones concern mr allworthy s family confirm she in this opinion
she therefore strictly deny any knowledge concern the place whither sophia be go nor could jones obtain more than a permission to wait on she again the next evening
when jones be depart mrs fitzpatrick communicate her suspicion concern mr blifil to she maid who answer sure madam he be too pretty a man in my opinion for any woman in the world to run away from
i have rather fancy it be mr jones mr jones say the lady what jones for sophia have not give the least hint of any such person in all their conversation but mrs honour have be much more communicative and have acquaint her sister abigail with the whole history of jones which this now again related to her mistress
mrs fitzpatrick no soon receive this information than she immediately agree with the opinion of she maid and what be very unaccountable see charm in the gallant happy lover which she have overlook in the slight squire
betty say she you be certainly in the right he be a very pretty fellow and i donot wonder that my cousin s maid should tell you so many woman be fond of he
i be sorry now i do not inform he where my cousin be and yet if he be so terrible a rake as you tell i it be a pity she should ever see he any more for what but her ruin can happen from marry a rake and a beggar against her father s consent
i protest if he be such a man as the wench describe he to you it be but an office of charity to keep she from he and i be sure it would be unpardonable in i to do otherwise who have taste so bitterly of the misfortune attend such marriage here she be interrupt by the arrival of a visitor which be no other than his lordship and as nothing pass at this visit either new or extraordinary or any way material to this history we shall here put an end to this chapter
chapter iii
a project of mrs fitzpatrick and her visit to lady bellaston
when mrs fitzpatrick retire to rest her thought be entirely take up by her cousin sophia and mr jones
she be indeed a little offend with the former for the disingenuity which she now discover
in which meditation she have not long exercise her imagination before the follow conceit suggest itself that could she possibly become the mean of preserve sophia from this man and of restore she to her father she should in all human probability by so great a service to the family reconcile to herself both her uncle and her aunt western
as this be one of her most favourite wish so the hope of success seem so reasonable that nothing remain but to consider of proper method to accomplish her scheme
to attempt to reason the case with sophia do not appear to her one of those method for as betty have report from mrs honour that sophia have a violent inclination to jones she conceive that to dissuade she from the match be an endeavour of the same kind as it would be very heartily and earnestly to entreat a moth not to fly into a candle
if the reader will please to remember that the acquaintance which sophia have with lady bellaston be contract at the house of mrs western and must have grow at the very time when mrs fitzpatrick live with this latter lady he will want no information that mrs fitzpatrick must have be acquaint with she likewise
they be besides both equally her distant relation
after much consideration therefore she resolve to go early in the morning to that lady and endeavour to see she unknown to sophia and to acquaint she with the whole affair
for she do not in the least doubt but that the prudent lady who have often ridicule romantic love and indiscreet marriage in her conversation would very readily concur in her sentiment concern this match and would lend her utmost assistance to prevent it
this resolution she accordingly execute and the next morning before the sun she huddle on her cloath and at a very unfashionable unseasonable unvisitable hour go to lady bellaston to whom she get access without the least knowledge or suspicion of sophia who though not asleep lie at that time awake in her bed with honour snoring by her side
mrs fitzpatrick make many apology for an early abrupt visit at an hour when she say she should not have think of disturb she ladyship but upon business of the utmost consequence she then open the whole affair tell all she have hear from betty and do not forget the visit which jones have pay to herself the precede evening
lady bellaston answer with a smile then you have see this terrible man madam pray be he so very fine a figure as he be represent
for etoff entertain i last night almost two hour with he
the wench i believe be in love with he by reputation here the reader will be apt to wonder but the truth be that mrs etoff who have the honour to pin and unpin the lady bellaston have receive compleat information concern the say mr jones and have faithfully convey the same to her lady last night or rather that morning while she be undressing on which account she have be detain in her office above the space of an hour and a half
the lady indeed though generally well enough please with the narrative of mrs etoff at those season give an extraordinary attention to her account of jones for honour have describe he as a very handsome fellow and mrs etoff in her hurry add so much to the beauty of his person to her report that lady bellaston begin to conceive he to be a kind of miracle in nature
the curiosity which her woman have inspire be now greatly increase by mrs fitzpatrick who speak as much in favour of the person of jones as she have before speak in dispraise of his birth character and fortune
when lady bellaston have hear the whole she answer gravely indeed madam this be a matter of great consequence
nothing can certainly be more commendable than the part you act and i shall be very glad to have my share in the preservation of a young lady of so much merit and for whom i have so much esteem doth not your ladyship think say mrs fitzpatrick eagerly that it would be the good way to write immediately to my uncle and acquaint he where my cousin be the lady ponder a little upon this and thus answered-- why no madam i think not
di western hath describe her brother to i to be such a brute that i cannot consent to put any woman under his power who hath escape from it
i have hear he behave like a monster to his own wife for he be one of those wretch who think they have a right to tyrannise over we and from such i shall ever esteem it the cause of my sex to rescue any woman who be so unfortunate to be under their power the business dear cousin will be only to keep miss western from see this young fellow till the good company which she will have an opportunity of meeting here give she a properer turn if he should find she out madam answer the other your ladyship may be assure he will leave nothing unattempted to come at she but madam reply the lady it be impossible he should come here--though indeed it be possible he may get some intelligence where she be and then may lurk about the house--i wish therefore i know his person
be there no way madam by which i could have a sight of he
for otherwise you know cousin she may contrive to see he here without my knowledge mrs fitzpatrick answer that he have threaten she with another visit that afternoon and that if her ladyship please to do she the honour of call upon she then she would hardly fail of see he between six and seven and if he come early she would by some mean or other detain he till her ladyship s arrival lady bellaston reply she would come the moment she could get from dinner which she suppose would be by seven at farth for that it be absolutely necessary she should be acquaint with his person
upon my word madam say she it be very good to take this care of miss western but common humanity as well as regard to our family require it of we both for it would be a dreadful match indeed mrs fitzpatrick fail not to make a proper return to the compliment which lady bellaston have bestow on her cousin and after some little immaterial conversation withdraw and get as fast as she could into her chair unseen by sophia or honour return home
chapter iv
which consist of visit
mr jones have walk within sight of a certain door during the whole day which though one of the short appear to he to be one of the long in the whole year
at length the clock have strike five he return to mrs fitzpatrick who though it be a full hour early than the decent time of visit receive he very civilly but still persist in her ignorance concern sophia
jones in ask for his angel have drop the word cousin upon which mrs fitzpatrick say then sir you know we be relate and as we be you will permit i the right of enquire into the particular of your business with my cousin here jones hesitate a good while and at last answer he have a considerable sum of money of her in his hand which he desire to deliver to she he then produce the pocket-book and acquaint mrs fitzpatrick with the content and with the method in which they come into his hand
he have scarce finish his story when a most violent noise shake the whole house
to attempt to describe this noise to those who have hear it would be in vain and to aim at give any idea of it to those who have never hear the like would be still more vain for it may be truly said-- non acuta sic geminant corybantes aera the priest of cybele do not so rattle their sound brass
in short a footman knock or rather thunder at the door
jones be a little surprized at the sound have never hear it before but mrs fitzpatrick very calmly say that as some company be come she could not make he any answer now but if he please to stay till they be go she intimate she have something to say to he
the door of the room now fly open and after push in her hoop sideways before she enter lady bellaston who have first make a very low courtesy to mrs fitzpatrick and as low a one to mr jones be usher to the upper end of the room
we mention these minute matter for the sake of some country lady of our acquaintance who think it contrary to the rule of modesty to bend their knee to a man
the company be hardly well settle before the arrival of the peer lately mention cause a fresh disturbance and a repetition of ceremonial
these be over the conversation begin to be as the phrase be extremely brilliant
however as nothing past in it which can be think material to this history or indeed very material in itself i shall omit the relation the rather as i have know some very fine polite conversation grow extremely dull when transcribe into book or repeat on the stage
indeed this mental repast be a dainty of which those who be exclude from polite assembly must be content to remain as ignorant as they must of the several dainty of french cookery which be serve only at the table of the great
to say the truth as neither of these be adapt to every taste they might both be often throw away on the vulgar
poor jones be rather a spectator of this elegant scene than an actor in it for though in the short interval before the peer s arrival lady bellaston first and afterwards mrs fitzpatrick have address some of their discourse to he yet no soon be the noble lord enter than he engross the whole attention of the two lady to himself and as he take no more notice of jones than if no such person have be present unless by now and then star at he the lady follow his example
the company have now staid so long that mrs fitzpatrick plainly perceive they all design to stay out each other
she therefore resolve to rid herself of jones he be the visitant to whom she think the least ceremony be due
take therefore an opportunity of a cessation of chat she address herself gravely to he and say sir i shall not possibly be able to give you an answer to-night as to that business but if you please to leave word where i may send to you to-morrow--- jones have natural but not artificial good-breeding
instead therefore of communicate the secret of his lodging to a servant he acquaint the lady herself with it particularly and soon after very ceremoniously withdraw
he be no soon go than the great personage who have take no notice of he present begin to take much notice of he in his absence but if the reader hath already excuse we from relate the more brilliant part of this conversation he will surely be very ready to excuse the repetition of what may be call vulgar abuse though perhaps it may be material to our history to mention an observation of lady bellaston who take her leave in a few minute after he and then say to mrs fitzpatrick at her departure i be satisfied on the account of my cousin she can be in no danger from this fellow our history shall follow the example of lady bellaston and take leave of the present company which be now reduce to two person between whom as nothing pass which in the least concern we or our reader we shall not suffer ourselves to be divert by it from matter which must seem of more consequence to all those who be at all interested in the affair of our heroe
chapter v
an adventure which happen to mr jones at his lodging with some account of a young gentleman who lodge there and of the mistress of the house and her two daughter
the next morning as early as it be decent jones attend at mrs fitzpatrick s door where he be answer that the lady be not at home an answer which surprize he the more as he have walk backwards and forwards in the street from break of day and if she have go out he must have see she
this answer however he be oblige to receive and not only now but to five several visit which he make she that day
to be plain with the reader the noble peer have from some reason or other perhaps from a regard for the lady s honour insist that she should not see mr jones whom he look on as a scrub any more and the lady have comply in make that promise to which we now see she so strictly adhere
but as our gentle reader may possibly have a well opinion of the young gentleman than her ladyship and may even have some concern should it be apprehend that during this unhappy separation from sophia he take up his residence either at an inn or in the street we shall now give an account of his lodging which be indeed in a very reputable house and in a very good part of the town
mr jones then have often hear mr allworthy mention the gentlewoman at whose house he use to lodge when he be in town
this person who as jones likewise know live in bond-street be the widow of a clergyman and be leave by he at his decease in possession of two daughter and of a compleat set of manuscript sermon
of these two daughter nancy the elder be now arrive at the age of seventeen and betty the young at that of ten
hither jones have despatch partridge and in this house he be provide with a room for himself in the second floor and with one for partridge in the fourth
the first floor be inhabit by one of those young gentleman who in the last age be call man of wit and pleasure about town and properly enough for as man be usually denominate from their business or profession so pleasure may be say to have be the only business or profession of those gentleman to whom fortune have make all useful occupation unnecessary
playhouse coffeehouse and tavern be the scene of their rendezvous
wit and humour be the entertainment of their loose hour and love be the business of their more serious moment
wine and the mus conspire to kindle the bright flame in their breast nor do they only admire but some be able to celebrate the beauty they admire and all to judge of the merit of such composition
such therefore be properly call the man of wit and pleasure but i question whether the same appellation may with the same propriety be give to those young gentleman of our time who have the same ambition to be distinguish for part
wit certainly they have nothing to do with
to give they their due they soar a step high than their predecessor and may be call man of wisdom and vertù take heed you do not read virtue
thus at an age when the gentleman above mention employ their time in toast the charm of a woman or in make sonnet in her praise in give their opinion of a play at the theatre or of a poem at will s or button s these gentleman be consider the method to bribe a corporation or meditating speech for the house of commons or rather for the magazine
but the science of gaming be that which above all other employ their thought
these be the study of their graver hour while for their amusement they have the vast circle of connoisseurship painting music statuary and natural philosophy or rather unnatural which deal in the wonderful and know nothing of nature except her monster and imperfection
when jones have spend the whole day in vain enquiry after mrs fitzpatrick he return at last disconsolate to his apartment
here while he be vent his grief in private he hear a violent uproar below-stair and soon after a female voice beg he for heaven s sake to come and prevent murder
jones who be never backward on any occasion to help the distressed immediately run down-stair when step into the dining-room whence all the noise issue he behold the young gentleman of wisdom and vertù just before mention pin close to the wall by his footman and a young woman stand by wring her hand and cry out he will be murder
he will be murder and indeed the poor gentleman seem in some danger of be choake when jones fly hastily to his assistance and rescue he just as he be breathe his last from the unmerciful clutch of the enemy
though the fellow have receive several kick and cuff from the little gentleman who have more spirit than strength he have make it a kind of scruple of conscience to strike his master and would have content himself with only choake he but towards jones he bear no such respect he no soon therefore find himself a little roughly handle by his new antagonist than he give he one of those punch in the gut which though the spectator at broughton s amphitheatre have such exquisite delight in see they convey but very little pleasure in the feeling
the lusty youth have no soon receive this blow than he meditate a most grateful return and now ensue a combat between jones and the footman which be very fierce but short for this fellow be no more able to contend with jones than his master have before be to contend with he
and now fortune accord to her usual custom reverse the face of affair
the former victor lie breathless on the ground and the vanquish gentleman have recover breath enough to thank mr jones for his seasonable assistance he receive likewise the hearty thank of the young woman present who be indeed no other than miss nancy the eld daughter of the house
the footman have now recover his leg shake his head at jones and with a sagacious look cried-- o d--n i iwill have nothing more to do with you you have be upon the stage or ibe d--nably mistaken and indeed we may forgive this his suspicion for such be the agility and strength of our heroe that he be perhaps a match for one of the first-rate boxer and could with great ease have beat all the muffled graduate of mr broughton s school
lest posterity should be puzzle by this epithet i think proper to explain it by an advertisement which be publish feb
1 1747
n b mr broughton propose with proper assistance to open an academy at his house in the haymarket for the instruction of those who be willing to be initiate in the mystery of boxing where the whole theory and practice of that truly british art with all the various stop blow cross-buttock c incident to combatant will be fully teach and explain and that person of quality and distinction may not be deter from enter into a course of those lecture they will be give with the utmost tenderness and regard to the delicacy of the frame and constitution of the pupil for which reason muffle be provide that will effectually secure they from the inconveniency of black eye broken jaw and bloody nose
the master foam with wrath order his man immediately to strip to which the latter very readily agree on condition of receive his wage
this condition be presently comply with and the fellow be discharge
and now the young gentleman whose name be nightingale very strenuously insist that his deliverer should take part of a bottle of wine with he to which jones after much entreaty consent though more out of complacence than inclination for the uneasiness of his mind fit he very little for conversation at this time
miss nancy likewise who be the only female then in the house her mamma and sister be both go to the play condescend to favour they with her company
when the bottle and glass be on the table the gentleman begin to relate the occasion of the precede disturbance
i hope sir say he to jones you will not from this accident conclude that i make a custom of strike my servant for i assure you this be the first time i have be guilty of it in my remembrance and i have pass by many provoking fault in this very fellow before he could provoke i to it but when you hear what hath happen this evening you will i believe think i excusable
i happen to come home several hour before my usual time when i find four gentleman of the cloth at whist by my fire and my hoyle sir--my good hoyle which cost i a guinea lie open on the table with a quantity of porter spill on one of the most material leaf of the whole book
this you will allow be provoke but i say nothing till the rest of the honest company be go and then give the fellow a gentle rebuke who instead of express any concern make i a pert answer that servant must have their diversion as well as other people that he be sorry for the accident which have happen to the book but that several of his acquaintance have buy the same for a shilling and that i might stop as much in his wage if i please i now give he a severer reprimand than before when the rascal have the insolence to---in short he impute my early come home to----in short he cast a reflection----he mention the name of a young lady in a manner--in such a manner that incense i beyond all patience and in my passion i strike he jones answer that he believe no person live would blame he for my part say he i confess i should on the last-mention provocation have do the same thing our company have not sit long before they be join by the mother and daughter at their return from the play
and now they all spend a very chearful evening together for all but jones be heartily merry and even he put on as much constrain mirth as possible
indeed half his natural flow of animal spirit join to the sweetness of his temper be sufficient to make a most amiable companion and notwithstanding the heaviness of his heart so agreeable do he make himself on the present occasion that at their break up the young gentleman earnestly desire his further acquaintance
miss nancy be well pleased with he and the widow quite charm with her new lodger invite he with the other next morning to breakfast
jones on his part be no less satisfied
as for miss nancy though a very little creature she be extremely pretty and the widow have all the charm which can adorn a woman near fifty
as she be one of the most innocent creature in the world so she be one of the most chearful
she never think nor speak nor wish any ill and have constantly that desire of pleasing which may be call the happy of all desire in this that it scarce ever fail of attain its end when not disgrace by affectation
in short though her power be very small she be in her heart one of the warm friend
she have be a most affectionate wife and be a most fond and tender mother
as our history doth not like a newspaper give great character to people who never be hear of before nor will ever be hear of again the reader may hence conclude that this excellent woman will hereafter appear to be of some importance in our history
nor be jones a little pleased with the young gentleman himself whose wine he have be drink
he think he discern in he much good sense though a little too much taint with town-foppery but what recommend he most to jones be some sentiment of great generosity and humanity which occasionally dropt from he and particularly many expression of the high disinterestedness in the affair of love
on which subject the young gentleman deliver himself in a language which might have very well become an arcadian shepherd of old and which appear very extraordinary when proceed from the lip of a modern fine gentleman but he be only one by imitation and mean by nature for a much well character
chapter vi
what arrive while the company be at breakfast with some hint concern the government of daughter
our company bring together in the morning the same good inclination towards each other with which they have separate the evening before but poor jones be extremely disconsolate for he have just receive information from partridge that mrs fitzpatrick have leave she lodging and that he could not learn whither she be go
this news highly afflict he and his countenance as well as his behaviour in defiance of all his endeavour to the contrary betray manifest indication of a disorder mind
the discourse turn at present as before on love and mr nightingale again express many of those warm generous and disintereste sentiment upon this subject which wise and sober man call romantic but which wise and sober woman generally regard in a well light
mrs miller for so the mistress of the house be call greatly approve these sentiment but when the young gentleman appeal to miss nancy she answer only that she believe the gentleman who have speak the least be capable of feel most this compliment be so apparently direct to jones that we should have be sorry have he pass it by unregarded
he make she indeed a very polite answer and conclude with an oblique hint that her own silence subject she to a suspicion of the same kind for indeed she have scarce open her lip either now or the last evening
i be glad nanny say mrs miller the gentleman hath make the observation i protest i be almost of his opinion
what can be the matter with you child
i never see such an alteration
what be become of all your gaiety
would you think sir i use to call she my little prattler
she hath not speak twenty word this week here their conversation be interrupt by the entrance of a maid-servant who bring a bundle in her hand which she say be deliver by a porter for mr jones she add that the man immediately go away say it require no answer jones express some surprize on this occasion and declare it must be some mistake but the maid persist that she be certain of the name all the woman be desirous of have the bundle immediately open which operation be at length perform by little betsy with the consent of mr jones and the content be find to be a domino a mask and a masquerade ticket
jones be now more positive than ever in assert that these thing must have be deliver by mistake and mrs miller herself express some doubt and say she know not what to think but when mr nightingale be ask he deliver a very different opinion
all i can conclude from it sir say he be that you be a very happy man for i make no doubt but these be send you by some lady whom you will have the happiness of meeting at the masquerade jones have not a sufficient degree of vanity to entertain any such flattering imagination nor do mrs miller herself give much assent to what mr nightingale have say till miss nancy have lift up the domino a card dropt from the sleeve in which be write as follows to mr jones
the queen of the fairy send you this use she favour not amiss
mrs miller and miss nancy now both agree with mr nightingale nay jones himself be almost persuade to be of the same opinion
and as no other lady but mrs fitzpatrick he think know his lodging he begin to flatter himself with some hope that it come from she and that he might possibly see his sophia
these hope have surely very little foundation but as the conduct of mrs fitzpatrick in not see he accord to her promise and in quit her lodging have be very odd and unaccountable he conceive some faint hope that she of whom he have formerly hear a very whimsical character might possibly intend to do he that service in a strange manner which she decline do by more ordinary method
to say the truth as nothing certain could be conclude from so odd and uncommon an incident he have the great latitude to draw what imaginary conclusion from it he please
as his temper therefore be naturally sanguine he indulge it on this occasion and his imagination work up a thousand conceit to favour and support his expectation of meet his dear sophia in the evening
reader if thou hast any good wish towards i i will fully repay they by wish thee to be possess of this sanguine disposition of mind since after have read much and consider long on that subject of happiness which hath employ so many great pen i be almost inclined to fix it in the possession of this temper which put we in a manner out of the reach of fortune and make we happy without her assistance
indeed the sensation of pleasure it give be much more constant as well as much keener than those which that blind lady bestow nature have wisely contrive that some satiety and languor should be annex to all our real enjoyment lest we should be so take up by they as to be stopt from further pursuit
i make no manner of doubt but that in this light we may see the imaginary future chancellor just call to the bar the archbishop in crape and the prime minister at the tail of an opposition more truly happy than those who be invest with all the power and profit of those respective office
mr jones have now determine to go to the masquerade that evening mr nightingale offer to conduct he thither
the young gentleman at the same time offer ticket to miss nancy and her mother but the good woman would not accept they
she say she do not conceive the harm which some people imagine in a masquerade but that such extravagant diversion be proper only for person of quality and fortune and not for young woman who be to get their living and could at good hope to be married to a good tradesman a tradesman cry nightingale you shallnot undervalue my nancy
there be not a nobleman upon earth above her merit o fie
mr nightingale answer mrs miller you must not fill the girl s head with such fancy but if it be her good luck say the mother with a simper to find a gentleman of your generous way of thinking i hope she would make a well return to his generosity than to give she mind up to extravagant pleasure
indeed where young lady bring great fortune themselves they have some right to insist on spend what be their own and on that account i have hear the gentleman say a man have sometimes a well bargain with a poor wife than with a rich one but let my daughter marry whom they will i shall endeavour to make they blessing to their husbands i beg therefore i may hear of no more masquerade
nancy be i be certain too good a girl to desire to go for she must remember when you carry she thither last year it almost turn her head and she do not return to herself or to her needle in a month afterwards though a gentle sigh which steal from the bosom of nancy seem to argue some secret disapprobation of these sentiment she do not dare openly to oppose they
for as this good woman have all the tenderness so she have preserve all the authority of a parent and as her indulgence to the desire of her child be restrain only by she fear for their safety and future welfare so she never suffer those command which proceed from such fear to be either disobey or dispute
and this the young gentleman who have lodge two year in the house know so well that he presently acquiesce in the refusal
mr nightingale who grow every minute fonder of jones be very desirous of his company that day to dinner at the tavern where he offer to introduce he to some of his acquaintance but jones beg to be excuse as his cloath he say be not yet come to town to confess the truth mr jones be now in a situation which sometimes happen to be the case of young gentleman of much well figure than himself
in short he have not one penny in his pocket a situation in much great credit among the antient philosopher than among the modern wise man who live in lombard-street or those who frequent white s chocolate-house
and perhaps the great honour which those philosopher have ascribe to an empty pocket may be one of the reason of that high contempt in which they be hold in the aforesaid street and chocolate-house
now if the antient opinion that man might live very comfortably on virtue only be as the modern wise man just above-mention pretend to have discover a notorious error no less false be i apprehend that position of some writer of romance that a man can live altogether on love for however delicious repast this may afford to some of our sens or appetite it be most certain it can afford none to other
those therefore who have place too great a confidence in such writer have experience their error when it be too late and have find that love be no more capable of allay hunger than a rise be capable of delight the ear or a violin of gratify the smell
notwithstanding therefore all the delicacy which love have set before he namely the hope of see sophia at the masquerade on which however ill-found his imagination might be he have voluptuously feast during the whole day the evening no soon come than mr jones begin to languish for some food of a gross kind
partridge discover this by intuition and take the occasion to give some oblique hint concern the bank-bill and when these be reject with disdain he collect courage enough once more to mention a return to mr allworthy
partridge cry jones you cannot see my fortune in a more desperate light than i see it myself and i begin heartily to repent that i suffer you to leave a place where you be settle and to follow i
however i insist now on your return home and for the expense and trouble which you have so kindly put yourself to on my account all the cloath i leave behind in your care i desire you would take as your own
i be sorry i can make you no other acknowledgment he speak these word with so pathetic an accent that partridge among whose vice ill-nature or hardness of heart be not number burst into tear and after swear he would not quit he in his distress he begin with the most earnest entreaty to urge his return home
for heaven s sake sir say he do but consider what can your honour do how be it possible you can live in this town without money
do what you will sir or go wherever you please i be resolve not to desert you
but pray sir consider--do pray sir for your own sake take it into your consideration and ibe sure say he that your own good sense will bid you return home how often shall i tell thee answer jones that i have no home to return to
have i any hope that mr allworthy s door would be open to receive i i want no distress to urge i--nay there be no other cause upon earth which could detain i a moment from fly to his presence but alas
that i be for ever banish from
his last word be--o partridge they still ring in my ear--his last word be when he give i a sum of money--what it be i know not but considerable ibe sure it be--his last word were-- i be resolve from this day forward on no account to converse with you any more here passion stopt the mouth of jones as surprize for a moment do that of partridge but he soon recover the use of speech and after a short preface in which he declare he have no inquisitiveness in his temper enquire what jones mean by a considerable sum--he know not how much--and what be become of the money
in both these point he now receive full satisfaction on which he be proceed to comment when he be interrupt by a message from mr nightingale who desire his master s company in his apartment
when the two gentleman be both attire for the masquerade and mr nightingale have give order for chair to be send for a circumstance of distress occur to jones which will appear very ridiculous to many of my reader
this be how to procure a shilling but if such reader will reflect a little on what they have themselves feel from the want of a thousand pound or perhaps of ten or twenty to execute a favourite scheme they will have a perfect idea of what mr jones feel on this occasion
for this sum therefore he apply to partridge which be the first he have permit he to advance and be the last he intend that poor fellow should advance in his service
to say the truth partridge have lately make no offer of this kind
whether it be that he desire to see the bank-bill break in upon or that distress should prevail on jones to return home or from what other motive it proceed i will not determine
chapter vii
contain the whole humour of a masquerade
our cavalier now arrive at that temple where heydegger the great arbiter deliciarum the great high-priest of pleasure preside and like other heathen priest impose on his votary by the pretended presence of the deity when in reality no such deity be there
mr nightingale have take a turn or two with his companion soon leave he and walk off with a female say now you be here sir you must beat about for your own game jones begin to entertain strong hope that his sophia be present and these hope give he more spirit than the light the music and the company though these be pretty strong antidote against the spleen
he now accost every woman he see whose stature shape or air bore any resemblance to his angel
to all of whom he endeavour to say something smart in order to engage an answer by which he might discover that voice which he think it impossible he should mistake
some of these answer by a question in a squeaking voice do you know i
much the great number say i donot know you sir and nothing more
some call he an impertinent fellow some make he no answer at all some say indeed i donot know your voice and i shall have nothing to say to you and many give he as kind answer as he could wish but not in the voice he desire to hear
whilst he be talk with one of these last who be in the habit of a shepherdess a lady in a domino come up to he and slap he on the shoulder whisper he at the same time in the ear if you talk any long with that trollop i will acquaint miss western jones no soon hear that name than immediately quit his former companion he apply to the domino beg and entreat she to show he the lady she have mention if she be then in the room
the mask walk hastily to the upper end of the innermost apartment before she speak and then instead of answer he sit down and declare she be tired
jones sit down by she and still persist in his entreaty at last the lady coldly answer i imagine mr jones have be a more discerning lover than to suffer any disguise to conceal his mistress from he be she here then madam reply jones with some vehemence
upon which the lady cried-- hush sir you will be observe
i promise you upon my honour miss western be not here jones now take the mask by the hand fall to entreat she in the most earnest manner to acquaint he where he might find sophia and when he could obtain no direct answer he begin to upbraid she gently for have disappoint he the day before and conclude say indeed my good fairy queen i know your majesty very well notwithstanding the affect disguise of your voice
indeed mrs fitzpatrick it be a little cruel to divert yourself at the expense of my torment the mask answer though you have so ingeniously discover i i must still speak in the same voice lest i should be know by other
and do you think good sir that i have no great regard for my cousin than to assist in carry on an affair between you two which must end in her ruin as well as your own
besides i promise you my cousin be not mad enough to consent to her own destruction if you be so much her enemy as to tempt she to it alas madam say jones you little know my heart when you call i an enemy of sophia and yet to ruin any one cry the other you will allow be the act of an enemy and when by the same act you must knowingly and certainly bring ruin on yourself be it not folly or madness as well as guilt
now sir my cousin hath very little more than her father will please to give she very little for one of her fashion--you know he and you know your own situation jones vow he have no such design on sophia that he would rather suffer the most violent of death than sacrifice her interest to his desire he say he know how unworthy he be of she every way that he have long ago resolve to quit all such aspire thought but that some strange accident have make he desirous to see she once more when he promise he would take leave of she for ever
no madam conclude he my love be not of that base kind which seek its own satisfaction at the expense of what be most dear to its object
i would sacrifice everything to the possession of my sophia but sophia herself though the reader may have already conceive no very sublime idea of the virtue of the lady in the mask and though possibly she may hereafter appear not to deserve one of the first character of her sex yet it be certain these generous sentiment make a strong impression upon she and greatly add to the affection she have before conceive for our young heroe
the lady now after silence of a few moment say she do not see his pretension to sophia so much in the light of presumption as of imprudence
young fellow say she can never have too aspiring thought
i love ambition in a young man and i would have you cultivate it as much as possible
perhaps you may succeed with those who be infinitely superior in fortune nay i be convinced there be women----but donot you think i a strange creature mr jones to be thus give advice to a man with whom i be so little acquainted and one with whose behaviour to i i have so little reason to be please here jones begin to apologize and to hope he have not offend in anything he have say of her cousin to which the mask answer and be you so little verse in the sex to imagine you can well affront a lady more than by entertain she with your passion for another woman
if the fairy queen have conceive no well opinion of your gallantry she would scarce have appoint you to meet she at the masquerade jones have never less inclination to an amour than at present but gallantry to the lady be among his principle of honour and he hold it as much incumbent on he to accept a challenge to love as if it have be a challenge to fight
nay his very love to sophia make it necessary for he to keep well with the lady as he make no doubt but she be capable of bring he into the presence of the other
he begin therefore to make a very warm answer to her last speech when a mask in the character of an old woman join they
this mask be one of those lady who go to a masquerade only to vent ill-nature by tell people rude truth and by endeavour as the phrase be to spoil as much sport as they be able
this good lady therefore have observe jones and his friend whom she well know in close consultation together in a corner of the room conclude she could nowhere satisfy her spleen well than by interrupt they
she attack they therefore and soon drive they from their retirement nor be she content with this but pursue they to every place which they shift to avoid she till mr nightingale see the distress of his friend at last relieve he and engage the old woman in another pursuit
while jones and his mask be walk together about the room to rid themselves of the teazer he observe his lady speak to several mask with the same freedom of acquaintance as if they have be bareface
he could not help express his surprize at this say sure madam you must have infinite discernment to know people in all disguise to which the lady answer you cannot conceive anything more insipid and childish than a masquerade to the people of fashion who in general know one another as well here as when they meet in an assembly or a drawing-room nor will any woman of condition converse with a person with whom she be not acquaint
in short the generality of person whom you see here may more properly be say to kill time in this place than in any other and generally retire from hence more tired than from the long sermon
to say the truth i begin to be in that situation myself and if i have any faculty at guess you be not much well pleased
i protest it would be almost charity in i to go home for your sake i know but one charity equal to it cry jones and that be to suffer i to wait on you home sure answer the lady you have a strange opinion of i to imagine that upon such an acquaintance i would let you into my door at this time of night
i fancy you impute the friendship i have show my cousin to some other motive
confess honestly donot you consider this contrived interview as little well than a downright assignation
be you use mr jones to make these sudden conquest i be not use madam say jones to submit to such sudden conquest but as you have take my heart by surprize the rest of my body hath a right to follow so you must pardon i if i resolve to attend you wherever you go he accompany these word with some proper action upon which the lady after a gentle rebuke and say their familiarity would be observe tell he she be go to sup with an acquaintance whither she hop he would not follow she for if you should say she i shall be think an unaccountable creature though my friend indeed be not censorious yet i hope you willnot follow i i protest i shall not know what to say if you do the lady presently after quit the masquerade and jones notwithstanding the severe prohibition he have receive presume to attend she
he be now reduce to the same dilemma we have mention before namely the want of a shilling and could not relieve it by borrowing as before
he therefore walk boldly on after the chair in which his lady rode pursue by a grand huzza from all the chairman present who wisely take the good care they can to discountenance all walk afoot by their better
luckily however the gentry who attend at the opera-house be too busy to quit their station and as the lateness of the hour prevent he from meet many of their brother in the street he proceed without molestation in a dress which at another season would have certainly raise a mob at his heel
the lady be set down in a street not far from hanover-square where the door be presently open she be carry in and the gentleman without any ceremony walk in after she
jones and his companion be now together in a very well-furnish and well-warm room when the female still speak in her masquerade voice say she be surprize at her friend who must absolutely have forget her appointment at which after vent much resentment she suddenly expr some apprehension from jones and ask he what the world would think of their have be alone together in a house at that time of night
but instead of a direct answer to so important a question jones begin to be very importunate with the lady to unmask and at length have prevail there appear not mrs fitzpatrick but the lady bellaston herself
it would be tedious to give the particular conversation which consist of very common and ordinary occurrence and which last from two till six o clock in the morning
it be sufficient to mention all of it that be anywise material to this history
and this be a promise that the lady would endeavour to find out sophia and in a few day bring he to an interview with she on condition that he would then take his leave of she
when this be thoroughly settle and a second meeting in the evening appoint at the same place they separate the lady return to her house and jones to his lodging
chapter viii
contain a scene of distress which will appear very extraordinary to most of our reader
jones have refresh himself with a few hour sleep summon partridge to his presence and deliver he a bank-note of fifty pound order he to go and change it
partridge receive this with sparkle eye though when he come to reflect far it raise in he some suspicion not very advantageous to the honour of his master to these the dreadful idea he have of the masquerade the disguise in which his master have go out and return and his have be abroad all night contribute
in plain language the only way he could possibly find to account for the possession of this note be by robbery and to confess the truth the reader unless he should suspect it be owe to the generosity of lady bellaston can hardly imagine any other
to clear therefore the honour of mr jones and to do justice to the liberality of the lady he have really receive this present from she who though she do not give much into the hackney charity of the age such as building hospital c be not however entirely void of that christian virtue and conceive very rightly i think that a young fellow of merit without a shilling in the world be no improper object of this virtue
mr jones and mr nightingale have be invite to dine this day with mrs miller
at the appoint hour therefore the two young gentleman with the two girl attend in the parlour where they wait from three till almost five before the good woman appear
she have be out of town to visit a relation of whom at her return she give the follow account
i hope gentleman you will pardon my making you wait i be sure if you know the occasion--i have be to see a cousin of mine about six mile off who now lie in it should be a warning to all person say she look at her daughter how they marry indiscreetly
there be no happiness in this world without a competency
o nancy
how shall i describe the wretched condition in which i find your poor cousin
she hath scarce lain in a week and there be she this dreadful weather in a cold room without any curtain to her bed and not a bushel of coal in her house to supply she with fire her second son that sweet little fellow lie ill of a quinzy in the same bed with his mother for there be no other bed in the house
poor little tommy
i believe nancy you will never see your favourite any more for he be really very ill
the rest of the child be in pretty good health but molly i be afraid will do herself an injury she be but thirteen year old mr nightingale and yet in my life i never see a well nurse she tend both her mother and her brother and what be wonderful in a creature so young she show all the chearfulness in the world to her mother and yet i see she--i see the poor child mr nightingale turn about and privately wipe the tear from her eye here mrs miller be prevent by her own tear from go on and there be not i believe a person present who do not accompany she in they at length she a little recover herself and proceed thus in all this distress the mother support her spirit in a surprizing manner
the danger of her son sit heavy upon she and yet she endeavour as much as possible to conceal even this concern on her husband s account
her grief however sometimes get the well of all her endeavour for she be always extravagantly fond of this boy and a most sensible sweet-temper creature it be
i protest i be never more affected in my life than when i hear the little wretch who be hardly yet seven year old while his mother be wet he with her tear beg she to be comfort
indeed mamma cry the child i shallnot die god almighty ibe sure willnot take tommy away let heaven be ever so fine a place i have rather stay here and starve with you and my papa than go to it pardon i gentleman i cannot help it say she wipe her eye such sensibility and affection in a child and yet perhaps he be least the object of pity for a day or two will most probably place he beyond the reach of all human evil
the father be indeed most worthy of compassion
poor man his countenance be the very picture of horror and he look like one rather dead than alive
oh heaven
what a scene do i behold at my first come into the room
the good creature be lie behind the bolster support at once both his child and his wife
he have nothing on but a thin waistcoat for his coat be spread over the bed to supply the want of blankets when he rise up at my entrance i scarce know he
as comely a man mr jones within this fortnight as you ever beheld mr nightingale hath see he
his eye sunk his face pale with a long beard
his body shiver with cold and wear with hunger too for my cousin say she can hardly prevail upon he to eat he tell i himself in a whisper--he tell i--i cannot repeat it--he say he could not bear to eat the bread his child want
and yet can you believe it gentleman
in all this misery his wife have as good caudle as if she lie in the midst of the great affluence i taste it and i scarce ever taste better the mean of procure she this he say he believe be send he by an angel from heaven
i know not what he mean for i have not spirit enough to ask a single question
this be a love-match as they call it on both side that be a match between two beggar
i must indeed say i never see a fonder couple but what be their fondness good for but to torment each other indeed mamma cry nancy i have always look on my cousin anderson for that be her name as one of the happy of woman i be sure say mrs miller the case at present be much otherwise for any one might have discern that the tender consideration of each other s suffering make the most intolerable part of their calamity both to the husband and wife
compare to which hunger and cold as they affect their own person only be scarce evil
nay the very child the young which be not two year old excepted feel in the same manner for they be a most love family and if they have but a bare competency would be the happy people in the world i never see the least sign of misery at her house reply nancy i be sure my heart bleed for what you now tell i o child answer the mother she hath always endeavour to make the good of everything
they have always be in great distress but indeed this absolute ruin hath be bring upon they by other
the poor man be bail for the villain his brother and about a week ago the very day before her lie-in their good be all carry away and sell by an execution
he send a letter to i of it by one of the bailiff which the villain never delivered what must he think of my suffering a week to pass before he hear of i it be not with dry eye that jones hear this narrative when it be end he take mrs miller apart with he into another room and deliver she his purse in which be the sum of 50 desire she to send as much of it as she think proper to these poor people
the look which mrs miller give jones on this occasion be not easy to be describe
she burst into a kind of agony of transport and cry out-- good heaven
be there such a man in the world but recollect herself she say indeed i know one such but can there be another i hope madam cry jones there be many who have common humanity for to relieve such distress in our fellow-creature can hardly be call more mrs miller then take ten guinea which be the utmost he could prevail with she to accept and say she would find some mean of convey they early the next morning add that she have herself do some little matter for the poor people and have not leave they in quite so much misery as she find they they then return to the parlour where nightingale express much concern at the dreadful situation of these wretch whom indeed he know for he have see they more than once at mrs miller s
he inveigh against the folly of make oneself liable for the debt of other vent many bitter execration against the brother and conclude with wish something could be do for the unfortunate family
suppose madam say he you should recommend they to mr allworthy
or what think you of a collection
i will give they a guinea with all my heart mrs miller make no answer and nancy to whom her mother have whisper the generosity of jones turn pale upon the occasion though if either of they be angry with nightingale it be surely without reason
for the liberality of jones if he have know it be not an example which he have any obligation to follow and there be thousand who would not have contribute a single halfpenny as indeed he do not in effect for he make no tender of anything and therefore as the other think proper to make no demand he keep his money in his pocket
i have in truth observe and shall never have a well opportunity than at present to communicate my observation that the world be in general divide into two opinion concern charity which be the very reverse of each other
one party seem to hold that all act of this kind be to be esteem as voluntary gift and however little you give if indeed no more than your good wish you acquire a great degree of merit in so do
other on the contrary appear to be as firmly persuade that beneficence be a positive duty and that whenever the rich fall greatly short of their ability in relieve the distress of the poor their pitiful largess be so far from be meritorious that they have only perform their duty by half and be in some sense more contemptible than those who have entirely neglect it
to reconcile these different opinion be not in my power
i shall only add that the giver be generally of the former sentiment and the receiver be almost universally inclined to the latter
chapter ix
which treat of matter of a very different kind from those in the precede chapter
in the evening jones meet his lady again and a long conversation again ensue between they but as it consist only of the same ordinary occurrence as before we shall avoid mention particular which we despair of render agreeable to the reader unless he be one whose devotion to the fair sex like that of the papist to their saint want to be raise by the help of picture
but i be so far from desire to exhibit such picture to the public that i would wish to draw a curtain over those that have be lately set forth in certain french novel very bungling copy of which have be present we here under the name of translation
jones grow still more and more impatient to see sophia and find after repeat interview with lady bellaston no likelihood of obtain this by her mean for on the contrary the lady begin to treat even the mention of the name of sophia with resentment he resolve to try some other method
he make no doubt but that lady bellaston know where his angel be so he think it most likely that some of her servant should be acquaint with the same secret
partridge therefore be employ to get acquaint with those servant in order to fish this secret out of they
few situation can be imagine more uneasy than that to which his poor master be at present reduce for besides the difficulty he meet with in discover sophia besides the fear he have of have disoblige she and the assurance he have receive from lady bellaston of the resolution which sophia have take against he and of she have purposely conceal herself from he which he have sufficient reason to believe might be true he have still a difficulty to combat which it be not in the power of his mistress to remove however kind her inclination might have be
this be the exposing of she to be disinherit of all her father s estate the almost inevitable consequence of their come together without a consent which he have no hope of ever obtain
add to all these the many obligation which lady bellaston whose violent fondness we can no long conceal have heap upon he so that by her mean he be now become one of the well-dress man about town and be not only relieve from those ridiculous distress we have before mention but be actually raise to a state of affluence beyond what he have ever know
now though there be many gentleman who very well reconcile it to their conscience to possess themselves of the whole fortune of a woman without make she any kind of return yet to a mind the proprietor of which doth not deserve to be hang nothing be i believe more irksome than to support love with gratitude only especially where inclination pull the heart a contrary way
such be the unhappy case of jones for though the virtuous love he bear to sophia and which leave very little affection for any other woman have be entirely out of the question he could never have be able to have make any adequate return to the generous passion of this lady who have indeed be once an object of desire but be now enter at least into the autumn of life though she wear all the gaiety of youth both in her dress and manner nay she contrive still to maintain the rose in her cheek but these like flower force out of season by art have none of that lively bloom freshness with which nature at the proper time bedeck her own production
she have besides a certain imperfection which render some flower though very beautiful to the eye very improper to be place in a wilderness of sweet and what above all other be most disagreeable to the breath of love
though jones see all these discouragement on the one side he feel his obligation full as strongly on the other nor do he less plainly discern the ardent passion whence those obligation proceed the extreme violence of which if he fail to equal he well know the lady would think he ungrateful and what be bad he would have think himself so
he know the tacit consideration upon which all her favour be confer and as his necessity oblige he to accept they so his honour he conclude force he to pay the price
this therefore he resolve to do whatever misery it cost he and to devote himself to she from that great principle of justice by which the law of some country oblige a debtor who be no otherwise capable of discharge his debt to become the slave of his creditor
while he be meditate on these matter he receive the follow note from the lady a very foolish but a very perverse accident hath happen since our last meeting which make it improper i should see you any more at the usual place
i will if possible contrive some other place by to-morrow
in the meantime adieu this disappointment perhaps the reader may conclude be not very great but if it be he be quickly relieve for in less than an hour afterwards another note be bring he from the same hand which contain as follows i have alter my mind since i write a change which if you be no strange to the tender of all passion you will not wonder at
i be now resolve to see you this evening at my own house whatever may be the consequence
come to i exactly at seven i dine abroad but will be at home by that time
a day i find to those that sincerely love seem long than i imagine
if you should accidentally be a few moment before i bid they show you into the drawing-room to confess the truth jones be less pleased with this last epistle than he have be with the former as he be prevent by it from comply with the earnest entreaty of mr nightingale with whom he have now contract much intimacy and friendship
these entreaty be to go with that young gentleman and his company to a new play which be to be act that evening and which a very large party have agree to damn from some dislike they have take to the author who be a friend to one of mr nightingale s acquaintance
and this sort of fun our heroe we be ashamed to confess would willingly have prefer to the above kind appointment but his honour get the well of his inclination
before we attend he to this intended interview with the lady we think proper to account for both the precede note as the reader may possibly be not a little surprized at the imprudence of lady bellaston in bring her lover to the very house where her rival be lodge
first then the mistress of the house where these lover have hitherto meet and who have be for some year a pensioner to that lady be now become a methodist and have that very morning wait upon her ladyship and after rebuke she very severely for her past life have positively declare that she would on no account be instrumental in carry on any of her affair for the future
the hurry of spirit into which this accident throw the lady make she despair of possibly find any other convenience to meet jones that evening but as she begin a little to recover from her uneasiness at the disappointment she set her thought to work when luckily it come into her head to propose to sophia to go to the play which be immediately consent to and a proper lady provide for her companion
mrs honour be likewise despatch with mrs etoff on the same errand of pleasure and thus her own house be leave free for the safe reception of mr jones with whom she promise herself two or three hour of uninterrupted conversation after her return from the place where she din which be at a friend s house in a pretty distant part of the town near her old place of assignation where she have engage herself before she be well apprized of the revolution that have happen in the mind and moral of her late confidante
chapter x
a chapter which though short may draw tear from some eye
mr jones be just dressed to wait on lady bellaston when mrs miller rap at his door and be admit very earnestly desire his company below-stair to drink tea in the parlour
upon his entrance into the room she presently introduce a person to he say this sir be my cousin who hath be so greatly beholden to your goodness for which he beg to return you his sincere thank the man have scarce enter upon that speech which mrs miller have so kindly preface when both jones and he look stedfastly at each other show at once the utmost token of surprize
the voice of the latter begin instantly to faulter and instead of finish his speech he sink down into a chair cry it be so i be convince it be so bless i
whatbe the meaning of this cry mrs miller you be not ill i hope cousin
some water a dram this instant be not fright madam cry jones i have almost as much need of a dram as your cousin
we be equally surprize at this unexpected meeting
your cousin be an acquaintance of mine mrs miller an acquaintance cry the man oh heaven ay an acquaintance repeat jones and an honour acquaintance too
when i do not love and honour the man who dare venture everything to preserve his wife and child from instant destruction may i have a friend capable of disown i in adversity oh you be an excellent young man cry mrs miller yes indeed poor creature
he hath venture everything if he have not have one of the good of constitution it must have kill he cousin cry the man who have now pretty well recover himself this be the angel from heaven whom i mean
this be he to whom before i see you i owe the preservation of my peggy
he it be to whose generosity every comfort every support which i have procure for she be owe
he be indeed the worthy bravest noblest of all human being
