my cousin tell i she acquaint you with the distress in which she find we
that sir be all greatly remove and chiefly by your goodness my child have now a bed to lie on----and they have----they have----eternal blessing reward you for it they have bread to eat
my little boy be recover my wife be out of danger and i be happy
all all owe to you sir and to my cousin here one of the good of woman
indeed sir i must see you at my house indeed my wife must see you and thank you my child too must express their gratitude indeed sir they be not without a sense of their obligation but what be my feeling when i reflect to whom i owe that they be now capable of express their gratitude oh sir the little heart which you have warm have now be cold as ice without your assistance here jones attempt to prevent the poor man from proceeding but indeed the overflowing of his own heart would of itself have stop his word
and now mrs miller likewise begin to pour forth thanksgiving as well in her own name as in that of her cousin and conclude with say she doubt not but such goodness would meet a glorious reward jones answer he have be sufficiently reward already
your cousin s account madam say he hath give i a sensation more pleasing than i have ever know
he must be a wretch who be unmove at hear such a story how transporting then must be the thought of have happily act a part in this scene
if there be man who cannot feel the delight of give happiness to other i sincerely pity they as they be incapable of taste what be in my opinion a great honour a high interest and a sweet pleasure than the ambitious the avaricious or the voluptuous man can ever obtain the hour of appointment be now come jones be force to take a hasty leave but not before he have heartily shake his friend by the hand and desire to see he again as soon as possible promise that he would himself take the first opportunity of visit he at his own house
he then stept into his chair and proceed to lady bellaston s greatly exult in the happiness which he have procure to this poor family nor could he forbear reflect without horror on the dreadful consequence which must have attend they have he listen rather to the voice of strict justice than to that of mercy when he be attack on the high road
mrs miller sing forth the praise of jones during the whole evening in which mr anderson while he stay so passionately accompany she that he be often on the very point of mention the circumstance of the robbery
however he luckily recollect himself and avoid an indiscretion which would have be so much the great as he know mrs miller to be extremely strict and nice in her principle
he be likewise well apprized of the loquacity of this lady and yet such be his gratitude that it have almost get the well both of discretion and shame and make he publish that which would have defame his own character rather than omit any circumstance which might do the full honour to his benefactor
chapter xi
in which the reader will be surprize
mr jones be rather early than the time appoint and early than the lady whose arrival be hinder not only by the distance of the place where she din but by some other cross accident very vexatious to one in her situation of mind
he be accordingly show into the drawing-room where he have not be many minute before the door open and in came----no other than sophia herself who have leave the play before the end of the first act for this as we have already say be a new play at which two large party meet the one to damn and the other to applaud a violent uproar and an engagement between the two party have so terrify our heroine that she be glad to put herself under the protection of a young gentleman who safely convey she to her chair
as lady bellaston have acquaint she that she should not be at home till late sophia expect to find no one in the room come hastily in and go directly to a glass which almost front she without once look towards the upper end of the room where the statue of jones now stand motionless in this glass it be after contemplate her own lovely face that she first discover the say statue when instantly turn about she perceive the reality of the vision upon which she give a violent scream and scarce preserve herself from faint till jones be able to move to she and support she in his arm
to paint the look or thought of either of these lover be beyond my power
as their sensation from their mutual silence may be judge to have be too big for their own utterance it cannot be suppose that i should be able to express they and the misfortune be that few of my reader have be enough in love to feel by their own heart what past at this time in theirs
after a short pause jones with faultere accent said-- i see madam you be surprize surprize answer she oh heaven
indeed i be surprize

oh
my sophia do you know the thousand torment i have suffer in this long fruitless pursuit pursuit of whom say sophia a little recollect herself and assume a reserve air can you be so cruel to ask that question cry jones need i say of you of i answer sophia hath mr jones then any such important business with me to some madam cry jones this might seem an important business give she the pocket-book
i hope madam you will find it of the same value as when it be lose sophia take the pocket-book and be go to speak when he interrupt she thus let we not i beseech you lose one of these precious moment which fortune hath so kindly send we
o my sophia
i have business of a much superior kind
thus on my knee let i ask your pardon my pardon cry she sure sir after what be past you cannot expect after what i have hear i scarce know what i say answer jones
by heaven
i scarce wish you should pardon i
o my sophia
henceforth never cast away a thought on such a wretch as i be
if any remembrance of i should ever intrude to give a moment s uneasiness to that tender bosom think of my unworthiness and let the remembrance of what pass at upton blot i for ever from your mind sophia stand tremble all this while
her face be white than snow and her heart be throb through her stay
but at the mention of upton a blush arise in her cheek and her eye which before she have scarce lift up be turn upon jones with a glance of disdain
he understand this silent reproach and reply to it thus o my sophia
my only love
you cannot hate or despise i more for what happen there than i do myself but yet do i the justice to think that my heart be never unfaithful to you
that have no share in the folly i be guilty of it be even then unalterably your
though i despair of possess you nay almost of ever see you more i doate still on your charming idea and could seriously love no other woman
but if my heart have not be engage she into whose company i accidently fall at that curse place be not an object of serious love
believe i my angel i never have see she from that day to this and never intend or desire to see she again sophia in her heart be very glad to hear this but force into her face an air of more coldness than she have yet assume why say she mr jones do you take the trouble to make a defence where you be not accuse
if i think it worth while to accuse you i have a charge of unpardonable nature indeed what be it for heaven s sake answer jones tremble and pale expect to hear of his amour with lady bellaston
oh say she how be it possible
can everything noble and everything base be lodge together in the same bosom lady bellaston and the ignominious circumstance of have be keep rise again in his mind and stopt his mouth from any reply
could i have expect proceed sophia such treatment from you
nay from any gentleman from any man of honour
to have my name traduce in public in inn among the mean vulgar
to have any little favour that my unguarded heart may have too lightly betray i to grant boast of there
nay even to hear that you have be force to fly from my love nothing could equal jones s surprize at these word of sophia but yet not be guilty he be much less embarrassed how to defend himself than if she have touch that tender string at which his conscience have be alarm
by some examination he presently find that she suppose he guilty of so shocking an outrage against his love and her reputation be entirely owe to partridge s talk at the inn before landlord and servant for sophia confess to he it be from they that she receive her intelligence
he have no very great difficulty to make she believe that he be entirely innocent of an offence so foreign to his character but she have a great deal to hinder he from go instantly home and put partridge to death which he more than once swear he would do
this point be clear up they soon find themselves so well pleased with each other that jones quite forget he have begin the conversation with conjure she to give up all thought of he and she be in a temper to have give ear to a petition of a very different nature for before they be aware they have both go so far that he let fall some word that sound like a proposal of marriage
to which she reply that do not her duty to her father forbid she to follow her own inclination ruin with he would be more welcome to she than the most affluent fortune with another man at the mention of the word ruin he start let drop her hand which he have hold for some time and strike his breast with his own cry out oh sophia
can i then ruin thee
no by heaven no
i never will act so base a part
dearest sophia whatever it cost i i will renounce you i will give you up i will tear all such hope from my heart as be inconsistent with your real good
my love i will ever retain but it shall be in silence it shall be at a distance from you it shall be in some foreign land from whence no voice no sigh of my despair shall ever reach and disturb your ear
and when i be dead he would have go on but be stopt by a flood of tear which sophia let fall in his bosom upon which she lean without be able to speak one word
he kiss they off which for some moment she allow he to do without any resistance but then recollect herself gently withdraw out of his arm and to turn the discourse from a subject too tender and which she find she could not support bethink herself to ask he a question she never have time to put to he before how he come into that room he begin to stammer and would in all probability have raise her suspicion by the answer he be go to give when at once the door open and in come lady bellaston
have advance a few step and see jones and sophia together she suddenly stopt when after a pause of a few moment recollect herself with admirable presence of mind she say--though with sufficient indication of surprize both in voice and countenance-- i thought miss western you have be at the play though sophia have no opportunity of learning of jones by what mean he have discover she yet as she have not the least suspicion of the real truth or that jones and lady bellaston be acquaint so she be very little confound and the less as the lady have in all their conversation on the subject entirely take her side against her father
with very little hesitation therefore she go through the whole story of what have happen at the play-house and the cause of her hasty return
the length of this narrative give lady bellaston an opportunity of rally her spirit and of consider in what manner to act

your ladyship may be pleased to remember i have often mention the loss of my pocket-book which this gentleman have very luckily find be so kind to return it to i with the bill in it jones ever since the arrival of lady bellaston have be ready to sink with fear
he sit kick his heel play with his finger and look more like a fool if it be possible than a young booby squire when he be first introduce into a polite assembly
he begin however now to recover himself and take a hint from the behaviour of lady bellaston who he see do not intend to claim any acquaintance with he he resolve as entirely to affect the stranger on his part
he say ever since he have the pocket-book in his possession he have use great diligence in enquire out the lady whose name be writ in it but never till that day could be so fortunate to discover she sophia have indeed mention the loss of her pocket-book to lady bellaston but as jones for some reason or other have never once hint to she that it be in his possession she believe not one syllable of what sophia now say and wonderfully admire the extreme quickness of the young lady in invent such an excuse
the reason of sophia s leave the playhouse meet with no well credit and though she could not account for the meeting between these two lover she be firmly persuade it be not accidental
with an affected smile therefore she say indeed miss western you have have very good luck in recover your money

i think you would not consent to have it advertised it be great good fortune sir that you find out to whom the note belong oh madam cry jones it be enclose in a pocket-book in which the young lady s name be write that be very fortunate indeed cry the lady and it be no less so that you hear miss western be at my house for she be very little known jones have at length perfectly recover his spirit and as he conceive he have now an opportunity of satisfy sophia as to the question she have ask he just before lady bellaston come in he proceed thus why madam answer he it be by the lucky chance imaginable i make this discovery

chapter xii
in which the thirteenth book be conclude
the elegant lord shaftesbury somewhere object to tell too much truth by which it may be fairly infer that in some case to lie be not only excusable but commendable
and surely there be no person who may so properly challenge a right to this commendable deviation from truth as young woman in the affair of love for which they may plead precept education and above all the sanction nay i may say the necessity of custom by which they be restrain not from submit to the honest impulse of nature for that would be a foolish prohibition but from own they
we be not therefore ashamed to say that our heroine now pursue the dictate of the above-mention right honourable philosopher
as she be perfectly satisfied then that lady bellaston be ignorant of the person of jones so she determine to keep she in that ignorance though at the expense of a little fib
jones have not be long go before lady bellaston cry upon my word a good pretty young fellow i wonder who he be for i donot remember ever to have see his face before nor i neither madam cry sophia
i must say he behave very handsomely in relation to my note yes and he be a very handsome fellow say the lady donot you think so i do not take much notice of he answer sophia but i think he seem rather awkward and ungenteel than otherwise you be extremely right cry lady bellaston you may see by his manner that he hath not keep good company
nay notwithstanding his return your note and refuse the reward i almost question whether he be a gentleman i have always observe there be a something in person well bear which other can never acquire i think i will give order not to be at home to he nay sure madam answer sophia one cannot suspect after what he hath done beside if your ladyship observe he there be an elegance in his discourse a delicacy a prettiness of expression that that---- i confess say lady bellaston the fellow hath words----and indeed sophia you must forgive i indeed you must i forgive your ladyship say sophia

book xiv
contain two day
chapter i
an essay to prove that an author will write the well for have some knowledge of the subject on which he write
as several gentleman in these time by the wonderful force of genius only without the least assistance of learning perhaps without be well able to read have make a considerable figure in the republic of letter the modern critic i be tell have lately begin to assert that all kind of learning be entirely useless to a writer and indeed no other than a kind of fetter on the natural sprightliness and activity of the imagination which be thus weigh down and prevent from soar to those high flight which otherwise it would be able to reach
this doctrine i be afraid be at present carry much too far for why should write differ so much from all other art
the nimbleness of a dancing-master be not at all prejudice by be teach to move nor doth any mechanic i believe exercise his tool the bad by have learn to use they
for my own part i cannot conceive that homer or virgil would have writ with more fire if instead of be master of all the learning of their time they have be as ignorant as most of the author of the present age
nor do i believe that all the imagination fire and judgment of pitt could have produce those oration that have make the senate of england in these our time a rival in eloquence to greece and rome if he have not be so well read in the writing of demosthenes and cicero as to have transfer their whole spirit into his speech and with their spirit their knowledge too
i would not here be understand to insist on the same fund of learn in any of my brother as cicero persuade we be necessary to the composition of an orator
on the contrary very little reading be i conceive necessary to the poet less to the critic and the least of all to the politician
for the first perhaps byshe s art of poetry and a few of our modern poet may suffice for the second a moderate heap of play and for the last an indifferent collection of political journal
to say the truth i require no more than that a man should have some little knowledge of the subject on which he treat accord to the old maxim of law quam quisque nôrit artem in eâ se exerceat
with this alone a writer may sometimes do tolerably well and indeed without this all the other learning in the world will stand he in little stead
for instance let we suppose that homer and virgil aristotle and cicero thucydides and livy could have meet all together and have club their several talent to have compose a treatise on the art of dancing i believe it will be readily agree they could not have equal the excellent treatise which mr essex hath give we on that subject entitle the rudiment of genteel education
and indeed should the excellent mr broughton be prevail on to set fist to paper and to complete the above-say rudiment by deliver down the true principle of athletic i question whether the world will have any cause to lament that none of the great writer either antient or modern have ever treat about that noble and useful art
to avoid a multiplicity of example in so plain a case and to come at once to my point i be apt to conceive that one reason why many english writer have totally fail in describe the manner of upper life may possibly be that in reality they know nothing of it
this be a knowledge unhappily not in the power of many author to arrive at
book will give we a very imperfect idea of it nor will the stage a much well the fine gentleman form upon read the former will almost always turn out a pedant and he who form himself upon the latter a coxcomb
nor be the character draw from these model well support
vanbrugh and congreve copy nature but they who copy they draw as unlike the present age as hogarth would do if he be to paint a rout or a drum in the dress of titian and of vandyke
in short imitation here will not do the business
the picture must be after nature herself
a true knowledge of the world be gain only by conversation and the manner of every rank must be see in order to be know
now it happen that this high order of mortal be not to be see like all the rest of the human specie for nothing in the street shop and coffee-house nor be they show like the upper rank of animal for so much a-piece
in short this be a sight to which no person be admit without one or other of these qualification viz either birth or fortune or what be equivalent to both the honourable profession of a gamester
and very unluckily for the world person so qualified very seldom care to take upon themselves the bad trade of writing which be generally enter upon by the low and poor sort as it be a trade which many think require no kind of stock to set up with
hence those strange monster in lace and embroidery in silk and brocade with vast wig and hoop which under the name of lord and lady strut the stage to the great delight of attorney and their clerk in the pit and of the citizen and their apprentice in the gallery and which be no more to be find in real life than the centaur the chimera or any other creature of mere fiction
but to let my reader into a secret this knowledge of upper life though very necessary for prevent mistake be no very great resource to a writer whose province be comedy or that kind of novel which like this i be write be of the comic class
what mr pope say of woman be very applicable to most in this station who be indeed so entirely make up of form and affectation that they have no character at all at least none which appear
i will venture to say the high life be much the dull and afford very little humour or entertainment
the various calling in low sphere produce the great variety of humorous character whereas here except among the few who be engage in the pursuit of ambition and the few still who have a relish for pleasure all be vanity and servile imitation
dressing and card eat and drink bow and courtesying make up the business of their life
some there be however of this rank upon whom passion exercise its tyranny and hurry they far beyond the bound which decorum prescribe of these the lady be as much distinguish by their noble intrepidity and a certain superior contempt of reputation from the frail one of mean degree as a virtuous woman of quality be by the elegance and delicacy of her sentiment from the honest wife of a yeoman and shopkeeper
lady bellaston be of this intrepid character but let not my country reader conclude from she that this be the general conduct of woman of fashion or that we mean to represent they as such
they might as well suppose that every clergyman be represent by thwackum or every soldier by ensign northerton
there be not indeed a great error than that which universally prevail among the vulgar who borrow their opinion from some ignorant satirist have affix the character of lewdness to these time
on the contrary i be convinced there never be less of love intrigue carry on among person of condition than now
our present woman have be teach by their mother to fix their thought only on ambition and vanity and to despise the pleasure of love as unworthy their regard and be afterwards by the care of such mother marry without have husband they seem pretty well confirm in the justness of those sentiment whence they content themselves for the dull remainder of life with the pursuit of more innocent but i be afraid more childish amusement the bare mention of which would ill suit with the dignity of this history
in my humble opinion the true characteristic of the present beau monde be rather folly than vice and the only epithet which it deserve be that of frivolous

chapter v
a short account of the history of mrs miller
jones this day eat a pretty good dinner for a sick man that be to say the large half of a shoulder of mutton
in the afternoon he receive an invitation from mrs miller to drink tea for that good woman have learn either by mean of partridge or by some other mean natural or supernatural that he have a connexion with mr allworthy could not endure the thought of part with he in an angry manner
jones accept the invitation and no soon be the tea-kettle remove and the girl send out of the room than the widow without much preface begin as follow well there be very surprizing thing happen in this world but certainly it be a wonderful business that i should have a relation of mr allworthy in my house and never know anything of the matter
alas
sir you little imagine what a friend that good of gentleman hath be to i and mine
yes sir i be not ashamed to own it it be owe to his goodness that i do not long since perish for want and leave my poor little wretch two destitute helpless friendless orphan to the care or rather to the cruelty of the world
you must know sir though i be now reduce to get my living by let lodging i be bear and breed a gentlewoman
my father be an officer of the army and die in a considerable rank but he live up to his pay and as that expire with he his family at his death become beggar
we be three sister
one of we have the good luck to die soon after of the small-pox a lady be so kind as to take the second out of charity as she say to wait upon she
the mother of this lady have be a servant to my grand-mother and have inherit a vast fortune from her father which he have get by pawnbroke be married to a gentleman of great estate and fashion
she use my sister so barbarously often upbraid she with her birth and poverty call she in derision a gentlewoman that i believe she at length break the heart of the poor girl
in short she likewise die within a twelvemonth after my father
fortune think proper to provide well for i and within a month from his decease i be married to a clergyman who have be my lover a long time before and who have be very ill use by my father on that account for though my poor father could not give any of we a shilling yet he breed we up as delicately consider we and would have have we consider ourselves as highly as if we have be the rich heiress
but my dear husband forget all this usage and the moment we be become fatherless he immediately renew his address to i so warmly that i who always like and now more than ever esteem he soon comply
five year do i live in a state of perfect happiness with that good of man till at last--oh
cruel
cruel fortune that ever separate we that deprive i of the kind of husband and my poor girl of the tender parent o my poor girl
you never know the blessing which ye lost i be ashamed mr jones of this womanish weakness but i shall never mention he without tear i ought rather madam say jones to be ashamed that i do not accompany you well sir continue she i be now leave a second time in a much bad condition than before besides the terrible affliction i be to encounter i have now two child to provide for and be if possible more pennyless than ever when that great that good that glorious man mr allworthy who have some little acquaintance with my husband accidentally hear of my distress and immediately writ this letter to i
here sir here it be i put it into my pocket to shew it you
this be the letter sir i must and will read it to you
madam i heartily condole with you on your late grievous loss which your own good sense and the excellent lesson you must have learn from the worthy of man will well enable you to bear than any advice which i be capable of give
nor have i any doubt that you whom i have hear to be the tender of mother will suffer any immoderate indulgence of grief to prevent you from discharge your duty to those poor infant who now alone stand in need of your tenderness
however as you must be suppose at present to be incapable of much worldly consideration you will pardon my have order a person to wait on you and to pay you twenty guineas which i beg you will accept till i have the pleasure of see you and believe i to be madam c this letter sir i receive within a fortnight after the irreparable loss i have mention and within a fortnight afterwards mr allworthy--the blessed mr allworthy come to pay i a visit when he place i in the house where you now see i give i a large sum of money to furnish it and settle an annuity of 50 a-year upon i which i have constantly receive ever since
judge then mr jones in what regard i must hold a benefactor to whom i owe the preservation of my life and of those dear child for whose sake alone my life be valuable
do not therefore think i impertinent mr jones since i must esteem one for whom i know mr allworthy hath so much value if i beg you not to converse with these wicked woman
you be a young gentleman and do not know half their artful wile
do not be angry with i sir for what i say upon account of my house you must be sensible it would be the ruin of my poor dear girl
besides sir you cannot but be acquaint that mr allworthy himself would never forgive my conniving at such matter and particularly with you upon my word madam say jones you need make no farth apology nor do i in the least take anything ill you have say but give i leave as no one can have more value than myself for mr allworthy to deliver you from one mistake which perhaps would not be altogether for his honour i do assure you i be no relation of his alas
sir answer she i know you be not i know very well who you be for mr allworthy hath tell i all but i do assure you have you be twenty time his son he could not have express more regard for you than he hath often express in my presence
you need not be ashamed sir of what you be i promise you no good person will esteem you the less on that account
no mr jones the word dishonourable birth be nonsense as my dear dear husband use to say unless the word dishonourable be apply to the parent for the child can derive no real dishonour from an act of which they be intirely innocent here jones heave a deep sigh and then say since i perceive madam you really do know i and mr allworthy hath think proper to mention my name to you and since you have be so explicit with i as to your own affair i will acquaint you with some more circumstance concern myself and these mrs miller have express great desire and curiosity to hear he begin and relate to she his whole history without once mention the name of sophia
there be a kind of sympathy in honest mind by mean of which they give an easy credit to each other
mrs miller believe all which jones tell she to be true and expr much pity and concern for he

mrs miller be at length prevail on and jones depart to his chamber where he sit alone till twelve o clock but no lady bellaston appear
as we have say that this lady have a great affection for jones and as it must have appear that she really have so the reader may perhaps wonder at the first failure of her appointment as she apprehend he to be confine by sickness a season when friendship seem most to require such visit
this behaviour therefore in the lady may by some be condemn as unnatural but that be not our fault for our business be only to record truth
chapter vi

chapter vii
the interview between mr jones and mr nightingale
the good or evil we confer on other very often i believe recoil on ourselves
for as man of a benign disposition enjoy their own act of beneficence equally with those to whom they be do so there be scarce any nature so entirely diabolical as to be capable of do injury without pay themselves some pang for the ruin which they bring on their fellow-creature
mr nightingale at least be not such a person
on the contrary jones find he in his new lodging sit melancholy by the fire and silently lament the unhappy situation in which he have place poor nancy
he no soon see his friend appear than he arise hastily to meet he and after much congratulation say nothing could be more opportune than this kind visit for i be never more in the spleen in my life i be sorry answer jones that i bring news very unlikely to relieve you nay what i be convinced must of all other shock you the most
however it be necessary you should know it
without further preface then i come to you mr nightingale from a worthy family which you have involve in misery and ruin mr nightingale change colour at these word but jones without regard it proceed in the lively manner to paint the tragical story with which the reader be acquaint in the last chapter
nightingale never once interrupt the narration though he discover violent emotion at many part of it
but when it be conclude after fetch a deep sigh he say what you tell i my friend affect i in the tender manner
sure there never be so cursed an accident as the poor girl s betray my letter
her reputation might otherwise have be safe and the affair might have remain a profound secret and then the girl might have go off never the bad for many such thing happen in this town and if the husband should suspect a little when it be too late it will be his wise conduct to conceal his suspicion both from his wife and the world indeed my friend answer jones this could not have be the case with your poor nancy
you have so entirely gain her affection that it be the loss of you and not of her reputation which afflict she and will end in the destruction of she and her family nay for that matter i promise you cry nightingale she hath my affection so absolutely that my wife whoever she be to be will have very little share in they and be it possible then say jones you can think of desert she why what can i do answer the other
ask miss nancy reply jones warmly
in the condition to which you have reduce she i sincerely think she ought to determine what reparation you shall make she
her interest alone and not your ought to be your sole consideration
but if you ask i what you shall do what can you do less cry jones than fulfil the expectation of her family and her own
nay i sincerely tell you they be mine too ever since i first see you together
you will pardon i if i presume on the friendship you have favour i with move as i be with compassion for those poor creature
but your own heart will well suggest to you whether you have never intend by your conduct to persuade the mother as well as the daughter into an opinion that you design honourably and if so though there may have be no direct promise of marriage in the case i will leave to your own good understanding how far you be bind to proceed nay i must not only confess what you have hint say nightingale but i be afraid even that very promise you mention i have give and can you after own that say jones hesitate a moment consider my friend answer the other i know you be a man of honour and would advise no one to act contrary to its rule if there be no other objection can i after this publication of she disgrace think of such an alliance with honour undoubtedly reply jones and the very good and true honour which be goodness require it of you
as you mention a scruple of this kind you will give i leave to examine it
can you with honour be guilty of have under false pretence deceive a young woman and her family and of have by these mean treacherously rob she of her innocence
can you with honour be the knowing the wilful occasion nay the artful contriver of the ruin of a human being
can you with honour destroy the fame the peace nay probably both the life and soul too of this creature
can honour bear the thought that this creature be a tender helpless defenceless young woman
a young woman who love who doat on you who die for you who hath place the utmost confidence in your promise and to that confidence hath sacrifice everything which be dear to she
can honour support such contemplation as these a moment common sense indeed say nightingale warrant all you say but yet you well know the opinion of the world be so contrary to it that be i to marry a whore though my own i should be ashamed of ever show my face again fie upon it mr nightingale say jones do not call she by so ungenerous a name when you promise to marry she she become your wife and she hath sin more against prudence than virtue
and what be this world which you would be ashamed to face but the vile the foolish and the profligate
forgive i if i say such a shame must proceed from false modesty which always attend false honour as its shadow but i be well assured there be not a man of real sense and goodness in the world who would not honour and applaud the action
but admit no other would would not your own heart my friend applaud it
and do not the warm rapturous sensation which we feel from the consciousness of an honest noble generous benevolent action convey more delight to the mind than the undeserved praise of million
set the alternative fairly before your eye
on the one side see this poor unhappy tender believe girl in the arm of her wretched mother breathe she last
hear her break heart in agony sigh out your name and lamenting rather than accuse the cruelty which weigh she down to destruction
paint to your imagination the circumstance of her fond despairing parent drive to madness or perhaps to death by the loss of her lovely daughter
view the poor helpless orphan infant and when your mind hath dwelt a moment only on such idea consider yourself as the cause of all the ruin of this poor little worthy defenceless family
on the other side consider yourself as relieve they from their temporary suffering
think with what joy with what transport that lovely creature will fly to your arm
see her blood return to her pale cheek her fire to her languid eye and rapture to her tortured breast
consider the exultation of her mother the happiness of all
think of this little family make by one act of yours completely happy
think of this alternative and sure i be mistaken in my friend if it require any long deliberation whether he will sink these wretch down for ever or by one generous noble resolution raise they all from the brink of misery and despair to the high pitch of human happiness
add to this but one consideration more the consideration that it be your duty so to do--that the misery from which you will relieve these poor people be the misery which you yourself have wilfully bring upon they o my dear friend cry nightingale i want not your eloquence to rouse i
i pity poor nancy from my soul and would willingly give anything in my power that no familiarity have ever pass between we
nay believe i i have many struggle with my passion before i could prevail with myself to write that cruel letter which hath cause all the misery in that unhappy family
if i have no inclination to consult but my own i would marry she to-morrow morning i would by heaven
but you will easily imagine how impossible it would be to prevail on my father to consent to such a match besides he hath provide another for i and to-morrow by his express command i be to wait on the lady i have not the honour to know your father say jones but suppose he could be persuade would you yourself consent to the only mean of preserve these poor people as eagerly as i would pursue my happiness answer nightingale for i never shall find it in any other woman o my dear friend
could you imagine what i have feel within these twelve hour for my poor girl i be convinced she would not engross all your pity
passion lead i only to she and if i have any foolish scruple of honour you have fully satisfy they could my father be induce to comply with my desire nothing would be want to compleat my own happiness or that of my nancy then i be resolve to undertake it say jones
you must not be angry with i in whatever light it may be necessary to set this affair which you may depend on it could not otherwise be long hid from he for thing of this nature make a quick progress when once they get abroad as this unhappily hath already
besides should any fatal accident follow as upon my soul i be afraid will unless immediately prevent the public would ring of your name in a manner which if your father hath common humanity must offend he
if you will therefore tell i where i may find the old gentleman i will not lose a moment in the business which while i pursue you cannot do a more generous action than by pay a visit to the poor girl
you will find i have not exaggerate in the account i have give of the wretchedness of the family nightingale immediately consent to the proposal and now have acquaint jones with his father s lodging and the coffee-house where he would most probably find he he hesitate a moment and then say my dear tom you be go to undertake an impossibility
if you know my father you would never think of obtain his consent stay there be one way--suppose you tell he i be already marry it might be easy to reconcile he to the fact after it be do and upon my honour i be so affected with what you have say and i love my nancy so passionately i almost wish it be do whatever might be the consequence jones greatly approve the hint and promise to pursue it
they then separate nightingale to visit his nancy and jones in quest of the old gentleman
chapter viii
what pass between jones and old mr nightingale with the arrival of a person not yet mention in this history
notwithstanding the sentiment of the roman satirist which deny the divinity of fortune and the opinion of seneca to the same purpose cicero who be i believe a wise man than either of they expressly hold the contrary and certain it be there be some incident in life so very strange and unaccountable that it seem to require more than human skill and foresight in produce they
of this kind be what now happen to jones who find mr nightingale the elder in so critical a minute that fortune if she be really worthy all the worship she receive at rome could not have contrive such another
in short the old gentleman and the father of the young lady whom he intend for his son have be hard at it for many hour and the latter be just now go and have leave the former delight with the thought that he have succeed in a long contention which have be between the two father of the future bride and bridegroom in which both endeavour to overreach the other and as it not rarely happen in such case both have retreat fully satisfied of have obtain the victory
this gentleman whom mr jones now visit be what they call a man of the world that be to say a man who direct his conduct in this world as one who be fully persuade there be no other be resolve to make the most of this
in his early year he have be breed to trade but have acquire a very good fortune he have lately decline his business or to speak more properly have change it from deal in good to deal only in money of which he have always a plentiful fund at command and of which he know very well how to make a very plentiful advantage sometimes of the necessity of private man and sometimes of those of the public
he have indeed converse so entirely with money that it may be almost doubted whether he imagine there be any other thing really exist in the world this at least may be certainly aver that he firmly believe nothing else to have any real value
the reader will i fancy allow that fortune could not have cull out a more improper person for mr jones to attack with any probability of success nor could the whimsical lady have direct this attack at a more unseasonable time
as money then be always uppermost in this gentleman s thought so the moment he see a stranger within his door it immediately occur to his imagination that such stranger be either come to bring he money or to fetch it from he
and accord as one or other of these thought prevail he conceive a favourable or unfavourable idea of the person who approach he
unluckily for jones the latter of these be the ascendant at present for as a young gentleman have visit he the day before with a bill from his son for a play debt he apprehend at the first sight of jones that he be come on such another errand
jones therefore have no soon tell he that he be come on his son s account than the old gentleman be confirm in his suspicion burst forth into an exclamation that he would lose his labour be it then possible sir answer jones that you can guess my business if i do guess it reply the other i repeat again to you you will lose your labour
what i suppose you be one of those spark who lead my son into all those scene of riot and debauchery which will be his destruction
but i shall pay no more of his bill i promise you
i expect he will quit all such company for the future
if i have imagine otherwise i should not have provide a wife for he for i would be instrumental in the ruin of nobody how sir say jones and be this lady of your providing pray sir answer the old gentleman how come it to be any concern of yours nay dear sir reply jones be not offend that i interest myself in what regard your son s happiness for whom i have so great an honour and value
it be upon that very account i come to wait upon you
i cannot express the satisfaction you have give i by what you say for i do assure you your son be a person for whom i have the high honour nay sir it be not easy to express the esteem i have for you who could be so generous so good so kind so indulgent to provide such a match for your son a woman who i dare swear will make he one of the happy man upon earth there be scarce anything which so happily introduce man to our good liking as have conceive some alarm at their first appearance when once those apprehension begin to vanish we soon forget the fear which they occasion and look on ourselves as indebted for our present ease to those very person who at first raise our fear
thus it happen to nightingale who no soon find that jones have no demand on he as he suspect than he begin to be pleased with his presence
pray good sir say he be pleased to sit down
i do not remember to have ever have the pleasure of see you before but if you be a friend of my son and have anything to say concern this young lady i shall be glad to hear you
as to she make he happy it will be his own fault if she doth not
i have discharge my duty in take care of the main article
she will bring he a fortune capable of make any reasonable prudent sober man happy undoubtedly cry jones for she be in herself a fortune so beautiful so genteel so sweet-temper and so well-educate she be indeed a most accomplished young lady sing admirably well and hath a most delicate hand at the harpsichord i do not know any of these matter answer the old gentleman for i never see the lady but i do not like she the bad for what you tell i and i be the well pleased with her father for not lay any stress on these qualification in our bargain
i shall always think it a proof of his understanding
a silly fellow would have bring in these article as an addition to her fortune but to give he his due he never mention any such matter though to be sure they be no disparagement to a woman i do assure you sir cry jones she hath they all in the most eminent degree for my part i own i be afraid you might have be a little backward a little less inclined to the match for your son tell i you have never see the lady therefore i come sir in that case to entreat you to conjure you as you value the happiness of your son not to be averse to his match with a woman who hath not only all the good quality i have mention but many more if that be your business sir say the old gentleman we be both oblige to you and you may be perfectly easy for i give you my word i be very well satisfied with her fortune sir answer jones i honour you every moment more and more
to be so easily satisfied so very moderate on that account be a proof of the soundness of your understanding as well as the nobleness of your mind not so very moderate young gentleman not so very moderate answer the father still more and more noble reply jones and give i leave to add sensible for sure it be little less than madness to consider money as the sole foundation of happiness
such a woman as this with her little her nothing of a fortune i find cry the old gentleman you have a pretty just opinion of money my friend or else you be well acquaint with the person of the lady than with her circumstance
why pray what fortune do you imagine this lady to have what fortune cry jones why too contemptible a one to be name for your son well well well say the other perhaps he might have do well that i deny say jones for she be one of the good of woman ay ay but in point of fortune i mean answer the other
and yet as to that now how much do you imagine your friend be to have how much cry jones how much
why at the utmost perhaps 200 do you mean to banter i young gentleman say the father a little angry
no upon my soul answer jones i be in earnest nay i believe i have go to the utmost farthing
if i do the lady an injury i ask her pardon indeed you do cry the father i be certain she hath fifty time that sum and she shall produce fifty to that before i consent that she shall marry my son nay say jones it be too late to talk of consent now if she have not fifty farthing your son be marry my son marry answer the old gentleman with surprize
nay say jones i think you be unacquainted with it my son marry to miss harris answer he again
to miss harris say jones no sir to miss nancy miller the daughter of mrs miller at whose house he lodge a young lady who though her mother be reduce to let lodgings-- are you bantering or be you in earnest cry the father with a most solemn voice
indeed sir answer jones i scorn the character of a banterer
i come to you in most serious earnest imagine as i find true that your son have never dare acquaint you with a match so much inferior to he in point of fortune though the reputation of the lady will suffer it no long to remain a secret while the father stand like one strike suddenly dumb at this news a gentleman come into the room and salute he by the name of brother
but though these two be in consanguinity so nearly related they be in their disposition almost the opposite to each other
the brother who now arrive have likewise be breed to trade in which he no soon see himself worth 6000 than he purchase a small estate with the great part of it and retire into the country where he marry the daughter of an unbeneficed clergyman a young lady who though she have neither beauty nor fortune have recommend herself to his choice entirely by her good humour of which she possess a very large share
with this woman he have during twenty-five year live a life more resemble the model which certain poet ascribe to the golden age than any of those pattern which be furnish by the present time
by she he have four child but none of they arrive at maturity except only one daughter whom in vulgar language he and his wife have spoil that is have educate with the utmost tenderness and fondness which she return to such a degree that she have actually refuse a very extraordinary match with a gentleman a little turn of forty because she could not bring herself to part with her parent
the young lady whom mr nightingale have intend for his son be a near neighbour of his brother and an acquaintance of his niece and in reality it be upon the account of his project match that he be now come to town not indeed to forward but to dissuade his brother from a purpose which he conceive would inevitably ruin his nephew for he foresaw no other event from a union with miss harris notwithstanding the largeness of her fortune as neither her person nor mind seem to he to promise any kind of matrimonial felicity for she be very tall very thin very ugly very affected very silly and very ill-natured
his brother therefore no soon mention the marriage of his nephew with miss miller than he expr the utmost satisfaction and when the father have very bitterly revile his son and pronounce sentence of beggary upon he the uncle begin in the following manner if you be a little cool brother i would ask you whether you love your son for his sake or for your own
you would answer i suppose and so i suppose you think for his sake and doubtless it be his happiness which you intend in the marriage you propose for he
now brother to prescribe rule of happiness to other hath always appear to i very absurd and to insist on do this very tyrannical
it be a vulgar error i know but it be nevertheless an error
and if this be absurd in other thing it be mostly so in the affair of marriage the happiness of which depend entirely on the affection which subsist between the party
i have therefore always think it unreasonable in parent to desire to chuse for their child on this occasion since to force affection be an impossible attempt nay so much doth love abhor force that i know not whether through an unfortunate but uncurable perverseness in our nature it may not be even impatient of persuasion
it be however true that though a parent will not i think wisely prescribe he ought to be consult on this occasion and in strictness perhaps should at least have a negative voice
my nephew therefore i own in marrying without ask your advice hath be guilty of a fault
but honestly speak brother have you not a little promote this fault
have not your frequent declaration on this subject give he a moral certainty of your refusal where there be any deficiency in point of fortune
nay doth not your present anger arise solely from that deficiency
and if he hath fail in his duty here do you not as much exceed that authority when you absolutely bargain with he for a woman without his knowledge whom you yourself never see and whom if you have see and know as well as i it must have be madness in you to have ever think of bring she into your family
still i own my nephew in a fault but surely it be not an unpardonable fault
he hath act indeed without your consent in a matter in which he ought to have ask it but it be in a matter in which his interest be principally concerned you yourself must and will acknowledge that you consult his interest only and if he unfortunately differed from you and hath be mistaken in his notion of happiness will you brother if you love your son carry he still wide from the point
will you increase the ill consequence of his simple choice
will you endeavour to make an event certain misery to he which may accidentally prove so
in a word brother because he hath put it out of your power to make his circumstance as affluent as you would will you distress they as much as you can by the force of the true catholic faith st anthony win upon the fish
orpheus and amphion go a little far and by the charm of music enchant thing merely inanimate
wonderful both
but neither history nor fable have ever yet venture to record an instance of any one who by force of argument and reason hath triumph over habitual avarice
mr nightingale the father instead of attempt to answer his brother content himself with only observe that they have always differ in their sentiment concern the education of their child
i wish say he brother you would have confine your care to your own daughter and never have trouble yourself with my son who hath i believe as little profit by your precept as by your example for young nightingale be his uncle s godson and have live more with he than with his father
so that the uncle have often declare he love his nephew almost equally with his own child
jones fall into rapture with this good gentleman and when after much persuasion they find the father grow still more and more irritated instead of appeased jones conduct the uncle to his nephew at the house of mrs miller
chapter ix
contain strange matter
at his return to his lodging jones find the situation of affair greatly alter from what they have be in at his departure
the mother the two daughter and young mr nightingale be now sit down to supper together when the uncle be at his own desire introduce without any ceremony into the company to all of whom he be well know for he have several time visit his nephew at that house
the old gentleman immediately walk up to miss nancy salute and wish her joy as he do afterwards the mother and the other sister and lastly he pay the proper compliment to his nephew with the same good humour and courtesy as if his nephew have marry his equal or superior in fortune with all the previous requisite first perform
miss nancy and her suppose husband both turn pale and look rather foolish than otherwise upon the occasion but mrs miller take the first opportunity of withdraw and have send for jones into the dining-room she throw herself at his foot and in a most passionate flood of tear call he her good angel the preserver of her poor little family with many other respectful and endear appellation and make he every acknowledgment which the high benefit can extract from the most grateful heart
after the first gust of her passion be a little over which she declare if she have not vent would have burst she she proceed to inform mr jones that all matter be settle between mr nightingale and her daughter and that they be to be marry the next morning at which mr jones have express much pleasure the poor woman fall again into a fit of joy and thanksgiving which he at length with difficulty silence and prevail on she to return with he back to the company whom they find in the same good humour in which they have leave they
this little society now past two or three very agreeable hour together in which the uncle who be a very great lover of his bottle have so well ply his nephew that this latter though not drunk begin to be somewhat fluster and now mr nightingale take the old gentleman with he upstairs into the apartment he have lately occupy unbosomed himself as follows as you have be always the good and kind of uncle to i and as you have show such unparalleled goodness in forgive this match which to be sure may be think a little improvident i should never forgive myself if i attempt to deceive you in anything he then confess the truth and open the whole affair
how jack say the old gentleman and be you really then not married to this young woman no upon my honour answer nightingale i have tell you the simple truth my dear boy cry the uncle kiss he i be heartily glad to hear it
i never be well pleased in my life
if you have be marry i should have assist you as much as be in my power to have make the good of a bad matter but there be a great difference between consider a thing which be already do and irrecoverable and that which be yet to do
let your reason have fair play jack and you will see this match in so foolish and preposterous a light that there will be no need of any dissuasive argument how sir reply young nightingale be there this difference between have already do an act and be in honour engage to do it pugh say the uncle honour be a creature of the world s making and the world hath the power of a creator over it and may govern and direct it as they please
now you well know how trivial these breach of contract be think even the gross make but the wonder and conversation of a day
be there a man who afterwards will be more backward in give you his sister or daughter
or be there any sister or daughter who would be more backward to receive you
honour be not concerned in these engagement pardon i dear sir cry nightingale i can never think so and not only honour but conscience and humanity be concerned
i be well satisfied that be i now to disappoint the young creature her death would be the consequence and i should look upon myself as her murderer nay as her murderer by the cruell of all method by break her heart break her heart indeed
no no jack cry the uncle the heart of woman be not so soon break they be tough boy they be tough but sir answer nightingale my own affection be engage and i never could be happy with any other woman
how often have i hear you say that child should be always suffer to chuse for themselves and that you would let my cousin harriet do so why ay reply the old gentleman so i would have they but then i would have they chuse wisely indeed jack you must and shall leave the girl indeed uncle cry the other i must and will have she you will young gentleman say the uncle i do not expect such a word from you
i should not wonder if you have use such language to your father who hath always treat you like a dog and keep you at the distance which a tyrant preserve over his subject but i who have live with you upon an equal footing might surely expect well usage but i know how to account for it all it be all owe to your preposterous education in which i have have too little share
there be my daughter now whom i have bring up as my friend never doth anything without my advice nor ever refuse to take it when i give it she you have never yet give her advice in an affair of this kind say nightingale for i be greatly mistaken in my cousin if she would be very ready to obey even your most positive command in abandon she inclination donot abuse my girl answer the old gentleman with some emotion donot abuse my harriet
i have bring she up to have no inclination contrary to my own
by suffer she to do whatever she please i have enure she to a habit of be pleased to do whatever i like pardon i sir say nightingale i have not the least design to reflect on my cousin for whom i have the great esteem and indeed i be convinced you will never put she to so severe a tryal or lie such hard command on she as you would do on me but dear sir let we return to the company for they will begin to be uneasy at our long absence
i must beg one favour of my dear uncle which be that he would not say anything to shock the poor girl or her mother oh
you need not fear i answer he i understand myself too well to affront woman so i will readily grant you that favour and in return i must expect another of you there be but few of your command sir say nightingale which i shall not very chearfully obey nay sir i ask nothing say the uncle but the honour of your company home to my lodging that i may reason the case a little more fully with you for i would if possible have the satisfaction of preserve my family notwithstanding the headstrong folly of my brother who in his own opinion be the wise man in the world nightingale who well know his uncle to be as headstrong as his father submit to attend he home and then they both return back into the room where the old gentleman promise to carry himself with the same decorum which he have before maintain
chapter x
a short chapter which conclude the book
the long absence of the uncle and nephew have occasion some disquiet in the mind of all whom they have leave behind they and the more as during the precede dialogue the uncle have more than once elevate his voice so as to be hear downstair which though they could not distinguish what he say have cause some evil foreboding in nancy and her mother and indeed even in jones himself
when the good company therefore again assemble there be a visible alteration in all their face and the good-humour which at their last meeting universally shine forth in every countenance be now change into a much less agreeable aspect
it be a change indeed common enough to the weather in this climate from sunshine to cloud from june to december
this alteration be not however greatly remark by any present for as they be all now endeavour to conceal their own thought and to act a part they become all too busily engage in the scene to be spectator of it
thus neither the uncle nor nephew see any symptom of suspicion in the mother or daughter nor do the mother or daughter remark the overact complacence of the old man nor the counterfeit satisfaction which grin in the feature of the young one
something like this i believe frequently happen where the whole attention of two friend be engage in the part which each be to act in order to impose on the other neither see nor suspect the art practise against himself and thus the thrust of both to borrow no improper metaphor on the occasion alike take place
from the same reason it be no unusual thing for both party to be overreach in a bargain though the one must be always the great loser as be he who sell a blind horse and receive a bad note in payment
our company in about half an hour break up and the uncle carry off his nephew but not before the latter have assure miss nancy in a whisper that he would attend she early in the morning and fulfil all his engagement
jones who be the least concerned in this scene see the most
he do indeed suspect the very fact for besides observe the great alteration in the behaviour of the uncle the distance he assume and his overstrained civility to miss nancy the carry off a bridegroom from his bride at that time of night be so extraordinary a proceeding that it could be account for only by imagine that young nightingale have reveal the whole truth which the apparent openness of his temper and his be fluster with liquor make too probable
while he be reason with himself whether he should acquaint these poor people with his suspicion the maid of the house inform he that a gentlewoman desire to speak with him he go immediately out and take the candle from the maid usher his visitant upstairs who in the person of mrs honour acquaint he with such dreadful news concern his sophia that he immediately lose all consideration for every other person and his whole stock of compassion be entirely swallow up in reflection on his own misery and on that of his unfortunate angel
what this dreadful matter be the reader will be inform after we have first relate the many precede step which produce it and those will be the subject of the follow book
book xv
in which the history advances about two day
chapter i
too short to need a preface
there be a set of religious or rather moral writer who teach that virtue be the certain road to happiness and vice to misery in this world
a very wholesome and comfortable doctrine and to which we have but one objection namely that it be not true
indeed if by virtue these writer mean the exercise of those cardinal virtue which like good housewife stay at home and mind only the business of their own family i shall very readily concede the point for so surely do all these contribute and lead to happiness that i could almost wish in violation of all the antient and modern sage to call they rather by the name of wisdom than by that of virtue for with regard to this life no system i conceive be ever wise than that of the antient epicureans who hold this wisdom to constitute the chief good nor foolisher than that of their opposite those modern epicure who place all felicity in the abundant gratification of every sensual appetite
but if by virtue be mean as i almost think it ought a certain relative quality which be always busy itself without-door and seem as much interested in pursue the good of other as its own i cannot so easily agree that this be the sure way to human happiness because i be afraid we must then include poverty and contempt with all the mischief which backbiting envy and ingratitude can bring on mankind in our idea of happiness nay sometimes perhaps we shall be oblige to wait upon the say happiness to a jail since many by the above virtue have bring themselves thither
i have not now leisure to enter upon so large a field of speculation as here seem open upon i my design be to wipe off a doctrine that lie in my way since while mr jones be act the most virtuous part imaginable in labour to preserve his fellow-creature from destruction the devil or some other evil spirit one perhaps cloathe in human flesh be hard at work to make he completely miserable in the ruin of his sophia
this therefore would seem an exception to the above rule if indeed it be a rule but as we have in our voyage through life see so many other exception to it we chuse to dispute the doctrine on which it be found which we donot apprehend to be christian which we be convince be not true and which be indeed destructive of one of the noble argument that reason alone can furnish for the belief of immortality
but as the reader s curiosity if he hath any must be now awake and hungry we shall provide to feed it as fast as we can
chapter ii
in which be open a very black design against sophia
i remember a wise old gentleman who use to say when child be do nothing they be do mischief i will not enlarge this quaint say to the most beautiful part of the creation in general but so far i may be allow that when the effect of female jealousy do not appear openly in their proper colour of rage and fury we may suspect that mischievous passion to be at work privately and attempt to undermine what it doth not attack above-ground
this be exemplified in the conduct of lady bellaston who under all the smile which she wear in her countenance conceal much indignation against sophia and as she plainly see that this young lady stand between she and the full indulgence of her desire she resolve to get rid of she by some mean or other nor be it long before a very favourable opportunity of accomplish this present itself to she
the reader may be pleased to remember that when sophia be throw into that consternation at the playhouse by the wit and humour of a set of young gentleman who call themselves the town we inform he that she have put herself under the protection of a young nobleman who have very safely conduct she to her chair
this nobleman who frequently visit lady bellaston have more than once see sophia there since her arrival in town and have conceive a very great liking to she which like as beauty never look more amiable than in distress sophia have in this fright so encreased that he might now without any great impropriety be say to be actually in love with she
it may easily be believe that he would not suffer so handsome an occasion of improve his acquaintance with the beloved object as now offer itself to elapse when even good breeding alone might have prompt he to pay she a visit
the next morning therefore after this accident he wait on sophia with the usual compliment and hop that she have receive no harm from her last night s adventure
as love like fire when once thoroughly kindle be soon blow into a flame sophia in a very short time compleate her conquest
time now fly away unperceived and the noble lord have be two hour in company with the lady before it enter into his head that he have make too long a visit
though this circumstance alone would have alarm sophia who be somewhat more a mistress of computation at present she have indeed much more pregnant evidence from the eye of her lover of what past within his bosom nay though he do not make any open declaration of his passion yet many of his expression be rather too warm and too tender to have be impute to complacence even in the age when such complacence be in fashion the very reverse of which be well known to be the reign mode at present
lady bellaston have be apprize of his lordship s visit at his first arrival and the length of it very well satisfied she that thing go as she wish and as indeed she have suspect the second time she see this young couple together
this business she rightly i think conclude that she should by no mean forward by mix in the company while they be together she therefore order her servant that when my lord be go they should tell he she desire to speak with he and employ the intermediate time in meditate how good to accomplish a scheme which she make no doubt but his lordship would very readily embrace the execution of
lord fellamar for that be the title of this young nobleman be no soon introduce to her ladyship than she attack he in the following strain bless i my lord be you here yet
i think my servant have make a mistake and let you go away and i want to see you about an affair of some importance indeed lady bellaston say he i donot wonder you be astonish at the length of my visit for i have staid above two hour and i do not think i have staid above half-a-one what be i to conclude from thence my lord say she
the company must be very agreeable which can make time slide away so very deceitfully upon my honour say he the most agreeable i ever see
pray tell i lady bellaston who be this blaze star which you have produce among we all of a sudden what blaze star my lord say she affect a surprize
i mean say he the lady i see here the other day whom i have last night in my arm at the playhouse and to whom i have be make that unreasonable visit o my cousin western say she why that blaze star my lord be the daughter of a country booby squire and hath be in town about a fortnight for the first time upon my soul say he i should swear she have be breed up in a court for besides her beauty i never see anything so genteel so sensible so polite o brave cry the lady my cousin hath you i find upon my honour answer he i wish she have for i be in love with she to distraction nay my lord say she it be not wish yourself very ill neither for she be a very great fortune i assure you she be an only child and her father s estate be a good 3000 a-year then i can assure you madam answer the lord i think she the good match in england indeed my lord reply she if you like she i heartily wish you have she if you think so kindly of i madam say he as she be a relation of your will you do i the honour to propose it to her father and be you really then in earnest cry the lady with an affected gravity
i hope madam answer he you have a well opinion of i than to imagine i would jest with your ladyship in an affair of this kind indeed then say the lady i will most readily propose your lordship to her father and i can i believe assure you of his joyful acceptance of the proposal but there be a bar which i be almost ashamed to mention and yet it be one you will never be able to conquer
you have a rival my lord and a rival who though i blush to name he neither you nor all the world will ever be able to conquer upon my word lady bellaston cry he you have strike a damp to my heart which hath almost deprive i of be fie my lord say she i should rather hope i have strike fire into you
a lover and talk of damp in your heart
i rather imagine you would have ask your rival s name that you might have immediately enter the list with he i promise you madam answer he there be very few thing i would not undertake for your charming cousin but pray who be this happy man why he be say she what i be sorry to say most happy man with we be one of the low fellow in the world
he be a beggar a bastard a foundling a fellow in mean circumstance than one of your lordship s footman and be it possible cry he that a young creature with such perfection should think of bestow herself so unworthily alas
my lord answer she consider the country--the bane of all young woman be the country
there they learn a set of romantic notion of love and i know not what folly which this town and good company can scarce eradicate in a whole winter indeed madam reply my lord your cousin be of too immense a value to be throw away such ruin as this must be prevent alas cry she my lord how can it be prevent
the family have already do all in their power but the girl be i think intoxicate and nothing less than ruin will content she
and to deal more openly with you i expect every day to hear she be run away with he what you tell i lady bellaston answer his lordship affect i most tenderly and only raise my compassion instead of lessen my adoration of your cousin
some mean must be find to preserve so inestimable a jewel
hath your ladyship endeavour to reason with she here the lady affect a laugh and cry my dear lord sure you know we well than to talk of reason a young woman out of her inclination
these inestimable jewel be as deaf as the jewel they wear time my lord time be the only medicine to cure their folly but this be a medicine which i be certain she will not take nay i live in hourly horror on her account
in short nothing but violent method will do what be to be do cry my lord what method be to be taken is there any method upon earth oh
lady bellaston
there be nothing which i would not undertake for such a reward i really know not answer the lady after a pause and then pause again she cry out-- upon my soul i be at my wit s end on this girl s account if she can be preserve something must be do immediately and as i say nothing but violent method will do if your lordship hath really this attachment to my cousin and to do her justice except in this silly inclination of which she will soon see her folly she be every way deserve i think there may be one way indeed it be a very disagreeable one and what i be almost afraid to think of it require a great spirit i promise you i be not conscious madam say he of any defect there nor be i i hope suspect of any such
it must be an egregious defect indeed which could make i backward on this occasion nay my lord answer she i be so far from doubt you i be much more inclined to doubt my own courage for i must run a monstrous risque
in short i must place such a confidence in your honour as a wise woman will scarce ever place in a man on any consideration in this point likewise my lord very well satisfy she for his reputation be extremely clear and common fame do he no more than justice in speak well of he
well then say she my lord i--i vow i cannot bear the apprehension of it no it must not be at least every other method shall be try
can you get rid of your engagement and dine here to-day
your lordship will have an opportunity of see a little more of miss western i promise you we have no time to lose
here will be nobody but lady betty and miss eagle and colonel hampsted and tom edwards they will all go soon--and i shall be at home to nobody
then your lordship may be a little more explicit
nay i will contrive some method to convince you of her attachment to this fellow my lord make proper compliment accept the invitation and then they part to dress it be now past three in the morning or to reckon by the old style in the afternoon
chapter iii
a further explanation of the foregoing design
though the reader may have long since conclude lady bellaston to be a member and no inconsiderable one of the great world she be in reality a very considerable member of the little world by which appellation be distinguish a very worthy and honourable society which not long since flourish in this kingdom
among other good principle upon which this society be found there be one very remarkable for as it be a rule of an honourable club of hero who assemble at the close of the late war that all the member should every day fight once at least so twa in this that every member should within the twenty-four hour tell at least one merry fib which be to be propagate by all the brother and sisterhood
many idle story be tell about this society which from a certain quality may be perhaps not unjustly suppose to have come from the society themselves
as that the devil be the president and that he sit in person in an elbow-chair at the upper end of the table but upon very strict enquiry i find there be not the least truth in any of those tale and that the assembly consist in reality of a set of very good sort of people and the fib which they propagate be of a harmless kind and tend only to produce mirth and good humour
edwards be likewise a member of this comical society
to he therefore lady bellaston apply as a proper instrument for her purpose and furnish he with a fib which he be to vent whenever the lady give he her cue and this be not to be till the evening when all the company but lord fellamar and himself be go and while they be engage in a rubber at whist

for the squire be so set against i and yet if you should ever have my lady as to be sure i now hop heartily you will for you be a generous good-natured gentleman and i be sure you love she and to be sure she love you as dearly as her own soul it be a matter in vain to deny it because as why everybody that be in the least acquainted with my lady must see it for poor dear lady she cannot dissemble and if two people who love one another a n t happy why who should be so
