to be sure therefore as one may say it would be all the pity in the world to keep two such lovier asunder nay i be convince for my part you will meet together at last for if it be to be there be no prevent it
if a marriage be make in heaven all the justice of peace upon earth cannot break it off
to be sure i wish that parson supple have but a little more spirit to tell the squire of his wickedness in endeavour to force his daughter contrary to her liking but then his whole dependance be on the squire and so the poor gentleman though he be a very religious good sort of man and talk of the badness of such doing behind the squirebe back yet he dare not say his soul be his own to his face
to be sure i never see he make so bold as just now i be afeard the squire would have strike he
i would not have your honour be melancholy sir nor despair thing may go well as long as you be sure of my lady and that i be certain you may be for she never will be bring to consent to marry any other man
indeed i be terribly afeare the squire will do she a mischief in his passion for he be a prodigious passionate gentleman and i be afeare too the poor lady will be bring to break her heart for she be as tender-hearted as a chicken
it be pity methink she have not a little of my courage
if i be in love with a young man and my father offer to lock i up iwould tear his eye out but iwould come at he but then therebe a great fortune in the case which it be in her father s power either to give she or not that to be sure may make some difference whether jones give strict attention to all the foregoing harangue or whether it be for want of any vacancy in the discourse i cannot determine but he never once attempt to answer nor do she once stop till partridge come run into the room and inform he that the great lady be upon the stair
nothing could equal the dilemma to which jones be now reduce
honour know nothing of any acquaintance that subsist between he and lady bellaston and she be almost the last person in the world to whom he would have communicate it
in this hurry and distress he take as be common enough the bad course and instead of expose she to the lady which would have be of little consequence he choose to expose the lady to she he therefore resolve to hide honour whom he have but just time to convey behind the bed and to draw the curtain
the hurry in which jones have be all day engage on account of his poor landlady and her family the terror occasion by mrs honour and the confusion into which he be throw by the sudden arrival of lady bellaston have altogether drive former thought out of his head so that it never once occur to his memory to act the part of a sick man which indeed neither the gaiety of his dress nor the freshness of his countenance would have at all support
he receive her ladyship therefore rather agreeably to her desire than to her expectation with all the good humour he could muster in his countenance and without any real or affected appearance of the least disorder
lady bellaston no soon enter the room than she squat herself down on the bed so my dear jones say she you find nothing can detain i long from you
perhaps i ought to be angry with you that i have neither see nor hear from you all day for i perceive your distemper would have suffer you to come abroad nay i suppose you have not sit in your chamber all day drest up like a fine lady to see company after a lying-in but however donot think i intend to scold you for i never will give you an excuse for the cold behaviour of a husband by put on the ill-humour of a wife nay lady bellaston say jones i be sure your ladyship will not upbraid i with neglect of duty when i only wait for order
who my dear creature hath reason to complain
who miss an appointment last night and leave an unhappy man to expect and wish and sigh and languish do not mention it my dear mr jones cry she
if you know the occasion you would pity i
in short it be impossible to conceive what woman of condition be oblige to suffer from the impertinence of fool in order to keep up the farce of the world
i be glad however all your languishing and wish have do you no harm for you never look well in your life
upon my faith
jones you might at this instant sit for the picture of adonis there be certain word of provocation which man of honour hold can properly be answer only by a blow
among lover possibly there may be some expression which can be answer only by a kiss
now the compliment which lady bellaston now make jones seem to be of this kind especially as it be attend with a look in which the lady convey more soft idea than it be possible to express with her tongue
jones be certainly at this instant in one of the most disagreeable and distressed situation imaginable for to carry on the comparison we make use of before though the provocation be give by the lady jones could not receive satisfaction nor so much as offer to ask it in the presence of a third person second in this kind of duel not be accord to the law of arm
as this objection do not occur to lady bellaston who be ignorant of any other woman be there but herself she wait some time in great astonishment for an answer from jones who conscious of the ridiculous figure he make stand at a distance and not dare to give the proper answer give none at all
nothing can be imagine more comic nor yet more tragical than this scene would have be if it have last much long
the lady have already change colour two or three time have get up from the bed and sit down again while jones be wish the ground to sink under he or the house to fall on his head when an odd accident free he from an embarrassment out of which neither the eloquence of a cicero nor the politic of a machiavel could have deliver he without utter disgrace
this be no other than the arrival of young nightingale dead drunk or rather in that state of drunkenness which deprive man of the use of their reason without deprive they of the use of their limb
mrs miller and her daughter be in bed and partridge be smoake his pipe by the kitchen fire so that he arrive at mr jones s chamber-door without any interruption
this he burst open and be enter without any ceremony when jones start from his seat and run to oppose he which he do so effectually that nightingale never come far enough within the door to see who be sit on the bed
nightingale have in reality mistaken jones s apartment for that in which himself have lodge he therefore strongly insist on come in often swear that he would not be keep from his own bed
jones however prevail over he and deliver he into the hand of partridge whom the noise on the stair soon summon to his master s assistance
and now jones be unwillingly oblige to return to his own apartment where at the very instant of his entrance he hear lady bellaston vent an exclamation though not a very loud one and at the same time see she fling herself into a chair in a vast agitation which in a lady of a tender constitution would have be an hysteric fit
in reality the lady frighten with the struggle between the two man of which she do not know what would be the issue as she hear nightingale swear many oath he would come to his own bed attempt to retire to her know place of hiding which to her great confusion she find already occupy by another
be this usage to be bear mr jones cry the lady bas of men what wretch be this to whom you have expose i wretch cry honour burst in a violent rage from her place of concealment-- marry come up wretch forsooth as poor a wretch as i be i be honest this be more than some folk who be rich can say jones instead of apply himself directly to take off the edge of mrs honour s resentment as a more experienced gallant would have do fall to curse his star and lament himself as the most unfortunate man in the world and presently after address himself to lady bellaston he fall to some very absurd protestation of innocence
by this time the lady have recover the use of her reason which she have as ready as any woman in the world especially on such occasion calmly reply sir you need make no apology i see now who the person be i do not at first know mrs honour but now i do i can suspect nothing wrong between she and you and i be sure she be a woman of too good sense to put any wrong construction upon my visit to you i have be always her friend and it may be in my power to be much more hereafter mrs honour be altogether as placable as she be passionate
hearing therefore lady bellaston assume the soft tone she likewise soften hers i m sure madam say she i have be always ready to acknowledge your ladyship s friendship to i sure i never have so good a friend as your ladyship----and to be sure now i see it be your ladyship that i speak to i could almost bite my tongue off for very mad i construction upon your ladyship--to be sure it doth not become a servant as i be to think about such a great lady--i mean i be a servant for indeed i be nobody s servant now the more miserable wretch be me i have lose the good mistress---- here honour think fit to produce a shower of tears don t cry child say the good lady way perhaps may be find to make you amend
come to i to-morrow morning she then take up her fan which lie on the ground and without even look at jones walk very majestically out of the room there be a kind of dignity in the impudence of woman of quality which their inferior vainly aspire to attain to in circumstance of this nature
jones follow her downstair often offer she his hand which she absolutely refuse he and get into her chair without take any notice of he as he stand bow before she
at his return upstairs a long dialogue past between he and mrs honour while she be adjust herself after the discomposure she have undergo
the subject of this be his infidelity to her young lady on which she enlarge with great bitterness but jones at last find mean to reconcile she and not only so but to obtain a promise of most inviolable secrecy and that she would the next morning endeavour to find out sophia and bring he a further account of the proceeding of the squire
thus end this unfortunate adventure to the satisfaction only of mrs honour for a secret as some of my reader will perhaps acknowledge from experience be often a very valuable possession and that not only to those who faithfully keep it but sometimes to such as whisper it about till it come to the ear of every one except the ignorant person who pay for the suppose concealing of what be publickly know
chapter viii
short and sweet
notwithstanding all the obligation she have receive from jones mrs miller could not forbear in the morning some gentle remonstrance for the hurricane which have happen the precede night in his chamber
these be however so gentle and so friendly profess and indeed truly to aim at nothing more than the real good of mr jones himself that he far from be offend thankfully receive the admonition of the good woman express much concern for what have past excuse it as well as he could and promise never more to bring the same disturbance into the house
but though mrs miller do not refrain from a short expostulation in private at their first meeting yet the occasion of his being summon downstairs that morning be of a much more agreeable kind be indeed to perform the office of a father to miss nancy and to give she in wedlock to mr nightingale who be now ready dr and full as sober as many of my reader will think a man ought to be who receive a wife in so imprudent a manner
and here perhaps it may be proper to account for the escape which this young gentleman have make from his uncle and for his appearance in the condition in which we have see he the night before
now when the uncle have arrive at his lodging with his nephew partly to indulge his own inclination for he dearly love his bottle and partly to disqualify his nephew from the immediate execution of his purpose he order wine to be set on the table with which he so briskly ply the young gentleman that this latter who though not much use to drinking do not detest it so as to be guilty of disobedience or want of complacence by refuse be soon completely finish
just as the uncle have obtain this victory and be prepare a bed for his nephew a messenger arrive with a piece of news which so entirely disconcert and shock he that he in a moment lose all consideration for his nephew and his whole mind become entirely take up with his own concern
this sudden and afflict news be no less than that his daughter have take the opportunity of almost the first moment of his absence and have go off with a neighbouring young clergyman against whom though her father could have have but one objection namely that he be worth nothing yet she have never think proper to communicate she amour even to that father and so artfully have she manage that it have never be once suspect by any till now that it be consummate
old mr nightingale no soon receive this account than in the utmost confusion he order a post-chaise to be instantly get ready and have recommend his nephew to the care of a servant he directly leave the house scarce know what he do nor whither he go
the uncle thus depart when the servant come to attend the nephew to bed have wake he for that purpose and have at last make he sensible that his uncle be go he instead of accept the kind office tender he insist on a chair be call with this the servant who have receive no strict order to the contrary readily comply and thus be conduct back to the house of mrs miller he have stagger up to mr jones s chamber as hath be before recount
this bar of the uncle be now remove though young nightingale know not as yet in what manner and all party be quickly ready the mother mr jones mr nightingale and his love stept into a hackney-coach which convey they to doctor commons where miss nancy be in vulgar language soon make an honest woman and the poor mother become in the pure sense of the word one of the happy of all human being
and now mr jones have see his good office to that poor woman and her family bring to a happy conclusion begin to apply himself to his own concern but here lest many of my reader should censure his folly for thus trouble himself with the affair of other and lest some few should think he act more disinterestedly than indeed he do we think proper to assure our reader that he be so far from be unconcerned in this matter that he have indeed a very considerable interest in bring it to that final consummation
to explain this seem paradox at once he be one who could truly say with he in terence homo sum humani nihil a me alienum puto
he be never an indifferent spectator of the misery or happiness of any one and he feel either the one or the other in great proportion as he himself contribute to either
he could not therefore be the instrument of raise a whole family from the low state of wretchedness to the high pitch of joy without convey great felicity to himself more perhaps than worldly man often purchase to themselves by undergo the most severe labour and often by wad through the deep iniquity
those reader who be of the same complexion with he will perhaps think this short chapter contain abundance of matter while other may probably wish short as it be that it have be totally spar as impertinent to the main design which i suppose they conclude be to bring mr jones to the gallow or if possible to a more deplorable catastrophe
chapter ix
contain love-letter of several sort
mr jones at his return home find the following letter lie on his table which he luckily open in the order they be send
letter i
surely i be under some strange infatuation i cannot keep my resolution a moment however strongly make or justly found
last night i resolve never to see you more this morning i be willing to hear if you can as you say clear up this affair
and yet i know that to be impossible
i have say everything to myself which you can invent perhap not
perhaps your invention be strong
come to i therefore the moment you receive this
if you can forge an excuse i almost promise you to believe it
betrayed too----i will think no more come to i directly this be the third letter i have writ the two former be burnt----i be almost inclined to burn this too----i wish i may preserve my senses come to i presently letter ii
if you ever expect to be forgive or even suffer within my door come to i this instant letter iii
i now find you be not at home when my note come to your lodging
the moment you receive this let i see you i shall not stir out nor shall anybody be let in but yourself
sure nothing can detain you long jones have just read over these three billet when mr nightingale come into the room
well tom say he any news from lady bellaston after last night s adventure for it be now no secret to any one in that house who the lady be
the lady bellaston answer jones very gravely nay dear tom cry nightingale donot be so reserve to your friend
though i be too drunk to see her last night i see she at the masquerade
do you think i be ignorant who the queen of the fairy be and do you really then know the lady at the masquerade say jones
yes upon my soul do i say nightingale and have give you twenty hint of it since though you seem always so tender on that point that i would not speak plainly
i fancy my friend by your extreme nicety in this matter you be not so well acquainted with the character of the lady as with her person
donot be angry tom but upon my honour you be not the first young fellow she hath debauch
her reputation be in no danger believe i though jones have no reason to imagine the lady to have be of the vestal kind when his amour begin yet as he be thoroughly ignorant of the town and have very little acquaintance in it he have no knowledge of that character which be vulgarly call a demirep that be to say a woman who intrigue with every man she like under the name and appearance of virtue and who though some over-nice lady will not be see with she be visit as they term it by the whole town in short whom everybody know to be what nobody call she
when he find therefore that nightingale be perfectly acquaint with his intrigue and begin to suspect that so scrupulous a delicacy as he have hitherto observe be not quite necessary on the occasion he give a latitude to his friend s tongue and desire he to speak plainly what he know or have ever hear of the lady
nightingale who in many other instance be rather too effeminate in his disposition have a pretty strong inclination to tittle-tattle
he have no soon therefore receive a full liberty of speak from jones than he enter upon a long narrative concern the lady which as it contain many particular highly to her dishonour we have too great a tenderness for all woman of condition to repeat
we would cautiously avoid give an opportunity to the future commentator on our work of make any malicious application and of force we to be against our will the author of scandal which never enter into our head
jones have very attentively hear all that nightingale have to say fetch a deep sigh which the other observe cry heyday
why thou art not in love i hope
have i imagine my story would have affect you i promise you should never have hear they o my dear friend cry jones i be so entangle with this woman that i know not how to extricate myself
in love indeed
no my friend but i be under obligation to she and very great one
since you know so much i will be very explicit with you
it be owing perhaps solely to she that i have not before this want a bit of bread
how can i possibly desert such a woman
and yet i must desert she or be guilty of the black treachery to one who deserve infinitely well of i than she can a woman my nightingale for whom i have a passion which few can have an idea of
i be half distracted with doubt how to act and be this other pray an honourable mistress cry nightingale
honourable answer jones no breath ever yet durst sully her reputation
the sweetest air be not pure the limpid stream not clear than her honour
she be all over both in mind and body consummate perfection
she be the most beautiful creature in the universe and yet she be mistress of such noble elevated quality that though she be never from my thought i scarce ever think of her beauty but when i see it and can you my good friend cry nightingale with such an engagement as this upon your hand hesitate a moment about quit such a-- hold say jones no more abuse of she i det the thought of ingratitude pooh answer the other you be not the first upon whom she hath confer obligation of this kind
she be remarkably liberal where she like though let i tell you her favour be so prudently bestow that they should rather raise a man s vanity than his gratitude in short nightingale proceed so far on this head and tell his friend so many story of the lady which he swear to the truth of that he entirely remove all esteem for she from the breast of jones and his gratitude be lessen in proportion
indeed he begin to look on all the favour he have receive rather as wage than benefit which depreciate not only she but himself too in his own conceit and put he quite out of humour with both
from this disgust his mind by a natural transition turn towards sophia her virtue her purity her love to he her suffering on his account fill all his thought and make his commerce with lady bellaston appear still more odious
the result of all be that though his turn himself out of her service in which light he now see his affair with she would be the loss of his bread yet he determine to quit she if he could but find a handsome pretence which be communicate to his friend nightingale consider a little and then say i have it my boy
i have find out a sure method propose marriage to she and i would venture hang upon the success marriage cry jones
ay propose marriage answer nightingale and she will declare off in a moment
i know a young fellow whom she keep formerly who make the offer to she in earnest and be presently turn off for his pain jones declare he could not venture the experiment
perhaps say he she may be less shocked at this proposal from one man than from another
and if she should take i at my word where be i then
catch in my own trap and undo for ever no answer nightingale not if i can give you an expedient by which you may at any time get out of the trap what expedient can that be reply jones
this answer nightingale
the young fellow i mention who be one of the most intimate acquaintance i have in the world be so angry with she for some ill office she hath since do he that i be sure he would without any difficulty give you a sight of her letter upon which you may decently break with she and declare off before the knot be tye if she should really be willing to tie it which i be convinced she will not after some hesitation jones upon the strength of this assurance consent but as he swear he want the confidence to propose the matter to her face he write the following letter which nightingale dictated madam i be extremely concerned that by an unfortunate engagement abroad i should have miss receive the honour of your ladyship s command the moment they come and the delay which i must now suffer of vindicate myself to your ladyship greatly add to this misfortune
o lady bellaston
what a terror have i be in for fear your reputation should be expose by these perverse accident
there be one only way to secure it
i need not name what that be
only permit i to say that as your honour be as dear to i as my own so my sole ambition be to have the glory of lay my liberty at your foot and believe i when i assure you i can never be make completely happy without you generously bestow on i a legal right of call you mine for ever i am madam with most profound respect your ladyship s most oblige obedient humble servant thomas jones to this she presently return the follow answer sir when i read over your serious epistle i could from its coldness and formality have swear that you already have the legal right you mention nay that we have for many year compose that monstrous animal a husband and wife
do you really then imagine i a fool
or do you fancy yourself capable of so entirely persuade i out of my sens that i should deliver my whole fortune into your power in order to enable you to support your pleasure at my expense
be these the proof of love which i expect
be this the return for--
but i scorn to upbraid you and be in great admiration of your profound respect
p s
i be prevent from revising perhap i have say more than i meant come to i at eight this evening jones by the advice of his privy-council reply madam it be impossible to express how much i be shock at the suspicion you entertain of i
can lady bellaston have confer favour on a man whom she could believe capable of so base a design
or can she treat the most solemn tie of love with contempt
can you imagine madam that if the violence of my passion in an unguarded moment overcome the tenderness which i have for your honour i would think of indulge myself in the continuance of an intercourse which could not possibly escape long the notice of the world and which when discover must prove so fatal to your reputation
if such be your opinion of i i must pray for a sudden opportunity of return those pecuniary obligation which i have be so unfortunate to receive at your hand and for those of a more tender kind i shall ever remain c and so conclude in the very word with which he have conclude the former letter
the lady answer as follow i see you be a villain
and i despise you from my soul
if you come here i shall not be at home though jones be well satisfied with his deliverance from a thraldom which those who have ever experience it will i apprehend allow to be none of the light he be not however perfectly easy in his mind
there be in this scheme too much of fallacy to satisfy one who utterly detest every specie of falshood or dishonesty nor would he indeed have submit to put it in practice have he not be involve in a distressful situation where he be oblige to be guilty of some dishonour either to the one lady or the other and surely the reader will allow that every good principle as well as love plead strongly in favour of sophia
nightingale highly exult in the success of his stratagem upon which he receive many thank and much applause from his friend
he answer dear tom we have confer very different obligation on each other
to i you owe the regain your liberty to you i owe the loss of mine
but if you be as happy in the one instance as i be in the other i promise you we be the two happy fellow in england the two gentleman be now summon down to dinner where mrs miller who perform herself the office of cook have exert her good talent to celebrate the wedding of her daughter
this joyful circumstance she ascribe principally to the friendly behaviour of jones her whole soul be fire with gratitude towards he and all her look word and action be so busied in express it that her daughter and even her new son-in-law be very little object of her consideration
dinner be just end when mrs miller receive a letter but as we have have letter enow in this chapter we shall communicate its content in our next
chapter x
consist partly of fact and partly of observation upon they
the letter then which arrive at the end of the precede chapter be from mr allworthy and the purport of it be his intention to come immediately to town with his nephew blifil and a desire to be accommodate with his usual lodging which be the first floor for himself and the second for his nephew
the chearfulness which have before display itself in the countenance of the poor woman be a little clouded on this occasion
this news do indeed a good deal disconcert she
to requite so disintereste a match with her daughter by presently turn her new son-in-law out of door appear to her very unjustifiable on the one hand and on the other she could scarce bear the thought of make any excuse to mr allworthy after all the obligation receive from he for deprive he of lodging which be indeed strictly his due for that gentleman in confer all his numberless benefit on other act by a rule diametrically opposite to what be practise by most generous people
he contrive on all occasion to hide his beneficence not only from the world but even from the object of it
he constantly use the word lend and pay instead of give and by every other method he could invent always lessen with his tongue the favour he confer while he be heap they with both his hand
when he settle the annuity of 50 a year therefore on mrs miller he tell she it be in consideration of always have her first-floor when he be in town which he scarce ever intend to be but that she might let it at any other time for that he would always send she a month s warning he be now however hurry to town so suddenly that he have no opportunity of give such notice and this hurry probably prevent he when he write for his lodging add if they be then empty for he would most certainly have be well satisfied to have relinquish they on a less sufficient excuse than what mrs miller could now have make
but there be a sort of person who as prior excellently well remark direct their conduct by something beyond the fix d and settle rule of vice and virtue in the school beyond the letter of the law
to these it be so far from be sufficient that their defence would acquit they at the old bailey that they be not even content though conscience the severest of all judge should discharge they
nothing short of the fair and honourable will satisfy the delicacy of their mind and if any of their action fall short of this mark they mope and pine be as uneasy and restless as a murderer who be afraid of a ghost or of the hangman
mrs miller be one of these
she could not conceal her uneasiness at this letter with the content of which she have no soon acquaint the company and give some hint of her distress than jones her good angel presently relieve her anxiety
as for myself madam say he my lodging be at your service at a moment s warning and mr nightingale i be sure as he cannot yet prepare a house fit to receive his lady will consent to return to his new lodging whither mrs nightingale will certainly consent to go with which proposal both husband and wife instantly agree
the reader will easily believe that the cheek of mrs miller begin again to glow with additional gratitude to jones but perhaps it may be more difficult to persuade he that mr jones have in his last speech call her daughter mrs nightingale it be the first time that agreeable sound have ever reach her ear give the fond mother more satisfaction and warm her heart more towards jones than his have dissipate her present anxiety
the next day be then appoint for the removal of the new-married couple and of mr jones who be likewise to be provide for in the same house with his friend
and now the serenity of the company be again restore and they past the day in the utmost chearfulness all except jones who though he outwardly accompany the rest in their mirth feel many a bitter pang on the account of his sophia which be not a little heighten by the news of mr blifil s come to town for he clearly see the intention of his journey and what greatly aggravate his concern be that mrs honour who have promise to inquire after sophia and to make her report to he early the next evening have disappoint he
in the situation that he and his mistress be in at this time there be scarce any ground for he to hope that he should hear any good news yet he be as impatient to see mrs honour as if he have expect she would bring he a letter with an assignation in it from sophia and bear the disappointment as ill
whether this impatience arise from that natural weakness of the human mind which make it desirous to know the bad and render uncertainty the most intolerable of pain or whether he still flatter himself with some secret hope we will not determine
but that it might be the last whoever have love cannot but know
for of all the power exercise by this passion over our mind one of the most wonderful be that of support hope in the midst of despair
difficulty improbability nay impossibility be quite overlook by it so that to any man extremely in love may be apply what addison say of caesar the alps and pyrenaeans sink before he yet it be equally true that the same passion will sometimes make mountain of molehill and produce despair in the midst of hope but these cold fit last not long in good constitution
which temper jones be now in we leave the reader to guess have no exact information about it but this be certain that he have spend two hour in expectation when be unable any long to conceal his uneasiness he retire to his room where his anxiety have almost make he frantick when the following letter be bring he from mrs honour with which we shall present the reader verbatim et literatim sir i shud sartenly haf kale on you a cordin too mi prommiss haddunt itt bin that hur lashipp prevent mee for to bee sur sir you nose very well that evere persun must luk furst at ome and sartenly such anuther offar mite not have ever hapne so as i shud ave bin justly to blam have i not excepted of it when her lashipp be so veri kind as to offar to mak mee hur one uman without mi ever askin any such thing to be sur shee be win of thee good ladi in thee wurld and pepil who sase to the kontrari must bee veri wiket pepil in thare hart
to bee sur if ever i ave sad any thing of that kine it as bin thru ignoren and i be hartili sorri for it
i nose your onur to be a genteelman of more onur and onesty if i ever say ani such thing to repete it to hurt a pore servant that as alwais add thee grat respect in thee wurld for ure onur
to be sur win shud kepe won tung within won tooth for no boddi nose what may hapen and to bee sur if ani boddi ad tolde mee yesterday that i shud haf bin in so gud a plase to day i shud not haf beleeve it for to be sur i never be a dremd of any such thing nor shud i ever have soft after ani other bodi s plase but as her lashipp wass so kine of her one a cord too give it mee without askin to be sur mrs etoff herself nor no other boddi can blam mee for exceptin such a thing when it fal in mi waye
i beg ure onur not to menshion ani thing of what i haf sad for i wish ure onur all thee gud luk in the wurld and i donot cuestion butt thatt u will haf madam sofia in the end butt ass to miself ure onur nose i kant bee of ani farder sarvis to u in that matar nou bein under thee cumand off anuther parson and nott mi one mistress i begg ure onur to say nothing of what past and belive i to be sir ure onur s umble servant to cumand till deth honour blackmore various be the conjecture which jones entertain on this step of lady bellaston who in reality have little farth design than to secure within her own house the repository of a secret which she choose should make no farth progress than it have make already but mostly she desire to keep it from the ear of sophia for though that young lady be almost the only one who would never have repeat it again her ladyship could not persuade herself of this since as she now hat poor sophia with most implacable hatred she conceive a reciprocal hatred to herself to be lodge in the tender breast of our heroine where no such passion have ever yet find an entrance
while jones be terrify himself with the apprehension of a thousand dreadful machination and deep political design which he imagine to be at the bottom of the promotion of honour fortune who hitherto seem to have be an utter enemy to his match with sophia try a new method to put a final end to it by throw a temptation in his way which in his present desperate situation it seem unlikely he should be able to resist
chapter xi
contain curious but not unprecedented matter
there be a lady one mrs hunt who have often see jones at the house where he lodge be intimately acquaint with the woman there and indeed a very great friend to mrs miller
her age be about thirty for she own six-and-twenty her face and person very good only incline a little too much to be fat
she have be marry young by her relation to an old turkey merchant who have get a great fortune have leave off trade
with he she live without reproach but not without pain in a state of great self-denial for about twelve year and her virtue be reward by his dying and leave she very rich
the first year of her widowhood be just at an end and she have past it in a good deal of retirement see only a few particular friend and divide her time between her devotion and novel of which she be always extremely fond
very good health a very warm constitution and a good deal of religion make it absolutely necessary for she to marry again and she resolve to please herself in her second husband as she have do her friend in the first
from she the follow billet be bring to jones sir from the first day i see you i doubt my eye have tell you too plainly that you be not indifferent to i but neither my tongue nor my hand should have ever avow it have not the lady of the family where you be lodge give i such a character of you and tell i such proof of your virtue and goodness as convince i you be not only the most agreeable but the most worthy of man
i have also the satisfaction to hear from they that neither my person understanding or character be disagreeable to you
i have a fortune sufficient to make we both happy but which cannot make i so without you
in thus dispose of myself i know i shall incur the censure of the world but if i do not love you more than i fear the world i should not be worthy of you
one only difficulty stop i i be inform you be engage in a commerce of gallantry with a woman of fashion
if you think it worth while to sacrifice that to the possession of i i be your if not forget my weakness and let this remain an eternal secret between you and arabella hunt at the reading of this jones be put into a violent flutter
his fortune be then at a very low ebb the source be stopt from which hitherto he have be supply
of all he have receive from lady bellaston not above five guineas remain and that very morning he have be dun by a tradesman for twice that sum
his honourable mistress be in the hand of her father and he have scarce any hope ever to get she out of they again
to be subsist at her expense from that little fortune she have independent of her father go much against the delicacy both of his pride and his love
this lady s fortune would have be exceed convenient to he and he could have no objection to she in any respect
on the contrary he like she as well as he do any woman except sophia
but to abandon sophia and marry another that be impossible he could not think of it upon any account yet why should he not since it be plain she could not be his
would it not be kind to she than to continue she long engage in a hopeless passion for he
ought he not to do so in friendship to she
this notion prevail some moment and he have almost determined to be false to she from a high point of honour but that refinement be not able to stand very long against the voice of nature which cry in his heart that such friendship be treason to love
at last he call for pen ink and paper and writ as follow to mrs hunt madam it would be but a poor return to the favour you have do i to sacrifice any gallantry to the possession of you and i would certainly do it though i be not disengage as at present i be from any affair of that kind
but i should not be the honest man you think i if i do not tell you that my affection be engage to another who be a woman of virtue and one that i never can leave though it be probable i shall never possess she
god forbid that in return of your kindness to i i should do you such an injury as to give you my hand when i cannot give my heart
no i have much rather starve than be guilty of that
even though my mistress be marry to another i would not marry you unless my heart have entirely efface all impression of she
be assure that your secret be not more safe in your own breast than in that of your most oblige and grateful humble servant t
jones when our heroe have finish and send this letter he go to his scrutore take out miss western s muff kiss it several time and then strut some turn about his room with more satisfaction of mind than ever any irishman feel in carry off a fortune of fifty thousand pound
chapter xii
a discovery make by partridge
while jones be exult in the consciousness of his integrity partridge come caper into the room as be his custom when he bring or fancy he bring any good tiding
he have be despatch that morning by his master with order to endeavour by the servant of lady bellaston or by any other mean to discover whither sophia have be convey and he now return and with a joyful countenance tell our heroe that he have find the lose bird
i have see sir say he black george the gamekeeper who be one of the servant whom the squire hath bring with he to town
i know he presently though i have not see he these several year but you know sir he be a very remarkable man or to use a purer phrase he hath a most remarkable beard the large and black i ever see
it be some time however before black george could recollect i well but what be your good news cry jones what do you know of my sophia you shall know presently sir answer partridge i be come to it as fast as i can
you be so impatient sir you would come at the infinitive mood before you can get to the imperative
as i be say sir it be some time before he recollect my face confound your face cry jones what of my sophia nay sir answer partridge i know nothing more of madam sophia than what i be go to tell you and i should have tell you all before this if you have not interrupt i but if you look so angry at i you will frighten all of it out of my head or to use a purer phrase out of my memory
i never see you look so angry since the day we leave upton which i shall remember if i be to live a thousand year well pray go on your own way say jones you be resolve to make i mad i find not for the world answer partridge i have suffer enough for that already which as i say i shall bear in my remembrance the long day i have to live well but black george cry jones
well sir as i be say it be a long time before he could recollect i for indeed i be very much alter since i see he
non sum quali eram i have have trouble in the world and nothing alter a man so much as grief
i have hear it will change the colour of a man s hair in a night
however at last know i he do thatbe sure enough for we be both of an age and be at the same charity school
george be a great dunce but no matter for that all man do not thrive in the world accord to their learning
i be sure i have reason to say so but it will be all one a thousand year hence
well sir where be i o--well we no soon know each other than after many hearty shake by the hand we agree to go to an alehouse and take a pot and by good luck the beer be some of the good i have meet with since i have be in town
now sir i be come to the point for no soon do i name you and tell he that you and i come to town together and have live together ever since than he call for another pot and swear he would drink to your health and indeed he drink your health so heartily that i be overjoyed to see there be so much gratitude leave in the world and after we have empty that pot i say i would buy my pot too and so we drink another to your health and then i make haste home to tell you the news what news cry jones you have not mention a word of my sophia bless i
i have like to have forget that
indeed we mention a great deal about young madam western and george tell i all that mr blifil be come to town in order to be married to she
he have well make haste then say i or somebody will have she before he come and indeed say i mr seagrim it be a thousand pity somebody should not have she for he certainly love she above all the woman in the world
i would have both you and she know that it be not for her fortune he follow she for i can assure you as to matter of that there be another lady one of much great quality and fortune than she can pretend to who be so fond of somebody that she come after he day and night here jones fall into a passion with partridge for have as he say betray he but the poor fellow answer he have mention no name besides sir say he i can assure you george be sincerely your friend and wish mr blifil at the devil more than once nay he say he would do anything in his power upon earth to serve you and so i be convince he will
betray you indeed
why i question whether you have a well friend than george upon earth except myself or one that would go far to serve you well say jones a little pacify you say this fellow who i believe indeed be enough incline to be my friend live in the same house with sophia in the same house answer partridge why sir he be one of the servant of the family and very well drest i promise you he be if it be not for his black beard you would hardly know he one service then at least he may do i say jones sure he can certainly convey a letter to my sophia you have hit the nail ad unguem cry partridge how come i not to think of it
i will engage he shall do it upon the very first mention well then say jones do you leave i at present and i will write a letter which you shall deliver to he to-morrow morning for i suppose you know where to find he o yes sir answer partridge i shall certainly find he again there be no fear of that
the liquor be too good for he to stay away long
i make no doubt but he will be there every day he stay in town so you donot know the street then where my sophia be lodge cry jones
indeed sir i do say partridge
what be the name of the street cry jones
the name sir
why here sir just by answer partridge not above a street or two off
i donot indeed know the very name for as he never tell i if i have ask you know it might have put some suspicion into his head
no no sir let i alone for that
i be too cunning for that i promise you thou art most wonderfully cunning indeed reply jones however i will write to my charmer since i believe you will be cunning enough to find he to-morrow at the alehouse and now have dismiss the sagacious partridge mr jones sit himself down to write in which employment we shall leave he for a time
and here we put an end to the fifteenth book
book xvi
contain the space of five day
chapter i
of prologue
i have hear of a dramatic writer who use to say he would rather write a play than a prologue in like manner i think i can with less pain write one of the book of this history than the prefatory chapter to each of they
to say the truth i believe many a hearty curse hath be devoted on the head of that author who first institute the method of prefix to his play that portion of matter which be call the prologue and which at first be part of the piece itself but of latter year hath have usually so little connexion with the drama before which it stand that the prologue to one play might as well serve for any other
those indeed of more modern date seem all to be write on the same three topic viz an abuse of the taste of the town a condemnation of all contemporary author and an eulogium on the performance just about to be represent
the sentiment in all these be very little varied nor be it possible they should and indeed i have often wonder at the great invention of author who have be capable of find such various phrase to express the same thing
in like manner i apprehend some future historian if any one shall do i the honour of imitate my manner will after much scratch his pate bestow some good wish on my memory for have first establish these several initial chapter most of which like modern prologue may as properly be prefix to any other book in this history as to that which they introduce or indeed to any other history as to this
but however author may suffer by either of these invention the reader will find sufficient emolument in the one as the spectator hath long find in the other
first it be well know that the prologue serve the critic for an opportunity to try his faculty of hiss and to tune his cat-call to the good advantage by which mean i have know those musical instrument so well prepare that they have be able to play in full concert at the first rise of the curtain
the same advantage may be draw from these chapter in which the critic will be always sure of meet with something that may serve as a whetstone to his noble spirit so that he may fall with a more hungry appetite for censure on the history itself
and here his sagacity must make it needless to observe how artfully these chapter be calculate for that excellent purpose for in these we have always take care to intersperse somewhat of the sour or acid kind in order to sharpen and stimulate the say spirit of criticism
again the indolent reader as well as spectator find great advantage from both these for as they be not oblige either to see the one or read the other and both the play and the book be thus protract by the former they have a quarter of an hour long allow they to sit at dinner and by the latter they have the advantage of begin to read at the fourth or fifth page instead of the first a matter by no mean of trivial consequence to person who read book with no other view than to say they have read they a more general motive to reading than be commonly imagine and from which not only law book and good book but the page of homer and virgil of swift and cervantes have be often turn over
many other be the emolument which arise from both these but they be for the most part so obvious that we shall not at present stay to enumerate they especially since it occur to we that the principal merit of both the prologue and the preface be that they be short
chapter ii
a whimsical adventure which befel the squire with the distressed situation of sophia
we must now convey the reader to mr western s lodging which be in piccadilly where he be place by the recommendation of the landlord at the hercules pillars at hyde park corner for at the inn which be the first he see on his arrival in town he place his horse and in those lodging which be the first he hear of he deposit himself
here when sophia alight from the hackney-coach which bring she from the house of lady bellaston she desire to retire to the apartment provide for she to which her father very readily agree and whither he attend she himself
a short dialogue neither very material nor pleasant to relate minutely then pass between they in which he press she vehemently to give her consent to the marriage with blifil who as he acquaint she be to be in town in a few day but instead of comply she give a more peremptory and resolute refusal than she have ever do before
this so incense she father that after many bitter vow that he would force she to have he whether she would or no he depart from she with many hard word and curse lock the door and put the key into his pocket
while sophia be leave with no other company than what attend the close state prisoner namely fire and candle the squire sit down to regale himself over a bottle of wine with his parson and the landlord of the hercules pillars who as the squire say would make an excellent third man and could inform they of the news of the town and how affair go for to be sure say he he know a great deal since the horse of many of the quality stand at his house
in this agreeable society mr western past that evening and great part of the succeed day during which period nothing happen of sufficient consequence to find a place in this history
all this time sophia past by herself for her father swear she should never come out of her chamber alive unless she first consent to marry blifil nor do he ever suffer the door to be unlocked unless to convey her food on which occasion he always attend himself
the second morning after his arrival while he and the parson be at breakfast together on a toast and tankard he be inform that a gentleman be below to wait on he
a gentleman quoth the squire who the devil can he be
do doctor go down and see who tis
mr blifil can hardly be come to town yet go down do and know what his business be the doctor return with an account that it be a very well-dr man and by the ribbon in his hat he take he for an officer of the army that he say he have some particular business which he could deliver to none but mr western himself
an officer cry the squire what can any such fellow have to do with i
if he want an order for baggage-waggon i be no justice of peace here nor can i grant a warrant let un come up then if he must speak to i a very genteel man now enter the room who have make his compliment to the squire and desire the favour of be alone with he deliver himself as follows sir i come to wait upon you by the command of my lord fellamar but with a very different message from what i suppose you expect after what past the other night my lord who cry the squire i never hear the name o un his lordship say the gentleman be willing to impute everything to the effect of liquor and the most trifling acknowledgment of that kind will set everything right for as he hath the most violent attachment to your daughter you sir be the last person upon earth from whom he would resent an affront and happy be it for you both that he hath give such public demonstration of his courage as to be able to put up an affair of this kind without danger of any imputation on his honour
all he desire therefore be that you will before i make some acknowledgment the slight in the world will be sufficient and he intend this afternoon to pay his respect to you in order to obtain your leave of visit the young lady on the footing of a lover i donot understand much of what you say sir say the squire but i suppose by what you talk about my daughter that this be the lord which my cousin lady bellaston mention to i and say something about his court my daughter
if so be that how that be the case--you may give my service to his lordship and tell un the girl be dispose of already perhaps sir say the gentleman you be not sufficiently apprize of the greatness of this offer
i believe such a person title and fortune would be nowhere refuse lookee sir answer the squire to be very plain my daughter be bespoke already but if she be not i would not marry she to a lord upon any account i hate all lord they be a parcel of courtier and hanoverians and i will have nothing to do with they well sir say the gentleman if that be your resolution the message i be to deliver to you be that my lord desire the favour of your company this morning in hyde park you may tell my lord answer the squire that i be busy and cannot come
i have enough to look after at home and cannot stir abroad on any account i be sure sir quoth the other you be too much a gentleman to send such a message you will not i be convince have it say of you that after have affront a noble peer you refuse he satisfaction
his lordship would have be willing from his great regard to the young lady to have make up matter in another way but unless he be to look on you as a father his honour will not suffer his put up such an indignity as you must be sensible you offer he i offer he cry the squire it be a d--n d lie
i never offer he anything upon these word the gentleman return a very short verbal rebuke and this he accompany at the same time with some manual remonstrance which no soon reach the ear of mr western than that worthy squire begin to caper very briskly about the room bellowing at the same time with all his might as if desirous to summon a great number of spectator to behold his agility
the parson who have leave great part of the tankard unfinishe be not retire far he immediately attend therefore on the squire s vociferation crying bless i
sir whatbe the matter matter quoth the squire herebe a highwayman i believe who want to rob and murder i--for he hath fall upon i with that stick there in his hand when i wish i may be d--n d if i gid un the least provocation how sir say the captain do you not tell i i lye no as i hope to be save answer the squire i believe i might say twas a lie that i have offer any affront to my lord--but i never say the word you lie i understand myself well and you might have understand yourself well than to fall upon a naked man
if i have a stick in my hand you would not have dare strike i
iwould have knock thy lantern jaw about thy ear
come down into yard this minute and iwill take a bout with thee at single stick for a break head that i will or i will go into naked room and box thee for a belly-full
at unt half a man at unt ibe sure the captain with some indignation reply i see sir you be below my notice and i shall inform his lordship you be below his
i be sorry i have dirty my finger with you at which word he withdraw the parson interpose to prevent the squire from stop he in which he easily prevail as the other though he make some effort for the purpose do not seem very violently bent on success
however when the captain be depart the squire send many curse and some menace after he but as these do not set out from his lip till the officer be at the bottom of the stair and grow loud and louder as he be more and more remote they do not reach his ear or at least do not retard his departure
poor sophia however who in her prison hear all her father s outcry from first to last begin now first to thunder with her foot and afterwards to scream as loudly as the old gentleman himself have do before though in a much sweet voice
these scream soon silence the squire and turn all his consideration towards his daughter whom he love so tenderly that the least apprehension of any harm happen to she throw he presently into agony for except in that single instance in which the whole future happiness of her life be concern she be sovereign mistress of his inclination
have end his rage against the captain with swear he would take the law of he the squire now mount upstairs to sophia whom as soon as he have unlock and open the door he find all pale and breathless
the moment however that she see her father she collect all her spirit and catch he hold by the hand she cry passionately o my dear sir i be almost frightened to death
i hope to heaven no harm hath happen to you no no cry the squire no great harm
the rascal hath not hurt i much but rat i if i donot ha the la o un pray dear sir say she tell i whatbe the matter who be it that hath insult you i donot know the name o un answer western some officer fellow i suppose that we be to pay for beat we but iwill make he pay this bout if the rascal hath get anything which i suppose he hath not
for thof he be drest out so vine i question whether he have get a voot of land in the world but dear sir cry she what be the occasion of your quarrel what should it be sophy answer the squire but about you sophy
all my misfortune be about you you will be the death of your poor father at last
herebe a varlet of a lord the lord know who forsooth
who hath a taan a liking to you and because i would not gi un my consent he send i a kallenge
come do be a good girl sophy and put an end to all your father s trouble come do consent to ha un he will be in town within this day or two do but promise i to marry un as soon as he come and you will make i the happy man in the world and i will make you the happy woman you shall have the fine cloath in london and the fine jewel and a coach and six at your command
i promise allworthy already to give up half my estate--od rabbet it
i should hardly stick at give up the whole will my papa be so kind say she as to hear i speak why wout ask sophy cry he when dost know i have rather hear thy voice than the musick of the good pack of dog in england hear thee my dear little girl
i hope i shall hear thee as long as i live for if i be ever to lose that pleasure i would not gee a brass varden to live a moment long
indeed sophy you do not know how i love you indeed you donot or you never could have run away and leave your poor father who hath no other joy no other comfort upon earth but his little sophy at these word the tear stand in his eye and sophia with the tear stream from her answer indeed my dear papa i know you have love i tenderly and heaven be my witness how sincerely i have return your affection nor could anything but an apprehension of be force into the arm of this man have drive i to run from a father whom i love so passionately that i would with pleasure sacrifice my life to his happiness nay i have endeavour to reason myself into do more and have almost work up a resolution to endure the most miserable of all life to comply with your inclination
it be that resolution alone to which i could not force my mind nor can i ever here the squire begin to look wild and the foam appear at his lip which sophia observing beg to be hear out and then proceed if my father s life his health or any real happiness of his be at stake here stand your resolved daughter may heaven blast i if there be a misery i would not suffer to preserve you no that most detest most loathsome of all lot would i embrace
i would give my hand to blifil for your sake i tell thee it will preserve i answer the father it will give i health happiness life everything upon my soul i shall die if dost refuse i i shall break my heart i shall upon my soul is it possible say she you can have such a desire to make i miserable i tell thee noa answer he loudly d--n i if there be a thing upon earth i would not do to see thee happy and will not my dear papa allow i to have the least knowledge of what will make i so
if it be true that happiness consist in opinion what must be my condition when i shall think myself the most miserable of all the wretch upon earth well think yourself so say he than know it by be marry to a poor bastardly vagabond if it will content you sir say sophia i will give you the most solemn promise never to marry he nor any other while my papa live without his consent
let i dedicate my whole life to your service let i be again your poor sophy and my whole business and pleasure be as it hath be to please and divert you lookee sophy answer the squire i be not to be chouse in this manner
your aunt western would then have reason to think i the fool she doth
no no sophy iwould have you to know i have a get more wisdom and know more of the world than to take the word of a woman in a matter where a man be concern how sir have i deserve this want of confidence say she have i ever break a single promise to you
or have i ever be find guilty of a falsehood from my cradle lookee sophy cry he thatbe neither here nor there
i be determine upon this match and have he you shall d--n i if shat unt
d--n i if shat unt though dost hang thyself the next morning at repeat which word he clinch his fist knit his brow bit his lip and thunder so loud that the poor afflict terrified sophia sunk tremble into her chair and have not a flood of tear come immediately to her relief perhaps bad have follow
western beheld the deplorable condition of his daughter with no more contrition or remorse than the turnkey of newgate feel at view the agony of a tender wife when take she last farewel of she condemn husband or rather he look down on she with the same emotion which arise in an honest fair tradesman who see his debtor drag to prison for 10 which though a just debt the wretch be wickedly unable to pay
or to hit the case still more nearly he feel the same compunction with a bawd when some poor innocent whom she hath ensnare into her hand fall into fit at the first proposal of what be call see company
indeed this resemblance would be exact be it not that the bawd hath an interest in what she doth and the father though perhaps he may blindly think otherwise can in reality have none in urge his daughter to almost an equal prostitution
in this condition he leave his poor sophia and depart with a very vulgar observation on the effect of tear he lock the room and return to the parson who say everything he durst in behalf of the young lady which though perhaps it be not quite so much as his duty require yet be it sufficient to throw the squire into a violent rage and into many indecent reflection on the whole body of the clergy which we have too great an honour for that sacred function to commit to paper
chapter iii
what happen to sophia during her confinement
the landlady of the house where the squire lodge have begin very early to entertain a strange opinion of her guest
however as she be inform that the squire be a man of vast fortune and as she have take care to exact a very extraordinary price for her room she do not think proper to give any offence for though she be not without some concern for the confinement of poor sophia of whose great sweetness of temper and affability the maid of the house have make so favourable a report which be confirm by all the squire s servant yet she have much more concern for her own interest than to provoke one whom as she say she perceive to be a very hastish kind of a gentleman
though sophia eat but little yet she be regularly serve with her meal indeed i believe if she have like any one rarity that the squire however angry would have spar neither pain nor cost to have procure it for she since however strange it may appear to some of my reader he really doate on his daughter and to give she any kind of pleasure be the high satisfaction of his life
the dinner-hour being arrive black george carry she up a pullet the squire himself for he have swear not to part with the key attend the door
as george deposit the dish some compliment pass between he and sophia for he have not see she since she leave the country and she treat every servant with more respect than some person shew to those who be in a very slight degree their inferior
sophia would have have he take the pullet back say she could not eat but george beg she to try and particularly recommend to she the egg of which he say it be full
all this time the squire be wait at the door but george be a great favourite with his master as his employment be in concern of the high nature namely about the game and be accustom to take many liberty
he have officiously carry up the dinner be as he say very desirous to see his young lady he make therefore no scruple of keep his master stand above ten minute while civility be pass between he and sophia for which he receive only a good-humour rebuke at the door when he return
the egg of pullet partridge pheasant c be as george well know the most favourite dainty of sophia
it be therefore no wonder that he who be a very good-natured fellow should take care to supply she with this kind of delicacy at a time when all the servant in the house be afraid she would be starve for she have scarce swallow a single morsel in the last forty hour
though vexation hath not the same effect on all person as it usually hath on a widow whose appetite it often render sharp than it can be render by the air on bansted down or salisbury plain yet the sublime grief notwithstanding what some people may say to the contrary will eat at last
and sophia herself after some little consideration begin to dissect the fowl which she find to be as full of egg as george have report it
but if she be pleased with these it contain something which would have delight the royal society much more for if a fowl with three leg be so invaluable a curiosity when perhaps time hath produce a thousand such at what price shall we esteem a bird which so totally contradict all the law of animal oeconomy as to contain a letter in its belly
ovid tell we of a flower into which hyacinthus be metamorphose that bear letter on its leaf which virgil recommend as a miracle to the royal society of his day but no age nor nation hath ever record a bird with a letter in its maw
but though a miracle of this kind might have engage all the académies des sciences in europe and perhaps in a fruitless enquiry yet the reader by barely recollect the last dialogue which pass between messieurs jones and partridge will be very easily satisfied from whence this letter come and how it find its passage into the fowl
sophia notwithstanding her long fast and notwithstanding her favourite dish be there before she no soon see the letter than she immediately snatch it up tear it open and read as follows madam be i not sensible to whom i have the honour of writing i should endeavour however difficult to paint the horror of my mind at the account bring i by mrs honour but as tenderness alone can have any true idea of the pang which tenderness be capable of feeling so can this most amiable quality which my sophia possess in the most eminent degree sufficiently inform she what her jones must have suffer on this melancholy occasion
be there a circumstance in the world which can heighten my agony when i hear of any misfortune which hath befall you
surely there be one only and with that i be accurse
it be my sophia the dreadful consideration that i be myself the wretched cause
perhaps i here do myself too much honour but none will envy i an honour which cost i so extremely dear
pardon i this presumption and pardon i a great still if i ask you whether my advice my assistance my presence my absence my death or my torture can bring you any relief
can the most perfect admiration the most watchful observance the most ardent love the most melting tenderness the most resign submission to your will make you amend for what you be to sacrifice to my happiness
if they can fly my lovely angel to those arm which be ever open to receive and protect you and to which whether you bring yourself alone or the rich of the world with you be in my opinion an alternative not worth regard
if on the contrary wisdom shall predominate and on the most mature reflection inform you that the sacrifice be too great and if there be no way leave to reconcile your father and restore the peace of your dear mind but by abandon i i conjure you drive i for ever from your thought exert your resolution and let no compassion for my suffering bear the least weight in that tender bosom
believe i madam i so sincerely love you well than myself that my great and principal end be your happiness
my first wish why would not fortune indulge i in it be and pardon i if i say still be to see you every moment the happy of woman my second wish be to hear you be so but no misery on earth can equal mine while i think you owe an uneasy moment to he who be madam in every sense and to every purpose your devoted thomas jones what sophia say or do or think upon this letter how often she read it or whether more than once shall all be leave to our reader s imagination
the answer to it he may perhaps see hereafter but not at present for this reason among other that she do not now write any and that for several good cause one of which be this she have no paper pen nor ink
in the evening while sophia be meditate on the letter she have receive or on something else a violent noise from below disturb her meditation
this noise be no other than a round bout at altercation between two person
one of the combatant by his voice she immediately distinguish to be her father but she do not so soon discover the shrill pipe to belong to the organ of her aunt western who be just arrive in town where have by mean of one of her servant who stopt at the hercules pillars learn where she brother lodge she drive directly to his lodging
we shall therefore take our leave at present of sophia and with our usual good-breeding attend her ladyship
chapter iv
in which sophia be deliver from her confinement
the squire and the parson for the landlord be now otherwise engage be smoake their pipe together when the arrival of the lady be first signify
the squire no soon hear her name than he immediately run down to usher her upstairs for he be a great observer of such ceremonial especially to his sister of whom he stand more in awe than of any other human creature though he never would own this nor do he perhaps know it himself
mrs western on her arrival in the dining-room have fling herself into a chair begin thus to harangue well surely no one ever have such an intolerable journey
i think the road since so many turnpike act be grow bad than ever
la brother how could you get into this odious place
no person of condition i dare swear ever set foot here before i donot know cry the squire i think they do well enough it be landlord recommend they
i think as he know most of the quality he could well shew i where to get among um well and wherebe my niece say the lady have you be to wait upon lady bellaston yet ay ay cry the squire your niece be safe enough she be upstairs in chamber how answer the lady be my niece in this house and do she not know of my be here no nobody can well get to she say the squire for she be under lock and key
i have she safe i vetche she from my lady cousin the first night i come to town and i have take care o she ever since she be as secure as a fox in a bag i promise you good heaven return mrs western what do i hear
i think what a fine piece of work would be the consequence of my consent to your come to town yourself nay it be indeed your own headstrong will nor can i charge myself with have ever consent to it
do not you promise i brother that you would take none of these headstrong measure
be it not by these headstrong measure that you force my niece to run away from you in the country
have you a mind to oblige she to take such another step z--ds and the devil cry the squire dash his pipe on the ground do ever mortal hear the like
when i expect you would have commend i for all i have do to be fall upon in this manner how brother say the lady have i ever give you the least reason to imagine i should commend you for lock up your daughter
have i not often tell you that woman in a free country be not to be treat with such arbitrary power
we be as free as the man and i heartily wish i could not say we deserve that freedom well
if you expect i should stay a moment long in this wretched house or that i should ever own you again as my relation or that i should ever trouble myself again with the affair of your family i insist upon it that my niece be set at liberty this instant this she speak with so command an air stand with she back to the fire with one hand behind she and a pinch of snuff in the other that i question whether thalestris at the head of her amazons ever make a more tremendous figure
it be no wonder therefore that the poor squire be not proof against the awe which she inspire
there he cry throw down the key there it be do whatever you please
i intend only to have keep she up till blifil come to town which cannot be long and now if any harm happen in the mean time remember who be to be blame for it i will answer it with my life cry mrs western but i shall not intermeddle at all unless upon one condition and that be that you will commit the whole entirely to my care without take any one measure yourself unless i shall eventually appoint you to act
if you ratify these preliminary brother i yet will endeavour to preserve the honour of your family if not i shall continue in a neutral state i pray you good sir say the parson permit yourself this once to be admonish by her ladyship peradventure by commune with young madam sophia she will effect more than you have be able to perpetrate by more rigorous measure what dost thee open upon i cry the squire if thee dost begin to babble i shall whip thee in presently fie brother answer the lady be this language to a clergyman
mr supple be a man of sense and give you the good advice and the whole world i believe will concur in his opinion but i must tell you i expect an immediate answer to my categorical proposal
either cede your daughter to my disposal or take she wholly to your own surprizing discretion and then i here before mr supple evacuate the garrison and renounce you and your family for ever i pray you let i be a mediator cry the parson let i supplicate you why there lie the key on the table cry the squire
she may take un up if she please who hinder she no brother answer the lady i insist on the formality of its be deliver i with a full ratification of all the concession stipulate why then i will deliver it to you there tis cry the squire
i be sure sister you cannot accuse i of ever deny to trust my daughter to you
she hath a-live wi you a whole year and muore to a time without my ever zeee she and it would have be happy for she answer the lady if she have always live with i

all your whole scheme of politic be wrong
i once more therefore insist that you do not intermeddle
remember only what be past z--ds and bl--d sister cry the squire what would you have i say

i see brother there be no talk to you

the young lady have on her hat and capuchin and the aunt acquaint mr western that she intend to take her niece with she to her own lodging for indeed brother say she these room be not fit to receive a christian soul in very well madam quoth western whatever you please
the girl can never be in well hand than your and the parson here can do i the justice to say that i have say fifty time behind your back that you be one of the most sensible woman in the world to this cry the parson i be ready to bear testimony nay brother say mrs western i have always ibe sure give you as favourable a character
you must own you have a little too much hastiness in your temper but when you will allow yourself time to reflect i never know a man more reasonable why then sister if you think so say the squire herebe your good health with all my heart
i be a little passionate sometimes but i scorn to bear any malice
sophy do you be a good girl and do everything your aunt order you i have not the least doubt of she answer mrs western
she hath have already an example before her eye in the behaviour of that wretch her cousin harriet who ruin herself by neglect my advice
o brother what think you
you be hardly go out of hearing when you set out for london when who should arrive but that impudent fellow with the odious irish name--that fitzpatrick
he break in abruptly upon i without notice or i would not have see he
he run on a long unintelligible story about his wife to which he force i to give he a hearing but i make he very little answer and deliver he the letter from his wife which i bid he answer himself
i suppose the wretch will endeavour to find we out but i beg you will not see she for i be determined i will not i zee she answer the squire you need not fear i
iwill ge no encouragement to such undutiful wench
