you zee sophy what undutifulness bring volk to
you have an example in your own family brother cry the aunt you need not shock my niece by such odious repetition
why will you not leave everything entirely to i well well i wull i wull say the squire
and now mrs western luckily for sophia put an end to the conversation by order chair to be call
i say luckily for have it continue much long fresh matter of dissension would most probably have arise between the brother and sister between whom education and sex make the only difference for both be equally violent and equally positive they have both a vast affection for sophia and both a sovereign contempt for each other
chapter v
in which jones receive a letter from sophia and go to a play with mrs miller and partridge
the arrival of black george in town and the good office which that grateful fellow have promise to do for his old benefactor greatly comfort jones in the midst of all the anxiety and uneasiness which he have suffer on the account of sophia from whom by the mean of the say george he receive the follow answer to his letter which sophia to whom the use of pen ink and paper be restore with her liberty write the very evening when she depart from her confinement sir as i do not doubt your sincerity in what you write you will be pleased to hear that some of my affliction be at an end by the arrival of my aunt western with whom i be at present and with whom i enjoy all the liberty i can desire
one promise my aunt hath insist on my making which be that i will not see or converse with any person without her knowledge and consent
this promise i have most solemnly give and shall most inviolably keep and though she hath not expressly forbid i writing yet that must be an omission from forgetfulness or this perhaps be include in the word converse
however as i cannot but consider this as a breach of her generous confidence in my honour you cannot expect that i shall after this continue to write myself or to receive letter without her knowledge
a promise be with i a very sacred thing and to be extend to everything understand from it as well as to what be express by it and this consideration may perhaps on reflection afford you some comfort
but why should i mention a comfort to you of this kind for though there be one thing in which i can never comply with the good of father yet be i firmly resolve never to act in defiance of he or to take any step of consequence without his consent
a firm persuasion of this must teach you to divert your thought from what fortune hath perhaps make impossible
this your own interest persuade you
this may reconcile i hope mr allworthy to you and if it will you have my injunction to pursue it
accident have lay some obligation on i and your good intention probably more
fortune may perhaps be some time kind to we both than at present
believe this that i shall always think of you as i think you deserve and be sir your obliged humble servant sophia western
i charge you write to i no more--at present at least and accept this which be now of no service to i which i know you must want and think you owe the trifle only to that fortune by which you find it meaning perhaps the bank-bill for 100
a child who hath just learn his letter would have spell this letter out in less time than jones take in read it
the sensation it occasion be a mixture of joy and grief somewhat like what divide the mind of a good man when he peruse the will of his deceased friend in which a large legacy which his distress make the more welcome be bequeath to he
upon the whole however he be more pleased than displeased and indeed the reader may probably wonder that he be displeased at all but the reader be not quite so much in love as be poor jones and love be a disease which though it may in some instance resemble a consumption which it sometimes cause in other proceed in direct opposition to it and particularly in this that it never flatter itself or see any one symptom in a favourable light
one thing give he complete satisfaction which be that his mistress have regain her liberty and be now with a lady where she might at least assure herself of a decent treatment
another comfortable circumstance be the reference which she make to her promise of never marry any other man for however disintereste he might imagine his passion and notwithstanding all the generous overture make in his letter i very much question whether he could have hear a more afflict piece of news than that sophia be married to another though the match have be never so great and never so likely to end in make she completely happy
that refined degree of platonic affection which be absolutely detach from the flesh and be indeed entirely and purely spiritual be a gift confine to the female part of the creation many of whom i have hear declare and doubtless with great truth that they would with the utmost readiness resign a lover to a rival when such resignation be prove to be necessary for the temporal interest of such lover
hence therefore i conclude that this affection be in nature though i cannot pretend to say i have ever see an instance of it
mr jones have spend three hour in read and kiss the aforesaid letter and be at last in a state of good spirit from the last-mention consideration he agree to carry an appointment which he have before make into execution
this be to attend mrs miller and her young daughter into the gallery at the play-house and to admit mr partridge as one of the company
for as jones have really that taste for humour which many affect he expect to enjoy much entertainment in the criticism of partridge from whom he expect the simple dictate of nature unimproved indeed but likewise unadulterated by art
in the first row then of the first gallery do mr jones mrs miller her young daughter and partridge take their place
partridge immediately declare it be the fine place he have ever be in
when the first music be play he say it be a wonder how so many fiddler could play at one time without put one another out while the fellow be light the upper candle he cry out to mrs miller look look madam the very picture of the man in the end of the common-prayer book before the gunpowder-treason service nor could he help observe with a sigh when all the candle be light that here be candle enough burn in one night to keep an honest poor family for a whole twelvemonth as soon as the play which be hamlet prince of denmark begin partridge be all attention nor do he break silence till the entrance of the ghost upon which he ask jones what man that be in the strange dress something say he like what i have see in a picture
sure it be not armour be it jones answer that be the ghost to which partridge reply with a smile persuade i to that sir if you can
though i cannot say i ever actually see a ghost in my life yet i be certain i should know one if i see he well than that come to
no no sir ghost donot appear in such dress as that neither in this mistake which cause much laughter in the neighbourhood of partridge he be suffer to continue till the scene between the ghost and hamlet when partridge give that credit to mr garrick which he have deny to jones and fall into so violent a trembling that his knee knock against each other
jones ask he what be the matter and whether he be afraid of the warrior upon the stage

he durst not go to bed all that night for fear of the ghost and for many night after sweat two or three hour before he go to sleep with the same apprehension and wake several time in great horror cry out lord have mercy upon we
there it be chapter vi
in which the history be oblige to look back
it be almost impossible for the good parent to observe an exact impartiality to his child even though no superior merit should bias his affection but sure a parent can hardly be blame when that superiority determine his preference
as i regard all the personage of this history in the light of my child so i must confess the same inclination of partiality to sophia and for that i hope the reader will allow i the same excuse from the superiority of her character
this extraordinary tenderness which i have for my heroine never suffer i to quit she any long time without the utmost reluctance
i could now therefore return impatiently to enquire what hath happen to this lovely creature since her departure from her father s but that i be oblige first to pay a short visit to mr blifil
mr western in the first confusion into which his mind be cast upon the sudden news he receive of his daughter and in the first hurry to go after she have not once think of send any account of the discovery to blifil
he have not go far however before he recollect himself and accordingly stopt at the very first inn he come to and dispatch away a messenger to acquaint blifil with his have find sophia and with his firm resolution to marry she to he immediately if he would come up after he to town
as the love which blifil have for sophia be of that violent kind which nothing but the loss of her fortune or some such accident could lessen his inclination to the match be not at all alter by she have run away though he be oblige to lay this to his own account
he very readily therefore embrace this offer
indeed he now propose the gratification of a very strong passion besides avarice by marry this young lady and this be hatre for he conclude that matrimony afford an equal opportunity of satisfy either hatred or love and this opinion be very probably verify by much experience
to say the truth if we be to judge by the ordinary behaviour of married person to each other we shall perhaps be apt to conclude that the generality seek the indulgence of the former passion only in their union of everything but of heart
there be one difficulty however in his way and this arise from mr allworthy
that good man when he find by the departure of sophia for neither that nor the cause of it could be conceal from he the great aversion which she have for his nephew begin to be seriously concerned that he have be deceive into carry matter so far
he by no mean concur with the opinion of those parent who think it as immaterial to consult the inclination of their child in the affair of marriage as to solicit the good pleasure of their servant when they intend to take a journey and who be by law or decency at least withhold often from use absolute force
on the contrary as he esteem the institution to be of the most sacred kind he think every preparatory caution necessary to preserve it holy and inviolate and very wisely conclude that the sure way to effect this be by lay the foundation in previous affection
blifil indeed soon cure his uncle of all anger on the score of deceit by many vow and protestation that he have be deceive himself with which the many declaration of western very well tally but now to persuade allworthy to consent to the renew his address be a matter of such apparent difficulty that the very appearance be sufficient to have deter a less enterprizing genius but this young gentleman so well know his own talent that nothing within the province of cunning seem to he hard to be achieve
here then he represent the violence of his own affection and the hope of subdue aversion in the lady by perseverance
he beg that in an affair on which depend all his future repose he might at least be at liberty to try all fair mean for success
heaven forbid he say that he should ever think of prevail by any other than the most gentle method
besides sir say he if they fail you may then which will be surely time enough deny your consent he urge the great and eager desire which mr western have for the match and lastly he make great use of the name of jones to whom he impute all that have happen and from whom he say to preserve so valuable a young lady be even an act of charity
all these argument be well second by thwackum who dwelt a little strong on the authority of parent than mr blifil himself have do
he ascribe the measure which mr blifil be desirous to take to christian motif and though say he the good young gentleman hath mention charity last i be almost convinced it be his first and principal consideration square possibly have he be present would have sing to the same tune though in a different key and would have discover much moral fitness in the proceeding but he be now go to bath for the recovery of his health
allworthy though not without reluctance at last yield to the desire of his nephew
he say he would accompany he to london where he might be at liberty to use every honest endeavour to gain the lady but i declare say he i will never give my consent to any absolute force be put on her inclination nor shall you ever have she unless she can be bring freely to compliance thus do the affection of allworthy for his nephew betray the superior understanding to be triumph over by the inferior and thus be the prudence of the good of head often defeat by the tenderness of the good of heart
blifil have obtain this unhoped-for acquiescence in his uncle rest not till he carry his purpose into execution
and as no immediate business require mr allworthy s presence in the country and little preparation be necessary to man for a journey they set out the very next day and arrive in town that evening when mr jones as we have see be divert himself with partridge at the play
the morning after his arrival mr blifil wait on mr western by whom he be most kindly and graciously receive and from whom he have every possible assurance perhaps more than be possible that he should very shortly be as happy as sophia could make he nor would the squire suffer the young gentleman to return to his uncle till he have almost against his will carry he to his sister
chapter vii
in which mr western pay a visit to his sister in company with mr blifil
mrs western be read a lecture on prudence and matrimonial politic to her niece when her brother and blifil break in with less ceremony than the law of visit require
sophia no soon see blifil than she turn pale and almost lose the use of all her faculty but her aunt on the contrary wax red and have all her faculty at command begin to exert her tongue on the squire
brother say she i be astonish at your behaviour will you never learn any regard to decorum
will you still look upon every apartment as your own or as belong to one of your country tenant
do you think yourself at liberty to invade the privacy of woman of condition without the least decency or notice why what a pox be the matter now quoth the squire one would think i have catch you at-- none of your brutality sir i beseech you answer she you have surprize my poor niece so that she can hardly i see support herself go my dear retire and endeavour to recruit your spirit for i see you have occasion at which word sophia who never receive a more welcome command hastily withdraw
to be sure sister cry the squire you be mad when i have bring mr blifil here to court she to force she away sure brother say she you be bad than mad when you know in what situation affair be to----i be sure i ask mr blifil s pardon but he know very well to whom to impute so disagreeable a reception
for my own part i be sure i shall always be very glad to see mr blifil but his own good sense would not have suffer he to proceed so abruptly have you not compel he to it blifil bow and stammer and look like a fool but western without give he time to form a speech for the purpose answer well well i be to blame if you will i always be certainly but come let the girl be fetch back again or let mr blifil go to her he s come up on purpose and there be no time to be lose brother cry mrs western mr blifil i be confident understand himself well than to think of see my niece any more this morning after what hath happen
woman be of a nice contexture and our spirit when disorder be not to be recompose in a moment
have you suffer mr blifil to have send his compliment to my niece and to have desire the favour of wait on she in the afternoon i should possibly have prevail on she to have see he but now i despair of bring about any such matter i be very sorry madam cry blifil that mr western s extraordinary kindness to i which i can never enough acknowledge should have occasioned-- indeed sir say she interrupt he you need make no apology we all know my brother so well i donot care what anybody know of i answer the squire but when must he come to see she
for consider i tell you he be come up on purpose and so be allworthy brother say she whatever message mr blifil think proper to send to my niece shall be deliver to she and i suppose she will want no instruction to make a proper answer
i be convinced she will not refuse to see mr blifil at a proper time the devil she willnot answer the squire odsbud don t we know--i say nothing but some volk be wise than all the world if i might have have my will she have not run away before and now i expect to hear every moment she be guone again
for as great a fool as some volk think i i know very well she hates---- no matter brother reply mrs western i will not hear my niece abuse
it be a reflection on my family
she be an honour to it and she will be an honour to it i promise you
i will pawn my whole reputation in the world on her conduct i shall be glad to see you brother in the afternoon for i have somewhat of importance to mention to you at present mr blifil as well as you must excuse i for i be in haste to dress well but say the squire do appoint a time indeed say she i can appoint no time
i tell you i will see you in the afternoon what the devil would you have i do cry the squire turn to blifil i can no more turn she than a beagle can turn an old hare
perhaps she will be in a well humour in the afternoon i be condemn i see sir to misfortune answer blifil but i shall always own my obligation to you he then take a ceremonious leave of mrs western who be altogether as ceremonious on her part and then they depart the squire mutter to himself with an oath that blifil should see his daughter in the afternoon
if mr western be little pleased with this interview blifil be less
as to the former he impute the whole behaviour of his sister to her humour only and to her dissatisfaction at the omission of ceremony in the visit but blifil see a little deep into thing
he suspect somewhat of more consequence from two or three word which dropt from the lady and to say the truth he suspect right as will appear when i have unfold the several matter which will be contain in the follow chapter
chapter viii
scheme of lady bellaston for the ruin of jones
love have take too deep a root in the mind of lord fellamar to be pluck up by the rude hand of mr western
in the heat of resentment he have indeed give a commission to captain egglane which the captain have far exceed in the execution nor have it be execute at all have his lordship be able to find the captain after he have see lady bellaston which be in the afternoon of the day after he have receive the affront but so industrious be the captain in the discharge of his duty that have after long enquiry find out the squire s lodging very late in the evening he sit up all night at a tavern that he might not miss the squire in the morning and by that mean miss the revocation which my lord have send to his lodging
in the afternoon then next after the intend rape of sophia his lordship as we have say make a visit to lady bellaston who lay open so much of the character of the squire that his lordship plainly see the absurdity he have be guilty of in take any offence at his word especially as he have those honourable design on his daughter
he then unbosom the violence of his passion to lady bellaston who readily undertake the cause and encourage he with certain assurance of a most favourable reception from all the elder of the family and from the father himself when he should be sober and should be make acquaint with the nature of the offer make to his daughter
the only danger she say lie in the fellow she have formerly mention who though a beggar and a vagabond have by some mean or other she know not what procure himself tolerable cloath and past for a gentleman
now say she as i have for the sake of my cousin make it my business to enquire after this fellow i have luckily find out his lodging with which she then acquaint his lordship
i be think my lord add she for this fellow be too mean for your personal resentment whether it would not be possible for your lordship to contrive some method of have he press and send on board a ship
neither law nor conscience forbid this project for the fellow i promise you however well drest be but a vagabond and as proper as any fellow in the street to be press into the service and as for the conscientious part surely the preservation of a young lady from such ruin be a most meritorious act nay with regard to the fellow himself unless he could succeed which heaven forbid with my cousin it may probably be the mean of preserve he from the gallow and perhaps may make his fortune in an honest way lord fellamar very heartily thank her ladyship for the part which she be pleased to take in the affair upon the success of which his whole future happiness entirely depend
he say he see at present no objection to the press scheme and would consider of put it in execution
he then most earnestly recommend to her ladyship to do he the honour of immediately mention his proposal to the family to whom he say he offer a carte blanche and would settle his fortune in almost any manner they should require
and after utter many ecstasy and rapture concern sophia he take his leave and depart but not before he have receive the strong charge to beware of jones and to lose no time in secure his person where he should no long be in a capacity of make any attempt to the ruin of the young lady
the moment mrs western be arrive at her lodging a card be despatch with her compliment to lady bellaston who no soon receive it than with the impatience of a lover she fly to her cousin rejoice at this fair opportunity which beyond her hope offer itself for she be much well pleased with the prospect of make the proposal to a woman of sense and who know the world than to a gentleman whom she honour with the appellation of hottentot though indeed from he she apprehend no danger of a refusal
the two lady be meet after very short previous ceremonial fall to business which be indeed almost as soon conclude as begin for mrs western no soon hear the name of lord fellamar than her cheek glow with pleasure but when she be acquaint with the eagerness of his passion the earnestness of his proposal and the generosity of his offer she declare her full satisfaction in the most explicit term
in the progress of their conversation their discourse turn to jones and both cousin very pathetically lament the unfortunate attachment which both agree sophia have to that young fellow and mrs western entirely attribute it to the folly of her brother s management
she conclude however at last with declare her confidence in the good understanding of her niece who though she would not give up her affection in favour of blifil will i doubt not say she soon be prevail upon to sacrifice a simple inclination to the address of a fine gentleman who bring she both a title and a large estate for indeed add she i must do sophy the justice to confess this blifil be but a hideous kind of fellow as you know bellaston all country gentleman be and hath nothing but his fortune to recommend he nay say lady bellaston i donot then so much wonder at my cousin for i promise you this jones be a very agreeable fellow and hath one virtue which the man say be a great recommendation to we
what do you think mrs western--i shall certainly make you laugh nay i can hardly tell you myself for laugh--will you believe that the fellow hath have the assurance to make love to i
but if you should be inclined to disbelieve it here be evidence enough his own handwriting i assure you she then deliver her cousin the letter with the proposal of marriage which if the reader hath a desire to see he will find already on record in the xvth book of this history
upon my word i be astonish say mrs western this be indeed a masterpiece of assurance
with your leave i may possibly make some use of this letter you have my full liberty cry lady bellaston to apply it to what purpose you please
however i would not have it shewn to any but miss western nor to she unless you find occasion well and how do you use the fellow return mrs western
not as a husband say the lady i be not married i promise you my dear
you know bell i have try the comfort once already and once i think be enough for any reasonable woman this letter lady bellaston think would certainly turn the balance against jones in the mind of sophia and she be embolden to give it up partly by her hope of have he instantly dispatch out of the way and partly by have secure the evidence of honour who upon sound she she see sufficient reason to imagine be prepare to testify whatever she please
but perhaps the reader may wonder why lady bellaston who in her heart hat sophia should be so desirous of promote a match which be so much to the interest of the young lady
now i would desire such reader to look carefully into human nature page almost the last and there he will find in scarce legible character that woman notwithstanding the preposterous behaviour of mother aunt c in matrimonial matter do in reality think it so great a misfortune to have their inclination in love thwart that they imagine they ought never to carry enmity high than upon these disappointment again he will find it write much about the same place that a woman who hath once be pleased with the possession of a man will go above halfway to the devil to prevent any other woman from enjoy the same
if he will not be content with these reason i freely confess i see no other motive to the action of that lady unless we will conceive she be bribe by lord fellamar which for my own part i see no cause to suspect
now this be the affair which mrs western be prepare to introduce to sophia by some prefatory discourse on the folly of love and on the wisdom of legal prostitution for hire when her brother and blifil break abruptly in upon she and hence arise all that coldness in her behaviour to blifil which though the squire as be usual with he impute to a wrong cause infuse into blifil himself he be a much more cunning man a suspicion of the real truth
chapter ix
in which jones pay a visit to mrs fitzpatrick
the reader may now perhaps be pleased to return with we to mr jones who at the appoint hour attend on mrs fitzpatrick but before we relate the conversation which now past it may be proper accord to our method to return a little back and to account for so great an alteration of behaviour in this lady that from change her lodge principally to avoid mr jones she have now industriously as hath be see seek this interview
and here we shall need only to resort to what happen the precede day when hear from lady bellaston that mr western be arrive in town she go to pay her duty to he at his lodging at piccadilly where she be receive with many scurvy compellation too coarse to be repeat and be even threaten to be kick out of door
from hence an old servant of her aunt western with whom she be well acquaint conduct she to the lodging of that lady who treat she not more kindly but more politely or to say the truth with rudeness in another way
in short she return from both plainly convince not only that her scheme of reconciliation have prove abortive but that she must for ever give over all thought of bring it about by any mean whatever
from this moment desire of revenge only fill her mind and in this temper meeting jones at the play an opportunity seem to she to occur of effecting this purpose
the reader must remember that he be acquaint by mrs fitzpatrick in the account she give of her own story with the fondness mrs western have formerly shew for mr fitzpatrick at bath from the disappointment of which mrs fitzpatrick derive the great bitterness her aunt have express toward she
she have therefore no doubt but that the good lady would as easily listen to the address of mr jones as she have before do to the other for the superiority of charm be clearly on the side of mr jones and the advance which her aunt have since make in age she conclude how justly i will not say be an argument rather in favour of her project than against it
therefore when jones attend after a previous declaration of her desire of serve he arise as she say from a firm assurance how much she should by so do oblige sophia and after some excuse for her former disappointment and after acquaint mr jones in whose custody his mistress be of which she think he ignorant she very explicitly mention her scheme to he and advise he to make sham address to the old lady in order to procure an easy access to the young inform he at the same time of the success which mr fitzpatrick have formerly owe to the very same stratagem
mr jones express great gratitude to the lady for the kind intention towards he which she have express and indeed testify by this proposal but besides intimate some diffidence of success from the lady s knowledge of his love to her niece which have not be her case in regard to mr fitzpatrick he say he be afraid miss western would never agree to an imposition of this kind as well from her utter detestation of all fallacy as from her avow duty to her aunt
mrs fitzpatrick be a little nettled at this and indeed if it may not be call a lapse of the tongue it be a small deviation from politeness in jones and into which he scarce would have fall have not the delight he feel in praise sophia hurry he out of all reflection for this commendation of one cousin be more than a tacit rebuke on the other
indeed sir answer the lady with some warmth i cannot think there be anything easy than to cheat an old woman with a profession of love when her complexion be amorous and though she be my aunt i must say there never be a more liquorish one than her ladyship
cannot you pretend that the despair of possess her niece from her be promise to blifil have make you turn your thought towards she
as to my cousin sophia i cannot imagine she to be such a simpleton as to have the least scruple on such an account or to conceive any harm in punish one of these haggs for the many mischief they bring upon family by their tragi-comic passion for which i think it be a pity they be not punishable by law
i have no such scruple myself and yet i hope my cousin sophia will not think it an affront when i say she cannot det every real specie of falsehood more than her cousin fitzpatrick
to my aunt indeed i pretend no duty nor doth she deserve any
however sir i have give you my advice and if you decline pursue it i shall have the less opinion of your understanding--thatbe all jones now clearly see the error he have commit and exert his utmost power to rectify it but he only faultere and stutter into nonsense and contradiction
to say the truth it be often safe to abide by the consequence of the first blunder than to endeavour to rectify it for by such endeavour we generally plunge deeply instead of extricate ourselves and few person will on such occasion have the good-nature which mrs fitzpatrick display to jones by say with a smile you need attempt no more excuse for i can easily forgive a real lover whatever be the effect of fondness for his mistress she then renew her proposal and very fervently recommend it omit no argument which her invention could suggest on the subject for she be so violently incense against her aunt that scarce anything be capable of afford her equal pleasure with expose she and like a true woman she would see no difficulty in the execution of a favourite scheme
jones however persist in decline the undertaking which have not indeed the least probability of success
he easily perceive the motif which induce mrs fitzpatrick to be so eager in press she advice
he say he would not deny the tender and passionate regard he have for sophia but be so conscious of the inequality of their situation that he could never flatter himself so far as to hope that so divine a young lady would condescend to think on so unworthy a man nay he protest he could scarce bring himself to wish she should
he conclude with a profession of generous sentiment which we have not at present leisure to insert
there be some fine woman for i dare not here speak in too general term with whom self be so predominant that they never detach it from any subject and as vanity be with they a rule principle they be apt to lay hold of whatever praise they meet with and though the property of other convey it to their own use
in the company of these lady it be impossible to say anything handsome of another woman which they will not apply to themselves nay they often improve the praise they seize as for instance if her beauty her wit her gentility her good humour deserve so much commendation what do i deserve who possess those quality in so much more eminent a degree
to these lady a man often recommend himself while he be commend another woman and while he be express ardour and generous sentiment for his mistress they be consider what a charming lover this man would make to they who can feel all this tenderness for an inferior degree of merit
of this strange as it may seem i have see many instance besides mrs fitzpatrick to whom all this really happen and who now begin to feel a somewhat for mr jones the symptom of which she much soon understand than poor sophia have formerly do
to say the truth perfect beauty in both sex be a more irresistible object than it be generally think for notwithstanding some of we be content with more homely lot and learn by rote as child to repeat what give they no idea to despise outside and to value more solid charm yet i have always observe at the approach of consummate beauty that these more solid charm only shine with that kind of lustre which the star have after the rise of the sun
when jones have finish his exclamation many of which would have become the mouth of oroöndates himself mrs fitzpatrick heave a deep sigh and take her eye off from jones on whom they have be some time fix and drop they on the ground she cry indeed mr jones i pity you but it be the curse of such tenderness to be throw away on those who be insensible of it
i know my cousin well than you mr jones and i must say any woman who make no return to such a passion and such a person be unworthy of both sure madam say jones you cannot mean---- mean cry mrs fitzpatrick i know not what i mean there be something i think in true tenderness bewitch few woman ever meet with it in man and few still know how to value it when they do
i never hear such truly noble sentiment and i cannot tell how it be but you force one to believe you
sure she must be the most contemptible of woman who can overlook such merit the manner and look with which all this be speak infuse a suspicion into jones which we donot care to convey in direct word to the reader
instead of make any answer he say i be afraid madam i have make too tiresome a visit and offer to take his leave
not at all sir answer mrs fitzpatrick indeed i pity you mr jones indeed i do but if you be go consider of the scheme i have mention--i be convinced you will approve it--and let i see you again as soon as you can to-morrow morning if you will or at least some time to-morrow
i shall be at home all day jones then after many expression of thank very respectfully retire nor could mrs fitzpatrick forbear make he a present of a look at parting by which if he have understand nothing he must have have no understanding in the language of the eye
in reality it confirm his resolution of return to she no more for faulty as he hath hitherto appear in this history his whole thought be now so confine to his sophia that i believe no woman upon earth could have now draw he into an act of inconstancy
fortune however who be not his friend resolve as he intend to give she no second opportunity to make the good of this and accordingly produce the tragical incident which we be now in sorrowful note to record

the consequence of the precede visit

upon which the constable inform jones that he must go before a justice
he answer wherever you please i be indifferent as to what happen to i for though i be convinced i be not guilty of murder in the eye of the law yet the weight of blood i find intolerable upon my mind jones be now conduct before the justice where the surgeon who dress mr fitzpatrick appear and depose that he believe the wound to be mortal upon which the prisoner be commit to the gatehouse
it be very late at night so that jones would not send for partridge till the next morning and as he never shut his eye till seven so it be near twelve before the poor fellow who be greatly frightened at not hear from his master so long receive a message which almost deprive he of his being when he hear it
he go to the gatehouse with tremble knee and a beat heart and be no soon arrive in the presence of jones than he lament the misfortune that have befall he with many tear look all the while frequently about he in great terror for as the news now arrive that mr fitzpatrick be dead the poor fellow apprehend every minute that his ghost would enter the room

book xvii
contain three day
chapter i
contain a portion of introductory writing
when a comic writer hath make his principal character as happy as he can or when a tragic writer hath bring they to the high pitch of human misery they both conclude their business to be do and that their work be come to a period
have we be of the tragic complexion the reader must now allow we be very nearly arrive at this period since it would be difficult for the devil or any of his representative on earth to have contrive much great torment for poor jones than those in which we leave he in the last chapter and as for sophia a good-natured woman would hardly wish more uneasiness to a rival than what she must at present be suppose to feel
what then remain to complete the tragedy but a murder or two and a few moral sentence
but to bring our favourite out of their present anguish and distress and to land they at last on the shore of happiness seem a much hard task a task indeed so hard that we do not undertake to execute it
in regard to sophia it be more than probable that we shall somewhere or other provide a good husband for she in the end--either blifil or my lord or somebody else but as to poor jones such be the calamity in which he be at present involve owe to his imprudence by which if a man doth not become felon to the world he be at least a felo de se so destitute be he now of friend and so persecute by enemy that we almost despair of bring he to any good and if our reader delight in see execution i think he ought not to lose any time in take a first row at tyburn
this i faithfully promise that notwithstanding any affection which we may be suppose to have for this rogue whom we have unfortunately make our heroe we will lend he none of that supernatural assistance with which we be entrust upon condition that we use it only on very important occasion
if he doth not therefore find some natural mean of fairly extricate himself from all his distress we will do no violence to the truth and dignity of history for his sake for we have rather relate that he be hang at tyburn which may very probably be the case than forfeit our integrity or shock the faith of our reader
in this the antient have a great advantage over the modern
their mythology which be at that time more firmly believe by the vulgar than any religion be at present give they always an opportunity of deliver a favourite heroe
their deity be always ready at the writer s elbow to execute any of his purpose and the more extraordinary the invention be the great be the surprize and delight of the credulous reader
those writer could with great ease have convey a heroe from one country to another nay from one world to another and have bring he back again than a poor circumscribe modern can deliver he from a jail
the arabians and persians have an equal advantage in write their tale from the genius and fairy which they believe in as an article of their faith upon the authority of the koran itself
but we have none of these help
to natural mean alone we be confine let we try therefore what by these mean may be do for poor jones though to confess the truth something whisper i in the ear that he doth not yet know the bad of his fortune and that a more shocking piece of news than any he hath yet hear remain for he in the unopened leaf of fate

why then sir cry allworthy i can truly say without a compliment either to you or the young lady that when this match be propose i embrace it very readily and heartily from my regard to you both
an alliance between two family so nearly neighbour and between whom there have always exist so mutual an intercourse and good harmony i think a most desirable event and with regard to the young lady not only the concurrent opinion of all who know she but my own observation assure i that she would be an inestimable treasure to a good husband
i shall say nothing of her personal qualification which certainly be admirable her good nature her charitable disposition her modesty be too well know to need any panegyric but she hath one quality which exist in a high degree in that good of woman who be now one of the first of angel which as it be not of a glaring kind more commonly escape observation so little indeed be it remark that i want a word to express it
i must use negative on this occasion
i never hear anything of pertness or what be call repartee out of her mouth no pretence to wit much less to that kind of wisdom which be the result only of great learning and experience the affectation of which in a young woman be as absurd as any of the affectation of an ape
no dictatorial sentiment no judicial opinion no profound criticism
whenever i have see she in the company of man she hath be all attention with the modesty of a learner not the forwardness of a teacher
youwill pardon i for it but i once to try she only desire her opinion on a point which be controvert between mr thwackum and mr square
to which she answer with much sweetness you will pardon i good mr allworthy i be sure you cannot in earnest think i capable of decide any point in which two such gentleman disagree thwackum and square who both alike think themselves sure of a favourable decision second my request
she answer with the same good humour i must absolutely be excuse for i will affront neither so much as to give my judgment on his side indeed she always shew the high deference to the understanding of man a quality absolutely essential to the making a good wife
i shall only add that as she be most apparently void of all affectation this deference must be certainly real here blifil sigh bitterly upon which western whose eye be full of tear at the praise of sophia blubber out donot be chicken-hearte for shat ha her d--n i shat ha she if she be twenty time as good remember your promise sir cry allworthy i be not to be interrupt well shat unt answer the squire i willnot speak another word now my good friend continue allworthy i have dwell so long on the merit of this young lady partly as i really be in love with her character and partly that fortune for the match in that light be really advantageous on my nephew s side might not be imagine to be my principal view in have so eagerly embrace the proposal
indeed i heartily wish to receive so great a jewel into my family but though i may wish for many good thing i would not therefore steal they or be guilty of any violence or injustice to possess myself of they
now to force a woman into a marriage contrary to her consent or approbation be an act of such injustice and oppression that i wish the law of our country could restrain it but a good conscience be never lawless in the worst regulated state and will provide those law for itself which the neglect of legislator hath forget to supply
this be surely a case of that kind for be it not cruel nay impious to force a woman into that state against her will for her behaviour in which she be to be accountable to the high and most dreadful court of judicature and to answer at the peril of her soul
to discharge the matrimonial duty in an adequate manner be no easy task and shall we lay this burthen upon a woman while we at the same time deprive she of all that assistance which may enable she to undergo it
shall we tear her very heart from she while we enjoin her duty to which a whole heart be scarce equal
i must speak very plainly here
i think parent who act in this manner be accessory to all the guilt which their child afterwards incur and of course must before a just judge expect to partake of their punishment but if they could avoid this good heaven
be there a soul who can bear the thought of have contribute to the damnation of his child
for these reason my good neighbour as i see the inclination of this young lady be most unhappily averse to my nephew i must decline any further thought of the honour you intend he though i assure you i shall always retain the most grateful sense of it well sir say western the froth burst forth from his lip the moment they be uncorked you cannot say but i have hear you out and now i expect youwill hear i and if i donot answer every word on t why then iwill consent to gee the matter up
first then i desire you to answer i one question--do not i beget she
do not i beget she
answer i that
they say indeed it be a wise father that know his own child but i be sure i have the good title to she for i breed she up

i ask you that be i not to govern my own child

why may i not hope then by such perseverance at last to gain those inclination in which for the future i shall perhaps have no rival for as for this lord mr western be so kind to prefer i to he and sure sir you will not deny but that a parent hath at least a negative voice in these matter nay i have hear this very young lady herself say so more than once and declare that she think child inexcusable who marry in direct opposition to the will of their parent

i can tell you landlord be a vast comical bitch you will like un hugely mr allworthy at last agree to this invitation and soon after the squire go off sing and caper at the hope of see the speedy tragical end of poor jones
when he be go mr allworthy resume the aforesaid subject with much gravity
he tell his nephew he wish with all his heart he would endeavour to conquer a passion in which i cannot say he flatter you with any hope of succeed
it be certainly a vulgar error that aversion in a woman may be conquer by perseverance
indifference may perhaps sometimes yield to it but the usual triumph gain by perseverance in a lover be over caprice prudence affectation and often an exorbitant degree of levity which excite woman not over-warm in their constitution to indulge their vanity by prolong the time of courtship even when they be well enough pleased with the object and resolve if they ever resolve at all to make he a very pitiful amend in the end

besides my dear i have another apprehension which you must excuse

chapter iv
an extraordinary scene between sophia and her aunt
the lowing heifer and the bleat ewe in herd and flock may ramble safe and unregarde through the pasture
these be indeed hereafter doom to be the prey of man yet many year be they suffer to enjoy their liberty undisturbed
but if a plump doe be discover to have escape from the forest and to repose herself in some field or grove the whole parish be presently alarm every man be ready to set his dog after she and if she be preserve from the rest by the good squire it be only that he may secure she for his own eating
i have often consider a very fine young woman of fortune and fashion when first find stray from the pale of her nursery to be in pretty much the same situation with this doe
the town be immediately in an uproar she be hunt from park to play from court to assembly from assembly to her own chamber and rarely escape a single season from the jaw of some devourer or other for if her friend protect she from some it be only to deliver she over to one of their own chusing often more disagreeable to she than any of the rest while whole herd or flock of other woman securely and scarce regard traverse the park the play the opera and the assembly and though for the most part at least they be at last devour yet for a long time do they wanton in liberty without disturbance or controul
of all these paragon none ever taste more of this persecution than poor sophia

chapter v
mrs miller and mr nightingale visit jones in the prison
when mr allworthy and his nephew go to meet mr western mrs miller set forwards to her son-in-law s lodging in order to acquaint he with the accident which have befall his friend jones but he have know it long before from partridge for jones when he leave mrs miller have be furnish with a room in the same house with mr nightingale
the good woman find her daughter under great affliction on account of mr jones whom have comfort as well as she could she set forwards to the gatehouse where she hear he be and where mr nightingale be arrive before she
the firmness and constancy of a true friend be a circumstance so extremely delightful to person in any kind of distress that the distress itself if it be only temporary and admit of relief be more than compensate by bring this comfort with it
nor be instance of this kind so rare as some superficial and inaccurate observer have report
to say the truth want of compassion be not to be number among our general fault
the black ingredient which foul our disposition be envy
hence our eye be seldom i be afraid turn upward to those who be manifestly great well wiser or happy than ourselves without some degree of malignity while we commonly look downwards on the mean and miserable with sufficient benevolence and pity
in fact i have remark that most of the defect which have discover themselves in the friendship within my observation have arise from envy only a hellish vice and yet one from which i have know very few absolutely exempt
but enough of a subject which if pursue would lead i too far
whether it be that fortune be apprehensive lest jones should sink under the weight of his adversity and that she might thus lose any future opportunity of torment he or whether she really abate somewhat of her severity towards he she seem a little to relax her persecution by send he the company of two such faithful friend and what be perhaps more rare a faithful servant
for partridge though he have many imperfection want not fidelity and though fear would not suffer he to be hang for his master yet the world i believe could not have bribe he to desert his cause

chapter vi

when she be drest therefore down she go resolve to encounter all the horror of the day and a most disagreeable one it prove for lady bellaston take every opportunity very civilly and slily to insult she to all which her dejection of spirit disable she from make any return and indeed to confess the truth she be at the very good but an indifferent mistress of repartee
another misfortune which befel poor sophia be the company of lord fellamar whom she meet at the opera and who attend she to the drum
and though both place be too publick to admit of any particularity and she be far relieved by the musick at the one place and by the card at the other she could not however enjoy herself in his company for there be something of delicacy in woman which will not suffer they to be even easy in the presence of a man whom they know to have pretension to they which they be disinclined to favour
have in this chapter twice mention a drum a word which our posterity it be hop will not understand in the sense it be here apply we shall notwithstanding our present haste stop a moment to describe the entertainment here mean and the rather as we can in a moment describe it
a drum then be an assembly of well-dress person of both sex most of whom play at card and the rest do nothing at all while the mistress of the house perform the part of the landlady at an inn and like the landlady of an inn pride herself in the number of her guest though she doth not always like she get anything by it
no wonder then as so much spirit must be require to support any vivacity in these scene of dulness that we hear person of fashion eternally complain of the want of they a complaint confine entirely to upper life
how insupportable must we imagine this round of impertinence to have be to sophia at this time how difficult must she have find it to force the appearance of gaiety into her look when her mind dictate nothing but the tender sorrow and when every thought be charge with torment idea
night however at last restore she to her pillow where we will leave she to soothe her melancholy at least though incapable we fear of rest and shall pursue our history which something whisper we be now arrive at the eve of some great event
chapter vii
a pathetic scene between mr allworthy and mrs miller
mrs miller have a long discourse with mr allworthy at his return from dinner in which she acquaint he with jones s have unfortunately lose all which he be pleased to bestow on he at their separation and with the distress to which that loss have subject he of all which she have receive a full account from the faithful retailer partridge
she then explain the obligation she have to jones not that she be entirely explicit with regard to her daughter for though she have the utmost confidence in mr allworthy and though there could be no hope of keep an affair secret which be unhappily know to more than half a dozen yet she could not prevail with herself to mention those circumstance which reflect most on the chastity of poor nancy but smother that part of her evidence as cautiously as if she have be before a judge and the girl be now on her trial for the murder of a bastard
allworthy say there be few character so absolutely vicious as not to have the least mixture of good in they
however say he i cannot deny but that you have some obligation to the fellow bad as he be and i shall therefore excuse what hath past already but must insist you never mention his name to i more for i promise you it be upon the full and plainest evidence that i resolve to take the measure i have take well sir say she i make not the least doubt but time will shew all matter in their true and natural colour and that you will be convince this poor young man deserve well of you than some other folk that shall be nameless madam cry allworthy a little ruffle i will not hear any reflection on my nephew and if ever you say a word more of that kind i will depart from your house that instant
he be the worthy and good of man and i once more repeat it to you he hath carry his friendship to this man to a blameable length by too long conceal fact of the black die
the ingratitude of the wretch to this good young man be what i most resent for madam i have the great reason to imagine he have lay a plot to supplant my nephew in my favour and to have disinherit he i be sure sir answer mrs miller a little frightened for though mr allworthy have the utmost sweetness and benevolence in his smile he have great terror in his frown i shall never speak against any gentleman you be pleased to think well of
i be sure sir such behaviour would very little become i especially when the gentleman be your near relation but sir you must not be angry with i you must not indeed for my good wish to this poor wretch
sure i may call he so now though once you would have be angry with i if i have speak of he with the least disrespect
how often have i hear you call he your son
how often have you prattle to i of he with all the fondness of a parent
nay sir i cannot forget the many tender expression the many good thing you have tell i of his beauty and his part and his virtue of his good-nature and generosity
i be sure sir i cannot forget they for i find they all true
i have experience they in my own cause
they have preserve my family
you must pardon my tear sir indeed you must
when i consider the cruel reverse of fortune which this poor youth to whom i be so much oblige hath suffer when i consider the loss of your favour which i know he value more than his life i must i must lament he
if you have a dagger in your hand ready to plunge into my heart i must lament the misery of one whom you have love and i shall ever love allworthy be pretty much move with this speech but it seem not to be with anger for after a short silence take mrs miller by the hand he say very affectionately to she come madam let we consider a little about your daughter
i cannot blame you for rejoice in a match which promise to be advantageous to she but you know this advantage in a great measure depend on the father s reconciliation
i know mr nightingale very well and have formerly have concern with he i will make he a visit and endeavour to serve you in this matter
i believe he be a worldly man but as this be an only son and the thing be now irretrievable perhaps he may in time be bring to reason
i promise you i will do all i can for you many be the acknowledgment which the poor woman make to allworthy for this kind and generous offer nor could she refrain from take this occasion again to express her gratitude towards jones to whom say she i owe the opportunity of give you sir this present trouble allworthy gently stop she but he be too good a man to be really offend with the effect of so noble a principle as now actuate mrs miller and indeed have not this new affair inflame his former anger against jones it be possible he might have be a little softened towards he by the report of an action which malice itself could not have derive from an evil motive
mr allworthy and mrs miller have be above an hour together when their conversation be put an end to by the arrival of blifil and another person which other person be no less than mr dowling the attorney who be now become a great favourite with mr blifil and whom mr allworthy at the desire of his nephew have make his steward and have likewise recommend he to mr western from whom the attorney receive a promise of be promote to the same office upon the first vacancy and in the meantime be employ in transact some affair which the squire then have in london in relation to a mortgage
this be the principal affair which then bring mr dowling to town therefore he take the same opportunity to charge himself with some money for mr allworthy and to make a report to he of some other business in all which as it be of much too dull a nature to find any place in this history we will leave the uncle nephew and their lawyer concern and resort to other matter
chapter viii
contain various matter
before we return to mr jones we will take one more view of sophia
though that young lady have bring her aunt into great good humour by those soothe method which we have before relate she have not bring she in the least to abate of her zeal for the match with lord fellamar
this zeal be now inflame by lady bellaston who have tell she the precede evening that she be well satisfied from the conduct of sophia and from her carriage to his lordship that all delay would be dangerous and that the only way to succeed be to press the match forward with such rapidity that the young lady should have no time to reflect and be oblige to consent while she scarce know what she do in which manner she say one-half of the marriage among people of condition be bring about
a fact very probably true and to which i suppose be owe the mutual tenderness which afterwards exist among so many happy couple
a hint of the same kind be give by the same lady to lord fellamar and both these so readily embrace the advice that the very next day be at his lordship s request appoint by mrs western for a private interview between the young party
this be communicate to sophia by her aunt and insist upon in such high term that after have urge everything she possibly could invent against it without the least effect she at last agree to give the high instance of complacence which any young lady can give and consent to see his lordship
as conversation of this kind afford no great entertainment we shall be excuse from recite the whole that past at this interview in which after his lordship have make many declaration of the most pure and ardent passion to the silent blushing sophia she at last collect all the spirit she could raise and with a trembling low voice say my lord you must be yourself conscious whether your former behaviour to i hath be consistent with the profession you now make be there answer he no way by which i can atone for madness
what i do i be afraid must have too plainly convinced you that the violence of love have deprive i of my sens indeed my lord say she it be in your power to give i a proof of an affection which i much rather wish to encourage and to which i should think myself more beholden name it madam say my lord very warmly
my lord say she look down upon her fan i know you must be sensible how uneasy this pretended passion of yours hath make i can you be so cruel to call it pretend say he
yes my lord answer sophia all profession of love to those whom we persecute be most insulting pretence
this pursuit of yours be to i a most cruel persecution nay it be take a most ungenerous advantage of my unhappy situation most lovely most adorable charmer do not accuse i cry he of take an ungenerous advantage while i have no thought but what be direct to your honour and interest and while i have no view no hope no ambition but to throw myself honour fortune everything at your foot my lord say she it be that fortune and those honour which give you the advantage of which i complain
these be the charm which have seduce my relation but to i they be thing indifferent
if your lordship will merit my gratitude there be but one way pardon i divine creature say he there can be none
all i can do for you be so much your due and will give i so much pleasure that there be no room for your gratitude indeed my lord answer she you may obtain my gratitude my good opinion every kind think and wish which it be in my power to bestow nay you may obtain they with ease for sure to a generous mind it must be easy to grant my request
let i beseech you then to cease a pursuit in which you can never have any success
for your own sake as well as mine i entreat this favour for sure you be too noble to have any pleasure in torment an unhappy creature
what can your lordship propose but uneasiness to yourself by a perseverance which upon my honour upon my soul cannot shall not prevail with i whatever distress you may drive i to here my lord fetch a deep sigh and then said-- is it then madam that i be so unhappy to be the object of your dislike and scorn or will you pardon i if i suspect there be some other here he hesitate and sophia answer with some spirit my lord i shall not be accountable to you for the reason of my conduct
i be oblige to your lordship for the generous offer you have make i own it be beyond either my desert or expectation yet i hope my lord you will not insist on my reason when i declare i cannot accept it lord fellamar return much to this which we do not perfectly understand and perhaps it could not all be strictly reconcile either to sense or grammar but he conclude his rant speech with say that if she have pre-engage herself to any gentleman however unhappy it would make he he should think himself bind in honour to desist perhaps my lord lay too much emphasis on the word gentleman for we cannot else well account for the indignation with which he inspire sophia who in her answer seem greatly to resent some affront he have give she
while she be speak with her voice more raise than usual mrs western come into the room the fire glare in her cheek and the flame burst from her eye
i be ashamed say she my lord of the reception which you have meet with
i assure your lordship we be all sensible of the honour do we and i must tell you miss western the family expect a different behaviour from you here my lord interfere on behalf of the young lady but to no purpose the aunt proceed till sophia pull out her handkerchief throw herself into a chair and burst into a violent fit of tear
the remainder of the conversation between mrs western and his lordship till the latter withdraw consist of bitter lamentation on his side and on hers of the strong assurance that her niece should and would consent to all he wish
indeed my lord say she the girl hath have a foolish education neither adapt to her fortune nor her family
her father i be sorry to say it be to blame for everything
the girl hath silly country notion of bashfulness
nothing else my lord upon my honour i be convinced she hath a good understanding at the bottom and will be bring to reason this last speech be make in the absence of sophia for she have some time before leave the room with more appearance of passion than she have ever show on any occasion and now his lordship after many expression of thank to mrs western many ardent profession of passion which nothing could conquer and many assurance of perseverance which mrs western highly encouraged take his leave for this time
before we relate what now pass between mrs western and sophia it may be proper to mention an unfortunate accident which have happen and which have occasion the return of mrs western with so much fury as we have see
the reader then must know that the maid who at present attend on sophia be recommend by lady bellaston with whom she have live for some time in the capacity of a comb-brush she be a very sensible girl and have receive the strict instruction to watch her young lady very carefully
these instruction we be sorry to say be communicate to she by mrs honour into whose favour lady bellaston have now so ingratiated herself that the violent affection which the good waiting-woman have formerly bear to sophia be entirely obliterate by that great attachment which she have to her new mistress
now when mrs miller be depart betty for that be the name of the girl return to her young lady find she very attentively engage in read a long letter and the visible emotion which she betray on that occasion might have well account for some suspicion which the girl entertain but indeed they have yet a strong foundation for she have overhear the whole scene which pass between sophia and mrs miller
mrs western be acquaint with all this matter by betty who after receive many commendation and some reward for her fidelity be order that if the woman who bring the letter come again she should introduce she to mrs western herself
unluckily mrs miller return at the very time when sophia be engage with his lordship
betty accord to order send she directly to the aunt who be mistress of so many circumstance relate to what have past the day before easily impose upon the poor woman to believe that sophia have communicate the whole affair and so pump everything out of she which she know relate to the letter and relate to jones
this poor creature might indeed be call simplicity itself
she be one of that order of mortal who be apt to believe everything which be say to they to whom nature hath neither indulge the offensive nor defensive weapon of deceit and who be consequently liable to be impose upon by any one who will only be at the expense of a little falshood for that purpose
mrs western have drain mrs miller of all she know which indeed be but little but which be sufficient to make the aunt suspect a great deal dismiss she with assurance that sophia would not see she that she would send no answer to the letter nor ever receive another nor do she suffer she to depart without a handsome lecture on the merit of an office to which she could afford no well name than that of procuress this discovery have greatly discompose her temper when come into the apartment next to that in which the lover be she overhear sophia very warmly protest against his lordship s address
at which the rage already kindle burst forth and she rush in upon her niece in a most furious manner as we have already describe together with what past at that time till his lordship s departure
no soon be lord fellamar go than mrs western return to sophia whom she upbraid in the most bitter term for the ill use she have make of the confidence repose in she and for her treachery in converse with a man with whom she have offer but the day before to bind herself in the most solemn oath never more to have any conversation
sophia protest she have maintain no such conversation
how how
miss western say the aunt will you deny your receive a letter from he yesterday a letter madam answer sophia somewhat surprized
it be not very well breed miss reply the aunt to repeat my word
i say a letter and i insist upon your show it i immediately i scorn a lie madam say sophia i do receive a letter but it be without my desire and indeed i may say against my consent indeed indeed miss cry the aunt you ought to be ashamed of own you have receive it at all but where be the letter
for i will see it to this peremptory demand sophia pause some time before she return an answer and at last only excuse herself by declare she have not the letter in her pocket which be indeed true upon which her aunt lose all manner of patience ask her niece this short question whether she would resolve to marry lord fellamar or no
to which she receive the strong negative
mrs western then reply with an oath or something very like one that she would early the next morning deliver she back into her father s hand

i will never marry contrary to my father s consent nor without ask yours----and when i ask the consent of either improperly it will be then time enough to force some other marriage upon i can i bear to hear this cry mrs western from a girl who hath now a letter from a murderer in her pocket i have no such letter i promise you answer sophia and if he be a murderer he will soon be in no condition to give you any further disturbance how miss western say the aunt have you the assurance to speak of he in this manner to own your affection for such a villain to my face sure madam say sophia you put a very strange construction on my word indeed miss western cry the lady i shall not bear this usage you have learn of your father this manner of treat i he hath teach you to give i the lie

i will withdraw all my force from the field and remain henceforth like the wise king of prussia in a state of perfect neutrality
you be both too wise to be regulate by my measure so prepare yourself for to-morrow morning you shall evacuate this house sophia remonstrate all she could but her aunt be deaf to all she say
in this resolution therefore we must at present leave she as there seem to be no hope of bring she to change it
chapter ix
what happen to mr jones in the prison
mr jones pass about twenty-four melancholy hour by himself unless when relieve by the company of partridge before mr nightingale return not that this worthy young man have desert or forgot his friend for indeed he have be much the great part of the time employ in his service
he have hear upon enquiry that the only person who have see the beginning of the unfortunate rencounter be a crew belong to a man-of-war which then lie at deptford
to deptford therefore he go in search of this crew where he be inform that the man he seek after be all go ashore
he then trace they from place to place till at last he find two of they drink together with a third person at a hedge-tavern near aldersgate
nightingale desire to speak with jones by himself for partridge be in the room when he come in
as soon as they be alone nightingale take jones by the hand cry come my brave friend be not too much deject at what i be go to tell you----i be sorry i be the messenger of bad news but i think it my duty to tell you i guess already what that bad news be cry jones
the poor gentleman then be dead i hope not answer nightingale
he be alive this morning though i will not flatter you i fear from the account i could get that his wound be mortal
but if the affair be exactly as you tell it your own remorse would be all you would have reason to apprehend let what would happen but forgive i my dear tom if i entreat you to make the bad of your story to your friend
if you disguise anything to we you will only be an enemy to yourself what reason my dear jack have i ever give you say jones to stab i with so cruel a suspicion have patience cry nightingale and i will tell you all

indeed what i be sorry to repeat as i be afraid of the consequence of it to you
they say that they be at too great a distance to overhear any word that pass between you but they both agree that the first blow be give by you then upon my soul answer jones they injure i
he not only strike i first but strike i without the least provocation
what should induce those villain to accuse i falsely nay that i cannot guess say nightingale and if you yourself and i who be so heartily your friend cannot conceive a reason why they should belie you what reason will an indifferent court of justice be able to assign why they should not believe they
i repeat the question to they several time and so do another gentleman who be present who i believe be a seafaring man and who really act a very friendly part by you for he beg they often to consider that there be the life of a man in the case and ask they over and over if they be certain to which they both answer that they be and would abide by their evidence upon oath
for heaven s sake my dear friend recollect yourself for if this should appear to be the fact it will be your business to think in time of make the good of your interest

my friend cry jones what interest hath such a wretch as i

if i have any friend as alas

the faith of nightingale be now again stagger and begin to incline to credit his friend when mrs miller appear and make a sorrowful report of the success of her embassy which when jones have hear he cry out most heroically well my friend i be now indifferent as to what shall happen at least with regard to my life and if it be the will of heaven that i shall make an atonement with that for the blood i have spill i hope the divine goodness will one day suffer my honour to be clear and that the word of a die man at least will be believe so far as to justify his character a very mournful scene now past between the prisoner and his friend at which as few reader would have be pleased to be present so few i believe will desire to hear it particularly related
we will therefore pass on to the entrance of the turnkey who acquaint jones that there be a lady without who desire to speak with he when he be at leisure
jones declare his surprize at this message
he say he know no lady in the world whom he could possibly expect to see there however as he see no reason to decline see any person mrs miller and mr nightingale presently take their leave and he give order to have the lady admit
if jones be surprize at the news of a visit from a lady how greatly be he astonish when he discover this lady to be no other than mrs waters
in this astonishment then we shall leave he awhile in order to cure the surprize of the reader who will likewise probably not a little wonder at the arrival of this lady
who this mrs waters be the reader pretty well know what she be he must be perfectly satisfied
he will therefore be pleased to remember that this lady depart from upton in the same coach with mr fitzpatrick and the other irish gentleman and in their company travel to bath
now there be a certain office in the gift of mr fitzpatrick at that time vacant namely that of a wife for the lady who have lately fill that office have resign or at least desert her duty
mr fitzpatrick therefore have thoroughly examine mrs waters on the road find she extremely fit for the place which on their arrival at bath he presently confer upon she and she without any scruple accept
as husband and wife this gentleman and lady continue together all the time they stay at bath and as husband and wife they arrive together in town
whether mr fitzpatrick be so wise a man as not to part with one good thing till he have secure another which he have at present only a prospect of regain or whether mrs waters have so well discharge her office that he intend still to retain she as principal and to make his wife as be often the case only her deputy i will not say but certain it be he never mention his wife to she never communicate to she the letter give he by mrs western nor ever once hint his purpose of repossess his wife much less do he ever mention the name of jones
for though he intend to fight with he wherever he meet he he do not imitate those prudent person who think a wife a mother a sister or sometimes a whole family the safe second on these occasion
the first account therefore which she have of all this be deliver to she from his lip after he be bring home from the tavern where his wound have be drest
as mr fitzpatrick however have not the clear way of tell a story at any time and be now perhaps a little more confused than usual it be some time before she discover that the gentleman who have give he this wound be the very same person from whom her heart have receive a wound which though not of a mortal kind be yet so deep that it have leave a considerable scar behind it
but no soon be she acquaint that mr jones himself be the man who have be commit to the gatehouse for this suppose murder than she take the first opportunity of commit mr fitzpatrick to the care of his nurse and hasten away to visit the conqueror
she now enter the room with an air of gaiety which receive an immediate check from the melancholy aspect of poor jones who start and bless himself when he see she
upon which she say nay i do not wonder at your surprize i believe you do not expect to see i for few gentleman be trouble here with visit from any lady unless a wife
you see the power you have over i mr jones
indeed i little thought when we part at upton that our next meeting would have be in such a place indeed madam say jones i must look upon this visit as kind few will follow the miserable especially to such dismal habitation i protest mr jones say she i can hardly persuade myself you be the same agreeable fellow i see at upton
why your face be more miserable than any dungeon in the universe
what can be the matter with you i think madam say jones as you know of my be here you know the unhappy reason pugh say she you have pink a man in a duel thatbe all jones expr some indignation at this levity and speak with the utmost contrition for what have happen
to which she answer well then sir if you take it so much to heart i will relieve you the gentleman be not dead and i be pretty confident be in no danger of die
the surgeon indeed who first dress he be a young fellow and seem desirous of represent his case to be as bad as possible that he might have the more honour from cure he but the king s surgeon hath see he since and say unless from a fever of which there be at present no symptom he apprehend not the least danger of life jones shew great satisfaction in his countenance at this report upon which she affirm the truth of it add by the most extraordinary accident in the world i lodge at the same house and have see the gentleman and i promise you he doth you justice and say whatever be the consequence that he be entirely the aggressor and that you be not in the least to blame jones express the utmost satisfaction at the account which mrs waters bring he
he then inform she of many thing which she well know before as who mr fitzpatrick be the occasion of his resentment c
