mrs waters with great pleasantry ridicule all this as the effect of low spirit and confinement

we shall therefore suppress the rest of this conversation and only observe that it end at last with perfect innocence and much more to the satisfaction of jones than of the lady for the former be greatly transport with the news she have bring he but the latter be not altogether so pleased with the penitential behaviour of a man whom she have at her first interview conceive a very different opinion of from what she now entertain of he

book xviii
contain about six day
chapter i
a farewel to the reader
we be now reader arrive at the last stage of our long journey
as we have therefore travel together through so many page let we behave to one another like fellow-traveller in a stage coach who have pass several day in the company of each other and who notwithstanding any bickering or little animosity which may have occur on the road generally make all up at last and mount for the last time into their vehicle with chearfulness and good humour since after this one stage it may possibly happen to we as it commonly happen to they never to meet more
as i have here take up this simile give i leave to carry it a little far
i intend then in this last book to imitate the good company i have mention in their last journey
now it be well know that all joke and raillery be at this time lay aside whatever character any of the passenger have for the jest-sake personate on the road be now throw off and the conversation be usually plain and serious
in the same manner if i have now and then in the course of this work indulge any pleasantry for thy entertainment i shall here lay it down
the variety of matter indeed which i shall be oblige to cram into this book will afford no room for any of those ludicrous observation which i have elsewhere make and which may sometimes perhaps have prevent thee from take a nap when it be begin to steal upon thee
in this last book thou wilt find nothing or at most very little of that nature
all will be plain narrative only and indeed when thou hast peruse the many great event which this book will produce thou wilt think the number of page contain in it scarce sufficient to tell the story
and now my friend i take this opportunity as i shall have no other of heartily wish thee well
if i have be an entertaining companion to thee i promise thee it be what i have desire
if in anything i have offend it be really without any intention
some thing perhaps here say may have hit thee or thy friend but i do most solemnly declare they be not point at thee or they
i question not but thou hast be tell among other story of i that thou wast to travel with a very scurrilous fellow but whoever tell thee so do i an injury
no man detest and despise scurrility more than myself nor hath any man more reason for none hath ever be treat with more and what be a very severe fate i have have some of the abusive writing of those very man father upon i who in other of their work have abuse i themselves with the utmost virulence
all these work however i be well convinced will be dead long before this page shall offer itself to thy perusal for however short the period may be of my own performance they will most probably outlive their own infirm author and the weakly production of his abusive contemporary

contain a very tragical incident

incest----with a mother

if the reader will please to refresh his memory by turn to the scene at upton in the ninth book he will be apt to admire the many strange accident which unfortunately prevent any interview between partridge and mrs waters when she spend a whole day there with mr jones
instance of this kind we may frequently observe in life where the great event be produce by a nice train of little circumstance and more than one example of this may be discover by the accurate eye in this our history
after a fruitless search of two or three hour partridge return back to his master without have see mrs waters

o mr jones little do i think when i past that happy day at upton the reflection upon which be like to embitter all my future life who it be to whom i owe such perfect happiness
believe i to be ever sincerely your unfortunate j
water p s
i would have you comfort yourself as much as possible for mr fitzpatrick be in no manner of danger so that whatever other grievous crime you may have to repent of the guilt of blood be not among the number jones have read the letter let it drop for he be unable to hold it and indeed have scarce the use of any one of his faculty
partridge take it up and have receive consent by silence read it likewise nor have it upon he a less sensible effect
the pencil and not the pen should describe the horror which appear in both their countenance
while they both remain speechless the turnkey enter the room and without take any notice of what sufficiently discover itself in the face of they both acquaint jones that a man without desire to speak with he
this person be presently introduce and be no other than black george
as sight of horror be not so usual to george as they be to the turnkey he instantly see the great disorder which appear in the face of jones
this he impute to the accident that have happen which be report in the very bad light in mr western s family he conclude therefore that the gentleman be dead and that mr jones be in a fair way of come to a shameful end
a thought which give he much uneasiness for george be of a compassionate disposition and notwithstanding a small breach of friendship which he have be over-tempt to commit be in the main not insensible of the obligation he have formerly receive from mr jones
the poor fellow therefore scarce refrain from a tear at the present sight
he tell jones he be heartily sorry for his misfortune and beg he to consider if he could be of any manner of service
perhaps sir say he you may want a little matter of money upon this occasion if you do sir what little i have be heartily at your service jones shake he very heartily by the hand and give he many thank for the kind offer he have make but answer he have not the least want of that kind upon which george begin to press his service more eagerly than before
jones again thank he with assurance that he want nothing which be in the power of any man live to give
come come my good master answer george do not take the matter so much to heart
thing may end well than you imagine to be sure you an t the first gentleman who hath kill a man and yet come off you be wide of the matter george say partridge the gentleman be not dead nor like to die
donot disturb my master at present for he be troubled about a matter in which it be not in your power to do he any good you donot know what i may be able to do mr partridge answer george if his concern be about my young lady i have some news to tell my master what do you say mr george cry jones
hath anything lately happen in which my sophia be concern
my sophia
how dare such a wretch as i mention she so profanely i hope she will be yours yet answer george
why yes sir i have something to tell you about she
madam western hath just bring madam sophia home and there hath be a terrible to do
i could not possibly learn the very right of it but my master he hath be in a vast big passion and so be madam western and i hear she say as she go out of door into her chair that she would never set she foot in master s house again
i donot know whatbe the matter not i but everything be very quiet when i come out but robin who wait at supper say he have never see the squire for a long while in such good humour with young madam that he kiss she several time and swear she should be her own mistress and he never would think of confine she any more
i think this news would please you and so i slip out though it be so late to inform you of it mr jones assure george that it do greatly please he for though he should never more presume to lift his eye toward that incomparable creature nothing could so much relieve his misery as the satisfaction he should always have in hearing of her welfare
the rest of the conversation which pass at the visit be not important enough to be here relate
the reader will therefore forgive we this abrupt break off and be pleased to hear how this great good-will of the squire towards his daughter be bring about
mrs western on her first arrival at her brother s lodging begin to set forth the great honour and advantage which would accrue to the family by the match with lord fellamar which her niece have absolutely refuse in which refusal when the squire take the part of his daughter she fall immediately into the most violent passion and so irritated and provoke the squire that neither his patience nor his prudence could bear it any long upon which there ensue between they both so warm a bout at altercation that perhaps the region of billingsgate never equal it
in the heat of this scold mrs western depart and have consequently no leisure to acquaint her brother with the letter which sophia receive which might have possibly produce ill effect but to say truth i believe it never once occur to her memory at this time
when mrs western be go sophia who have be hitherto silent as well indeed from necessity as inclination begin to return the compliment which her father have make she in take her part against her aunt by take his likewise against the lady
this be the first time of she so do and it be in the high degree acceptable to the squire
again he remember that mr allworthy have insist on an entire relinquishment of all violent mean and indeed as he make no doubt but that jones would be hang he do not in the least question succeed with his daughter by fair mean he now therefore once more give a loose to his natural fondness for she which have such an effect on the dutiful grateful tender and affectionate heart of sophia that have her honour give to jones and something else perhaps in which he be concern be remove i much doubt whether she would not have sacrifice herself to a man she do not like to have oblige her father
she promise he she would make it the whole business of her life to oblige he and would never marry any man against his consent which bring the old man so near to his high happiness that he be resolve to take the other step and go to bed completely drunk
chapter iii
allworthy visit old nightingale with a strange discovery that he make on that occasion
the morning after these thing have happen mr allworthy go accord to his promise to visit old nightingale with whom his authority be so great that after have sit with he three hour he at last prevail with he to consent to see his son
here an accident happen of a very extraordinary kind one indeed of those strange chance whence very good and grave man have conclude that providence often interpose in the discovery of the most secret villany in order to caution man from quit the path of honesty however warily they tread in those of vice
mr allworthy at his entrance into mr nightingale s see black george he take no notice of he nor do black george imagine he have perceive he
however when their conversation on the principal point be over allworthy ask nightingale whether he know one george seagrim and upon what business he come to his house
yes answer nightingale i know he very well and a most extraordinary fellow he be who in these day hath be able to hoard up 500 from rent a very small estate of 30 a year and be this the story which he hath tell you cry allworthy
nay it be true i promise you say nightingale for i have the money now in my own hand in five bank-bill which i be to lay out either in a mortgage or in some purchase in the north of england the bank-bill be no soon produce at allworthy s desire than he bless himself at the strangeness of the discovery
he presently tell nightingale that these bank-bill be formerly his and then acquaint he with the whole affair
as there be no man who complain more of the fraud of business than highwayman gamester and other thief of that kind so there be none who so bitterly exclaim against the fraud of gamester c as usurer broker and other thief of this kind whether it be that the one way of cheat be a discountenance or reflection upon the other or that money which be the common mistress of all cheat make they regard each other in the light of rival but nightingale no soon hear the story than he exclaim against the fellow in term much severe than the justice and honesty of allworthy have bestow on he
allworthy desire nightingale to retain both the money and the secret till he should hear far from he and if he should in the meantime see the fellow that he would not take the least notice to he of the discovery which he have make
he then return to his lodging where he find mrs miller in a very dejected condition on account of the information she have receive from her son-in-law
mr allworthy with great chearfulness tell she that he have much good news to communicate and with little further preface acquaint she that he have bring mr nightingale to consent to see his son and do not in the least doubt to effect a perfect reconciliation between they though he find the father more sowere by another accident of the same kind which have happen in his family
he then mention the run away of the uncle s daughter which he have be tell by the old gentleman and which mrs miller and her son-in-law do not yet know
the reader may suppose mrs miller receive this account with great thankfulness and no less pleasure but so uncommon be her friendship to jones that i be not certain whether the uneasiness she suffer for his sake do not overbalance her satisfaction at hear a piece of news tend so much to the happiness of her own family nor whether even this very news as it remind she of the obligation she have to jones do not hurt as well as please she when her grateful heart say to she while my own family be happy how miserable be the poor creature to whose generosity we owe the beginning of all this happiness allworthy have leave she a little while to chew the cud if i may use that expression on these first tiding tell she he have still something more to impart which he believe would give her pleasure
i think say he i have discover a pretty considerable treasure belong to the young gentleman your friend but perhaps indeed his present situation may be such that it will be of no service to he the latter part of the speech give mrs miller to understand who be mean and she answer with a sigh i hope not sir i hope so too cry allworthy with all my heart but my nephew tell i this morning he have hear a very bad account of the affair good heaven
sir say she-- well i must not speak and yet it be certainly very hard to be oblige to hold one s tongue when one hear madam say allworthy you may say whatever you please you know i too well to think i have a prejudice against any one and as for that young man i assure you i should be heartily pleased to find he could acquit himself of everything and particularly of this sad affair
you can testify the affection i have formerly bear he
the world i know censure i for love he so much
i do not withdraw that affection from he without think i have the just cause
believe i mrs miller i should be glad to find i have be mistaken mrs miller be go eagerly to reply when a servant acquaint she that a gentleman without desire to speak with she immediately
allworthy then enquire for his nephew and be tell that he have be for some time in his room with the gentleman who use to come to he and whom mr allworthy guess rightly to be mr dowling he desire presently to speak with he
when dowling attend allworthy put the case of the bank-note to he without mention any name and ask in what manner such a person might be punish
to which dowling answer he think he might be indict on the black act but say as it be a matter of some nicety it would be proper to go to counsel
he say he be to attend counsel presently upon an affair of mr western s and if mr allworthy please he would lay the case before they this be agree to and then mrs miller open the door cry i ask pardon i do not know you have company but allworthy desire she to come in say he have finish his business
upon which mr dowling withdraw and mrs miller introduce mr nightingale the young to return thank for the great kindness do he by allworthy but she have scarce patience to let the young gentleman finish his speech before she interrupt he say o sir
mr nightingale bring great news about poor mr jones he hath be to see the wound gentleman who be out of all danger of death and what be more declare he fall upon poor mr jones himself and beat he
i be sure sir you would not have mr jones be a coward
if i be a man myself i be sure if any man be to strike i i should draw my sword
do pray my dear tell mr allworthy tell he all yourself nightingale then confirm what mrs miller have say and conclude with many handsome thing of jones who be he say one of the well-natured fellow in the world and not in the least inclined to be quarrelsome
here nightingale be go to cease when mrs miller again beg he to relate all the many dutiful expression he have hear he make use of towards mr allworthy
to say the utmost good of mr allworthy cry nightingale be do no more than strict justice and can have no merit in it but indeed i must say no man can be more sensible of the obligation he hath to so good a man than be poor jones
indeed sir i be convince the weight of your displeasure be the heavy burthen he lie under
he hath often lament it to i and hath as often protest in the most solemn manner he hath never be intentionally guilty of any offence towards you nay he hath swear he would rather die a thousand death than he would have his conscience upbraid he with one disrespectful ungrateful or undutiful thought towards you
but i ask pardon sir i be afraid i presume to intermeddle too far in so tender a point you have speak no more than what a christian ought cry mrs miller
indeed mr nightingale answer allworthy i applaud your generous friendship and i wish he may merit it of you
i confess i be glad to hear the report you bring from this unfortunate gentleman and if that matter should turn out to be as you represent it and indeed i doubt nothing of what you say i may perhaps in time be bring to think well than lately i have of this young man for this good gentlewoman here nay all who know i can witness that i love he as dearly as if he have be my own son
indeed i have consider he as a child send by fortune to my care
i still remember the innocent the helpless situation in which i find he
i feel the tender pressure of his little hand at this moment
he be my darling indeed he be at which word he cease and the tear stand in his eye
as the answer which mrs miller make may lead we into fresh matter we will here stop to account for the visible alteration in mr allworthy s mind and the abatement of his anger to jones
revolution of this kind it be true do frequently occur in history and dramatic writer for no other reason than because the history or play draw to a conclusion and be justify by authority of author yet though we insist upon as much authority as any author whatever we shall use this power very sparingly and never but when we be drive to it by necessity which we do not at present foresee will happen in this work
this alteration then in the mind of mr allworthy be occasion by a letter he have just receive from mr square and which we shall give the reader in the beginning of the next chapter
chapter iv
contain two letter in very different stile
my worthy friend i inform you in my last that i be forbid the use of the water as they be find by experience rather to increase than lessen the symptom of my distemper
i must now acquaint you with a piece of news which i believe will afflict my friend more than it hath afflict i
dr harrington and dr brewster have inform i that there be no hope of my recovery
i have somewhere read that the great use of philosophy be to learn to die
i will not therefore so far disgrace mine as to shew any surprize at receive a lesson which i must be think to have so long study
yet to say the truth one page of the gospel teach this lesson well than all the volume of antient or modern philosopher
the assurance it give we of another life be a much strong support to a good mind than all the consolation that be draw from the necessity of nature the emptiness or satiety of our enjoyment here or any other topic of those declamation which be sometimes capable of arm our mind with a stubborn patience in bear the thought of death but never of raise they to a real contempt of it and much less of make we think it be a real good
i would not here be understand to throw the horrid censure of atheism or even the absolute denial of immortality on all who be call philosopher
many of that sect as well antient as modern have from the light of reason discover some hope of a future state but in reality that light be so faint and glimmering and the hope be so incertain and precarious that it may be justly doubt on which side their belief turn
plato himself conclude his phaedon with declare that his good argument amount only to raise a probability and cicero himself seem rather to profess an inclination to believe than any actual belief in the doctrine of immortality
as to myself to be very sincere with you i never be much in earnest in this faith till i be in earnest a christian
you will perhaps wonder at the latter expression but i assure you it hath not be till very lately that i could with truth call myself so
the pride of philosophy have intoxicate my reason and the sublimest of all wisdom appear to i as it do to the greeks of old to be foolishness
god hath however be so gracious to shew i my error in time and to bring i into the way of truth before i sink into utter darkness forever
i find myself begin to grow weak i shall therefore hasten to the main purpose of this letter
when i reflect on the action of my past life i know of nothing which sit heavy upon my conscience than the injustice i have be guilty of to that poor wretch your adopt son
i have indeed not only connive at the villany of other but be myself active in injustice towards he
believe i my dear friend when i tell you on the word of a die man he hath be basely injure
as to the principal fact upon the misrepresentation of which you discard he i solemnly assure you he be innocent
when you lay upon your suppose deathbe he be the only person in the house who testify any real concern and what happen afterwards arise from the wildness of his joy on your recovery and i be sorry to say it from the baseness of another person but it be my desire to justify the innocent and to accuse none
believe i my friend this young man hath the noble generosity of heart the most perfect capacity for friendship the high integrity and indeed every virtue which can ennoble a man
he hath some fault but among they be not to be number the least want of duty or gratitude towards you
on the contrary i be satisfied when you dismiss he from your house his heart bleed for you more than for himself
worldly motif be the wicked and base reason of my concealing this from you so long to reveal it now i can have no inducement but the desire of serve the cause of truth of do right to the innocent and of make all the amend in my power for a past offence
i hope this declaration therefore will have the effect desire and will restore this deserve young man to your favour the hearing of which while i be yet alive will afford the utmost consolation to sir your most oblige obedient humble servant thomas square the reader will after this scarce wonder at the revolution so visibly appear in mr allworthy notwithstanding he receive from thwackum by the same post another letter of a very different kind which we shall here add as it may possibly be the last time we shall have occasion to mention the name of that gentleman
sir i be not at all surprize at hear from your worthy nephew a fresh instance of the villany of mr square the atheist s young pupil
i shall not wonder at any murder he may commit and i heartily pray that your own blood may not seal up his final commitment to the place of wail and gnashing of tooth
though you cannot want sufficient call to repentance for the many unwarrantable weakness exemplify in your behaviour to this wretch so much to the prejudice of your own lawful family and of your character i say though these may sufficiently be suppose to prick and goad your conscience at this season i should yet be want to my duty if i spar to give you some admonition in order to bring you to a due sense of your error
i therefore pray you seriously to consider the judgment which be likely to overtake this wicked villain and let it serve at least as a warning to you that you may not for the future despise the advice of one who be so indefatigable in his prayer for your welfare
have not my hand be withhold from due correction i have scourge much of this diabolical spirit out of a boy of whom from his infancy i discover the devil have take such entire possession
but reflection of this kind now come too late
i be sorry you have give away the living of westerton so hastily
i should have apply on that occasion early have i think you would not have acquaint i previous to the disposition your objection to plurality be be righteous over-much
if there be any crime in the practice so many godly man would not agree to it
if the vicar of aldergrove should die as we hear he be in a decline way i hope you will think of i since i be certain you must be convince of my most sincere attachment to your high welfare--a welfare to which all worldly consideration be as trifle as the small tithe mention in scripture be when compare to the weighty matter of the law
i be sir your faithful humble servant roger thwackum this be the first time thwackum ever write in this authoritative stile to allworthy and of this he have afterwards sufficient reason to repent as in the case of those who mistake the high degree of goodness for the low degree of weakness
allworthy have indeed never like this man
he know he to be proud and ill-natured he also know that his divinity itself be tincture with his temper and such as in many respect he himself do by no mean approve but he be at the same time an excellent scholar and most indefatigable in teach the two lad
add to this the strict severity of his life and manner an unimpeached honesty and a most devout attachment to religion
so that upon the whole though allworthy do not esteem nor love the man yet he could never bring himself to part with a tutor to the boy who be both by learning and industry extremely well qualified for his office and he hop that as they be breed up in his own house and under his own eye he should be able to correct whatever be wrong in thwackum s instruction
chapter v
in which the history be continue
mr allworthy in his last speech have recollect some tender idea concern jones which have bring tear into the good man s eye
this mrs miller observe say yes yes sir your goodness to this poor young man be know notwithstanding all your care to conceal it but there be not a single syllable of truth in what those villain say
mr nightingale hath now discover the whole matter
it seem these fellow be employ by a lord who be a rival of poor mr jones to have press he on board a ship i assure they i donot know who they will press next
mr nightingale here hath see the officer himself who be a very pretty gentleman and hath tell he all and be very sorry for what he undertake which he would never have do have he know mr jones to have be a gentleman but he be tell that he be a common stroll vagabond allworthy star at all this and declare he be a stranger to every word she say
yes sir answer she i believe you are it be a very different story i believe from what those fellow tell this lawyer what lawyer madam
what be it you mean say allworthy
nay nay say she this be so like you to deny your own goodness but mr nightingale here see he see whom madam answer he
why your lawyer sir say she that you so kindly send to enquire into the affair i be still in the dark upon my honour say allworthy
why then do you tell he my dear sir cry she
indeed sir say nightingale i do see that very lawyer who go from you when i come into the room at an alehouse in aldersgate in company with two of the fellow who be employ by lord fellamar to press mr jones and who be by that mean present at the unhappy rencounter between he and mr fitzpatrick i own sir say mrs miller when i see this gentleman come into the room to you i tell mr nightingale that i apprehend you have send he thither to inquire into the affair allworthy shew mark of astonishment in his countenance at this news and be indeed for two or three minute strike dumb by it
at last address himself to mr nightingale he say i must confess myself sir more surprized at what you tell i than i have ever be before at anything in my whole life
be you certain this be the gentleman i be most certain answer nightingale
at aldersgate cry allworthy
and be you in company with this lawyer and the two fellows i be sir say the other very near half an hour well sir say allworthy and in what manner do the lawyer behave
do you hear all that past between he and the fellow no sir answer nightingale they have be together before i came in my presence the lawyer say little but after i have several time examine the fellow who persist in a story directly contrary to what i have hear from mr jones and which i find by mr fitzpatrick be a rank falshood the lawyer then desire the fellow to say nothing but what be the truth and seem to speak so much in favour of mr jones that when i see the same person with you i conclude your goodness have prompt you to send he thither and do you not send he thither say mrs miller indeed i do not answer allworthy nor do i know he have go on such an errand till this moment i see it all say mrs miller upon my soul i see it all
no wonder they have be closet so close lately
son nightingale let i beg you run for these fellow immediately----find they out if they be above-ground
i will go myself dear madam say allworthy be patient and do i the favour to send a servant upstairs to call mr dowling hither if he be in the house or if not mr blifil mrs miller go out mutter something to herself and presently return with an answer that mr dowling be go but that the t other as she call he be come allworthy be of a cool disposition than the good woman whose spirit be all up in arm in the cause of her friend
he be not however without some suspicion which be near akin to her
when blifil come into the room he ask he with a very serious countenance and with a less friendly look than he have ever before give he whether he know anything of mr dowling s have see any of the person who be present at the duel between jones and another gentleman there be nothing so dangerous as a question which come by surprize on a man whose business it be to conceal truth or to defend falshood
for which reason those worthy personage whose noble office it be to save the life of their fellow-creature at the old bailey take the utmost care by frequent previous examination to divine every question which may be ask their client on the day of tryal that they may be supply with proper and ready answer which the most fertile invention cannot supply in an instant
besides the sudden and violent impulse on the blood occasion by these surprize cause frequently such an alteration in the countenance that the man be oblige to give evidence against himself
and such indeed be the alteration which the countenance of blifil undergo from this sudden question that we can scarce blame the eagerness of mrs miller who immediately cry out guilty upon my honour
guilty upon my soul mr allworthy sharply rebuke she for this impetuosity and then turn to blifil who seem sink into the earth he say why do you hesitate sir at give i an answer
you certainly must have employ he for he would not of his own accord i believe have undertake such an errand and especially without acquaint i blifil then answer i own sir i have be guilty of an offence yet may i hope your pardon my pardon say allworthy very angrily nay sir answer blifil i know you would be offend yet surely my dear uncle will forgive the effect of the most amiable of human weakness
compassion for those who do not deserve it i own be a crime and yet it be a crime from which you yourself be not entirely free
i know i have be guilty of it in more than one instance to this very person and i will own i do send mr dowling not on a vain and fruitless enquiry but to discover the witness and to endeavour to soften their evidence
this sir be the truth which though i intend to conceal from you i will not deny i confess say nightingale this be the light in which it appear to i from the gentleman s behaviour now madam say allworthy i believe you will once in your life own you have entertain a wrong suspicion and be not so angry with my nephew as you be mrs miller be silent for though she could not so hastily be please with blifil whom she look upon to have be the ruin of jones yet in this particular instance he have impose upon she as well as upon the rest so entirely have the devil stand his friend
and indeed i look upon the vulgar observation that the devil often desert his friend and leave they in the lurch to be a great abuse on that gentleman s character
perhaps he may sometimes desert those who be only his cup acquaintance or who at most be but half his but he generally stand by those who be thoroughly his servant and help they off in all extremity till their bargain expire
as a conquer rebellion strengthen a government or as health be more perfectly establish by recovery from some disease so anger when remove often give new life to affection
this be the case of mr allworthy for blifil have wipe off the great suspicion the less which have be raise by square s letter sink of course and be forget and thwackum with whom he be greatly offend bore alone all the reflection which square have cast on the enemy of jones
as for that young man the resentment of mr allworthy begin more and more to abate towards he
he tell blifil he do not only forgive the extraordinary effort of his good-nature but would give he the pleasure of follow his example then turn to mrs miller with a smile which would have become an angel he cry what say you madam
shall we take a hackney-coach and all of we together pay a visit to your friend
i promise you it be not the first visit i have make in a prison every reader i believe will be able to answer for the worthy woman but they must have a great deal of good-nature and be well acquaint with friendship who can feel what she feel on this occasion
few i hope be capable of feel what now pass in the mind of blifil but those who be will acknowledge that it be impossible for he to raise any objection to this visit
fortune however or the gentleman lately mention above stand his friend and prevent his undergoing so great a shock for at the very instant when the coach be send for partridge arrive and have call mrs miller from the company acquaint she with the dreadful accident lately come to light and hear mr allworthy s intention beg she to find some mean of stop he for say he the matter must at all hazard be keep a secret from he and if he should now go he will find mr jones and his mother who arrive just as i leave he lament over one another the horrid crime they have ignorantly commit the poor woman who be almost deprived of her sens at his dreadful news be never less capable of invention than at present
however as woman be much readier at this than man she bethink herself of an excuse and return to allworthy say i be sure sir you will be surprize at hear any objection from i to the kind proposal you just now make and yet i be afraid of the consequence of it if carry immediately into execution
you must imagine sir that all the calamity which have lately befall this poor young fellow must have throw he into the low dejection of spirit and now sir should we all on a sudden fle he into such a violent fit of joy as i know your presence will occasion it may i be afraid produce some fatal mischief especially as his servant who be without tell i he be very far from be well be his servant without cry allworthy pray call he hither
i will ask he some question concern his master partridge be at first afraid to appear before mr allworthy but be at length persuade after mrs miller who have often hear his whole story from his own mouth have promise to introduce he
allworthy recollect partridge the moment he come into the room though many year have pass since he have see he
mrs miller therefore might have spar here a formal oration in which indeed she be something prolix for the reader i believe may have observe already that the good woman among other thing have a tongue always ready for the service of her friend
and be you say allworthy to partridge the servant of mr jones i cannot say sir answer he that i be regularly a servant but i live with he an t please your honour at present
non sum qualis eram as your honour very well know mr allworthy then ask he many question concern jones as to his health and other matter to all which partridge answer without have the least regard to what be but consider only what he would have thing appear for a strict adherence to truth be not among the article of this honest fellow s morality or his religion
during this dialogue mr nightingale take his leave and presently after mrs miller leave the room when allworthy likewise despatch blifil for he imagine that partridge when alone with he would be more explicit than before company
they be no soon leave in private together than allworthy begin as in the following chapter
chapter vi
in which the history be far continue sure friend say the good man you be the strange of all human being
not only to have suffer as you have formerly for obstinately persist in a falshood but to persist in it thus to the last and to pass thus upon the world for a servant of your own son
what interest can you have in all this
what can be your motive i see sir say partridge fall down upon his knee that your honour be prepossess against i and resolve not to believe anything i say and therefore what signify my protestation
but yet there be one above who know that i be not the father of this young man how say allworthy will you yet deny what you be formerly convict of upon such unanswerable such manifest evidence
nay what a confirmation be your be now find with this very man of all which twenty year ago appear against you
i think you have leave the country
nay i think you have be long since dead in what manner do you know anything of this young man
where do you meet with he unless you have keep some correspondence together
do not deny this for i promise you it will greatly raise your son in my opinion to find that he hath such a sense of filial duty as privately to support his father for so many year if your honour will have patience to hear i say partridge i will tell you all being bid go on he proceed thus when your honour conceive that displeasure against i it end in my ruin soon after for i lose my little school and the minister think i suppose it would be agreeable to your honour turn i out from the office of clerk so that i have nothing to trust to but the barber s shop which in a country place like that be a poor livelihood and when my wife die for till that time i receive a pension of 12 a year from an unknown hand which indeed i believe be your honour s own for nobody that ever i hear of doth these thing besides but as i be say when she die this pension forsook i so that now as i owe two or three small debt which begin to be troublesome to i particularly one which an attorney bring up by law-charge from 15 s
to near 30 and as i find all my usual mean of living have forsook i i pack up my little all as well as i could and go off
this be a fact which i know happen to a poor clergyman in dorsetshire by the villany of an attorney who not content with the exorbitant cost to which the poor man be put by a single action bring afterwards another action on the judgment as it be call
a method frequently use to oppress the poor and bring money into the pocket of attorney to the great scandal of the law of the nation of christianity and even of human nature itself
the first place i come to be salisbury where i get into the service of a gentleman belong to the law and one of the good gentleman that ever i know for he be not only good to i but i know a thousand good and charitable act which he do while i staid with he and i have know he often refuse business because it be paultry and oppressive you need not be so particular say allworthy i know this gentleman and a very worthy man he be and an honour to his profession well sir continue partridge from hence i remove to lymington where i be above three year in the service of another lawyer who be likewise a very good sort of a man and to be sure one of the merry gentleman in england
well sir at the end of the three year i set up a little school and be likely to do well again have it not be for a most unlucky accident
here i keep a pig and one day as ill fortune would have it this pig break out and do a trespass i think they call it in a garden belong to one of my neighbour who be a proud revengeful man and employ a lawyer one--one--i cannot think of his name but he send for a writ against i and have i to size
when i come there lord have mercy upon i--to hear what the counsellor say
there be one that tell my lord a parcel of the confoundedest lie about i he say that i use to drive my hog into other folk s garden and a great deal more and at last he say he hop i have at last bring my hog to a fair market
to be sure one would have think that instead of be owner only of one poor little pig i have be the great hog-merchant in england
well-- pray say allworthy do not be so particular i have hear nothing of your son yet o it be a great many year answer partridge before i see my son as you be pleased to call him i go over to ireland after this and teach school at cork for that one suit ruin i again and i lie seven year in winchester jail well say allworthy pass that over till your return to england then sir say he it be about half a year ago that i land at bristol where i staid some time and not find it do there and hearing of a place between that and gloucester where the barber be just dead i go thither and there i have be about two month when mr jones come thither he then give allworthy a very particular account of their first meeting and of everything as well as he could remember which have happen from that day to this frequently interlard his story with panegyric on jones and not forget to insinuate the great love and respect which he have for allworthy
he conclude with say now sir i have tell your honour the whole truth and then repeat a most solemn protestation that he be no more the father of jones than of the pope of rome and imprecate the most bitter curse on his head if he do not speak truth
what be i to think of this matter cry allworthy
for what purpose should you so strongly deny a fact which i think it would be rather your interest to own nay sir answer partridge for he could hold no long if your honour will not believe i you be like soon to have satisfaction enough
i wish you have mistake the mother of this young man as well as you have his father and now be ask what he mean with all the symptom of horror both in his voice and countenance he tell allworthy the whole story which he have a little before express such desire to mrs miller to conceal from he
allworthy be almost as much shock at this discovery as partridge himself have be while he relate it
good heaven say he in what miserable distress do vice and imprudence involve man
how much beyond our design be the effect of wickedness sometimes carry he have scarce utter these word when mrs waters come hastily and abruptly into the room
partridge no soon see she than he cry here sir here be the very woman herself
this be the unfortunate mother of mr jones
i be sure she will acquit i before your honour
pray madam---- mrs waters without pay any regard to what partridge say and almost without take any notice of he advance to mr allworthy
i believe sir it be so long since i have the honour of see you that you do not recollect i indeed answer allworthy you be so very much alter on many account that have not this man already acquaint i who you be i should not have immediately call you to my remembrance
have you madam any particular business which bring you to i allworthy speak this with great reserve for the reader may easily believe he be not well pleased with the conduct of this lady neither with what he have formerly hear nor with what partridge have now deliver
mrs waters answered-- indeed sir i have very particular business with you and it be such as i can impart only to yourself
i must desire therefore the favour of a word with you alone for i assure you what i have to tell you be of the utmost importance partridge be then order to withdraw but before he go he beg the lady to satisfy mr allworthy that he be perfectly innocent
to which she answer you need be under no apprehension sir i shall satisfy mr allworthy very perfectly of that matter then partridge withdraw and that past between mr allworthy and mrs waters which be write in the next chapter

i never can nor shall forget your goodness which i own i have very little deserve but be pleased to wave all upbraid i at present as i have so important an affair to communicate to you concern this young man to whom you have give my maiden name of jones have i then say allworthy ignorantly punish an innocent man in the person of he who hath just leave we

remember there be one from whom you can conceal nothing and before whose tribunal falshood will only aggravate your guilt indeed sir say she i be not his mother nor would i now think myself so for the world i know your reason say allworthy and shall rejoice as much as you to find it otherwise yet you must remember you yourself confest it before i so far what i confest say she be true that these hand convey the infant to your bed convey it thither at the command of its mother at her command i afterwards own it and think myself by her generosity nobly reward both for my secrecy and my shame who could this woman be say allworthy

just after your departure for london miss bridget come one day to the house of my mother
she be pleased to say she have hear an extraordinary character of i for my learning and superior understanding to all the young woman there so she be pleased to say
she then bid i come to she to the great house where when i attend she employ i to read to she
she express great satisfaction in my reading shew great kindness to i and make i many present
at last she begin to catechise i on the subject of secrecy to which i give she such satisfactory answer that at last have lock the door of her room she take i into her closet and then lock that door likewise she say she should convince i of the vast reliance she have on my integrity by communicate a secret in which her honour and consequently her life be concern
she then stopt and after a silence of a few minute during which she often wipe her eye she enquire of i if i think my mother might safely be confide in
i answer i would stake my life on her fidelity
she then impart to i the great secret which labour in her breast and which i believe be deliver with more pain than she afterwards suffer in child-birth
it be then contrive that my mother and myself only should attend at the time and that mrs wilkins should be send out of the way as she accordingly be to the very furth part of dorsetshire to enquire the character of a servant for the lady have turn away her own maid near three month before during all which time i officiate about her person upon trial as she say though as she afterwards declare i be not sufficiently handy for the place
this and many other such thing which she use to say of i be all throw out to prevent any suspicion which wilkins might hereafter have when i be to own the child for she think it could never be believe she would venture to hurt a young woman with whom she have intrust such a secret
you may be assure sir i be well pay for all these affront which together with be inform with the occasion of they very well content i
indeed the lady have a great suspicion of mrs wilkins than of any other person not that she have the least aversion to the gentlewoman but she think she incapable of keep a secret especially from you sir for i have often hear miss bridget say that if mrs wilkins have commit a murder she believe she would acquaint you with it
at last the expect day come and mrs wilkins who have be keep a week in readiness and put off from time to time upon some pretence or other that she might not return too soon be dispatch
then the child be bear in the presence only of myself and my mother and be by my mother convey to her own house where it be privately keep by she till the evening of your return when i by the command of miss bridget convey it into the bed where you find it
and all suspicion be afterwards lay asleep by the artful conduct of your sister in pretend ill-will to the boy and that any regard she shew he be out of meer complacence to you mrs waters then make many protestation of the truth of this story and conclude by say thus sir you have at last discover your nephew for so i be sure you will hereafter think he and i question not but he will be both an honour and a comfort to you under that appellation i need not madam say allworthy express my astonishment at what you have tell i and yet surely you would not and could not have put together so many circumstance to evidence an untruth
i confess i recollect some passage relate to that summer which formerly give i a conceit that my sister have some like to he

a further continuation

chapter xi

he cast himself on his bed where he lie abandon himself to despair and drown in tear not in such tear as flow from contrition and wash away guilt from mind which have be seduce or surprize into it unawares against the bent of their natural disposition as will sometimes happen from human frailty even to the good no these tear be such as the fright thief shed in his cart and be indeed the effect of that concern which the most savage nature be seldom deficient in feeling for themselves
it would be unpleasant and tedious to paint this scene in full length
let it suffice to say that the behaviour of jones be kind to excess
he omit nothing which his invention could supply to raise and comfort the droop spirit of blifil before he communicate to he the resolution of his uncle that he must quit the house that evening
he offer to furnish he with any money he want assure he of his hearty forgiveness of all he have do against he that he would endeavour to live with he hereafter as a brother and would leave nothing unattempte to effectuate a reconciliation with his uncle
blifil be at first sullen and silent balance in his mind whether he should yet deny all but find at last the evidence too strong against he he betake himself at last to confession
he then ask pardon of his brother in the most vehement manner prostrate himself on the ground and kiss his foot in short he be now as remarkably mean as he have be before remarkably wicked
jones could not so far check his disdain but that it a little discover itself in his countenance at this extreme servility
he raise his brother the moment he could from the ground and advise he to bear his affliction more like a man repeat at the same time his promise that he would do all in his power to lessen they for which blifil make many profession of his unworthiness pour forth a profusion of thank and then he have declare he would immediately depart to another lodging jones return to his uncle

indeed my dear uncle you must suffer i to call it weakness rather than ingratitude for i be convince the poor fellow love i and hath do i some kindness which i can never forget nay i believe he hath repent of this very act for it be not above a day or two ago when my affair seem in the most desperate situation that he visit i in my confinement and offer i any money i want
consider sir what a temptation to a man who hath taste such bitter distress it must be to have a sum in his possession which must put he and his family beyond any future possibility of suffer the like child cry allworthy you carry this forgiving temper too far
such mistaken mercy be not only weakness but border on injustice and be very pernicious to society as it encourage vice
the dishonesty of this fellow i might perhaps have pardon but never his ingratitude
and give i leave to say when we suffer any temptation to atone for dishonesty itself we be as candid and merciful as we ought to be and so far i confess i have go for i have often pity the fate of a highwayman when i have be on the grand jury and have more than once apply to the judge on the behalf of such as have have any mitigating circumstance in their case but when dishonesty be attend with any blacker crime such as cruelty murder ingratitude or the like compassion and forgiveness then become fault
i be convince the fellow be a villain and he shall be punish at least as far as i can punish he this be speak with so stern a voice that jones do not think proper to make any reply besides the hour appoint by mr western now draw so near that he have barely time leave to dress himself
here therefore end the present dialogue and jones retire to another room where partridge attend accord to order with his cloath
partridge have scarce see his master since the happy discovery
the poor fellow be unable either to contain or express his transport

chapter the last
in which the history be conclude
young nightingale have be that afternoon by appointment to wait on his father who receive he much more kindly than he expect
there likewise he meet his uncle who be return to town in quest of his new-married daughter
this marriage be the lucky incident which could have happen to the young gentleman for these brother live in a constant state of contention about the government of their child both heartily despise the method which each other take
each of they therefore now endeavour as much as he could to palliate the offence which his own child have commit and to aggravate the match of the other
this desire of triumph over his brother add to the many argument which allworthy have use so strongly operate on the old gentleman that he meet his son with a smile countenance and actually agree to sup with he that evening at mrs miller s
as for the other who really love his daughter with the most immoderate affection there be little difficulty in incline he to a reconciliation
he be no soon inform by his nephew where his daughter and her husband be than he declare he would instantly go to she
and when he arrive there he scarce suffer she to fall upon her knee before he take she up and embrace she with a tenderness which affect all who see he and in less than a quarter of an hour be as well reconcile to both she and her husband as if he have himself join their hand
in this situation be affair when mr allworthy and his company arrive to complete the happiness of mrs miller who no soon see sophia than she guess everything that have happen and so great be her friendship to jones that it add not a few transport to those she feel on the happiness of her own daughter
there have not i believe be many instance of a number of people meet together where every one be so perfectly happy as in this company
amongst whom the father of young nightingale enjoy the least perfect content for notwithstanding his affection for his son notwithstanding the authority and the argument of allworthy together with the other motive mention before he could not so entirely be satisfied with his son s choice and perhaps the presence of sophia herself tend a little to aggravate and heighten his concern as a thought now and then suggest itself that his son might have have that lady or some other such
not that any of the charm which adorn either the person or mind of sophia create the uneasiness it be the content of her father s coffer which set his heart a longing
these be the charm which he could not bear to think his son have sacrifice to the daughter of mrs miller
the bride be both very pretty woman but so totally be they eclipse by the beauty of sophia that have they not be two of the well-temper girl in the world it would have raise some envy in their breast for neither of their husband could long keep his eye from sophia who sit at the table like a queen receive homage or rather like a superior be receive adoration from all around she
but it be an adoration which they give not which she exact for she be as much distinguish by her modesty and affability as by all her other perfection
the evening be spend in much true mirth
all be happy but those the most who have be most unhappy before
their former suffering and fear give such a relish to their felicity as even love and fortune in their full flow could not have give without the advantage of such a comparison
yet as great joy especially after a sudden change and revolution of circumstance be apt to be silent and dwell rather in the heart than on the tongue jones and sophia appear the least merry of the whole company which western observe with great impatience often cry out to they why dost not talk boy
why dost look so grave
hast lose thy tongue girl
drink another glass of wine sha t drink another glass and the more to enliven she he would sometimes sing a merry song which bear some relation to matrimony and the loss of a maidenhead
nay he would have proceed so far on that topic as to have drive she out of the room if mr allworthy have not checkt he sometimes by look and once or twice by a fie
mr western he begin indeed once to debate the matter and assert his right to talk to his own daughter as he think fit but as nobody second he he be soon reduce to order
notwithstanding this little restraint he be so pleased with the chearfulness and good-humour of the company that he insist on their meeting the next day at his lodging
they all do so and the lovely sophia who be now in private become a bride too officiate as the mistress of the ceremony or in the polite phrase do the honour of the table
she have that morning give her hand to jones in the chapel at doctors common where mr allworthy mr western and mrs miller be the only person present
sophia have earnestly desire her father that no other of the company who be that day to dine with he should be acquaint with her marriage
the same secrecy be enjoin to mrs miller and jones undertake for allworthy
this somewhat reconcile the delicacy of sophia to the public entertainment which in compliance with her father s will she be oblige to go to greatly against her own inclination
in confidence of this secrecy she go through the day pretty well till the squire who be now advanced into the second bottle could contain his joy no long but fill out a bumper drink a health to the bride
the health be immediately pledge by all present to the great confusion of our poor blushing sophia and the great concern of jones upon her account
to say truth there be not a person present make wise by this discovery for mrs miller have whisper it to her daughter her daughter to her husband her husband to his sister and she to all the rest
sophia now take the first opportunity of withdraw with the lady and the squire sit in to his cup in which he be by degree desert by all the company except the uncle of young nightingale who love his bottle as well as western himself
these two therefore sit stoutly to it during the whole evening and long after that happy hour which have surrender the charming sophia to the eager arm of she enrapture jones
thus reader we have at length bring our history to a conclusion in which to our great pleasure though contrary perhaps to thy expectation mr jones appear to be the happy of all humankind for what happiness this world afford equal to the possession of such a woman as sophia i sincerely own i have never yet discover
as to the other person who have make any considerable figure in this history as some may desire to know a little more concern they we will proceed in as few word as possible to satisfy their curiosity
allworthy hath never yet be prevail upon to see blifil but he hath yield to the importunity of jones back by sophia to settle 200 a-year upon he to which jones hath privately add a third
upon this income he live in one of the northern county about 200 mile distant from london and lay up 200 a-year out of it in order to purchase a seat in the next parliament from a neighbouring borough which he have bargain for with an attourney there
he be also lately turn methodist in hope of marry a very rich widow of that sect whose estate lie in that part of the kingdom
square die soon after he writ the before-mention letter and as to thwackum he continue at his vicarage
he hath make many fruitless attempt to regain the confidence of allworthy or to ingratiate himself with jones both of whom he flatter to their face and abuse behind their back
but in his stead mr allworthy hath lately take mr abraham adams into his house of whom sophia be grow immoderately fond and declare he shall have the tuition of her child
mrs fitzpatrick be separate from her husband and retain the little remain of her fortune
she live in reputation at the polite end of the town and be so good an economist that she spend three time the income of her fortune without run into debt
she maintain a perfect intimacy with the lady of the irish peer and in act of friendship to her repay all obligation she owe her husband
mrs western be soon reconcile to her niece sophia and hath spend two month together with she in the country
lady bellaston make the latter a formal visit at her return to town where she behave to jones as a perfect stranger and with great civility wish he joy on his marriage
mr nightingale hath purchase an estate for his son in the neighbourhood of jones where the young gentleman his lady mrs miller and her little daughter reside and the most agreeable intercourse subsist between the two family
as to those of low account mrs waters return into the country have a pension of 60 a-year settle upon she by mr allworthy and be married to parson supple on whom at the instance of sophia western hath bestow a considerable living
black george hear the discovery that have be make run away and be never since hear of and jones bestow the money on his family but not in equal proportion for molly have much the great share
as for partridge jones hath settle 50 a-year on he and he hath again set up a school in which he meet with much well encouragement than formerly and there be now a treaty of marriage on foot between he and miss molly seagrim which through the mediation of sophia be likely to take effect
we now return to take leave of mr jones and sophia who within two day after their marriage attend mr western and mr allworthy into the country
western hath resign his family seat and the great part of his estate to his son-in-law and hath retire to a less house of his in another part of the country which be well for hunting
indeed he be often as a visitant with mr jones who as well as his daughter hath an infinite delight in do everything in their power to please he
and this desire of theirs be attend with such success that the old gentleman declare he be never happy in his life till now
he hath here a parlour and ante-chamber to himself where he get drunk with whom he please and his daughter be still as ready as formerly to play to he whenever he desire it for jones hath assure she that as next to please she one of his high satisfaction be to contribute to the happiness of the old man so the great duty which she express and perform to her father render she almost equally dear to he with the love which she bestow on himself
sophia hath already produce he two fine child a boy and a girl of whom the old gentleman be so fond that he spend much of his time in the nursery where he declare the tattling of his little grand-daughter who be above a year and a half old be sweet music than the fine cry of dog in england
allworthy be likewise greatly liberal to jones on the marriage and hath omit no instance of shew his affection to he and his lady who love he as a father
whatever in the nature of jones have a tendency to vice have be correct by continual conversation with this good man and by his union with the lovely and virtuous sophia
he hath also by reflection on his past folly acquire a discretion and prudence very uncommon in one of his lively part
to conclude as there be not to be find a worthy man and woman than this fond couple so neither can any be imagine more happy
they preserve the pure and tender affection for each other an affection daily encrease and confirm by mutual endearment and mutual esteem
nor be their conduct towards their relation and friend less amiable than towards one another
and such be their condescension their indulgence and their beneficence to those below they that there be not a neighbour a tenant or a servant who doth not most gratefully bless the day when mr jones be married to his sophia
finis
